\begin{array} { r l } {  { \mathrm { ~ \sum ~ } _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 } \lfloor _ { S _ { 1 } } \cos { z } - \frac { 1 } { S _ { 1 } } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } + \frac { 2 } { 2 S _ { 1 } } U _ { 5 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) } \rfloor } \quad } & { } \\ & { -  l _ { 1 } l _ { 2 } ^ { 1 2 } \Big [ \frac { 2 } { 2 S _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } U _ { 4 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } + \frac { 2 } { 2 s _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } } U _ { 5 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) } \Big ]  } \\ & { -  l _ { 1 } ^ { 1 2 } l _ { 2 } \Big [ \frac { 1 } { S _ { 1 } } U _ { 6 } \cos { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { s _ { 1 } + 2 s _ { 2 } } U _ { \tau } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ) }  } \\ & { \qquad \quad +  \frac { 1 } { \mathrm { ~ \sum ~ } U _ { 5 } } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } ) } ] } \end{array}
$$

$$
x _ { 2 } = l _ { 2 } - l _ { 1 } l _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Big [ \frac { 1 } { S _ { 2 } } U _ { 3 } \cos y _ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } U _ { 4 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) }
$$

$$
\displaystyle - \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } } U _ { 5 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) } \Biggr ]
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle - \frac { l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } l _ { 2 } } { l _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + 2 s _ { 2 } } U _ { \tau } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ) } - \frac { 2 } { s _ { 1 } - 2 s _ { 2 } } U _ { \tt s } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } ) } } } \\ { { \displaystyle - \frac { l _ { 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 } } { l _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } } \Big [ \frac { 1 } { S _ { 2 } } U _ { \tt s } \cos y _ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { s _ { 2 } } U _ { \tt 1 0 } \cos 3 y _ { 2 } \Big ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle + \ \cdot \cdot \cdot . } } \end{array}
$$

Or we have

$$
x _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { 1 } } , \qquad x _ { 2 } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
W = l _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + l _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } - l _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } \Big [ \frac { 1 } { S _ { 1 } } U _ { 1 } \sin y _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 S _ { 1 } } U _ { 2 } \sin 3 y _ { 1 } \Big ]
$$

$$
- l _ { 1 } l _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Bigl [ \frac { 1 } { s _ { 2 } } U _ { 3 } \sin y _ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } U _ { 4 } \sin ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } } U _ { s } \sin { ( 2 y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) } \Bigg ] } } \\ { { - \displaystyle h _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { U } _ { \delta } \sin y _ { 1 } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 2 } + s _ { 1 } } U _ { \tau } \sin { ( 2 y _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ) } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 2 } - s _ { 1 } } U _ { \star } \sin { ( 2 y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } ) } \Bigg ] } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \frac { b _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \left[ \frac { 1 } { s _ { 2 } } U _ { s } \sin y _ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 s _ { 2 } } U _ { \parallel s } \sin { 3 y _ { 2 } } \right] } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \ldots . . } } \end{array}
$$

Terms which contain $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$ not as arguments of trigonometric functions are of the form $\alpha _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } y _ { 2 }$ ，where

$\alpha _ { 1 } = l _ { 1 } +$ terms of the fourth and higher orders in $l _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and $l _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ， $\alpha _ { 2 } = l _ { 2 } +$ terms of the fourth and higher orders in $l _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and $l _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ·

Inverting these series we obtain $l _ { 1 }$ and $l _ { 2 }$ in terms of $\alpha _ { 1 }$ and $\alpha _ { 2 }$ . Thus

$$
W = \alpha _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } \Bigl [ \frac { 1 } { s _ { 1 } } U _ { 1 } \sin y _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 s _ { 1 } } U _ { 2 } \sin 3 y _ { 1 } \Bigr ]
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } } U _ { \bar { s } } \sin { ( 2 y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) } \Big ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } \Big [ \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { s _ { 1 } } U _ { \bar { s } } \sin y _ { 1 } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 2 } + s _ { 1 } } U _ { \tau } \sin { ( 2 y _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ) } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 s _ { 2 } - s _ { 1 } } U _ { \bar { s } } \sin { ( 2 y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } ) } \Big ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 } \Big [ \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { s _ { 2 } } U _ { \bar { s } } \sin y _ { 2 } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 3 s _ { 2 } } U _ { \bar { s } ^ { 3 } } \sin { 3 y _ { 2 } } \Big ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + \ . . . } } \end{array}
$$

The variables $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$ do not appear except in the combination $\alpha _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } y _ { 2 }$ and in the arguments of trigonometric functions. The system

$$
x _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { 1 } } , \qquad x _ { 2 } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { 2 } } , \qquad \beta _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } , \qquad \beta _ { 2 } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { 2 } }
$$

defines a contact transformation from $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } )$ to $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } )$ and the system

$$
\frac { d \alpha _ { 1 } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \beta _ { 1 } } , \frac { d \alpha _ { 2 } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \beta _ { 2 } } , \frac { d \beta _ { 1 } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } , \frac { d \beta _ { 2 } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial \alpha _ { 2 } }
$$

admits integrals $\alpha _ { 1 } =$ constant, $\alpha _ { 2 } =$ constant. The second pair of these equations give

$$
{ \beta _ { 1 } } = - \frac { { \partial { \cal H } ( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } ) } } { { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } } t + \varepsilon _ { 1 } , \qquad \beta _ { 2 } = - \frac { { \partial { \cal H } ( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } ) } } { { \partial \alpha _ { 2 } } } t + \varepsilon _ { 2 } ,
$$

where $\varepsilon _ { 1 }$ and $\varepsilon _ { 2 }$ are arbitrary constants. Thus the solution is

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = x _ { 1 } , \qquad \frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = x _ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } = - \frac { \partial H ( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } t + \varepsilon _ { 1 } , \qquad \frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { 2 } } = - \frac { \partial H ( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { 2 } } t + \varepsilon _ { 2 } .
$$

Hence the solution for the coordinates and momenta is expressed in trigonometric series with linear functions of $t$ as arguments. The arguments are of the form $m \beta _ { 1 } + n \beta _ { 2 }$ . The change of $\varepsilon _ { 1 }$ without any change of $\bar { \varepsilon } _ { 2 } , \alpha _ { 1 } ,$ and $\alpha _ { 2 }$ makes $l _ { 1 }$ and $l _ { 2 }$ invariant,and hence affects neither the integral (1O3) nor the energy integral,and makes the constant in the integral (1O3) and the energy constant invariant.Thus the infinitesimal transformation corresponding to the integral (1O3) transforms such orbits among such orbits themselves.Hence (1O5） is an adelphic integral of the dynamical system.

Case II.The ratio $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 }$ is rational and equal to $m / n$ , and further there is no term cos $( n y _ { 1 } - m y _ { 2 } )$ in $H _ { 3 }$

The integral (1O3) of Case I has a factor $( n s _ { 1 } - m s _ { 2 } )$ in the denominator, which vanishes for $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 } = m / n$ . This is the real root of the principal difficulty in celestial mechanics,as has been often quoted in the preceding chapters.We remove such terms from the series. Suppose that $s _ { 1 } = 2$ ， $s _ { 2 } = 1$ . The factor $s _ { 1 } - 2 s _ { 2 }$ appearing often in (103) is zero.The equation for $\phi _ { 3 }$ in this case is

$$
2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial \phi _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = 2 \frac { \partial H _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } - \frac { \partial H _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } ,
$$

and those for $\phi _ { 4 } , \phi _ { 5 } , . . .$ are

$2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { r } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial \phi _ { r } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = \mathsf { a }$ know su of ters th type

$$
x _ { 1 } ^ { m / 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { n / 2 } \sin { ( k _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + k _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ) } .
$$

In integrating the equations for $\phi _ { \tau }$ in Case I we have used a particular integral corresponding to each known term of the right-hand member of each equation. If $s _ { 1 } = 2$ ， $s _ { 2 } = 1$ , then the arbitrary part of the solution contains terms of the type proper to $\phi _ { 3 }$ . Hence we write the integral of the equations

$$
2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial \phi _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } \sin y _ { 1 }
$$

in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \phi _ { 3 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } \cos y _ { 1 } + \alpha x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 2 } \cos { ( y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } ) } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\alpha$ is an arbitrary constant.Thus terms with arbitrary coefficients in $\phi _ { 3 } , \phi _ { 4 } , \ldots$ are obtained. These arbitrary coefficients should then be computed so that terms with vanishing denominators are removed from the subsequently determined parts of $\phi$ . Thus an adelphic integral without vanishing denominator is obtained in Case II.

Consider a dynamical system with the Hamiltonian

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { 2 x _ { 1 } \sin ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } y _ { 2 } } \\ & { ~ + { \cfrac { 1 } { 2 ( 1 + 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos y _ { 1 } + x _ { 1 } \cos ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } } \\ & { ~ - { \cfrac { 1 + x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos y _ { 1 } } { ( 1 + 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos y _ { 1 } + x _ { 1 } \cos ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } } , } \end{array} }
$$

or, by expanding this in powers of $x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and $x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { H = 2 x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } [ - \frac { 9 } { 2 } \cos y _ { 1 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \cos 3 y _ { 1 } ] } \\ & { \qquad + \ x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } [ \frac { 7 5 } { 1 6 } + \frac { 2 5 } { 4 } \cos 2 y _ { 1 } + \frac { 2 5 } { 1 6 } \cos 4 y _ { 1 } ] } \\ & { \qquad + \ x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } [ - 3 - 3 \cos 2 y _ { 1 } - 3 \cos 2 y _ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + 2 y _ { 2 } ) } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } ) } ] } \\ & { \qquad + \ x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } [ - \frac { 9 } { 1 6 } - \frac { 5 } { 4 } \cos 2 y _ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \cos 4 y _ { 2 } ] } \\ & { \qquad + \ \cdot \cdot . } \end{array}
$$

Since the lowest degree term of the adelphic integral is $x _ { 2 }$ ， we have

$$
\phi = x _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 3 } + \phi _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

The $\phi _ { 3 }$ is determined by

$$
2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial \phi _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = 0 ,
$$

or by

$$
\phi _ { 3 } = \alpha x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 2 } \cos { ( y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } ) } ,
$$

with an arbitrary constant $\alpha$ . Similarly $\phi _ { 4 }$ is obtained from

$$
2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { 4 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial \phi _ { 4 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } [ ( 6 + { \textstyle \frac { 9 } { 2 } } \alpha ) \sin 2 y _ { 2 } + ( 3 + { \textstyle \frac { 9 } { 4 } } \alpha ) \sin ( 2 y _ { 1 } + 2 y _ { 2 } )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { - \left( 3 + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \alpha \right) \sin \left( 2 y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } \right) ] } } \\ { { + \ x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } [ \frac { 3 } { 2 } \sin 2 y _ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \sin 4 y _ { 2 } ] } } \end{array}
$$

in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \phi _ { 4 } = x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } [ - ( 3 + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \alpha ) \cos 2 y _ { 2 } - ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 8 } \alpha ) \cos ( 2 y _ { 1 } + 2 y _ { 2 } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { + ( \frac { 9 } { 2 } + \frac { 9 } { 8 } \alpha ) \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } ) } ] + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } [ - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \cos { 2 y _ { 2 } } - \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \cos { 4 y _ { 2 } } ] . } \end{array}
$$

The equation for $\phi _ { 5 }$ is

$$
2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { 5 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial y _ { 5 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = \frac { \partial H _ { 5 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } + ( \phi _ { 3 } , H _ { 4 } ) + ( \phi _ { 4 } , H _ { 3 } ) .
$$

We determine $_ { \alpha }$ so that the terms sin $( y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } )$ should disappear from the right-hand members of this equation,that is,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 3 9 } { 2 } - \frac { 4 5 } { 2 } ( 1 + \frac { 3 } { 4 } \alpha ) + \frac { 1 2 3 } { 8 } \alpha = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\alpha = - 2 .
$$

Substituting this value of $_ { \alpha }$ in $\phi _ { 3 }$ and $\phi _ { 4 }$ the integral is

$$
= x _ { 2 } - 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 2 } \cos { ( y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } ) }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { + \ x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } [ \frac { 3 } { 2 } \cos \ 2 y _ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \cos \ ( 2 y _ { 1 } + 2 y _ { 2 } ) - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \cos \ ( 2 y _ { 1 } - 2 y _ { 2 } ) ] } \\ & { + \ x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } [ - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \cos \ 2 y _ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } \cos \ 4 y _ { 2 } ] } \\ & { + \ \cdot \cdot \cdot . } & { { \mathrm { ( 1 1 0 ) } } } \end{array}
$$

Thus we see that the dynamical system specified by (1O9) has an integral of the form

$$
\mathrm { \Delta t } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ \sqrt { 2 x _ { 2 } } \sin y _ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \sqrt { 2 x _ { 2 } } \sin y _ { 1 } \sin y _ { 2 } - 2 \sqrt { 2 x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } \sin y _ { 1 } \sin y _ { 2 } ] ^ { 2 }
$$

$$
- \frac { 1 + x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos y _ { 1 } } { ( 1 + 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos y _ { 1 } + x _ { 1 } \cos ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } ,
$$

This is an adelphic integral, as can be seen easily. By expanding (111) in ascending powers of $x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and $x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ by the multinomial theorem,we see that the series is identical with (11O).Hence the series obtained in this Case II is convergent in all real values of $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$ as far as $\left| x _ { 1 } \right|$ and $\left| x _ { 2 } \right|$ are smaller than a fixed number.

Case III. The ratio $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 }$ is rational and equal to $m / n$ ，but there is a term cos $( n y _ { 1 } - m y _ { 2 } )$ in $H _ { 3 }$ ·

Let one of the integrals of the diferential equations in terms of $( x _ { 1 } ,$ $x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } )$ be

$$
\begin{array} { r } { f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } ) + \frac { g ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } ) } { \mu } = \gamma , } \end{array}
$$

where $\gamma$ is an arbitrary constant and $\mu$ is a definite constant expressed in terms of the quantities appearing in the differential equations.This kind of integral loses its meaning when $\mu$ tends to zero.However, we can deduce an integral which has its meaning even for $\mu \to 0$ Suppose that $\mu \neq 0$ and write

$$
\mu f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } ) + g ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } ) = \mu \gamma .
$$

Let $\mu \to 0$ ，then

$$
g ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } ) = c , \qquad c = \operatorname * { l i m } _ { \mu  0 } { ( \mu \gamma ) } .
$$

We apply this principle to the dynamical system defined by the Hamiltonian

$$
\begin{array} { r } { H = x _ { 1 } - 2 x _ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } U _ { 1 } \cos y _ { 1 } + x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 4 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } . } \end{array}
$$

The adelphic integral obtained by the method which has been used for Case I from the Hamiltonian

$$
H = s _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } U _ { 1 } \cos y _ { 1 } + x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 4 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) }
$$

with $s _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 }$ arbitrary is in the form

constant

$$
\begin{array} { r l } {  { = s _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } U _ { 1 } \cos y _ { 1 } } } \\ & { + \frac { 2 s _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } } { 2 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 4 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } } \\ & { + \frac { s _ { 2 } } { 2 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } U _ { 1 } U _ { 4 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \Big [ \frac { 4 \cos { ( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } } { s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } } \\ & { \phantom { + } + \frac { 2 \cos { ( 3 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } } { 3 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } \Big ] } \end{array}
$$

$$
+ \ U _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } U _ { 4 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \frac { S _ { 2 } } { 2 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } \biggl [ - \frac { 3 } { 3 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } \frac { \cos { ( 4 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } } { 4 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } }
$$

$2 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 }$ is the vanishing denominator in this series for $s _ { 1 } = 1$ ， $s _ { 2 } = - 2$ We modify this series so that the vanishing denominator disappears.

By the principle just described we proceed as follows.

The lowest term affected by the vanishing denominator is

$$
\frac { 2 s _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } } { 2 s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 4 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } .
$$

Hence the corresponding lowest term in the integral is

$$
x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } .
$$

Write this integral in the form

$$
= \phi = x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } + \phi _ { 4 } + \phi _ { 5 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $\phi _ { \tau }$ is an aggregate of terms of order $r$ in $x _ { 1 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and $x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ . Substituting this in the equation $( \phi , H ) = 0$ we get from the fourth-order terms the equation for $\phi _ { 4 }$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { 4 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } - 2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { 4 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 1 } [ 2 \sin { ( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } + \sin { ( 3 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } ] . } \end{array}
$$

The integral is

$$
\phi _ { 4 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 1 } [ 2 \cos { ( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } - \cos { ( 3 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } ] .
$$

We may add terms of the form

$$
\alpha x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \beta x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } + \gamma x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }
$$

with arbitrary constants $\alpha , \beta , \gamma$ to this integral. This is equivalent to an addition of an arbitrary quadratic function of the energy integral and the adelphic integral. However the adelphic integral in the present case does not begin with linear terms in $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ . Hence we can determine the arbitrary constants of (114) so that terms with the vanishing denominator disappear from higher-order terms of $\phi$ . Thus we write

$$
\phi _ { 4 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 / 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 1 } [ 2 \cos { ( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } - \cos { ( 3 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } ] + \alpha x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }
$$

and substitute in the equation for $\phi _ { 5 }$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { 5 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } - 2 \frac { \partial \phi _ { 5 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = ( \phi _ { 4 } , H _ { 3 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

The terms containing sin $( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } )$ on the right-hand side are

$$
- 3 x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \sin { ( 2 y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) } - 4 \alpha x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } U _ { 4 } \sin { ( }
$$

If

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \alpha = - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \frac { U _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { U _ { 4 } } , } \end{array}
$$

then all these terms vanish.In this way we can obtain the adelphic integral without the vanishing denominator by repeated application of this procedure.

Thus the adelphic integral is obtained for Case II and Case III.It can be employed for solving the system of differential equations in each of the three cases.The coordinates are expanded, however,in different forms according as the three cases. This circumstance supplies an underlying explanation of Poincaré's theorem that the series employed in celestial mechanics do not converge uniformly for any continuous range of values,because the series referred to are of the type discussed in Case I,and the series in Case I should be replaced by the series in Case II or in Case III when the constants $s _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 }$ vary continuously from an irrational value to a rational value of the ratio


<!-- chunk 0003: pages 141-210 -->
$s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 }$ . This solution by the use of the adelphic integral has the advantage of enabling us to define the form of the corresponding integral in an easy manner.

A similar circumstance to this adelphic integral,which has different representations according as the value of the ratio, may occur in some dynamical problems.

# 14.16THE THIRD INTEGRAL

Contopoulos (1958,1960,1963) pointed out the existence of a third integral for the differential equations of motion of an individual star in a galactic system with a potential of a certain type.

Let $\pmb { I }$ be the integral in question and $I , I _ { i }$ be the complete set of independent integrals. Consider the space $\mathbf { \Phi } _ { I _ { i } } = a _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \dotsc , p )$ ， where $a _ { i }$ are constants.The integral $\pmb { I }$ is said to isolate in this space if there exists at least one pair of sets $\boldsymbol { Q }$ ， $Q ^ { \prime }$ each with positive measure in this space, such that for all $P \in { \cal Q }$ ， $P ^ { \prime } \in Q ^ { \prime }$ we have $I ( P ) \neq I ( P ^ { \prime } )$ . If for almost all sets $( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { p } )$ the integral $\pmb { I }$ does not isolate in the corresponding space $\bar { I } _ { i } = a _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \dotsc , p )$ ， then $\pmb { I }$ is said to be non-isolating (Lynden-Bell, 1962;Wintner, 1947).

Recently Hénon and Heiles (1964) examined the existence of a third isolating integral of motion in an axially symmetric potential

$$
V ( x , y ) = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + 2 x ^ { 2 } y - { \textstyle { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } } y ^ { 3 } )
$$

by numerical experiments. We have the energy integral

$$
V ( x , y ) + { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \dot { x } ^ { 2 } + \dot { y } ^ { 2 } ) = E .
$$

A trajectory for a point in the phase space $( x , y , { \dot { x } } , { \dot { y } } )$ is plotted in a three-dimensional space $( x , y , \dot { y } )$ by the presence of the energy integral. The condition expressed by the inequality

$$
V ( x , y ) + \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \dot { y } ^ { 2 } \le E
$$

defines normally a bounded volume.If there is no other isolating integral,then the trajectory fills everywhere densely the volume defined by the inequality and is called ergodic (Sections 2.7,2.8,and 2.9). Consider the successive intersections of a trajectory with the plane $x = 0$ in the upward direction，that is,the successive points of the trajectory lying on the $( x , { \dot { y } } )$ -plane with $x = 0$ ， $\dot { x } > 0$ . In general, there will be an infinite sequence of such points,called successive consequents by Poincaré (1885a, 1899； Section 23.4). A transformation of a point of intersection to the next succeeding point of intersection is a kind of surface transformation (Section 25.18). An invariant point of this transformation corresponds to a periodic solution. The curve connecting the successive consequents is the projection on $x = 0$ of the trajectory.If there exists no isolating integral other than the known integrals, then these successive consequents fill an area $A : V ( 0 , y ) + \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \dot { y } ^ { 2 } \equiv E$ ， $x = 0$ ，of the surface $x = 0$ .But,if there exists another isolating integral,then such points lie on a curve on the surface.This is the method followed by Henon and Heiles for examining the existence of a third integral.

Consider a system with given energy and angular momentum. If the energy is small, then,according to Henon and Heiles,a third integral seems to exist.If the energy is higher than a certain critical energyin this case $E = 0$ —-then there are an infinite number of separated regions in the phase space where such a third integral still seems to exist. The space left free between these regions is the ergodic region, where the third integral is non-isolating.If the energy is further increased, then the proportion of allowable phase space occupied by this ergodic region increases very rapidly with the increasing energy and tends to occupy the whole space.

Figure 1a shows the distribution of the successive consequents on the plane $x = 0$ for $E = 1 / 1 2$ Each set of points linked by a curve

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/950768c083715ecfa6045f0c465b29075884b29b2348bb5fc68de77a00fa114c.jpg)  
Figure 14.1a

$$
E = { \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } }
$$

(Hénon and Heiles,1964)

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/ed342263bb955acdaee9bec5a5e4b50aec2deef9c801cd7d127962a57c6be163.jpg)  
Figure 14.1b

$$
E = \frac 1 8
$$

(Hénon and Heiles,1964)

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/2901f431c7cfb92727414cf5c63097c57de4ec19bc49d18b8f00e4dd9f51b784.jpg)  
Figure 14.1c

$$
E = { \frac { 1 } { 1 6 } }
$$

(Hénon and Heiles,1964)

corresponds to one computed trajectory and such curves form a one-parameter family which fills the available area $\pmb { A }$ completely.This shows the existence of a third integral, as was indicated by Contopoulos (1958,1963) and Ollongren (1962)．At the middle of the four small loops are four invariant points of the transformation from a point to the following successive points of the trajectory which correspond to stable periodic orbits. The three double-points are also invariant points but correspond to unstable periodic orbits (Sections 9.22,12.1).

Figure 1b fora higher energy $E = 1 / 8$ shows that such curves no longer fill the whole area but the isolated points are distributed at random in the area left free from the closed curves.The five small loops in the right of the diagram belong to the same trajectory.The successive consequents jump from one loop to the next. Hénon and Heiles called this a chain of islands.As the number $q$ of the islands in a chain increases，the dimensions of the islands decrease.Each chain is associated with a stable periodic orbit,the $q$ islands each surrounding the $q$ points which correspond to that orbit.Hénon and Heiles have seen that there is an infinite number of islands,the set of all islands being dense everywhere, but that the islands do not cover the whole area,there being a sea,so to speak,between the islands,and that the ergodic trajectory is dense everywhere.

In Figure 1c with still higher energy, $E = 1 / 6$ ，which is the energy of escape,all isolated points correspond to one trajectory and the ergodic trajectory seems to cover almost the whole area. The open circles in the middle of the diagram correspond to a trajectory of a new kind, intermediate between the closed curves and the ergodic behavior. They are approximately on a figure-eight-shaped curve, but with some dispersion from it.

The figures suggest different behavior of motion accordingly to different regions. Thus the third integral, even though it may be locally convergent,is not uniformly convergent. This is in accord with Poincaré's theorem (Section 6.18).

Contopoulos (1963a) further discussed the resonance cases in an axially symmetric potential field

$$
U = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( - \frac { C ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + { \cal P } \hat { \xi } ^ { 2 } + { \cal Q } z ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \xi z ^ { 2 } \right) ,
$$

where $r , \theta , z$ are cylindrical coordinates, $\xi = r - r _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } , r _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } , z _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } = 0$ being the initial point on the symmetry plane, $C$ is the angular momentum along the $z \cdot$ -axis,and $P , Q$ ， $\varepsilon$ are constants. If $R , Z$ are the projections of the velocity $v$ on the axes of $r$ and $z$ ，then the energy integral is

$$
F = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( P \xi ^ { 2 } + R ^ { 2 } + Q z ^ { 2 } + Z ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \xi z ^ { 2 } ) = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } v _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

The so-called third integral is obtained in a formal series

$$
\Phi = \frac { R _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { \varepsilon ^ { 2 } ( 2 P + Q ) R _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } Z _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { ( 4 Q - P ) ( Q - P ) 2 P Q } + \cdots .
$$

There are an infinite number of divisors of the form $m ^ { 2 } P - n ^ { 2 } Q$ in this formal series.If $P ^ { 1 / 2 } / Q ^ { 1 / 2 } = n / m$ is rational, then we call it the resonance case.

Put

$$
{ \cal F } = { \cal F } _ { 0 } + \varepsilon { \cal F } _ { 1 } , \Phi = \Phi _ { 0 } + \varepsilon \Phi _ { 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \Phi _ { 2 } + \cdots .
$$

If $\Phi$ is an integral of motion, then we have

$$
( \Phi _ { \nu } , F _ { \imath } ) + ( \Phi _ { \nu + 1 } , F _ { 0 } ) = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r } { R \frac { \partial \Phi _ { * + 1 } } { \partial \xi } + Z \frac { \partial \Phi _ { * + 1 } } { \partial z } - P \xi \frac { \partial \Phi _ { * + 1 } } { \partial R } - Q z \frac { \partial \Phi _ { * + 1 } } { \partial Z } = - ( \Phi _ { * } , F _ { 1 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where（,）denotes a Poisson bracket.The solution is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \boldsymbol { \xi } = \frac { ( 2 \bar { \Phi } _ { 0 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } { P ^ { 1 / 2 } } \sin { [ P ^ { 1 / 2 } ( T - T _ { 0 } ) ] } , \qquad \boldsymbol { z } = \frac { ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } { Q ^ { 1 / 2 } } \sin { [ Q ^ { 1 / 2 } T ] } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
R = ( 2 \Phi _ { 0 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos { [ P ^ { 1 / 2 } ( T - T _ { 0 } ) ] } , \qquad Z = ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos { [ Q ^ { 1 / 2 } T ] } ,
$$

where $\Phi _ { 0 } , V _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } } , T _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } }$ are integrals of the ordinary differential equations corresponding to the partial differential equation (116) for $\Phi _ { \nu + 1 }$ . Then

$$
\Phi _ { * + 1 } = - \int ( \Phi _ { * } , F _ { 1 } ) d T + f ( \Phi _ { 0 } , V _ { 0 } , T _ { 0 } ) , \qquad V _ { \nu } = F _ { \nu } - \Phi _ { \nu } ,
$$

with an arbitrary function $f$ ·

If $( \Phi _ { \mathfrak { v } } , F _ { 1 } )$ contains a constant term， then this integration gives a secular term. If $P ^ { 1 / 2 } / Q ^ { 1 / 2 }$ is irrational, then no secular term can appear. If $P ^ { 1 / 2 } / Q ^ { 1 / 2 } = n / m$ ，then Contopoulos found three isolating integrals of the system

$$
\frac { d \xi } { R } = \frac { d z } { Z } = \frac { d R } { - P \xi } = \frac { d Z } { - Q z } ,
$$

namely, $\Phi _ { 0 } , V _ { 0 } ,$ and

$$
S _ { 0 } = ( 2 \Phi _ { 0 } ) ^ { m / 2 } ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) ^ { n / 2 } \sin { [ n Q ^ { 1 / 2 } T - m P ^ { 1 / 2 } ( T - T _ { 0 } ) ] } .
$$

Consider

$$
C _ { 0 } = ( 2 \Phi _ { 0 } ) ^ { m / 2 } ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) ^ { n / 2 } \cos { [ n Q ^ { 1 / 2 } T - m P ^ { 1 / 2 } ( T - T _ { 0 } ) ] } .
$$

Every polynomial integral of the ordinary differential equations (117) is shown to be a polynomial of $\Phi _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ ， $V _ { \mathfrak { o } } , S _ { \mathfrak { o } } ,$ and $C _ { 0 }$ . Contopoulos devised a method for eliminating the secular terms in the resonance case. Namely,he assumed an integral of the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \phi = \phi _ { 0 } + \varepsilon \phi _ { 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { \phi _ { 0 } = x _ { 1 } ( 2 \Phi _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ( 2 \Phi _ { 0 } ) ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) + x _ { 3 } ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi _ { k }$ is of degree $k + 4$ in $\xi , R , z , Z$ . He defined $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 }$ so that the secular terms in $\phi _ { m + n - 2 }$ disappear, the secular terms being

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { q _ { 1 } ( 2 \Phi _ { 0 } ) ^ { { ( m + 2 ) / 2 } } ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) ^ { { n / 2 } } \cos { [ ( m P ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 0 } ) T ] } } \\ & { \phantom { { = } } + q _ { 2 } ( 2 \Phi _ { 0 } ) ^ { { m / 2 } } ( 2 V _ { 0 } ) ^ { { ( n + 2 ) / 2 } } \phantom { { = } } \sin { [ ( m P ^ { 1 / 2 } T _ { 0 } ) T , ] } } \end{array}
$$

where $q _ { 1 } , \ q _ { 2 }$ are numerical coefficients.

If $P = 4 Q$ , then the third integral is found to be

$$
\phi = - \frac { \varepsilon } { 6 4 Q ^ { 2 } } ( R _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 4 } + 1 2 R _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } Z _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } - 1 4 Z _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 4 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $\scriptstyle { R _ { 0 } , Z _ { 0 } }$ are the components of the initial velocity $v _ { 0 }$ for $\xi _ { 0 } = z _ { 0 } = 0$

The third integral permits the calculation of the boundary of the orbits on the $( \xi , z )$ -plane.

Barbanis (1962,1965) studied the behavior of the hodographs of galactic orbits by means of the formal third integral.He also considered the resonance case in which the nonperturbed frequencies in two perpendicular directions are in the ratio $2 : 1$ . The hodographs and the projections of the orbits on the two perpendicular planes are shown to be explained satisfactorily by means of the third integral.

Suppose that the orbits in the four-dimensional phase space lie on two-dimensional surfaces called the integral surfaces,which are the sections of the energy surface cut by the surfaces representing the third integral. The intersections of these surfaces by the different planes are called the invariant curves.

By changing the notation suitably (Contopoulos,1965a), denote the potential by

$$
V = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( A x ^ { 2 } + B y ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon x y ^ { 2 } ;
$$

then we have the energy integral

$$
{ \cal H } = { \cal H } _ { 0 } + \varepsilon { \cal H } _ { 1 } = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } + A x ^ { 2 } + B y ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon x y ^ { 2 } = h ,
$$

where $X = d x / d t$ ， $Y = d y / d t$

If $A ^ { 1 / 2 } / B ^ { 1 / 2 }$ is irrational, then the so-called third integral is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Phi _ { 1 1 } = \cfrac { 1 } { 4 B - A } \left[ ( A - 2 B ) x y ^ { 2 } - 2 x y Y ^ { 2 } + 2 X y Y \right] , } \\ & { \Phi _ { 1 2 } = - \cfrac { 1 } { 4 B - A } \left[ \cfrac { y ^ { 4 } } { 2 } + \cfrac { ( 2 A + B ) X ^ { 2 } Y ^ { 2 } } { 2 A B ( A - B ) } + \cfrac { ( 2 A - 5 B ) x ^ { 2 } Y ^ { 2 } } { 2 B ( A - B ) } \right. } \\ & { \qquad \left. - \cfrac { ( 4 A - B ) X ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } } { 2 A ( A - B ) } + \cfrac { 3 B x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( A - B ) } + \cfrac { 6 x X y Y } { A - B } \right] , } \\ & { \qquad \ldots , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Phi _ { 2 } = H - \Phi _ { 1 } = \Phi _ { 2 0 } + \varepsilon \Phi _ { 2 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \Phi _ { 2 2 } + \cdots , } } \\ { { \Phi _ { 2 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( X ^ { 2 } + B y ^ { 2 } ) , \qquad \Phi _ { 2 1 } = H _ { 1 } - \Phi _ { 1 1 } , \qquad \Phi _ { 2 2 } = - \Phi _ { 1 2 } , \cdots } } \end{array}
$$

The intersection of the integral surfaces by $y = 0$ in the $( x , y , X )$ - space is obtained by eliminating $\pmb { Y }$ between (118) and (119),

$$
Y ^ { 2 } = 2 h - X ^ { 2 } - A x ^ { 2 } .
$$

The equation of the invariant curves is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \mathrm { { \ell ^ { \mathrm { { ? } } } + \ell } } A x ^ { \mathrm { { 2 } } } = X _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } } ^ { \mathrm { { 2 } } } + A x _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } } ^ { \mathrm { { 2 } } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { { } } + { \frac { 4 \varepsilon Y _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } } ^ { 2 } ( x - x _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } } ) } { 4 B - A } } - { \frac { 2 \varepsilon ^ { 2 } Y _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } } ^ { 2 } } { 4 B - A } } \left[ { \frac { 8 x ( x - x _ { \mathrm { { 0 } } } ) } { 4 B - A } } + { \frac { 3 ( x ^ { 2 } - x \mathrm { { 0 } } } { A - B } } \right] } } \end{array}
$$

The invariant curves are inside the limiting curve $X ^ { 2 } + A x ^ { 2 } = 2 h$ In the zero-order approximation the invariant curves are ellipses around the center $x = X = 0$ which corresponds to a stable periodic orbit. In higher-order approximation the ellipses are distorted but always symmetrical with respect to the $x$ -axis.

f $X _ { 0 } = 0$ ， we have for $X = 0$ two solutions, namely $x = x _ { 0 }$ and

$$
x = - x _ { 0 } + \frac { 4 \varepsilon Y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { A ( 4 B - A ) } + \frac { 1 6 \varepsilon ^ { 2 } Y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 0 } } { A ( 4 B - A ) } ,
$$

both of which coincide if

$$
x _ { 0 } = \frac { 4 h \varepsilon } { A ( 4 B - A ) }
$$

in the second approximation. The orbit with initial conditions

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x _ { 0 } = 4 h \varepsilon / [ A ( 4 B - A ) ] , \qquad y _ { 0 } = 0 , \qquad X _ { 0 } = 0 } \\ & { Y _ { 0 } = ( 2 h - A x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \end{array}
$$

is a stable periodic orbit.

A periodic solution in the general case is seen to be

$$
x = { \frac { ( 4 h - A y ^ { 2 } ) \varepsilon } { A ( 4 B - A ) } } ,
$$

which is stable.

If $A ^ { 1 / 2 } / B ^ { 1 / 2 }$ is equal to a rational number $n / m$ ， then the integral $\Phi _ { 1 }$ has a secular term in $\Phi _ { 1 , m + n - 2 }$ of the form $q S _ { 0 } T$ for $m + n$ even or $q C _ { 0 } T$ for $m + n$ odd,where $S _ { \mathfrak { d } }$ and $C _ { 0 }$ are expressed as double power series over $q _ { 1 }$ and $q _ { 2 }$ in $( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x ) ^ { 2 q _ { 1 } }$ ， $X ^ { m - 2 q _ { 1 } }$ ， $( B ^ { 1 / 2 } y ) ^ { 2 q _ { 2 } + 1 }$ ， $Y ^ { n - 2 q _ { 2 } - 1 }$ .

In resonance cases of the box type, in which orbits are confined in the domain bounded by two straight lines, the equation (121） gives the initial point of the periodic orbits on the $x$ -axis in the first appro-ximation except the cases $A ^ { 1 / 2 } / B ^ { 1 / 2 } = 1$ , 2, and 4. We have in these exceptional cases secular terms in the integral $\Phi _ { 1 }$ . By disposing of these secular terms we have an integral $\phi = \phi _ { 0 } + \varepsilon \phi _ { 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ .This integral can be written in the zero-th order

$$
( 2 \Phi _ { 1 0 } - 2 \Phi _ { 1 0 ; 0 } ) [ ( c _ { 1 } - c _ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } ) ( 2 \Phi _ { 1 0 } + 2 \Phi _ { 1 0 ; 0 } ) + 2 h ( c _ { 2 } - 2 c _ { 3 } ) ] =
$$

where the subscript; O means that we are to compute the function at the initial epoch and $c _ { 1 } , \ : c _ { 2 }$ ， $c _ { 3 }$ are constants. This equation represents two stable ellipses

$$
X ^ { 2 } + A x ^ { 2 } = 2 \Phi _ { 1 0 ; 0 } , \qquad X ^ { 2 } + A x ^ { 2 } = - 2 \Phi _ { 1 0 ; 0 } - \frac { 2 h ( c _ { 2 } - 2 c _ { 3 } ) } { c _ { 1 } - c _ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } } .
$$

The second elipse is real and inside the limiting curve at least for a set of values of $2 \Phi _ { 1 0 ; 0 }$ if

$$
4 h > - \frac { 2 h ( c _ { 2 } - 2 c _ { 3 } ) } { c _ { 1 } - c _ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } } > 0 .
$$

The ellipses (123） surround the point $^ \mathrm { o }$ which represents a stable periodic orbit in the zero-th approximation. If the first-order terms are included, then the same periodic orbit (123) is of the box type in the first approximation as in the general irrational case for $m + n > 5$

In the case $A ^ { 1 / 2 } = B ^ { 1 / 2 }$ there are two unstable periodic orbits, namely $y = 0$ and $x \cong 0$ ， and three stable periodic orbits，namely， two straight-line orbits passing through the origin and symmetric with re-spect to the axes, and one closed periodic orbit which is approximately an ellipse.Orbits near the last periodic orbit form rings around the origin； they are called shell orbits (Figure 6C).

In the general rational case we may have also another kind of periodic orbit. If the two roots of (122) are not equal, then two ellipses (123) are separated and only the first one represents the invariant curve passing through $( x _ { 0 } , X _ { 0 } )$ . Even we include higher-order terms in $\phi$ these ellipses are distorted but still remain separated.If the two roots of (122)are equal or very nearly equal, then higher-order terms may make the two ellipses combine into one curve whose form is quite different from an ellipse.

For $m = 3$ the resonant invariant curves are shown in Figure 2a. The invariant points are

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/b56fc3400f6e3529649eb0705c856fa7fb88fe7e6430e38fc8b077c76931018a.jpg)  
Figure 14.2a (Contopoulos,1965a)

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/97921e589f580e9c6cbff50308d1b7d5ec936d5c683bd06c65e865b752978210.jpg)  
Figure 14.2b (Contopoulos, 1965a)

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { P _ { 1 } ( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 0 } > 0 , X _ { 0 } = 0 ) , } \\ & { P _ { 2 } , P _ { 3 } ( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x = - A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 0 } / 2 , X = \pm \sqrt { 3 } \ A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 0 } / 2 ) , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { P _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ( - A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 0 } , X _ { 0 } = 0 ) , } \\ & { P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , P _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } ( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x = A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 0 } / 2 , X = \pm \sqrt { 3 } A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 0 } / 2 ) . } \end{array}
$$

For $A ^ { 1 / 2 } / B ^ { 1 / 2 } \sim 4 / 3$ ，the stable invariant points are $P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } , P _ { 3 }$ and the unstable invariant points $P _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , P _ { 3 } ^ { \prime }$ ; and vice versa for $A ^ { 1 / 2 } / B ^ { 1 / 2 } \sim 2 / 3$

For $m = 2$ the resonant invariant curves are as shown in Figure 2b. If $c _ { 1 } - c _ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } > 0$ ，then $P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 }$ are stable invariant points,and $P _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , P _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are unstable invariant points.

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/7590376c05ddf1b6b5d96732bb302208a5ca0d292338faca0ad9ca4361fb2c74.jpg)  
Figure 14.3a (Contopoulos,1965a)

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/66017fb2fae0a1c41d8ef0421c42c5573a7bbc8c5e938995c5f4db67900737a2.jpg)  
Figure 14.3b

(Contopoulos, 1965a)

For $A ^ { 1 / 2 } = 4 B ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ,i.e., $m = 1$ ， $n = 4$ ，the point $P ( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { \mathrm { m a x } } = 0 . 1 1 8$ $X = 0 )$ is a stable invariant point, and the point $P ^ { \prime } ( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { \mathrm { m i n } } = - 0 . 1 1 8$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { \boldsymbol { X } = \boldsymbol { 0 } , } \end{array}$ ） is an unstable invariant point.Figure 3a represents a tube orbit and Figure 3b an almost periodic orbit.The dashed curve is the curve of zero velocity.

Thus the resonant periodic orbits are characterized by a small region

$$
\Delta \left| \frac { { \cal A } ^ { 1 / 2 } } { { \cal B } ^ { 1 / 2 } } - \frac { n } { m } \right| = \frac { 2 h \varepsilon ^ { 2 } | q _ { 1 } - q _ { 2 } | } { m { \cal B } ^ { 1 / 2 } } ,
$$

and such a periodic orbit makes $\pmb { n }$ oscillations along the $x$ -axis， while it makes m oscillations along the $\boldsymbol { y }$ -axis.Orbits which are near this periodic orbit remain always in its neighborhood,and they form elon-gated tube orbits. For the existence of such resonant invariant curves we should have $q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } < 0$ ,where $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 }$ are the coefficients of the secular terms in $S _ { z }$ or $C _ { 2 }$ . If this condition is not satisfied but $q _ { 1 } \neq q _ { 2 }$ ,then the resonance phenomena occur for a range of values of ${ \cal A } ^ { 1 / 2 } / { \cal B } ^ { 1 / 2 }$ near ${ \pmb n } / m$ but not for $A ^ { 1 / 2 } / B ^ { 1 / 2 } = n / m$

Suppose in particular that the nonperturbed frequencies in two perpendicular directions are equal.The form of the third integral is different from that in the non-resonance cases. Contopoulos and Moutsoulas (1965) found by numerical calculation with a computer that the third integral is better conserved in actual orbits when more higher-order terms are included,and that the invariant curves give the main characteristics of the orbit representing the theoretical invariant curves when terms up to the fourth degree in the perturbation parameter are included (Contopoulos,1965a；Barbanis,1965).

Consider the energy integral in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { H = H _ { 0 } + \varepsilon H _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( A x ^ { 2 } + X ^ { 2 } + A y ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon x y ^ { 2 } = h , } \end{array}
$$

where $H _ { 0 } = \Phi _ { 1 0 } + \Phi _ { 2 0 }$ ， $H _ { 1 } = - x y ^ { 2 }$ ,and the third integral up to the second degree in $\varepsilon$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \phi = X ^ { \prime } Y ^ { 2 } - A x ^ { 2 } Y ^ { 2 } - A X ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } + A ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } + 4 A x X y Y } \\ & { \phantom { \phi } - \frac { ( 2 \Phi _ { 1 0 } ) ^ { 2 } } { 3 } + \frac { ( 2 \Phi _ { 2 0 } ) ^ { 2 } } { 1 2 } } \\ & { \phantom { \phi } - \frac { 2 \varepsilon } { 9 A } ( 4 A ^ { 2 } x y ^ { 4 } + 4 A ^ { 2 } x ^ { 3 } y ^ { 2 } + 2 A x ^ { 3 } Y ^ { 2 } + 2 2 A x ^ { 2 } X y Y - 2 0 A . } \\ & { \phantom { \phi } + 1 4 x X ^ { 2 } Y ^ { 2 } - 1 4 X ^ { 3 } y Y + 1 3 A X y ^ { 3 } Y - 7 x Y ^ { 4 } } \\ & { \phantom { \phi } + 7 X y Y ^ { 3 } - 9 A x y ^ { 2 } Y ^ { 2 } ) } \\ & { \phantom { \phi } + \cdots , } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi _ { 2 }$ has no secular term. We determine the constants so that no secular term appears in $\phi _ { 4 }$ and so forth.The equation of an invariant curve passing through the point $( x _ { 0 } , X _ { 0 } )$ when the energy is $h =$ $\frac { 1 } { 2 } ( A x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + X _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + Y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } )$ is found by putting $y = 0$ ， $Y ^ { 2 } = 2 h - A x ^ { 2 } - X ^ { 2 }$ in (125):

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { 1 5 X ^ { 4 } - 2 X ^ { 2 } ( 1 0 h - 3 A x ^ { 2 } ) - 9 A ^ { 2 } x ^ { 4 } + 2 8 h A x ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \qquad + { \displaystyle \frac { 8 \varepsilon x } { 3 A } } ( 2 h - X ^ { 2 } - A x ^ { 2 } ) ( 2 1 X ^ { 2 } + 9 A x ^ { 2 } - 1 4 h ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \end{array}
$$

or, in the zero-th order,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { 9 ( A x ^ { 2 } , X ^ { 2 } ) \equiv 1 5 X ^ { 4 } - 2 X ^ { 2 } ( 1 0 h - 3 A x ^ { 2 } ) - 9 A ^ { 2 } x ^ { 4 } + 2 8 h A x ^ { 2 } - 1 ^ { 4 } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + ~ 2 X _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 0 h - 3 A x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) + 9 A ^ { 2 } x _ { 0 } ^ { 4 } - 2 8 h A x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

The invariant curves are always inside the limiting curve $X ^ { 2 } + A x ^ { 2 } =$ $2 h$ ,which is a circle in the $( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x , X )$ -plane. If $1 0 h + 1 5 X _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - 9 A x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } < 0 _ { : }$ then we have a solution for $X ^ { 2 }$ if $( A ^ { 1 / 2 } x _ { 0 } , X _ { 0 } )$ is inside the limiting curve (Case C).In Figure 4 the regions $\mathbf { C }$ are on both sides of the two branches of the hyperbola $1 0 h = 9 A x ^ { 2 } - 1 5 X ^ { 2 }$ ，which intersects the $\pmb { A } ^ { 1 / 2 } \pmb { x } .$ -axis at the points $[ A ^ { 1 / 2 } x = \pm ( 1 0 h / 9 ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ， $X = 0 ]$ and the limiting curve at the points $[ A ^ { 1 / 2 } x = \pm ( 5 h / 3 ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ， $X = \pm ( h / 3 ) ^ { 1 / 2 } ]$ .If $1 0 h + 1 5 X _ { \circ } ^ { 2 } - 9 A x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } > 0$ ， then，in the condition for the existence of the real solution $X ^ { 2 }$ of (126),i.e., $\Im ( A x ^ { 2 } ) \geq 0$ ， the equation $\Im ( A x ^ { 2 } ) = 0$ admits two real roots $\boldsymbol { A } \boldsymbol { x } _ { \boldsymbol { b } } ^ { 2 }$ and $A x _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } ( x _ { b } ^ { 2 } \geq \ x _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } )$ , two roots for $\theta ( A x ^ { 2 } , 0 ) > 0$ and one root for $\theta ( A x ^ { 2 } , 0 ) < 0$ .If $A x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } < 0$ ， then there are two roots for $X ^ { 2 }$ for $0 < A x ^ { 2 } < A x _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 }$ (Case B). If $A x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } > 0$ ，then there is one root for $0 < A x ^ { 2 } < A x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ and there are two roots for $A x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } < A x ^ { 2 } < A x _ { a } ^ { 2 }$ (Case A). The transition between Cases A and $\mathbf { B }$ occurs for $x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = 0$ or $\theta ( 0 , 0 ) = 0$ . This is in the form of a figure eight (Figure 4). In Case B the invariant curves surround the points $P _ { 1 }$ ， $P _ { \mathrm { 2 } } \left[ x _ { 0 } = 0 \right.$ ， $X _ { 0 } =$ $\pm ( 2 h / 3 ) ^ { 1 / 2 } ]$ ，which represent stable periodic orbits. The invariant curves near the periodic ones represent tube orbits.Figure 4 gives the invariant curves for $\varepsilon \to 0$ . The dashed line is the limiting curve $A x ^ { 2 }$ $+ X ^ { 2 } = 2 h$ .If $\varepsilon \neq 0$ ，higher-order terms come in and the curves are distorted (Figure 5).

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/1bc7ba5d7b4ae4c210344bad31e82281f81b2446deb1c6ea954494ea04d652d0.jpg)

Eliminating $\pmb { X }$ and $\pmb { Y }$ from (124) and (125) and (Contopoulos and Moutsoulas,1965)

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/e6cd7fde6a481f23d6fa5486e56094fb1729a5ebcdb02fe3d720c9b91d83a8a9.jpg)  
Figure 14.5

$$
\boldsymbol { J } = \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \boldsymbol { X } } \boldsymbol { Y } - \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \boldsymbol { Y } } \boldsymbol { X } = \boldsymbol { 0 }
$$

we find the boundary of the invariant curves (Barbanis,1965).

Figures 6A,B,C show the boundaries of orbits in the form of an hourglass orbit,a tube orbit and a shell orbit, respectively.

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/b76ffdd16f5ae9cc14d8b1bdcdc37be488776c407076bf781c2bc3eea8cc6a80.jpg)

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/9f39e2bfe1033df788a44e91e16730007f473210e06b1c13adc0a98d0ce5c8bf.jpg)  
Figure 14.6C (shell orbit) (Contopoulos and Moutsoulas,1965)

The resonance case $A ^ { 1 / 2 } = B ^ { 1 / 2 }$ is exceptional；in other resonance cases (except $A ^ { 1 / 2 } = 2 B ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ）the form of the orbits is in general of the box type, similar to those of the non-resonance cases.For $A ^ { 1 / 2 } = B ^ { 1 / 2 }$ there is no box type orbit.In the A-type orbits angular points of the boundaries are on the $x$ -axis,in the inner boundaries,and different invariant curves.The third integral can be applied in this case,too.

Contopoulos and Woltjer (1964) considered the perturbation potential in the form of a wave parallel to the $y .$ -axis, or the product of two waves parallel to the $x -$ and $y .$ -axes, i.e.,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { V = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( A x ^ { 2 } + B y ^ { 2 } ) + \varepsilon V _ { \varepsilon } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
V _ { \varepsilon } = q _ { s } y ^ { 2 } \sin { \left( \frac { 2 \pi x } { \lambda _ { x } } \right) } + q _ { c } y ^ { 2 } \cos { \left( \frac { 2 \pi x } { \lambda _ { x } } \right) } ,
$$

or

$$
V _ { \ast } = q _ { s s } \sin \left( \frac { 2 \pi x } { \lambda _ { x } } \right) \sin \left( \frac { 2 \pi y } { \lambda _ { y } } \right) + q _ { s c } \sin \left( \frac { 2 \pi x } { \lambda _ { x } } \right) \cos \left( \frac { 2 \pi y } { \lambda _ { y } } \right)
$$

$$
+ q _ { c s } \cos \left( \frac { 2 \pi x } { \lambda _ { x } } \right) \sin \left( \frac { 2 \pi y } { \lambda _ { y } } \right) + q _ { c e } \cos \left( \frac { 2 \pi x } { \lambda _ { x } } \right) \cos \left( \frac { 2 \pi y } { \lambda _ { y } } \right) .
$$

The third integral is constructed step by step as a series whose terms are multiple series in the coordinates and velocities.These multiple series are seen to be convergent,and no secular term appears.Numerical integration shows that if the amplitudes of the waves are sufficiently small, the orbits have a well-defined boundary for a long time interval. As the amplitude of the wave increases,the orbits become quasi-isolating and finally ergodic.

Contopoulos and Moutsoulas (1966) discussed two cases in which small divisors play an important role in the third integral. The Hamiltonian is assumed to be

$$
{ \cal H } = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } + A x ^ { 2 } + B y ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon x y ^ { 2 } = h ,
$$

which corresponds to the potential

$$
V = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( A x ^ { 2 } + B y ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon x y ^ { 2 }
$$

on the plane of symmetry of a non-axially symmetric galaxy. In the first case the two nonperturbed frequencies are nearly equal. If $\varepsilon$ is very small and we set $B = A + K \varepsilon ^ { 2 }$ we find resonance phenomena when $\kappa$ is in the range $\left( - 5 h / 3 A ^ { 2 } , 1 0 h / 3 A ^ { 2 } \right)$ . For larger or smaller values of $\kappa$ all orbits are boxes.This range is divided into four parts by the values $K = - 2 h / 3 A ^ { 2 }$ ， $K = 5 h / 6 A ^ { 2 }$ ，and $K = 7 h / 3 A ^ { 2 }$ . The form of the invariant curves is different for the four integrals.The corresponding orbits are either·box type,or similar to the orbits of the resonance case $A = B$ ， except for the D-type orbits (the curve a, Figure 6D),the orbits with an angular point on the y-axis,which appear only in the third and fourth intervals (Figure 6D,where the curve b is of the $C \mathrm { \cdot }$ -type).

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/f6d2702730acfae278b45c91d64d8aeb84da569149ca47ea61378611d6ac94ac.jpg)  
Figure 14.6D

a:D-type orbit b:C-type orbit (Contopoulos and Moutsoulas,1965)

In the second case one nonperturbed frequency is almost double the other. If we set $4 B = A + \varepsilon k$ we find resonance phenomena when

$$
- 4 ( 2 h / A ) ^ { 1 / 2 } < k < 4 ( 2 h / A ) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

The boundaries of the orbits are approximately arcs of three parabolas, as in the resonance case.

Gustavson (1966) gave a procedure for constructing formal integrals of a Hamiltonian system of $\pmb { n }$ degrees of freedom near an equilibrium point.The method consists of carrying out a series of canonical transformations which reduces the Hamiltonian to a normal form in the manner of Birkhoff,Siegel,and Moser (Sections 21.5-21.11).Then the integrals appear as quadratic forms.By means of a computer program for the construction procedure involving the symbolic manipulation of polynomials he determined approximate integrals,as represented in truncated power series,for a given Hamiltonian system and expressed them in the original coordinates.In the case of two degrees of freedom,level lines of the intersection of the integral with some appropriate surface were plotted.As an application he discussed the problem of the existence of a third isolating integral of motion in an axially symmetric potential.

Arnold and Moser (Sections21.12-21.21;Arnold and Avez,1967) showed the existence of invariant surfaces for nearly integrable

Hamiltonian systems. Since the nonperturbed Hamiltonian is integrable,a continuous family of invariant surfaces exists.For small perturbations the majority of these invariant surfaces are preserved while a small number dissolves or breaks up.The surfaces that dissolve are densely intertwined with the surfaces that persist,which circumstance shows that no integral exists in the strict sense.These results are true for sufficiently small perturbations,and no concrete results exist in the large.Numerical evidence indicates that the surfaces do continue for large perturbations but then suddenly disappear completely. The results of Henon and Heiles are examples of this (Section 17.6).

The construction of an approximate integral can be viewed as an attempt to find an asymptotic representation for this family of invariant surfaces. The approximate integral is a continuous family of surfaces.The flow is in general very complicated between the existing invariant surfaces and cannot be described analytically. Numerical integration also fails to detect this behavior except where the surfaces begin to disappear.

Contopoulos (1965) applied the idea to the restricted three-body problem (Section 17.1) in which

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { H = H _ { 0 } + \mu H _ { 1 } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( p _ { x } ^ { 2 } + p _ { y } ^ { 2 } ) - ( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } ) - \frac { 1 - \mu } { r } - \frac { \mu } { \sqrt { 1 + 2 x + r ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { \mu } { ( x - y ) } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle H _ { 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( p _ { x } ^ { 2 } + p _ { y } ^ { 2 } ) - ( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } ) - \frac { 1 } { r } } , } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle H _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { r } + \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \cdots , \quad 0 < r < 1 . } } \end{array}
$$

The third integral was found in this expanded form of $\pmb { H } _ { 1 }$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \phi _ { 1 } = 3 a ^ { 2 } \Big \{ \cos 2 N \Big [ - { \frac { 1 } { 4 ( n - 1 ) } } + { \frac { 2 e ( n - 1 ) } { ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } } \cos M + \cdots \Big ] } } \\ { { + \sin 2 N \sin M \Big [ { \frac { ( 5 n - 4 ) e } { ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } } \Big ] \Big \} + \cdots . } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
N = ( n - 1 ) t + g - n _ { 0 } t , \qquad M = n ( t - t _ { 0 } ) ,
$$

with the usual osculating elements.Numerical integrations by Bozis (1966) indicate the existence of a new integral besides the isolated

Jacobi integral in the restricted three-body problem，which will be discussed in Sections 17.1-17.8.Barbanis (1965,1966a) studied the behavior and topology of the family of periodic orbits of the problem. Anderle (1966) discussed a special form of the third integral including that of Idlis,Kuzmin,and Van Albada in the problem of galactic dynamics and obtained the integral both in Contopoulos's series form and in an integral form by means of elliptic functions.

Barbanis (1966) examined the transition between the isolating case of the third integral and the non-isolating case by numerically calculating with various values of the energy and the initial conditions for the potential ${ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } A ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon x y ^ { 2 }$ . He found that when the total energy is smaller than the critical value the third integral is an isolating or very nearly an isolating integral and that when the total energy is increased the third integral is no longer an isolating integral anywhere in the phase space.The increase of the energy corresponds to an increase of the perturbation in the adopted potential field.A similar phenomenon was noticed by Hénon and Heiles (1964； Section 17.6) and Contopoulos and Woltjer (1964).For total energy higher than the escape energy there are shell orbits which not only remain in a finite area but also have well-defined invariant curves.This phenomenon takes place, in Barbanis's potential, for total energy up to 4O percent larger than the escape energy.For larger values of the energy the shell orbits remain in a finite area for a range of total energy but their invariant curves are no longer well defined.

Bozis (1966) presented the problem in a regularized form in order to include collision and near-collsion orbits by transforming the equations of motion according to Birkhoff's transformation (Section 17.13)

$$
\begin{array} { c c c } { { z = \displaystyle \frac { w ^ { 2 } + \mu ( 1 - \mu ) } { 2 w + 1 - 2 \mu } , } } & { { } } & { { z = x + \sqrt { - 1 } y , \nonumber } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { w = u + \sqrt { - 1 } v , } } & { { | f ^ { \prime } ( w ) | = \displaystyle \frac { p _ { 1 } p _ { 2 } } { 2 p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } , \nonumber } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { | w ^ { \prime } | ^ { 2 } = 2 \overline { { { \Omega } } } , } } & { { d t = | f ^ { \prime } | ^ { 2 } d \tau , \nonumber } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { \dot { u } = \displaystyle \frac { d u } { d \tau } , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\dot { \mathrm { \scriptsize ~  ~ } } = \frac { p _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } p _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { 3 2 p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } [ ( 1 - \mu ) p _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } + \mu p _ { 2 } ^ { 4 } ] + \frac { 1 } { 2 p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } [ ( 1 - \mu ) p _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \mu p _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ] - \frac { C } { 2 } ( \frac { p _ { 1 } p _ { 2 } } { 2 p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } ) ^ { 2 }
$$

to the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } u } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { p _ { 1 } p _ { 2 } } { p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { d v } { d \tau } = \frac { \partial \overline { { \Omega } } } { \partial u } , \qquad \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { p _ { 1 } p _ { 2 } } { p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \right) \frac { d u } { d \tau } = \frac { \partial \overline { { \Omega } } } { \partial v } .
$$

The energy integral $| \pmb { w } ^ { \prime } | ^ { 2 } = 2 \overline { { \Omega } }$ is of the form $I _ { 1 } ( u , v , \dot { u } , \dot { v } ) = C _ { 1 }$ If there exists another integral $I _ { 2 } ( u , v , \dot { u } , \dot { v } ) = C _ { 2 }$ ，then we should have $\phi ( u , v , \dot { v } ) = 0 .$ ，and the intersection of this surface with $\dot { v } = 0$ gives the invariant curves.If we have $\phi ( u , v , \dot { v } ) = 0 $ ，then there is a boundary in the $( u , v )$ -plane (Figure 7). The invariant curves a,b,c, d, e lie on the planes ${ \dot { v } } = 0$ ,0.25,O.50,2 and 4,respectively.Figure 8 shows the orbits confined in a ring region inside the zero-velocity curve. It is drawn for $\mu = 0 . 0 1$ ， $C = 5$ ， $u _ { 0 } = 0$ ， $v _ { 0 } = 0 . 6 0$ ， $\dot { u } _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } = 1 . 0 9 8 6 4 7 _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { : } }$ ， $\dot { v } _ { 0 } = 0$ .The margin spaces surrounded by the ring region and situated between the zero-velocity curve and the outer boundary of the ring shrink to zero as we approach the collision orbit (Figure $9 ; \mu = 0 . 0 5$ ， $C = 7$ ， $u _ { 0 } = 0 . 0 5$ ， $v _ { 0 } = 0$ ， $\dot { u } _ { 0 } = 0$ ， $\dot { v } _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } = 2 . 7 5 6 8 1 0 )$ ·

Bozis expanded the function

$$
\phi = \phi _ { 0 } + \mu \phi _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

according to Contopoulos (1965),where

$$
\phi _ { 0 } = x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } , \qquad p _ { x } = x ^ { \prime } - y , \qquad p _ { y } = y ^ { \prime } + x ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad - \frac { 3 \kappa ^ { 6 } } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } \left[ 1 + \frac { 2 n ( \bar { s } n - 4 ) } { ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } e ^ { \theta } \right] } \\ & { \quad + \frac { 6 \alpha ( 2 n - 1 ) } { ( n - 2 ) ( n - 3 ) } e ^ { \theta } - \frac { 6 ( 2 n - 1 ) x y _ { 2 } } { n ( n - 1 ) ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } e ^ { \theta } } \\ & { \quad + \frac { 6 \alpha k x } { n - 1 } \left[ 1 - \frac { 5 n ^ { 4 } } { 2 n - 2 } - 6 n + 2 e \right] - \frac { 6 \alpha ^ { 4 } ( 5 n - 3 ) y _ { 2 } } { n ( n - 1 ) ( 2 n - 1 ) } e ^ { \theta } } \\ & { \quad + \frac { 1 5 \alpha ^ { 4 } } { 2 } \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \left[ x _ { | } ^ { 3 } \left( 1 - \frac { x ^ { 3 } } { 3 \alpha ^ { 4 } } \right) - e \right] _ { 1 } ^ { \alpha } \left( 1 - \frac { x ^ { 5 } } { \alpha ^ { 4 } } \right) \left( \varepsilon + \frac { 2 y _ { \alpha } \varepsilon ^ { 7 } } { n ^ { 4 } } \right) \right\} } \\ & { \quad + \frac { 3 \alpha ^ { 4 } } { 4 } \Big [ \frac { 5 n - 3 } { ( 2 n - 3 ) ( 4 n - 2 ) } \chi _ { \varepsilon } \left( \frac { 4 x ^ { 2 } } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } - 3 \right) - \frac { 7 n - 6 } { n ^ { 2 } ( 2 n - 3 ) ( 4 n - 2 ) } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \end{array}
$$

$$
\rho = - \cos u = \frac { r - a } { a e } , \rho ^ { } = \frac { d \rho } { d t } , r ^ { } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } .
$$

If $\pmb { n }$ takes the values which make the denominators vanish, then the integral does not exist. This situation is in conformity with Poincaré's theorem (Sections 6.15-6.17) that there cannot exist any uniform integral other than the known integrals.If $\boldsymbol { n }$ is irrational,then the integral is represented ina formal series.The boundary of the domain for the orbits are obtained by eliminating $p _ { x }$ and $\boldsymbol { p } _ { y }$ from $\phi _ { 0 } = x p _ { y } -$ $\mathtt { y } p _ { \pmb { x } }$ and $H = H _ { 0 } + \mu H _ { 1 : }$ ，where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle H _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( p _ { \scriptscriptstyle x } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } + p _ { \scriptscriptstyle y } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } ) - ( x p _ { \scriptscriptstyle y } - y p _ { \scriptscriptstyle x } ) - \frac { 1 } { r } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle H _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } = \frac { 1 } { r } - 1 + \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 x r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { 5 } { 2 } x ^ { 3 } + \cdots . } } \end{array}
$$

We have after computation

$$
J = \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial p _ { x } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { y } } - \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial p _ { y } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { x } }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { = - ( x p _ { x } + y p _ { y } ) + \mu \Big [ ( p _ { y } - x ) \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial p _ { x } } - ( p _ { x } + y ) \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial p _ { y } } \Big ] } \\ { = J _ { \ L _ { 0 } } + \mu J _ { \ L _ { 1 } } = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { - ( \nabla p _ { x } + \nabla p _ { y } ) , } \\ & { } & { - \frac { 3 \rho ^ { 2 } } { n } ( p _ { y } - x ) \Big [ \frac { 2 ( 2 n - 1 ) x } { ( n - 1 ) ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } + \frac { 2 a ^ { 2 } ( 5 n - 3 ) } { ( n - 1 ) ( 2 n - 1 ) } } \\ & { } & { - \frac { 5 a ^ { 2 } } { n ( n - 1 ) } \Big \{ 1 - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 4 ( 5 a ^ { 2 } ( n - 6 ) } { 8 n ( 2 n - 2 ) ( 4 n - 3 ) } } \\ & { } & { \cdot \Big \{ \frac { 4 \rho _ { y } ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } - 1 \Big \} - \frac { 1 5 ( 5 n - 2 ) x ^ { 2 } } { 5 n ( 2 n - 1 ) } \Big ] } \\ & { } & { - \frac { 3 \rho _ { z } } { n ( n - 1 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) ( 2 n - 1 ) } + \frac { 2 a ^ { 2 } ( 5 n - 3 ) } { ( n - 1 ) ( 2 n - 1 ) } } \\ & { } & { - \frac { 5 a ^ { 2 } } { 2 n } \Big [ \frac { - 2 } { n ( n - 1 ) } \Big [ - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 n } - \frac { 3 } { 4 n ( n - 2 ) } - \frac { 3 ( 7 n - 6 ) } { 3 ( 2 n - 3 ) } } \\ & { } & { \cdot \Big ( \frac { 4 \rho _ { y } ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } - 3 \Big ) + \frac { 4 ( 5 n - 2 ) } { 4 ( 2 n - 1 ) } \Big ] . } \end{array}
$$

For the zero-th order approximation with regard to $\pmb { \mu }$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { p _ { x } = - \frac { y \phi _ { 0 0 } } { r ^ { 2 } } , \qquad p _ { y } = \frac { x \phi _ { 0 0 } } { r ^ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi _ { \infty }$ is the value of $\phi _ { 0 }$ for the initial point of the orbit.

Define

$$
p _ { x } = - \left( { \frac { y \phi _ { 0 0 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } \right) + \mu Q _ { 1 } , \qquad p _ { y } = { \frac { x \phi _ { 0 0 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } + \mu Q _ { 2 } ,
$$

and

$$
x Q _ { 1 } + y Q _ { 2 } = A , \qquad y Q _ { 1 } - x Q _ { 2 } = B ,
$$

then $A , B$ represent the values of $- J _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 1 }$ ，respectively， for $p _ { z } =$ $- y \phi _ { 0 0 } / r ^ { 2 }$ ，and

$$
B = - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } - \frac { 3 a ^ { 2 } x } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } + \frac { 5 x ^ { 3 } } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } - \phi _ { 1 0 } + \rho e K ,
$$

$$
K = - \frac { 3 n ( 5 n - 4 ) x ^ { 2 } } { ( n - 1 ) ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } + \frac { 6 a ^ { 2 } ( 2 n - 1 ) } { ( n - 2 ) ( n - 3 ) }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle - \frac { 6 a ^ { 2 } ( 5 n ^ { 2 } - 6 n + 2 ) x } { ( n - 1 ) ( 2 n - 1 ) } + \frac { 1 5 a ^ { 2 } x } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } \left( 1 - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle + \frac { 4 5 a ^ { 2 } ( 5 n - 3 ) x } { 8 ( 2 n - 3 ) ( 4 n - 3 ) } \left( \frac { 4 x ^ { 2 } } { a ^ { 2 } } - 3 \right) + \frac { 4 5 a ^ { 2 } ( 5 n - 3 ) x } { 8 ( 2 n - 1 ) } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phi _ { 1 0 } = - \frac { 3 x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } - \frac { 3 a ^ { 2 } x _ { 0 } } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } + \frac { 5 x _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } + \rho _ { 0 } e \Lambda , \qquad \Lambda = { \bar { F } } } } \end{array}
$$

The boundary is given by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle H _ { \boldsymbol { \mathfrak { o } } \boldsymbol { + } } \ \mu H _ { 1 \boldsymbol { \mathfrak { o } } } = \frac { \phi _ { \boldsymbol { 0 } \boldsymbol { 0 } } ^ { 2 } } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } - \phi _ { \boldsymbol { 0 } \boldsymbol { 0 } } - \frac { 1 } { r } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \ \mu \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r } + B + \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - 1 - \frac { B \phi _ { \boldsymbol { 0 } \boldsymbol { 0 } } } { r ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 3 x r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { 5 } { 2 } \boldsymbol { x } } } \end{array}
$$

As an example Bozis took

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { \mu = 0 . 0 5 , \quad } } & { { x _ { 0 } = 0 . 5 2 5 , \quad \quad y _ { 0 } = 0 , } } \\ { { x _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = 0 , \quad } } & { { y _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = - 1 . 8 2 0 1 8 6 5 , } } \end{array}
$$

which gave

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { r _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } = 0 . 5 2 5 , \quad } & { p _ { \scriptscriptstyle y } = - 1 . 2 9 5 1 9 , \quad \phi _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 0 } = - 0 . 6 7 9 9 7 3 , } \\ & { 2 ( \phi _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 0 } + H _ { 0 0 } ) = - 2 . 1 3 2 0 2 . } \end{array}
$$

The orbit is one with

$$
{ \bf \tau } = 0 . 4 6 9 0 4 , \qquad e = 0 . 1 1 9 3 , \qquad e = - 3 . 1 1 3 0 5
$$

The outer boundary is given by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { - 0 . 9 4 4 8 4 r x ^ { 3 } + 0 . 6 5 4 2 5 r x ^ { 2 } + 0 . 4 8 8 5 2 x ^ { 3 } } & { { } } \\ { + 0 . 1 6 3 3 1 r x - 0 . 3 3 4 0 8 x ^ { 2 } + 0 . 8 5 9 5 3 r } & { { } } \\ { - 0 . 0 9 2 2 3 x - 0 . 4 4 9 3 5 = 0 , } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

and the inner boundary by

$$
0 . 8 4 1 0 3 r x ^ { 3 } - 0 . 5 8 2 3 7 r x ^ { 2 } - 0 . 3 5 6 5 8 x ^ { 3 }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \imath ^ { * } - \mathsf { u } . 0 0 2 9 1 { r } x ^ { * } - \mathsf { u } . 0 0 0 9 0 \mathsf { { o } } x } \\ & { \quad - 0 . 1 4 5 3 8 r x + 0 . 2 5 0 4 1 x ^ { 2 } + 1 . 1 2 5 0 4 r } \\ & { \quad + 0 . 0 5 7 8 8 x - 0 . 5 0 4 0 3 = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

The ring domain for the two-body problem is between $r ^ { * } = 0 . 5 2 5$ and $r ^ { * } = 0 . 4 1 3$ . The point A for the three-body problem (Figure 10, the outer dotted curve) is near the point $r ^ { * } = 0 . 5 2 5$ ,but the point B is near $r = 0 . 5 1 7$ ，slightly shifted inside $r ^ { * } = 0 . 5 2 5$ ，and the point

![](../chunks/Celestial mechanics - Volume 3, Part 2 Differential Equations in Celestial Mechanics (Yusu_part_0003_pages_0141-0210/auto/images/7b12eff992502cbf99c3cb9aa12d69ea1850cb279a70d56ab55812058bb50a8c.jpg)  
Figure 14.10 (Bozis, 1967)

C is well outside the inner boundary for the two-body problem (solid curve).

It is noted that Contopoulos (1966a) gave tables for the third integrals for various cases of potential functions.

Hori (1967) applied a method of canonical transformation to the solution of a dynamical system given by $\ddot { x } _ { 1 } + A _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } = \varepsilon x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$ ， $\ddot { x } _ { 2 } + A _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } =$ $2 \varepsilon x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 }$ ，with a small parameter ε. In nonresonance cases the solution is obtained through the second order in periodic perturbations and the third order in secular perturbations. He treated two types of resonance, $A _ { 1 } \approx 4 A _ { 2 }$ and $\ b { A _ { 1 } } \approx \ b { A _ { 2 } }$ . In both cases periodic and secular perturbations are obtained to the first and second order respectively.In the former case long-periodic solutions are discussed with the help of elliptic functions. They admit an equilibrium solution as a particular case,leading to a periodic solution in the original system.In all cases the third integrals are obtained and compared with those of Contopoulos.

Bozis (1967) gave the new integral in terms of the Delaunay variables $q _ { 1 } = l _ { \cdot }$ $q _ { 2 } = \theta - v _ { \mathrm { ~ ~ } }$ $P _ { 1 } = a ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ， $P _ { 2 } = [ a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 }$ as a power series in the mass ratio $\mu$ ，the eccentricity $^ e$ and the semimajor axis $\pmb { a }$ of the osculating ellipse, for purposes of comparison with Poincaré's discussion.At first he obtained the expansion for the third integral in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \phi = \phi _ { 0 } + \mu \phi _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \phi _ { 0 } = P _ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \phi _ { 1 } = - \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \left[ A ^ { - 1 / 2 } + P _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \cos \left( q _ { 1 } + q _ { 2 } \right) \right] } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + e [ P _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } f _ { 4 } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } ) + P _ { 1 } ^ { 6 } f _ { 6 } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } ) + P _ { 1 } ^ { 8 } f _ { 8 } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } ) ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + e ^ { 2 } [ P _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } g _ { 4 } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } ) + P _ { 1 } ^ { 6 } g _ { 6 } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } ) + P _ { 1 } ^ { 8 } g _ { 8 } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } ) ] } } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
n = P _ { 1 } ^ { - 3 } , \qquad e = ( 1 - P _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } / P _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

Bozis suggested using $P _ { 2 }$ instead of $\boldsymbol { P } _ { 1 }$ in the expansion in order to show that the eccentricity varies widely while the semimajor axis changes slightly.

Contopoulos (1967) found two non-isolating integrals corresponding to the z-component of the angular momentum and the total angular momentum,besides the Jacobi integral, in the three-dimensional restricted three-body problem.He constructed these integrals in the form of formal series and showed by numerical computation that the firstorder integrals are fairly well conserved for two high-inclination orbits；in other words, the semimajor axis and the orbital inclination near the primaries in the three-dimensional restricted three-body problem do not change very much.

Further, Contopoulos (1967a) found two integrals in the plane case of the elliptic restricted three-body problem with small eccentricity near the primaries.The integrals are given in the form of formal series of $\mu$ ,the eccentricities $e ^ { \prime }$ ， $^ e$ of the second and third masses,and combinations of the coordinates and the momenta ； such integrals depend also periodically on time,with frequency equal to that of the second body.The periodic terms have a factor $e ^ { \prime }$ .When $e ^ { \prime } = 0$ the integrals reduce to the Jacobi integral and the third integral of the restricted problem (Section 18.23).

Take for the second body $n ^ { \prime } = 1$ ， $a ^ { \prime } = 1$ ， $e ^ { \prime }$ small, and write $\hat { \xi } - \mu = x _ { \mathrm { { ; } } }$ ， $\eta = y$ Px= $( d \pmb { \xi } / d v ^ { \prime } ) - y , p _ { y } = ( d \eta / d v ^ { \prime } ) + x , r ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 }$ ， $C ^ { \prime } = ( 1 - e ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ，and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { H = \displaystyle \frac { C ^ { \prime } } { { r ^ { \prime } } } \left. \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ ( p _ { x } + y ) ^ { 2 } + ( p _ { y } - x ) ^ { 2 } \right] \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \left. - \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { C ^ { \prime } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) + \frac { 1 - \mu } { r } + \frac { \mu } { ( 1 + 2 x + x ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } + \mu x + \frac { \mu } { 2 } \right. \right. } } \end{array}
$$

where $r ^ { \prime } \xi , r ^ { \prime } \eta$ are the coordinates of the third mass in the rotating

reference frame around the center of mass； then the equations of motion are in canonical form:

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { x } } , \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { y } } , \frac { d p _ { x } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x } , \frac { d p _ { y } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial y } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { H = H _ { 0 } + \mu H _ { 1 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \frac { C ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ p _ { x } ^ { 2 } + p _ { y } ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } - 2 ( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } ) \right] \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \left. \qquad - \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { C ^ { \prime 2 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { r } \right] \right\} + \mu \frac { 1 } { C ^ { \prime } r ^ { \prime } } \left( \frac { 1 } { r } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } + \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \right. } } \end{array}
$$

Let us take the origin at the primary，and take the axis going through the pericentron of the second body ； then

$$
X = r ^ { \prime } x \cos v ^ { \prime } - r ^ { \prime } y \sin v ^ { \prime } , \qquad Y = r ^ { \prime } x \sin v ^ { \prime } + r ^ { \prime } y \cos v ^ { \prime } .
$$

The angular momentum of the third body is $\phi _ { 0 } = X \dot { Y } - \dot { X } Y =$ $C ^ { \prime } ( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } )$ , since $\dot { v } ^ { \prime } = C ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime 2 }$ . Next consider the second integral $I _ { 0 }$ ， the energy-angular momentum integral， which corresponds to the Jacobi integral:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { I _ { \circ } { } = { } \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \dot { X } ^ { 2 } + \dot { Y } ^ { 2 } ) - { \frac { 1 } { ( X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } } ( X \dot { Y } - \dot { X } Y ) } } \\ { { \mathrm { } ~ } } \\ { { { } ~ = { } \displaystyle { \frac { C ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 r ^ { \prime 2 } } } ( p _ { x } ^ { 2 } + p _ { y } ^ { 2 } ) + { \frac { \dot { r } ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 } } ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) + { \frac { C ^ { \prime } \dot { r } ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } } ( x p _ { x } + y p _ { y } ) } } \\ { { \mathrm { } ~ } } \\ { { \displaystyle { ~ - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime } r } - C ^ { \prime } ( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } ) } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\dot { r } ^ { \prime } = ( e ^ { \prime } / C ^ { \prime } )$ sin $v ^ { \prime }$ .For $e ^ { \prime } = 0$ this reduces to $I _ { 0 } = H _ { 0 }$ In the first power of $e ^ { \prime }$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { I _ { 0 } = H _ { 0 } + e ^ { \prime } \sin { M ^ { \prime } } \cdot ( x p _ { y } + y p _ { x } ) } \\ & { \qquad + \ e ^ { \prime } \cos { M ^ { \prime } } \cdot [ 2 ( x p _ { y } - y p _ { x } ) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ] . } \end{array}
$$

We construct two integrals of the form

$$
\phi = \phi _ { 0 } + \mu \phi _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad I = I _ { 0 } + \mu I _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } I _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$
