where $\phi _ { i } , I _ { i }$ are functions of $x , y , p _ { x } , p _ { y }$ ，and depend on $t$ periodically. Any integral $\Phi$ should satisfy

$$
\frac { d \Phi } { d t } = \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial t } + \left( \Phi , H \right) = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\frac { \partial \Phi _ { \nu + 1 } } { \partial t } + \ c { ( \Phi _ { \nu + 1 } , H _ { 0 } ) } = - \ c { ( \Phi _ { 0 } , H _ { 1 } ) } ,
$$

which gives $\Phi _ { \nu + 1 }$ when $\Phi _ { \nu }$ is known. We have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \phi _ { 1 } = - \int \left( \phi _ { 0 } , H _ { 1 } \right) d t } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { I _ { 1 } = - \int \left( I _ { 0 } , H _ { 1 } \right) d t } . } } \end{array}
$$

The integrands are computed with the preceding approximations. The result is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \phi _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac { 3 a ^ { 2 } } { 4 ( n - 1 ) } - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( n - 1 ) } \left[ 1 + \frac { 2 n ( 5 n - 4 ) e _ { \rho } } { ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } \right] } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \frac { 6 a ^ { 2 } ( 2 n - 1 ) e _ { \rho } } { ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } + \frac { 6 ( 2 n - 1 ) e x y p } { ( n - 1 ) ( n - 2 ) ( 3 n - 2 ) } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \frac { 3 e ^ { \prime } } { ( n - 1 ) ( 2 n - 1 ) ( 2 n - 3 ) } \{ [ 3 n ^ { 2 } - 4 n + 1 ) a ^ { 2 } - ( 2 n ^ { 2 } - } }  \end{array}
$$

:cos $M ^ { \prime } + ( 1 - 3 n ) x y \sin { M ^ { \prime } } \}$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle I _ { 1 } = \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { a } + \frac { 1 } { r } + \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad \quad + \frac { 3 e ^ { \prime } } { ( 2 n - 1 ) ( 3 - 2 n ) } \{ [ ( 2 n ^ { 2 } - 1 ) x ^ { 2 } - ( 3 n ^ { 2 } - 4 n + 1 ) a ^ { 2 } ] \mathrm { c } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + ( 3 n - 1 ) x y \sin { M ^ { \prime } } \} , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\rho$ is defined by $r r ^ { \prime } = a ( 1 + e \rho )$ and cos $M = - \rho + \cdots , \sin M$ $= ( p / n ) + \cdots _ { ; }$ with $p = d \rho / d t$ .For $e ^ { \prime } = 0$ the function $\not \phi _ { 1 }$ gives the third integral of the circular restricted three-body problem，already derived by Contopoulos, except the unimportant term $3 a ^ { 2 } / [ 4 ( n - 1 ) ]$

Roels and Hénon (1967) reduced the search for a third integral in the dynamics of galaxies and in the restricted three-body problem to the study of a plane mapping and its invariant curves (see Chapter 25 on surface transformations),such as

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { 1 } = x \cos \alpha - y \sin \alpha + x ^ { 2 } \sin \alpha , } \\ { y _ { 1 } = x \sin \alpha + y \cos \alpha - x ^ { 2 } \cos \alpha , } \end{array}
$$

of the so-called the eliptic case (Section 17.6),and compared the exact results with those given by Birkhoff's series (Section 21.1) truncated at the twentieth order according to the procedure of Siegel (Section 21.8).They found a general agreement except in the neighborhood of the resonance cases,in which $\alpha / 2 \pi$ is rational. They also determined the limit of existence of the invariant curves from such series.Thus the Birkhoff series,although divergent, is of great interest for practical applications.It gives estimates on the dimension of the region where the invariant curves exist and also on the error of the order $( R / R _ { 0 } ) ^ { j }$ ， where $R _ { 0 }$ is the radius of the region of existence and $j$ is the order of truncation.Whittaker's adelphic integral (Section 14.15) is of such a nature.

Contopoulos (1970a) discussed further the resonance phenomena in spiral galaxies.

Recently Bartlett and Wagner (197O) discussed the stability of motion in a periodic cubic field,whose equation of motion is

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + p ( t ) x ^ { 3 } = 0 ,
$$

in which $p ( t )$ is a periodic square-wave function with period $\pmb { \tau }$ and $\begin{array} { r } { p = p _ { 0 } } \end{array}$ ， $p _ { 0 } > 0$ ,for $- \tau / 4 < t \leq \tau / 4$ ,and $p = - p _ { 0 }$ for $\tau / 4 < t \leq 3 \tau / 4$ · Previously Bartlett (1968) located fixed points for the transformation $T ^ { n } P = P$ in the phase plane for values of $_ n$ from 1 to 14 and studied the neighborhood of the elliptic fixed point for $n = 1 1$ ，and obtained fixed points of higher multiplicity，namely for $T ^ { 3 3 }$ and $\pmb { T } ^ { 5 5 }$ playing a central role in the discussion of stability (Section 12.1).

To the accuracy $1 0 ^ { - 1 2 }$ Bartlett and Wagner found a closed region of phase space surrounding the $T ^ { 1 1 }$ elliptic fixed point on the $x$ -axis which is invariant under successive $T ^ { 5 5 }$ mappings of the phase space onto itself.Many points initially outside this region escape to infinity after a finite number of $T ^ { 5 5 }$ mappings. Some points are stable for a long time. but eventually escape to infinity also.The outer $\pmb { T } ^ { 3 3 }$ invariant curves. oscillate and intersect the inner $T ^ { 1 1 }$ oscillating invariant curves.The. inner oscillations of the $T ^ { 3 3 }$ mapping do not intersect the hyperbolic invariant curves of the $\pmb { T } ^ { 5 5 }$ mapping. The union of all $\pmb { T } ^ { 5 5 }$ hyperbolic invariant curves is actually one continuous closed curve that bounds a stable region around the $T ^ { 1 1 }$ elliptic fixed point. This region of stable motions consists of six cells: a large inner region and five smaller islands which surround the five elliptic fixed points of $\pmb { T } ^ { 5 5 }$ mapping. Points initially inside.this inner region always remain inside the region under iterations of $T ^ { 1 1 }$ . Points initially inside any one of the five island regions remain always inside it under iterations of $T ^ { 5 5 }$ but jump discontinuously through all five island regions under $T ^ { 1 1 }$ (see Figure 1b).

Contopoulos and Hadjidemetriou (1968） studied the intersections of the orbits in a three-dimensional subspace of the phase space for a two-dimensional potential corresponding to fixed values of the energy. They distinguished three types of orbits:(i) the isolating orbits whose intersection points lie on a smooth curve,which they call an invariant curve；(ii) the quasi-isolating or semi-ergodic orbits whose intersection points look as though they are scattered at random,filling parts of the available space, the limiting case being the ergodic orbits whose intersection points fill the whole available space ; and (ii) the escaping orbits, which appear only if the energy-integral surface extends to infinity.

Goudas and Barbanis (1963) showed that at the initial conditions of one family of periodic orbits of a system of differential equations, the third integral of Contopoulos (1960,1963 ；Barbanis,1962） and the Hamiltonian have collinear gradient to a fairly close approximation，and also that the third integral conserves its constant along the same family up to three significant figures, thus exhibiting the behavior of an approximate, time-independent, single-valued,and perhaps analytic,integral. But the analyticity may or may not be true for cases where the number $( A / B ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ is rational, in which case a denumerably infinite number of vanishing denominators appear in the infinite series of the third integral and cause the divergence of the series. For irrational values of $( A / B ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ ，small divisors occur and the possibility of convergence exists. Goudas (1968） showed， in accordance with Poincaré's theorem concerning the characteristic exponents in systems with multiple integrals (Section 19.2)， that the gradients of the Hamiltonian and the third integral are collinear along each member of a certain family of periodic orbits.In this family the constant of the third integral is conserved to about two or three significant figures ; but there is a new family of periodic orbits along each member of which the third integral is not conserved at all.

Bray and Goudas (1967) concluded that a new integral with a single-valued gradient cannot exist unless its gradient is collinear to the gradient of the energy integral along all singular closed solutions. Ordinary solutions,on the other hand,have never been found after extensive search by many investigators ；thus the conclusion is that general stable members of low inclination in the cases of $L _ { 2 }$ and $L _ { 3 }$ in the restricted three-body problem (Section 18.22) indeed exist.They discussed the question of a possible new integral in the neighborhood of these points.

Contopoulos and Barbanis (1968） presented numerical evidence that,although the third integral is tangent to the Hamiltonian along some periodic orbits as has been shown by Goudas (1968),it is not tangent along nonperiodic orbits,and accordingly that it is not a function of the Hamiltonian.The set of periodic orbits is probably dense in general,but a given form of the third integral is valid in the neighborhood of a limited number of these orbits,and no form of the third integral is valid for all periodic orbits except in integrable cases.

Deprit and Henrard (197O),after their experience in discussing the behavior of motion in the neighborhood of the $L _ { 4 }$ equilibrium point in the restricted three-body problem (Sections 18.24-18.25),considered the haloing effects of the third integral.

Deprit's search for a third integral having an equilibrium proceeded in two steps: he first used the given Hamiltonian system to generate separable systems related to it,and then checked to what extent in the phase space and within what threshold of accuracy he could trust the physical. information brought forth by the separable model. Birkhoff's normalization (Section 21.1） rather than Whittaker's adelphic integral (Section 14.15)，provides a better approach.Indeed, Birkhoff's normalization not only yields all geometric information contained in the invariant sections computed from the adelphic integral but also exhibits the dynamical features of the problem. In fact, if the system admits an adelphic integral, then there would appear around the elliptic equilibrium (Section 23.1) tori filled with periodic orbits (Section 21.12), that is,ordinary families of the periodic orbits of Whittaker.According to Siegel (1954；Sections 21.7-21.9)，the systems presenting this feature forms a rare set in a certain set of dynamical systems.Hence, in order to assess the credibility of an adelphic integral in the neighborhood of an ellptic equilibrium,Deprit recommends analyzing the behavior of the actual system on the tori of ordinary families of periodic orbits exhibited by the normalized approximations. Birkhoff's normalization shows immediately the locations where these tori could be.

The Hamiltonian of Contopoulos (1960)

$$
H = { \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } ) + { \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( A x ^ { 2 } + B y ^ { 2 } ) - { \varepsilon } x y ^ { 2 } - { \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 3 } } { \varepsilon } ^ { \prime } x ^ { 3 } ,
$$

where $A , B , \varepsilon , \varepsilon ^ { \prime }$ are constants and $X , Y$ and $x , y$ are conjugate pairs, can be transformed to

$$
H = H _ { 0 } + H _ { 1 } , \qquad H _ { 0 } = \omega _ { l } L + \omega _ { s } S , \qquad H _ { 1 } = H _ { 1 } ( l , s , L , S ) ,
$$

$$
\omega _ { s } = B ^ { 1 / 2 } ;
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { x = ( 2 L / \omega _ { l } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \sin l , \quad } & { { } y = ( 2 S / \omega _ { s } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \sin s , } \\ { X = ( 2 L \omega _ { l } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos l , \quad } & { { } Y = ( 2 S \omega _ { s } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos s . } \end{array}
$$

Contopoulos's Hamiltonian presents four configurations of equilibrium $E _ { 1 } , E _ { 2 } , E _ { 3 } , E _ { 4 }$ ，where $E _ { \imath }$ is in the allowable region with positive energy, $E _ { 2 }$ is in the prohibited region with negative energy，and $E _ { 3 } , E _ { 4 }$ are the intersections of the boundaries of these two regions.

Deprit further transformed the Hamiltonian from $( l , s , L , S )$ to $( l ^ { \prime } , s ^ { \prime } , L ^ { \prime } , S ^ { \prime } )$ by his Lie transforms (Section 10.44) in the form $\pmb { H } =$ $N + R$ ，where $N$ is a polynomial in $L ^ { \prime } , s ^ { \prime }$ ，and $\pmb R$ is a series in ${ \cal L } ^ { \prime 1 / 2 }$ ， $S ^ { \prime 1 / 2 }$ beginning with terms of degree $2 n + 1$ . He computed up to ${ \pmb n } =$ 10,and compared with Barbanis's (1962) third integral in which only $L ^ { \prime }$ ， $s \prime$ appear.

Suppose that the truncated system described by the normalized portion $N$ is separable.The energy manifolds $N ( L ^ { \prime } , S ^ { \prime } ) = h$ for small values of $h$ surround the equilibrium $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ at which $L ^ { \prime } = S ^ { \prime } = 0$ They are represented by polynomial curves.The solutions of the normalized system fall into three classes (Section 21.24):

（i） the natural family $\mathcal { L } _ { s }$ of singular periodic solutions for which $L ^ { \prime } = 0$ ； these are the short-period orbits emanating from $E _ { 1 }$ in association with $\omega _ { s }$ ；

（ii） the natural family $\mathcal { L } _ { \iota }$ of singular periodic solutions for which$S ^ { \prime } = 0$ ；

(iii) invariant tori of quasi-periodic orbits,each of which is characterized by

$$
\sigma ( L ^ { \prime } , S ^ { \prime } ) = { \frac { \partial N } { \partial L ^ { \prime } } } \bigg / { \frac { \partial N } { \partial S ^ { \prime } } } ,
$$

the rotation number of the torus (Sections 13.2,23.17,24.16,and 25.21).The tori degenerate into families of ordinary periodic orbits whenever the rotation number takes rational values (compare with Section 18.25).

The natural families $\mathcal { L } _ { s }$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { \iota }$ survive in the full dynamical system from its normalizing truncation.The perturbation $\pmb R$ introduces variations in the initial conditions,the period and the index of stability, the size of which may be interpreted as a measure of the normalization to the nonintegrable problem.As the energy increases from its absolute minimum at $E _ { 1 }$ ， the agreement deteriorates progressively.

The orbits of the long-period family $\mathcal { L } _ { \iota }$ have not appeared in the computation of the galactic potential. Actually the family $\mathcal { L } _ { \iota }$ ，according to the calculation of Deprit,is totally degenerate:it constitutes the class of periodic oscillations for the conservative system

$$
\begin{array} { r } { H = \frac { 1 } { 2 } X ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } A x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \varepsilon ^ { \prime } x ^ { 3 } , } \end{array}
$$

which is a kind of anharmonic oscillator. The family $\mathcal { L } _ { \iota }$ terminates with an orbit doubly asymptotic to the equilibrium $E _ { 2 }$

In the complete system as well as in the normalized system,the orbits of $\mathcal { L } _ { s }$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { \iota }$ are singular ； in particular, their four characteristic exponents are not all equal to zero.Hence any integral must have its gradient collinear with the energy integral at every point of any orbit in the families $\mathcal { L } _ { s }$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { \imath }$ . This basic theorem of Poincaré may serve as a check for assessing the accuracy of the numerical integration,and the test of collinearity may serve to decide whether the given state function is an integral on the cones defined by the families $\mathcal { L } _ { s }$ and $\mathcal { L } _ { \iota }$ . Goudas's orbits belong to the family $\mathcal { L } _ { s }$ and his expression does not meet the test of collinearity,and is thus not an integral,according to Deprit.

The numerical test,as long as it is restricted to singular periodic orbits,does not by itself give information on the functional relation between the energy integral and the third integral. If the set of periodic orbits is everywhere dense in the set of solutions,then the third integral would be in a pathological dependence with respect to the energy integral since the points at which its gradient is collinear with that of the energy integral would be everywhere dense. Consequently one way of advancing the question of the third integral consists in examining whether the galactic system admits periodic orbits other than those which are singular.The normalizing approximation $N$ offers an example of a system with an everywhere-dense family of ordinary periodic orbits ；at any point of these orbits the gradient of each action integral $L ^ { \prime }$ or $s ^ { \prime }$ is not parallel to that of energy.

Thus Deprit offered a more significant test of the third integral. Take an ordinary family of periodic orbits for $N$ and check whether it survives in the full system or breaks down into a finite number of natural families of periodic orbits.At the intervals of energy where the breakdown occurs we know that $N$ is not a valid approximation of $\pmb { H }$ In the intervals of energy where the ordinary family survives we could only conclude that the method is unable to separate the system from its approximate portrait as given by the truncation $N$

Deprit examined Goudas's (1968) computation on the symmetric orbits originated from the resonance $\sigma = 1 / 3$ in the normalized system $N$

At the energy levels where the orbits are located, $N$ is no longer an adequate picture of $\pmb { H }$ . Yet the normalization yields an important feature of the phase space. The tori and the instability zones are themselves contained in a halo of invariant quasi-periodic orbits.Deprit suggests that the normalization may still be a satisfactory approximation of the galactic potential on the halo of enveloping quasi-periodic tori.The situation is similar to the two-lane bridge of long-period orbits discovered in the Trojan problem (Section 18.25).

Contopoulos (1966b) compared the adiabatic invariants (Section 4.23)with the so-called third integral. In a dynamical system of $\pmb { n }$ degrees of freedom with a slowly varying parameter $_ n$ adiabatic invariants in the form of action integrals can be found if the system is separable.Gardner (1959) has given a method of constructing adiabatic invariants by successive approximations. Suppose that the Hamiltonian $\pmb { H }$ is a function of $t , x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 2 } , \dots$ with a small parameter $\gimel$ ，such that it is separable in the zero-th order and slowly varying in $t$ and $x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , \ldots ,$ ，as for example

$$
H = H _ { 0 } ( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , \lambda x _ { 2 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda t ) + H _ { 0 } ^ { * } ( y _ { 2 } , \lambda x _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda t ) ,
$$

where $H _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ includes all terms containing $x , y$ ,but has no zero-th order term in $y _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ ，and that the curves $\pmb { H } =$ constant for $\lambda = 0$ ， $y _ { i } =$ constant, $i > 1$ ，are closed for $\lambda$ small and $t , x _ { i } , y _ { i } , i > 1$ ， fixed. Gardner's first transformation is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = r \cos \theta , \qquad y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = r \sin \theta , \qquad J ^ { ( 1 ) } / \pi = x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } , } \\ & { } & { \theta = 2 \pi \Bigl ( \displaystyle \int ^ { x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } } \frac { d s } { | \nabla H | } \Bigr ) \bigg / \Bigl ( \displaystyle \oint \frac { d s } { | \nabla H | } \Bigr ) , \qquad r = \Bigl ( \frac { J ^ { ( 1 ) } } { \pi } \Bigr ) ^ { 1 / 2 } , \qquad } \end{array}
$$

where the transformed curves $\pmb { H } =$ constant are circles in the new variables,or

$$
x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } } , \qquad y _ { 1 } = { \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } ,
$$

$$
F _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \psi _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \lambda x _ { 2 } , y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \lambda t ) .
$$

The second transformation is

$$
x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } = x _ { 2 } + \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } , \qquad y _ { 2 } = y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \lambda \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial ( \lambda x _ { 2 } ) } , \ldots ,
$$

$$
H ^ { \prime } = H + \frac { \partial F } { \partial t } = H + \lambda \frac { \partial \psi _ { 1 } } { \partial ( \lambda t ) }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { = H _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } , \lambda t ) + H _ { 1 } ^ { * } ( y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \dots , \lambda t ) } \\ & { \phantom { = } + \phantom { } \lambda \bar { H } _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \dots , \lambda t ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\lambda \overline { { H } } _ { 1 }$ contains all terms of higher orders including $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ .The next step is to transform $H _ { 1 } + \lambda F _ { 1 } =$ constant into circles for $t$ $t , x _ { i } , y _ { i } , i > 1$ ， constant. The deviation of these curves from circles is of the order λ. The new principal function is

$$
F _ { 2 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } + x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda \psi _ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \lambda t ) ,
$$

where $\psi _ { 2 }$ does not contain terms independent of $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ . We have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } = x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \lambda \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } } , \quad } & { y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } + \lambda \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } } , } \\ & { } & \\ & { \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } = \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \lambda ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } } , \quad } & { y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } + \lambda ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial ( \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } ) } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { H ^ { \prime \prime } = H _ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime 2 } , \lambda t ) + H _ { 2 } ^ { * } ( y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , \lambda t ) } } \\ { { \phantom { H _ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } 2 + y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , \lambda t ) } } } \\ { { \phantom { H _ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } 2 + y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \lambda x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , \lambda t ) } . } } \end{array}
$$

We repeat the procedure $N$ times，then $J ^ { ( N ) } = [ ( x _ { 1 } ^ { ( N ) } ) ^ { 2 } + ( y _ { 1 } ^ { ( N ) } ) ^ { 2 } ]$ is constant up to the order $N - 1$ ，and $d J ^ { ( N ) } / d t = { \cal O } ( \lambda ^ { N } )$ .For $N \to \infty$ the function ${ \pmb J } ^ { ( N ) }$ is a formal integral of the problem.This is an adiabatic invariant. If for $t < t _ { 1 }$ and $t > t _ { 2 }$ the variables $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ are such that $\pmb { H }$ has zero derivatives of all orders with respect to $t , x _ { 2 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots ;$ then the action $\begin{array} { r } { J = \oint y _ { 1 } d x _ { 1 } } \end{array}$ is defined in $t < t _ { 1 } , t > t _ { 2 }$ ， and its change in the interval $t _ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } t _ { 1 }$ is of an order higher than $\lambda ^ { N }$

For Contopoulos's example

$$
H = { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }
$$

the invariant is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { J = 2 \int _ { x _ { 1 } ( \mathrm { m i n } ) } ^ { x _ { 1 } ( \mathrm { m a x } ) } y _ { 1 } d x _ { 1 } } } \\ { \displaystyle { = 2 \int _ { x _ { 1 } ( \mathrm { m i n } ) } ^ { x _ { 1 } ( \mathrm { m a x } ) } \left[ \left( \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \varepsilon ( d y _ { 1 } / d t ) ( d x _ { 2 } / d t ) ( d y _ { 2 } / d t ) } { \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 4 \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] d t . } } \end{array}
$$

The adiabatic invariants and the third integral are both formal series expansions in terms of a small parameter.When the third integral is given in power series of the variables with no small parameter， the energy is considered as the small parameter.The small parameter in the third integral refers to a term or terms while the adiabatic invariants refer to a variable or variables.The third integral is more general in the sense that it does not require $\pmb { H }$ to depend on $\lambda x _ { 2 }$ ，...rather than $x _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ .In the above example of Contopoulos we cannot find an adiabatic invariant because $x _ { 2 }$ ， $y _ { 2 }$ appear in the zero-th order. If $\omega _ { 2 } \ll \omega _ { 1 } ,$ then an adiabatic invariant expansion can be constructed in powers of $\omega _ { 2 }$ ，while the third integral expansion is not valid for $\omega _ { 1 } \to 0$ or $\omega _ { 2 } \to 0$ . On the other hand, the adiabatic invariants are more general in the sense that they apply also to the nonperiodic time-dependent Hamiltonian.The practical construction of a third integral is comparatively easy when $H$ is represented in series,while the change of variables to transform the curves $H _ { 0 } =$ constant to circles in the adiabatic invariants cannot in general be given analytically in a simple form.If the Hamiltonian cannot be expanded in a power series, then the method for constructing the third integral is not applicable,while Gardner's method is still valid.

For example, consider

$$
\begin{array} { r } { H = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) - \varepsilon \sin { \lambda t } \cdot y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

The third integral is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { 2 \epsilon [ \lambda \cos { \lambda t } \cdot x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } - \sin { \lambda t } \cdot \left( y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) ] } { \lambda ^ { 2 } - 4 \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } + \frac { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } { 2 \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( \tilde { \lambda } ^ { 2 } - 4 \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) } \left\{ \cos { 2 \lambda t } \cdot ( y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) - 2 ( y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) \right. } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left. - \frac { 1 } { ( \tilde { \lambda } ^ { 2 } - \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) } [ ( \tilde { \lambda } ^ { 2 } + 2 \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) \cos { 2 \lambda t } \cdot ( y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \tilde { \lambda } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) \right. } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left. \qquad \quad + \ 6 \lambda \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \sin { 2 \lambda t } \cdot x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } ] \right\} + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . . } \end{array}
$$

The adiabatic invariant obtained by Gardner's method is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { ^ { 3 } = \frac { \pi } { ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \sin \lambda t ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \bigg [ y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \sin \lambda t ) x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { \lambda \varepsilon \cos \lambda t \cdot x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } } { ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \sin \lambda t ) } } } \\ & { } & { + \{ \lambda ^ { 2 } \varepsilon ( \varepsilon \cos ^ { 2 } \lambda t [ 7 x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \sin \lambda t ) - 5 y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ]  } \\ & { } & {  - 2 \sin \lambda t ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \sin \lambda t )  } \\ & { } & {  \cdot [ x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \sin \lambda t ) - y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ] ) \} } \\ & { } & { \quad \times \{ 8 ( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \varepsilon \sin \lambda t ) ^ { 3 } \} \bigg ] . } \end{array}
$$

Both expansions are equivalent if $\varepsilon$ and $\lambda$ are small. Up to terms of order two in $\varepsilon$ or $\lambda$ we have

$$
\Phi \Big [ 1 + { \frac { \varepsilon ^ { 2 } ( 4 \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + 3 \lambda ^ { 2 } ) } { 1 6 \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } \Big ] = { \frac { J ^ { ( 3 ) } \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 \pi } } .
$$

It is seen that for small values of $\gimel$ the second-order adiabatic invariant $\pmb { J } ^ { ( 3 ) }$ is better conserved than the second-order third integral $\Phi$ But if $\lambda / \lambda _ { 1 }$ approaches unity then $\Phi$ is beter conserved than $\pmb { J } ^ { ( 3 ) }$ . This shows the range of values of $\lambda$ for which an adiabatic invariant is useful. If λ is near a resonance case then the foregoing method cannot be applied；especially, $\boldsymbol { \jmath }$ is not an adiabatic invariant for a resonance case.

Periodic orbits are the main landmarks in the study of highly perturbed dynamical systems.Contopoulos (1970,1971） found the characteristic curves of many families of periodic orbits in a galactic-type potential (124a) with increasing perturbation. There are two types of families: (a) regular families generated from the nonperturbed system, and (b） irregular families independent of the nonperturbed system. The appearance of the latter families seems to be connected with the dissolution of the invariant curves of nearby nonperiodic orbits.The number of families crossing the $x$ -axis a given number of times increases considerably as the perturbation increases. Contopoulos then used the variational equations,and found a number of relations between the solutions of the variational equations.He distinguishes two noncritical cases of periodic orbits when the characteristic exponent is $\neq 0$ and $\neq \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } / \omega$ ，and five critical cases when it is zero.He discussed the exchange of stability near the critical point of the latter case.Contopoulos found examples of three types of nonresonant and four types of resonant critical points. Any given family was found to become extremely unstable when the perturbation becomes large.For large perturbation there are stable orbits but their regions of stability are extremely small.

The intersections of oribits by a surface of section (Section 24.5; in this case the plane $x = 0$ )either form closed invariant curves or are scattered in some zone of instability. Contopoulos (1967) previously found invariant curves surrounding the central invariant point,an exceptional ring containing resonant invariant points corresponding respectively to stable and unstable periodic orbits-invariant curves near the resonant stable invariant points composed of islands—，and some dissolution of invariant curves,such that the successive intersections of orbits near an unstable periodic orbit do not lie on a simple smooth curve but seem to fill a certain area in the diagram $\bar { x } \dot { x }$ on the surface of section in the space (xxy),where $\bar { x } = A ^ { 1 / 2 } x$ ， $\bar { y } = B ^ { 1 / 2 } y$ .The dissolution depends on the magnitude of $\varepsilon$ in the equation (124a) for the potential. Contopoulos (1971) followed the evolution of invariant curves as the perturbation increases while the total energy is kept constant.The theory of the third integral predicts an increase in the area of the islands with increasing e.As $\varepsilon$ increases further the area decreases,at the same time the region of invariant curves surrounding the central invariant point decreases and disappears.If a family of periodic orbits can be transformed into periodic orbits of nonperturbed case for $\varepsilon = 0$ by a continuous change of $\varepsilon$ and of the initial conditions,then the family is said to be regular and otherwise irregular.

The empirically defined regions of dissolution of the invariant curves coincide with the zones of instability (cf., Section 25.23). The zones of instability increase with increasing $\varepsilon$ and some of the zones combine; then there appear heteroclinic doubly asymptotic orbits and new periodic orbits belonging to irregular families.When an orbit approaches a same asymptotic curve both for $t \to \infty$ and $t \longrightarrow - \infty$ , the asymptoticity is called homoclinic,and when it approaches two different asymptotic curves for $t \to \infty$ and $t \to - \infty$ it is called heteroclinic (Section 19.18). He found also open invariant curves of very complicated form.The points of intersection of most orbits in a zone of instability seem approximately ergodically distributed, but there are many exceptional regions occupied by islands around stable invariant points. Contopoulos calculated the rotation number (Section 26.21) against the distance of each invariant curve from the central invariant point along a straight line；in the zones of instability only the rotation numbers of regular periodic orbits are defined unambiguously,but near unstable invariant points the abrupt change of the rotation number indicates that we have interaction of more than one resonance in each zone of instability. The islands and the zones of instability are confirmed in rings around the central invariant point.

# I4.17INTEGRALS DEVELOPABLE ABOUT A SINGULAR POINT

Cherry (1924a,1924b,1928) considered the equation

$$
( Z , H ) = 0 ,
$$

where the left-hand member is Poisson's bracket satisfied by an integral $Z$ (Section 4.3),which is a function of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ but not of $\pmb { t }$ ，of the equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { H }$ is independent of $t$ and expressible in a convergent power series in $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ ，with the lowest-degree terms

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } + \dotsb + \lambda _ { n } x _ { n } y _ { n } .
$$

The origin is a singular point of the Hamiltonian equations,whose exponents are $\pm \lambda _ { 1 } , \pm \lambda _ { 2 } , . . . , \pm \lambda _ { n }$ . It is assumed that there exists no linear homogeneous relation with integer coefficients among the $\lambda _ { i }$ Cherry has shown that there exist $_ n$ independent power series in $x _ { 1 }$ ， $\ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ formally satisfying the condition (128) for the integrals (129),and has given the procedure for constructing such integrals.

Suppose that $Z$ is a power series in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ and write

$$
{ \cal Z } = { \cal Z } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } + { \cal Z } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 2 ) } + \cdots ,
$$

where $Z ^ { ( r ) }$ denotes the aggregate of terms of degree $r$ .Put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r l } & { H = H _ { 2 } + H _ { 3 } + \cdots , } \\ & { } \\ & { H _ { 2 } = \lambda _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } + \ldots + \lambda _ { n } x _ { n } y _ { n } . } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

Then, substituting in (128) we obtain successively

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( Z ^ { ( 1 ) } , H _ { 2 } ) = 0 , } \\ & { ( Z ^ { ( 2 ) } , H _ { 2 } ) + ( Z ^ { ( 1 ) } , H _ { 3 } ) = 0 , } \\ & { ( Z ^ { ( 3 ) } , H _ { 2 } ) + ( Z ^ { ( 2 ) } , H _ { 3 } ) + ( Z ^ { ( 1 ) } , H _ { 4 } ) = 0 , } \\ & { \cdot \cdot . . } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
Z ^ { ( 1 ) } = c _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { n } x _ { n } + c _ { n + 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { 2 n } y _ { n } ,
$$

then the first equation is satisfed by

$$
c _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = c _ { n } \lambda _ { n } = c _ { n + 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = c _ { 2 n } \lambda _ { n } = 0 .
$$

Since no $\lambda _ { r }$ vanishes, ${ Z ^ { ( 1 ) } }$ should be identically zero. Put

$$
Z ^ { { ( 2 ) } } = \sum _ { i , j } A _ { i j } x _ { i } x _ { j } + \sum _ { i , j } B _ { i j } x _ { i } y _ { j } + \sum _ { i , j } C _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } ;
$$

then the second equation is satisfied if

$$
A _ { i j } ( \lambda _ { i } + \lambda _ { j } ) = B _ { i j } ( \lambda _ { i } - \lambda _ { j } ) = C _ { i j } ( - \lambda _ { i } - \lambda _ { j } ) = 0 .
$$

Thus the coefficients of $x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdots + x _ { n } y _ { n }$ in $\boldsymbol { Z } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ are arbitrary. Since no two of the $\lambda _ { \tau }$ are equal, every other coefficient must vanish.We may thus determine the second-degree terms for $\pmb { n }$ and only $\pmb { n }$ independent integrals $Z _ { 1 } , Z _ { 2 } , \ldots , Z _ { n }$ ·

$$
Z _ { k } = Z _ { k } ^ { ( 2 ) } + Z _ { k } ^ { ( 3 ) } + \cdots ,
$$

$$
Z _ { k } ^ { ( 2 ) } = x _ { k } y _ { k } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Suppose that we have thus determined $Z ^ { ( 2 ) } , \ldots , Z ^ { ( n - 1 ) }$ in the form of polynomials of degree 2,..., $( m - 1 )$ , respectively; then the equation for $Z ^ { ( m ) }$ is

$$
( Z ^ { ( m ) } , H _ { 2 } ) + ( Z ^ { ( m - 1 ) } , H _ { 3 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( Z ^ { ( 2 ) } , H _ { m } ) = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \lambda _ { 1 } \biggr ( x _ { 1 } \frac { \partial Z ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } - y _ { 1 } \frac { \partial Z ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } \biggr ) + \cdots + \lambda _ { n } \biggr ( x _ { n } \frac { \partial Z ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial x _ { n } } - y _ { n } \frac { \partial Z ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial y _ { n } } \biggr ) } } \\ { { = F ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $F$ is a known homogeneous polynomial of degree m. Write

$$
\begin{array} { r } { F = \displaystyle \sum _ { \vphantom { \sum } } A x _ { 1 } ^ { r _ { 1 } } y _ { 1 } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { r _ { n } } y _ { n } ^ { s _ { n } } , } \\ { Z ^ { ( m ) } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \vphantom { \sum } } B x _ { 1 } ^ { r _ { 1 } } y _ { 1 } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { r _ { n } } y _ { n } ^ { s _ { n } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $r _ { 1 } + s _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { n } + s _ { n } = m$ and $\pmb { A }$ and $\pmb { B }$ are constants; then we should have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { { \cal B } } B x _ { 1 } ^ { r _ { 1 } } y _ { 1 } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { r _ { n } } y _ { n } ^ { s _ { n } } \{ \lambda _ { 1 } ( r _ { 1 } - s _ { 1 } ) + \cdots + \lambda _ { n } ( r _ { n } - s _ { n } ) \} } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \sum _ { { \cal A } } A x _ { 1 } ^ { r _ { 1 } } y _ { 1 } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { r _ { n } } y _ { n } ^ { s _ { n } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
B = \frac { A } { \lambda _ { 1 } ( r _ { 1 } - s _ { 1 } ) + \dots + \lambda _ { n } ( r _ { n } - s _ { n } ) } .
$$

Thus $Z ^ { ( m ) }$ is expressed in the form of a polynomial of degree $m$ , provided that each term in $\pmb { F }$ , for which the relation

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } ( r _ { 1 } - s _ { 1 } ) + \dots + \lambda _ { n } ( r _ { n } - s _ { n } ) = 0
$$

is satisfied, has a vanishing coefficient. Such terms are called critical. The terms for which $r _ { 1 } = s _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } = s _ { 2 } , . . . , r _ { n } = s _ { n }$ are always critical. When the coefficient of a critical term in the equation for $Z ^ { ( m ) }$ vanishes, this term appears in $Z ^ { ( m ) }$ with an arbitrary constant.

That the coefficients of the critical terms vanish can be shown in the following manner by proving indirectly that there exist $\pmb { n }$ independent power series which are integrals of the equations (129).

If we solve (128) by successive approximations after substituting

$$
x _ { r } = x _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } + x _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , y _ { r } = y _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } + y _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

and equating separately the terms of equal orders, then we obtain

$$
\frac { d x _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } } { d t } = \lambda _ { r } x _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } , \qquad \frac { d y _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } } { d t } = - \lambda _ { r } y _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } ,
$$

$\frac { d x _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { d t } - \lambda _ { r } x _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) } = \mathsf { a }$ homogeneous quadratic function of $x _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } , y _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ ， $\frac { d y _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { d t } + \lambda _ { r } y _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) } = \mathsf { a }$ homogeneous quadratic function of $x _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } , y _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ ， (1

From the first pair we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } = \alpha _ { r } e ^ { \lambda _ { r } t } , \qquad y _ { r } ^ { ( 1 ) } = \beta _ { r } e ^ { - \lambda _ { r } t } . } \end{array}
$$

Substitute these in the second pair of (132)；then

$$
\begin{array} { c }  { { \frac { d x _ { r } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 2 ) } } { d t } - \lambda _ { r } x _ { r } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 2 ) } = \displaystyle \sum _ { + } A \alpha _ { k } \alpha _ { s } e ^ { ( \lambda _ { k } + \lambda _ { s } ) t } + \displaystyle \sum _ { - } B \alpha _ { k } \beta _ { s } e ^ { ( \lambda _ { k } - \lambda _ { s } ) t } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \sum _ { - } C \beta _ { k } \beta _ { s } e ^ { - ( \lambda _ { k } + \lambda _ { s } ) t } . } } \end{array}
$$

It can be shown that $x _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ and similarly $y _ { r } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ are homogeneous quadratic functions of $\alpha _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t }$ ，., $\alpha _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t }$ ， $\beta _ { 1 } e ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } t }$ ，.., $\beta _ { n } e ^ { - \lambda _ { n } t }$ with constants or linear functions of $t$ as the coefficients. Similarly $x _ { \tau } ^ { ( \ell ) }$ and $y _ { r } ^ { ( \ell ) }$ are homogeneous functions of degree $\iota$ of the same exponential functions with polynomials of $t$ at most of degree $l - 1$ . Thus (129) may be formally solved by power series in

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } , \qquad \beta _ { 1 } e ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } t } , \ldots , \beta _ { n } e ^ { - \lambda _ { n } t } ,
$$

where $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { n }$ are arbitrary constants :

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x _ { r } = f _ { r } ( \alpha _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } , \beta _ { 1 } e ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } t } , \ldots , \beta _ { n } e ^ { - \lambda _ { n } t } ) , } \\ & { y _ { r } = g _ { r } ( \alpha _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } , \beta _ { 1 } e ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } t } , \ldots , \beta _ { n } e ^ { - \lambda _ { n } t } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Solve these equations in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \alpha _ { r } e ^ { \lambda _ { r } t } = \phi _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } , t ) , } \\ { \beta _ { r } e ^ { - \lambda _ { r } t } = \psi _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } , t ) . } \end{array}
$$

Substitute (135) in (134); then $2 n$ identities in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ will be obtained.Differentiating these identities partially with respect to $t$ we get

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 0 = \frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial t } + \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \Big ( \frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { s } } \frac { \partial \alpha _ { s } } { \partial t } + \frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial \beta _ { s } } \frac { \partial \beta _ { s } } { \partial t } \Big ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 0 = \frac { \partial y _ { r } } { \partial t } + \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial y _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { s } } \frac { \partial \alpha _ { s } } { \partial t } + \frac { \partial y _ { r } } { \partial \beta _ { s } } \frac { \partial \beta _ { s } } { \partial t } \right) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { n }$ are supposed to be constants in the differentiation for $\partial x _ { r } / \partial t$ and $\partial y _ { r } / \partial t$ . Thus we may put

$$
\frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { r } } , \qquad \frac { \partial y _ { \tau } } { \partial t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { r } } .
$$

Since

$$
\sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { n } \Big ( \frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial t } \frac { \partial y _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { p } } - \frac { \partial y _ { r } } { \partial t } \frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { p } } \Big ) = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \alpha _ { p } } ,
$$

we see that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial { \cal H } } { \partial \alpha _ { p } } } + \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left\{ \frac { \partial \alpha _ { s } } { \partial t } [ \alpha _ { s } , \alpha _ { p } ] + \frac { \partial \beta _ { s } } { \partial t } [ \beta _ { s } , \alpha _ { p } ] \right\} = 0 } , } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial { \cal H } } { \partial \beta _ { p } } } + \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left\{ \frac { \partial \alpha _ { s } } { \partial t } [ \alpha _ { s } , \beta _ { p } ] + \frac { \partial \beta _ { s } } { \partial t } [ \beta _ { s } , \beta _ { p } ] \right\} = 0 } , } \end{array}
$$

where [] denotes Lagrange's bracket (Section 1.9).

From (134) we see that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { s } } = e ^ { \lambda s t } P _ { r s } , \qquad \frac { \partial y _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { s } } = e ^ { \lambda s t } P _ { r s } ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { \frac { \partial x _ { r } } { \partial \beta _ { s } } = e ^ { - \lambda s t } P _ { r s } ^ { \prime \prime } , \qquad \frac { \partial y _ { r } } { \partial \beta _ { s } } = e ^ { - \lambda s t } P _ { r s } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } , } \end{array}
$$

where the ${ \pmb { P } } ^ { * } { \bf s }$ are power series in the arguments (133). Hence

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \left[ \alpha _ { r } , \alpha _ { s } \right] = e ^ { ( \lambda _ { r } + \lambda _ { s } ) t } \underline { { { Q } } } , \qquad \left[ \alpha _ { r } , \beta _ { s } \right] = e ^ { ( \lambda _ { \tau } - \lambda _ { s } ) t } \underline { { { Q } } } ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { \left[ \beta _ { r } , \beta _ { s } \right] = e ^ { - ( \lambda _ { \tau } + \lambda _ { s } ) t } \underline { { { Q } } } ^ { \prime \prime } , } } \end{array}
$$

where the $Q$ 's are also power series in the arguments (133). Since a Lagrange bracket is independent of $t$ but is a function of $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } ,$ $\ldots , { \beta _ { n } }$ (Section 1.9),we get from these

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { [ \alpha _ { r } , \alpha _ { s } ] = e ^ { ( \lambda _ { r } + \lambda _ { s } ) t } \beta _ { r } e ^ { - \lambda _ { r } t } \beta _ { s } e ^ { - \lambda _ { s } t } R , } } \\ { { [ \beta _ { r } , \beta _ { s } ] = e ^ { - ( \lambda _ { r } + \lambda _ { s } ) t } \alpha _ { r } e ^ { \lambda _ { r } t } \alpha _ { s } e ^ { \lambda _ { s } t } R ^ { \prime } , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { { [ \alpha _ { r } , \beta _ { s } ] = e ^ { ( \lambda _ { r } - \lambda _ { s } ) t } \beta _ { r } e ^ { - \lambda _ { r } t } \alpha _ { s } e ^ { \lambda _ { s } t } R ^ { \prime \prime } ~ } } } & { { ( r \not = s ) , } } \\ { { { [ \alpha _ { r } , \beta _ { r } ] = R ^ { \prime \prime } , } } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

where $R , R ^ { \prime } , R ^ { \prime \prime } , R ^ { \prime \prime \prime }$ are power series in the arguments (133), which should reduce to functions of $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { n }$ only， so that the terms varying with $t$ must vanish identically. The general term is thus of the form

$$
A t ^ { m } \cdot ( \alpha _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } ) ^ { p _ { 1 } } ( \beta _ { 1 } e ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } t } ) ^ { q _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \alpha _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } ) ^ { p _ { n } } ( \beta _ { n } e ^ { - \lambda _ { n } t } ) ^ { q _ { n } } ,
$$

where $\pmb { A }$ is a constant and $m , p _ { 1 } , q _ { 1 } , . . . , p _ { n } , q _ { n }$ are positive integers. Since the $\lambda ^ { \prime } { \sf s }$ are not connected with any linear homogeneous relation (131),this general term is independent of $t$ only if $m = 0$ ， $p _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 }$ ， .., $p _ { n } = q _ { n }$ . Hence any term independent of $t$ is,to a constant multiple, equal to the product of powers of $\alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } \beta _ { n }$ .Write

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } = \phi _ { 1 } \psi _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad \alpha _ { n } \beta _ { n } = \phi _ { n } \psi _ { n } = u _ { n } ;
$$

then the $\pmb R$ 's are power series in $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ . When $u _ { 1 } = \ldots { } = u _ { n } = 0$ ， we have $R ^ { \prime \prime \prime } = 0$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { [ \alpha _ { \tau } , \alpha _ { s } ] = \beta _ { r } \beta _ { s } A _ { r s } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) , } & { { } \quad [ \beta _ { r } , \beta _ { s } ] = \alpha _ { r } \alpha _ { s } B _ { r s } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } . } \\ { [ \alpha _ { r } , \beta _ { s } ] = \beta _ { r } \alpha _ { s } C _ { r s } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) , } & { { } \quad [ \alpha _ { r } , \beta _ { r } ] = 1 + D _ { r } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } } \end{array}
$$

Since $\pmb { H }$ is independent of $t$ ,the equations (129) possess the integral $H =$ constant, and, when $\pmb { H }$ is expressed in terms of $t$ $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } ,$ $\ldots , { \boldsymbol { \beta } } _ { n }$ ，then $\pmb { H }$ should be independent of $t$

It can be seen that $A _ { \tau s } , B _ { \tau s } , C _ { r s }$ are connected by

$$
A _ { r s } + B _ { r s } - C _ { r s } + C _ { s r } = 0 .
$$

In fact,by using the identities

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \frac { \partial } { \partial \beta _ { s } } \left[ \alpha _ { r } , \alpha _ { s } \right] + \frac { \partial } { \partial \alpha _ { r } } \left[ \alpha _ { s } , \beta _ { s } \right] + \frac { \partial } { \partial \alpha _ { s } } \left[ \beta _ { s } , \alpha _ { r } \right] \equiv 0 , } \\ & { } & { \frac { \partial } { \partial \alpha _ { s } } \left[ \beta _ { r } , \beta _ { s } \right] + \frac { \partial } { \partial \beta _ { r } } \left[ \beta _ { s } , \alpha _ { s } \right] + \frac { \partial } { \partial \beta _ { s } } \left[ \alpha _ { s } , \beta _ { r } \right] \equiv 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and substituting (137) in (136),we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { A _ { r s } - C _ { r s } + u _ { s } \frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { s } } ( A _ { r s } \underset {  s } { \underbrace { - } } C _ { r s } ) + \frac { \partial D _ { s } } { \partial u _ { r } } = 0 , } \\ { B _ { r s } + C _ { s r } + u _ { s } \frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { s } } ( B _ { r s } + C _ { s r } ) - \frac { \partial D _ { s } } { \partial u _ { r } } = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and accordingly

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { s } } \left\{ u _ { s } ( A _ { r s } + B _ { r s } - C _ { r s } + C _ { s r } ) \right\} = 0 ,
$$

in which $u _ { s } ( A _ { r s } + B _ { r s } - C _ { r s } + C _ { s r } )$ is a power series in $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ,$ vanishing with $\pmb { u } _ { s }$ ,and should be independent of $\pmb { u } _ { s }$ . Thus we find that the relation (138) is true.

Also we obtain from the left-hand member of (136),after substituting (137),

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \cdot \frac { \partial H } { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } - \beta _ { 1 } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \beta _ { 1 } } } \\ & { \qquad = \beta _ { 1 } ( 1 + D _ { 1 } ) \frac { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } { \partial t } - \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 2 } ^ { n } \left( \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { s } \beta _ { 1 } A _ { s 1 } - \beta _ { 1 } \beta _ { s } \alpha _ { 1 } C _ { s 1 } \right) \frac { \partial \alpha _ { s } } { \partial t } } \\ & { \qquad + \alpha _ { 1 } ( 1 + D _ { 1 } ) \frac { \partial \beta _ { 1 } } { \partial t } + \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 2 } ^ { n } \left( \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } \alpha _ { s } C _ { 1 s } + \beta _ { 1 } \alpha _ { s } \alpha _ { 1 } B _ { s 1 } \right) \frac { \partial \beta _ { 1 } } { \partial t } } \end{array}
$$

As we know, $\pmb { H }$ is a function of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ only.Hence the left-hand side is equal to

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } \frac { \partial H } { \partial u _ { 1 } } - \beta _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } \frac { \partial H } { \partial u _ { 1 } } = 0 .
$$

Or,from the relation (138),we obtain

$$
+ \ D _ { 1 } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { 1 } } { \partial t } } + \ u _ { 1 } ( B _ { 2 1 } + C _ { 1 2 } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { 2 } } { \partial t } } \ + \ \cdot \cdot \cdot + \ u _ { n } ( B _ { n 1 } + C _ { 1 n } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { n } } { \partial t } } = 0 .
$$

Again, from the remaining part of the equations (136), we find

$$
B _ { 1 2 } + C _ { 2 1 } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { 1 } } { \partial t } } + ( 1 + D _ { 2 } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { 2 } } { \partial t } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u _ { 2 } ( B _ { n 2 } + C _ { 2 n } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { n } } { \partial t } } = 0 ,
$$

$$
{ \bf \ddot { \theta } } _ { 1 n } + C _ { n 1 } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { 1 } } { \partial t } } + u _ { n } ( B _ { 2 n } + C _ { n 2 } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { 2 } } { \partial t } } + \cdots + ( 1 + D _ { n } ) { \frac { \partial u _ { n } } { \partial t } } = 0
$$

This is a set of $_ n$ homogeneous linear equations in $\partial u _ { 1 } / \partial t , \ldots , \partial u _ { n } / \partial t ,$ of which the determinant is 1 for $u _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = u _ { n } = 0$ .Thus we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial u _ { 1 } } { \partial t } = \cdots = \frac { \partial u _ { n } } { \partial t } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Hence $\phi _ { 1 } \psi _ { 1 } = \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n } \psi _ { n } = \alpha _ { n } \beta _ { n } = u _ { n }$ are $\pmb { n }$ independent integrals of (129) arranged in power series of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } .$ and independent of $t$

It can be seen by induction that the complete series is of the form

$$
Z _ { k } = x _ { k } y _ { k } + \sum { ( - 1 ) ^ { p + 1 } A _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } . . . A _ { p } }
$$

$$
. \frac { ( r _ { k } ^ { \prime } - s _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) ( . . . ( ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ) , \phi _ { 3 } ) , . . . , \phi _ { p } ) } { D _ { 1 } D _ { 1 2 } D _ { 1 2 3 } . . . D _ { 1 2 } . . . p } ,
$$

where $A _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { p } \phi _ { p }$ are any $p$ terms from $H$ and the summation is over all permutations of terms of all degrees from $H ; r _ { k } ^ { ( \alpha ) } , s _ { k } ^ { ( \alpha ) }$ ....are the exponents in the expressions similar to the expressions (13O),such as

$$
\phi _ { \alpha } = x _ { 1 } ^ { \tau _ { 1 } ^ { ( \alpha ) } } y _ { 1 } ^ { s _ { 1 } ^ { ( \alpha ) } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { \tau _ { n } ^ { ( \alpha ) } } y _ { n } ^ { s _ { n } ^ { ( \alpha ) } } ;
$$

and

$$
D _ { \alpha } = \lambda _ { 1 } ( r _ { 1 } ^ { ( \alpha ) } - s _ { 1 } ^ { ( \alpha ) } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { n } ( r _ { n } ^ { ( \alpha ) } - s _ { n } ^ { ( \alpha ) } ) ,
$$

$$
D _ { \alpha \beta } = D _ { \alpha } + D _ { \beta } , \qquad D _ { \alpha \beta \gamma } = D _ { \alpha \beta } + D _ { \tau } = D _ { \alpha } + D _ { \beta } + D _ { \tau } ,
$$

Thus the solution of (128) is expressed as a power series in the fundamental solutions,of which the initial terms are respectively $x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 }$ ， $x _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } y _ { n }$ . The function $\pmb { H }$ is expressed as a power series of $Z _ { 1 }$ ， $\ldots , Z _ { n }$ ，of which the linear terms are $\lambda _ { 1 } Z _ { 1 } + \cdots + \lambda _ { n } Z _ { n }$ . When the arbitrary constants equated respectively to $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ are assigned, those in $Z _ { n }$ may be so chosen that $H = \lambda _ { 1 } Z _ { 1 } + \dots + \lambda _ { n } Z _ { n }$ Moreover the integrals $Z _ { 1 } , Z _ { 2 } , \ldots , Z _ { n }$ can be shown to be in involution (Section 4.5).

The difficulty in discussing the convergence of the series (139) lies in the presence of the denominator $A _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } + \cdots + A _ { n } \lambda _ { n }$ ，which may be made as small as we desire.Bruns has shown,as will be discussed in Section 15.4, that the presence of such small divisors does not necessarily make a series divergent, for example,the series

$$
\sum _ { m \ { n } = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { x ^ { m } y ^ { n } } { m \lambda _ { 1 } - n \lambda _ { 2 } } }
$$

is absolutely convergent if the ratio $\lambda _ { 1 } / \lambda _ { 2 }$ is an algebraic number.However in general the series (139),as it stands-i.e.,if not rearranged in powers of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } $ -cannot be convergent for any given nonzero value of $| x _ { 1 } | , \ldots , | x _ { n } | , | y _ { 1 } | , \ldots , | y _ { n } | .$ ，however small.

Critical case. Suppose that there are $p$ and only $\pmb { p }$ commensurability relations of the form

$$
A _ { k 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { k n } \lambda _ { n } = 0 \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p )
$$

where $A _ { k 1 } , \ldots , A _ { k n }$ are integers, positive, negative, or zero, among the exponents $\pm \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pm \lambda _ { n }$ . Cherry (1924a,1924b) showed that the equations (129) possess $( n - p )$ independent integrals not involving $t$ formally developable about the origin and independent of $\pmb { H }$

As in the ordinary case we have the equations (132) or (132a). The solution will be in the form

$$
x _ { k } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } = \alpha _ { k } e ^ { \lambda _ { k } t } , \qquad y _ { k } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } = \beta _ { k } e ^ { - \lambda _ { k } t } ,
$$

$x _ { k } ^ { ( m ) } = \mathtt { a }$ homogeneous polynomial of degree $_ { m }$ in the arguments

$$
+ \ c _ { k } e ^ { \lambda _ { k } t } ,
$$

$y _ { k } ^ { ( m ) } = \mathtt { a }$ homogeneous polynomial of degree $_ { m }$ in the arguments

$$
( 1 3 3 ) + d _ { k } e ^ { - \lambda _ { k } t } .
$$

Suppose that there exist commensurability relations

$$
a _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } - b _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } + \dots + a _ { n } \lambda _ { n } - b _ { n } \lambda _ { n } = 0 ,
$$

where $a _ { 1 } , b _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n } , b _ { n }$ are positive integers or zero whose sum is $_ m$ Then in $c _ { k } e ^ { \lambda _ { k } t }$ we may have a term

$$
p _ { k } ^ { a _ { 1 } b _ { 1 } \cdots } \alpha _ { k } e ^ { \lambda _ { k } t } ( \alpha _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } ) ^ { a _ { 1 } } ( \beta _ { 1 } e ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } t } ) ^ { b _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \alpha _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } ) ^ { a _ { n } } ( \beta _ { n } e ^ { - \lambda _ { n } t } ) ^ { b _ { n } } ,
$$

and in $d _ { k } e ^ { - \lambda _ { k } t }$ a term

$$
q _ { k } ^ { \alpha _ { 1 } b _ { 1 } \cdots } \beta _ { k } e ^ { - \lambda _ { k } t } ( \alpha _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } ) ^ { \alpha _ { 1 } } ( \beta _ { 1 } e ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } t } ) ^ { \delta _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \alpha _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } ) ^ { \alpha _ { n } } ( \beta _ { n } e ^ { - \lambda _ { n } t } ) ^ { b _ { n } } ,
$$

where $p$ and $q$ are arbitrary constants. It can be shown that the Lagrange brackets have simple forms for satisfying the condition of a contact transformation (Section 1.9)

$$
\alpha _ { r } , \beta _ { r } ] = 1 , \qquad [ \alpha _ { r } , \beta _ { s } ] = 0 \qquad ( r \neq s ) , \qquad [ \alpha _ { r } , \alpha _ { s } ] = [ \beta _ { r } , \beta _ { s } ] =
$$

by properly assigning those arbitrary constants at each step of successive approximations.

Since $\pmb { H }$ is an integral, it must become independent of $t$ when expressed in terms of $\alpha _ { k } , \beta _ { k }$ ，and $t$ 、Being a power series in the arguments (133),it is a series,of which the typical term is

$$
B \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { a _ { 1 } } \beta _ { 1 } ^ { b _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { n } ^ { a _ { n } } \beta _ { n } ^ { b _ { n } } ,
$$

where $\pmb { B }$ is a constant.We have from the relation (131a)

$$
a _ { k } - b _ { k } = c _ { 1 } A _ { 1 k } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { p } A _ { p k } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ are integers. Hence the typical term in $\pmb { H }$ becomes

$$
B ( \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { A _ { 1 1 } } \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { A _ { 1 2 } } . . . \alpha _ { n } ^ { A _ { 1 n } } ) ^ { c _ { 1 } } . . . ( \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { A _ { p 1 } } \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { A _ { p 2 } } . . . \alpha _ { n } ^ { A _ { p n } } ) ^ { c _ { p } } ( \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } ) ^ { b _ { 1 } } . . . ( \alpha _ { n } \beta _ { n } ) ^ { b _ { n } } .
$$

Put

$$
u _ { k } = \alpha _ { k } \beta _ { k } , \qquad w _ { j } = \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { A _ { j 1 } } \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { A _ { j 2 } } . . . \alpha _ { n } ^ { A _ { j n } } \qquad \binom { k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n } { j = 1 , 2 , . . . , p } ,
$$

then $\pmb { H }$ is a series of positive and negative integral powers of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $u _ { n } , w _ { 1 } , \ldots , w _ { p }$ .Then the equations (129a) are written

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial \beta _ { k } } { \partial t } = \frac { u _ { k } } { \alpha _ { k } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial u _ { k } } + A _ { 1 k } \frac { w _ { 1 } } { \alpha _ { k } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial w _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { p k } \frac { w _ { p } } { \alpha _ { k } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial w _ { p } } , } \\ & { } \\ & { \frac { \partial \alpha _ { k } } { \partial t } = - \frac { u _ { k } } { \beta _ { k } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial u _ { k } } \quad \quad ( k = 1 , 2 , \cdot \cdot \cdot , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

Since $\alpha _ { k } , ~ \beta _ { k }$ are constants of integration，any function $f ( \alpha _ { k } , \beta _ { k } )$ should be an integral when expressed in terms of $x _ { k } , ~ y _ { k }$ ，and $t$ by means of (135), such that

$$
\sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \Big ( \frac { \partial f } { \partial \alpha _ { k } } \frac { \partial \alpha _ { k } } { \partial t } + \frac { \partial f } { \partial \beta _ { k } } \frac { \partial \beta _ { k } } { \partial t } \Big ) = 0 .
$$

We can find $( n - p )$ independent functions of $\alpha _ { k } , \beta _ { k }$ that satisfy such a relation, because we have from (140)

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial t } \left( \alpha _ { k } \beta _ { k } \right) = A _ { 1 k } w _ { 1 } \frac { \partial H } { \partial w _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { p k } w _ { p } \frac { \partial H } { \partial w _ { p } } ,
$$

so that

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial t } \left( \mu _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } + \mathbf { \alpha } \cdot \mathbf { \beta } \cdot \mathbf { \beta } + \mu _ { n } \alpha _ { n } \beta _ { n } \right) = 0 ,
$$

provided that

$$
\mu _ { 1 } A _ { j 1 } + \mu _ { 2 } A _ { j 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \mu _ { n } A _ { j n } = 0 \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) .
$$

These latter equations are linearly independent. Hence there are $( n - p )$ independent developable integrals,say $Z _ { 1 } , \ldots , Z _ { n - p }$ not involving $t$ ，whose expressions in terms of $\alpha _ { k } , \beta _ { k }$ are

$$
\mu _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( r ) } \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } + \mathrm { ~ \ldots ~ } + \mu _ { n } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( r ) } \alpha _ { n } \beta _ { n } ,
$$

where $\mu _ { 1 } ^ { ( r ) } , \ldots , \mu _ { n } ^ { ( r ) } \left( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , ( n - p ) \right)$ are the independent solutions of (141),the initial terms of these developments of $Z _ { r }$ being $\mu _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( r ) } x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \ldots + \mu _ { n } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( r ) } x _ { n } y _ { n }$ (see Birkhoff's transformation in Section 25.1).

# 14.18CHERRY'S THEORY ON THE FORM OF SOLUTION

In Sections 2.5-2.7 we discussed the form of solution in the cases in which the separability of variables is possible,i.e., for the conditionally periodic motions.In such cases the solution is expressed as a quasiperiodic function of Bohl (Section 2.1O),a special case of the almost periodic function of Bohr (Sections 2.11 and 2.14).If the solution of the three-body problem were known to have the properties of a quasiperiodic function, then the form of the solution would be in the form of a trigonometric series with linear functions of the independent variable $t$ as arguments,as we have obtained in the previous sections for the formal solution of the three-body problem. However the very properties of such a quasi-periodic function are those which are to be proved，except under the condition imposed by Kolmogorov and Arnold (Section 21.13). On the other hand,the question on the uniform convergence of the formal series obtained in the foregoing sections has been answered in the negative, so that the quasi-periodic property of the solution does not always follow from the apparent form of the formal expansion in trigonometric series with linear functions of $t$ as arguments.

Cherry (1923,1924) obtained under certain conditions quasi-periodic solutions without recourse to the separability property of the variables as in Section 2.7.

Consider a system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { r } } { d t } = X _ { r } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $X _ { r }$ are single-valued analytic functions of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ , independent of $t$ ,in a particular range.This system of equations is assumed (a) to possess a positive nth order integral invariant (Sections 4.17-4.20):

$$
\int M d x _ { 1 } . . . d x _ { n } ,
$$

which is said to be positive if $M$ is a single-valued positive bounded function of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ．This condition is satisfied if (142）are Hamiltonian,in which case $M = 1$ gives Liouville's theorem.The equations (142） give in general unique finite values for the velocity components $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ of the representative point in the phase space, and thus in general a determinate direction for the trajectory through that point. The exceptional points are called the singular points of the equations.A singular point at which $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ all vanish is said to be of the first type and all other singular points are of the second type. The trajectories through the points of a finite $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional domain $V$ ， defined by $a _ { r } < x _ { r } < b _ { r }$ $( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ， are assumed (b) not to pass through or arbitrarily close to any singular point of the second type.

They are assumed,(c) to be wholly contained in a finite portion of the phase space,defined by $A _ { \tau } < x _ { \tau } < B _ { \tau }$ $( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ . These assumptions are considered to be rather too strong.

Under these conditions (a),(b),and (c) Cherry (1924) proved that the general solution of (142),valid for initial values of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ within a finite $_ n$ -dimensional domain Ω,is of the form

$$
x _ { r } = \phi _ { r } \Big ( \frac { 2 \pi t } { T _ { 1 } } + \varepsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \frac { 2 \pi t } { T _ { p } } + \varepsilon _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } \Big )
$$

where $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n }$ are analytic functions of the $_ n$ arguments,periodic with period $2 \pi$ as regards the first $p , p \leq n - 1$ ，of the arguments, that these functions are single-valued within the domain $\Omega$ ，and that the periods $T _ { 1 } , \dots , T _ { p }$ are analytic functions of $z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ singlevalued within $\Omega$ ，and are connected by no identical relation of the form

$$
\frac { A _ { 1 } } { T _ { 1 } } + \cdots + \frac { A _ { p } } { T _ { p } } = 0 ,
$$

where $A _ { 1 } , \dotsc A _ { p }$ are integers not all zero, that $\varepsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \varepsilon _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ are the independent constants of integration such that the Jacobian of $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n }$ with respect to $\varepsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \varepsilon _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ is not identically zero,and that, if (144) is solved for these last quantities, $z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ are single valued functions of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ within $\Omega$

Since $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are independent of $t$ ，the general solution of (142) should contain an arbitrary constant additively to $t$ in the form

$$
x _ { r } = f _ { r } ( t + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ are analytic functions and $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ are arbitrary constants such that the Jacobian $\pmb { J }$ of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ with respect to $c _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $c _ { n }$ is not identically zero.Let $^ \mathrm { o }$ be a point where $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ are holomorphic and at which $J \neq 0$ ，such that $x _ { r } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = f _ { r } ( 0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 )$ $( r = 1$ ， $2 , \ldots , n )$ . Suppose that trajectories passing in the neighborhood of O satisfy the condition (b) ； then $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ are holomorphic and singlevalued for all real values of $t + c _ { 1 }$ and for a certain domain of $c _ { 2 } , . . . ,$ $c _ { n }$ such that $| c _ { r } | < a _ { r }$ $( r = 2 , \ldots , n )$ . Since $J \neq 0$ at $^ \mathrm { o }$ ， the equations (145） give

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { t + c _ { 1 } = \psi _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) , \ } & { { } } \\ { c _ { r } = \psi _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \ } & { { } ( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ are analytic and holomorphic at $\mathrm { ^ o }$ . Since $\pmb { J }$ is not

zero at $\mathrm { ^ \circ }$ and is a continuous function of $t + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n } ,$ it cannot vanish for any value of these arguments within the range

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { | t + c _ { 1 } | < a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \quad \quad | c _ { 2 } | < a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \quad } & { \ldots , \quad | c _ { n } | < a _ { n } ^ { \prime } , } \\ { a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } < a _ { 2 } , \quad \quad \ldots , \quad } & { a _ { n } ^ { \prime } < a _ { n } , } \end{array}
$$

and keep the same sign for $- \infty < t + c _ { 1 } < + \infty$ ， $| c _ { 2 } | < a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ ..， $| c _ { n } | < a _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ . In proceeding along any path in the phase space for $t + c _ { 1 } ,$ $c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ within these ranges, we shal not pass any singular point of $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ . The region of the phase space corresponding to these ranges is a trajectory domain $\Omega _ { 1 }$

By means of the integral invariant it follows that

$$
\frac { \partial ( M X _ { 1 } ) } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial ( M X _ { n } ) } { \partial x _ { n } } = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial M } { \partial t } = \frac { \partial M } { \partial x _ { 1 } } X _ { 1 } + \cdots + \frac { \partial M } { \partial x _ { n } } X _ { n } = X _ { 1 } \frac { \partial M } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \cdots + X _ { n } \frac { \partial M } { \partial x _ { n } } } \\ & { \qquad = - M \Big ( \frac { \partial X _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \cdots + \frac { \partial X _ { n } } { \partial x _ { n } } \Big ) = - \frac { M } { J } \frac { \partial J } { \partial t } , } \end{array}
$$

and hence that

$$
\frac { 1 } { M } \frac { \partial M } { \partial t } + \frac { 1 } { J } \frac { \partial J } { \partial t } = 0 .
$$

Thus JM as expressed in terms of $t + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ by means of (145) is a function of $c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ only， say $\psi ( c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n } )$ , and is equal to a constant $\pmb { A }$ . Hence $\pmb { J }$ is single-valued within $\Omega _ { 1 }$ ，since $M$ is so.Thus $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ are holomorphic throughout $\Omega _ { 1 }$ ，but $\Omega _ { 1 }$ is not simply connected.

Suppose that $\Omega _ { 1 }$ contain $q$ separate circuits,no one of which can be continuously deformed into combinations of the others or continuously contracted to zero while remaining within $\Omega _ { 1 }$ .Let these circuits be $C _ { 1 : }$ ， $C _ { 2 } , \ldots , C _ { q }$ ，and call $q$ the order of the trajectory domain $\Omega _ { 1 }$ with respect to the set $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ .If $\Omega _ { 2 }$ be a trajectory domain contained within $\Omega _ { 1 }$ such that $| c _ { \tau } | < a _ { r } ^ { \prime \prime } < a _ { r } ^ { \prime }$ $( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n )$ ，then $\Omega _ { 2 }$ has a definite order $q ^ { \prime }$ such that $q ^ { \prime } \leqq q$ . There must be a definite lower bound $p$ of the orders for all trajectory domains contained within $\Omega _ { 1 }$ Let Ω be a domain of order $\pmb { p }$ . Any trajectory domain contained within $\Omega$ is of order $\pmb { p }$ with respect to $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$

A system of difference equations (Cherry, 1923)

$$
\phi _ { \tau } ( s + 1 ) = f _ { \tau } \{ \phi _ { 1 } ( s ) , \ldots , \phi _ { n } ( s ) \} \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $f _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ) , \dots , f _ { n } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } )$ are analytic functions whose Jacobian is not identically zero,has a general solution of the form

$$
\phi _ { \tau } = \phi _ { \tau } ( s + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n }$ are analytic functions whose Jacobian is not identically zero and $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ are arbitrary periodic functions of $s$ with period 1. The functions are holomorphic for any value of $s + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } ,$ $\ldots , c _ { n }$ which gives them such values that $f _ { 1 } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n } )$ $f _ { n } ( \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n } )$ are holomorphic with nonvanishing Jacobian. A system of difference equations

$$
\phi _ { r } ( s + 1 ) = f _ { r } \{ s , \phi _ { 1 } ( s ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( s ) \} \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

may be reduced to the above form by taking a new independent variable and increasing the order of the system by one.A system of difference equations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \phi _ { r } ( s _ { 1 } + \lambda ) - \phi _ { r } ( s _ { 1 } ) = f _ { r } \{ s _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ( s _ { 1 } ) , \ldots , s _ { n } + \phi _ { n } ( s _ { 1 } ) \} } } \\ { { - \bar { f } _ { r } ( s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { n } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $f _ { r } ( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } )$ is an analytic function, periodic with respect to $z$ with period 1 and the mean value $\bar { f } _ { r } ( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } )$ ，while $s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { n }$ are arbitrary parameters,possesses a solution

$$
\phi _ { r } = \psi _ { r } ( s _ { 1 } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

in which $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ are analytic functions, periodic with respect to $s _ { 1 }$ with period 1 and independent of $s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { n }$

Take the general solution (145) of (142) and apply the first transformation

$$
x _ { r } = f _ { r } ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

then

$$
y _ { 1 } = t + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad y _ { r } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) ,
$$

where $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ have been seen to be holomorphic, single-valued, and with nonvanishing Jacobian within a trajectory domain Ω given by $- \infty < y _ { 1 } < + \infty$ ， $| y _ { r } | < a _ { r }$ .Let $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ be sufficiently small such that the domain $\Omega$ is of the minimum order $p$ with respect to the inverses of $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ . Let the inverse transformation to (147) be

$$
y _ { r } = \psi _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ are holomorphic throughout Ω.There are three possible cases according to the nature of the inverse functions. Case I:

when $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ are single-valued in $\Omega$ ，which case cannot occur under the assumptions (a), (b), (c).Case II:when $y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ are singlevalued within $\Omega$ but $y _ { 1 }$ is multiple-valued. Case III: when $y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ are not all single-valued within Ω. In the latter two cases a point of Ω represented by $( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ is also represented by the coordinates $y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = g _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ ，..., $y _ { n } ^ { \prime } = g _ { n } ^ { \prime \prime } ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ obtained by continuation of $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ around a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 2 }$ . In Case II we have the identities $g _ { r } ^ { \prime \prime } \equiv y _ { r }$ $\equiv y _ { r } \ ( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n )$ ，and $g _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } \not \equiv y _ { 1 }$ ; but in Case III the former identities are not all satisfied.

We can see that

$$
\frac { \partial ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) } { \partial ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) } = \frac { \partial ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) } { \partial ( y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , y _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) } = 1 ;
$$

that by writing $g _ { r } ^ { \prime \prime } ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \equiv y _ { r } + g _ { r } ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ the functions $g _ { \mathfrak { l } ; \mathfrak { n } }$ $\ldots , g _ { n }$ are independent of $y _ { 1 }$ ，so that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { g _ { 1 } ( y _ { 1 } + t , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) + y _ { 1 } + t \equiv g _ { 1 } ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) + y _ { 1 } + t , } } \\ { { g _ { r } ( y _ { 1 } + t , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) + y _ { r } \equiv g _ { r } ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) + y _ { r } } } \\ { { ( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ) ; } } \end{array}
$$

and that no one of $g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { n }$ vanishes within $\Omega$ unless identically equal to zero,

On making a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 2 }$ from a point $( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ the functions $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ return to the values

$$
