y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } + g _ { r } ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

respectively.We shall find a transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { y _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 2 } , \dots , u _ { n } ) , } \\ & { y _ { r } = \phi _ { r } ( u _ { 2 } , \dots , u _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 2 , 3 , \dots , n ) , } \end{array}
$$

in order to transform (149) to

$$
u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 2 } + 1 , \qquad u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 2 ) ,
$$

and (148) to

$$
u _ { 1 } = t + { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } , \qquad u _ { r } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

Such a transformation should satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \cdot ( u _ { 2 } + 1 , u _ { 3 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) = \phi _ { r } ( u _ { 2 } , u _ { 3 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) \qquad } \\ { + \ { g _ { r } } \{ \phi _ { 2 } ( u _ { 2 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) , \dotsc , \phi _ { n } ( u _ { 2 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) \} } \\ { \ \qquad \ ( r = 2 , 3 , \dotsc , n } \end{array}
$$

This is a set of difference equations for $\phi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n }$ . The general solution is

$$
\phi _ { \tau } = \phi _ { \tau } ( s + c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 } , \ldots , c _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

and the required transformation is

$$
y _ { r } = \phi _ { r } ( u _ { 2 } , u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

which is holomorphic and single-valued for all such values of $u _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ $u _ { n }$ corresponding to points of $\Omega$ ，and for which the Jacobian is nonvanishing and of the same value

$$
\frac { \partial ( \phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n } ) } { \partial ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) } = \frac { \partial ( \phi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n } ) } { \partial ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) }
$$

for certain ranges of $u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ for every pair of real values of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 }$

To complete the transformation from (149) to (151),insert in the first equation of (149),which has not yet been transformed, the values (152), giving, say,

$$
y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 1 } + h ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) ,
$$

where $h$ is analytic, and put

$$
y _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) .
$$

Let $\phi _ { 1 }$ be so determined that (153) is transformed to $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 1 }$ The condition for this is

$$
u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , u _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) = y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 1 } + h ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) ,
$$

or

u ${ \bf { \nabla } } + \phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 2 } + 1 , u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) = u _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) + h ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) ,$ which is a difference equation of the type already considered. The solution gives $\phi _ { 1 }$ and the complete transformation (15O) is determined.

The inverse of (150) is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { u _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 1 } ( y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) , } } \\ { { u _ { r } = \psi _ { r } ( y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The general solution of (148) is in the new coordinates

$$
u _ { 1 } = t + { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } , \qquad u _ { r } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

From(147) and (150) the old variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are .expressed in terms of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ as holomorphic,single-valued,and with nonvanishing Jacobian for such values of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ as correspond to points of the trajectory domain Ω. The inverse of this transformation is

$$
u _ { \tau } = \theta _ { \tau } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \dots , n ) ,
$$

where $\theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { n }$ are holomorphic but not all single-valued within $\Omega$

For any circuit equivalent to $C _ { 2 }$ the functions $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ return to their initial values,while $u _ { 2 }$ increases by 1.

Let $u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ be not all single-valued within Ω.A point of $\Omega$ represented by $( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ is also represented by the coordinates $g _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } ( u _ { 1 } , \dots , u _ { n } ) , \dots , g _ { n } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } ( u _ { 1 } , \dots , u _ { n } )$ obtained by continuation of $\theta _ { 1 } ,$ $\cdots , \theta _ { n }$ around a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 3 }$ and,by hypothesis,the identities $g _ { r } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } \equiv u _ { r }$ $( r = 3 , 4 , \ldots , n )$ are not all satisfied. The functions $g _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } , \ldots , g _ { n } ^ { \prime \prime \prime }$ are holomorphic and single-valued throughout Ω, with the Jacobian 1. Write $g _ { \tau } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } ( u _ { 1 } , \dots , u _ { n } ) = u _ { \tau } + g _ { \tau } ( u _ { 1 } , \dots , u _ { n } )$ , where each of $g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { n }$ is independent of $u _ { 1 }$ Also $g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , \ldots , g _ { n }$ are periodic with period 1 with respect to $u _ { 2 }$ ，that is,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { g _ { \tau } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } + 1 , u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \equiv g _ { \tau } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

On making a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 3 }$ from the point $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ the functions $\theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } , \ldots , \theta _ { n }$ return to the values

$$
u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } + g _ { r } ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

The mean value of the periodic function $g _ { r }$ with respect to $u _ { 2 }$ is in general a function of $u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ say $h ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ . We find a third transformation

$$
u _ { r } = y _ { r } + \phi _ { r } ( y _ { 2 } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

so that (155) is transformed to

$$
y _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } = y _ { \tau } + h _ { \tau } ( y _ { 3 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

The conditions for this to be so are

$$
y _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } + \phi _ { \tau } ( y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) = u _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } = u _ { \tau } + g _ { \tau } ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \phi _ { r } ( y _ { 2 } + h _ { 2 } ) = \phi _ { r } ( y _ { 2 } ) + g _ { r } \{ y _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } ( y _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , y _ { n } + \phi _ { n } ( y _ { 2 } ) \} } } \\ { { - h _ { r } ( y _ { 3 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

a set of difference equations of the type already considered. They possess a solution $\phi _ { r } \equiv \phi _ { r } ( y _ { 2 } )$ , in which $\phi _ { r }$ is single-valued and periodic with respect to $y _ { 2 }$ with period 1,and independent of $y _ { 3 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ . The transformation (156) is determined and the Jacobian does not vanish within $\Omega$

If $( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ and $( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ are the corresponding sets of values, then we see from (156) that the sets of values $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } + 1 , u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ and $( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } + 1 , y _ { 3 } , . . . , y _ { n } )$ will also correspond, since $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n }$ are periodic with respect to $y _ { 2 }$ with period 1. In terms of $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ (151) takes the same form, so that around a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 2 }$ from the point $( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ they return to the values $y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , y _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ ， respectively， where

$$
y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 } + 1 , \qquad y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 2 ) .
$$

The general solution of (151a) is

$$
y _ { 1 } = t + { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } , \qquad y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

We shal now find a transformation

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { y _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) , \quad } } & { { y _ { 2 } = u _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { y _ { r } = \phi _ { r } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \quad } } & { { ( r = 3 , 4 , \ldots , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

whereby (157) becomes

$$
u _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 3 } + 1 , \qquad u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 3 ) .
$$

We first take the last $( n - 2 )$ of (157) and by means of (159) transform them to this last form. The functions $\phi _ { 3 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n }$ are determined by solving the difference equations

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { c l l } { ( u _ { 3 } + 1 , u _ { 4 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) = \phi _ { r } ( u _ { 3 } , u _ { 4 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) } \\ { + \ h _ { r } \{ \phi _ { 3 } ( u _ { 3 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) , \dotsc , \phi _ { n } ( u _ { 3 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) \} } \\ { \ } & { \ \qquad ( r = 3 , 4 , \dotsc , n } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

and are holomorphic, single-valued,and with nonvanishing Jacobian. From (159) and (157) we have

$$
y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 1 } + h _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) , \qquad y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 } + h _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) .
$$

The functions $\phi _ { 1 }$ and $\phi _ { 2 }$ in (158) are so determined that these equations are transformed to $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 2 }$ .This gives the difference equations

$$
\phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 3 } + 1 , u _ { 4 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) = \phi _ { 1 } ( u _ { 3 } , u _ { 4 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) + h _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) ,
$$

$$
\phi _ { 2 } ( u _ { 3 } + 1 , u _ { 4 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) = \phi _ { 2 } ( u _ { 3 } , u _ { 4 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) + h _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) .
$$

From the relations $y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 } + 1$ ， $y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r }$ ， $r \neq 2$ ， we see that on making a circuit around $C _ { 2 }$ we have

$$
u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 2 } + 1 , \qquad u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 2 ) ,
$$

so that $\theta _ { 2 }$ increases by 1 while $\theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 3 } , \ldots , \theta _ { n }$ return to their initial values, where $u _ { r } = \theta _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .$ .Thus the general solution is derived from (151b),(158),(159),as

$$
u _ { 1 } = t + { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } , \qquad u _ { r } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

On making a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 4 }$ from the point $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ ， the functions $\theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { n }$ as functions of $x _ { 1 } , \ x _ { 2 } , \ \ldots , \ x _ { n }$ return to the values

$$
u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } + g _ { r } ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $g _ { \tau }$ is periodic with respect to each of $u _ { 2 }$ and $u _ { 3 }$ .Let $\overline { { { g } } } _ { r } ( u _ { 3 } , \ldots ,$ $u _ { n } )$ be the mean value of $g _ { r }$ with respect to $u _ { 2 }$ ; then we can find a function

$$
u _ { r } = z _ { r } + \phi _ { r } ( z _ { 2 } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

so that (160) is transformed to

$$
z _ { r } ^ { \prime } = z _ { r } + \bar { g } _ { r } ( z _ { 3 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .

We then find a transformation

$$
z _ { r } = y _ { r } + \psi _ { r } ( y _ { 3 } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

such that (161) becomes

$$
y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } + h _ { r } ( y _ { 4 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $h _ { \tau } ( z _ { 4 } , \dots , z _ { n } )$ is the mean value of $\vec { g } _ { r } ( z _ { 3 } , \ldots , z _ { n } )$ with respect to $z _ { 3 }$ .Each of these transformations is obtained by solving difference equations.The functions $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { n }$ and $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { n }$ are periodic with period 1. Thus $u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 2 } + 1$ and $u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r }$ ， $r \neq 2$ ，are transformed to

$$
y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 } + 1 , \qquad y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 2 ) ,
$$

and $u _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 3 } + 1 , u _ { r } ^ { \prime } , = u _ { r } , r \neq 3 ,$ to

$$
y _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 } + 1 , \qquad y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 3 ) ,
$$

and the general solution of (151c) to

$$
y _ { 1 } = t + { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } , \qquad y _ { r } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

We may find a transformation

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { y _ { r } = u _ { r } + \phi _ { r } ( u _ { 4 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) } } & { { } } & { { ( r = 1 , 2 , 3 ) , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } & { { y _ { r } = \phi _ { r } ( u _ { 4 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) } } & { { } } & { { ( r = 4 , \ldots , n ) , } } \end{array}

under which (162) becomes

u _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 4 } + 1 , \qquad u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq 4 ) .

Thus (163), (164),(165) become respectively

\begin{array} { l l l } { { u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 2 } + 1 , } } & { { u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } } } & { { ( r \ne 2 ) , } } \\ { { u _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } = u _ { 3 } + 1 , } } & { { u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } } } & { { ( r \ne 3 ) , } } \\ { { u _ { 1 } = t + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , } } & { { u _ { r } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } } & { { ( r \ne 1 ) . } } \end{array}

Finally $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are single-valued holomorphic functions of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $u _ { n }$ with nonvanishing Jacobian for certain ranges of $u _ { 5 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ cor-responding to every set of real values of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ . Inversely $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ are holomorphic functions of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ within Ω,their behavior on continuation around circuits equivalent to $C _ { 2 } , C _ { 3 } ,$ $C _ { 4 }$ being given by (167),(168),(166),respectively.

The functions $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are single-valued functions of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ ， holomorphic and with nonvanishing Jacobian for all values of $u _ { 1 } , . . . ,$ $u _ { n }$ belonging to Ω. The inverse functions are

$$
u _ { r } = \theta _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

$u _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ are single-valued functions of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ within Ω. On continuation around a circuit equivalent to $C _ { r }$ ， $( r = 2 , 3 , \ldots , p )$ the function $u _ { r }$ increases by 1 and the remaining $\pmb { u } ^ { * } \pmb { \mathrm { s } }$ return to their initial values.The general solution of (142） is

u _ { 1 } = t + { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } , \qquad u _ { r } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

We come finally to our final transformation.

The function $\theta _ { 1 }$ in (170) is such that on continuation around circuits equivalent to each of $C _ { 2 } , \ldots , C _ { p }$ ,it returns to its initial value.By(171) $\theta _ { 1 }$ increases uniformly for a representative point describing any trajectory.Hence it cannot be single-valued within Ω.Thus there is through any point a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 1 }$ ，along which $\theta _ { 1 { \mathrm { : } } }$ ，starting from a value $\pmb { u } _ { 1 }$ , returns to a value $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ not equal to $u _ { 1 }$ . We have then;

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } + g _ { r } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \quad } } & { { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } } \\ { { u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } \quad } } & { { ( r = p + 1 , \ldots , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

It can be seen that $g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { p }$ are independent of $u _ { 1 }$ and periodic with period 1 with respect to each of $u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { p }$

By a series of transformations as in the above we transform (172) to

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { z _ { r } ^ { \prime } = z _ { r } + h _ { r } ( z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) \ ~ } } & { { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } } \\ { { z _ { r } ^ { \prime } = z _ { r } \ ~ } } & { { ( r = p + 1 , \ldots , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $h _ { r } ( u _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ is the mean value of $g _ { r } ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ such as

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } d u _ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } d u _ { 3 } \ldots \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } g _ { p } ( u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ) \mathop { } { d u _ { p } } .
$$

The first transformation

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { u _ { r } = y _ { r } + \phi _ { r } ( y _ { 2 } ) \quad } } & { { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } } \\ { { u _ { \tau } = y _ { r } \quad } } & { { ( r = p + 1 , \ldots , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

replaces (172) by

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } + \bar { g } _ { r } ( y _ { 3 } , . . . , y _ { n } ) \quad } } & { { ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) , } } \\ { { \quad y _ { r } ^ { \prime } = y _ { r } \quad } } & { { ( r = p + 1 , . . . , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\bar { g } _ { r } ( y _ { 3 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ is the mean value of $g _ { \tau } ( u _ { 2 } , u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ with respect to $\pmb { u } _ { 2 }$ . The second transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { y _ { r } = v _ { r } + \psi _ { \tau } ( v _ { 3 } ) } & & { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } \\ & { y _ { \tau } = v _ { r } } & & { ( r = p + 1 , \ldots , n ) , } \end{array}
$$

replaces this last by

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { v _ { r } ^ { \prime } = v _ { r } + \bar { \overline { { { g } } } } _ { r } ( v _ { 4 } , \ldots , v _ { n } ) \ ~ } } & { { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } } \\ { { \ } } & { { \begin{array} { l } { { \begin{array} { l } { { } } \\ { { v _ { r } ^ { \prime } = v _ { r } } } \end{array} } } \end{array} } } \end{array}
$$

where $\overline { { \overline { { g } } } } _ { r } ( y _ { 4 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ is the mean of $\bar { g } _ { r } ( y _ { 3 } , \ldots , y _ { n } )$ with respect to $y _ { 3 }$ The functions $\phi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \phi _ { p }$ and $\psi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \psi _ { p }$ are all periodic with period 1. If $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } )$ and $( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \ldots , z _ { n } )$ are the corresponding sets of values, then also

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } + 1 , u _ { 3 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } } & { \mathrm { a n d } \quad z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } + 1 , z _ { 3 } , \dotsc , z _ { n } , } \\ { u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , u _ { 3 } + 1 , \dotsc , u _ { n } } & { \mathrm { a n d } \quad z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , z _ { 3 } + 1 , \dotsc , z _ { n } , } \\ { \dotsc } & { \dotsc } \end{array}

are the corresponding sets. Thus the equations

$$
u _ { s } ^ { \prime } = u _ { s } + 1 , \qquad u _ { r } ^ { \prime } = u _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq s ) \qquad ( s = 2 , 3 , \ldots , p ) ,
$$

giving the behavior of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ for a circuit equivalent to $C _ { s }$ ， are transformed to

$$
z _ { s } ^ { \prime } = z _ { s } + 1 , \qquad z _ { r } ^ { \prime } = z _ { r } \qquad ( r \neq s ) .

Thus the general solution (171) is transformed to

$$
z _ { 1 } = t + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad z _ { r } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

This completes the transformations for which the solution of difference equations is necessary. Summarizing:

$x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are single-valued functions of $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ ，holomor-phic and with nonvanishing Jacobian for certain ranges of $z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ corresponding to each set of real values of $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \dotsc , z _ { p }$ .Let

$$
x _ { r } = f _ { r } ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

then $z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ are single-valued functions of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ within Ω.The general solution of (142) is

$$
z _ { r } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) .
$$

On continuation around a circuit within Ω equivalent to $C _ { r }$ $( r = 2 , 3$ ， $\cdots , p )$ ， the coordinates $z _ { r }$ increases by 1 and the remaining $z$ return to their initial values.

By (173) the behavior of $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ with respect to a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 1 }$ is that, on continuation around a circuit within Ω equivalent to $C _ { 1 }$ from the point $( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \ldots , z _ { n } )$ ， these coordinates return to the respective values

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { z _ { r } ^ { \prime } = z _ { r } + h _ { r } ( z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) \ } } & { { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } } \\ { { z _ { r } ^ { \prime } = z _ { r } \ } } & { { ( r = p + 1 , \ldots , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The function $h _ { 1 } ( z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } )$ can never vanish for such values of $z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ that belong to points of . It can be also shown that $h _ { 2 } ( z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) , \ldots , h _ { p } ( z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } )$ are connected by no identical relation of the form

$$
A _ { 1 } + A _ { 2 } h _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { p } h _ { p } \equiv 0 ,
$$

where $A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { p }$ are integers not all zero.

The increments of $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ around circuits equivalent to $C _ { 1 }$ ， $C _ { 2 } , \ldots , C _ { p }$ are given in Table 1.We make a linear transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 2 \pi z _ { 1 } = h _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } , \quad 2 \pi z _ { 2 } = h _ { 2 } v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } , \ldots , } \\ { 2 \pi z _ { p } = h _ { p } v _ { 1 } + v _ { p } , \qquad } \end{array}
$$

where the determinant, being equal to $h _ { \scriptscriptstyle { 1 3 } }$ ,is nonzero within Ω.The increments of $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { p }$ are shown in Table 2. The equations (174)

TABLE 1   

<table><tr><td></td><td>C1</td><td>C</td><td></td><td>C</td></tr><tr><td>71</td><td>h</td><td>0</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>Z2</td><td>h</td><td>1</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>：</td><td>：</td><td>：</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Zp</td><td>hp</td><td>0</td><td></td><td>1</td></tr></table>

TABLE 2   

<table><tr><td></td><td>C1</td><td>C</td><td></td><td>Cp</td></tr><tr><td>V1</td><td>2π</td><td>0</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>V2</td><td>0</td><td>2π</td><td></td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>：</td><td></td><td>：</td><td></td><td>：</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Up</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td></td><td>2π</td></tr></table>

and (176） show that $x _ { 1 } , \ x _ { 2 } , \ . \ . \ . \ , x _ { n }$ are single-valued holomorphic functions of $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ with nonvanishing Jacobian within the trajectory domain $\Omega$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } { x _ { r } = \phi _ { r } ( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ) } & { { } } & { ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

The behavior of $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { p }$ in the circuits ${ C _ { 1 } , C _ { 2 } , \ldots , C _ { p } }$ shows that $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { n }$ are periodic with period $2 \pi$ with respect to each of $v _ { 1 }$ ， $v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { p }$ If $v _ { 1 }$ increases by $2 \pi$ while $v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ remain constant, then a circuit equivalent to $C _ { 1 }$ is made and hence $x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ return to their initial values.From (176) we obtain

$$
v _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 \pi } { h _ { 1 } } z _ { 1 } , \qquad v _ { 2 } = - \frac { 2 \pi h _ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } } z _ { 1 } + 2 \pi z _ { 2 } , \ldots ,
$$

$$
v _ { p } = - \frac { 2 \pi h _ { p } } { h _ { 1 } } z _ { 1 } + 2 \pi z _ { p } .
$$

From (175) we see that the general solution of (142) is

$$
v _ { r } = \frac { 2 \pi t } { T _ { r } } + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

where

$$
T _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { h _ { 1 } } , \qquad T _ { 2 } = - \frac { h _ { 1 } } { h _ { 2 } } , \qquad \ldots , \qquad T _ { p } = - \frac { h _ { 1 } } { h _ { p } } ;
$$

$T _ { 1 } , T _ { 2 } , \dots , T _ { p }$ are called the periods,and the integer $p$ the class of the trajectory. Their reciprocals are holomorphic functions of $z _ { p + 1 }$ $\ldots , z _ { n }$ ，since $h _ { 1 } \neq 0$ within Ω. They are connected by no identical relations of the form

$$
{ \frac { A _ { 1 } } { T _ { 1 } } } + { \frac { A _ { 2 } } { T _ { 2 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \frac { A _ { p } } { T _ { p } } } = 0 ,
$$

where $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { p }$ are integers not all zero. The coordinates are expressed as periodic functions of the arguments $2 \pi t / T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , 2 \pi t / T _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots ,$ $2 \pi t / T _ { p } ^ { \prime }$ ，where

$$
\frac { 1 } { T _ { r } ^ { \prime } } = \frac { a _ { r 1 } } { T _ { 1 } } + \frac { a _ { r 2 } } { T _ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { a _ { r p } } { T _ { p } } ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) ,
$$

and the $a _ { \tau s }$ are any integers whose determinant has the value 1. Thus there are infinitely many sets of periods for the trajectory,their aggregate being called the field of periods by Esclangon (Section 2.5). From (177)and (178) the general solution has the form

$$
x _ { \tau } = \phi _ { r } \Big ( \frac { 2 \pi t } { T _ { 1 } } + \varepsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \frac { 2 \pi t } { T _ { p } } + \varepsilon _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } \Big ) \qquad \quad 
$$

where $\varepsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \varepsilon _ { p } , z _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ are the $_ n$ independent constants of integration. The functions $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { n }$ are called quasi-periodic functions of $t$ with periods $T _ { 1 } , T _ { 2 } , \dots , T _ { p }$ . Such a motion is called quasi-periodic.We have already discussed the nature of quasi-periodic motions on the basis of the separability of the variables in the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation of the problem in Chapter 2.

Cherry (1925,1928) gave some examples of trajectories defined by differential equations of a generalized dynamical type,and discussed the general qualitative characteristics of this type of equation over an unlimited period. He presented examples of quasi-periodic trajectories and of asymptotic trajectories.

# ALMOST PERIODIC DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

14.19INTEGRAL OF A QUASI-PERIODIC FUNCTION

Poincaré (1893) considered,for applying to perturbation theory, an equation of the form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + x \phi ( t ) = \psi ( t ) ,
$$

where $\phi ( t )$ and $\psi ( t )$ are functions developable in trigonometric series with arguments proportional to $t$ .Particularly, Poincaré discussed the equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + x \phi ( t ) = 0 ,
$$

$$
\phi ( t ) = 1 + \mu \cdot \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i } \sin { ( \alpha _ { i } t + \beta _ { i } ) } ,
$$

where the $\alpha _ { i }$ are mutually incommensurable,and showed that the solution expanded in powers of $\mu$ is not uniformly convergent, but is only formal.

Bohl (1906) assumed that $\phi ( t )$ of (180) can be expanded as a uniformly convergent series

$$
u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \cdots + u _ { n } + \cdots ,
$$

where the $u _ { i }$ are rational integral functions of cos $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ ，sin $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )$ with m nonzero constants $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m }$ .Then $\phi ( t )$ represents what has been called a quasi-periodic function with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ (Section 2.10) by Bohl (1893) and Esclangon (1904).Assume further that $\phi ( t )$ and $\psi ( t )$ of (179) are quasi-periodic with the same period field and that all solutions of (179) are bounded between two finite bounds. Then Bohl (1906) obtained the general solution of（179) in the form

$$
x = c _ { 1 } \frac { \cos { \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t } } { \sqrt { R } } + c _ { 2 } \frac { \sin { \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t } } { \sqrt { R } } + T
$$

with arbitrary constants $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 }$ ，where $_ T$ and $\pmb R$ are quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ ，with the first- and second-order derivatives of the same quasi-periodic character,and $\pmb R$ is either equal to or larger than a positive constant for all values of $\pmb { t }$ Conversely,if $\pmb R$ and $_ { T }$ satisfy these conditions, then (181） represents the general solution of a differential equation of the type (179). Thus (179) admits a quasiperiodic solution other than $x = T$ if and only if $\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R \mathop { d t }$ can be written in the form

$\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t = \gamma t + \mathfrak { a }$ quasi-periodic function with period feld

$$
( \alpha _ { 1 } , \dots , \alpha _ { m } ) ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { \gamma }$ is a constant.If such representation is possible, then all solutions of (179） are quasi-periodic functions.In this case the general solution takes the form which Lindstedt obtained only as a formal solution (Sections 12.5 and 14.5). It is not always possible, however, to represent $\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R \mathop { d t }$ in the form (182). The assumption imposed on (179) does not guarantee the quasi-periodicity of all solutions.

At first we introduce certain results from Bohl's study on differential equations of a general character that are applicable to mechanics (1910). Consider

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = a _ { i 1 } x _ { 1 } + a _ { i 2 } x _ { 2 } + \dots + a _ { i n } x _ { n } + \xi _ { i } } } \\ { \displaystyle { ( i = 1 , 2 , \dots , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where the $\boldsymbol { \xi } _ { i }$ are functions, which may be continuous, of $t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } $ ， defined for all values of $t$ and for all values of $_ x$ in the domain $| x _ { i } | < \gamma ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ， such that $\mathfrak { J } \xi _ { i } / \partial x _ { k } \ ( i , k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ exist for such values of $t$ and $x$ ，and where the $a _ { i j }$ are constants such that

$$
{ \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l } { a _ { 1 1 } - \omega } & { a _ { 1 2 } } & { \cdots } & { a _ { 1 n } } \\ { a _ { 2 1 } } & { a _ { 2 2 } - \omega } & { } & { a _ { 2 n } } \\ { \cdots } \\ { a _ { n 1 } } & { a _ { n 2 } } & { \cdots } & { a _ { n n } - \omega } \end{array} \right| } = 0
$$

possesses no root whose real part is zero.Moreover assume that the allowed values of $t$ and $_ x$ satisfy

$$
\left| \frac { \partial \hat { \xi } _ { i } } { \partial x _ { k } } \right| < \Delta , \left| \frac { \xi _ { j } } { \gamma } \right| < E ,
$$

for $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ and for all values of $t$ ，where $\Delta$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathbf { \mathit { E } } }$ are positive constants determined by the matrix $\| a _ { i j } \|$ . Under these assumptions Bohl (191O) proved that there exists one and only one solution λ of the equations (183) for which $| x _ { i } | < \gamma / 2 ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ for all values of $t$ ，and that each solution for which these conditions are satisfied for sufficiently large values of $t$ tends for $t \to \infty$ asymptotically to the solution $\lambda$ ，that is,the difference of the corresponding values of $x$ tends to zero for $t \to \infty$ . A corresponding result holds for $t \to - \infty$

We assume further that the $\xi _ { i }$ can be represented in trigonometric form，that is,the $\xi _ { i }$ can be derived by the substitution $u _ { 1 } = t / \alpha _ { 1 } ,$ $u _ { 2 } = t / \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { m } = t / \alpha _ { m }$ from the functions $\rho _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ; u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } ,$ $\ldots , u _ { m } )$ for the allowed values of $t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ，where the $\rho ^ { \prime } \mathbf { s }$ are continuous for the allowed values of $x$ and for all values of $\pmb { u }$ and periodic with period 1 in each $\boldsymbol { u }$ ， and where $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m }$ are nonzero constants such that there is no relation of the form

$$
{ \frac { n _ { 1 } } { \alpha _ { 1 } } } + { \frac { n _ { 2 } } { \alpha _ { 2 } } } + \cdots + { \frac { n _ { m } } { \alpha _ { m } } } = 0
$$

with integers $n _ { i }$ not all zero.Under these assumptions Bohl proved that the elements $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ of the solution $\gimel$ are quasi-periodic functions with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ . If, furthermore, the functions $\pmb { \rho }$ possess continuous derivatives with respect to $_ x$ and $\pmb { u }$ up to a sufficiently high order, then the elements $x _ { i }$ of the solution $\gimel$ can be represented in the form of infinite series

$$
x _ { i } = \sum { A \ \sum _ { \sin } ^ { \cos } \left( \nu _ { 1 } { \frac { 2 \pi t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } } \right) \cos \left( \nu _ { 2 } { \frac { 2 \pi t } { \alpha _ { 2 } } } \right) \cdot \cdot \cdot } \ { \sin } ^ { \cos } \left( \nu _ { m } { \frac { 2 \pi t } { \alpha _ { m } } } \right) ,
$$

where the $\nu _ { i }$ are nonnegative integers, and the series converge absolutely and uniformly for all values of $t$

This idea has been applied by Bohl (191O) to problems in dissipative mechanics,to the motion of a mechanical system near an equilibrium position,and to the theory of Saturn's rings (Section 12.19).He considered

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } - [ a + \lambda ( t ) ] z = \psi ( t ) \qquad ( a > 0 ) ,
$$

where $\lambda ( t )$ ， $\psi ( t )$ are derived by the substitution $u _ { 1 } = t / \alpha _ { 1 }$ ， $u _ { 2 } = t / \alpha _ { 2 } ;$ ， $\dots , u _ { m } = t / \alpha _ { m }$ from $\Lambda ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { m } )$ ， $\Psi ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { m } )$ ，which are defined for all values of $\pmb { u }$ and continuous for all values of $t$ and periodic with period 1 in each $\pmb { u }$ ，and where $| \lambda ( t ) |$ is bounded superiorly by finite bounds determined by $_ { \alpha }$ for all values of $t$ .Then the general solution is represented by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \overline { { T } } + c _ { 1 } \frac { \exp { \left[ \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } T d t \right] } } { \sqrt { T } } + c _ { 2 } \frac { \exp { \left[ \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } T d t \right] } } { \sqrt { T } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\bar { \tau }$ and $T$ are quasi-periodic functions with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 }$ $\ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ . If $\Lambda$ and $\Psi$ possess continuous derivatives with respect to $\pmb { u }$ up to a sufficiently high order， then $\overline { T }$ and $_ T$ can be represented as multiple Fourier series (Section 16.9).

As was shown in Section 2.1O,a quasi-periodic function $f ( t )$ with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ can be expanded as the series $u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } +$ $\cdots + u _ { n } + \cdots$ By replacing $t / \alpha _ { \mu }$ by $x _ { \mu } \equiv t / \alpha _ { \mu }$ ， $( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )$ and denoting it by $\Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ ， we get a uniformly convergent series $U _ { 1 } + U _ { 2 } + \cdots + U _ { n } + \cdots$ . Denote the domain of such an $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ -place by $X$ where a set of values such as $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ $x _ { n }$ is called a place (Stelle； c.f., Section 2.2O). This series is uniformly convergent for the domain $X$ and the domain E,which consists of the accumulation places of the points of $\ b X$ . Thus

$$
\Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ) = U _ { 1 } + U _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

is uniformly convergent in E.If the place $( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi _ { m } )$ belongs to ,then the places $( x _ { 1 } \equiv \pmb { \xi } _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \equiv \pmb { \xi } _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } \equiv \pmb { \xi } _ { m } )$ belong to . Let $( \eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } , \dots , \eta _ { m } )$ be another place belonging to $\Xi$ ，then the places $( x _ { 1 } \equiv \mu \pmb { \xi } _ { 1 } + \nu \eta _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m } \equiv \mu \pmb { \xi } _ { m } + \nu \eta _ { m } )$ ，where $\pmb { \mu }$ ， $\nu$ are integers, belong to E.The function $\Psi$ is periodic with period 1 for all its congruent places. If $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ is an accumulation place of $\Xi$ of the first class (Section 2.10), then

$$
f ( t ) = \Psi \left( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 2 } } , \dots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } \right) .
$$

Let $( \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ be a place of E. By replacing $t / \alpha _ { \mu }$ by $t / \alpha _ { \mu } + \sigma _ { \mu }$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )$ we obtain a function

$$
\phi ( t ) = \Psi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 2 } } + \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } + \sigma _ { m } \Big ) ,
$$

which is quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ . The function $\phi ( t )$ is said to be related (verwandt) to $f ( t )$ and to possess the phase field $( \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ relative to $f ( t )$ . In fact, $\Psi ( x _ { 1 } + \sigma _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } + \sigma _ { 2 } , . . . ,$ $x _ { m } + \sigma _ { m } )$ corresponds to $\phi ( t )$ in the same way as $\Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ to $f ( t )$ . Put $\phi ( t ) = f ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ . If $\sigma _ { 1 } \equiv \tau _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } \equiv \tau _ { 2 } , . . . , \sigma _ { m } \equiv \tau _ { m } ,$ then we have $f ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } ) = f ( t , \tau _ { 1 } , \tau _ { 2 } , \ldots , \tau _ { m } )$ . Let $( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ $s _ { m } )$ be a place of E, then $\phi ( t , s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , . . . , s _ { m } ) = f ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } + s _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } + s _ { 2 } , . . . ,$ $\sigma _ { m } + s _ { m } )$ . Thus the set of functions related to $\phi ( t )$ coincides with the set of functions related to $f ( t )$ ，where ${ \boldsymbol { \phi } } ( t ) = \phi ( t , 0 , 0 , \dots , 0 )$ and $f ( t ) = f ( t , 0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 )$ are included in each of the sets.

Given a set of functions ${ f _ { 1 } ( t ) , f _ { 2 } ( t ) , . . . , f _ { n } ( t ) }$ ，whose elements are quasi-periodic functions with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ . The functions ${ f } _ { 1 } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } ) , \ldots , { f } _ { n } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ are related to the first :set and characterized by the phase field $( \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ . It can be shown that $\phi ( t )$ and $f ( t )$ have the same constant parts.In fact,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \phi ( t ) d t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \Psi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 2 } } + \sigma _ { 2 } , \dots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } + \sigma _ { m } \Big ) d t } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad = \int _ { \ast } ^ { t + \tau } \Psi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 2 } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 2 } } + \sigma _ { 2 } , \dots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } + \sigma _ { m } \Big ) d t } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad = \int _ { \ast } ^ { 0 } W d t + \int _ { \ast } ^ { t + \tau } W d t + \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } W d t , } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad W = \Psi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \dots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } + \sigma _ { m } \Big ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac 1 t \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \phi ( t ) d t - \displaystyle \frac 1 t \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) d t = \frac { \displaystyle \int _ { \tau } ^ { 0 } W d t + \displaystyle \int _ { t } ^ { t + \tau } W d t } { \displaystyle t } } \\ { \displaystyle +  \frac 1 t \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } [ W - \Psi \Bigl ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , . . . , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } \Bigr ) ] d t . } \end{array}
$$

Since $| \Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ) | \leq k$ ，we can take $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ so that we have with a given value of $\varepsilon > 0$

$$
\left| W - \Psi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 2 } } , \dots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } \Big ) \right| \leq \frac { \varepsilon } { 2 } .
$$

Thus we get for $t > 0$

$$
\left| { \frac { 1 } { t } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \phi ( t ) ~ d t - { \frac { 1 } { t } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) ~ d t \right| \leq { \frac { 2 k ( \tau ) } { t } } + { \frac { \varepsilon } { 2 } } .
$$

If $t \geq 4 k ( \tau ) / \varepsilon$ ， then the left-hand member is $\leqq \varepsilon$ .Hence we obtain

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \left( { \frac { 1 } { t } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \phi ( t ) d t - { \frac { 1 } { t } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) d t \right) = 0 .
$$

Suppose that the integral of a quasi-periodic function $f ( t )$ with the period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ is such that

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) d t = \phi ( t )
$$

remains between finite bounds for all values of $t$ .Then Bohl (1906) proved that $\phi ( t )$ is also quasi-periodic with the same period field.In fact, the function $\phi ( t )$ is bounded superiorly by $g$ and inferiorly by $k$ ， $g > k$ . Choose $\gamma$ so that, $0 < \gamma < g - k _ {  }$ ，then $t _ { 1 }$ and $t _ { 2 }$ can be found such that $\phi ( t _ { 1 } ) > g - ( \gamma / 6 )$ ， $\phi ( t _ { 2 } ) < k + ( \gamma / 6 )$ hold for $t _ { 1 } \neq t _ { 2 }$ . By an auxiliary theorem proved by Bohl (1906) we can see that there exists. a $T > 0$ such that, between $r$ and $r + T$ ，whatever $r$ may be, two numbers $\vartheta$ and $\vartheta + | t _ { 2 } - t _ { 1 } |$ can be found so that the behavior of $f ( t )$ between $\vartheta$ and $\vartheta + | t _ { 2 } - t _ { 1 } |$ differs from that of $f ( t )$ between $t _ { 1 }$ and $t _ { z }$ with the fluctuation which is smaller in absolute value than $\gamma / [ 6 ( t _ { 2 } - t _ { 1 } ) ]$ Thus we have

$$
\left| \int _ { \vartheta } ^ { \vartheta + | t _ { 2 } - t _ { 1 } | } f ( t ) d t - \int _ { \rho } ^ { \rho + | t _ { 2 } - t _ { 1 } | } f ( t ) d t \right| < \frac { \gamma } { 6 } ,
$$

where $\rho$ denotes the smaller of $t _ { 1 }$ and $t _ { 2 }$ .Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \vdash \left| { t } _ { 2 } - { t } _ { 1 } \right| ) - \phi ( \vartheta ) = \phi ( \rho + | { t } _ { 2 } - { t } _ { 1 } | ) - \phi ( \rho ) + \omega , \qquad | \omega | < \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 6 } , } \\ & { - \left| { t } _ { 2 } - { t } _ { 1 } \right| ) - \phi ( \vartheta ) | \equiv | \phi ( \rho + | { t } _ { 2 } - { t } _ { 1 } | ) - \phi ( \rho ) | - | \omega | } \\ & { \qquad > g - k - \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 3 } - \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 6 } = g - k - \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Since $k \leq \phi ( t ) \leq g$ ，the larger of $\phi ( \vartheta + | t _ { 2 } - t _ { 1 } | )$ and $\phi ( \vartheta )$ is larger than $g - ( \gamma / 2 )$ and the smaller is smaller than $\pm \ : ( \gamma / 2 )$ . Hence, independently of the choice of $r$ ，there are between $r$ and $r + T$ such values of $t$ for which $\phi ( t ) > g - ( \gamma / 2 )$ and for which $\phi ( t ) < k + ( \gamma / 2 )$ ，

If $\tau / \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \tau / \alpha _ { m }$ do not differ from some integers by less than $\varepsilon$ ， then there is an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that

$$
| f ( t + \tau ) - f ( t ) | < \frac { \tau } { 2 T } .
$$

Choose the first value of $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ which satisfies this condition,and form

$$
\phi ( t _ { 0 } + \tau ) - \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) = \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 0 } + \tau } f ( t ) d t \qquad ( t _ { 0 } \mathrm { a r b i t r a r y } ) .
$$

There are between $t _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 0 } + T$ the numbers $^ { c }$ and $^ d$ such that

$$
\phi ( c ) > g - \frac { \gamma } { 2 } , \phi ( d ) < k + \frac { \gamma } { 2 } .
$$

Now

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( t _ { 0 } + \tau ) - \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) - \left[ \phi ( c + \tau ) - \phi ( c ) \right] = \displaystyle \int _ { c + \tau } ^ { t _ { 0 } + \tau } f ( t ) d t - \displaystyle \int _ { c } ^ { t _ { 0 } } f ( t ) d t } \\ { = \displaystyle \int _ { c } ^ { t _ { 0 } } \left[ f ( t + \tau ) - f ( t ) \right] d t . } \end{array}
$$

From

$$
\phi ( c + \tau ) \equiv g , ~ \phi ( c ) > g - \frac { \gamma } { 2 } , ~ \phi ( c + \tau ) - \phi ( c ) < \frac { \gamma } { 2 }
$$

we obtain

$$
\int _ { c } ^ { t _ { 0 } } \left[ f ( t + \tau ) - f ( t ) \right] d t \leq \frac { \gamma } { 2 T } | t _ { 0 } - c | < \frac { \gamma } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\phi ( t _ { 0 } + \tau ) - \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) < \gamma .
$$

Similarly we have

$$
( t _ { 0 } + \tau ) - \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) - [ \phi ( d + \tau ) - \phi ( d ) ] = \int _ { d } ^ { t _ { 0 } } [ f ( t + \tau ) - f ( t ) ] d t .
$$

From

$$
\phi ( d + \tau ) \equiv k , \qquad \phi ( d ) < k + \frac { \gamma } { 2 } , \qquad \phi ( d + \tau ) - \phi ( d ) > - \frac { \gamma } { 2 }
$$

we obtain

$$
\left| \int _ { d } ^ { t _ { 0 } } \left[ f ( t + \tau ) - f ( t ) \right] d t \right| \leq \frac { \gamma } { 2 T } | t _ { 0 } - d | < \frac { \gamma } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\int _ { a } ^ { t _ { 0 } } \left[ f ( t + \tau ) - f ( t ) \right] d t > - \frac { \tau } { 2 } , \phi ( t _ { 0 } + \tau ) - \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) > - \tau .
$$

Hence we obtain

$$
| \phi ( t _ { 0 } + \tau ) - \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) | < \gamma .
$$

There exists an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that for $\eta > 0$

$$
| \phi ( t + \tau ) - \phi ( t ) | < \eta ,
$$

if $\tau / \alpha _ { 1 } , \dots , \tau / \alpha _ { m }$ differ from some integers by less than $\varepsilon$ Thus $\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) \ d t$ is quasi-perodic with period feld $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$

f $\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) \ d t$ lies betweenfinitebounds,thentheconstantpartof $f ( t )$

is equal to zero. Denote by $\lambda ( t )$ the part of $f ( t )$ substracted by its constant part. Then the integral of $f ( t )$ can be put in the form

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) d t = c t + F ( t ) \qquad ( c = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ) ,
$$

where $F ( t )$ is a function remaining between finite bounds for all values of $t$ and $^ c$ is the constant part of $f ( t )$ , if and only if $\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \lambda ( t ) d t$ remains between finite bounds for all values of $t$ ,i.e.， if and only if $\pmb { F } ( t )$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ ·

Let $f ( t )$ be quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ , and let $\phi ( t )$ be an integral of $f ( t )$ that remains between finite bounds.Then $\phi ( t )$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ .If $( \pmb { \sigma } _ { 1 } , \dots , \pmb { \sigma } _ { m } )$ is any phase field, then $\phi ( t , \pmb { \sigma } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pmb { \sigma } _ { m } )$ is an integral of $f ( t , \pmb { \sigma } _ { 1 } , \dots , \pmb { \sigma } _ { m } )$ .In fact,by associating $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ and $\Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ with $f ( t )$ and $\phi ( t )$ ， respectively,we have for any $\boldsymbol { \tau }$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t , \sigma ) d t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } F \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \dots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } + \sigma _ { m } \Big ) d t } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad = \int _ { \tau } ^ { t + \tau } F \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \dots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } + \sigma _ { m } \Big ) d t } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad = \int _ { \tau } ^ { t + \tau } \Big [ F \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \dots \Big ) - F \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \dots \Big ) \Big ] d t } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \phi ( t + \tau ) - \phi ( \tau ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t , \sigma ) d t - [ \phi ( t , \sigma ) - \phi ( 0 , \sigma ) ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \int _ { - \tau } ^ { t + \tau } \Big [ F \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } - \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \ldots \Big ) - F \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots \Big ) \Big ] d t } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \left[ \Phi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots \Big ) - \Phi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \sigma _ { 1 } , \ldots \Big ) \right] } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \left[ \Phi ( \sigma _ { 1 } , \ldots ) - \Phi \Big ( \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots \Big ) \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

By taking $\pmb { \tau } \_ \mathbf { s0 }$ that the three terms on the right-hand side are arbitrarily small in absolute value, the left-hand member can be made arbitrarily small by a proper choice of $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ .Since the left-hand member is independent of $\tau$ ，it should be zero.Hence we obtain

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t , \sigma ) d t = \phi ( t , \sigma ) - \phi ( 0 , \sigma ) .
$$

It should be noted that the integral of a quasi-periodic function minus its constant part does not necessarily remain between finite bounds. If the constant part of a quasi-periodic function $f ( t )$ is equal to zero and if $f ( t )$ does not admit any integral which remains between finite bounds, then a phase feld $( \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ such that $\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t , \sigma ) \ d t$ $\geq 0$ ，and a phase field $( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { m } )$ such that $\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t , s ) d t \cong 0$ can be constructed (Hagihara,1967).

# 14.20HOMOGENEOUS EQUATION WITH QUASI-PERIODIC COEFFICIENTS

Consider a differential equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \phi ( t ) \cdot x = 0 ,
$$

where $\phi ( t )$ is a quasi-periodic function defined for all $t$ with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ ,or more generally

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \phi ( t , \sigma ) \cdot x = 0 ,
$$

where $( \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ is a phase field. Give to this ${ \pmb \sigma } ^ { * }$ -period field special values $s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots , s _ { m }$ ，such that the equation

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + \phi ( t , s ) \cdot x = 0
$$

admits a pair of independent integrals $\pmb { u }$ and $v$ ，both of which are bounded by finite bounds for all $t$ ，Then the functions $u ( t + \tau )$ and $v ( t + \tau )$ are the solutions of

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + \phi \Big ( t , s _ { 1 } + { \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } } , \ldots , s _ { m } + { \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } } \Big ) \cdot x = 0 .
$$

There exist $\tau _ { 1 } , \tau _ { 2 } , \ldots , \tau _ { m }$ and $A , B , \alpha , \beta$ such that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } \left( s _ { 1 } + { \frac { \tau _ { i } } { \alpha _ { 1 } } } + \nu _ { 1 i } \right) = r _ { 1 } , \dots , \qquad \operatorname* { l i m } \left( s _ { m } + { \frac { \tau _ { i } } { \alpha _ { m } } } + \nu _ { m i } \right) = r _ { m } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \operatorname* { l i m } u ( \tau _ { i } ) = A , } & { { } } & { \operatorname* { l i m } v ( \tau _ { i } ) = B , } \\ { \operatorname* { l i m } u ^ { \prime } ( \tau _ { i } ) = \alpha , } & { { } } & { \operatorname* { l i m } v ^ { \prime } ( \tau _ { i } ) = \beta , } \end{array}
$$

where $\nu _ { 1 i } , \nu _ { 2 i } , \dots , \nu _ { m i }$ are integers. Denote by $g ( t )$ and $h ( t )$ the solutions of

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + { \mathcal { O } } ( t , r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { m } ) \cdot x = 0
$$

such that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { g ( 0 ) = A , \qquad g ^ { \prime } ( 0 ) = \alpha , } } \\ { { h ( 0 ) = B , \qquad h ^ { \prime } ( 0 ) = \beta , \qquad A \beta - B \alpha = c } } \end{array}
$$

with a nonzero constant $^ c$ . Then we have

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } u ( t + \tau _ { i } ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \phi \Big ( t , s _ { 1 } + \frac { \tau _ { i } } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , s _ { m } + \frac { \tau _ { i } } { \alpha _ { m } } \Big ) \cdot u ( t + \tau _ { i } ) = 0 ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } g ( t ) } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \phi ( t , r _ { 1 } , . . . , r _ { m } ) \cdot g ( t ) = 0 ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } \left[ u ( t + \tau _ { i } ) - g ( t ) \right] + \phi ( t , r _ { 1 } , \ldots , r _ { m } ) [ u ( t + \tau _ { i } ) - g ( t ) ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \left[ \phi \Big ( t , s _ { 1 } + \frac { \tau _ { i } } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , s _ { m } + \frac { \tau _ { i } } { \alpha _ { m } } \Big ) - \phi ( t , r _ { 1 } , \ldots , r _ { m } ) \right] u ( t } . }  \end{array}
$$

If an arbitrary finite $\scriptstyle { \mathbf { } } t \mathbf { } \cdot { \mathbf { } }$ -interval is chosen，then $| u ( t + \tau _ { i } ) - g ( t ) |$ and $| u ^ { \prime } ( t + \tau _ { \lambda } ) - g ^ { \prime } ( t ) |$ can be made arbitrarily small in this interval by choosing the index $_ i$ sufficiently large.A similar circumstance can occur for the functions $v ( t )$ and $h ( t )$ .Moreover $s _ { 1 } + ( \tau _ { i } / \alpha _ { 1 } ) , \ldots , s _ { m }$ $+ \ ( \tau _ { i } / \alpha _ { m } )$ can be made to differ from $r _ { 1 } , \ldots , r _ { m }$ ，respectively，by arbitrarily small amounts,by subtracting proper integers, if necessary, again by choosing the index $_ i$ sufficiently large. Hence the assumption made on the equation (185) is valid also for any equation of the type (184).

Consider the constants $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ and the function $f$ defined by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { c _ { 1 } u ^ { * } + c _ { 2 } u v + c _ { 3 } v ^ { * } = J , } \\ & { 2 c _ { 1 } u u ^ { \prime } + c _ { 2 } ( u v ^ { \prime } + u ^ { \prime } v ) + 2 c _ { 3 } v v ^ { \prime } = f ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { c _ { 1 } [ 2 u ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 u ^ { 2 } \phi ( t , s ) ] + c _ { 2 } [ 2 u ^ { \prime } v ^ { \prime } - 2 u v \phi ( t , s ) ] } \\ & { ~ + ~ c _ { 3 } [ 2 v ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 v ^ { 2 } \phi ( t , s ) ] = f ^ { \prime \prime } , } \end{array}
$$

where the determinant of the coefficients is $2 ( u v ^ { \prime } - u ^ { \prime } v ) ^ { 3 } = 2 c ^ { 3 }$ By varying $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ we obtain a set of such functions $f$ Taking another pair of solutions of (184) we obtain the same set.Let $p > 0$ . There is a function $f _ { 1 }$ which is $\geq p$ for all $t$ among the functions of this set. Choose such a function $f _ { 1 }$ and denote the corresponding values of $c _ { \mathrm { 1 3 } }$ $c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ by $c _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , c _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , c _ { 3 } ^ { \prime }$ .We can assign a constant $\kappa$ such that $\sqrt { f _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime 2 } }$ $\leq \kappa$ for all $t$ .We repeat the same procedure for the equation (187) with the pair of solutions $g ( t )$ and $h ( t )$ , and put $\psi _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } g ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } g h +$ $c _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } h ^ { 2 }$ . We have $\psi _ { 1 } \geq p$ ， $\sqrt { \psi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \psi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + \psi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } \leq K$ for all $t$ .Then we see that, if a function $f ( t )$ of the set for a differential equation of the type (184) satisfies the condition

$$
\begin{array} { r } { f ( t ) \geq a , \qquad \sqrt { f ^ { 2 } + f ^ { \prime 2 } + f ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } \leq b , \qquad ( a > 0 , b \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ) , } \end{array}
$$

then the functions of the set for another differential equation of the same type,i.e.， for a related equation (Section 14.19)， satisfy the same condition. By suitable choice of the constants $^ { a }$ and $^ { b }$ a function of this character can always be found,and indeed $_ a$ can be chosen to be an arbitrary positive number.

Consider all functions $f$ satisfying $f \geq p > 0$ for all $t$ for the equation (185).The expression $\sqrt { f ^ { 2 } + f ^ { \prime 2 } + f ^ { \prime \prime 2 } }$ for such functions admits an upper bound $G ( t )$ ，which itself admits a lower bound $^ { g }$ . It can be shown that one and only one function satisfying $G ( f ) = g$ can be found.If we start,instead of from (185） itself,from an equation related to (185) and choose the same value of $p$ , then the same value of $\pmb { g }$ can be obtained for the new equation,as that for (185). The uniqueness proof of the function satisfying $G ( f ) = g$ is carried out in the following manner. Suppose that there were two such functions $f _ { 1 }$ and $f _ { 2 }$ so that $G ( f _ { 1 } ) = G ( f _ { 2 } ) = g$ .Then $\omega = ( f _ { \mathrm { 1 } } + f _ { \mathrm { 2 } } ) / 2$ would belong to the set of functions for (185) and we should have $\omega \geq p$ . Hence $\omega$ would be one of such functions and we should have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \left( \frac { f _ { 1 } + f _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + f _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } + f _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { + \left( \frac { f _ { 1 } - f _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - f _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - f _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
= \frac { f _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + f _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime 2 } + f _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + f _ { 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } + f _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } { 2 } \leq g ^ { 2 } ,
$$

accordingly

$$
\omega ^ { 2 } + \omega ^ { \prime 2 } + \omega ^ { \prime \prime 2 } \leq g ^ { 2 } - ( \chi ^ { 2 } + \chi ^ { \prime 2 } + \chi ^ { \prime \prime 2 } ) ,
$$

where $\chi = ( f _ { 1 } - f _ { 2 } ) / 2$ would belong to the set. Denote by $\pmb q$ the lower bound of $x ^ { 2 } + x ^ { \prime 2 } + x ^ { \prime \prime 2 }$ as a function of $t$ ； then we should have

$$
\sqrt { \omega ^ { 2 } + \omega ^ { \prime 2 } + \omega ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } \leq \sqrt { g ^ { 2 } - q ^ { 2 } } , \qquad g \leq G ( \omega ) \leq \sqrt { g ^ { 2 } - q ^ { 2 } } .
$$


<!-- chunk 0004: pages 211-280 -->
Hence $| \chi | , | \chi ^ { \prime } | , | \chi ^ { \prime \prime } |$ could have arbitrarily smallvalues. The parameters $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ of $\chi$ would be zero. Thus we should have $\chi = 0$ ，and hence $t _ { 1 } = t _ { 2 }$ . This is absurd.Hence with a fixed $p > 0$ there exists for the equation (185) and for any related equation one and only one function that satisfies the condition $f \geq p$ ， $G ( f ) = g$

It can be proved that functions satisfying $G ( f ) = g$ are， together with the first- and second-order derivatives,quasi-periodic functions with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$

Under the condition that the solutions $u , v$ of (185) are bounded for all $t$ there exist constants $g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , g _ { 3 }$ such that the function $f = g _ { 1 } u ^ { 2 }$ $+ ~ 2 g _ { 2 } u v + g _ { 3 } v ^ { 2 }$ satisfies the condition $f \geq p$ with an arbitrarily chosen positive number $\pmb { p }$ and that $f , f ^ { \prime } , f ^ { \prime \prime }$ are quasi-periodic with the same period field,where $g _ { 1 } g _ { 3 } - g _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$ is positive.

Put

$$
z = \sqrt { \overline { { f } } } \cos \left[ c \sqrt { \overline { { g _ { 1 } g _ { 3 } - \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ \it ~ g _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } } } } \cdot \int _ { a } ^ { t } \frac { d t } { f } + k \right] ;
$$

then

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } = z \Big [ \frac { 2 f f ^ { \prime \prime } - f ^ { \prime 2 } - 4 c ^ { 2 } ( g _ { 1 } g _ { 3 } - g _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ) } { 4 f ^ { 2 } } \Big ] = - \phi z .
$$

Thus $z$ is a solution of (185). The general solution is

$$
x = c _ { 1 } \sqrt { f } \cos \left( h \int _ { a } ^ { t } \frac { d t } { f } \right) + c _ { 2 } \sqrt { f } \sin \left( h \int _ { a } ^ { t } \frac { d t } { f } \right) ,
$$

$$
h = c { \sqrt { g _ { 1 } g _ { 3 } - g _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } } .
$$

Put $| h | / f = R$ ; then $\pmb R$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ Also, $\pmb R$ is greater than a positive constant for all $t$ ，Thus the general solution is writen, with constants $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , k , c _ { 1 } ,$

$$
x = k _ { 1 } \frac { \cos \int _ { a } ^ { t } R d t } { \sqrt { R } } + k _ { 2 } \frac { \sin \int _ { a } ^ { t } R d t } { \sqrt { R } } ,
$$

or

$$
x = \frac { k } { \sqrt { R } } \cos \left( \int _ { a } ^ { t } R d t + c _ { 1 } \right) .
$$

The integral $\int _ { 0 } ^ { u } R \mathop { d t }$ increases with $\pmb { u }$ and takes all real values only once.Hence the general integral is

$$
x = { \frac { k } { \sqrt { R } } } \cos \left( \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t \right) .
$$

Now assume that the equation

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + \Phi ( t ) \cdot x = 0 ,
$$

where $\Phi ( t )$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ defined for all $t$ ，admits as solutions two independent quasi-periodic functions with period field $( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu } )$ . The solutions can be written

$$
u = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { R } } \cos { \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t } , \qquad v = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { R } } \sin { \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t } ,
$$

where $\pmb R$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ ， so that positive constants $a , b$ can be chosen such that $a \leq R \leq b$ . Write

$$
r = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { R } } = \sqrt { u ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } } , \phi = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R \ : d t .
$$

Let $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 }$ and $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 }$ be the values of $r$ and $\phi$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$ and $t = t _ { 2 }$ ， respectively.Since $\phi$ increases with $t$ ，we have

$$
| \phi ( t _ { 2 } ) - \phi ( t _ { 1 } ) | \leq b | t _ { 2 } - t _ { 1 } | .
$$

If $( t _ { 1 } - t _ { 2 } ) / b _ { 1 } , \ldots , ( t _ { 1 } - t _ { 2 } ) / b _ { \mu }$ are very close to some integers， then we see that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { A = r _ { 1 } \cos \phi _ { 1 } - r _ { 2 } \cos \phi _ { 2 } , \qquad B = r _ { 1 } \sin \phi _ { 1 } - r _ { 2 } \sin \phi _ { 2 } , } \\ & { A r _ { 2 } \sin \phi _ { 2 } - B r _ { 2 } \cos \phi _ { 2 } = r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } \sin { ( \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } ) } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
A r _ { 2 } \cos \phi _ { 2 } + B r _ { 2 } \sin \phi _ { 2 } = r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } \left[ \cos \left( \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } \right) - 1 \right] + r _ { 2 } ( r _ { 1 } - r _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

as well as sin $( \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } )$ ，cos $( \phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 } ) - 1$ ，, are all arbitrarily small in absolute value. Hence $\phi _ { 2 } - \phi _ { 1 }$ differs from an integral multiple of $2 \pi$ by an arbitrarily small amount. If $T / b _ { 1 } , \ldots , T / b _ { \mu } , T \ge 0$ are very nearly equal to some integers, then $\phi ( t + T ) - \phi ( t ) = n \cdot 2 \pi + \varepsilon ,$ where $| \pmb \varepsilon |$ is small.By choosing $T$ so that $| \varepsilon | < \pi / 2$ ，we can make $_ n$ take a value corresponding to $T$ for all $t$ Choose $T > 1$ so that $| \varepsilon | < q , 0 < q \leq \pi / 2$ .Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \phi ( t + T ) - \phi ( t ) = n \cdot 2 \pi + \varepsilon _ { 1 } , } \\ & { \phi ( t + 2 T ) - \phi ( t + T ) = n \cdot 2 \pi + \varepsilon _ { 2 } , } \\ & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ & { \phi ( t + \nu T ) - \phi ( t + \overline { { \nu - 1 } } T ) = n \cdot 2 \pi + \varepsilon _ { \nu } , } \end{array}
$$

accordingly

$$
\phi ( t + \nu T ) - \phi ( t ) = \nu \Big [ n \cdot 2 \pi + \frac { \varepsilon _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \varepsilon _ { \nu } } { \nu } \Big ] ,
$$

$$
\left| \varepsilon _ { 1 } \right| , \ldots , \left| \varepsilon _ { \nu } \right| < q .
$$

Put $\theta = \nu T + \vartheta , 0 \leq \vartheta < T$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \phi ( t + \theta ) - \phi ( t ) = \phi ( t + \theta ) - \phi ( t + \nu T ) + \phi ( t + \nu T ) - \phi ( t ) } \\ & { \qquad = \nu [ n \cdot 2 \pi + \{ q \} ] + \{ b | \vartheta | \} , } \end{array}
$$

where $\{ z \}$ denotes a quantity $\leq z$ in absolute value.Thus

$$
\frac { \phi ( t + \theta ) - \phi ( t ) } { \theta } = \frac { { n \cdot 2 \pi } + \{ q \} + \{ b T / \nu \} } { T + \{ T / \nu \} } ,
$$

or, if $\nu$ is suficiently large,

$$
\frac { \phi ( t + \theta ) - \phi ( t ) } { \theta } = n { \cdot } \frac { 2 \pi } { T } + \{ 2 q \} .
$$

If $\theta _ { 1 } > K$ ， $\theta _ { 2 } > K$ ,then we finally find

$$
\left| \frac { \phi ( t _ { 1 } + \theta _ { 1 } ) - \phi ( t _ { 1 } ) } { \theta _ { 1 } } - \frac { \phi ( t _ { 2 } + \theta _ { 2 } ) - \phi ( t _ { 2 } ) } { \theta _ { 2 } } \right| \leq 4 q .
$$

Hence we arrive at the theorem that corresponding to an arbitrary positive integer $q$ there exists a constant $\kappa$ such that the inequality (190) holds if $\theta _ { 1 }$ and $\theta _ { 2 }$ are greater than $\kappa$ . Thus there exists a number $\pmb { \sigma }$ such that

$$
\left| \frac { \phi ( t + \theta ) - \phi ( t ) } { \theta } - \sigma \right|
$$

can be made arbitrarily small for all $t$ if $\theta$ is chosen sufficiently large. The proof consists in constructing a sequence $\theta ^ { \prime } , \theta ^ { \prime \prime }$ 。... and the cor-responding sequence $t ^ { \prime } , t ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots$ ， such that both sequences tend to infinity.The sequence

$$
\frac { \phi ( t ^ { \prime } + \theta ^ { \prime } ) - \phi ( t ^ { \prime } ) } { \theta ^ { \prime } } , ~ \frac { \phi ( t ^ { \prime \prime } + \theta ^ { \prime \prime } ) - \phi ( t ^ { \prime \prime } ) } { \theta ^ { \prime \prime } } ,
$$

converges to a fixed number $\pmb { \sigma }$

Furthermore it can be shown by reductio ad absurdum that $\psi ( t ) =$ $\phi ( t ) - \sigma t$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \ldots , b _ { \mu } )$ . Thus

where $\pmb { \sigma }$ is the constant part of $\pmb R$ . The second term on the right-hand side is a quasi-periodic function with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ by the theorem shown in Section 14.19 on the integral of a quasi-periodic function.

The assumption of the validity of this theorem can be generalized. It is suficient to impose the assumption that the equation (189) admits two independent solutions,one of which is quasi-periodic with a certain period field while the other remains between finite bounds for all $t$ .In fact, let $( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu } )$ be the period feld of $\omega$ ， so that the field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \dots , \dot { \alpha } _ { m } )$ is contained in the feld $( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu } )$ . Let two independent solutions of (189) be

$$
u = { \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { R } } } \cos { \Bigl ( } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t { \Bigr ) } , \qquad v = { \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { R } } } \sin { \Bigl ( } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t { \Bigr ) } ,
$$

then we have, with constants $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \alpha$ ，

$$
\omega = c _ { 1 } u + c _ { 2 } v = \frac { \sqrt { c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { R } } \cos { \left( \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R \ : d t + \alpha \right) } ,
$$

for which $\omega ^ { \prime } = d \omega / d t$ is bounded on both sides.Similarly

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \omega ^ { \prime \prime } d t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { d ^ { 2 } \omega } { d t ^ { 2 } } d t
$$

is bounded on both sides. Since $\omega ^ { \prime \prime }$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu } )$ by assumption, $\omega ^ { \prime }$ is quasi-periodic with period field $( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu } )$ . Hence

$$
\omega ^ { \prime } = - \frac { R ^ { \prime } } { 2 R } \omega - R \frac { \sqrt { c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { R } } \sin \left( \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t + \alpha \right) .
$$

Accordingly we see that

$$
{ \frac { \sqrt { c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { R } } } \sin \left( \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t + \alpha \right) = - { \frac { \omega ^ { \prime } + ( R ^ { \prime } / 2 R ) \omega } { R } }
$$

is quasi-periodic with period field $( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu } )$ .But the left-hand member is a solution of (189) which is independent of $\omega$ . Thus we come to the same assumption as before.

# 14.2INONHOMOGENEOUS EQUATION WITH

QUASI-PERIODIC COEFFICIENTS

Consider a system of nonhomogeneous equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = p _ { i 1 } x _ { 1 } + p _ { i 2 } x _ { 2 } + . . . + p _ { i n } x _ { n } + R _ { i }
$$

$$
( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { p } _ { i k }$ and $\pmb { R } _ { i }$ are quasi-periodic functions defined for all $t$ with

period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ . Replace $p _ { i k } ( t ) , R _ { i } ( t )$ by $p _ { i k } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ ， $R _ { i } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ ,respectively,with a phase field $( \sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } )$ ; then we get the related system of equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { \iota } } { d t } = p _ { i 1 } ( t , \sigma ) x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { i n } ( t , \sigma ) x _ { n } + R _ { \iota } ( t , \sigma )
$$

The corresponding homogeneous system is

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = p _ { i 1 } ( t , \sigma ) x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { i n } ( t , \sigma ) x _ { n }
$$

$$
( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Theorem 1: Let it be known that at least for a definite special phase field the nonhomogeneous equations (192） admit a solution whose absolute value cannot take any arbitrarily large value,and that the homogeneous equations (193),whatever phase field may be chosen，never admit any solution whose absolute value may become arbitrarily small but cannot become arbitrarily large. Then the equations (192) admit for all phase fields at least a solution whose elements are quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ ·

Thus, if the equations (192) are known to admit a solution whose elements $( x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t ) )$ are quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $\alpha _ { m } )$ for a definite phase feld $( \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } )$ ，then $x _ { 1 } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime }$ $- \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } )$ ,，..., $x _ { n } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } )$ ，where $( \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \dots , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } )$ is any phase field,are the elements of a solution of the equations formed with phase field $( \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \dots , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } )$ . In fact, let $( \omega _ { 1 } , \ldots , \omega _ { n } )$ be the elements of the solution determined by the condition $\omega _ { 1 } ( 0 ) = x _ { 1 } ( 0 , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots ,$ $\sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } )$ ， $, \omega _ { n } ( 0 ) = x _ { n } ( 0 , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } )$ of the equations (192) with phase field $( \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \dots , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } )$ . Consider the elements $x _ { 1 } ( t + \tau )$ ， $\ldots , x _ { n } ( t + \tau )$ of the solution of (192) with phase field $( \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + ( \tau / \alpha _ { 1 } )$ ， $\cdot \cdot \cdot , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } + ( \tau / \alpha _ { m } ) )$ . We have

$$
x _ { 1 } ( t + \tau ) = x _ { 1 } \Big ( t , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \dots , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } \Big ) ,
$$

$$
x _ { n } ( t + \tau ) = x _ { n } \Big ( t , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } \Big ) ,
$$

$$
x _ { 1 } ( \tau ) = x _ { 1 } \Bigl ( 0 , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } \Bigr ) , \qquad \cdots \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
x _ { n } ( \tau ) = x _ { n } \Big ( 0 , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \dots , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } \Big ) .
$$

Choose $\tau$ so that

$$
{ \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 2 } } } , \qquad \dots , \qquad { \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } } ; \qquad \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + { \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } } , \qquad \dots , \qquad \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } + { \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } } ;
$$

and

$$
\sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \qquad \ldots , \qquad \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } ; \qquad \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime }
$$

differ,respectively，by arbitrarily small amounts if subtracted by suitable integers.Thus $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ can be taken in a finite $\pmb { t }$ -interval so that $x _ { 1 } ( t + \tau ) , . . . , x _ { n } ( t + \tau )$ are arbitrarily close to

$$
x _ { 1 } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } ) , . . . , x _ { n } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Thus we write

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \omega _ { 1 } ( t ) = x _ { 1 } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } ) , \qquad \ldots , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \omega _ { n } ( t ) = x _ { n } ( t , \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } - \sigma _ { m } ^ { \prime } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Theorem 2: Given the equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = q _ { i 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + q _ { i n } x _ { n } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $q _ { i k }$ are quasi-periodic with period feld $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ defined for all $t$ .These equations are known to have only solutions with bounded absolute values. Suppose further that

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } ( q _ { 1 1 } + q _ { 2 2 } + \cdots + q _ { n n } ) d t
$$

is bounded between finite bounds for all $t$ . Then none of the related equations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = q _ { i 1 } ( t , \sigma ) x _ { 1 } + \cdots + q _ { i n } ( t , \sigma ) x _ { n } } } \\ { \displaystyle { ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

admits a solution with arbitrarily small absolute value.

For example, if all solutions of the equation

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + x f ( t ) = \phi ( t )
$$

are bounded, where $f ( t ) , \phi ( t )$ are quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } ,$ $\ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ for all $t _ { i }$ ，then this equation admits a solution for which $x$ ， $d x / d t , d ^ { 2 } x / d t ^ { 2 }$ are quasi-periodic with period field $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m } )$ ·

Gelman (1957) showed that the question of reducibility of a linear homogeneous first-order differential equation with quasi-periodic coefficients is, in the case of order two, connected to the questions of the existence of quasi-periodic solution of the Riccati equation and the arithmetic nature of the characteristic exponents in the Fourier series of the coefficients.Adrianova (1962) considered a matrix equation $d x / d t = P ( t ) \cdot x$ where $P ( t )$ is quasi-periodic.Liapounov called this equation reducible if there is a nonsingular matrix $Z ( t )$ sufficiently smooth for the transformation $x = Z ( t )$ to give the equivalent system ${ d y } / { d t } = C y$ with a constant matrix $c$ (see Chapter 21).If $P ( t )$ is periodic, then the equation is reducible since the Floquet theory (Section 12.1) implies that every solution has the form $Z ( t )$ exp $\{ C t \}$ with a periodic function $Z ( t )$ . In general,a quasi-periodic system is not reducible.Adrianova proved that the system is reducible if the eigenvalues of $\pmb { P } _ { 0 }$ ，the mean value of $P ( t )$ ，are real and distinct,if the elements of the matrix $P ( t )$ are not too large and if an additional condition on the basic frequencies of $P ( t )$ is satisfied.

# 14.22 SECULAR CONSTANT

Consider a differential equation

$$
\frac { d s } { d t } = \phi ( s , t ) ,
$$

where $\phi ( s , t )$ is continuous and periodic with period 1 with respect to both $\pmb { S }$ and $t$ for all values of $s , t$ .Then there exists a constant $\pmb { \mu }$ such that the difference of an arbitrarily chosen solution of (196) and $\mu t$ remains bounded between finite bounds for all $t$ .This theorem has been stated by Poincaré (1885a) in connection with the stability of a motion in the sense of Poisson (Sections 2.19 and 23.11),by Levi (1911) in connection with a differential equation with periodic coefficients,and by Levi-Civita(1911) and Trevisani(1912) in connection with the motion of the lunar node.Bohl (1916) raised the question of whether a solution of (196) can be put in the form

$$
s = \mu t + f ( t , \mu t ) ,
$$
