where $\textstyle f ( x , y )$ is continuous and periodic with period 1 with respect to both $_ x$ and $y$ ，and $f ( t , \mu t )$ can be approximated by an integral polynomial of sin $2 \pi t$ cos $2 \pi t$ ，sin $2 \pi \mu t$ ，and cos $2 \pi \mu t$ . It may be thought that,if a solution can be written in the form (197) in the case of irrational values of $\pmb { \mu }$ ， then all solutions can be put in the form

$$
s = \mu t + c + f ( t , \mu t + c )
$$

by a suitable choice of $^ c$ ，and conversely that (197a） represents a solution for any value of $^ c$ . Let a solution be represented on the surface of a torus for an irrational value of $\mu$ ； then the necessary and

sufficient condition is based on the circumstance whether the representative curve fills the surface of the torus everywhere densely.Bohl (1916) also considered a differential equation

$$
\frac { d s } { d t } = \phi ( s , t ) + \varPsi ( \varepsilon , s , t ) ,
$$

where $\psi ( \varepsilon , s , t )$ satisfies the same condition as that for $\phi ( s , t )$ for any special value of the parameter ε smaller in absolute value than an assigned positive number，so that $| \psi |$ can be made arbitrarily small by the choice of a sufficiently small $| \pmb \varepsilon |$ Let $\rho$ be the characteristic number of (196a） such that $| \mu - \rho |$ can be made arbitrarily small by the choice of a sufficiently small $| \varepsilon |$ .Bohl (Section 27.2） proved the following

Theorem:If (197a) is a solution of (196) that coincides for $t = 0$ with an arbitrary solution $\pmb { \sigma }$ of (196a), then

$$
\sigma = \rho t + c + f ( t , \rho t + c ) + \eta ,
$$

in which $| \eta |$ can be made arbitrarily small by the choice of a sufficiently small $| \pmb \varepsilon |$

Bohl's discussion (1916) is based on the representation of the solution on rigorous ground,while Poincaré's theory (1885) is concerned with the expansion of the integral in powers of a parameter in the formal solution. The foregoing is a special case of quasi-periodic functions with two arguments $x = t$ ， $y = \rho t + c$ .The problem can be extended to general quasi-periodic functions and also to almost periodic functions.It has been proved by Bohr (1930,1932), Jessen (1935,1937),Fenchel and Jessen (1935) and Jessen and Tornehave (1952) that an almost periodic function $f ( t )$ can be put in the form

$$
r ( t ) \exp \{ 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \phi ( t ) \} , \phi ( t ) = \mu t + \omega ( t ) ,
$$

with a constant $\pmb { \mu }$ and an almost periodic function $\omega ( t )$ . The quantity $\pmb { \mu }$ is called the secular constant or the mean motion.Further discus-sion is postponed to Sections 27.1-27.8 on almost periodic motions, where we will take up the question of the existence of mean motions in Lagrange's theory of secular perturbation (Section 8.14).

# 14.23LINEAR DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION WITH

# QUASI-PERIODIC RIGHT-HAND MEMBER

Esclangon (1913,1914,1915a,1915b) considered a linear diferential equation with constant coefficients $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { n }$ and with a quasi-periodic function $\phi ( t )$ as the right-hand member

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { n } y } { d t ^ { n } } } + A _ { 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } y = { \phi ( t ) } ,
$$

where $\phi ( t )$ is a uniformly continuous quasi-periodic function with period field $( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { p } )$ . Let $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { k }$ be the roots of the characteristic equation

$$
F ( r ) = r ^ { n } + A _ { 1 } r ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } = 0
$$

with the degree of multiplicity $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { k }$ respectively. A particular integral is, whatever $\phi ( t )$ may be, represented by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { y = - \frac { \alpha _ { 1 } } { F ^ { \alpha _ { 1 } } ( r _ { 1 } ) } \int _ { t } ^ { c _ { 1 } } \exp { \{ r _ { 1 } ( t - u ) \} } \cdot ( t - u ) ^ { \alpha _ { 1 } - 1 } \phi ( u ) d u - \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \quad \quad - \frac { \alpha _ { k } } { F ^ { \alpha _ { k } } ( r _ { k } ) } \int _ { t } ^ { c _ { k } } \exp { \{ r _ { k } ( t - u ) \} } \cdot ( t - u ) ^ { \alpha _ { k } - 1 } \phi ( u ) d u , } } \end{array}
$$

with arbitrary constants $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { k }$

To extend this to the case in which the characteristic equation admits zero roots or purely imaginary roots,we consider bounded integrals of a linear differential equation

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { n } y } { d t ^ { n } } } + p _ { 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { n } y = { \phi } ( t ) ,
$$

where $p _ { n } , p _ { n - 1 } , p _ { n - 1 } ^ { \prime } , p _ { n - 2 } , p _ { n - 2 } ^ { \prime } , p _ { n - 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , p _ { 1 } , p _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , p _ { 1 } ^ { ( n - 1 ) }$ ，and $\phi ( t )$ are all bounded for $0 < t < \infty$ ．Esclangon (1915） proved that，if (202) admits a continuous and bounded integral $y$ in the $\scriptstyle { \mathbf { \pmb { t } } } \cdot { \mathbf { \pmb { t } } } \cdot { \mathbf { \pmb { t } } } \cdot { \mathbf { \pmb { t } } } \cdot { \mathbf { \pmb { t } } } \cdot { \mathbf { \pmb { t } } } \left. { \begin{array} { r l } \end{array} } \right.$ -interval in question，then the successive derivatives of this integral,up to the order $_ n$ inclusive,are bounded for the $t$ -interval (see Erugin,1966).

The extracting operation which Esclangon adopted for the proof is that of the normal functions of Bochner (1927) as will be described in Section 14.25. The weak point of Esclangon's proof was remedied by Landau (1927) on the basis of the Hardy-Litlewood proposition.

Recently Nakajima (1972) proved the existence of quasi-periodic solutions of quasi-periodic systems.

# I4.24 LINEAR DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION WITH ALMOST

# PERIODIC RIGHT-HAND MEMBER

Bohr and Neugebauer (1926) extended the theorems of Esclangon on a quasi-periodic differential equation to those on an almost periodic differential equation,by modifying the proof of Esclangon. They have shown that,if a differential equation

$$
L ( y ) \equiv { \frac { d ^ { n } y } { d t ^ { n } } } + A _ { 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } y = \phi ( t )
$$

with constant coefficients and an almost periodic right-hand member admits a bounded integral $f ( t )$ ，then $f ( t )$ is almost periodic (Section 2.11).If it admits in general an almost periodic solution, then there exists always one and only one almost periodic solution with the same Fourier exponents as those of the given function $\phi ( t )$

Let $( r - \kappa _ { 1 } ) ( r - \kappa _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( r - \kappa _ { n } )$ be the factorized product of the polynomial $P ( r ) = r ^ { n } + A _ { 1 } r ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n }$ in linear factors. Symbolically write the operator $\pmb { L } ( y )$ as

$$
( D - \kappa _ { 1 } ) ( D - \kappa _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( D - \kappa _ { n } ) y .
$$

Suppose that $\phi ( t )$ is almost periodic. We show that, if a solution $y =$ $f ( t )$ is bounded,then it is almost periodic.

From the assumption that $y = f ( t )$ is bounded and that the above operator of order $_ n$ applied to $f ( t )$ gives a bounded almost periodic function，it follows,by the repetition of Esclangon's procedure for proving the theorems of Section 14.23, that the operators respectively with $n - 1 , n - 2 , \cdots , 1$ linear factors

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { ( D - \kappa _ { 2 } ) ( D - \kappa _ { 3 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( D - \kappa _ { n } ) , } \\ & { } & { ( D - \kappa _ { 3 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( D - \kappa _ { n } ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
( D - \kappa _ { \mathfrak { n } } )
$$

applied to $y = f ( t )$ give bounded functions.Then the theorem follows from the fact that the almost periodicity of $y$ is deduced from the almost periodicity of $( D - \kappa ) y = d y / d t - \kappa y$ and the boundedness of $y$

It is remarked that in the absence of purely imaginary roots of the characteristic equation there exists always one and only one almost periodic solution if $\phi ( t )$ is almost periodic,and that in this case the strong assumption of almost periodicity can be replaced by the weaker assumption of the existence of translation numbers (Section 2.11).

If $y = f ( t )$ is an almost periodic solution of $L ( y ) = \phi ( t )$ ， then each of the differential operators applied to $y = f ( t )$ gives an almost periodic function. Hence the derivatives $d ^ { n } y / d t ^ { n } , \ldots , d y / d t$ and $y$ are almost periodic.Let (Section 2.11)

$$
\sum B _ { n } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } M _ { n } t \right\}
$$

be the Fourier expansion of an almost periodic function $y = f ( t )$ ; then the expansion of $L ( y ) = L ( f ( t ) )$ is given by

$$
\sum B _ { n } P ( \sqrt { - 1 } M _ { n } ) \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } M _ { n } t \} ,
$$

where $P ( r )$ denotes the polynomial $r ^ { n } + A _ { 1 } r ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n - 1 } r + A _ { n }$ By the uniqueness theorem (Section 2.11)， the expansion of $L ( y )$ formally coincides with the expansion

$$
\sum C _ { n } \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \Lambda _ { n } t \right\}
$$

of the given function $\phi ( t )$ . Hence, if the characteristic equation $P ( r )$ $= 0$ admits no purely imaginary root, that is, if $P ( \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda )$ is different from zero for all real $\gimel$ ，then this almost periodic solution should be expanded in the form

$$
\sum { \frac { C _ { n } } { P ( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \Lambda _ { n } ) } } \exp { \{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \Lambda _ { n } t \} } .
$$

If the characteristic equation admits purely imaginary roots ${ \sqrt { - 1 } } \beta _ { 1 } ,$ $\sqrt { - 1 } \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , \sqrt { - 1 } \beta _ { \nu } , \beta _ { k } \ne \beta _ { l }$ ，then it is necessary for the existence of an almost periodic solution that none of the exponents $\Lambda _ { n }$ in the expansion of $\phi ( t )$ should be equal to one of these numbers $\beta$ ，similarly to the special case treated for $d y / d t = \phi ( t )$ ，where the absence of a constant term in the expansion of $\phi ( t )$ is necessary for the almost periodicity of the integral；but even this condition is not sufficient. If there exists an almost periodic solution $f ( t )$ ，then there exist an infinite number of such solutions and, indeed,a set of functions

$$
f ( t ) + c _ { 1 } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \beta _ { 1 } t \} + \cdots + c _ { \ast } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \beta _ { \ast } t \} ,
$$

where the sum following $f ( t )$ represents the set of almost periodic solutions of the homogeneous equation $L ( y ) = 0$ . If there exists an almost periodic solution of the nonhomogeneous equation, then among the almost periodic solutions there exists one and only one $y = f ( t )$ such that the numbers $\beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \ldots , \beta _ { \nu }$ do not appear among the Fourier exponents $M _ { n }$ . From the formal coincidence of the two expansions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { n } B _ { n } P ( \sqrt { - 1 } M _ { n } ) \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } M _ { n } t \} } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { n } C _ { n } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \} } , } } \end{array}
$$

it follows that the Fourier expansion of such a solution must be

$$
\sum { \frac { C _ { n } } { P ( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ \Lambda _ { n } ) } } \exp { \{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ \Lambda _ { n } t \} } .
$$

Walther (1927) extended this study to a linear difference equation with constant coeffcients with an almost periodic right-hand member

$$
\underset { \omega } { \Delta } F ( t ) \equiv \frac { F ( t + \omega ) - F ( t ) } { \omega } = \phi ( t ) .
$$

In order to have an almost periodic solution the function $\phi ( t )$ should be almost periodic.Walther proved that it is necessary for the almost periodicity of $F ( t )$ that either $\phi ( t )$ should vanish identically or all terms for which $\Lambda _ { n } \omega \equiv 0$ (mod $2 \pi$ ）should be absent from the Fourier expansion of

$$
\phi ( t ) \sim \sum C _ { n } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \} ,
$$

that is, the $\Lambda _ { n } \omega$ should be integral multiples of $2 \pi$ . If there exists an almost periodic solution of (204) for $\phi ( t ) \neq 0$ ， then the Fourier series of one among the almost periodic solutions can be derived by

$$
F ( t ) \sim \sum \frac { C _ { n } \omega } { \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } \omega \right\} - 1 } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \right\}
$$

from the Fourier series (205) for $\phi ( t )$ . If the Fourier exponents $\Lambda _ { n }$ of $\phi ( t )$ are bounded, i.e.， if $| \Lambda _ { n } | < K$ ， then each bounded continuous solution of (204) is almost periodic for $| \omega | < 2 \pi / K$ .If the Fourier exponents $\Lambda _ { n }$ of (205)are linearly independent with $\Lambda _ { n } \omega \neq 0$ ，and

$$
\phi ( t ) \sim \sum C _ { n } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \right\}
$$

with convergent $\sum | C _ { n } |$ ，then the necessary and sufficient condition for the almost periodicity of the continuous solution of (2O4） is that the series

$$
\sum \left| \frac { C _ { \pi } \omega } { \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { \pi } \omega \right\} - 1 } \right| = \sum \left| \frac { C _ { \pi } \omega } { 2 \sin \Lambda _ { \pi } \omega } \right|
$$

should be convergent. Then an almost periodic solution is expressed by

$$
f ( t ) \sim \sum \frac { C _ { n } \omega } { \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } \omega \right\} - 1 } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \right\} .
$$

For the almost periodicity of a uniformly continuous solution $f ( t )$ of the difference equation (204) with almost periodic $\phi ( t )$ it is not only necessary but also sufficient that $f ( t )$ should remain bounded.

Consider a difference equation

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \Delta u ( t ) - a u ( t ) = \phi ( t ) . } \end{array}
$$

For $| 1 + a \omega | \neq 1$ and for an almost periodic $\phi ( t )$ there exists just one bounded almost periodic solution of (206).For $| 1 + a \omega | > 1$ the solution is expressed by

$$
u ( t ) = - \frac { \omega } { 1 + a \omega } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \phi ( t + \nu \omega ) } { ( 1 + a \omega ) ^ { \nu } } ,
$$

and for $| 1 + a \omega | < 1$ by

$$
u ( t ) = \frac { \omega } { 1 + a \omega } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \infty } { ( 1 + a \omega ) ^ { \nu } } { \phi } ( t - \nu \omega ) .
$$

For $| 1 + a \omega | = 1$ and for an almost periodic $\phi ( t )$ each bounded uniformly continuous solution of (206) is almost periodic.

Finally consider a difference equation

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 0 } ^ { m } c _ { \mu } \overset { \mu } { \Delta } u ( t ) = \phi ( t ) ,
$$

where $c _ { \mu }$ are constant and $\overset { \mu } { \Delta }$ denotes the μth difference. Any bounded, uniformly continuous solution of (2O7) with almost periodic $\phi ( t )$ is almost periodic. If $| 1 + a _ { \mu } \omega | \neq 1 ( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )$ for all roots $a _ { \mu }$ of the characteristic equation

$$
g ( z ) = \sum _ { \mu = 0 } ^ { m } c _ { \mu } z ^ { \mu } = z ^ { m } + c _ { m - 1 } z ^ { m - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { 0 } = 0 ,
$$

then the assumption of the uniform continuity can be dropped. Then there exists just one bounded almost periodic solution.If there exists in general an almost periodic solution with almost periodic

$$
\phi ( t ) \sim \sum C _ { n } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ \Lambda _ { n } t \} ,
$$

then the Fourier expansion of an almost periodic particular solution is

$$
u ( t ) \sim \sum _ { } \frac { C _ { n } } { g ( [ \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } \omega \} - 1 ] / \omega ) } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \}
$$

with the same Fourier exponents as those of $\phi ( t )$ ，where

$$
g ( [ \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } \omega \} } - 1 ] / \omega )
$$

is the value of the characteristic function $\begin{array} { r } { g ( z ) = \sum _ { \mu = 0 } ^ { m } c _ { \mu } z ^ { \mu } } \end{array}$ of the difference equation for $z = [ \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } \omega \} } - 1 ] / \omega$ If all $\displaystyle | 1 + a _ { \mu } \omega |$ $\neq 1$ ， then the Fourier expansion (2O8） represents only the solution that is almost periodic.

As has been shown by Bochner (1927),a single-valued and continuous complex function $F ( f ( t ) )$ of an almost periodic function $f ( t )$ of a real argument $t$ is also almost periodic.Walther (1933) extended it to many-valued functions $F ( f ( t ) )$ .For $F ( z ) = \log z $ the periodicity is destroyed. For $F ( z ) = z ^ { \lambda }$ ， $\lambda \neq 0$ ，the periodicity is conserved.If $F ( z ) = { \sqrt { z } } .$ $z = a + \cos t$ with real $^ a$ ， then the period is $2 \pi$ for $| a | > 1$ ， and is $4 \pi$ for $a = \pm 1$ with the assumption of analyticity,and the analyticity is destroyed for $| a | < 1$

Let an algebraic function $w ( t )$ be defined by

$$
w ^ { n } + f _ { n - 1 } ( t ) w ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { 1 } ( t ) w + f _ { 0 } ( t ) = 0 ,
$$

with almost periodic $f _ { i }$ of a real $t$ of common translation numbers, and $D ( a _ { 0 } , a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n - 1 } )$ be the discriminant of the irreducible equation

$$
w ^ { n } + a _ { n - 1 } w ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { 1 } w + a _ { 0 } = 0 ,
$$

then the requirement

$$
| D ( f _ { 0 } ( t ) , f _ { 1 } ( t ) , \dots , f _ { n - 1 } ( t ) ) | = | D ( t ) | \ge k > 0
$$

excludes multiple roots.Walther showed that, if the discriminant (210) for $- \infty < t < \infty$ possesses a nonzero lower bound in absolute value, then the algebraic function $w ( t )$ defined by (209） with almost periodic coefficients is also almost periodic with translation number which is in general $\left( n ! \right)$ -times the common translation number of the coefficients.Bohr and Flanders (1937) studied the same problem by basing on substitution groups. Cameron (1935) sharpened Walther's result by dealing with the general question of implicitly given almost periodic functions.

Seifert (1971) considered the system

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = A x + b ( t ) ,
$$

where $b ( t )$ is almost periodic with

$$
b ( t ) = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } c _ { k } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { k } t \right\}
$$

and $\{ \mu _ { k } \} ( k = 1 , \ldots , n )$ is the set of eigenvalues of the matrix $\pmb { A }$ .He showed that if there exists a constant $\eta > 0$ such that $| \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { k } - \mu _ { k } |$

$\geq \eta$ for $k = 1 , 2 , \ldots$ , then the given system admits an almost periodic solution.

Seifert further discussed by the averaging method (Section 13.1) a system of perturbed differential equations

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = [ A + \varepsilon C ( t ) ] x + \varepsilon g ( x , \varepsilon ) + \varepsilon b ( t ) ,
$$

where $x , g ( x , \varepsilon )$ and $b ( t )$ are in $R ^ { n }$ ，a set of $_ n$ -dimensional real numbers, $\varepsilon > 0$ ， $\pmb { A }$ is a real $n \times n$ matrix similar to a diagonal matrix with purely imaginary entries,the elements of the $n \times n$ matrix $C ( t )$ and $b ( t )$ are almost periodic,and $g$ and its first partial derivatives with respect to $x$ are continuous on $R ^ { n } \times [ 0 , \infty )$ . Let

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C _ { 0 } = M \left[ \exp \left\{ - t A \right\} \cdot C ( t ) \cdot \exp \left\{ t A \right\} \right] , } } \\ { { g _ { 0 } = M \left[ \exp \left\{ - t A \right\} \cdot b ( t ) \right] , } } \\ { { g _ { 0 } ( y ) = M \left[ \exp \left\{ - t A \right\} \cdot g \left( \exp \left\{ t A \right\} \cdot y , 0 \right) \right] , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
M ( f ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \tau \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \tau } f ( t ) \ d t .
$$

Suppose that

$$
C _ { \ L _ { 0 } } y + g _ { \ L _ { 0 } } ( y ) + b _ { \ L _ { 0 } } = 0
$$

admits a solution $\bar { y }$ such that the eigenvalues of $C _ { \mathfrak { o } } + \partial g _ { \mathfrak { o } } ( \bar { y } ) / \partial \bar { y }$ all have nonzero real parts. Then Seifert proved that there exists an $\varepsilon _ { \boldsymbol { 0 } } > 0$ such that for each $\varepsilon$ ， $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq \varepsilon _ { 0 }$ ,the above system of differential equations admits an almost periodic solution $x ( t , \varepsilon )$ such that $| x ( t , \varepsilon ) - \exp \{ t A \} \cdot { \bar { y } } |  0$ as $\varepsilon \to 0$ uniformly for $t$ in $\pmb R$ . The theorem can be extended to the second-order system

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = x _ { 2 } , \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = - x _ { 1 } + \varepsilon \nu x _ { 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { m } g ( x _ { 1 } ) + p ( t ) ,
$$

where

$$
g ( x ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { j } x ^ { j } , \qquad \nu a _ { m } < 0 , \qquad m = 2 k + 1 ,
$$

$k$ is a positive integer and $p$ is almost periodic and of the form

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \bigl ( } A _ { j } \cos { \lambda _ { j } t } + B _ { j } \sin { \lambda _ { j } t } { \bigr ) } , \qquad \lambda _ { 1 } = 1 .
$$

# 14.25NORMAL FUNCTIONS

Favard (1926,1933) based his theory of linear differential equations with almost periodic coefficients on Bochner's normal functions (Bochner, 1927),and simplified Bohl's and Esclangon's procedure.

If $f ( t )$ of a real variable $t$ defined in $- \infty < t < + \infty$ is almost periodic (Section 2.11), then the function $f ( t + h )$ with an arbitrary real number $h$ is also almost periodic and represented by the Fourier expansion

$$
f ( t + h ) \sim \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } A _ { n } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } h \right\} \cdot \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \right\}
$$

The closed set, Huille or hull, $H \{ f ( t + h ) \}$ is a set of functions $f ( t + h )$ containing the limit function $g ( t )$ when the convergence to the limit is uniform in $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ,i.e.,if $g ( t )$ is the limit of the sequence $f ( t + h _ { n } )$ $( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ ，then a number $N$ can be determined such that for any given $\varepsilon > 0$ and for $n > N$ we have $| g ( t ) - f ( t + h _ { n } ) | \leqq \varepsilon .$ $- \infty < t < + \infty$ .The function $g ( t )$ is also almost periodic.If $g ( t )$ is any function of the closed set $H \{ f ( t + h ) \}$ and is almost periodic, then the set $H \{ g ( t + h ) \}$ is identical with the set $H \{ f ( t + h ) \}$ .Hence, in order that an almost periodic function $f ^ { \ast } ( t )$ belongs to a closed set $H \{ f ( t + h ) \}$ it is necessary and sufficient that it has the expansion

$$
f ^ { * } ( t ) \sim \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } A _ { n } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } h _ { n } ^ { * } \} \cdot \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \} ,
$$

$$
\Lambda _ { \pi } h _ { n } ^ { * } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { m  \infty } \Lambda _ { \pi } h _ { m } \qquad ( \mathrm { m o d } \ : 2 \pi ) .
$$

Then $f ^ { * } ( t )$ is the limit of the sequence $f ( t + h _ { m } )$ as $m \to \infty$ ·

An infinite sequence of functions

$$
f ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , f ( t + h _ { n } ) , \ldots .
$$

corresponds to a sequence of points $H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , \dots , H _ { m } , \dots .$ in a space of infinite dimensions,in which two points with coordinates difering by multiples of $2 \pi$ are considered to be identical. According to the pointset theory this sequence of points admits at least a limit point, and at least a sequence which admits a single limit point can be extracted from this sequence of points.Hence，if $f ( t )$ is an almost periodic function, then from the sequence $f ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \dots , f ( t + h _ { n } )$ ， ... another sequence can be extracted which converges uniformly to a limit function, which is equally almost periodic in $- \infty < t < + \infty$

A function which is defined and continuous in $- \infty < t < + \infty$ and is such that, from any sequence $f ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \dots , f ( t + h _ { n } )$ ， ...，where $h _ { i }$ are arbitrary real numbers,we can extract another sequence $f ( t + k _ { 1 } )$ ， $f ( t + k _ { 2 } )$ ，., $f ( t + k _ { n } )$ ，... which converges uniformly in $- \infty < t < + \infty$ to a limit function, is called a normal function． This idea is originated from that of Montel's on normal families of functions (Montel, 1927).

Theorem $^ { \small t }$ :Any almost periodic function is a normal function. Conversely,any normal function is almost periodic (Bochner,1927).

Since the sum or the product of normal functions is also almost periodic,we can show immediately that the sum or the product of almost periodic functions is also almost periodic.Thus the normal functions are equivalent to the almost periodic functions (Bochner, 1927).

A set of numbers is called reduced if there is no linear relation

$$
\mathrm { { } } _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { n } \alpha _ { n } = 0 \qquad ( | r _ { 1 } | + | r _ { 2 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | r _ { n } | > 0 )
$$

with rational coefficients $r _ { \nu }$ .The basis (Section 2.13) of a set of numbers $\boldsymbol { Z } = ( \zeta _ { 1 } , \zeta _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ is a reduced set of numbers $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ ，by means of which any number $\zeta$ can be represented in the form $r _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } +$ $r _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } + \cdots + r _ { n } \alpha _ { n }$ . A modulus $M$ is a set of numbers,which with $_ { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ of the set contains also the difference $\alpha - \beta$ in the set (Section 2.11).By putting $\beta = \alpha$ we see that any modulus contains the number zero,and consequently the number $- \beta = 0 - \beta$ and the number $\alpha + \beta = \alpha - ( - \beta )$ . Hence if a modulus contains two numbers,then it contains their sum and their difference in the modulus.The set $M ( z )$ is the smallest modulus which contains any set $Z$ of numbers. Hence the sum-modulus (Vereinigungsmodul) $M ( M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ is the smallest modulus containing all moduli $M _ { \nu }$ . If all linear combinations with integer coefficients $g _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } + g _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } + \ldots + g _ { n } \alpha _ { n }$ are constructed, then an integral modulus $G ( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ with integral basis $A = ( \alpha _ { 1 } ,$ $\alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots . )$ is defined (Bohr,1925,1926；Bochner,1927).

Theorem 2: Given two almost periodic functions $f ( t )$ and $g ( t )$ ， consider two sequences

$$
f ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , f ( t + h _ { n } ) , \ldots ,
$$

$$
g ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , g ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , g ( t + h _ { n } ) , \ldots .
$$

For the uniform convergence of the sequence (A） to follow from the uniform convergence of the sequence (B) and vice versa, it is neces-sary and suffcient that these functions should be of the same modulus $M _ { f } = M _ { g }$

Theorem 3: If the integral of an almost periodic function is bounded,then it is also almost periodic.

This theorem has been already proved in Section 2.11 according to Bohr (1924).Bohr extended the proof of Bohl (Section 2.10） on quasi-periodic functions to almost periodic functions,although both functions were independently discovered.Favard's proof (1926） is based on the notion of normal functions. Suppose that

$$
{ \frac { d x } { d t } } = { \dot { f } } ( t )
$$

admits only bounded solutions.Let one of such solutions be $F ( t )$ ， then other solutions are $F ( t ) + C$ ， $C$ being a constant. $F ( t )$ is determined if $\pmb { F } ( 0 )$ for $t = 0$ is given. Let $g \leqq F ( t ) \leq G$ .Consider

$$
{ \frac { d x } { d t } } = f ^ { * } ( t )
$$

with an arbitrary function $f ^ { * } ( t )$ out of the set $H \{ f ( t + h ) \}$ . Let a solution of this last equation be ${ \pmb F } ^ { * } ( t )$ which has the same bounds as that of $F ( t )$ . Let $f ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , f ( t + h _ { n } ) , \ldots .$ be a sequence which tends to $f ^ { * } ( t )$ . The equation

$$
{ \frac { d x } { d t } } = f ( t + h _ { n } )
$$

admits one and only one solution with the bounds $g$ and $G$ ，and this is the one which takes the value $F ( h _ { n } )$ for $t = 0$ . Since $F ( h _ { n } )$ is bounded, it has at least a limit.Extract another sequence $f ( t + k _ { 1 } )$ ， $f ( t + k _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , f ( t + k _ { n } ) , \ldots ,$ which converges also to $f ^ { \ast } ( t )$ ， such that the sequence $F ( k _ { 1 } ) , F ( k _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , F ( k _ { n } ) .$ ，...has a limit $F ^ { * }$ . Consider the integral $F ^ { * } ( t )$ of $f ^ { \ast } ( t )$ which takes the value $F ^ { * } = F ^ { * } ( 0 )$ for $t = 0$ This is the function we require. In fact,with arbitrary positive numbers $_ \alpha$ and $\beta$ we can find an integer $N$ such that

$$
| F ( k _ { n } ) - F ^ { * } | \leq \alpha , \qquad | f ( t + k _ { n } ) - f ^ { * } ( t ) | \leq \beta
$$

for $n \geq N$ and consequently

$$
\frac { d [ F ( t + k _ { n } ) - F ^ { * } ( t ) ] } { d t } = f ( t + k _ { n } ) - f ^ { * } ( t ) ,
$$

$$
| F ( t + k _ { n } ) - F ^ { * } ( t ) | \leq \alpha + \beta | t | .
$$

If we want to make the difference smaller than a preassigned number $\varepsilon$ in the interval $| t | \leq T$ ，where $_ T$ is arbitrarily large,then it is sufficient to put $\alpha < \varepsilon / 2 , \ \beta = \varepsilon / ( 2 T )$ . Hence $F ( t + k _ { n } )$ converges to ${ \pmb F } ^ { * } ( t )$ uniformly in a finite interval.

The normality of the function $F ( t )$ ，i.e.，that the convergence of $F ( t + k _ { n } )$ to ${ \cal F } ^ { * } ( t )$ is uniform in the infinite interval $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ， can be proved by considering that $F ( t )$ is a normal function.The proof was carried out by reductio ad absurdum by Favard (1926). Hence,if the sequence $f ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , f ( t + h _ { n } ) , \ldots .$ converges uniformly to a limit, then the same is true for the sequence $F ( t + h _ { 1 } ) , F ( t + h _ { 2 } ) , \ldots , F ( t + h _ { n } ) ,$ ...,and vice versa. The functions $f$ and $F$ are of the same modulus.Further, the Fourier expansion of the integral is obtained by formal integration of $f$ so that

$$
f ( t ) \sim \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } A _ { n } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \} } ,
$$

$$
F ( t ) \sim c + \ \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { A _ { n } } { \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } } } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \right\} .
$$

For the boundedness of $f ( t )$ it is necessary that its expansion does not contain the constant term $\scriptstyle A _ { 0 }$ ，that is,the mean value is zero:

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ( t ) d t = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \left[ F ( t ) - F ( 0 ) \right] = A _ { 0 } ,
$$

and generally

$$
F ( t ) = A _ { 0 } t + O ( | t | ) .
$$

The condition $A _ { 0 } = 0$ is not sufficient. Favard (1926) considered

$$
\int _ { \rho } ^ { \tau } \frac { f ( t ) } { t } d t \qquad ( 0 < \rho < T ) ,
$$

and proved the existence of an almost periodic function with zero mean value such that the integral does not remain bounded when $\pmb { T }$ increases indefinitely. As to a function of zero mean value whose integral is not bounded，we see by extending Bohl's theorem on quasiperiodic functions (Section 14.18) that, if an almost periodic function $f ( t )$ with zero mean value has a non-bounded integral, then there exist in the set $H \{ f ( t + h ) \}$ at least two functions $f ^ { + }$ and $f ^ { - }$ such that

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ^ { + } ( t ) d t \geq 0 , \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } f ^ { - } ( t ) d t \leq 0 \qquad ( - \infty < t < + \infty ) .
$$

As is known by a simple generalization of Bohl's theorem on quasiperiodic functions，the integral of an almost periodic function is almost periodic if the integral is bounded (Sections 2.12 and 14.1; Bohr,1932). Bochner (1933,1933a) obtained a supplementary condition for the almost periodicity of the integral

$$
y ( x ) = \exp \left\{ \int ^ { x } f ( x ) d x \right\}
$$

of an almost periodic function $f ( x )$ . At first he stated the

Lemma: If $z = F ( x ) = \int ^ { x } f ( x ) d x$ is almost periodic and if $\Phi ( z )$ is a uniformly continuous function in a bounded $z$ -region, then

$$
y ( x ) = \Phi \{ F ( x ) \}
$$

is almost periodic and the modulus of $y ( x )$ is contained in the modulus of $F ( x )$

If the constant term of the Fourier series for $f ( t )$ is not zero, then $F ( x )$ cannot be almost periodic.Put

$$
a _ { 0 } = M \{ f ( x ) \} = 0 .
$$

Generally $a _ { 0 }$ is purely imaginary $a _ { 0 } = { \sqrt { - 1 } } c$ Let

$$
G ( x ) = \int ^ { x } \left\{ f ( x ) - { \sqrt { - 1 } } c \right\} d x
$$

be bounded and almost periodic. Then

$$
y ( x ) = \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } c x + G ( x ) \} }
$$

is almost periodic. The condition of boundedness is not only necessary but sufficient for almost periodicity. Suppose that $c = 0$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is real,then the almost periodicity of $y ( x )$ contains the boundedness of $F ( x )$

The function $y ( x )$ is defined by

$$
y ^ { \prime } ( x ) = f ( x ) y ( x ) ,
$$

or

$$
y ( x ) - y ( 0 ) = \int ^ { x } f ( x ) y ( x ) d x .
$$

It is sufficient to prove that

$$
y ( x ) \geq k > 0 .
$$

Bochner proved the following theorem by means of the properties of his normal functions.

Theorem 4: For the almost periodicity of

$$
y ( x ) = \exp \left\{ \int ^ { x } f ( x ) d x \right\} ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is almost periodic,it is necessary and sufficient that $f ( x )$ should be of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { f ( x ) = \sqrt { - 1 } c + g ( x ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $^ c$ is a constant and

$$
\int ^ { x } g ( x ) d x
$$

is bounded. The modulus of

$$
G ( x ) = \int ^ { x } \left\{ f ( x ) - { \sqrt { - 1 } } c \right\} d x ,
$$

so that

$$
y ( x ) = \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } c x + G ( x ) \} } ,
$$

is equal to the modulus of $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ . The modulus of $y ( x )$ is the smallest of the moduli which contain the moduli of $^ { c }$ and of $f ( x )$

Let a system of linear diferential equations

$$
( S _ { t } ) \colon \ { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } = f _ { i 1 } ( t ) x _ { 1 } + f _ { i 2 } ( t ) x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { i n } ( t ) x _ { n } + g _ { i } ( t )
$$

$$
( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

be given, where $f _ { i k } ( t )$ and $g _ { i } ( t )$ are real continuous functions defined for all real $t$ .Consider a sequence of the systems

$$
( S _ { t } ^ { ( p ) } ) \colon \ { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } = f _ { i 1 } ^ { ( p ) } ( t ) x _ { 1 } + f _ { i 2 } ^ { ( p ) } ( t ) x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { i n } ^ { ( p ) } ( t ) x _ { n } + g _ { i } ^ { ( p ) } ( t )
$$

$$
( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ; p = 1 , 2 , \ldots ) ,
$$

where $f _ { i k } ^ { ( p ) } ( t )$ and $g _ { i } ^ { ( { \pmb { p } } ) } ( t )$ tend respectively to $f _ { i \kappa } ( t )$ and $g _ { i } ( t )$ uniformly in any finite interval as $p \to \infty$ , and a sequence of the solutions of this system whose initial values $x _ { i } ^ { ( p ) } ( 0 )$ tend to the limit $x _ { i } ( 0 )$ for $p \to \infty$ . These solutions $x _ { i } ^ { ( p ) } ( t )$ tend uniformly to the solution $x _ { i } ( t )$ of $( S _ { t } )$ in the interval of $t$ of an arbitrarily large length.

Suppose that $f$ and $g$ are almost periodic.Together with $( S _ { t } )$ we consider

$$
( S _ { t } ^ { * } ) \colon { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } = f _ { i 1 } ^ { * } ( t ) x _ { 1 } + f _ { i 2 } ^ { * } ( t ) x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { i n } ^ { * } ( t ) x _ { n } + g _ { i } ^ { * } ( t )
$$

with

$$
f _ { i k } ^ { * } ( t ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } f _ { i k } ( t + h _ { \nu } ) , \qquad g _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } g _ { i } ( t + h _ { \nu } ) .
$$

We say that the system $( S _ { t + h _ { y } } )$ converges to $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ ， and write $( S _ { t } ^ { * } ) =$ $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } ( S _ { t + h _ { \nu } } )$ ，and denote the set by $H ( S _ { t + h _ { \nu } } )$ . The set of the corresponding homogeneous systems

$$
( \Sigma _ { t } ) \colon { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } = f _ { i 1 } ( t ) x _ { 1 } + f _ { i 2 } ( t ) x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { i n } ( t ) x _ { n } ,
$$

$$
( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

is denoted by $H ( \Sigma _ { t + h } )$ . If

$$
\begin{array} { r } { | f _ { i k } ^ { * } ( t ) - f _ { i k } ^ { * * } ( t ) | \leq \varepsilon , ~ | g _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) - g _ { i } ^ { * * } ( t ) | \leq \varepsilon ~ } \\ { ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ; k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) , ~ } \end{array}
$$

for $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ， then we say that the difference of the two systems $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ and $( S _ { t } ^ { * * } )$ of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ is smaller than $\varepsilon > 0$ ， and write $| ( S _ { t } ^ { * } ) - ( S _ { t } ^ { * * } ) | \leq \varepsilon$ . If we consider two solutions $x _ { i } ^ { * } ( t )$ and $x _ { i } ^ { * * } ( t )$ of each of these two systems whose initial values satisfy $| x _ { i } ^ { * } ( 0 ) - x _ { i } ^ { * * } ( 0 ) |$ $\leq \alpha$ ，then we can determine $\omega = \omega ( \alpha , \varepsilon ) > 0$ and $T > 0$ such that $| x _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) - x _ { i } ^ { * * } ( t ) | \leq \omega ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ for $- T \leq t \leq T$ . Conversely, if $\omega$ is given an arbitrarily small value and $_ { T }$ an arbitrarily large value, then we can determine $_ { \alpha }$ and $\varepsilon$ which are independent of $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ and $( S _ { t } ^ { * * } )$ but dependent only on the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ . The modulus of the system $( S _ { t } )$ is the smallest modulus containing the moduli of $f _ { i k } ( t )$ and $g _ { i } ( t )$ . If a solution $x _ { 1 } ( t ) , x _ { 2 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t )$ of the system is composed of almost periodic functions, then the smallest modulus containing those of $x _ { i } ( t )$ is the modulus of the solution,and this system is almost periodic.It can be seen immediately that, if a system of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ admits an almost periodic solution, then the same is true of any system of the set.

Theorem 1: If the nonhomogeneous system $( S _ { t } )$ admits a bounded solution,and if none of the systems of the set $H ( \Sigma _ { t + h } )$ admits a bounded solution, except the trivial solution $x _ { i } = 0$ ， then the bounded solution of $( S _ { t } )$ is composed of almost periodic functions，and any other system of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ admits also an almost periodic solution.

For the proof we suppose that the bounded solution $x _ { i } = u _ { i } ( t )$ is composed of normal functions. Let the bounded solution of the system $( S _ { t } )$ be defined by the initial values $u _ { 1 } ( 0 ) , u _ { 2 } ( 0 ) , \ldots , u _ { n } ( 0 )$ .By the hypothesis made on the solution of each system of the set $H ( \Sigma _ { t + \hbar } )$ each system of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ admits one bounded solution.In fact, if $\begin{array} { r } { ( S _ { t } ^ { * } ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } \left( S _ { t + h _ { p } } \right) } \end{array}$ ， then we can suppose that $u _ { i } ( h _ { \nu } )$ tend to the limit $\begin{array} { r } { u ^ { * } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } u _ { i } ( h _ { \nu } ) \left( i = 1 , 2 , \dots , n \right) } \end{array}$ ， and the solution $[ u _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) ]$ of $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ which takes the value $[ u _ { i } ^ { * } ]$ for $t = 0$ is bounded and the conver-gence of $[ u _ { i } ( t + h _ { \nu } ) ]$ to $[ u _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) ]$ is uniform in any arbitrarily large interval.It is sufficient to prove that the convergence is uniform in $- \infty < t < + \infty$ . In order to see it we assume that it were not so, then we should be able to determine $\alpha > 0$ and a sequence of numbers indefinitely increasing in absolute value with the indices $t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ and two sequences of indices $\nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } , \dots .$ and $\pi _ { 1 } , \pi _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ and a function $u _ { j } ( t )$ out of $_ n$ functions $u _ { 1 } ( t ) , u _ { 2 } ( t ) , \ldots , u _ { n } ( t )$ such that $| u _ { j } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \nu ( p ) } ) -$ $u _ { j } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \pi ( p ) } ) | \geq \alpha ( p = 1 , 2 , . . . )$ . But from the sequence of numbers $[ u _ { j } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \nu ( p ) } ) ]$ and $[ u _ { j } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \pi ( p ) } ) ]$ and from the sequence of the systems $\left( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { y ( p ) } } \right)$ and $\left( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { \pi ( p ) } } \right)$ we can extract at least another sequence such that these numbers and these systems tend to the limits $[ u _ { i } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ]$ and $[ u _ { i } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ] , \left( S ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \right)$ and $( S ^ { ( 2 ) } )$ respectively. For avoiding the complicacy of notation we suppose that these sequences are the above sequences with such properties. The system of numbers $[ u _ { i } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } ]$ and $[ u _ { i } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ]$ are then the initial values of the bounded solutions of the systems $( S ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } )$ and $( S ^ { ( 2 ) } )$ respectively. We shal show that these two systems are identical.

However small a positive number $\varepsilon$ may be taken,we can determine an index $P$ such that for $p > P$ we have $| ( S _ { t + h _ { y ( p ) } } ) - ( S _ { t + h _ { \pi ( p ) } } ) | \leq \varepsilon / 3 ,$ because the systems $( S _ { t + h _ { y } } )$ tend to $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ .Hence we obtain also

$$
| ( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { \nu ( \mathcal { P } ) } } ) - ( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { \pi ( p ) } } ) | \mathop { \le } \frac { \varepsilon } { 3 } ,
$$

and then

$$
| ( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { \nu ( p ) } } ) - ( S ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } ) | \le \frac { \varepsilon } { 3 } , \qquad | ( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { \pi ( p ) } } ) - ( S ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 2 ) } ) | \le \frac { \varepsilon } { 3 } .
$$

Hence

$$
| ( S ^ { ( 1 ) } ) - ( S ^ { ( 2 ) } ) | \leq \varepsilon .
$$

Since ε is arbitrarily small,we should have $( S ^ { ( 1 ) } ) = ( S ^ { ( 2 ) } )$

Now the system has only one bounded solution by hypothesis.We have found two systems with different initial values for the system and for the solution, because we should have $| u _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } - u _ { j } ^ { ( 2 ) } | \geq \alpha$ .Thus we come to contradiction.Hence the convergence should be uniform and the theorem is established.

We obtain,by reasoning similar to that for showing the theorem on moduli of Section 14.25, that the modulus of the almost periodic solution of $( S _ { t } )$ is at most equal to the modulus of the system.

Corollary: The equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } + f _ { 1 } ( t ) \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } x } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } + \ \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { n } ( t ) x = g ( t ) ,
$$

where $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ and $g$ are almost periodic, is equivalent to a linear system such as just studied.Hence, if the equation admits a solution, which is bounded together with its derivatives up to the order $_ n$ ，and if none of the equations

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } } + f _ { 1 } ^ { * } ( t ) { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } x } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { n } ^ { * } ( t ) x = 0 ,
$$

$$
f _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu  \infty } f _ { i } ( t + h _ { \nu } ) ,
$$

admits any bounded solution,then the bounded solution of the nonhomogeneous system is almost periodic,together with its derivatives up to the order $_ n$

A linear differential equation with constant coefficients studied by Esclangon (Section 14.22) and Bohr and Neugebauer (Section 14.23) is a special case of this type, i.e.,

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } } + a _ { 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } x } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { n } x = { \phi } ( t ) ,
$$

where $\phi ( t )$ is almost periodic and $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ are constants. Suppose that the characteristic equation

$$
P ( r ) = r ^ { n } + a _ { 1 } r ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { n } = 0
$$

does not admit purely imaginary root.If the equation admits a bounded solution, then this solution is almost periodic.

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \phi ( t ) \sim \displaystyle \sum _ { n } A _ { n } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \} } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle x ( t ) \sim \displaystyle \sum _ { n } B _ { n } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } M _ { n } t \} } . } } \end{array}
$$

The theorem shows also that the derivatives of $x ( t )$ up to the order $n - 1$ are almost periodic ;consequently the same is true for the derivatives of order $_ n$ ，as the sum of almost periodic functions.The expansion is obtained by formal differentiation so that

$$
\sum _ { n } B _ { n } P ( { \sqrt { - 1 } } M _ { n } ) \exp \{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } M _ { n } t \} = \sum _ { n } A _ { n } \exp \{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \Lambda _ { n } t \} .
$$

Thus the expansion of $x ( t )$ does not contain exponents other than those of $\phi ( t )$ , but contains all exponents of $\phi ( t )$ ，since $P ( r )$ admits no

purely imaginary root. Hence

$$
x ( t ) \sim \ \sum _ { n } \frac { A _ { n } } { P ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ \Lambda _ { n } ) } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ \Lambda _ { n } t \} } .
$$

Theorem 2: The square root of $x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( t )$ is called the absolute value of a solution $[ x _ { i } ( t ) ]$ at the point $t$ If none of the system of the set $H ( \Sigma _ { t + h } )$ admits any such solution whose absolute value can become arbitrarily small, except the trivial solution $x _ { i } = 0$ ，and if $( S _ { t } )$ admits bounded solutions, then at least one of the bounded solutions is almost periodic,and this is true also for any other system of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ ·

Suppose that a system $( S _ { t } )$ admits a solution $[ x _ { i } ( t ) ]$ whose absolute value is smaller than or at most equal to $k > 0$ , in other words,

$$
{ \underset { - \infty < t < + \infty } { \operatorname { b o u n d } } } \ \operatorname* { s u p } _ { \mathbb { X } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) } \ { \sqrt { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \cdots + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( t ) } } = k .
$$

By reasoning similar to the above we can show that any other system of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ possesses a solution whose absolute value does not exceed $k$ . The number $k$ ，for which a solution of $( S _ { t } )$ exists with the absolute value superiorly bounded by $k$ ，is inferiorly bounded by $g \leq k$ . It can be shown that $g$ belongs also to the set $k$ . In fact, otherwise，we could determine a sequence of solutions $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( p ) } ( t ) ]$ ，whose superior bound $k _ { p }$ of the absolute values tends to $g$ as $p \to \infty$ .Then the system of initial values $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( p ) } ( 0 ) ]$ would have at least a limit system, and from the sequence of initial values we could extract another sequence which tends to a limit $[ \xi _ { i } ( 0 ) ]$ . The solution $[ \xi _ { i } ( t ) ]$ of the system $( S _ { t } )$ which takes the initial values $[ \xi _ { i } ( 0 ) ]$ for $t = 0$ is such that

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { b o u n d \ s u p \sqrt { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \xi _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \dots + \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( t ) } } = g , } \end{array}
$$

because, if we suppose that

$$
\sqrt { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \xi _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \dots + \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( t ) } = g + j \qquad ( j > 0 ) ,
$$

then, since the convergence of the sequence $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( p ) } ( t ) ]$ is uniform in any finite interval, we should be able to find an index $P$ such that, for $p > P$ ，we should have

$$
\sqrt { \chi _ { 1 } ^ { ( p ) 2 } ( t ) + \chi _ { 2 } ^ { ( p ) 2 } ( t ) + \cdots + \chi _ { n } ^ { ( p ) 2 } ( t ) } < g + \frac { j } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\sqrt { [ \xi _ { 1 } ( t ) - x _ { 1 } ^ { ( p ) } ( t ) ] ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { n } ( t ) - x _ { n } ^ { ( p ) } ( t ) ] ^ { 2 } } < \frac { j } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
p > P , \qquad | t | \leq T ,
$$

from which we should obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \sqrt { \dot { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( T ) + \dot { \xi } _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ( T ) + \cdots } \leq \sqrt { x _ { 1 } ^ { { ( p ) } 2 } ( T ) + \cdots } } \\ & { \qquad + \ \sqrt { [ \xi _ { 1 } ( T ) - x _ { 1 } ^ { { ( p ) } } ( T ) ] ^ { 2 } + \cdots } < g + j . } \end{array}
$$

This is contradictory to the hypothesis.Hence $g$ belongs to the set $k$ It can be shown that any other system of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ admits also a solution whose superior bound of the absolute values is exactly $g$ ， since $g$ is the inferior bound of absolute values of allsolutions of $( S _ { t } )$ Thus to each system of the set $H ( S _ { t + h } )$ there corresponds a solution whose superior bound of absolute values is exactly $g$

We show now that there is only one such solution. Suppose that $( S _ { t } )$ possessed two such solutins $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) ]$ and $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( t ) ]$ .Then $[ ( \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) + \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( t ) ) / 2 ]$ would also be a solution of $( S _ { t } )$ ，while $[ ( \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) - \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( t ) ) / 2 ]$ is a solution of $( \Sigma _ { t } )$ . We should have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left( \frac { \hat { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \hat { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \dots + \left( \frac { \hat { \xi } _ { n } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \hat { \xi } _ { n } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad + \left( \frac { \hat { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } - \hat { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \dots + \left( \frac { \hat { \xi } _ { n } ^ { ( 1 ) } - \hat { \xi } _ { n } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad = \frac { ( \hat { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } ) ^ { 2 } + ( \hat { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } ) ^ { 2 } + \dots + ( \hat { \xi } _ { n } ^ { ( 1 ) } ) ^ { 2 } + ( \hat { \xi } _ { n } ^ { ( 2 ) } ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \leq g ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

By hypothesis $( \Sigma _ { t } )$ admits no solution whose absolute value can become arbitrarily small. Hence we can find a number $\iota$ such that

$$
\Big ( \frac { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } - \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { 2 } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \geq l ^ { 2 } , ~ \Big ( \frac { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { 2 } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \leq g ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } .
$$

This is contradictory to the property of $g$

Let $[ \xi _ { i } ( t + h _ { p } ) ]$ be a sequence of solutions such that the system of numbers $[ \xi _ { i } ( h _ { p } ) ]$ tends to a limit $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { * } ( 0 ) ]$ ， while the differential systems $\langle S _ { t + h _ { p } } \rangle$ tend to $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ . We can extract from a given sequence a subsequence having this property. The solution of $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ whose superior bound is $g$ has the initial values $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { * } ( 0 ) ]$ . If the sequence of the system $( S _ { t + h _ { p } } )$ tends to $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ ， then certainly the system of numbers $[ \xi _ { i } ( h _ { p } ) ]$ has a single limit. The convergence of $[ \xi _ { i } ( t + h _ { p } ) ]$ to $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) ]$ is uniform in any finite interval.It must be shown that it is uniform also in the infinite interval $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ·

Suppose that the convergence were not uniform； then we could find (i) a positive number $\alpha$ ， (ii) a sequence of numbers infinitely increasing with the indices $t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ，and (ii) two sequences with indices $\nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } , \dots$ and $\pi _ { 1 } , \pi _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ， such that the systems $\left( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { y ( p ) } } \right)$ and $\left( S _ { t + t _ { p } + h _ { \pi ( p ) } } \right)$ converge to two other systems $( S ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } )$ and $( S ^ { ( 2 ) } )$ ，and that the systems of numbers $[ \xi _ { i } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \nu ( p ) } ) ]$ and $[ \xi _ { i } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \pi ( p ) } ) ]$ converge to the systems $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( 0 ) ]$ and $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( 0 ) ]$ ,respectively, and also (iv) a function $\xi _ { j } ( t )$ from among the $_ n$ functions $\pmb { \xi } _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , \pmb { \xi } _ { n } ( t )$ ， such that

$$
| \xi _ { j } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \nu ( p ) } ) - \xi _ { j } ( t _ { p } + h _ { \ast ( p ) } ) | \equiv \alpha \qquad ( p = 1 , 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

Similarly to the proof of Theorem 1 we can show that the systems $( S ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } )$ and $( S ^ { ( 2 ) } )$ would be the same and that $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( 0 ) ]$ and $[ \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( 0 ) ]$ would be the same,contrary to the construction.

Thus from the convergence of a sequence of systems $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } ( S _ { t + h _ { \mathcal { P } } } )$ $\mathbf { \xi } = ( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ follows the convergence of the sequence of solutions $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } \left[ \xi _ { i } ( t + h _ { \nu } ) \right] = \left[ \xi _ { i } ^ { * } ( t ) \right] } \end{array}$ in $- \infty < t < { + \infty } ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ Hence the modulus of the solution $[ \xi _ { i } ( t ) ]$ is inferior or equal to the modulus of the system $( S _ { t } )$ .If $[ x _ { i } ( t ) ]$ is another solution of the system, then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { b o u n d ~ s u p } \left[ \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \cdots + \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( t ) \right] } \\ & { \quad - \infty < t < + \infty } \\ & { \qquad < \mathrm { b o u n d ~ s u p } \left[ x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ( t ) + \cdots + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( t ) \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Thus we say that the smallest system of $( S _ { t } )$ is almost periodic.

Suppose that $( \Sigma _ { t } )$ possesses bounded solutions only and that

$$
F ( t ) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } [ f _ { 1 1 } ( t ) + f _ { 2 2 } ( t ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { n n } ( t ) ] d t
$$

is bounded and hence almost periodic； then any system of the set $H ( \Sigma _ { t + h } )$ possesses only bounded solutions whose absolute values cannot become arbitrarily small.

In fact, let $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) ] , \dots , [ x _ { i } ^ { ( n ) } ( t ) ]$ be a system of $_ n$ linearly independent solutions of $( \Sigma _ { t } )$ . The Wronskian is known to be

$$
\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) } & { x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) } \\ { x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( t ) } & { x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( t ) } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( t ) } \\ { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & & \\ { x _ { 1 } ^ { ( n ) } ( t ) } & { x _ { 2 } ^ { ( n ) } ( t ) \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { ( n ) } ( t ) } \end{array} \right| = C \cdot \exp \left\{ F ( t ) \right\} ~ ( C \neq 0 ) .
$$

According to Hadamard's theorem (Hadamard, 1893；Section 12.4) on the maximum of the absolute value of a determinant that, if the elements of a determinant $\Delta$ are smaller in absolute value than a positive number $\pmb { A }$ ，then $| \Delta | < n ! A ^ { n }$ ，we see that,if the solutions $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } ( t ) ]$ are bounded and $\pmb { F } ( t )$ is also bounded, then the absolute value of the solution cannot become arbitrarily small. Consider a sequence of the systems $( \Sigma _ { t + h _ { y } } )$ tending to $( \Sigma _ { t } ^ { * } )$ for $\nu \to \infty$ , such that the systems of numbers $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } ( h _ { \nu } ) ]$ tend also to the limits $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( j ) ^ { \ast } } ( 0 ) ]$ ，then the determinant of these limit numbers has a value $C ^ { * }$ different from zero.Let $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( j ) ^ { * } } ( t ) ]$ be the solutions of $( S _ { t } ^ { * } )$ with these numbers as initial values and put

$$
F ^ { * } ( t ) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left[ f _ { 1 1 } ^ { * } ( t ) + f _ { 2 2 } ^ { * } ( t ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { n n } ^ { * } ( t ) \right] d t ,
$$

then

$$
\left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } & { \boldsymbol { x } _ { 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot \boldsymbol { x } _ { n } ^ { ( 1 ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } \\ { \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } & { \boldsymbol { x } _ { 2 } ^ { ( 2 ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot \boldsymbol { x } _ { n } ^ { ( 2 ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } \\ { \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ { \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( n ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } & { \boldsymbol { x } _ { 2 } ^ { ( n ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot \boldsymbol { x } _ { n } ^ { ( n ) ^ { * } } ( t ) } \end{array} \right| = C ^ { * } \cdot \exp \left\{ F ^ { * } ( t ) \right\} ~ ( C ^ { * } \neq 0 ) .
$$

Since $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } ( t + h _ { \nu } ) ]$ converge uniformly to $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } { } ^ { * } ( t ) ]$ in any finite interval, it follows that the solutions $[ x _ { i } ^ { ( j ) ^ { * } } ( t ) ]$ are bounded,and that the absolute value cannot become arbitrarily small.

By combining these two theorems we obtain

Theorem 3: If $( S _ { t } )$ admits bounded solutions and if none of the systems of the set $H ( \Sigma _ { t + h } )$ admits any bounded solution,except the trivial solution $x _ { i } = 0$ ，whose absolute value can become arbitrarily small, then $( S _ { t } )$ admits an almost periodic solution whose modulus is inferior or equal to the modulus of $( S _ { t } )$ .

These theorems can be applied to linear differential equations with constant coefficients and with almost periodic right-hand member. If

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } } + A _ { 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } x } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } x = \phi ( t ) ,
$$

where $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { n }$ are real constants and $\phi ( t )$ is almost periodic, admits bounded solutions, then the solutions are almost periodic. Suppose that $P ( r ) = r ^ { n } + A _ { 1 } r ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } = 0$ can have purely imaginary roots $ ; \pm { \sqrt { - 1 } } \omega _ { 1 } , \pm { \sqrt { - 1 } } \omega _ { 2 } , \ldots , \pm { \sqrt { - 1 } } \omega _ { k }$ ，but no zero root.:If $f ( t )$ is one of the bounded solutions,then the bounded solutions are

$$
x ( t ) = f ( t ) + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { k } C _ { \nu } \cos { ( \omega _ { \nu } t + \alpha _ { \nu } ) } , \qquad C _ { \nu } , \alpha _ { \nu } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

It can be shown that the solution

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { k } C _ { \nu } \cos { ( \omega _ { \nu } t + \alpha _ { \nu } ) }
$$

of the homogeneous system has a positive inferior bound. Hence $f ( t )$ is almost periodic. One of the solutions of the nonhomogeneous equation has its modulus contained in that of $\phi ( t )$ . If

$$
\phi ( t ) \sim \sum _ { n } A _ { n } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } t \} } ,
$$

then

$$
f ( t ) \sim \sum _ { n } \frac { A _ { n } } { P ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ : \Lambda _ { n } ) } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ : \Lambda _ { n } t \} } .
$$

Hence,in order that the nonhomogeneous equation (212) admits a bounded solution, it is necessary that none of the exponents $\Lambda _ { n }$ of $\phi ( t )$ should be the roots of the equation $P ( \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { n } ) \stackrel { \textstyle - } { = } 0$

Further, those theorems proved in the foregoing can be applied to the equation in perturbation theory (Section 14.18)

$$
( E _ { t } ) : \qquad { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } = \phi ( t ) \cdot x + \psi ( t ) , \qquad \phi ( t ) \neq 0 ,
$$

where $\phi ( t )$ and $\psi ( t )$ are real almost periodic functions of $t$ . Consider

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( E _ { t } ^ { * } ) : \quad \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \phi ^ { * } ( t ) \cdot x + \psi ^ { * } ( t ) , \smallskip \qquad } \\ { \phi ^ { * } ( t ) = \displaystyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } \phi ( t + h _ { \nu } ) , \qquad \psi ^ { * } ( t ) = \displaystyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \nu \to \infty } \psi ( t + h _ { \nu } ) } \end{array}
$$

and let the set of the equations be $H ( E _ { t + h } )$ . Take the homogeneous equation

$$
( \mathfrak { E } _ { t } ) \colon \quad \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \phi ( t ) \cdot x
$$

and the set $H ( \mathfrak { E } _ { t + h } )$ . Since it is yet impossible to treat the last equation,we restrict ourselves to discuss three cases in which an almost periodic function is introduced.

Case I. The equation $( \mathfrak { E } _ { t } )$ admits only bounded solutions.

Denote by $u , v$ any two such linearly independent solutions,and by $k$ an arbitrary positive number. Then there exists a quadratic form

$$
F ( t ) = A u ^ { 2 } + 2 B u v + C v ^ { 2 } ,
$$

such that $F ( t ) \geq k$ and which is almost periodic together with its first- and second-order derivatives.The modulus of this function is contained in the modulus of $\phi ( t )$

The general solution of $( \mathfrak { E } _ { t } )$ is

$$
x = c _ { 1 } \sqrt { F } \cos \left( \int _ { a } ^ { t } \frac { c } { F } d t \right) + c _ { 2 } \sqrt { F } \sin \left( \int _ { a } ^ { t } \frac { c } { F } d t \right) ,
$$

where $u v ^ { \prime } - u ^ { \prime } v = c$ . Put $| c | / F = R$ ，which is almost periodic and with positive inferior bound. The general solution of $( E _ { t } )$ is

$$
x = \Re + \frac { k } { \sqrt { R } } \cos \left( \int _ { c } ^ { t } R d t \right) ,
$$

where $\Re$ is one of the bounded solutions which is almost periodic.In order that the equation $( \mathfrak { E } _ { t } )$ admit almost periodic solutions it is necessary and sufficient that

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } R d t = r t + { \mathrm { a n ~ a l m o s t ~ p e r i o d i c ~ f u n c t i o n } } ,
$$

where $r > 0$ denotes the mean value of $\pmb R$

Case II. The homogeneous equation $( \mathfrak { E } _ { t } )$ admits only one bounded solution,whose absolute value cannot become arbitrarily small and all other solutions independent of this solution are not with this property.

Denote by $S ( t )$ the solution of $( \mathfrak { E } _ { t } )$ , and by ${ \mathfrak { S } } ( t )$ the solution of $\left( E _ { t } \right)$ Then $S ( t )$ is almost periodic. If $v$ is a solution independent of $S ( t )$ ， then $S v ^ { \prime } - S ^ { \prime } v = c$ . The general solution of $( E _ { t } )$ is

$$
x = \mathfrak { S } + c _ { 1 } S + c _ { 2 } S \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { d t } { S ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Case III. $\phi ( t ) \geq 0$

According to Murray (1923) the general solution of $\left( E _ { t } \right)$ is

$$
x = c _ { 1 } \exp \left\{ - \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \lambda d t \right\} + c _ { 2 } \exp \left\{ \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \bar { \lambda } d t \right\} ,
$$

where $\lambda ( t )$ and $\bar { \lambda } ( t )$ are nonnegative functions of $t$ and are bounded when $\phi ( t )$ is bounded.Put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { T ( t ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ \lambda ( 0 ) + \bar { \lambda } ( 0 ) ] [ \lambda ( t ) + \bar { \lambda } ( t ) ] . } \end{array}
$$

Denote by $\mathfrak { T } ( t )$ a bounded particular solution of the nonhomogeneous equation $\left( E _ { t } \right)$ ； then the solution of $( E _ { t } )$ is

$$
x = \mathfrak { T } ( t ) + \frac { k } { \sqrt { T } } \cosh \left( \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } T d t \right) ,
$$

where $\mathfrak { T } ( t )$ and $\pmb { T } ( t )$ are almost periodic and $T ( t ) > 0$ (Fink,1973).

14.27FUNCTIONAL EQUATION WITH ALMOST PERIODIC SOLUTION

Bochner (1929,1930, 1931) considered the functional equation

$$
L ( y ) = f ( x ) ,
$$

with

$$
{ \cal L } ( y ) \equiv \sum _ { \rho = 0 } ^ { r } \sum _ { \sigma = 0 } ^ { s } a _ { \rho \sigma } y ^ { ( \rho ) } ( x + \delta _ { \sigma } ) ,
$$

$$
f ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } a _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} } ,
$$

with complex numbers $a _ { \rho \sigma }$ ，where $\delta _ { \sigma }$ is an integral multiple of a constant $\omega$ .He showed at first that a solution of (214),which is bounded and uniformly continuous together with its first $r$ derivatives,is almost periodic together with its frst $r$ derivatives.

Suppose that $L ( y )$ is such that

$$
\left| \sum _ { \sigma = 0 } ^ { s } a _ { \rho \sigma } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \delta _ { \sigma } \lambda \right\} \right| \geq \eta ,
$$

and that the expansion

$$
G ( \lambda ) = \sum _ { \rho = 0 } ^ { r } \sum _ { \sigma = 0 } ^ { s } a _ { \rho \sigma } ( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \lambda ) ^ { \rho } \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \delta _ { \sigma } \lambda \right\}
$$

admits at most a finite number of zero points $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \Lambda _ { k }$ respectively with multiplicity $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , \ldots , l _ { k }$ . He showed that, in order for the equation (214) to admit a solution which is almost periodic together with its first $r$ derivatives,it is necessary and suficient that there should exist an almost periodic function whose $^ { l , \mathrm { t h } }$ derivative is almost periodic and coincides with exp $\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { v } x \} \cdot f ( x )$ for each $\nu$ in the interval $1 \leq \nu \leq k$ . Such a solution is single-valued. If such a solution exists,then there exists the Fourier series

$$
\begin{array} { c l } { { y ( x ) \sim \displaystyle \sum _ { n } G ^ { - 1 } ( \lambda _ { n } ) a _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { k } C _ { \nu } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { \nu } x \} } } } \end{array}
$$

with arbitrary constants $C _ { y }$ .Especially,under the assumption (216) if $G ( \lambda )$ has no real zero-point in general, then there exists just one almost periodic solution with the first $r$ derivatives for each $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { f } } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ ，that is,

$$
y ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } G ^ { - 1 } ( \lambda _ { n } ) a _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} } .
$$

If $\lambda _ { n }$ in the expansion (215) is not any infinity-point of $\Gamma ( \lambda )$ ，then the factor $\Gamma ( \lambda )$ in the Fourier series

$$
\sum _ { n } \Gamma ( \lambda _ { n } ) a _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} }
$$

is called the multiplicator of the Fourier series (215) for $f ( x )$ . Denote the function represented by this Fourier series by $f _ { \boldsymbol { \Gamma } } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ . The sum of a finite number of multiplicators of $f ( x )$ is also a multiplicator of $f ( x )$

The coefficients $\Gamma _ { 1 } ( \lambda )$ and $\Gamma _ { 2 } ( \lambda )$ with the same values of the exponents $\lambda _ { n }$ in (215) are both multiplicators or both not multiplicators.In such a case $\Gamma _ { 1 } ( \lambda )$ and $\Gamma _ { 2 } ( \lambda )$ are said to be equivalent. To each almost periodic function there corresponds a function $\Gamma ( \lambda )$ which is a multiplicator, that is，a function $\gamma ( \lambda ) = \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \delta \lambda \} }$ such that $f _ { \tau } ( x ) = f ( x + \delta )$ with real $\delta$ .The product $\Gamma _ { 1 } ( \lambda ) \Gamma _ { 2 } ( \lambda )$ is also a multiplicator.

Bochner proved that a multiplicator is a continuously differentiable function $\Gamma ( \lambda )$ which vanishes outside a sufficiently large interval, i.e., $\Gamma ( \lambda ) = 0$ for $| \lambda | \geq \alpha$ .For the proof he formed an auxiliary function

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle K ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } x \lambda \right\} \cdot \Gamma ( \lambda ) d \lambda , } } \\ { { \displaystyle F ( x ) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f ( t ) K ( t - x ) d t = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f ( t + x ) K ( t ) d x , } } \end{array}
$$

This function $F ( x )$ exists due to Plancherel's theorem on the Fourier integral (Bochner, 1932,1959；Wiener，1933； Paley and Wiener, 1934；Bochner and Chandrasekharan,1949) that,if

$$
g ( x ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { A \to \infty } { \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } } \int _ { - A } ^ { A } f ( y ) \exp { \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } x y \} } \cdot d y
$$

exists, then

$$
f ( x ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { A \to \infty } { \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } } \int _ { - A } ^ { A } g ( y ) \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } x y \} } \cdot d y .
$$

Thus we see that $F ( x )$ is almost periodic and $F ( x ) = f _ { \tau } ( x )$

The function $f _ { r } ( x )$ has bounded exponents for any $f ( x )$ ， because $\Gamma ( \lambda ) = 0$ for $| \lambda | > \alpha$ Accordingly $f _ { r } ( x )$ possesses by (221) an unlimited number of almost periodic derivatives

$$
K ^ { ( \rho ) } ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda x \right\} \cdot ( - \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda ) ^ { \rho } \Gamma ( \lambda ) d \lambda ,
$$

$$
f _ { \cal F } ^ { ( \rho ) } ( x ) = ( - 1 ) ^ { \rho } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f ( t ) K ^ { ( \rho ) } ( t - x ) d t \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , \ldots ) ,
$$

because the $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \vert K ^ { ( \rho ) } ( x ) \vert d x$ are finite.

Take four constants $\alpha _ { 1 } < \alpha _ { 2 } < \alpha _ { 3 } < \alpha _ { 4 }$ Let $\Gamma ( \lambda ) = 0$ outside the interval $\alpha _ { 1 } \leq \lambda \leqq \alpha _ { 4 }$ and $\Gamma ( \lambda ) = 1$ in $\alpha _ { 2 } \leq \lambda \leqq \alpha _ { 3 }$ .This function $\Gamma ( \lambda )$ is a multiplicator.The almost periodic function (215） with this property presents two gaps between $( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } )$ and between $( \alpha _ { 3 } , \alpha _ { 4 } )$ in which no Fourier exponent can be found. Then

$$
\sum _ { \alpha _ { 2 } \leq \lambda _ { n } \leq \alpha _ { 3 } } a _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} }
$$

is also a Fourier series.

Now let

$$
y ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } A _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} } ,
$$

with the same exponents as in (215),be an almost periodic solution of the homogeneous equation (214).If the first $r$ derivatives of (223) are almost periodic,then, since we have for an arbitrary $\delta _ { \sigma } , 1 \leqq \rho \leqq r ;$ y() $( x + \partial _ { \circ } ) \sim \sum _ { n } ^ { } ( \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } ) ^ { \rho } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } \partial _ { \circ } \} } \cdot { \cal A } _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} }$ ， we obtain

$$
L ( y ) \sim \sum _ { n } G ( \lambda _ { n } ) A _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } x \} } ,
$$

where $G ( \lambda )$ is given by (217).If (214) admits a solution (223), then

$$
G ( \lambda _ { n } ) A _ { \lambda _ { n } } = a _ { \lambda _ { n } }
$$

for all $\pmb { n }$ Hence $G ( \lambda )$ must be a divisor of (215). Since $G ( \lambda )$ is an entire function,it has a denumerably infinite number of real zeropoints $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ，such that $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { k \to \infty } | \Lambda _ { k } | = \infty } \end{array}$ ，with multiplicity $l _ { 1 }$ ， $l _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ .In order for (224) to hold it is necessary that (215) should have no exponent that is equal to any of $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } ,$ ..．．A nonhomogeneous equation (214） might admit a solution (223) with exponents extracted from these $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ ， $\Lambda _ { 2 }$ ,... ．A solution in which none of the exponents $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 }$ 。... appears is called a pure solution. If $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are finite in number, then there exists a pure solution if (223) exists,

$$
\sum _ { \nu } A _ { \wedge \nu } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ \Lambda _ { \nu } x \} .
$$

Theorem 1: Suppose that $G ( \lambda )$ of (214) admits zero-points $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 }$ ， ... with multiplicity $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ，respectively. In order that (223） shall be a solution of (214) which is almost periodic with its first $r$ derivatives,it is necessary that there should exist an almost periodic function, whose l,th derivative coincides with exp $\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda _ { \nu } x \} \cdot f ( x )$ for each $\nu$ .If the Fourier exponents with nonzero coefficients are bounded, then this condition is also sufficient and the solution is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle y ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } G ^ { - 1 } ( \lambda _ { n } ) a _ { \lambda _ { n } } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ \lambda _ { n } x \right\} + } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu } A _ { \lambda _ { \nu } } \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ \Lambda _ { \nu } x \right\} , } } \end{array}
$$

where the second sum is almost periodic,together with its first $r$ derivatives, and the exponents are extracted from $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots$

Consider a system of equations

$$
{ \frac { d y _ { \mu } ( x ) } { d x } } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { \mu \nu } y _ { \nu } ( x ) + f _ { \mu } ( x ) ,
$$

$$
( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m ; - \infty < x < + \infty )
$$

with constants $a _ { \mu \nu }$ ，where the $f _ { \mu } ( x )$ are almost periodic. Put

$$
G ( \lambda ) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { { \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda - a _ { 1 1 } } } & { { - a _ { 1 2 } } } & { { \cdots \cdots } } \\ { { - a _ { 2 1 } } } & { { \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda - a _ { 2 2 } \cdots \cdots } } \\ { { \cdots } } & { { } } & { { } } \end{array} \right| .
$$

Theorem 2: In order that (226) shall admit an almost periodic solution,it is sufficient either that the characteristic function $G ( \lambda )$ of (217) should not admit any real zero-point-in which case the solution is single-valued—or that for each $\mu$ there should exist an almost periodic function whose lth derivative coincides with

$$
\exp \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \Lambda x \} \cdot f _ { \mu } ( x ) \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

to each real zero-point $\Lambda$ of multiplicity $\imath$ of $G ( \lambda )$ -in which case the solution exists but with the exception of an exponential polynomial.

What is the condition for the almost periodicity of the solution of the equations

$$
\frac { d y _ { \mu } ( x ) } { d x } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { \mu \nu } ( x ) y _ { \nu } ( x ) + b _ { \mu } ( x ) ( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m ) ,
$$

where $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ and ${ b _ { \mu } ( x ) }$ are almost periodic? The condition for the almost periodicity of the solution of the homogeneous equations

$$
\frac { d y _ { \mu } ( x ) } { d x } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { \mu \nu } ( x ) y _ { \nu } ( x )
$$

is very restricted insofar as the present study is concerned (Section 14.24).

Let $( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { m } )$ be the Cartesian coordinates of a point in an $_ { m }$ -dimensional space.The functions $a _ { \mu } ( x )$ are supposed to be all real. We restrict ourselves to the consideration of real solutions

$$
\mathfrak { i } ( x ) = \{ y _ { 1 } ( x ) , y _ { 2 } ( x ) , \ldots , y _ { m } ( x ) \} .
$$

Consider the closed set of all points in the $m$ -dimensional space which represents the values of a vector $\eta ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ when any solution (230) of the equations (228) is given. Such a closed set or a closure is called the range of $\eta ( x )$ and is denoted by $R ( \eta )$

If $\eta ( x )$ is almost periodic,that is, if the components $y _ { \mu } ( x )$ are all almost periodic,then the range $R ( \eta )$ is bounded and does not contain the origin.In fact,if it contained $( 0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 )$ ，then there would be a sequence $\{ x _ { n } \}$ such that $\eta ( x _ { n } ) \to 0$ for $n \to \infty$ . Since $y _ { \mu } ( x )$ and $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ are normal functions, the sequence $\left\{ h _ { n } \right\}$ contains a subsequence $\{ k _ { n } \}$ such that all sequences $\{ y _ { \mu } ( { \pmb x } + k _ { n } ) \}$ and $\{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + k _ { n } ) \}$ would be uniformly convergent for $n \to \infty$ . Let a subsequence of $\{ x _ { n } \}$ of this kind be $\left\{ k _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right\}$ ， and the limit functions of these two sequences be $\tilde { y } _ { \mu } ( x )$ and $\tilde { a } _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ ，respectively. Then the vector $\tilde { \eta } ( x ) = \{ \tilde { y } _ { 1 } ( x ) , \dots , \tilde { y } _ { m } ( x ) \}$ would be the solution of

$$
\frac { d \tilde { y } _ { \mu } ( x ) } { d x } { \cdot } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { m } \tilde { a } _ { \mu \nu } ( x ) \tilde { y } _ { \nu } ( x )
$$

with coefficients that are not identically zero.But $\tilde { \eta } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ should be zero at the origin.This is contradictory.Hence

$$
0 < g \leq \sqrt { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + y _ { m } ^ { 2 } } \leq G
$$

is a necessry condition for the almost periodicity of $\eta ( \boldsymbol { x } )$

But this condition is not sufficient. In fact, suppose that $a ( x )$ is real and almost periodic with mean value zero and that its integral is not almost periodic.Thus the solution of the equation

$$
{ \frac { d y ( x ) } { d x } } = { \sqrt { - 1 } } a ( x ) y ( x )
$$

would be

$$
y ( x ) = C \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } a ( x ) \ d x \right\} ,
$$

and would satisfy (232). But according to Bohr (Sections 14.19 and 14.24),a function of the form (233) cannot be almost periodic unless it can be written in the form

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { x } a ( x ) d x = \alpha x + \phi ( x ) ,
$$

with constant $\alpha$ and an almost periodic function $\phi ( x )$ . This is contradictory.Hence we obtain

Theorem 3: Let $\mathfrak { \eta } _ { 1 } ( x ) , \dots , \mathfrak { \eta } _ { m } ( x )$ be the given set of linearly independent solutions of the homogeneous equations (229). Then all the given solutions are almost periodic if the following conditions are satisfied: (i) The range $R ( \eta _ { \mu } )$ is bounded for $\mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m ;$ (ii) there does not exist any solution $\eta ( x )$ of (229) such that the range $\pmb { R } ( \eta )$ is a part of any range $R ( \eta _ { \mu } )$ ；and (ii) the Wronskian $z ( x ) =$ $\| \eta _ { 1 } ( x ) , \ldots , \eta _ { m } ( x ) \|$ does not take any arbitrarily small value near zero. The modulus of the solution is equal to the modulus of the equations.

The Wronskian $z ( x )$ is equal to

$$
C \exp \left\{ \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } \left[ a _ { 1 1 } ( x ) + \cdots + a _ { m m } ( x ) \right] d x \right\} ,
$$

which is bounded by the condition (i).Hence the condition (iii) is equivalent to the boundedness of the integral of the real part of an almost periodic function $a _ { 1 1 } ( x ) + \dotsb + a _ { m m } ( x )$ ,i.e., $z ( x )$ is almost periodic. It is easily seen that the range $R ( \eta _ { \nu } )$ does not contain the origin by the conditions (i) and (iii).
