For the proof of the theorem Bochner (1933) proposed the lemma that, if the sequences $\{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + h _ { n } ) \}$ are all uniformly convergent for a sequence $\left\{ h _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right\}$ ， then the sequences $\{ \eta _ { \mu } ( x + h _ { n } ) \}$ are also uniformly convergent.

Let $\{ l _ { n } \}$ be such that the $\{ a _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu \nu } ( x + l _ { \scriptscriptstyle \pi } ) \}$ are uniformly convergent and the $\{ \eta _ { \mu } ( l _ { n } ) \}$ are convergent,and let the limit of $\{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + l _ { n } ) \}$ be $\tilde { a } _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ Then the sequences $\{ \eta _ { \mu } ( x + l _ { n } ) \}$ are uniformly convergent for any finite interval.Let the limits be $\tilde { \eta } _ { \mu } ( x )$ . Then each limit vector is a solution of (231).We have

$$
R ( \tilde { \eta } _ { \mu } ) \subset R ( \eta _ { \mu } ) .
$$

Put $\tilde { k } _ { n } = - l _ { n }$ ， then the $\{ \tilde { a } _ { \mu \nu } ( x + \tilde { k } _ { n } ) \}$ are_uniformly convergent to $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ .For a suitably chosen subsequence $\{ \tilde { l } _ { n } \}$ of $\{ \tilde { k } _ { \mathfrak { n } } \}$ the sequences $\{ \dot { \eta } _ { \mu } ( x + \tilde { l } _ { n } ) \}$ converge to the limit vectors $\tilde { \bar { \eta } } _ { \mu }$ ，which satisfy

$$
\frac { d \tilde { \hat { \eta } } _ { \mu } ( x ) } { d x } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { \mu \nu } ( x ) \tilde { \hat { y } } _ { \nu } ( x ) .
$$

We have

$$
R ( \tilde { \tilde { \eta } } _ { \mu } ) \subset R ( \tilde { \eta } _ { \mu } ) .
$$

Combining these two relations and referring to the condition (ii) we have $\tilde { \eta } _ { \mu } = \eta _ { \mu }$

It can be shown that the solutions $\tilde { \eta } _ { 1 } , \dots , \tilde { \eta } _ { m }$ of(231） also satisfy the conditions of the theorem. The condition (i) is trivial. The Wronskian $\widetilde { z } ( x ) = \| \widetilde { \eta } _ { 1 } , \dots , \widetilde { \eta } _ { m } \|$ is the limit of $\{ z ( x + l _ { n } ) \}$ and the condition (ii) is satisfied.Any solution $\tilde { \eta } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ of (231) can be written

$$
\tilde { \eta } ( x ) = c _ { 1 } \tilde { \eta } _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { m } \tilde { \eta } _ { m } .
$$

The sequence $\{ \tilde { \eta } ( x + \tilde { l } _ { n } ) \}$ converges to

$$
c _ { 1 } \tilde { \tilde { \eta } } _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { m } \tilde { \tilde { \eta } } _ { m } = c _ { 1 } \eta _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { m } \eta _ { m } .
$$

If we suppose that $R ( \tilde { \eta } ) \subset R ( \tilde { \eta } _ { 1 } )$ , for example, then we should have

$$
R ( c _ { 1 } \eta _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { m } \eta _ { m } ) \subset R ( \eta _ { 1 } ) .
$$

Hence we should have $c _ { 1 } = 1 , c _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = c _ { m } = 0$ and $\tilde { \eta } = \tilde { \eta } _ { 1 }$ . Thus the condition (ii) is satisfied for the solution $\tilde { \eta } _ { 1 } , \dots , \tilde { \eta } _ { m }$

By combining these two results we see that, if $\left\{ l _ { \mathit { n } } \right\}$ and $\left\{ l _ { \pi } ^ { \prime } \right\}$ be two sequences such that all sequences $\{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + l _ { n } ) \}$ and $\{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + l _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) \}$ converge uniformly to the same limit functions $\{ \tilde { a } _ { \mu \nu } ( x ) \}$ ，and if $\{ \eta _ { \mu } ( l _ { n } ) \}$ and $\{ \eta _ { \mu } ( l _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) \}$ are convergent, then.

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \gamma _ { \mu } ( l _ { n } ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \gamma _ { \mu } ( l _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m ) .
$$

Now suppose that $\{ \eta _ { \mu } ( x + h _ { n } ) \}$ of the lemma were not uniformly convergent. Then there would be a positive number $\varepsilon$ ，two subsequences $\{ k _ { n } \} , \{ k _ { n } ^ { \prime } \}$ of $\left\{ h _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right\}$ ， and a sequence $\{ x _ { \pi } \}$ such that

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } | \eta _ { \mu } ( x _ { n } + k _ { n } ) - \eta _ { \mu } ( x _ { n } + k _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) | ^ { 2 } \equiv \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \ \quad ( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

Then we should be able to assume that the three sets of sequences

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \{ \eta _ { \mu } ( x _ { n } + k _ { n } ) \} , } & & { \{ \eta _ { \mu } ( x _ { n } + k _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) \} ; } \\ & { \{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + x _ { n } + k _ { n } ) \} , } & & { \{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + x _ { n } + k _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) \} ; } \\ & { \{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + x _ { n } ) \} , } & & { \{ a _ { \mu \nu } ( x + x _ { n } + h _ { n } ) \} } \end{array}
$$

would be uniformly convergent. The second set of two sequences tends to the common limit $\tilde { a } _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ . From what has been just proved for the solution $\tilde { \eta } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \tilde { \eta } _ { m }$ we can see, by putting $l _ { n } = x _ { n } + k _ { n } , l _ { n } ^ { \prime } = x _ { n } + k _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ ， that the relation (235) contradicts the assumption (236).

If the range satisfies the condition $R [ y ( x ) ] = R [ c y ( x ) ]$ ，with $| c | = 1$ ， $c \neq 1$ ， then $y ( x )$ is called symmetric (Cameron,1936).Let $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ of (229)and ${ \boldsymbol { b } } _ { \mu } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ of (228) be almost periodic,and $M$ be its modulus. Cameron stated Bochner's theorem in the following manner.

Theorem 4: All solutions of the homogeneous equations (229) are almost periodic if the following conditions are satisfied:

(i)The integral

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { x } \Re [ a _ { 1 1 } ( \xi ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { m m } ( \xi ) ] d \xi
$$

is bounded, where $\Re$ denotes the real part.

(ii) All solutions of the homogeneous equations (229） are bounded.

(ii)）If $y ( x )$ is a solution of (229),then there exist linearly independent solutions $y ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } ( x ) , \ldots , y ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( n ) } ( x )$ with the property that $R [ y ( x ) ] \subset$ $R [ y ^ { ( \nu ) } ( x ) ]$ is not true unless $y ( x ) \equiv y ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( \nu ) } ( x )$

If these conditions are satisfied,then the modulus of the set of solutions of (229) is exactly equal to $M$

An alternative form of the condition (ii) is :

(iiia） There exists a set of linearly independent solutions $y ^ { ( 1 ) } \big ( x \big )$ ， $\ldots , y ^ { ( n ) } ( x )$ of (229） with the property that $R [ y ( x ) ] = R [ y ^ { ( \nu ) } ( x ) ]$ is not true unless $y ( x ) \equiv y ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( \nu ) } ( x )$ . Or

(iib) There does not exist any symmetric solution of (229).

The conditions (i) and (i) are necessary.Cameron proved that, if $a _ { \mu \nu }$ is almost periodic with modulus $M$ ,then (i),(i),(iia),or (i),(i), (iiib） are sufficient conditions that all solutions of (229) are almost periodic with modulus $M$

Another alternative is :

(iiic） There exists a set of linearly independent solutions $y ^ { ( 1 ) } ( x )$ ， $\ldots , y ^ { ( n ) } ( x )$ of (229） with the property that $R [ y ( x ) ] = R [ y ^ { ( \nu ) } ( x ) ]$ is not true unless $y ( x ) \equiv y ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( \nu ) } ( x )$ ，or a solution $y ( x )$ of (229） is almost periodic,or

(iiid） There is no symmetric solution other than those which are almost periodic.

Hence we see that (i),(i),(ic),or (i),(i),(iid)are the necessary and sufficient conditions.

Theorem 5:It is when and only when the conditions (i), (ii),(iii) are satisfied that all solutions of the homogeneous equations (229) are almost periodic and then the modulus of allsolutions is equal to the modulus of the set composed of all coefficients $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ and all solutions of (229) which are of the same range as one of $y ^ { ( \nu ) } ( x )$ but different from it.

Theorem 6: It is when and only when the conditions (i),(i),(iib) are satisfied that all solutions of (229) are almost periodic,and then the modulus of all solutions of (229) is equal to the modulus of the set composed of all coefficients $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ and all symmetric solutions of (229).

Although not all solutions are almost periodic, there may be some particular solutions which are almost periodic.A suffcient condition is :

Theorem 7: If the conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied and if $y ( x )$ is a solution of (229) such that there exists no solution $z ( x )$ which is different from $y ( x )$ and is not almost periodic but with the same range as that of $y ( x )$ ，then $y ( x )$ is almost periodic,and the modulus of $y ( x )$ is contained in the modulus of the set composed of all coefficients $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ and all solutions $z ( x )$ of (229),other than $y ( x )$ ，which satisfy $R [ z ( x ) ] = R [ y ( x ) ]$

The proof is carried out by referring to Bochner's theorem that a continuous function or a vector function $f ( x )$ is almost periodic when and only when to a sequence $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots$ of real numbers there corresponds a subsequence $h _ { p 1 } , h _ { p 2 } , \ldots .$ ，such that $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } f ( x + h _ { p i } ) } \end{array}$ exists uniformly for all $_ x$ . The method of proof is similar to Favard's (Section 14.25). If $f ( x + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( x + h _ { 2 } ) ,$ ... are uniformly convergent for any almost periodic function $f ( x )$ with modulus $M$ for all $x$ ，then $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are said to converge with respect to the modulus $M$ .If the iterated limit of $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots , k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots$ exists for all $_ x$ and if the sequence $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots$ . is a sequence which converges to $- k _ { 1 } , - k _ { 2 } , \ldots$ with respect to $M$ ，and if

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } \operatorname* { l i m } _ { j \to \infty } y ( x + h _ { i } + k _ { j } ) = y ( x )
$$

for all $x$ ， then the continuous function or the vector function $y ( x )$ is said to be stationary relative to $M$ . If the iterated limit

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } \operatorname* { l i m } _ { j \to \infty } y ( x + h _ { i } + k _ { j } ) = c y ( x )
$$

holds with respect to such a sequence $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots , k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots$ for all $x$ ， and if $c \neq 1$ ， $| c | = 1$ ， then $y ( x )$ is said to be twistable with respect to $M$ .A function which is twistable relative to an arbitrary modulus is symmetric.Thus the condition is put forward:

(iv) There does not exist，other than those which are almost periodic,any solution of (229) twistable with respect to $M$

Theorem 8: All solutions of (229) are almost periodic when and only when the conditions (i), (ii),(iv) are satisfied,and then the modulus $M$ of all solutions of (229) is equal to the modulus $M ^ { \prime }$ of the set composed of all coefficients $a _ { \mu \nu } ( x )$ and all solutions of (229) which are twistable with respect to $M$

Cameron deduced the necessary and sufficient condition for the almost periodicity of the solution of the nonhomogeneous equatins (228) from that of the homogeneous equations (229).Put

$$
b _ { \mu } ( x ) = a _ { \mu , n + 1 } ( x ) , \qquad 1 = z _ { n + 1 } ( x ) ,
$$

then (228) are transformed to (Hagihara, 1928)

$$
\frac { d z _ { \mu } ( x ) } { d x } = a _ { \mu 1 } ( x ) z _ { 1 } ( x ) + \cdots + a _ { \mu n } ( x ) z _ { n } ( x ) + b _ { \mu } ( x ) z _ { n + 1 } ( x ) ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { d z _ { n + 1 } ( x ) } { d x } } = 0 \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Theorem 9: The necessary and sufficient conditions for the almost periodicity of all solutions of the nonhomogeneous equations (228) are that at least one solution of the homogeneous equations (229) is bounded for all positive $_ x$ ，and that all solutions of the associated homogeneous equations (229) are almost periodic. Then the modulus of all solutions of (229) is equal to the modulus $M$ of the set composed of $\pmb { n }$ functions $b _ { 1 } ( x ) , \ldots , b _ { n } ( x )$

Recently Seifert (1965,1966,1968) studied almost periodic solutions and the stability of an almost periodic system of differential equations (Amerio and Prouse, 1971).

14.28SOLUTIONS OF THE ALMOST PERIODIC TYPE

Cameron (1938) wrote the equations (228)and (229) in vector form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \cal D } [ x ( t ) ] = A ( t ) x ( t ) + b ( t ) , } \\ { { \cal D } [ x ( t ) ] = A ( t ) x ( t ) . } \end{array}
$$

If $\mathbfcal { A } ( t )$ and $b ( t )$ are periodic with the same period， then all solutions are represented by

$$
x ( t ) = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } e ^ { \lambda _ { \nu } t } t ^ { \tau _ { \nu } } y ^ { ( \nu ) } ( t ) ,
$$

with a positive integer $p$ ，and complex numbers $\lambda _ { y }$ and nonnegative integers $r _ { \flat }$ ，where the $y ^ { ( \pmb { \nu } ) } ( t )$ are almost periodic vector functions.If there exist such a positive integer $p$ ， real numbers $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p }$ ,nonnegative integers $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { p }$ and almost periodic vector functions $y ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) , \ldots , y ^ { ( n ) } ( t )$ such that (229a) holds identically in $t$ ,then a vector function $x ( t )$ is said to be of the almost periodic type. The smallest common modulus of $y ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) , \ldots , y ^ { ( n ) } ( t )$ is called the modulus of $x ( t )$ . The question is,under what conditions all solutions are of the almost periodic type,i.e.,can be represented in the form (237） with almost periodic $y ^ { ( \upsilon ) } ( t )$ in the general case in which $\boldsymbol { A } ( t )$ and $b ( t )$ are almost periodic. Cameron (1938) has found the necessary and sufficient condition.

Write the equation (228a) in the form

$$
D [ x ( t ) ] = A ( t ) x ( t ) + \xi _ { n + 1 } ( t ) b ( t ) , \qquad { \frac { d } { d t } } \xi _ { n + 1 } ( t ) = 0 ,
$$

where $\xi _ { n + 1 } ( t )$ is a scalar function. Hence the theorems for homogeneous equations can be rephrased so as to be applicable to the corresponding nonhomogeneous equations.

At first we study the properties of the solutions which are of the almost periodic type, by supposing not only that $A ( t )$ is a continuous matrix function but also that one or more solutions of (228a) is of the almost periodic type.Write the norm of the vector $x$ in the form $| x _ { 1 } | + \cdots + | x _ { n } | = \| x \|$ . If there exists a constant $C$ such that $\| x ( t ) \|$ $< C f ( t )$ for all sufficiently large $t$ ，then we say that $x ( t ) = O [ f ( t ) ]$ for $t \to + \infty$ .If there exist two constants $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ such that $C _ { 1 } f ( t ) <$ $\parallel x ( t ) \parallel < C _ { 2 } f ( t )$ for sufficiently large $t$ ,then we say that $x ( t ) = O ^ { * } [ f ( t ) ]$ for $t \to + \infty$ . If $x ( t )$ is written in the form (237) with the largest real $\lambda _ { y }$ ，an almost periodic $y ^ { ( \nu ) } ( t )$ and nonnegative integers $r _ { \nu }$ ，then we write

$$
x ( t ) = O [ e ^ { \lambda t } t ^ { \tau } ]
$$

for $t \to \infty$ ，where $\lambda$ is the largest value of $\lambda _ { y }$ occurring in (237） and $r$ the largest value of $r _ { \nu }$ for values of $\nu$ such that $\lambda _ { y } = \lambda$ .If $\lambda$ is the smallest value instead of the largest, then (238) holds for $t \to - \infty$ ， and if $\lambda ,$ is a constant independent of $\nu$ ,then (238)holds for $t \to \pm \infty$ . A solution $x ( t )$ of $( 2 2 9 \mathsf { a } )$ is called a primary solution of order $\lambda$ and degree $\boldsymbol { r }$ if $x ( t )$ is written in the form (238). Any solution $x ( t )$ of the almost periodic type can be decomposed into the sum of a finite number of primary solutions.

Let $\mathbf { \nabla } \_ { A \left( t \right) }$ be almost periodic and $x ( t )$ be a solution of the almost periodic type ； then we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x ( t ) = e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } [ y _ { 1 0 } ( t ) + t y _ { 1 1 } ( t ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + t ^ { r _ { 1 } } y _ { 1 r _ { 1 } } ( t ) ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ { + e ^ { \lambda p t } [ y _ { p 0 } ( t ) + t y _ { p 1 } ( t ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + t ^ { r p } y _ { p r _ { p } } ( t ) ] , } \end{array}
$$

each term of which is a solution of (229a). Consider one of the terms of (239) by omitting the suffices

$$
y _ { \mu } ( t ) = ( r - \mu ) ! y _ { 1 , r - \mu } ( t ) , x _ { \mu } ( t ) = e ^ { \imath t } \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \mu } \frac { t ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } y _ { \mu - \nu } ( t )
$$

We write

$$
x ( t ) = x _ { r } ( t ) + x ^ { * } ( t ) ,
$$

where

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } e ^ { - \lambda t } t ^ { s } e ^ { \ast } ( t ) = 0
$$

for any $s$ .We can choose $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots \ldots \ldots$ so that we have

$$
\operatorname * { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } A ( t + h _ { i } ) = A ( t ) , \qquad \operatorname * { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } y _ { \mu } ( t + h _ { i } ) = y _ { \mu } ( t )
$$

uniformly in $t$ for $\mu \leqq r$ .From (240) we obtain

$$
r ! \operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } \left[ e ^ { - \lambda h _ { i } } \cdot h _ { i } ^ { - r } x ( t + h _ { i } ) \right] = e ^ { \lambda t } \cdot y _ { 0 } ( t ) = x _ { 0 } ( t ) .
$$

But the expression in the square bracket is a solution of

$$
D [ z ( t ) ] = A ( t + h _ { i } ) z ( t ) .
$$

Hence the limit $x _ { 0 } ( t )$ of the sequence is not a solution unless $r = 0$ ， since the factor $t ^ { \gamma }$ is missing.

We carry out the transformation

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { x _ { r } ( t + h _ { i } ) = e ^ { \lambda ( t + h _ { i } ) } \cdot { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { r } } y _ { \tau - \nu } ( t + h _ { i } ) \sum _ { \rho = 0 } ^ { \nu } { \displaystyle \frac { t ^ { \nu - \rho } h _ { i } ^ { \rho } } { ( \nu - \rho ) ! \rho ! } } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { = e ^ { \lambda ( t + h _ { i } ) } \cdot { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r ! } } y _ { 0 } ( t + h _ { i } ) h _ { i } ^ { \tau } + e ^ { \lambda ( t + h _ { i } ) } \cdot { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { ( r - 1 ) ! } } } } } \\ { { { \qquad \cdot [ y _ { 1 } ( t + h _ { i } ) + t y _ { 0 } ( t + h _ { i } ) ] h _ { i } ^ { \tau - 1 } } } } \\ { { { \qquad + \ \cdot \ \cdot \cdot . } } } \end{array}
$$

The first term is

$$
\frac { h _ { i } ^ { r } } { r ! } x _ { 0 } ( t + h _ { i } ) , \qquad \mathrm { a n d } \qquad x ( t + h _ { i } ) - \frac { h _ { i } ^ { r } } { r ! } x _ { 0 } ( t + h _ { i } )
$$

is a solution of (241). Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { x _ { 1 } ( t ) = e ^ { x t } [ y _ { 1 } ( t ) + t y _ { 0 } ( t ) ] \qquad } \\ { \qquad = ( r - 1 ) ! \operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } \frac { x ( t + h _ { i } ) - ( h _ { i } ^ { r } / r ! ) x _ { 0 } ( t + h _ { i } ) } { e ^ { \lambda h _ { i } } h _ { i } ^ { r - 1 } } } \end{array}
$$

is a solution of (229a). In general, the second set of $\boldsymbol { r }$ equations of (240) admits a unique solution when $\pmb { n }$ equations $y _ { \mu } ( t )$ are taken as the unknown functions. The set of $r$ equations is satisfied by

$$
y _ { \mu } ( t ) = e ^ { - \lambda t } \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \mu } { \frac { ( - t ) ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } } x _ { \mu - \nu } ( t ) \qquad ( \mu = 0 , 1 , \ldots , r ) .
$$

Assume that $x _ { \mu } ( t )$ is a solution of (229a) for all $\mu , \mu < q \leq r$ Consider again $x _ { r } ( t + h _ { i } )$ by changing the order of the summation in the form

$$
x _ { r } ( t + h _ { i } ) = \sum _ { \rho = 0 } ^ { r } \frac { h _ { i } ^ { \rho } } { \rho ! } \tilde { x } _ { r - \rho } ( t ; h _ { i } ) ,
$$

$$
\tilde { x } _ { \mu } ( t ; h ) = e ^ { \imath ( t + h ) } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \mu } \frac { t ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } y _ { \mu - \nu } ( t + h )
$$

Substituting (242) in (243) we obtain

$$
\tilde { x } _ { \mu } ( t ; h ) = \sum _ { \sigma = 0 } ^ { \mu } \frac { ( - h ) ^ { \sigma } } { \sigma ! } x _ { \mu - \sigma } ( t + h ) .
$$

Hence $\tilde { x } _ { \mu } ( t ; h )$ is a solution of (241) for $\mu < q$ .Thus

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \displaystyle X ( t , h _ { i } ) \equiv x ( t + h _ { i } ) - \sum _ { \rho = r - q + 1 } ^ { r } \frac { h _ { i } ^ { \rho } } { \rho ! } \tilde { x } _ { r - \rho } ( t ; h _ { i } ) } } } \\ { { { \mathrm { } } } } \\ { { { \mathrm { } = x ^ { * } ( t + h _ { i } ) + e ^ { \lambda ( t + h _ { i } ) } \cdot { \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho = 0 } ^ { r - q } \frac { h _ { i } ^ { \rho } } { \rho ! } \sum _ { \nu = \rho } ^ { r } \frac { t ^ { \nu \cdot \rho } } { ( \nu - \rho ) ! } } y _ { r - \nu } ( t + \cdot } } } \end{array}
$$

is a solution of (241),and

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } { \frac { ( r - q ) ! X ( t , h _ { i } ) } { e ^ { \lambda h _ { i } } h _ { i } ^ { r - q } } } = e ^ { \lambda t } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = r - q } ^ { r } { \frac { t ^ { \nu - r + q } } { ( \nu - r + q ) ! } } y _ { r - \nu } ( t ) = x _ { q } ( t )
$$

is a solution of (229a). Thus $x _ { \mu } ( t )$ satisfes (229a) for all $\mu , \mu < q + 1$

Since ${ \boldsymbol x } _ { r } ( t )$ is a solution,so is $x ^ { * } ( t )$ . The same argument can be applied,and each group of terms in $x ( t )$ with the same exponential factors forms a solution. Moreover the expansion (239) is unique and $y _ { 1 0 } ( t ) , \ldots , y _ { 1 r _ { 1 } } ( t )$ are uniquely defined in terms of $x _ { \mu } ( t )$ ，and then $y _ { 2 0 } ( t ) , \ldots , y _ { 2 r _ { 2 } } ( t )$ are uniquely determined, and so forth.

Suppose that $\mathbfcal { A } ( t )$ is a contiuous square matrix function, hence necessarily of the almost periodic type, and that $x _ { 0 } ( t ) \not \equiv 0 , x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots ,$ $x _ { r } ( t )$ are solutions of (229a) with real $\lambda$ such that

$$
y _ { \mu } ( t ) = e ^ { - \lambda t } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \mu } \frac { ( - t ) ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } x _ { \mu - \nu } ( t )
$$

is bounded for all $t$ if $\mu \leqq r$ .Then $y _ { r } ( t )$ is called，according to Cameron,a pseudo-solution of (229a) of order $\lambda$ and degree $r$ The functions $x _ { \mu } ( t )$ and $y _ { \mu } ( t )$ are called its generator and its minor of degree $\mu$ ，and $x _ { r } ( t )$ its leader.If a solution $x ( t )$ of (229a) is the leader of a pseudo-solution $y ( t )$ of order $\lambda$ and degree $r$ ，then it is called a satisfactory solution of order $\lambda$ and degree $r$ ，and $y ( t )$ is called its associated pseudo-solution. If we express

$$
e ^ { \imath t } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \mu } \frac { t ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } y _ { \mu - \nu } ( t )
$$

in terms of $x _ { 0 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { \mu } ( t )$ by (244), then we obtain $x _ { \mu } ( t )$ . Hence,if $y _ { r } ( t )$ is a pseudo-solution of order $\lambda$ and degree $r$ and has the minors $y _ { 0 } ( t ) , \ldots , y _ { r } ( t )$ and the generators $x _ { 0 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { r } ( t )$ ， then for $\mu \leqq r$ we have

$$
x _ { \mu } ( t ) = e ^ { \lambda t } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \mu } \frac { t ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } y _ { \mu - \nu } ( t ) .
$$

Hence every satisfactory solution is a primary solution.A solution $x ( t )$ which is the sum of a finite number of satisfactory solutions $x ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } ( t )$ ， $\cdots , x ^ { \left( p \right) } ( t )$ with distinct orders $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p }$ is called a decomposable solution.If  is the largest of $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p }$ ，then $x ( t )$ is said to be a decomposable solution of order λ. Thus we get

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :A solution $x ( t )$ of $( 2 2 9 \mathsf { a } )$ of the almost periodic type can be expressed in one and only one way in the form

$$
x ( t ) = \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { p } e ^ { \imath _ { \sigma } t } \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \tau _ { \sigma } } \frac { t ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } y _ { \sigma , \tau _ { \sigma } - \nu } ( t ) ,
$$

where the $\lambda _ { \sigma }$ are real and distinct, the $y _ { \sigma , \nu } ( t )$ are almost periodic,and none of the $y _ { \sigma , 0 } ( t )$ is identically zero.For each $\sigma \leq p$ the function $y _ { \sigma , \nu _ { \sigma } } ( t )$ is a pseudo-solution of (229a) of order $\lambda _ { \sigma }$ and degree $r _ { \sigma }$ with minors $y _ { \sigma , 0 } ( t ) , . . . , y _ { \sigma , r _ { \sigma } } ( t )$ . The function $x ( t )$ is decomposable.

Condition I. The system $( 2 2 9 \mathsf { a } )$ is said to satisfy Condition I if to any finite set of solutions $x ^ { ( 0 ) } ( t ) , x ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) , \ldots , x ^ { ( p ) } ( t )$ there correspond a real number $\lambda$ and a nonnegative integer $r$ such that

$$
\sum _ { v = 0 } ^ { p } t ^ { v } x ^ { ( v ) } ( t ) = O ( e ^ { \imath t } { \cdot } t ^ { r } )
$$

for $t \to + \infty$

Since Theorem 1 enables us to express solutions in terms of pseudosolutions,we study.the pseudo-solutions.

Suppose that $A ( t )$ is any continuous matrix function. Any pseudosolution has a unique order and degree and a unique set of minors and generators. The property of being a pseudo-solution of a given order is invariant under addition and under multiplication by a constant. If the absolute value of one of the minors $y _ { 0 } ( t )$ is bounded inferiorly for sufficiently large positive values of $t$ ，then we have for $t \to + \infty$

$$
x _ { \mu } ( t ) = O ^ { * } [ e ^ { 2 t } t ^ { \mu } ] .
$$

Suppose that $A ( t )$ is an almost periodic matrix function and $y _ { r } ( t )$ is a pseudo-solution of (229a) of order $\gimel$ and degree $r$ whose minors and generators are $y _ { 0 } ( t ) , \ldots , y _ { r } ( t )$ and $x _ { 0 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { r } ( t )$ respectively. Let $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots$ .be a sequence such that $\begin{array} { r } { \tilde { A } ( t ) \equiv \operatorname* { l i m } _ { i  \infty } A ( t + h _ { i } ) } \end{array}$ exists uniformly in $t$ and $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } y _ { \mu } ( h _ { i } )$ exists for each $\mu$ and does not vanish for $\mu = 0$ ，then $\begin{array} { r } { \tilde { y } _ { r } ( t ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } y _ { r } ( t + h _ { i } ) } \end{array}$ exists for all $t$ and is a pseudo-solution of order $\lambda$ and degree $r$ of the equations

$$
D [ \tilde { x } ( t ) ] = \tilde { A } ( t ) \cdot \tilde { x } ( t )
$$

and its minor of degree $\mu$ is

$$
\tilde { y } _ { \mu } ( t ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } y _ { \mu } ( t + h _ { i } ) \qquad ( \mu = 0 , 1 , \ldots , r ) ,
$$

and its generator of degree $\mu$ is

$$
\tilde { x } _ { \mu } ( t ) = \operatorname * { l i m } _ { i \to \infty } \left\{ e ^ { - \lambda h _ { i } } \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \mu } \frac { ( - h _ { i } ) ^ { \nu } } { \nu ! } x _ { \mu - \nu } ( t + h _ { i } ) \right\} \qquad ( \mu = 0 , 1 , \dots , \nu
$$

If $\boldsymbol { A } ( t )$ is almost periodic and all solutions of $( 2 2 9 \mathsf { a } )$ are of the almost periodic type,then the absolute value of every pseudo-solution of(229a) is bounded inferiorly,as can be seen by referring to Bochner's theorem.

Theorem 2: If $\mathbfcal { A } ( t )$ is an almost periodic matrix function， then a necessary condition that all solutions of (229a) be of the almost periodic type is that the system (229a) should satisfy Condition I.

Cameron proved related theorems by following Favard's method based on Bochner's normal functions (Section 14.24), among others :

Theorem 3: Assuming that $\pmb { A } ( t )$ is almost periodic with modulus contained in $M$ and that (229a) satisfies Condition I, then a pseudosolution $y ( t )$ is almost periodic with modulus contained in $M$ if and only if $y ( t )$ is a positive stationary function with respect to $M$

By means of this theorem Cameron derived several theorems on almost periodic pseudo-solutions by employing his definition on range and on symmetric,twistable,stationary,decomposable functions,and proved the necessary and sufficient condition for all solutions of (228a) to be of the almost periodic type.

Markus and Moore (1956) classified the real solutions of

$$
\frac { d } { d x } \left[ r ( x ) \frac { d y } { d x } \right] + K ( x ) \cdot y = 0 ,
$$

where $r ( x ) > 0$ and $K ( x )$ are real continuous in $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ， into the oscillatory,for which the solution vanishes for an arbitrarily large abscissa,and the nonoscillatory, for which the solution never vanishes.

Lillo (1959) discussed the perturbation problem for the continuing of real almost periodic solutions of the real nonlinear differential equation

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } = F ( z , \mu , t ) .
$$

Assume that for $\mu = \mu _ { 0 }$ the equation (246) possesses a stable almost periodic solution $p ( \mu , t )$ ，and also that $F ( z , \mu , t )$ for a fixed $\mu$ is almost periodic in $t$ uniformly with respect to $z$ in acylindrical neighborhood of $p ( \mu _ { 0 } , t )$ of radius $\gamma > 0$ . A function $F ( z , \mu , t )$ for a fixed $\pmb { \mu }$ is said to be almost periodic in $t$ uniformly with respect to $z$ in a set $\pmb R$ if for any $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a relatively dense set $S ( \varepsilon )$ such that, if $z$ belongs to $\pmb R$ and $\pmb { \tau }$ belongs to $s$ ,then $\tau$ is an $\varepsilon ^ { \cdot }$ -translation number of $F ( z , \mu , t )$ Lillo showed that for $\mu$ nearly equal to $\pmb { \mu } _ { 0 }$ there exists a unique stable almost periodic solution $p ( \mu , t )$ nearly equal to $p ( \mu _ { 0 } , t )$ .Using geometrical considerations, he discussed the equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \mu f ( x ) \frac { d x } { d t } + g ( x ) = 0 ,
$$

where $f ( x )$ and $g ( x )$ are real almost periodic and $\mu$ is a real parameter, and showed that the behavior of this equation is analogous to the classical pendulum problem

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } } + \mu { \frac { d x } { d t } } + B - \sin x = 0 .
$$

He further discussed the almost periodic solution of

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } + g ( t ) \frac { d x } { d t } + p ( \mu , t ) = 0 ,
$$

where $g ( t )$ and $p ( \mu , t )$ are almost periodic.

The differential equations of perturbation theory cannot in general satisfy any of the foregoing requirements. Hence the solution cannot in general be a quasi-periodic function or an almost periodic function which is representable by uniformly convergent trigonometric series with arguments proportional to time,so that the motion cannot be said to be stable in the sense of Poisson.

# Problem of Uniform Convergence

# BOREL SERIES AND LIOUVILLE NUMBERS

# 15.IBOREL SERIES

If every part of a point-set $\mathfrak { X }$ with an infinite number of points $\{ a _ { n } \}$ has at least a limiting point (Häufungspunkt), then $\mathfrak { X }$ is called compact. In order that a point-sequence $\left\{ a _ { n } \right\}$ of a compact set $\mathfrak { X }$ shall have a limit-point $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } a _ { n } = a$ ,it is necessary and suffcient that $\left\{ a _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right\}$ have a single limiting point.A point-set $\mathfrak { X }$ is called closed if it contains all its limiting points. The complementary set of a closed set is called open. The sum-set (Vereinigung) of a finite number of closed sets is closed (Section 2.1).The sum-set of a finite or infinite number of open sets is open. The product-set (Durchschnitt) of a finite number of open sets is open.The product-set of a finite or infinite number of closed sets is closed. Suppose that the point-set $\mathfrak { X }$ is in a metric space $\Re$ with metric $r ( a , a ^ { \prime } )$ for the distance between two points $^ { a }$ and $\scriptstyle { a ^ { \prime } }$ .If $\mathfrak { A }$ and $\mathfrak { B }$ are two nonzero (nicht leer),distinct, closed sets,of which at least one is compact, then $r ( \mathfrak { A } , \mathfrak { B } ) > 0$

Every open point-set which contains a point $^ { a }$ or a set $\mathfrak { X }$ is called a neighborhood of $^ { a }$ or $\mathfrak { X }$ ，and denoted by $\mathfrak { U } ( a )$ or l(). A set of all points $^ { b }$ of space $\Re$ whose distance from $^ { a }$ or $\mathfrak { X }$ satisfies $r ( a , b ) < \rho$ or $r ( \mathfrak { A } , b ) < \rho _ { \mathrm { : } }$ ， $\rho > 0$ ，is a neighborhood of $^ a$ or of $\mathfrak { X }$ ， and is a closed set. In order that $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } a _ { n } = a$ it is necessary and sufficient that almost all $a _ { n }$ lie in every neighborhood of $\mathfrak { U } ( a )$ . “Almost all ${ a _ { n } } ^ { \dag }$ means with the exception of a denumerable number of $a _ { n }$ . In order that $^ { a }$ shall be a limiting point of a set $\mathfrak { X }$ it is necessary and sufficient that an infinite number of points of $\mathfrak { X }$ lie in every neighborhood of $\mathfrak { U } ( a )$ . Denote by ${ \mathfrak { A } } ^ { \prime }$ the set of all limiting points of $\mathfrak { X }$ . This is called the derived set of $\mathfrak { X }$ . The set ${ \mathfrak { A } } ^ { \prime }$ is closed. The sum-set $\overleftarrow { \mathfrak { A } }$ of $\mathfrak { A }$ and ${ \mathfrak { A } } ^ { \prime }$ is closed and is called the closure or hull (Hulle) of Y.

If every point of $\mathfrak { B }$ is a point or a limiting point of the product set YB, then a set $\mathfrak { X }$ is called dense in B.A set which is dense in $\mathfrak { R }$ is called everywhere dense (Section 2.1). Any point of $\mathfrak { X }$ which is not a limiting point of $\mathfrak { X }$ is called an isolated point of &. If all points of $\mathfrak { X }$ are isolated, then $\mathfrak { X }$ is called an isolated set.On the other hand,a set $\mathfrak { X }$ ,every point of which is a limiting point of $\mathfrak { X }$ ,is called dense in itself. A set which is closed and dense is said to be perfect. If a set $\mathfrak { X }$ is perfect,then all its derived sets are identical with $\mathfrak { X }$ . A closure $\overleftarrow { \mathfrak { A } }$ of a set $\mathfrak { X }$ that is dense in itself is perfect.If there is no part $\mathfrak { B } ^ { \prime }$ of $\mathfrak { B }$ which is nonzero and open in $\mathfrak { B }$ such that $\mathfrak { X }$ is dense in $\mathfrak { B ^ { \prime } }$ ，then $\mathfrak { X }$ is called nowhere dense in B. The sum-set of all parts of a set & that are dense in themselves is also dense in itself.It is called the nucleus which is dense in itself.If such a nucleus is a zero set, then the set $\mathfrak { X }$ is called separable.

A set $\mathfrak { A }$ is called connected if it is not the sum of two nonzero parts which are closed in $\mathfrak { Y }$ . Two points $^ a$ and $a ^ { \prime }$ of $\mathfrak { X }$ are said to be connected by a $\rho$ -chain, if there is a finite point-sequence $a = a _ { 0 } , a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } { \mathrm { _ { \Omega } } }$ $\dots , a _ { k - 1 } , a _ { k } = a ^ { \prime }$ such that $r ( a _ { i - 1 } , a _ { i } ) \leqq \rho$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \dots , k )$ . A closed connected set which does not consist of only a single point is called a continuum,and an open connected set is called a domain (Gebiet). A part ${ \mathfrak { A } } ^ { \prime }$ of a set $\mathfrak { Y }$ is called a component of $\mathfrak { X }$ ，if ${ \mathfrak { A } } ^ { \prime }$ is connected and each connected part of $\mathfrak { X }$ which is not distinct from ${ \mathfrak { A } } ^ { \prime }$ is also a part of $\mathfrak { Y }$ . Each point of $\mathfrak { X }$ belongs to one and only one component of $\mathfrak { X }$ Each set $\mathfrak { X }$ is in one and only one manner the sum of its components, and each component of $\mathfrak { X }$ is closed in $\mathfrak { A }$ and consists of a single point or a continuum. If a set $\mathfrak { B } _ { \alpha }$ corresponds to every point $^ { a }$ of a compact closed set $\mathfrak { A }$ , in which $\pmb { a }$ is an inner point of $\mathfrak { B } _ { \alpha }$ , then there are among the sets $\mathfrak { B } _ { \alpha }$ a finite number of sets $\mathfrak { B } ^ { ( 1 ) } , \mathfrak { B } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ ,..., $\mathfrak { B } ^ { ( k ) }$ such that any point of $\mathfrak { Y }$ is an inner point of at least one of this finite number of sets. This is called Heine-Borel's covering theorem (Uberdeckungs-satz).

A set is separable if it is a denumerable set that is dense in itself. A separable set is of the power (Mächtigkeit) of continuum or less. If to any positive number $\varepsilon$ there is an integer $_ n$ such that we have $r ( a _ { n ^ { \prime } } , a _ { n ^ { \prime \prime } } ) < \varepsilon$ for $n ^ { \prime } \geq n$ ， $n ^ { \prime \prime } \geq n$ , then a point-sequence $\left\{ a _ { \mathfrak { n } } \right\}$ is called a Cauchy sequence. Any set which converges to a point $a$ is a Cauchy sequence.If any Cauchy sequence $\left\{ a _ { n } \right\}$ of $\mathfrak { X }$ has a limiting point belonging to $\mathfrak { A }$ ，then $\mathfrak { X }$ is called complete (vollständig).A complete set is closed. A closed and conmpact set is complete. If there are non-denumerable parts of $\mathfrak { A }$ in every neighborhood $\mathfrak { U } ( a )$ of $a$ ，then $^ { a }$ is called a condensation-point of $\mathfrak { X }$ . A set of all condensation-points of $\mathfrak { X }$ is closed (Sierpinski, 1928,1934; Kestelman,1937; Section 2.1).

Let a real number $f ( a )$ correspond to an element $^ a$ of a point-set $\mathfrak { X }$ Then we say that a real function $f ( a )$ is defined by this correspondence on $\mathfrak { A }$ . A real function on $\mathfrak { A }$ is nothing but a representation of a pointset $\mathfrak { X }$ in the set of all real numbers.A function $f$ defined on a pointset $\mathfrak { X }$ is called continuous on $\mathfrak { X }$ at a point $a$ of $\mathfrak { A }$ , if for each pointsequence $\{ a _ { n } \}$ from $\mathfrak { X }$ with the limiting point $\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } a _ { n } = a$ we have

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } f ( a _ { n } ) = f ( a ) .
$$

If a function $f$ is not continuous at $^ a$ on $\mathfrak { X }$ , then it is discontinuous at $\pmb { a }$ on $\mathfrak { X }$ .Each point at which $f$ is continuous on $\mathfrak { A }$ is called a continuitypoint of $f$ on $\mathfrak { A }$ . The distance $r ( a , { \bar { a } } )$ of a point $^ { a }$ from another fixed point $\bar { a }$ is a continuous function of $^ { a }$ in the whole space $\Re$ .If $\mathfrak { X }$ is compact and closed, then any finite and continuous function $f \ \mathrm { o n } \ \mathfrak { A }$ is bounded,and there isa point $^ { a }$ in $\mathfrak { X }$ for each point $^ { b }$ of $\mathfrak { R }$ such that $r ( b , \mathfrak { A } ) = r ( b , a )$ . In order that $f$ shall be continuous on $\mathfrak { X }$ , it is necessary and sufficient that for every number $^ c$ the set of all points of $\mathfrak { Y }$ with $f \geq c$ and the set of all points of $\mathfrak { X }$ with $f \leq { } c$ be both closed in $\mathfrak { A }$ .If $f$ is continuous on $\mathfrak { Y }$ ，then for every $^ c$ the set of all points of $\mathfrak { X }$ with $f = c$ is closed in $\mathfrak { A }$ . A bounded function $f$ is called uniformly continuous on $\mathfrak { X }$ ,if there is a number $\rho > 0$ for every $\varepsilon > 0$ such that, for each pair $a ^ { \prime }$ and $a ^ { \prime \prime }$ of $\mathfrak { X }$ whose distance $r ( a ^ { \prime } , a ^ { \prime \prime } ) < \rho$ ，we have the inequality

$$
| f ( a ^ { \prime } ) - f ( a ^ { \prime \prime } ) | < \varepsilon .
$$

If $\mathfrak { X }$ is compact and closed, then every continuous function on $\mathfrak { X }$ is uniformly continuous on $\mathfrak { A }$ (Section 2.1).

Let an infinite sequence of functions $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \dots , f _ { \nu } , \dots .$ be given on $\mathfrak { A }$ and be denoted by $\{ f _ { \nu } \}$ . A sequence $\{ f _ { \nu } \}$ given on $\mathfrak { A }$ is called properly uniformly convergent (eigentlich gleichmassig konvergent) on $\mathfrak { A }$ at a point $^ a$ of $\bar { \mathfrak { A } }$ ，if for every point-set $\left\{ a _ { n } \right\}$ from $\mathfrak { Y }$ with $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } a _ { n } = a$ we have

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { \stackrel { n \to \infty } { \nu , \nu ^ { \prime } \to \infty } } \{ f _ { \nu ^ { \prime } } ( a _ { n } ) - f _ { \nu } ( a _ { n } ) \} = 0 ;
$$

or,in other words,if there are,for every sequence $\{ a _ { n } \}$ from $^ { a }$ with $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } a _ { n } = a$ and for every $\varepsilon > 0$ ，an index $n _ { 0 }$ and an index $\nu _ { 0 }$ such that for $n \geq n _ { 0 } , \ \nu \geq \nu _ { 0 } , \ \nu ^ { \prime } \geq \nu _ { 0 } .$ we have

$$
| f _ { \nu ^ { \prime } } ( a _ { n } ) - f _ { \nu } ( a _ { n } ) | < \varepsilon .
$$

In order that $\{ f _ { \nu } \}$ at a point $^ a$ of $\overbar { \mathfrak { A } }$ shall be properly uniformly con-

vergent on $\mathfrak { X }$ , it is necessary and sufficient that for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there be a neighborhood $\mathfrak { U }$ of $^ { a }$ in $\mathfrak { Y }$ and an index $\nu _ { 0 }$ such that

$$
| f _ { \nu ^ { \prime } } ( a ^ { \prime } ) - f _ { \nu } ( a ^ { \prime } ) | < \varepsilon
$$

for every $a ^ { \prime }$ of $\mathfrak { U }$ and for all $\nu ^ { \prime } \geq \nu _ { 0 }$ ， $\nu \geq \nu _ { 0 }$ . If a sequence $\{ f _ { \upsilon } \}$ is convergent in a neighborhood of $^ { a }$ of $\mathfrak { A }$ ，then,in order that it shall converge to its limit-function $f$ properly uniformly convergent in $a$ of $\mathfrak { X }$ ， it is necessary and sufficient that there be an index $\nu _ { 0 }$ and an index $n _ { 0 }$ for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and for every sequence $\left\{ a _ { n } \right\}$ in $\mathfrak { A }$ with $\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } a _ { n } = a _ { \mathrm { { \ O } } }$ such that

$$
| f _ { \nu } ( a _ { n } ) - f ( a _ { n } ) | < \varepsilon
$$

for $n \geq n _ { 0 } , \nu \geq \nu _ { 0 }$ In order that $\{ f _ { \nu } \}$ shall be properly uniformly convergent on $\mathfrak { X }$ it is necessary and sufficient that there exists a finite function $f$ on $\mathfrak { X }$ such that for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and for almost all $\nu$ on the whole $\mathfrak { A }$ we have

$$
| f _ { \nu } ( a ) - f ( a ) | < \varepsilon .
$$

If a sequence $\{ f _ { \nu } \}$ is uniformly convergent on $\mathfrak { A }$ ，then its limit function is continuous on $\mathfrak { A }$ at every point of $\mathfrak { X }$ in which an infinite number of $f _ { y }$ are continuous on $\mathfrak { A }$ ，and has a limiting value on $\mathfrak { X }$ at every point of ${ \mathfrak { A } } ^ { 1 }$ in which an infinite number of $f _ { y }$ have limiting values on X.

A convergent sequence of functions may provide a very useful tool for analytical representation of functions that show a preassigned singular character. We are interested in the principle of condensation (Verdichtung) of singularities.The problem is to derive from an analytical expression with singular character at a given point an analytical expression which shows the same singular character in an infinite point-set. Let $\phi ( y )$ be a function of a real variable y showing singular character at a certain point $y = 0$ .Put

$$
s _ { \nu } ( x ) = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \nu } A _ { k } \phi ( \sin k \pi x ) ,
$$

$$
s ( x ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { y \to \infty } s _ { \nu } ( x ) = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } A _ { k } \phi ( \sin k \pi x ) .
$$

Since $\phi \left( \sin { k \pi x } \right)$ shows at all rational points with the numerator $k$ the singular character that $\phi$ shows at $y = 0$ ，the function $s ( x )$ can be expected to show the same singular character at all rational points of the $x \cdot$ -axis,at least by a suitable choice of the coeffcients $\pmb { A } _ { k }$ . This solution due to Hankel has the following defects: (i) since the infinite number of terms of $s ( x )$ shows the singular character in question at rational points $p / q$ ,such singularities may be distinct from each other; (ii) the introduction of the sine function may cause an unnecessary complication that has nothing to do with the real nature of the problem； and (ii) the set of points to which the singular character is transferred may be of special nature.

Cantor's solution is as follows. Consider a linear space $\Re _ { 1 }$ , in which any domain $\Delta$ reduces to an open or closed interval. Let $\xi _ { k }$ $( k = 1$ ， 2,...） be an arbitrary denumerable point-set of $\Re _ { 1 }$ and let $\phi ( y )$ be a a function showing the given singular character at $y = 0$ . The function

$$
s ( x ) = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } A _ { k } \phi ( x - \xi _ { k } )
$$

may be expected to show the singular character in question at every point $\xi _ { k }$ at least with a suitable choice of the coefficients $\scriptstyle A _ { k }$ .If $\phi ( y )$ is a bounded function discontinuous at $y = 0$ but otherwise continuous,and if $\scriptstyle \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } ( A _ { k } )$ is properly convergent, then the above series of Cantor converges properly uniformly. Then the function $s ( x )$ represented by this series is continuous at all points on the $x \cdot$ -axis except at the points $\xi _ { k }$ , by the theorem on the continuity of the limit function of a finite sequence.If we drop the kth term from the above series, then the remaining series represents a function continuous at the points $\xi _ { k }$ ， so that $s ( x )$ shows at the points $\xi _ { \kappa }$ the same discontinuity as $\phi ( y )$ at $y = 0$

For example, take Riemann's function $( x )$ which has a period 1 in the closed interval [0,1], such that $( x ) = x$ in [0,1/2), $( 1 / 2 ) = 0$ ， and $( x ) = x - 1$ in (1/2,1] (Riemann, 1892). The series

$$
s ( x ) = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { ( \nu x ) } { \nu ^ { 2 } } }
$$

converges properly uniformly. The sum $s ( x )$ is discontinuous only at points where at least one of the terms is discontinuous,i.e.,at the rational points $x = \pm ( 2 m + 1 ) / ( 2 n )$

Another example is Brodén's analytical representation of a pointwise discontinuous function (Sections 2.1 and 15.2)，all continuity-points of which are zero-points. Let $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , \ldots , l _ { \ast } , \ldots$ be given integers $\geq 2$ Any real number $x$ can be represented by

$$
x = \varepsilon _ { 0 } + \frac { \varepsilon _ { 1 } } { l _ { 1 } } + \frac { \varepsilon _ { 2 } } { l _ { 1 } l _ { 2 } } + \cdots + \frac { \varepsilon _ { \ast } } { l _ { 1 } l _ { 2 } \cdots l _ { \ast } } + \cdots ,
$$

where $\varepsilon _ { 0 }$ is an integer and $\mathfrak { E } _ { \nu } , \nu \ne 1$ ， are integers such that

$$
0 \leq \varepsilon _ { \nu } \leq l _ { \nu } - 1 .
$$

Put

$$
L _ { \nu } = l _ { 1 } l _ { 2 } \cdots l _ { \nu } ,
$$

and let $\pmb { R } ( \pmb { x } )$ be the distance of a number $x$ from the integer immediately following. When the coefficient $\pmb { \varepsilon } _ { \nu + 1 }$ has one of the values 0,1, $l _ { \nu + 1 } - 2 , l _ { \nu + 1 } - 1$ ， then

$$
R ( L , x ) \mathop \leq _ { l _ { y } + 1 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

If all the $l _ { \nu }$ are odd and if we construct a function $_ { 2 \pi L , x }$ ，then its zeropoints will fill a linear space (Section 16.18) $\Re _ { 1 }$ everywhere densely $\yen 1$ increases.Brodén considered the product of these functions

$$
p ( x ) = \prod _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \infty } \phi \left( \cos 2 \pi L , x \right) ,
$$

where

$$
\phi ( y ) = y e ^ { y - 1 }
$$

converges to a finite value if all $| y _ { \nu } | \leq 1$ ；and this limiting value is either $\neq 0 \ \mathrm { o r } = 0$ according as $\scriptstyle \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \infty } ( 1 - y _ { \nu } )$ is properly convergent or not, provided that all $y _ { \nu } \neq 0$ . Hence the product converges for all $x$ to a finite value. It can be seen that its zero-points are all continuitypoints and that the values of $x$ for which $p ( x ) > 0$ and the values of $x$ for which $p ( x ) < 0$ are dense on the $x$ -axis.

Cantor's condensation process is related to the behavior of Borel's series.Let $\xi _ { \nu }$ $( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ be an arbitrary denumerable point-set $\mathfrak { X }$ of $\Re _ { 1 }$ ,let $m _ { \nu }$ $( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ be a bounded sequence of positive numbers satisfying $m _ { \nu } \equiv m _ { \nu } ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . )$ and let $\sum { \infty } _ { \bf u = 1 } A _ { \nu }$ be a series of positive numbers with finite sum.Consider a Borel's series

$$
s ( x ) = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { A _ { \nu } } { | x - \xi _ { \nu } | ^ { m _ { \nu } } } } \qquad ( m _ { \nu } \leq m ) .
$$

Suppose that $| x - \xi _ { \nu } | ^ { - m _ { \nu } }$ takes the value $+ \infty$ for $x = \xi ,$ ， then this series has the value $+ \infty$ at all points of $\mathfrak { X }$ and is properly convergent at all points not belonging to $\overleftarrow { \mathfrak { A } }$ . Since a partial sum of this series is continuous, the set $\mathfrak { B } _ { \nu } ( k )$ of all points,such that the vth partial sum $s ( x ) > k$ ， is open.Hence the set $\mathfrak { B } _ { \kappa }$ of all points at which $s ( x ) > k$ ， as the sum-set of all ${ \mathfrak { B } } _ { \nu } ( k ) \ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ is also open. Hence the set of all points $s ( x ) = + \infty$ is the product-set of denumerable open sets, as it is the product-set of the sets $\mathfrak { B } _ { n }$ $( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ . If $\mathfrak { X }$ ，and hence $\mathfrak { B }$ ，has a part which is dense in itself, then $\mathfrak { B }$ is of the power of continuum.Hence,in addition to the denumerably infinite number of points of $\mathfrak { X }$ ，there is still a set of the power of continuum of points for which $s ( x ) = + \infty$ .

By a suitable choice of the coefficients $A _ { y }$ we obtain a further property of the set $\mathfrak { B }$ .Let $\sum \infty _ { = 1 } u ,$ be a properly convergent set of positive numbers.In all points at which

$$
\frac { A _ { \nu } } { | x - \xi _ { \nu } | ^ { m _ { \nu } } } \cong u _ { \nu }
$$

for all $\nu$ ，or,in other words,at which

$$
| x - \xi _ { \nu } | \geq \left( \frac { A _ { \nu } } { u _ { \nu } } \right) ^ { 1 / m } \equiv v _ { \nu } ,
$$

Borel's series is properly convergent, that is,at any point $x$ not in the interval $( \xi _ { \nu } - v _ { \nu } , \xi _ { \nu } + v _ { \nu } )$ $( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ ，Borel's series is properly convergent. If A $, ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ are positive numbers for which $\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \infty } A _ { \nu } ^ { 1 / ( m + 1 ) }$ is properly convergent,and if the $m _ { \nu }$ are positive and $\leq m$ ， then Borel's series is almost everywhere properly convergent (Borel,1928,1950；Cαrathéodory,1948；Hahn,1921,1932；Hausdorff,1914；Hobson，1926，1927；Lusin，1930；Vallée Poussin, 1935；Hardy,1949；Beckenbach and Bellman,1961).

# 15.2CLASS OF BAIRE

The measure of a point-set $\mathfrak { A }$ is a positive number or zero attached to $\mathfrak { X }$ and dependent on $\mathfrak { X }$ ,and is a function of the set X.A measure is additive even in the extension to a denumerably infinite number of sets.We have a preliminary lemma that, if a bounded linear point-set can be considered in two different ways as the sum of a denumerably infinite number of intervals without any duplicated point, then the sum of the measure of the intervals is the same for the two cases.Thus the measure $m ( { \mathfrak { A } } )$ of point-set $\mathfrak { X }$ is the sum of the component-intervals $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } , \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 }$ ，....Whether the intervals are without any duplicated point or not, we have for open sets

$$
m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } + \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \subseteq m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } ) + m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

If $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathfrak { A } _ { 2 }$ have common points, then

$$
m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } ) + m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } ) = m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } + \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } ) + m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } . \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } ) .
$$

The measure of a closed set $\mathfrak { X }$ is the complement of its complementary set. If an open set $\mathfrak { X }$ contains a closed subset $\mathfrak { B }$ ，then $\mathfrak { A } - \mathfrak { B }$ is open and $m ( \mathfrak { A } - \mathfrak { B } ) = m ( \mathfrak { A } ) - m ( \mathfrak { B } )$ . Any open set contains a closed set of measure infinitely near its measure.Reciprocally,any closed set is contained in an open set of measure infinitely near its measure. Thus the measure of a closed set is the lower bound of the measure of the open sets containing the closed set. The sum of a denumerably infinite number of open sets $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } , \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 }$ ，... is an open set $\mathfrak { X }$ and we have

$$
m ( \mathfrak { A } ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } + \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) .
$$

These properties can be extended to spaces of more than one dimension.

A point-set $\mathfrak { A }$ is measurable if we can make $\varepsilon > 0$ correspond to an open set $\Re > \Re$ and a closed set $\mathfrak { C } < \mathfrak { T }$ ， such that $m ( \mathfrak { B } - \mathfrak { C } ) < \mathfrak { \varepsilon }$ The measure $m ( { \mathfrak { A } } )$ is the common limit of $m ( { \mathfrak { A } } )$ and $m ( \mathfrak { E } )$ as $\varepsilon$ tends to zero.If a measurable set $\mathfrak { A }$ contains a measurable set $\mathfrak { B }$ ，then $m ( \mathfrak { A } ) \geq m ( \mathfrak { B } )$ . The sum-set and the product-set of measurable sets are also measurable. The sum-set and the product-set of a denumerably infinite number of measurable sets are measurable,and the measure is completely additive.Thus an open or a closed set is measurable and the sets obtained by a repeated application of addition, subtraction,and multiplication, even a denumerably infinite number of times,are measurable.The family of such sets constitutes a closed field. Consider an infinite sequence of measurable sets satisfyig

$$
\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } < \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } < \mathfrak { A } _ { 3 } < \cdots ,
$$

then

$$
\operatorname * { l i m } _ { n  \infty } ( m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { n } ) ) = m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } + \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) ;
$$

if they satisfy $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } > \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } > \mathfrak { A } _ { 3 } > \cdots$ ， then

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n  \infty } ( m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { n } ) ) = m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } \cdot \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot ) .
$$

If this infinite sequence has a unique limit $\mathfrak { X }$ , then the limit is measurable and

$$
m ( \mathfrak { A } ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n  \infty } ( m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { n } ) ) .
$$

Let $f ( P )$ be a function of the coordinates of a point $P$ variable in a measurable set $\mathfrak { X }$ ,and suppose that $f ( P )$ admits a unique value at every point of $\mathfrak { X }$ . Denote by $\mathfrak { A } ( { \mathfrak { f } } \geq A )$ ， $\Im ( f < A )$ ,... the point-sets of $\mathfrak { X }$ where $f$ satisfies the indicated condition. If at least one of the four sets $\ P ( f \geq A )$ ， $\Im ( f < A )$ ， $\Im ( f > A )$ ， $\mathfrak { A } ( { \mathfrak { f } } \leq A )$ ）is measurable for any constant $\pmb { A }$ ， then the function $f ( A )$ is measurable in $\mathfrak { X }$ . The four sets are all measurable. If $f$ is measurable, then $a + f , a f$ ， $a - f$ are also measurable,where $a$ is a constant. If $f$ and $\phi$ are finite and measurable, then the set $\mathfrak { A } ( f > \phi )$ is measurable,and $f + \phi , f - \phi , f \phi$ are measurable. The limit of a denumerable sequence $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \dots , f _ { n } , \dots$ of measurable functions is measurable.

Such a point-set,as constructed by denumerably infinite operations of addition，subtraction，and multiplication,is called a point-set measurable $( B )$ . It contains open sets and closed sets obtained by addition，multiplication,and subtraction，and these sets constitute a closed field.A point-set measurable $( B )$ is constructed from closed domains by addition and multiplication. Any measurable set $\mathfrak { X }$ is an intermediary of two sets measurable $( B )$ of the same measure,that is, $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } < \mathfrak { A } < \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 }$ ， $m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 1 } ) = m ( \mathfrak { A } ) = m ( \mathfrak { A } _ { 2 } )$ . When a set is measurable $( B )$ , then $f$ is said to be measurable in the domain. The sum, difference, product,and limit of functions measurable $( B )$ are measurable $( B )$ . We have the theorem that a bounded and measurable function $f$ is intermediary between two functions measurable $\left( B \right)$ that differ from $f$ by a set of measure zero.

For various sequences $\left\{ a _ { n } \right\}$ converging to a point $a$ of $\mathfrak { X }$ we denote the superior limit of $f ( a _ { n } )$ by $\begin{array} { r } { \overline { { \operatorname* { l i m } } } _ { n \to \infty } f ( \boldsymbol { a } _ { n } ) = G ( \boldsymbol { a } ; \boldsymbol { f } , \mathfrak { A } ) } \end{array}$ and the inferior limit by $\begin{array} { r } { \underline { { \operatorname* { l i m } } } _ { n \to \infty } f ( a _ { n } ) = g ( a ; f , \mathfrak { A } ) } \end{array}$ .If $G ( a ; f , \mathfrak { X } ) = g ( a ; f , \mathfrak { Y } )$ ，then $f$ is continuous at $^ { a }$ on $\mathfrak { Y }$ . A function, continuous at every point of $\mathfrak { X }$ ， such that $G ( f , \mathfrak { V } ) = g ( f , \mathfrak { V } )$ , is continuous on X. We call $\omega ( a ; f , \mathfrak { A } ) =$ $G ( a ; f , \mathfrak { X } ) - g ( a ; f , \mathfrak { X } )$ the fluctuation (Schwankung) of $f$ on $\mathfrak { A }$ In order that we shall have $\omega ( a ; f , \mathfrak { W } ) = 0$ it is necessary and sufficient that $f$ be continuous at $a$ with respect to $\mathfrak { A }$ . Otherwise $f$ is discontinuous.A set of points,at which the fluctuation of $f$ is larger or equal to $\varepsilon$ ， is closed.Hence a set of discontinuous points of $f$ on $\mathfrak { X }$ is a closed set.

A finite but discontinuous function $f$ was called by Baire totally discontinuous (totalement discontinue； Sections 2.1 and 2.5) if the points of discontinuity of $f$ are compact, and pointwise discontinuous if they are not compact. If $f$ is totally discontinuous on $\mathfrak { X }$ ，then one of the sets $E _ { k }$ of the points of discontinuity,which should be closed, is compact on $\mathfrak { A }$ .Reciprocally， if none of the $E _ { k }$ is compact on $\mathfrak { A }$ ， then the finite function $f$ is pointwise discontinuous on $\mathfrak { X }$ (Vallée Poussin, 1935).

According to Baire a continuous function is said to be of class $o$ ； a discontinuous function obtained as the limit of continuous functions is said to be of class 1；the limit of functions of class 1 which is neither of class 1 nor of class O is said to be of class 2;and so on.The limit of the increasing number of classes obtained in this manner is a class transfinite $\omega$ . Those functions obtained up to this stage are said to be of the first species.The limits obtained by further applications of such processes to the $\omega$ -limit are said to be of the second species $\omega + 1$ ， ε+2,..., ${ 2 \omega }$ ，..., $\omega ^ { 2 }$ ,.... Functions of these classes are called functions of Baire.Any function of Baire is measurable $( B )$ . Conversely，any function measurable $( B )$ is a function of Baire. Any set that is the limit of sets of class $< \alpha$ ，is of class $\alpha$ at most. The sum, difference,and finite product of sets of ${ \mathsf { c l a s s } } \leq \alpha$ are of ${ \mathsf { c l a s s } } \leq \alpha$ .A set of class 1 is at the same time the limit of open sets and the limit of closed sets.A set of class 1 is at the same time the product of open sets and the sum of closed sets.

A function is at most of class 1 on a denumerable point set.A function that differs from a function of class 1 at a denumerable number of points is at most of class 2. The superior limit $\scriptstyle { \overline { { \operatorname* { l i m } } } } _ { n \to \infty } f _ { n }$ and the inferior limit $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } f _ { n }$ of a sequence of continuous functions $\left\{ f _ { \pi } \right\}$ on $\mathfrak { X }$ are at most of class 2 on &. If a bounded function $f$ is not of class $\leq 1$ on a perfect set $\mathfrak { X }$ ，then $\mathfrak { X }$ forms a perfect set $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 }$ ，on which $f$ is totally discontinuous.Conversely，if there exists a perfect set $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 }$ contained in $\mathfrak { X }$ on which a finite function $f$ is totally discontinuous, then $f$ is not of class 1 on $\mathfrak { X }$ . Hence the necessary and sufficient condition that a finite and discontinuous function $f$ should be, as was proved by Baire,of class 1 on a bounded and perfect set $\mathfrak { A }$ is that $f$ be pointwise discontinuous on all perfect sets $\mathfrak { A } _ { 1 }$ contained in $\mathfrak { Y }$

Let $f ( P )$ be a function of a point $P$ ,bounded and measurable in a finite and measurable point-set $\mathfrak { X }$ . Denote by $\pmb { A }$ and $\pmb { B }$ the bounds of $f$ and divide the interval $[ A , B ]$ into $_ n$ subintervals at the points :

$$
A = l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , l _ { 3 } , \ldots , l _ { n } , l _ { n + 1 } = B .
$$

Denote by $e _ { i }$ the measure of the point-set of $\mathfrak { X }$ where $l _ { i } \le f < l _ { i + 1 }$ .Let

$$
s = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } l _ { i } e _ { i } , \qquad S = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } l _ { i + 1 } e _ { i } , \qquad s \leq S .
$$

There exists the common limit of the two sums when the two consecutive numbers $l _ { i }$ tend to be infinitely equal to each other.This limit is called the Lebesgue integral of $f ( P )$ in $\mathfrak { X }$ ，and is denoted by

$$
\int _ { \mathfrak { g } } f ( P ) \ d P
$$

(Section 2.1；Kamke,1960; Lebesgue, 1928；Vallée Poussin,1935;   
Saks,1933).

# 15.3LIOUVILLE'S NUMBERS

Consider a continued fraction (Borel， 1905， 1928； 1952;Minkowski, 1907,1925；Perron,1921,1929； Koksma,1936;Wall,1948;Cassels, 1959)

$$
b _ { 0 } + \frac { a _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } + \frac { a _ { 2 } } { b _ { 2 } + \frac { a _ { 3 } } { b _ { 3 } + } } } = \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } ,
$$

$$
+ \frac { a _ { n } } { b _ { n } }
$$

or

$$
b _ { 0 } + \frac { a _ { 1 } } { | b _ { 1 } } + \frac { a _ { 2 } | } { | b _ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { a _ { n } | } { | b _ { n } } = \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } ,
$$

where $b _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ is called the initial term, $^ { b _ { \nu } }$ the partial denominator, and $\frac { a _ { \nu } | } { | b _ { \nu } }$ a term of the continued fraction, and $b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \ldots , \ a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ the elements. The successive convergents or reduced fractions (Naherungsbriichen) are

$$
\frac { A _ { \circ } } { B _ { \circ } } = b _ { \circ } = \frac { b _ { \circ } } { 1 } ,
$$

$$
A _ { 0 } = b _ { \mathfrak { o } } ,
$$

$$
\boldsymbol { B } _ { 0 } = 1 ;
$$

$$
\frac { A _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } { B _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } = b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } + \frac { a _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } { b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } = \frac { b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } + a _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } { b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } ,
$$

$$
A _ { 1 } = b _ { 0 } b _ { 1 } + a _ { 1 } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { B _ { 1 } = b _ { 1 } ; } \end{array}
$$

$$
\frac { A _ { 2 } } { B _ { 2 } } = \frac { ( b _ { 0 } b _ { 1 } + a _ { 1 } ) b _ { 2 } + b _ { 0 } a _ { 2 } } { b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + a _ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
A _ { 2 } = ( b _ { 0 } b _ { 1 } + a _ { 1 } ) b _ { 2 } + b _ { 0 } a _ { 2 } , B _ { 2 } = b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + a _ { 2 } ;
$$

where

$$
A _ { 2 } = b _ { 2 } A _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } A _ { 0 } , \qquad B _ { 2 } = b _ { 2 } B _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } B _ { 0 } ;
$$

or generally

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { \cal A } _ { n } = b _ { n } { \cal A } _ { n - 1 } + a _ { n } { \cal A } _ { n - 2 } , } & { { } { \cal B } _ { n } = b _ { n } { \cal B } _ { n - 1 } + a _ { n } { \cal B } _ { n - 2 } \qquad ( n \geq 2 ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \dot { \cal A } _ { n } } { { \cal B } _ { n } } = \frac { b _ { n } { \cal A } _ { n - 1 } + a _ { n } { \cal A } _ { n - 2 } } { b _ { n } { \cal B } _ { n - 1 } + a _ { n } { \cal B } _ { n - 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

If a reduced fraction $A _ { n } / B _ { n }$ has its limiting value $\xi$ so that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n  \infty } { \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } } = \xi ,
$$

then $A _ { n } / B _ { n }$ is said to be convergent；if the limit is zero,then $A _ { n } / B _ { n }$ is said to be non-essentially (unwesentlich) divergent； otherwise essentially divergent.A continued fraction with its partial numerator $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots$ 、all equal to 1,and its partial denominators $b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 }$ ... all positive integers (the initial term $b _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ being excepted)，is called regular (regelmässig). Such a continued fraction is expressed by $\xi _ { 0 } = [ b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } ,$ $\boldsymbol { b } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \boldsymbol { b } _ { n } ]$ . A rational fraction can be developed in one and only one finite regular continued fraction,and its last partial denominator is at least equal to 2.

The solution of a Diophantine linear equation

$$
p x - q y = r ,
$$

where $p , q , r$ are integers, can be represented by a regular continued fraction

$$
{ \frac { p } { q } } = [ b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n } ]
$$

of even-degree terms.In order that an irreducible fraction $P / Q$ ， $P > Q > 1$ ，can be developed in a symmetric regular continued fraction of an even or an odd number of terms in the form

$$
\frac { P } { Q } = [ b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \ldots , b _ { 2 } , b _ { 1 } , b _ { 0 } ] = \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } ,
$$

it is necessary and sufficient that $Q ^ { 2 } + 1$ or $Q ^ { 2 } - 1$ be divisible by $P$ If positive integers $p$ and $q$ satisfy

$$
| p ^ { 2 } - \xi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } q ^ { 2 } | < \xi _ { 0 } ,
$$

then $p / q$ is equal to the reduced fraction of $\xi _ { 0 }$

At least one of two consecutive reduced fractions of $\pmb { \xi } _ { 0 }$ satisfies

$$
\left| \xi _ { 0 } - \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } \right| < \frac { 1 } { 2 B _ { n } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

For an irrational $\xi _ { 0 }$ there are an infinite number of such fractions.At least one of three consecutive reduced fractions of $\pmb { \xi } _ { 0 }$ satisfies

$$
\left| \xi _ { 0 } - \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } \right| < \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 5 } B _ { n } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

In particular, if $\xi _ { 0 }$ is irrational, then an inequality

$$
\left| \xi _ { 0 } - \frac { A } { B } \right| < \frac { 1 } { c B ^ { 2 } } , \qquad c > \sqrt { 5 } ,
$$

cannot be satisfied by an infinite number of rational fractions $A / B$ If there is a relation

$$
\gamma = \frac { a \beta + b } { c \beta + d } ,
$$

where $a , b , c , d$ are integers such that $a d - b c = \pm 1$ ， then irrational numbers $\beta$ and $\boldsymbol { \gamma }$ are said to be equivalent.

If the elements of a continued fraction with an infinite number of terms

$$
b _ { 0 } + \frac { a _ { 1 } | } { | b _ { 1 } } + \frac { a _ { 2 } | } { | b _ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

are real numbers such that $| a _ { \lambda } | = 1 , \ b _ { \lambda } \geq 1 , \ b _ { \lambda } + a _ { \lambda + 1 } \geq 1 \ ( \lambda =$ $( \lambda = 1$ ， 2,...), then we have ${ \cal B } _ { \lambda } \equiv 1 \ ( \lambda = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . )$ ，and $\operatorname* { l i m } _ { \lambda \to \infty } B _ { \lambda } = \infty$ and the continued fraction is convergent. The limiting value is between $b _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ (exclusive) and $b _ { 0 } + a _ { 1 }$ (inclusive).A convergent continued fraction with all its partial numerators different from zero

$$
b _ { 0 } + \frac { a _ { 1 } | } { | b _ { 1 } } + \frac { a _ { 2 } | } { | b _ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

is said to be non-conditionally convergent, if the truncated fractions obtained by dropping an arbitrary number ^ of its first terms

$$
b _ { \imath } + \frac { a _ { \imath + 1 } | } { | b _ { \imath + 1 } } + \frac { a _ { \imath + 2 } | } { | b _ { \imath + 2 } } + \cdots \qquad ( \imath = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots )
$$

are all convergent. Otherwise it is conditionally convergent.A convergent continued fraction with all its partial numerators different from zero is non-conditionally convergent if its value is not equal to any of its reduced fractions. Otherwise it is conditionally convergent.

If $\textstyle \sum _ { n } | b _ { n } |$ is convergent, then the continued fraction

$$
b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } + \frac { 1 | } { | b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } + \frac { 1 | } { | b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } } + \cdots
$$

is divergent. If all partial denominators of this continued fraction are positive, then the necessary and sufficient condition for the conver-gence of the continued fraction is that $\textstyle \sum _ { n } b _ { n }$ be divergent. A continued fraction

$$
b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } + \frac { a _ { 1 } | } { | b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } } + \frac { a _ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } | } { | b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } } + \cdots
$$

with positive elements is convergent, if

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 2 } ^ { \infty } \frac { A _ { 2 \lambda , \nu + 1 } } { B _ { 2 \lambda , \nu + 1 } } \frac { a _ { \nu } b _ { \nu - 2 } } { a _ { \nu + 1 } ( b _ { \nu - 1 } b _ { \nu - 2 } + a _ { \nu - 1 } ) }
$$

is divergent for $\lambda = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots$ ，where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { A _ { n } = b _ { 0 } A _ { n - 1 , 1 } + a _ { 1 } B _ { n - 1 , 1 } , } & { { } B _ { n } = A _ { n - 1 , 1 } , } \\ { B _ { n + 1 , \ i - 1 } = b _ { \ i } B _ { n , \ i } + a _ { \ i + 1 } B _ { n - 1 , \ i + 1 } . } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

If the elements of an infinite continued fraction

$$
b _ { 0 } + \frac { a _ { 1 } | } { | b _ { 1 } } + \frac { a _ { 2 } | } { | b _ { 2 } } + \cdots \qquad a _ { n } \neq 0 ,
$$

are all integral rational numbers, and if they satisfy

$$
b _ { n } \geq | a _ { n } | , \qquad b _ { n } \geq | a _ { n } | + 1 \qquad \mathrm { f o r } a _ { n + 1 } < 0 ,
$$

from a certain index $_ n$ up,then the continued fraction is convergent, and the number $\xi _ { 0 }$ represented by this continued fraction is irrational, except when $a _ { 0 } < 0$ ， $b _ { n } = \vert a _ { n } \vert + 1$ for all $_ n$ above a certain value.In this exceptional case the fraction is either non-conditionally divergent or rational. If its partial divisors of

$$
{ \frac { 1 | } { | b _ { 1 } } } + { \frac { 1 | } { | b _ { 2 } } } + \cdots
$$

satisfy

$$
\left| { \frac { 1 } { b _ { 2 n - 1 } } } \right| + \left| { \frac { 1 } { b _ { 2 n } } } \right| \leq 1 \qquad ( n = 1 , 2 , 3 , \ldots ) ,
$$

then the continued fraction is convergent.

Take an irational number $\xi$ A Diophantine inequality

$$
| \xi x + y - \eta | < \varepsilon
$$

is satisfied by integers $x$ and $y$ ，for all values of $\eta$ and for all positive numbers $\varepsilon$ however small. If a rational number $\xi$ is equal to an irreducible rational fraction $a / b$ , then the above inequality can be satisfied by integers $x$ and $y$ for an arbitrary $\varepsilon > 0$ ， when and only when $\xi x +$ $y = \eta$ ，that is,if and only if $\eta$ is a rational number with denominator $^ { b }$ . If the determinant of the coefficients in the linear transformation

$$
F _ { \nu } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } \xi _ { \nu \mu } x _ { \mu } \qquad ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \dots , n ) ,
$$

is zero, then $\pmb { n }$ inequalities

$$
\left| \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } \hat { \xi } _ { \nu \mu } x _ { \mu } \right| < \varepsilon \qquad ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

cannot be satisfied simultaneously by integers $x _ { \mu }$ . There exists such an $\varepsilon$ if $_ n$ equations

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } \xi _ { \nu \mu } x _ { \mu } = 0
$$

are not satisfied.

A number $\xi$ is said to be algebraic, if there exists a positive number $\pmb { n }$ such that $1 , \xi , \xi ^ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi ^ { n }$ are mutually rationally dependent, that is, if there exists a relation

$$
r _ { 0 } + r _ { 1 } \xi + r _ { 2 } \xi ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { n } \xi ^ { n } = 0
$$

with rational numbers $r _ { 0 } , r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { n }$ among $1 , \xi , \xi ^ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi ^ { n }$ . Otherwise $\xi$ is said to be transcendental. Consequently, if there exists a positive number $_ n$ such that

$$
a _ { 0 } + a _ { 1 } \xi + a _ { 2 } \xi ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { n } \xi ^ { n } = 0 \qquad a _ { n } \neq 0 ,
$$

with integers $a _ { 0 } , a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ ，then $1 , \xi , \xi ^ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi ^ { n }$ are rationally dependent. If $\pmb { n }$ is the lowest degree of such an algebraic equation, then $\xi$ is said to be an algebraic number of degree $\pmb { n }$ . The set of algebraic numbers is of the power of natural numbers, that is, denumerable.

If $\xi$ is an algebraic number of degree n, $n > 1$ ，then it can be proved that there are only a finite number of rational fractions $g / h$ satisfying

$$
\left| \xi - \frac { g } { h } \right| < \frac { 1 } { h ^ { n + 1 } } .
$$

When we can find a rational fraction such that for any positive integer  we have

$$
h _ { \lambda } > 1 , \quad \quad \left| \xi - \frac { g _ { \lambda } } { h _ { \lambda } } \right| < \frac { 1 } { h _ { \lambda } ^ { \lambda } } ,
$$

then the irrational number $\pmb { \xi }$ is called a Liouville number.ALiouville number is transcendental. If an irrational number $\zeta _ { 0 }$ is written in the form


<!-- chunk 0005: pages 281-350 -->
$$
\left| \zeta _ { 0 } - \frac { P } { Q } \right| < \frac { 1 } { 2 Q ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

then $P / Q$ is equal to a reduced fraction of a regular continued fraction

$$
\zeta _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } = [ b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } , . . . ] .
$$

A Liouville number should satisfy

$$
\left| \zeta _ { 0 } - \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } \right| < \frac { 1 } { B _ { n } ^ { \lambda - 2 } }
$$

for any large integer λ. A number $\zeta _ { 0 }$ defined by

$$
\zeta _ { 0 } = [ b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \ldots , b _ { n } , b _ { n + 1 } ]
$$

satisfies

$$
\zeta _ { 0 } - \frac { A _ { n } } { B _ { n } } = \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n } } { B _ { n } ( B _ { n } \zeta _ { n + 1 } + B _ { n - 1 } ) } .
$$

Hence

$$
B _ { n } \zeta _ { n + 1 } + B _ { n - 1 } > B _ { n } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle \lambda + 1 } .
$$

Since

$$
b _ { n + 1 } < \zeta _ { n + 1 } < b _ { n + 1 } + 1 ,
$$

this inequality holds when and only when $\lambda$ satisfies $b _ { n + 1 } > B _ { n } ^ { \lambda }$ . Hence a regular continued fraction $[ b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \dots ]$ with the reduced fraction $A _ { n } / B _ { n }$ represents a Liouville number when and only when there exists an index $_ n$ satisfying $b _ { n + 1 } > B _ { n } ^ { \ i }$ for any large integer λ (Perron, 1921). If $\xi$ is an algebraic number different from zero,then its reciprocal $\eta$ is also an algebraic number.If there is a relation

$$
\eta = r _ { 0 } + r _ { 1 } \xi + r _ { 2 } \xi ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { m } \xi ^ { m }
$$

with rational coefficients $r _ { n }$ between two irrational numbers $\xi$ and $\eta$ ， then $\eta$ is an algebraic number different from zero in the same way as $\xi$ .A similar situation exists if $\eta$ is an irrational number defined by

$$
\eta = \frac { p _ { 0 } + p _ { 1 } \xi + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { m } \xi ^ { m } } { q _ { 0 } + q _ { 1 } \xi + \cdot \cdot \cdot + q _ { m } \xi ^ { m } }
$$

with rational numbers $p _ { 0 } , p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { m } ; q _ { 0 } , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { m }$

An important question to be referred to later is the distribution of the rational fractions $p / q$ defined by the Diophantine inequality

$$
\left| x - { \frac { p } { q } } \right| < { \frac { C } { q ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad C > 0 .
$$

The approximation of an irrational number by rational fractions is .called Diophantine approximation.It is shown that if the irrational .number $x$ is fixed then exists at least a rational number $p / q$ satisfying this inequality and that the Lebesgue measure of points $p / q$ satisfying this inequality is zero (Koksma,1936).A Liouville's number is a :special case of this.In particular,Thue-Siegel's theorem states that if $_ x$ is a root of an rth degree algebraic equation then there exist at most a finite number of rational fractions $p / q$ such that

$$
| q x - p | < \frac { C } { q ^ { r / 2 + k } } , \qquad C > 0 ,
$$

with a positive integer $k > 1$ (Skolem, 1938; Osgood,1972).

# BRUNS'S PROOF OF NONUNIFORM CONVERGENCE

# 15.4BRUNS's PROOF

We have seen that the expansions in perturbation theory are of the form

$$
F = \sum { A _ { i _ { 1 } i _ { 2 } \cdots i _ { r } } \cos { ( i _ { 1 } l _ { 1 } + i _ { 2 } l _ { 2 } + \cdots + i _ { r } l _ { r } ) } } ,
$$

$$
l _ { j } = n _ { j } t + \varepsilon _ { j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

In the integration of the equations by the method of the variation of elements,divisors of the form $i _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } + i _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } + \cdots + i _ { r } n _ { r }$ appear，and, for certain sets of values of $n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } , \ldots , n _ { r }$ distributed densely in the domain of $n _ { j }$ ， the series obtained by term-by-term integration of the differential equations may become divergent. It was Bruns (1884) who discussed the divergence in a very ingenious manner. Bruns took a simpler case

$$
\sum _ { m , n } { \frac { p ^ { m } q ^ { n } } { m - n A } } \qquad ( m , n = 1 , 2 , \ldots , \infty ) ,
$$

where $p$ and $q$ are pure fractions and $\pmb { A }$ is a positive irrational number. Let $\pmb { A }$ be an algebraic number satisfying an irreducibe algebraic equation

$$
A ^ { s } + G _ { 1 } A ^ { s - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + G _ { s } = 0
$$

with integer coefficients $G _ { i }$ . Multiply the numerator and the denominator of this fraction (1) by the product

$$
( m - n A ^ { \prime } ) ( m - n A ^ { \prime \prime } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( m - n A ^ { ( s - 1 ) } )
$$

of linear factors formed of the remaining $s - 1$ roots of this algebraic equation. Then the denominator becomes a rational function of m and $\pmb { n }$ with integer coefficients. Since $\pmb { A }$ is irrational, the denominator is never equal to zero but at least equal to 1； while the numerator is a rational function of $m$ and $_ n$ of degree $s - 1$ .Hence the series is convergent.

In order to see if the series (1） is divergent for other irrational numbers,Bruns considered a representation of an irrational number. Take rositive pure fractions $\theta$ and $\eta$ such that

$$
0 \leq \theta < \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \qquad 0 \leq \eta \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,
$$

and let $\varepsilon$ be $+ 1$ or $^ { - 1 }$ ，and $0 < A < \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ . Define a set of positive integers $h _ { \alpha }$ according to the algorithm

$$
