The constant $z _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ is very small and of the order of the square of the eccentricity, and $\boldsymbol { x } _ { i } ^ { ( k ) }$ and $y _ { i } ^ { ( k ) }$ ，except $y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $y _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ , can be expanded in powers of $\sqrt { z _ { 1 } } \cos w _ { 1 }$ and $\sqrt { z _ { 1 } } \sin w _ { 1 }$ ， each term depending on $w _ { 2 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ and periodic with respect to $w _ { 2 }$

It is noted that an infinite number of other series of the same form can be derived from these series.In fact, replacing $z$ and $\varpi$ by the expansions

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { z _ { i } = z _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu z _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } z _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } \\ & { \varpi _ { i } = \varpi _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu \varpi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } \varpi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } \end{array}
$$

gives $\boldsymbol { z } _ { i } ^ { ( k ) }$ and ${ \varpi _ { i } ^ { ( k ) } }$ as functions of four new constants $z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \varpi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ;$ except for $z _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = z _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ， $\varpi _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = \varpi _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ . If the constants $z _ { i }$ and $\varpi _ { i }$ are replaced by (34) and arranged with respect to $\mu$ ， then new expansions for $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ are obtained which are of the same form as (31) proceeding in powers of $\mu$ and in sines and cosines of multiples of $w _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = n _ { 1 } t +$ $\varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ ， $w _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = n _ { 2 } t + \varpi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ ， with coefficients depending on the new constants $z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \varpi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ . The most remarkable case is when these coefficients depend only on $z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ ，and are independent of $\varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \varpi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ ，like the series (31) itself.

How can we choose in general the expansions (34) that define the constants $z _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and $\varpi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ?$ First we arrange so that the ratio of the two mean motions $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ shall be independent of $\mu$ ：

$$
\frac { n _ { 1 } } { n _ { 2 } } = \frac { 8 + ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 3 } } { 8 - ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 3 } } .
$$

If there were no relation between ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and ${ n } _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ，then we should be able to choose the expansions (34) so that the mean motions $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ are both independent of $\mu$ .But there is such a relation.The equation （33） gives $n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } - n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } = 1$ .Let $n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } - n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } = \phi ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } )$ ，and write

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Bigl [ \frac { 4 } { ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Bigr ] [ 1 + \mu \phi ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ] } } \\ { { { } ~ = n _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } + \mu n _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { \prime } n _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } } \\ { { { \Bigl [ \frac { 4 } { ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Bigr ] [ 1 + \mu \phi ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ] } } } \\ { { { } ~ = n _ { 2 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } + \mu n _ { 2 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { \prime } n _ { 2 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 2 ) } + \cdots . } } \end{array}
$$

Since ${ n } _ { i } ^ { ( k ) }$ are known functions of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ ，the variables $z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ can be obtained from (36) as functions of $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 }$ ，and $\mu$ ； thus the expansions of the form (34) are obtained,where ${ z _ { i } ^ { ( k ) } }$ and ${ \widetilde { \pmb { \varpi } } } _ { i } ^ { ( k ) }$ depend only on $z _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and not on $\varpi _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ . Thus the equations (36) are related to the relation (35).

We can also choose for the constants $z _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ the initial values of $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ for $t = 0$ In fact, replace $\pmb { t }$ by O in (31), $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ by $z _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and $\varpi _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ，respectively； then we get

$$
\begin{array} { r } { z _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu x _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } \\ { \varpi _ { i } ^ { \prime } = y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu y _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } y _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } \end{array}
$$

where the arguments $w _ { 1 }$ and $w _ { 2 }$ in the expansions for $\boldsymbol { x } _ { i } ^ { ( k ) }$ and $y _ { i } ^ { ( k ) }$ should be replaced by $\varpi _ { 1 }$ and $\widetilde { \omega } _ { 2 }$ . The right-hand members of (37) are known functions of $z , \varpi$ ,and $\mu$ .For $\mu = 0$ they reduce to $x _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = z _ { i } , y _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) }$ $= \varpi _ { i }$ . Hence $z$ and $\varpi$ are obtained from (37) as functions of new variables $z ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime }$ ，and $\mu$ in series proceeding in powers of $\mu$ . These expansions are nothing but the expansions (34) in question. The inconven-ience of such expansions arises from the dependence of $z$ and $\varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime }$ not only on $z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ but also on $\smash { \widetilde { \omega } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } }$ and $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ . This inconvenience disappears if we proceed by arranging the series in the form (36).Hence the series (31) are replaced by the series,which we call (31a),of the same form but with coefficients depending not only on $z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ but also on $\varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $\varpi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ . If we proceed otherwise，we obtain the series in the same form, but the coefficients are independent of $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$

Before proceeding to such new expansions we examine how to derive from the series (31) the expansions obtained in classical celestial mechanics.With the constants $z ^ { \prime }$ and $\varpi ^ { \prime }$ ，consider the series (31a) just defined. The equations (32） give $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ as functions of $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } ,$ and $\pmb { \mu }$ . Combining these with (34)，we obtain $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ as functions of $z ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime }$ ，and $\pmb { \mu }$ ，which we write as

$$
n _ { i } = n _ { i } ^ { \prime ( 0 ) } + \mu n _ { i } ^ { \prime ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } n _ { i } ^ { \prime ( 2 ) } + \cdots .
$$

Also, the $n _ { i } ^ { \prime ( k ) }$ are given as functions of $z ^ { \prime }$ and $\varpi ^ { \prime }$ and

$$
n _ { i } ^ { \prime ( 0 ) } = \frac { 4 } { ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + z _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 3 } } \pm \frac { 1 } { 2 } .
$$

Consider the series

$$
x _ { i } = \sum _ { k } \mu ^ { k } x _ { i } ^ { \prime ( k ) } , \qquad y _ { i } = \sum _ { k } \mu ^ { k } y _ { i } ^ { \prime ( k ) } .
$$

Denote a term of $x _ { i } ^ { \prime ( k ) }$ or of $y _ { i } ^ { \prime ( k ) }$ by ${ \cal A } \sin { ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) }$ ，where $\pmb { A }$

depends on $z ^ { \prime }$ and $\varpi ^ { \prime }$ ，while $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ are integers. This term, however,depends on $\mu$ indirectly,since

$$
m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = ( m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ) t + ( m _ { 1 } \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + m _ { 2 } \varpi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

with $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ depending on $\mu$ . If we expand this term in powers of $\mu$ ， it becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \boldsymbol { A } \sin { ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) } = \boldsymbol { A } \sin { \omega } } & \\ & { \qquad + \ \mu \boldsymbol { A } t ( m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { \prime ( 1 ) } + m _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { \prime ( 1 ) } ) \cos { \omega } + \cdots , } & \\ & { \boldsymbol { \omega } = ( m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { \prime ( 0 ) } + m _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { \prime ( 0 ) } ) t + ( m _ { 1 } \widetilde { \omega } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + m _ { 2 } \widetilde { \omega } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) . } & { ( 3 8 ) } \end{array}
$$

Replace each term of (31a) by its expansion (38) and arrange the resulting series with respect to $\mu$ .Then we obtain the series

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime ( 0 ) } + \mu x _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ { y _ { i } = y _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime ( 0 ) } + \mu y _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } y _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

which consist of purely trigonometric terms and also terms with $t$ outside the trigonometric signs as factors,in the same way as in the expansion (38).Each term is entirely independent of $\mu$ .Thus the series (31b）are the same as those obtained by means of the classical procedure.

These series contain non-secular terms, that is, trigonometric terms with arguments $m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，where the $ { \boldsymbol { w } } _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime }$ are linear functions of time， i.e.，where $w _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } = n _ { i } ^ { \prime ( 0 ) } t + \varpi _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ . They also contain such trigonometric terms multiplied by $t$ or integral powers of $t _ { i }$ ，that is,mixed secular terms (Section 8.2).We separate these two kinds of terms in the form.

$$
x _ { 1 } = \begin{array} { r } { \sum A \sin \omega + t \cdot \sum _ { \mod } B \cos \omega + t ^ { 2 } \cdot \sum C \sin \omega + \cdots , } \\ { \omega = m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } + h , } \end{array}
$$

where $h$ is a constant. We can derive the coefficients $B , C , \ldots$ from the cofficients $\pmb { A }$ by

$$
B = \frac { { \cal A } } { 1 } ( m _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } ) , ~ { \cal C } = - \frac { { \cal A } } { 1 \cdot 2 } ( m _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } , \ldots ,
$$

where $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 }$ denote the constants $n _ { 1 } - n _ { 1 } ^ { \prime ( 0 ) }$ ， $n _ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } n _ { 2 } ^ { \prime ( 0 ) }$ ， respectively. Thus, if we know $\alpha _ { 1 }$ and $\alpha _ { 2 }$ and the non-secular terms,then we can obtain the secular terms immediately. We see also that the purely secular terms should always be cancelled as we proceed to a higher approximation.

If we compare the series (31b） given by classical procedure with the series (31） given by our new procedure, then we at first see that the series (31b) arranged in powers of $\mu$ are convergent, as long as t is sufficiently small. The convergence is destroyed if $t$ is larger than a certain bound.The first terms of the series (31） converge rapidly enough for practical purpose, but no rigorous mathematical proof of the convergence of the series has yet been discovered. However,as we have shown,the convergence,if any,is not uniform for all values of t,nor for all values of the integration constants in certain intervals. Hence the question is whether our new method, in which expansions in powers of $t$ are avoided, has any real advantage. The answer de-pends on whether we are dealing with the motion over a comparatively short time interval with high accuracy or over a long time interval with medium accuracy.

We now abandon the classical procedure and refer to the expansions (31). These expansions contain certain terms that may become very large; but it is often possible to compensate by an appropriate grouping of the terms.It occurs frequently that a group of terms contains very large terms but the sum of such terms in a group is nevertheless very small. In fact, the terms of (31) are obtained by a set of operations including integrations,additions,multiplications,and differentiations.For example,in integrating a term of the form $\pmb { A }$ cos αt we obtain a term $( A \sin \alpha t ) / \alpha$ .If $\alpha$ is very small, then this integrated term becomes very large.But this is not so for a finite value of $t$ ，since the term is smaller than $\boldsymbol { A } t$ . If we integrate a term $A \sin \alpha t$ ，we obtain $- ( A \cos \alpha t ) / \alpha$ or $( A / \alpha ) - ( A \sin \alpha t ) / \alpha$ .Each of these two terms is very large,but the set of these two terms is finite and smaller than $A \alpha t ^ { 2 } / 2$ .These examples show the advantage of grouping the terms.

Consider a term $A \cos \alpha t$ and its integrated result $( A \sin \alpha t ) / \alpha$ Multiply this term by sin $\beta t$ ； then we obtain

$$
\frac { A \sin { \alpha t } \sin { \beta t } } { \alpha } = \frac { A } { 2 \alpha } \cos { ( \beta - \alpha ) } t - \frac { A } { 2 \alpha } \cos { ( \beta + \alpha ) } t .
$$

Integration gives

$$
\frac { A \sin { ( \beta - \alpha ) t } } { 2 \alpha ( \beta - \alpha ) } - \frac { A \sin { ( \beta + \alpha ) t } } { 2 \alpha ( \beta + \alpha ) } .
$$

If $\beta$ is finite and $\alpha$ is very small,each term is very large. Since both terms are of nearly equal period,the two nearly cancel, both being of order $\boldsymbol { A } t / \beta$ . This circumstance often appears in the computation of the second-order perturbation.

Next, let us study_ the form of the expansions (31) by examining some of their terms.Let

$$
\mu ^ { p } A \operatorname * { s i n } _ { \mathrm { { c o s } } } ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } )
$$

be one of these terms, $\pmb { A }$ being a function of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ . We see that the expansions (32) reduce to the first two terms

$$
n _ { 1 } = n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } , ~ n _ { 2 } = n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } ,
$$

such that

$$
\frac { n _ { 1 } } { n _ { 2 } } = \frac { n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } } { n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } } = \frac { 8 + ( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } { 8 - ( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } }
$$

is independent of $\mu$ but depends only on $z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 }$ . Otherwise we should be able to pass from the constants $z$ to the constants $z ^ { \prime }$ as defined by (34).The successive integration introduces in the function $\pmb { A }$ the divisors of the form $q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ with integers $q _ { 1 }$ and $q _ { 2 }$ ，which we call small divisors. Write $A = B / \Pi$ ，where $\Pi$ is the product of small divisors and $B$ is a function of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ . Then $\pmb { B }$ remains finite for all values of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ ，but $\pmb { A }$ may become infinite,since certain small divisors can vanish for a commensurable value of $n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } / n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . Now consider $\pmb { B }$ as a function of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 }$ ,orof $z _ { 1 }$ and ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . The product $\Pi$ is an integral polynomial of ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ , because $q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } = ( q _ { 1 } + q _ { 2 } ) n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ $- q _ { 2 } ( n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } - n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } )$ . For a moment, suppose that $z _ { 1 }$ is a constant； then $\pmb { A }$ will be equal to a function of ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ , holomorphic in a certain domain, dividedbya polynomial in ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ .Let $B = C \mathbb { 1 } + D$ ,where $C$ is another function holomorphic in ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $D$ is a polynomial of degree lower than $\pi$ .Then $A = C + D / \Pi$ . If we decompose the rational fraction into a sum of simple fractions,we find

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \mu ^ { p } C _ { \cos } ^ { \sin } \left( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } \right) } } \\ { { \qquad + \displaystyle \sum ^ { p } \mu ^ { p } { \frac { E } { ( q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) ^ { h } } } \cos \left( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } \right) } , } \end{array}
$$

where $C$ is a holomorphic function in $z _ { 1 }$ and ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ,and other terms contain in the numerator a function $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ ，which is holomorphic in $z _ { 1 }$ only, and in the denominator an integral power $h$ of a single small divisor. By taking into account the way we have obtained the expansions (31), we see that the polynomial $\Pi$ is the product of at most $2 p - 1$ small divisors for the expansions of $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ ，and the product of at most $2 p$ small divisors for the expansions of $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$ . Hence the polynomial is at most of degree $2 p - 1$ or $2 p$ , where $p$ is the exponent of $\mu$ of the term in question. In each term of (39) the exponent $h$ is at most equal to $2 p - 1$ or $2 p$ . If it attains its upper limit $2 p - 1$ or $2 p$ ， then we have $m _ { 1 } / q _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } / q _ { 2 }$

Now we group the terms obtained by decomposing each term of the series into the form (39) in the following manner: the first group contains all terms without any small divisor such as the first term of (39), while each of the other groups consists of the terms containing a power of a small divisor in the denominator. Consider a group of terms containing a power of $\delta = q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ in the denominator and call it the group $( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } )$ . Each term of the group $( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } )$ contains $\mu / \delta ^ { \hbar }$ as a factor, where the difference $2 p - 1 - h$ is positive or zero for the variables $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ ,and $2 p - h$ is positive or zero for $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$ . Arrange a sub-group of terms for which this difference has the same value； the principal sub-group is the one with a difference equal to zero. If $\delta$ is very small of order $\sqrt { \mu }$ ， then certain terms of a higher order with respect to $\mu$ may become of sensible magnitude due to the presence of a power of $\delta$ in the denominator.The importance of a term is measured by the difference $2 p - 1 - h$ . A term with $2 p - 1 - h = 0$ is the most important. The numbers $q _ { 1 }$ and $q _ { 2 }$ can be considered to be mutually prime,so that we can find two integers $r _ { 1 }$ and $r _ { 2 }$ such that $q _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } - q _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } = 1$ . Put $m _ { 1 } = \alpha q _ { 1 } + \beta r _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 } = \alpha q _ { 2 } + \beta r _ { 2 }$ ，with two integers $_ \alpha$ and $\beta$ . Classify in one partial series those terms of a subgroup that correspond to the same value of $\beta$ . Since $\delta$ is very small, $q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 }$ is also very small，so that all terms of one of the partial series have periods nearly equal to $2 \pi / [ \beta ( r _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ) ]$ . A principal sub-group contains only one partial series， since $m _ { 1 } / q _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } / q _ { 2 }$ and accordingly $\beta = 0$ for such a sub-group. The terms of the first group are generally very small, since they do not contain any small divisor.

A singular situation may arise where each of the partial series is easily summable. The summation is carried out by a finite number of quadratures.Generally we need only sum a few isolated terms and a few partial series.Bohlin's theory is concerned with the classical procedure that leads to the series (31). If we limit ourselves to summing the partial series that correspond to the principal sub-group，then we come to Delaunay's theory (Section 9.1). If the mean motions are nearly commensurable, the partial series may diverge.Neverthless,an analytical expression obtained after this summation still has meaning. Thus the expression $1 / ( 1 - x )$ conserves its meaning for $x > 1$ ，even though the series $1 + x + x ^ { 2 } + \cdots$ is not convergent. Even when a partial series may diverge,we can replace it by such an analytical expression. This is why Bohlin's and Delaunay's theories are applicable to the case when the direct use of the series (31) becomes illusory,and even


<!-- chunk 0006: pages 351-420 -->
to the case of libration where the orbital form is so different from the ordinary form. In spite of this difference，the libration case can be related to the theory from which the series (31) is obtained.

# 15.14PASSAGE TO PERIODIC AND ASYMPTOTIC SOLUTIONS

We can derive periodic and asymptotic solutions from the series (31) by means of the considerations just discussed. The existence of periodic and asymptotic solutions will be discussed in Chapters 18 and 19. They will be shown to exist under certain conditions.

As we have seen the series are developable in powers of $\sqrt { z _ { 1 } } \cos w _ { 1 }$ and $\sqrt { z _ { 1 } }$ sin $w _ { 1 }$ with the coefficients depending on $z _ { 2 }$ and $w _ { 2 }$ .Let $z _ { 1 } = 0$ ； then this series no longer contains the argument $w _ { 1 }$ . It is periodic. Thus we reach periodic solutions of the first sort in Poincaré's sense, which will be discussed in Section 18.13.

We can obtain periodic solutions of the second sort (Section 18.16) by assuming in the series (31） that

$$
n _ { i } = n _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu n _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } , \qquad { \frac { n _ { 1 } } { n _ { 2 } } } = { \frac { n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } } { n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } } } .
$$

If ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and ${ n } _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ are given mutually cəmmensurable numbers, then one of the small divisors $q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ vanishes,so that certain terms of the expansion become infinite. Assume that $\varpi _ { 2 } = 0$ and replace $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { 1 }$ by its expansion

$$
\varpi _ { 1 } = \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

whose coefficients are new constants that will be determined later. If the constant $\varpi _ { 1 }$ is replaced by this expansion (4O) in any term of the series (31), then the term is expanded in powers of $\mu$ . Similarly if we expand all terms of the series (31) and arrange in powers of $\mu$ ，then we obtain a new series which we call (31C). Let

$$
\frac { \mu ^ { p } } { ( q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) ^ { h } } A _ { \mathrm { \small ~ c o s } } ^ { \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ s i n } } ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } )
$$

be a term of the new series,where the coefficient $\pmb { A }$ does not contain the divisor $q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ·

We introduce a new definition of the order of a term.The order of a term in the expansions of $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ is $2 p - h$ when $m _ { 1 } / m _ { 2 } = q _ { 1 } / q _ { 2 } ;$ ， and $2 p - 1 - h$ when $m _ { 1 } / m _ { 2 } \gtrless q _ { 1 } / q _ { 2 }$ . The order of a term in the expansions of $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$ is $2 p . + 1 { - h }$ when $m _ { 1 } / m _ { 2 } = q _ { 1 } / q _ { 2 }$ ，and $2 p - h$ when $m _ { 1 } / m _ { 2 } \gtrless q _ { 1 } / q _ { 2 }$ . Terms for which $h = 0$ are excluded from this definition. The principal term of each order is the term in the expansions of $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ for which $h = 1$ ， $m _ { 1 } / m _ { 2 } = q _ { 1 } / q _ { 2 }$ . According to this definition a principal term is always of an odd order.Terms of the first and second order depend only on ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ； terms of the third and fourth order depend on ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ ； terms of the fth and sixth order depend on $\varpi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ ，and $\varpi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ ; and so on.

An aggregate of principal terms of the first order in $x _ { 1 }$ is represented in a form with a certain numerator divided by $q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . Since we have

$$
\frac { m _ { 1 } } { m _ { 2 } } = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q _ { 2 } } = ~ - \frac { n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } } { n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } } = ~ - \frac { n _ { 2 } } { n _ { 1 } }
$$

for such terms,we obtain $m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } = 0$ . Hence the coefficient of $t$ disappears from all such terms.Thus the numerator is a constant depending on ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . We choose ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ so that the numerator vanishes. This determines ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . Thus such principal terms as might become infinite disappear. Principal terms of the first order in the expansion of $x _ { 2 }$ disappear, too,by virtue of the energy integral. Moreover,all terms of the first and second order actually disappear in these four expansions.

An aggregate of principal terms of the third order in $x _ { 1 }$ gives a numerator divided by $q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . This numerator reduces to a constant depending on ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ . We choose $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ so that $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ and the principal terms in question disappear. At the same time the principal terms of the third order in $x _ { 2 }$ disappear.Thus all terms of the third and fourth order disappear in all these four expansions.Similarly we choose ${ \widetilde { \varpi } } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ so that the principal terms of the fth order in $x _ { 1 }$ vanish.At the same time all terms of the ffth and sixth order vanish, and so on. Thus all terms which would become infinite disappear altogether. Since the mean motions are mutually commensurable, the series in question depends only on a unique argument and hence the solution is periodic.The series thus obtained are convergent,although the series (31) are not so in the strict sense.

Let $C$ be the energy constant. The value of $C$ depends on two constants $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ ，or on $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ . It is represented by a series arranged in powers of $\mu$ ，whose terms are functions of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ or of $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ In certain cases $C$ can be considered as given in the problem.Then ${ \pmb { F } } = { \pmb { C } }$ is a relation between $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ or between $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ ， whence $\pmb { n } _ { 2 }$ is derived as a function of $n _ { 1 }$ in the form of a series in powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ with coefficients depending o $n _ { 1 }$

We obtain periodic solutions by starting with the series (31) in this manner.At the same time,asymptotic solutions can be deduced in the

following way.

We know that the coordinates of a disturbed planet can be represented by an expansion quite different from the expansions (31)， of which the latters proceed in sines and cosines of multiples of two arguments $w _ { i }$ . To each periodic solution there corresponds one of the expansions. Let

$$
x _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 , 0 ) } ( W ) , \qquad \cos y _ { i } = \psi _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 , 0 ) } ( W ) , \qquad \sin y _ { i } = \theta _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 , 0 ) } ( W )
$$

be a periodic solution, where $\phi _ { i } ^ { ( 0 , 0 ) } , \ \psi _ { i } ^ { ( 0 , 0 ) } , \ \theta _ { i } ^ { ( 0 , 0 ) }$ are periodic functions with period $2 \pi$ of a unique argument $W = N _ { 0 } t + h$ ，where $N _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ is supposed to be a given constant and $h$ is an arbitrary integration constant. Denote by $C _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ the corresponding value of the energy constant $C$ .Then the general solution is represented by the expansions

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { x _ { i } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i } \mathrm { \Lambda } ( A e ^ { \alpha t } ) ^ { p } ( A ^ { \prime } e ^ { - \alpha t } ) ^ { q } \phi _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) } ( W ) , } \\ & { } & { \displaystyle \cos y _ { i } = \sum _ { i } \mathrm { \Lambda } ( A e ^ { \alpha t } ) ^ { p } ( A ^ { \prime } e ^ { - \alpha t } ) ^ { q } \psi _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) } ( W ) , } \\ & { } & { \displaystyle \sin y _ { i } = \sum _ { i } \mathrm { \Lambda } ( A e ^ { \alpha t } ) ^ { p } ( A ^ { \prime } e ^ { - \alpha t } ) ^ { q } \theta _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) } ( W ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) } , \ \psi _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) } , \ \theta _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) }$ are periodic functions of $W = N t \_ h$ In these expansions $A$ ， $A ^ { \prime }$ ,and $h$ are the integration constants,while $\alpha$ and $N$ are given constants that can be expanded in powers of the product $A A ^ { \prime }$ and the difference $C - C _ { 0 }$ .If $\phi _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) } , \ \psi _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) } , \ \theta _ { i } ^ { ( p , q ) }$ are expanded in trigonometric series, then the coefficients of the series can be equally expanded in powers of $C - C _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ .If $C = C _ { 0 }$ and one of the constants $\pmb { A }$ or $\boldsymbol { A ^ { \prime } }$ vanishes, then $N$ reduces to a constant $N _ { 0 }$ just defined and $\alpha$ to a constant $\alpha _ { 0 }$ . Then the remaining terms of the series (41） are those for which $p = 0$ or those for which $q = 0$ ．Thus，solutions called asymptotic are obtained. The series is convergent.If $\pmb { A }$ and $\pmb { A } ^ { \prime }$ vanish at the same time, then $x _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ^ { ( 0 , 0 ) } ( W )$ only are left. They are periodic solutions.

Now we will show how one can pass from the expansions (31) to the expansions (41). The series (41） are arranged in powers of two integration constants $\pmb { A }$ and $A ^ { \prime }$ . We may expand each term of the series (31） in powers of $\pmb { A }$ and $A ^ { \prime }$ ，and combine similar terms and arrange in powers of these constants. But this is not the way to arrive at the expansions (41).

Consider a series

$$
\sum _ { n } \alpha e ^ { - n \alpha } \sin n \alpha t = { \frac { \alpha e ^ { - \alpha } \sin \alpha t } { 1 - 2 e ^ { - \alpha } \cos \alpha t + e ^ { - 2 \alpha } } } ,
$$

for example (Section 15.9). The sum of the series on the left-hand side is developable in powers of $\alpha$ and $t$ .The same is true for each term of this series.Even if we expand each term of the series,combine similar terms,and then arrange in powers of $\alpha$ and $t$ ，we do not get the expansion obtainable by direct summation of the series.

In order to pass from one expansion to the other,we consider the following example :

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial y _ { 1 } } , \qquad \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial y _ { 2 } } , \qquad \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 1 } } , \qquad \frac { d y _ { 2 } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
F = x _ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \mu \cos y _ { 1 } .
$$

Since $\partial F / \partial y _ { 2 } = 0$ ， the variable $x _ { 2 }$ should be a constant. Hence we get

$$
x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = C - \mu \cos y _ { 1 } .
$$

Substituting in the third equation of the canonical set, we obtain

$$
t + h = \int \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { 2 \sqrt { C - \mu \cos y _ { 1 } } } .
$$

Thus $x _ { 1 }$ cos $y _ { 1 }$ ,and sin $y _ { 1 }$ are doubly periodic functions of $t + h$ . One, of period $\omega$ , is real and the other,of period $\sqrt { - 1 } \omega ^ { \prime }$ , is purely imaginary. The function $x _ { 1 }$ ，being periodic with period $\omega$ ,is developable by Fourier's theorem in sines and cosines of multiples of $2 \pi t / \omega$ ，and the expansion is valid for all real values of $t$ .This function $x _ { 1 }$ , being also periodic with period $\sqrt { - 1 } \omega ^ { \prime }$ ，is equally developable in sines and cosines of multiples of $2 \pi t / ( \sqrt { - 1 } \omega ^ { \prime } )$ , or in powers of the exponentials exp $\scriptstyle \{ 2 \pi t / \omega ^ { \prime } \}$ and exp $\left\{ - 2 \pi t / \omega ^ { \prime } \right\}$ . This new expansion is not valid for all real values of $t$ but only for those in the interval between $t _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 0 } + \omega$ . For an interval between $t _ { 0 } + \omega$ and $t _ { 0 } + 2 \omega$ or between $t _ { 0 } +$ $2 \omega , t _ { 0 } + 3 \omega , \ldots ,$ ，the expansion must be replaced by another expansion of a similar form.The first of these expansions corresponds to the series (31),and the second to the series (41).From the theory of doubly periodic functions,we know of several methods for passing from one expansion to the other.Delaunay's theory (Section 9.1) leads to the equations analogous to the one just discussed, in which only a single term is retained in the disturbing function.

In the general case the expansions for the four variables $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ can be given, from which we can easily derive the series (31) and the series (41).The variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 }$ ,cos $y _ { 1 }$ ,cos $y _ { 2 }$ ,sin $y _ { 1 }$ ,and sin $y _ { 2 }$ are developed in powers of $\mu , A A ^ { \prime }$ ，and $C - C _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ ，which play the role of the integration constants.Each coefficient of these expansions is itself expanded in series,of which the terms are the product of two factors. The first factor is the sine or cosine of a multiple of a certain argument $W$ .The second factor is a doubly periodic function of the second argument $W ^ { \prime }$ . The two arguments $W$ and $W ^ { \prime }$ are linear functions of $t$ ，

$$
W = N t + \Pi , \qquad W ^ { \prime } = N ^ { \prime } t + \Pi ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where $\pi$ and $\Pi ^ { \prime }$ are the integration constants. We can assign an arbitrary value to $N ^ { \prime }$

Denote by $\omega$ and $\sqrt { - 1 } \omega ^ { \prime }$ the two periods of the doubly periodic function considered as a function of $W ^ { \prime }$ . The periods are $\omega / N ^ { \prime }$ and $\sqrt { - 1 } \omega ^ { \prime } / N ^ { \prime }$ if it is considered as a function of t. These periods are the same for all the terms. Now $N$ ， $\omega / N ^ { \prime }$ ， $\sqrt { - 1 } \omega ^ { \prime } / N ^ { \prime }$ are developable in powers of $\mu$ ， $A A ^ { \prime }$ ， $C - C _ { \circ }$ .Expand each doubly periodic function in series proceeding in sines and cosines of multiples of $2 \pi W ^ { \prime } / \omega$ . Then a series is obtained that proceeds in sines and cosines of multiples of two arguments $W$ and $2 \pi W ^ { \prime } / \omega$ ，or，what is the same thing,of multiples of two arguments $w _ { 1 } = n _ { 1 } t + \varpi _ { 1 }$ ， $w _ { 2 } = n _ { 2 } t + \varpi _ { 2 }$ ,where $\boldsymbol { \mathcal { W } } = \boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 } \boldsymbol { w } _ { 1 } +$ $r _ { \mathfrak { z } } w _ { \mathfrak { z } }$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { W ^ { \prime } = q _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + q _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } } \end{array}$ and $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 }$ are four integers defined in the above such that $q _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } - q _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } = 1$ with the small divisor $\delta = q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } +$ ${ q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } }$ . Thus we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { N = r _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } , } & { { } \quad \Pi = r _ { 1 } \varpi _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } \varpi _ { 2 } , } \\ { N ^ { \prime } = q _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } + q _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } , } & { { } \quad \Pi = q _ { 1 } \varpi _ { 1 } + q _ { 2 } \varpi _ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence we arrive at the series (31) by considering $A A ^ { \prime }$ and $C - C _ { 0 }$ as functions of the constants $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ .Next expand each doubly periodic function in a series proceeding in sines and cosines of multiples of $2 \pi W ^ { \prime } / ( \sqrt { - 1 } \omega ^ { \prime } )$ ，or in powers of the exponential exp $\left\{ \pm 2 \pi W ^ { \prime } / \omega ^ { \prime } \right\}$ The the series (41) is derived by writing

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \overline { { \alpha } } = \frac { 2 \pi N ^ { \prime } } { \omega ^ { \prime } } , \qquad \overline { { A } } = \sqrt { A A ^ { \prime } } \exp { \{ 2 \pi \Pi ^ { \prime } / \omega ^ { \prime } \} } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \overline { { A } } ^ { \prime } = \sqrt { A A ^ { \prime } } \exp \{ - 2 \pi \Pi ^ { \prime } / \omega ^ { \prime } \} . } \end{array}
$$

Thus either one of the two different series (31） and (41） can be derived from the other and vice versa.

# 15.15HILL'S ARGUMENT

Hill (1896,1896a) argued against Poincaré on the nonconvergence of the expansions in celestial mechanics (Section 15.15).Hill (1896, p.93) said “the reasons brought forward to sustain this opinion are scarcely convincing,and I think there has been some skepticism among astronomers in reference to the matter.”He pointed out a class of cases where the convergence of the series can be shown in spite of the incommensurability of the component arguments. He took an equation

$$
\frac { 1 } { \bar { n } } \frac { d \lambda } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \alpha ^ { i + | i ^ { \prime } | } \cos { ( i l + i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } ) } ,
$$

$$
l = n t + c , \quad \quad l ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + c ^ { \prime } , \quad \quad \alpha < 1 .
$$

The series on the right-hand side is convergent. Integrate this equation and we have

$$
\lambda = \varepsilon + \bar { n } t + \frac { \bar { n } } { \bar { n } } et { } { ' } { \sum } ^ { \prime } \frac 1 { i + i ^ { \prime } \mu } \alpha ^ { i + | i ^ { \prime } | } \sin { ( i l + i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } ) } , ~ \mu = \frac { n ^ { \prime } } { \bar { n } } ,
$$

where ε is an arbitrary constant, and $i = i ^ { \prime } = 0$ is excluded from the summation.When $\mu$ is irrational, then the summation constitutes a divergent series according to Poincaré (1893).Write the equations (43) in the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \ell = \varepsilon + \bar { n } t + \frac { \bar { n } } { n } \Big [ \sum _ { i } \frac { 1 } { i } \alpha ^ { i } \Big ( \sin i l + \frac { 1 } { \mu } \sin i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } \Big ) } } \\ { { + \left. 2 \sum _ { i } \sum _ { i ^ { \prime } } \frac { i } { i ^ { 2 } - i ^ { \prime 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } } \alpha ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } } \sin i l \cos i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } \right. } } \\ { { - \left. 2 \mu \sum _ { i } \frac { i ^ { \prime } } { i ^ { 2 } - i ^ { \prime 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } } \alpha ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } } \cos i l \sin i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } \right] , } } \end{array}
$$

where the sums are to be extended from 1 to $+ \infty$

The series (42) admits summation. For， arranging it according to multiples of ${ \mathbf { } } l { \mathbf { \prime } }$ ， we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { 1 } { \bar { n } } \frac { d \lambda } { d t } = ( 1 + \alpha \cos l + \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { \bar { n } } \frac { d \lambda } { d t } = } + ( 1 + 2 \alpha \cos l + 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \alpha \cos l ^ { \prime } } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { \bar { n } } \frac { d \lambda } { d t } = } + ( 1 + 2 \alpha \cos l + 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l ^ { \prime } } \\ & { + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { = ( 1 + \alpha \cos l + \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) } \\ & { \phantom { = } + ( 1 + 2 \alpha \cos l + 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) } \\ & { \phantom { = } \phantom { + } \times ( \alpha \cos l ^ { \prime } + \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) } \\ & { = \frac { 1 + \alpha ^ { 4 } - \alpha ( 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } ) ( \cos l + \cos l ^ { \prime } ) + 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos l \cos l ^ { \prime } } { ( 1 - 2 \alpha \cos l + \alpha ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - 2 \alpha \cos l ^ { \prime } + \alpha ^ { 2 } ) } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus we obtain

$$
\lambda = \varepsilon + \bar { n } t + \frac { \bar { n } } { n } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t }
$$

$$
\left\{ \frac { ( 1 - \alpha ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { [ 1 - 2 \alpha \cos { ( n t + c ) } + \alpha ^ { 2 } ] [ 1 - 2 \alpha \cos { ( n ^ { \prime } t + c ^ { \prime } ) } + \alpha ^ { 2 } ] } - 1 \right\} d t
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { X _ { i } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 1 - i ^ { 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } } \alpha \cos l + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 4 - i ^ { 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } } \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 9 - i ^ { 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } } \alpha ^ { 3 } \cos 3 l + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
P _ { i } = - \frac { d X _ { i } } { d t } , \qquad Q _ { i } = i X _ { i } , \qquad L = \frac { \alpha \cos l - \alpha ^ { 2 } } { 1 - 2 \alpha \cos l + \alpha ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

then (44) takes the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \lambda = \displaystyle { \varepsilon + \bar { n } t + \frac { \bar { n } } { n } \left[ \sum _ { i } \frac { 1 } { i } \alpha ^ { i } \Bigl ( \sin i l + \frac { 1 } { \mu } \cos i l ^ { \prime } \Bigr ) \right. } } } \\ { { \left. \qquad + \ 2 ( P _ { 1 } \alpha \cos l ^ { \prime } + P _ { 2 } \alpha ^ { 2 } \cos 2 l ^ { \prime } + P _ { 3 } \alpha ^ { 3 } \cos 3 l ^ { \prime } + \cdots ) \right. } } \\ { { \left. \qquad - \ 2 \mu ( Q _ { 1 } \alpha \sin l ^ { \prime } + Q _ { 2 } \alpha ^ { 2 } \sin 2 l + Q _ { 3 } \alpha ^ { 3 } \sin 3 l ^ { \prime } + \cdots \right. } } \end{array}
$$

The function $X _ { i }$ satisfies

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } X _ { i } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + i ^ { 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } X _ { i } + L = 0 .
$$

By integrating we find

$$
X _ { i } = \cos { ( i \mu l ) } \int L \sin { ( i \mu l ) } d l - \sin { ( i \mu l ) } \int L \cos { ( i \mu l ) } d l .
$$

The first sum of (47) may be obtained by the use of the well-known relation

$$
\sum { \frac { 1 } { i } } \alpha ^ { i } \sin i l = - { \frac { 1 } { i } } l + \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ { \frac { 1 + \alpha } { 1 - \alpha } } \tan { \frac { l } { 2 } } \right] .
$$

Since the convergence character does not alter even if we substitute

$$
\sin i l = \cos i l = \sin i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } = \cos i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } = 1
$$

in (44), it is sufficient to consider two series

$$
\sum \sum { \frac { i } { i ^ { 2 } - i ^ { \prime 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } } } e ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } } , ~ \sum \sum \frac { i ^ { \prime } } { i ^ { 2 } - i ^ { \prime 2 } \mu ^ { 2 } } e ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } } .
$$

We assume that $\mu = { \sqrt { h } }$ ，where $h$ is a non-square integer. The divisor is $i ^ { 2 } - h i ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 }$ . From the theory of indeterminate equations of the second degree (Section 15.3) we learn that the least absolute value of this expression is unity ; i.e.,we can write

$$
| i ^ { 2 } - h i ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 } | \geq 1 .
$$

Thus we are led to the consideration of the series $\sum \sum i \alpha ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } }$ . But this series is convergent, since

$$
\begin{array}{c} \left( \sum _ { \begin{array} { l } { i \alpha ^ { i } } \end{array} } \left( \sum _ { \begin{array} { l } { \alpha ^ { i } } \end{array} } \right) \right. \qquad \\ { \qquad = ( \alpha + 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } + 3 \alpha ^ { 3 } + \cdots ) ( \alpha + \alpha ^ { 2 } + \alpha ^ { 3 } + \cdots ) . } \end{array}
$$

If we take $j$ terms of each factor as an approximation, then the error committed is

$$
{ \frac { j ( 1 - \alpha ) + 2 } { ( 1 - \alpha ) ^ { 3 } } } \alpha ^ { j + 2 } ,
$$

which we may make as small as we please by taking $j$ sufficiently large.

Next Hill took

$$
\mu ^ { 2 } = { \frac { p } { q } } + \ { \sqrt { \frac { p ^ { \prime } } { q ^ { \prime } } } } ,
$$

where $p , q , p ^ { \prime } , q ^ { \prime }$ are integers and $p ^ { \prime } / q ^ { \prime }$ is not an exact square. Substitute this in $i ^ { 2 } - i ^ { \prime 2 } \mu ^ { 2 }$ ，and rationalize so that this denominator becomes an integer, after multiplying by a proper factor ; then it becomes

$$
q ^ { \prime } ( q ^ { 2 } i ^ { 2 } - p ^ { 2 } i ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 2 } - p ^ { \prime } q ^ { 4 } i ^ { 4 } .
$$

This expression cannot vanish, for that would make $\sqrt { p ^ { \prime } / q ^ { \prime } }$ rational. Hence its absolute value is at least unity. Thus we can substitute unity for it in the summation without affecting the convergence.This latter series is seen to depend on the convergence of two series

$$
\sum \sum i ^ { 3 } \alpha ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } } , \qquad \sum \sum i ^ { 2 } \dot { a } ^ { i ^ { \prime } } \alpha ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } } .
$$

These summations are obviously convergent.

As a still greater generalization, suppose that $\mu$ is an irrational root of an algebraic equation with rational coefficients.In a similar manner we rationalize $i ^ { 2 } - i ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 } \mu ^ { 2 }$ and make it an integer by multiplying with an appropriate factor.The absolute value of the denominator modified in this manner is at least unity. The convergence of this series depends on that of various summations involved, which are of the type

$$
\sum \sum i ^ { \nu } i ^ { \prime \nu ^ { \prime } } \alpha ^ { i + i ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

where $\nu$ and $\nu ^ { \prime }$ are finite positive. The convergence is obvious.

When $\mu$ is rational,we simply transfer the terms which become proportional to $t$ in the integration to the term ${ \bar { n } } t$ of (44). Then the remainder constitutes a convergent periodic series. Thus in all cases: where $\mu$ is a root of an algebraic equation with rational coefficients, the series (44) is convergent.

This analysis cannot be applied when $\mu$ is a root of a transcendental equation. The higher the degree of the equation having $\mu$ as a root is, the larger become the exponents $\nu$ and $\nu ^ { \prime }$ . Thus one is led to think that when $\mu$ is a root of a transcendental equation these exponents become infinite.Should this be so,the summations whose general type has just been given become divergent. But we could not be warranted in concluding thence the divergence of (44). This is Hil's argument.

Poincaré $( 1 8 9 5 , \mathbb { : } 1 8 9 6 , 1 8 9 6 \mathrm { a } )$ said that Hill had proved the theorem which Poincaré had proved and that there was no disagreement be-tween them. It should be borne in mind that what Poincaré proved was the nonuniformity of the convergence ； this was the point that Hill missed in his argument.

# DIVERGENCE OF BOHLIN'S SERIES

In Section 14.9 the solutions of the differential equations for the three-body problem

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , \cdot \cdot \cdot , n ) ,
$$

is obtained in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu x _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } \\ { y _ { i } = y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu y _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } y _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x _ { i } ^ { ( k ) } , y _ { i } ^ { ( k ) } = A _ { 0 } + \displaystyle \sum _ { } { \cal A } \cos \left( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } w _ { n } + h \right) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { w _ { i } = n _ { i } t + \displaystyle \varpi . } } \end{array}
$$

The following questions arise: (i) Are the partial series (49) conver-gent and is the convergence absolute and uniform? (i) If we admit that the series (49) are not absolutely convergent, can we arrange the terms so that the series are semi-convergent (Section 14.3)? (iii) By admiting that the series (49) are convergent, are the series (48) convergent and is the convergence uniform?

We obtain the series (49) (Section 14.9) by integrating

$$
\sum n _ { k } ^ { ( 0 ) } { \frac { \partial x _ { i } ^ { ( k ) } } { \partial w _ { k } } } = \ \sum B \cos \left( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } w _ { n } + h \right)
$$

in the form

$$
x _ { i } ^ { ( k ) } = \sum { \frac { B \sin { ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } w _ { n } + h ) } } { m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + m _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } n _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } } } + A _ { 0 } ,
$$

where $\pmb { A } _ { 0 }$ are arbitrary constants.The first question is:Are the series (50) absolutely and uniformly convergent? If they were so, then the series should be finite for all values of t.Poincaré (1884, Section 15.10) proved that they are not. Hence, in order that the series be uniformly convergent, the absolute value of the coefficient

$$
\frac { B } { m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \dots + m _ { n } n _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } }
$$

should be bounded.For fixing the idea,Poincaré (1893) took the case of two degrees of freedom and let $n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } = n$ ， $n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } = - 1$ ； then (50) becomes

$$
A _ { 0 } + \displaystyle et { } { ' } \sum \ d \frac { B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } \sin \left( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } + h _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } \right) } { m _ { 1 } n - m _ { 2 } }
$$

and the absolute value of $B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } / ( m _ { 1 } n - m _ { 2 } )$ should be bounded. This cannot happen for a commensurable value of $_ n$ ， unless $B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } }$ are zero for $m _ { 2 } / m _ { 1 } = n$

Take an incommensurable value of $_ n$ and consider the divisor $m _ { 1 } n -$ $m _ { 2 }$ that corresponds to the successive reduced fraction for $_ n$ . Whatever the series for $B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } }$ may be, we can find an incommensurable number $\pmb { n }$ such that the absolute value of the coefficients $B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } / ( m _ { 1 } n - m _ { 2 } )$ is not bounded.In fact, let the successive reduced fractions for $_ n$ be ${ \alpha _ { 1 } } / { \beta _ { 1 } } , { \alpha _ { 2 } } / { \beta _ { 2 } } , \ldots , { \alpha _ { p } } / { \beta _ { p } } , \ldots ,$ ，and consider a sequence of indefinitely increasing positive numbers $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { p } , \ldots .$ 、We can choose the number $_ n$ such that

$$
\left| \frac { B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } \beta _ { p } \alpha _ { p } } { n \beta _ { p } - \alpha _ { p } } \right| > \lambda _ { p } .
$$

From the definition of the reduced fraction (Section 15.3) we have

$$
\alpha _ { p + 1 } = \alpha _ { p - 1 } + \alpha _ { p } a _ { p + 1 } , \qquad \beta _ { p + 1 } = \beta _ { p - 1 } + \beta _ { p } a _ { p + 1 } ,
$$

where $a _ { p + 1 }$ is a positive integer to be chosen arbitrarily without affecting the first $p$ reduced fractions. On the other hand,

$$
| n \beta _ { p } - \alpha _ { p } | < \frac { 1 } { \beta _ { p - 1 } + \beta _ { p } a _ { p + 1 } } .
$$

Hence we can choose the integer $a _ { p + 1 }$ such that the absolute value of $n \beta _ { p } - \alpha _ { p }$ will be as small as we please, and consequently (51) is satisfied whatever the values of $\bar { B } _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } \beta _ { p } \alpha _ { p }$ and $\lambda _ { p }$ may be. Since the sequence $\{ \lambda _ { p } \}$ is indefinitely increasing，we can choose arbitrarily the first $q$ numbers $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { q }$ and hence the first $q$ reduced fractions.Thus $\pmb { n }$ can be made as close to any assigned number as we please.

On the other hand, we can find a number $_ n$ such that the series (50) are convergent.In fact,if the series

$$
\sum B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } \cos { ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } + h _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } ) }
$$

be convergent, then for any pair of values of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ we should have

$$
| B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } | < K \alpha ^ { | m _ { 1 } | } \beta ^ { | m _ { 2 } | } ,
$$

where $\kappa$ is a positive constant, and $_ { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers less than 1. Put $n = { \sqrt { p / q } }$ with two mutually prime integers $p$ and $\pmb q$ such that $p q$ is not a perfect square.Then

$$
\left| \frac { 1 } { m _ { 1 } n - m _ { 2 } } \right| = \left| \frac { m _ { 1 } n + m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } n ^ { 2 } - m _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right| = \left| \frac { ( m _ { 1 } n + m _ { 2 } ) q } { p m _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - q m _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right| < q ( | m _ { 1 } | n + | m _ { 2 } | )
$$

or

$$
\frac { B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } \sin { ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } w _ { 2 } + h _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } ) } } { m _ { 1 } n - m _ { 2 } } \Big | < K q ( | m _ { 1 } | n + | m _ { 2 } | ) \alpha ^ { | m _ { 1 } | } \beta ^ { | m _ { 2 } | } .
$$

Thus (5O) are convergent.However we can evidently choose integers $p$ and $\pmb q$ such that ${ \sqrt { p / q } } = n$ is as close to any assigned number as we please.Thus we obtain the

Theorem: Let $\kappa$ be any positive number and $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ be posi-tive numbers less than 1. Suppose that we have an inequality analogous to (52)

$$
| B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } | < K \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { 1 m _ { 1 } | } \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 1 m _ { 2 } | } \ \cdot \ \cdot \ \alpha _ { n } ^ { 1 m _ { n } | } .
$$

Then we can choose a sequence of numbers $n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , n _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ，such that (i) they are as close to $_ n$ given numbers as we please and the series (5O) are not uniformly convergent; (ii) they are as close to the same $_ n$ given numbers as we please and the series (5O) are uniformly convergent.

Since the observations are restricted to the accuracy of the observational technique, the mean motions, for example,can be obtained only as an approximation. Thus it is within the accuracy of the observations that the series (5O) may be arranged so that the series (50) are convergent. However, there is a question whether the series are convergent for the values of the integration constants $x _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ within a certain interval. This would be possible according to the theorem just proved only when the series

$$
\sum B \cos ( m _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } w _ { n } + h )
$$

contains only a limited number of terms.

The next question is whether the series (48) are convergent, that is, (i) whether the series are uniformly convergent for all values of $\mu$ and the integration constants $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ within a certain interval,and (ii) whether the series are uniformly convergent for all small values of $\mu$ when the values of $\boldsymbol { x } _ { 0 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ are suitably chosen.

The first question has been answered in the negative. Suppose that the series (48) were uniformly convergent and written in the form

$$
x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { 0 } + \mu \phi _ { i } ( w _ { k } , x _ { k } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \mu ) , ~ y _ { i } = w _ { i } + \mu \psi _ { i } ( w _ { k } , x _ { k } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \mu ) ,
$$

where $\phi _ { i }$ and $\psi _ { i }$ are functions developable in ascending powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ ， periodic with respect to $w$ ，and dependent on $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . Suppose that we solve these equations with respect to $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $w _ { i }$ in the form of series proceeding in powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ with coefficients depending on $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ This would be possible,because the equations reduce to $x _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = x _ { i }$ ， $\dot { \boldsymbol { w } } _ { i } = \boldsymbol { y } _ { i }$ for $\mu = 0$ ，and the Jacobian is equal to 1.Then we should have

$$
x _ { i } ^ { 0 } = x _ { i } + \mu \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y _ { k } , x _ { k } , \mu ) , \qquad w _ { i } = y _ { i } + \mu \psi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y _ { k } , x _ { k } , \mu ) ,
$$

where $\phi _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and $\psi _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ are functions developable in ascending powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ ， uniform with regard to $x _ { k }$ and $y _ { k }$ ， and periodic with respect to $y _ { k }$ The first set of equations would then define $_ n$ uniform integrals.On the other hand we have $w _ { i } = n _ { i } t + \varpi _ { i }$ ，where $n _ { i }$ depend on $\mu$ and $x _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ · By varying these latter constants within a certain range,we can make the coefficients $n _ { i }$ commensurable to each other. Thus a number $T$ can be chosen so that the $n _ { i } T$ are multiples of $2 \pi$ ，i.e.,the solution (53) would represent a periodic solution of period $_ T$ ，if the constants $\mu$ and $\boldsymbol { x } _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ were chosen in this manner. But the existence of uniform integrals shows that the $n + 1$ characteristic exponents relative to this periodic solution (Section 19.1） are zero,according to the theorem which wil be proved in Section 19.2. This cannot, however, happen in general.

If the series (53) were uniformly convergent and if we chose the integration constants $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ so that the mean motions $n _ { i }$ were commensurable to each other, then the differential equations would admit a periodic solution $x _ { i } = f _ { i } ( t )$ ， $y _ { i } = f _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( t )$ . Suppose that we wrote $x _ { i } =$ $f _ { i } + \xi _ { i }$ ， $y _ { i } = f _ { i } ^ { \prime } + \eta _ { i }$ ，and formed the equations for variations (Sections 12.1 and 19.1). These latter equations would be satisfied, whatever the values of $x _ { k } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $\varpi _ { k }$ might be, by $2 n$ linearly independent particular solutions

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle { \hat { s } _ { i } = \varepsilon _ { i k } + t \mu \cdot \sum _ { p } \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial w _ { p } } \frac { \partial n _ { p } } { \partial x _ { k } ^ { ( i ) } } + \mu \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial x _ { k } ^ { ( i ) } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \gamma _ { i } = \frac { \partial n _ { i } } { \partial x _ { k } ^ { ( i ) } } t + t \mu \cdot \sum _ { p } \frac { \partial \Psi _ { i } } { \partial w _ { p } } \frac { \partial n _ { p } } { \partial x _ { k } ^ { ( i ) } } + \mu \frac { \partial V _ { i } } { \partial x _ { k } ^ { ( i ) } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \hat { s } _ { i } = \mu \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial w _ { k } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \gamma _ { i } = \varepsilon _ { i k } + \mu \frac { \partial \psi _ { i } } { \partial w _ { k } } } } } & { { ( i , k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\varepsilon _ { i k } = 1$ for $i = k$ and $\varepsilon _ { i k } = 0$ for $i \neq k$ . When the constants $\boldsymbol { x } _ { k } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $\varpi _ { k }$ were replaced in $\partial \phi _ { i } / \partial w _ { p }$ ， $\partial \phi _ { i } / \partial x _ { k } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ， $\partial \psi _ { i } / \partial w _ { p }$ ， $\partial \psi _ { i } / \partial x _ { k } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ by their values corresponding to the periodic solution，these partial derivatives would become periodic in $t$ Thus the $_ { 2 n }$ characteristic exponents would be zero,according to the theorem on characteristic exponents which will be proved in Section 19.2. This case cannot happen in general. Hence the series (48) cannot be uniformly convergent.

Now consider the second question whether the series are convergent for all small values of $\mu$ when the values of $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ are suitably chosen.

Suppose that $n _ { i }$ depend on $\mu$ and, for the sake of argument, that we are dealing with two degrees of freedom. Then we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { n _ { i } = n _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu n _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu ^ { 2 } n _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdots , } } \\ { { w _ { i } = n _ { i } t + \infty _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

We can expand $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ in powers of $\mu$ so that $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i } - w _ { i }$ are periodic with regard to $w _ { i }$ . Among small values of $\mu$ within a certain limit we can choose such a value of $\mu$ as to make the numbers $n _ { 1 }$ and $n _ { 2 }$ mutually commensurable,since their ratio is a continuous function of $\mu$ . For such a commensurable ratio the series (48) represent a periodic solution for all values of the integration constants $\widetilde { \varpi } _ { 1 }$ and $\widetilde { \omega } _ { 2 }$ If the series (49) were convergent, then a doubly infinite number of periodic solutions would correspond to the commensurable ratio $n _ { 1 } / n _ { 2 }$ Such periodic solutions would appear only in very particular cases. Hence it is improbable that the series (49) are uniformly convergent.

If $n _ { i }$ do not depend on $\mu$ ， then a similar situation can be proved to exist,and it is also improbable that the series (49) are uniformly convergent.

This is how Poincaré (1893) proved the nonuniform convergence of the series in celestial mechanics.

Recently Merman (1961) proved the divergence of a Lindstedt series in connection with almost periodic functions.

# 15.17DIVERGENCE OF BOHLIN'S SERIES

Consider the series discussed in Section 14.11. These series depend on a certain number of arbitrary constants $\boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \boldsymbol { x } _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , \boldsymbol { x } _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ，among which there is a relation

$$
m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + m _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } n _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } = 0 .
$$

Suppose,as in Section 14.11, that $m _ { 1 } = 1$ ， $m _ { 2 } = m _ { 3 } = { \cdot \cdot \cdot } = m _ { n } = 0$ ； then the relation becomes

$$
- n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } = \frac { \partial \bar { F } _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } } = 0 .
$$

By solving this relation we obtain

$$
x _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = \phi ( x _ { 2 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } , x _ { 3 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } ) .
$$

We have another relation

$$
F _ { 0 } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) = C _ { 0 } .
$$

We introduced in Section 14.11 further constants $C _ { 2 } , C _ { 4 } , \ldots ,$ and $\alpha _ { 1 }$ ， $\alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } ; ~ \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } , ~ \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { ( 2 ) } , \ldots , ~ \alpha _ { n } ^ { ( 2 ) } ~ ; ~ \ldots ~ ; ~ \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { ( p ) } , ~ \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { ( p ) } , \ldots , ~ \alpha _ { n } ^ { ( p ) }$ We have taken $\alpha _ { 1 } = \alpha _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \alpha _ { n } = 0$ ，since the constants $\alpha _ { i }$ are arbitrary. We have supposed (see formula (57) Section 14.11) that

$$
\frac { \alpha _ { 1 } ^ { ( p ) } } { m _ { 1 } } = \frac { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { ( p ) } } { m _ { 2 } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { \alpha _ { n } ^ { ( p ) } } { m _ { n } } ,
$$

or that all the $\alpha _ { i }$ and $\alpha _ { i } ^ { ( { p } ) }$ are zero，without loss of generality. If we suppose that all the $\alpha _ { i }$ and $\alpha _ { i } ^ { ( { p } ) }$ are zero, then $C _ { 4 } , C _ { 6 } , \ldots$ .become functions of $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ . Thus we are left with $_ n$ arbitrary constants $x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 3 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ,and $C _ { 2 }$ . Now, if we solve the relations

$$
x _ { 1 } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial y _ { 1 } } , \qquad x _ { 2 } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial y _ { 2 } } , \qquad \ldots , \qquad x _ { n } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial y _ { n } } ,
$$

in which the right-hand members are functions of $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n } , x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ， $x _ { 3 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , C _ { 2 } ,$ with regard to $x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 3 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , C _ { 2 } ,$ then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { x _ { j } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = \psi _ { j } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) \quad } & { { } ( j = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ) , } \\ { C _ { 2 } = \theta ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ) . } \end{array}
$$

If the series $s$ were convergent, then $\psi _ { j }$ and $\theta$ would be the integrals of the differential equations.

In the case of revolution, $C _ { 2 }$ is greater than the maximum of $[ F _ { 1 } ]$ Thus

$$
\sqrt { C _ { 2 } - [ F _ { 1 } ] } , \qquad \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { C _ { 2 } - [ F _ { 1 } ] } }
$$

are holomorphic functions of $C _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ ， for all real values of $y _ { i }$ and for the values of $C _ { 2 }$ in the region that we consider. We have supposed that $F$ is a holomorphic function of $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ for all real values of $y _ { i }$ and for the values of $x _ { i }$ in the region of $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ that we consider. Furthermore the second derivative of $F _ { 0 }$ with regard to $x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ is not zero in general, so that $x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ is a holomorphic function of the other $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . Hence the ${ \partial S _ { p } } / { \partial y _ { i } }$ are holomorphic functions for all real values of $y _ { i }$ and for the values of $x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , C _ { 2 }$ in the region that we consider. Hence, if we denote the values of these constants within the region under consideration by $\lambda _ { 2 } , \lambda _ { 3 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } .$ $\boldsymbol { \gamma }$ and put $\lambda _ { 1 } = \phi ( \lambda _ { 2 } , \lambda _ { 3 } , . . . , \lambda _ { n } )$ ， then both sides of (54) can be expanded in powers of

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \sqrt \mu , \quad } & { { } x _ { 1 } - \lambda _ { 1 } , \quad \quad \ldots , \quad \quad x _ { n } - \lambda _ { n } , } \\ { x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } - \lambda _ { 1 } , \quad } & { { } \ldots , \quad } & { x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } - \lambda _ { n } , \quad \quad C _ { 2 } - \gamma , } \end{array}
$$

proceeding in sines and cosines of multiples of $y _ { i }$

In order to be able to apply the theorem for implicit functions (Section 18.3),we write

$$
x _ { 1 } = \phi ( x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) + \sqrt { \mu } x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and obtain the first equation of (54) in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \phi ( x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) + \sqrt { \mu } x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } \\ & { \phantom { x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x } } \\ & { \phantom { x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x } = \phi ( x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) + \sqrt { \mu } \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \mu \frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \cdots , } \end{array}
$$

or,using the remaining equations of (54),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \sqrt \mu x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \phi \left( \displaystyle { \frac { \partial S } { \partial y _ { 2 } } } , \ldots , \displaystyle { \frac { \partial S } { \partial y _ { n } } } \right) - \phi ( x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } + \sqrt \mu \displaystyle { \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } + \mu \displaystyle { \frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } + \cdots . } } \end{array}
$$

Since we know that $\partial S _ { 1 } / \partial y _ { 2 } , . . . , \partial S _ { 1 } / \partial y _ { n }$ are zero, the difference $( { \hat { \partial } \mathbf { S } } / { \hat { \partial } { y _ { j } } } ) - x _ { j } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ，and hence $\phi ( \partial S / \partial y _ { j } ) - \phi ( x _ { j } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } ) ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n )$ ，are divisible by $\mu$ .Hence we can write

$$
\phi \Bigl ( \frac { \partial S } { \partial y _ { 2 } } , \ldots , \frac { \partial S } { \partial y _ { n } } \Bigr ) - \phi ( x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) = \mu H .
$$

Thus we have

$$
x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } + H \sqrt { \mu } + { \frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } \sqrt { \mu } + { \frac { \partial S _ { 3 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } \mu + \cdots .
$$

Adding the last $n - 1$ equations of (54) to this equation we obtain $\pmb { n }$ equations in which both sides are developable in powers of $\sqrt { \mu } , x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ,$ $x _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } - \lambda _ { n } , x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } - \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { ( 0 ) } - \lambda _ { n } , C _ { 2 } - \gamma$ ，and proceeding in sines and cosines of multiples of $y _ { i }$ .For $\mu = 0$ these equations reduce to

$$
x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } , \qquad x _ { j } = x _ { j } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

The Jacobian of $x _ { j } ^ { ( 0 ) } - x _ { j }$ and $( \partial S _ { 1 } / \partial y _ { 1 } ) - x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ with respect to $\boldsymbol { x } _ { j } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ reduces to the derivative of ${ \partial S _ { 1 } } / { \partial y _ { 1 } }$ with regard to $C _ { 2 }$ ，or,if

$$
\frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = A \sqrt { C _ { 2 } - | F _ { 1 } | } ,
$$

then to

$$
{ \frac { A } { 2 { \sqrt { C _ { 2 } - | F _ { 1 } | } } } } .
$$

This cannot be zero and the theorem for implicit functions can be applied.If the series in question were convergent, then the differential equations of our problem would admit $_ n$ integrals $\psi _ { i }$ and $\theta$ ，uniform with respect to $x _ { k }$ and $y _ { k }$ ， and periodic with regard to $y _ { \kappa }$ . This is impossible, because of the nonexistence of uniform integrals as proved by Poincaré's theorem (Sections 6.15-6.18). Hence the series is divergent.

The same result holds for the case of libration.The series are also divergent for the limiting case, too (Section 14.12).

15.18MECHANISM OF DIVERGENCE OF BOHLIN'S SERIES

Poincaré(1893) extended the study to clarifying the mechanism of divergence of Bohlin's series (Section 14.12), that is,what term of the series causes the increase of the sum so that the series is no longer convergent.

Consider a dynamical system with two degrees of freedom. There is no small divisor in this case. The equations to be integrated are either of the form

$$
n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } \frac { \partial S _ { p } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = \Phi , \qquad \mathrm { o r } \qquad \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial [ S _ { p } ] } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = \Phi ,
$$

and the divisors expected to appear are $n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } m _ { 2 }$ and ${ \partial S _ { 1 } } / { \partial y _ { 1 } }$ ， that are not so small. In differentiating a term containing sines and cosines of $p _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \ldots + p _ { n } y _ { n }$ one of the integers $p _ { i }$ is introduced as factor,which can be very large. This prevents the series from being convergent. This is not because of a small divisor introduced by integration,but a large factor introduced by differentiation.

We may understand the circumstance in another way. In Section 14.9 we have met small divisors of the form $m _ { 1 } n _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + m _ { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ in the case of two degrees of freedom. Replace ${ n } _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and ${ n } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ by the expansions similar to

$$
n _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = \alpha _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \sqrt { \mu } \alpha _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mu \alpha _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

for example, by

$$
n _ { 2 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = \alpha _ { 2 } , \qquad n _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = \alpha _ { 1 } \sqrt { \mu } .
$$

The small divisor is now $m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } + m _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } \sqrt { \mu }$ . The expression

$$
\frac { 1 } { m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } + m _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } \sqrt { \mu } }
$$

can be expanded in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ ；

$$
\frac { 1 } { m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } } + \sqrt { \mu } \frac { m _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } } { ( m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } + \mu \frac { ( m _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } { ( m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } + \cdots .
$$

Since $m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 }$ never becomes very small, none of the terms of this expansion contains a very small divisor. However small $\mu$ may be taken, the integers $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ can be found so that

$$
\sqrt { \mu } \frac { m _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } } { m _ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } } > 1 ,
$$

and so that the above expansion in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ is divergent. Thus

we can see how the series become divergent by substituting for the mean motions their expansions in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$

We consider the following problem in order to understand in more detail the mechanism of divergence.

Let

$$
- F = p + q ^ { 2 } - 2 \mu \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } - \mu \varepsilon \phi ( y ) \cos x ,
$$

where $p , x ; q , y$ are two pairs of canonically conjugate variables, $\phi ( y )$ is periodic with regard to $y$ with period $2 \pi$ ，and $\mu$ and $\varepsilon$ are small arbitrary constants. From the canonical equations

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d x } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial p } = 1 , \qquad \displaystyle \frac { d y } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial q } = 2 q , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d p } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial x } = - \mu \varepsilon \phi ( y ) \sin { x } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d q } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial y } = \mu \sin { y } + \mu \varepsilon \phi ^ { \prime } ( y ) \cos { x } , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = 2 \mu \sin y + 2 \mu \varepsilon \phi ^ { \prime } ( y ) \cos x .
$$

With a constant $C$ we write the Hamilton-Jacobi equation

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial S } { \partial x } + \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial y } \right) ^ { 2 } = 2 \mu \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } + \mu \varepsilon \phi ( y ) \cos x + C , } } \end{array}
$$

and expand in powers of $\varepsilon$ ：

$$
S = S _ { 0 } + \varepsilon S _ { 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } S _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad C = C _ { 0 } + \varepsilon C _ { 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } C _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

For $\varepsilon = 0$ we have

$$
\frac { \partial S _ { 0 } } { \partial x } + \left( \frac { \partial S _ { 0 } } { \partial y } \right) ^ { 2 } = 2 \mu \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } + C _ { 0 } .
$$

The complete integral is obtained with a constant $\scriptstyle A _ { 0 }$

$$
\frac { \partial S _ { 0 } } { \partial x } = A _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } \qquad C _ { 0 } = A _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } + 2 h \mu ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial S _ { 0 } } { \partial y } = \sqrt { 2 \mu } \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
S _ { 0 } = A _ { 0 } x + \sqrt { 2 \mu } \int \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } } \cdot d y .
$$

Thus we arrive at an example of the method which we have discussed in Section 14.11. The case $h > 0$ corresponds to revolution, the case $h < 0$ to libration,and the case $h = 0$ to asymptotic motion.

First we have a simple solution

$$
x = t , \qquad p = 0 , \qquad y = 0 , \qquad q = 0 ,
$$

which is a periodic solution. What is the corresponding asymptotic solution? Put $A _ { 0 } = h = 0$ in $S _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ ， then we find

$$
S _ { \circ } = \mp 2 \sqrt { 2 \mu } \cos \frac { y } { 2 } ,
$$

from which we can derive

$$
p = 0 , \qquad q = \mp \sqrt { 2 \mu } \sin \frac { y } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\tan { \frac { y } { 4 } } = C \exp \{ \pm t { \sqrt { 2 \mu } } \} , \qquad x = t .
$$

This shows that the characteristic exponents are equal to $\pm { \sqrt { 2 \mu } }$

Equating the coefficients of $\varepsilon$ in (56) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial x } + 2 \frac { \partial S _ { 0 } } { \partial y } \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y } = \mu \phi ( y ) \cos x + C _ { 1 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $C _ { 1 }$ is a constant which is supposed to be zero without loss of generality, or

$$
{ \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial x } } + 2 \sqrt { 2 \mu } \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { y } { 2 } } } \cdot { \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial y } } = \mu \phi ( y ) \cos x .
$$

$S _ { 1 }$ is the real part of a function $\Sigma$ defined by

$$
\frac { \partial \Sigma } { \partial x } + 2 \sqrt { 2 \mu } \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } } \cdot \frac { \partial \Sigma } { \partial y } = \mu \phi ( y ) \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } x \} } .
$$

Put

$$
\sum = \psi \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } x \} } ,
$$

then we get

$$
{ \sqrt { - 1 } } \psi + 2 { \sqrt { \mu } } { \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { y } { 2 } } } } { \cdot } { \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial y } } = \mu \phi .
$$

The solution of

$$
\alpha \psi + \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } } \cdot \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial y } = 0 , \alpha = \frac { \sqrt { - 1 } } { 2 \sqrt { 2 \mu } } ,
$$

is

$$
\psi = K \exp \{ - \alpha u \} , \qquad \int \frac { d y } { \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { 1 } { 2 } y } } = u ,
$$

where $\kappa$ is a constant.Next consider $\kappa$ to be a function of $y$ and take the equation

$$
2 \sqrt { 2 \mu } \frac { d K } { d y } \exp \{ - \alpha u \} \cdot \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } } = \mu \phi .
$$

The solution is

$$
\psi = \exp \left\{ - u \alpha \right\} \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \int \exp \left\{ \alpha u \right\} \cdot \phi d u .
$$

If we put $\alpha = \beta \sqrt { - 1 }$ ， $\beta$ being real, then we obtain

$$
{ \mathrm { ~  ~ \psi ~ } } _ { \mathrm { { s } } } = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \biggl [ \cos \left( \beta u - x \right) \int \phi \cos \beta u \cdot d u + \sin \left( \beta u - x \right) \int \phi \sin \beta u \cdot d
$$

The next approximation is derived from

$$
\frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial x } + 2 \sqrt { 2 \mu } \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } } \cdot \frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial y } = \Phi ,
$$

where $\Phi$ is a known function of $y$ and $x$ ， periodic with regard to $x$ Put

$$
\Phi = \sum _ { n = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \phi _ { n } \exp { \{ n \sqrt { - 1 } x \} } , ~ S _ { 2 } = \sum _ { n = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \psi _ { n } \exp { \{ n \sqrt { - 1 } x \} } ,
$$

then we get

$$
\sqrt { - 1 } n \psi _ { n } + 2 \sqrt { 2 \mu } \sqrt { h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } } \cdot \frac { d \psi _ { n } } { d y } = \phi _ { n } .
$$

This equation has the same form as the equation (57a),and can be solved in a similar manner. $S _ { 3 } , S _ { 4 } , \ldots$ are obtained similarly by solving equations similar to (60).

Consider the equations (58) and (59).

In the revolution case $h > 0$ the function $\phi ( y )$ is periodic with regard to $\boldsymbol { y }$ and also with regard to $\pmb { u }$ . Put

$$
\phi = \sum A _ { m } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } m \lambda u \} ,
$$

where $\lambda$ is a real constant depending on the period of the elliptic integral $\pmb { u }$ ，and $_ m$ is an integer.We obtain

$$
