= \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \sum { A _ { m } \frac { \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } m \lambda u \} } } { \alpha + \sqrt { - 1 } m \lambda } } = \sum { \frac { \mu A _ { m } } { \sqrt { - 1 } } \frac { \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } m \lambda u \} } } { 1 + m \lambda \sqrt { 8 \mu } } } .
$$

Let $\rho _ { m }$ and $\omega _ { m }$ be respectively the modulus and the argument of $A _ { m }$ then

$$
S _ { 1 } = \sum \mu \rho _ { m } \frac { \sin { ( m \lambda u + x + \omega _ { m } ) } } { 1 + m \lambda \sqrt { 8 \mu } } .
$$

Each term of $S _ { \mathfrak { i } }$ is developable in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ .We carry out the expansion and then unite the terms factored by $\sqrt { \mu }$ into one.Then we get a formal expansion of $S _ { 1 }$ in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ ：

$$
S _ { 1 } = \sum T _ { p } \mu ^ { p / 2 } ,
$$

where

$$
T _ { p + 2 } = ( - \lambda \sqrt { 8 } ) ^ { p } \sum _ { \ L } { m ^ { p } \rho _ { \ L { m } } \sin { ( m \lambda u + x + \omega _ { m } ) } } .
$$

We might as well expand in the form

$$
S = S _ { 0 } + \sqrt { \mu } S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \mu S _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \mu ^ { p / 2 } S _ { p } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $S _ { p } ^ { \prime }$ are expanded in powers of $\varepsilon$ . The coefficient of $\varepsilon$ is $T _ { p }$ .The series $T _ { p }$ is convergent.In fact, we have

$$
\rho _ { m } < k h _ { 0 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 m 1 } , \qquad h _ { 0 } < 1 , \qquad k , h _ { 0 } > 0 ,
$$

since $\phi ( y )$ is holomorphic for all real values of $y$ .Hence the series $\sum m ^ { p } \rho _ { m }$ is absolutely convergent. Thus $T _ { p + 2 }$ is absolutely convergent.

On the other hand, the series (61) is convergent but the series (62) is not.To see this we consider a very particular example. Put

$$
= \frac { \pi } { 2 } , ~ u = 0 , ~ \omega _ { m } = 0 , ~ \rho _ { m } = A ^ { { \scriptscriptstyle m } 1 } , ~ 0 < A < 1 , ~ \lambda = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 8 } } ,
$$

then we have

$$
T _ { p + 2 } = \sum \ d ( - m ) ^ { p } A ^ { | m | } \qquad ( m = - \infty , \ldots , + \infty ) ,
$$

which shows that $T _ { p + 2 }$ is equal to zero for $p$ odd and equal to $2 \sum m ^ { p } A ^ { m } \left( m = 1 , 2 , . . . , + \infty \right)$ otherwise.Evidently

$$
\sum m ^ { p } A ^ { m } > \sum m ( m - 1 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( m - p + 1 ) A ^ { m } = \frac { p ! A ^ { p } } { ( 1 - A ) ^ { p } }
$$

Hence, for $A = 1 / 2$ for example

$$
T _ { p + 2 } > 2 ( p ! ) .
$$

Thus every other terms of the expansion (62) are zero and the remaining terms are larger than the corresponding terms of $2 \sum q ! \ : \mu ^ { q + 1 }$ ，of which the latter series is divergent. The circumstance is similar for the expansion of $S _ { 2 }$ and higher terms.

The libration case $h < 0$ can be discussed similarly， the only difference being that the real period of the integral is

$$
u _ { 1 } = \int _ { - \beta } ^ { + \beta } \frac { d y } { \sqrt { R } } , \qquad R = h + \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { y } { 2 } , \qquad \beta = 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \sqrt { - h } \right) ,
$$

in place of

$$
u _ { 0 } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { d y } { \sqrt { R } } ,
$$

and that $\lambda$ is not equal to $2 \pi / u _ { 0 }$ but to $2 \pi / u _ { 1 }$

The asymptotic case $h = 0$ is more interesting. We have

$$
u = \int \frac { d y } { \sin { ( y / 2 ) } } = 2 \log \tan \frac { y } { 4 } ,
$$

or

$$
\tan { \frac { y } { 4 } } = t , \qquad d u = \frac { 2 d t } { t } .
$$

Take for example

$$
\phi ( y ) = \sin y ,
$$

then we have

$$
\phi ( y ) = \frac { 4 t ( 1 - t ^ { 2 } ) } { ( 1 + t ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } , \qquad \psi = t ^ { - 2 \alpha } \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \int \frac { 4 t ^ { 2 \alpha } ( 1 - t ^ { 2 } ) d t } { ( 1 + t ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

or

$$
\psi = 4 \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \frac { t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } - \sqrt { - 1 } t ^ { - 2 \alpha } \int \frac { t ^ { 2 \alpha } d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Develop $\psi$ in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ only formally. For that purpose we find it better to treat the general case.While $y$ varies from O to $2 \pi , u$ varies from $- \infty$ to $+ \infty$ . The function $\phi ( y )$ , being a function of $u$ ， can be represented by a Fourier integral

$$
\phi ( y ) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } q u \} } \cdot \theta ( q ) d q ,
$$

provided that $\phi ( 0 ) = 0$ ，because $\phi ( y )$ is analytic and periodic for all real values of $y$ . Thus we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c c c } { \displaystyle { \psi = \sqrt { \frac \mu 8 } \exp \left\{ - \alpha u \right\} \cdot \int d u \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \exp \left\{ ( \alpha + \sqrt { - 1 } q ) u \right\} \cdot \theta ( q ) d q , } } \end{array}
$$

or,by inverting the order of integration,

$$
\begin{array} { c c c } { { \displaystyle { \mid }  { { \boldsymbol { r } } = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \exp \left\{ - \alpha u \right\} \cdot \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \left[ \frac { \exp \left\{ ( \alpha + \sqrt { \mathrm { - 1 } } q ) u \right\} } { \alpha + \sqrt { \mathrm { - 1 } } q } \theta ( q ) + \gamma ( q ) \theta ( q ) \right] } \mathrm { { } } } } \end{array}
$$

where $\eta ( q )$ is an arbitrary function of $q$ introduced in the integration. We can suppose $\eta ( q ) = 0$ for certain cases. Then we have

$$
\psi = \frac { \mu } { \sqrt { - 1 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \frac { \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } q u \right\} \cdot \theta ( q ) d q } { 1 + q \sqrt { 8 \mu } } \bullet
$$

Let the modulus and the argument of $\theta ( q )$ be $\pmb { \rho }$ and $\omega$ ，respectively, then

$$
S _ { 1 } = \mu \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } { \frac { \rho \sin { ( q u + x + \omega ) } } { 1 + \sqrt { 8 \mu } } } d q ,
$$

where $\rho$ and $\omega$ are functions of $q$ . It is necessary that the integral in (64) should be finite,that is,the integrand should not become infinite for $q = - 1 / \sqrt { 8 \mu }$ ，that is,we should have $\theta ( - 1 / \sqrt { 8 \mu } ) = 0$ But this relation does not always hold. Hence we replace (64) and (65) by

$$
\hat { \bf \Phi } = \frac { \mu } { \sqrt { - 1 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \frac { \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } q u \right\} - \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } q u _ { 0 } + \alpha ( u - u _ { 0 } ) \right\} } { 1 + q \sqrt { 8 } \mu } \theta ( q )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { S _ { 1 } = \mu \displaystyle { \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } } \frac { \rho { d q } } { 1 + q \sqrt { 8 \mu } } \biggl [ \sin { ( q u + x + \omega ) } } } \\ { { - \sin \biggl ( q u _ { 0 } + x + \omega + \frac { u _ { 0 } - u } { \sqrt { 8 \mu } } \biggr ) \biggr ] , } } \end{array}
$$

$\scriptstyle u _ { 0 }$ being an arbitrary constant.

Now

$$
\phi = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } q u } \right\} \cdot \theta ( q ) d q
$$

remains valid when the integral is taken no longer along the real axis but along a curve $c$ which lies always above but so near the real axis that no singular point of $\theta ( q )$ can be found between the curve and the real axis.Then (64) and (65) are valid by taking the integral along the contour $c$ ， since the integrand does not become infinite along the path of integration, whatever $\theta ( q )$ may be. The modulus of exp $\{ \sqrt { - 1 } q u \}$ is very small,because the imaginary part of $\pmb q$ is positive for $\pmb { u }$ real positive and very large.Hence the functions $\psi$ and $S _ { \imath }$ vanish for $u =$ $+ \infty$ ，that is,for $y = 2 \pi$ . Likewise we can replace the path of integration $c$ by another path $C ^ { \prime }$ which lies below but always near the real axis, so that there is no singular point of $\theta$ between $C ^ { \prime }$ and the real axis.The integrals (64） and (65） taken along $C ^ { \prime }$ give $\psi ^ { \prime }$ and $S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ respectively， that differ from $\psi$ and $S _ { \imath }$ . Since the imaginary part of $q$ is negative for $\pmb { u }$ real negative and very large, the exponential exp $\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } q u \}$ has very small modulus.Hence the functions $\psi ^ { \prime }$ and $S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ vanish for $u = - \infty$ ,that is, for $y = 0$

Between the two paths $C$ and $C ^ { \prime }$ the integrand has a singular point $q = - 1 / \sqrt { 8 \mu }$ . This singular point is a pole. Hence the difference $\psi ^ { \prime }$ $- \psi$ is equal to $2 \pi$ times the residue, that is,

$$
\psi ^ { \prime } - \psi = \pi \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 2 } } \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } u / \sqrt { 8 \mu } \right\} \cdot \theta \left( - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 8 \mu } } \right) .
$$

Denote the modulus and the argument of $\theta ( - 1 / { \sqrt { 8 \mu } } )$ by $\rho _ { 0 }$ and $\omega _ { 0 }$ ， respectively, then we have

$$
S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - S _ { 1 } = \pi \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 2 } } \rho _ { 0 } \cos \left( x - \frac { u } { \sqrt { 8 \mu } } + \omega _ { 0 } \right) .
$$

Hence it follows that $\psi ^ { \prime }$ is not equal to $\psi$ unless

$$
\theta \Bigl ( - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 8 \mu } } \Bigr ) = \theta ( \sqrt { - 1 } \alpha ) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \frac { \phi } { 2 \pi } \exp { \{ \alpha u \} } \cdot d u = 0 .
$$

Thus we obtain the expansions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \psi = \sum _ { i } \mu ^ { p / 2 } \psi _ { p } , \qquad \psi ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i } \mu ^ { p / 2 } \psi _ { p } ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \psi _ { p } = \int _ { c } \frac { \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } q u \} } } { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta ( q ) ( - q \sqrt { 8 } ) ^ { p - 2 } d q , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \psi _ { p } ^ { \prime } = \int _ { c ^ { \prime } } \frac { \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } q u \} } } { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta ( q ) ( - q \sqrt { 8 } ) ^ { p - 2 } d q . } } \end{array}
$$

The integrand has no singular point between $c$ and $C ^ { \prime }$ . Hence $\psi _ { p } = $ $\psi _ { p } ^ { \prime }$ .Thus,although the functions $\psi$ and $\psi ^ { \prime }$ are not equal, their formal expansions in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ are identical. This circumstancce shows further that the expansions are not convergent. We see moreover that, if $\mu$ is of the first order of a small quantity,then $\psi - \psi ^ { \prime }$ is of order infinity, like exp $\{ - 1 / \mu \}$ . In fact, in the particular case $\phi ( y ) = \sin y ^ { \prime }$ we have by (63)

$$
\theta \Bigl ( - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 8 \mu } } \Bigr ) = \frac { - \sqrt { - 1 } \sqrt { \mu / 2 } } { \exp { \{ \pi / ( 4 \sqrt { 2 \mu } ) \} } + \exp { \{ - \pi / ( 4 \sqrt { 2 \mu } ) \} } } .
$$

Hence $\psi - \psi ^ { \prime }$ and $S _ { 1 } - S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ are of the same order as

$$
\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { \mu } } \exp \left\{ - \frac { \pi } { 4 \sqrt { 2 \mu } } \right\} .
$$

Now we return from this general discussion to the case $h = 0$ .We suppose generally that $\phi ( y )$ vanishes for $y = 0$ .Then we have from (58)

$$
\psi = t ^ { - 2 \alpha } \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \int \phi t ^ { 2 \alpha - 1 } d t .
$$

The function $\phi$ vanishes for $y = 0$ ,that is, for $t = 0$ and $y = 2 \pi$ ，that is,for $t = \infty$ : At first consider $t$ small and expand $\phi$ in powers of $t$

$$
\phi = \sum A _ { n } t ^ { n } .
$$

Then we obtain

$$
\psi = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } t ^ { - 2 \alpha } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \phi t ^ { 2 \alpha - 1 } d t + C t ^ { - 2 \alpha } = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \sum A _ { n } { \frac { t ^ { n } } { 2 \alpha + n } } + C t ^ { - }
$$

In order to have this expansion vanish for $t = 0$ it is necessary and sufficient that $C = 0$ . Hence

$$
\psi ^ { \prime } = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } t ^ { - 2 \alpha } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \phi t ^ { 2 \alpha - 1 } d t = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \sum A _ { n } { \frac { t ^ { n } } { 2 \alpha + n } } .
$$

Next consider $t$ to be large and expand $\phi$ in descending powers of $t$

$$
\phi = \sum B _ { n } t ^ { - n } ,
$$

then we get

$$
= - \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } t ^ { - 2 \alpha } \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \phi t ^ { 2 \alpha - 1 } d t + C ^ { \prime } t ^ { - 2 \alpha } = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \sum _ { - } \frac { B _ { n } t ^ { - n } } { 2 \alpha - n } + C ^ { \prime } t ^ { - 2 \alpha } .
$$

In order to have this expansion vanish for $t = \infty$ it is necessary and sufficient that $C ^ { \prime } = 0$ Thus

$$
\psi = - \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } t ^ { - 2 \alpha } \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \phi t ^ { 2 \alpha - 1 } d t = \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \sum \frac { B _ { n } t ^ { - n } } { 2 \alpha - n } .
$$

For $\psi = \psi ^ { \prime }$ we should have

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \phi t ^ { 2 \alpha - 1 } d t = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \phi \exp { \{ \alpha u \} } \cdot d u = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\theta ( \sqrt { - 1 } \alpha ) = 0 ,
$$

which cannot be valid in general.

Now we expand (66) and (67) in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ . We have from (66)

$$
\psi ^ { \prime } = \frac { \mu } { 2 \sqrt { - 1 } } \sum A _ { \scriptscriptstyle {  } } \frac { t ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } } { 1 - \sqrt { - 1 } n \sqrt { 2 \mu } } ,
$$

which gives

$$
\psi ^ { \prime } = \sum T _ { p \mu ^ { ( p + 2 ) / 2 } } ^ { \prime } , \qquad T _ { p } ^ { \prime } = \sum \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { - 1 } } A _ { n } t ^ { n } n ^ { p } ( \sqrt { - 2 } ) ^ { p } .
$$

The formula (67) gives similarly

$$
\psi = \sum T _ { p } \mu ^ { { ( p + 2 ) } / { 2 } } , \qquad T _ { p } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { - 1 } } \sum B _ { n } t ^ { n } n ^ { p } ( - \sqrt { - 2 } ) ^ { p } .
$$

In these forms it is not so easy to see that these two expansions are identical. However we can show that we can pass from one expansion to the other in the following manner. The function

$$
\theta ( q ) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \frac { \phi } { 2 \pi } \exp { \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } q u \} } \cdot d u
$$

is a meromorphic function of $q$ that has poles as the only singularities. These only singularities are poles equal to $\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \sqrt { \scriptstyle - 1 } }$ times a positive or a negative integer. Write

$$
2 \pi \theta ( q ) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \phi \exp \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } q u \} \cdot d u + \int _ { - \infty } ^ { 0 } \phi \exp \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } q u \} \cdot d
$$

If the imaginary part of $q$ is positive, then the second integral is a holomorphic function of $\pmb q$ without any singularity, because $\phi$ and exp $\{ - { \sqrt { - 1 } } q u \}$ vanish for $u = - \infty$ . But it is not so for the first integral. If the imaginary part of $\pmb q$ is negative, then the first integral is a holomorphic function of $q$ , but it is not so for the second integral. The expansion

$$
\phi = \sum A _ { n } t ^ { n } = \sum A _ { n } \exp \left\{ \frac { n u } { 2 } \right\}
$$

is written

$$
\phi = A _ { 1 } \exp \left\{ \frac { u } { 2 } \right\} + A _ { 2 } \exp \left\{ u \right\} + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } \exp \left\{ \frac { n u } { 2 } \right\} + R _ { n } ,
$$

where $R _ { n } \exp \left\{ - n u / 2 \right\}$ tends to zero as $\boldsymbol { u }$ tends to $- \infty$ . Then the second integral is written

$$
J _ { 1 } + J _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + J _ { n } + S _ { n } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { J _ { k } = A _ { k } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { 0 } \exp \left\{ \left( \displaystyle \frac k 2 - \sqrt { - 1 } \ : q \right) u \right\} \cdot d u , } } \\ { { S _ { n } = \displaystyle \int _ { - \infty } ^ { 0 } R _ { n } \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \ : q u \right\} \cdot d u . } } \end{array}
$$

The integral $\scriptstyle { \boldsymbol { J } } _ { k }$ loses its meaning when the imaginary part of $q$ is smaller than $- k / 2$ . By analytic continuation we have

$$
J _ { k } = { \frac { A _ { k } } { ( k / 2 ) - { \sqrt { - 1 } } q } } .
$$

If the imaginary part of $q$ is larger than $- n / 2$ ，then $S _ { n }$ is a function of $q$ without any singularity,because the integrand vanishes for $u =$ $\infty$ . Thus the second integral is a meromorphic function of $q$ admitting $q = - \sqrt { - 1 } k / 2$ ，for a positive integer $k$ ，as poles with residue $\sqrt { - 1 } A _ { \kappa }$ . Similarly the first integral is a meromorphic function of $q$ admitting $q = \dot { \sqrt { - 1 } } k / 2$ ，for a positive integer $k$ ,as poles with residue $- \sqrt { - 1 } B _ { k }$ . Hence the poles of $\theta ( q )$ are $q = + \sqrt { - 1 } k / 2$ with residue $B _ { \kappa } / ( 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \pi )$ ，and $q = - \sqrt { - 1 } k / 2$ with residue $- \dot { A _ { \kappa } } / ( 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \pi )$

We now return to (64) and suppose that the integral is taken along the path $C$ . Construct a circle $\kappa$ with the origin as center and with radius $( 2 m + 1 ) / 4 ,$ $m$ being very large.Let $K _ { 1 }$ be the part of $\pmb { K }$ which lies above the curve $C$ .Let $C _ { 1 }$ be the part of $C$ which lies inside the circle $\kappa$ . The two arcs $C _ { 1 }$ and $K _ { \imath }$ form a closed contour.The integral (64) taken along this contour is equal to $2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 }$ times the sum of the residues for the poles inside the contour,that is, the sum of the first $_ { m }$ terms of the series(67).We can see that the integral (64) taken along $K _ { 1 }$ tends to zero as m increases indefinitely. The integral $C _ { 1 }$ tends to $\psi$ . Hence $\psi$ is equal to the sum of the series (67), that is,conver-gent. Thus we come again to the expansions (66) and the expansions (68) and (68a). Thus we have seen how we can pass from the expansions (64) and (65) to the expansions (67) and (68).

# 15.19 RELATION TO ASYMPTOTIC SOLUTIONS

We have shown that (55) admits a simple periodic solution

$$
x = t , \qquad p = y = q = 0
$$

for $\varepsilon = 0$ ，with the characteristic exponents $\pm { \sqrt { 2 \mu } }$ The corresponding asymptotic solutions are

$$
p = 0 , \qquad q = \pm \sqrt { 2 \mu } \sin \frac { y } { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\tan { \frac { y } { 4 } } = C \exp { \{ \pm t / { \overline { { 2 \mu } } } \} } , ~ x = t .
$$

The third equation can be writen

$$
\cot { \frac { y } { 4 } } = C \exp { \{ t { \sqrt { 2 \mu } } \} } ,
$$

or

$$
\tan { \frac { y } { 4 } } = C \exp { \{ t \sqrt { 2 \mu } \} } .
$$

Since the characteristic exponents are not zero,there exists,by what will be proved in Section 18.5,another periodic solution for small values of $\varepsilon$ ，for which $x = t$ ，but $p , y , q$ are functions of $\pmb { t }$ and $\varepsilon$ ， developable in increasing powers of $\varepsilon$ ，vanishing for $\varepsilon = 0$ ，and periodic with period $2 \pi$ with respect to $t$ ，The characteristic exponents themselves, being equal and of opposite sign of the form $\pm \beta$ ，can be developed in increasing powers of $\varepsilon$ ，where $\beta$ reduces to $+ \sqrt { 2 \mu }$ for $\varepsilon = 0$ .Moreover there exist two series of asymptotic solutions.For the first series we have

$$
x = t , \ p = \eta _ { 1 } , \ q = \eta _ { 2 } , \cot \frac { y } { 4 } = \eta _ { 3 } ,
$$

where $\eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } , \eta _ { 3 }$ are series expanded in powers of $C \exp \left\{ - \beta t \right\}$ with periodic coefficients.For the second series we have

$$
x = t , \qquad p = \eta _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \qquad q = \eta _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \qquad \tan \frac { y } { 4 } = \eta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where $\eta _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \eta _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \eta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime }$ are series expanded in powers of $C \exp \left\{ \beta t \right\}$ with periodic coefficients.If we consider these exponentials as functions of $\varepsilon$ ,then the six functions $\eta$ are developable in increasing powers of ε. If we consider them as functions of $\pmb { \mu }$ ,then each term of the six functions $\eta$ are factored by coefficients of the form $N / \pi$ ，where $N$ isa polynomial expanded in increasing _powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ and $\beta$ and $\pi$ is a product of factors of the form $m { \sqrt { - 1 } } + n \beta , m$ and $\pmb { n }$ being positive or negative integers. The quotient $N / \pi$ can be expanded in powers of $\sqrt { \mu } .$ ， but the expansion is in general purely formal, since the characteristic exponents vanish for $\mu = 0$

We transform (69) and (69a) by replacing $t$ by $x$ ，and solve the equation cot $( y / 4 ) = \eta _ { 3 }$ with regard to $C$ . Then we obtain $C$ exp $\{ \beta x \}$ $= \zeta$ .Since $\eta _ { 3 }$ reduces to $C$ exp $\{ \beta x \}$ for $\varepsilon = 0$ ,we see that $\zeta$ is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and of cot $( y / 4 )$ with coefficients periodic in $x$ Substitute $\zeta$ in place of $C \exp \left\{ \beta x \right\}$ in $\eta _ { 1 }$ and $\eta _ { 2 }$ ，then $\eta _ { 1 }$ and $\eta _ { 2 }$ become functions of $x$ and $y$ .The expression $\eta _ { 1 } d x + \eta _ { 2 } d y$ is an exact differential $d S$ ，where $s$ has the property that (i) its derivatives are periodic in $x$ ，(ii) $s$ is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and of cot $( y / 4 )$ ， (iii) any term of $\partial S / \partial x = \eta _ { 1 }$ or of $\partial S / \partial y = \eta _ { 2 }$ consists of sines or cosines of a multiple of $x$ ，multiplied by a power of cot $( y / 4 )$ ，a power of $\varepsilon$ ，and a coefficient of the form $N / \pi$ ，where $N$ is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ ， $\sqrt { \mu }$ and $\beta$ and $\Pi$ is a product of factors of the form $m { \sqrt { - 1 } } + n \beta$ ， and (iv) the expression $N / \pi$ is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and $\sqrt { \mu }$ ,and hence the same as $s$ ，the only difference being that the expansion of $s$ in powers of $\varepsilon$ is convergent but that the expansion in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ is only formal.We obtain a similar function $s \prime$ from (69a),but the expansion is made in $\varepsilon$ and tan $( y / 4 )$ , in place of in $\varepsilon$ and cot $( y / 4 )$

Thus $s$ is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$

$$
{ \cal S } = { \cal S } _ { 0 } + \varepsilon { \cal S } _ { 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } { \cal S } _ { 2 } + \cdots ,
$$

where $S _ { 1 }$ is the real part of $\psi \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } x \right\}$ and $\psi$ is developable in powers of cot $( y / 4 )$ ,that is,in descending powers of the variable $\pmb { t }$ Hence the expansion is the same as the expansion (67).

The function $N$ is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ ,and $\beta$ is also in powers of $\varepsilon$ .Hence $1 / ( m \sqrt { - 1 } + n \beta )$ is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and the first term is $1 / ( m \sqrt { - 1 } + n \sqrt { 2 \mu } )$ . Suppose that $N / \pi$ is expanded in powers of $\varepsilon$ in such a form,that the first term of $N$ reduces to $( \varepsilon \mu / 2 )$ $B _ { n }$ and the product $\Pi$ reduces to one factor ${ \sqrt { - 1 } } - n \beta$ . Then the first term of the expansion of $N / \Pi$ is

$$
\frac { \varepsilon \mu } { 2 } \frac { B _ { n } } { \sqrt { - 1 - n \sqrt { 2 \mu } } } = \varepsilon \sqrt { \frac { \mu } { 8 } } \frac { B _ { n } } { 2 \alpha - n } .
$$

This agrees with the expansion (67)， in which the coefficient $B _ { n } / ( 2 \alpha - n )$ appears.

Similarly $S ^ { \prime }$ is expanded in powers of $\varepsilon$

$$
S ^ { \prime } = S _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + \varepsilon S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } S _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \cdots ,
$$

where $S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ is the real part of $\psi ^ { \prime } \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } x \right\}$ and $\psi ^ { \prime }$ is expanded in powers of $\pmb { t } .$ 、This agrees with the expansion (66).

Thus $s$ is expanded in powers of cot $( y / 4 )$ and the expansion is convergent only when $y$ is nearly equal to $2 \pi$ ，while $S ^ { \prime }$ is expanded in a similar form in powers of tan $( y / 4 )$ and the expansion is conver-gent only when $y$ is nearly equal to O. By analytic continuation the functions $s$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ can be defined for any value of $y$ between $y _ { 0 }$ and $y _ { 1 }$ ，wherə $y _ { 0 }$ and $y _ { 1 }$ are in the interval between O and $2 \pi$ The question is whether the functions $s$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ are equal to each other in the domain of $y$ for which both functions are defined in this manner. The answer is in the negative.In fact,if we had $s = s \prime$ ，then the corresponding terms of the convergent expansions of $s$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ in powers of ε would be equal, and in particular we should have $S _ { 1 } = S _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and hence $\psi = \psi ^ { \prime }$ . We have seen that $\psi \neq \psi ^ { \prime }$ . Thus $s$ is not equal to $S ^ { \prime }$

We have seen also that $s$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ are formally developable in powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ .Let the expansions be obtained in a way similar to that discussed in Section 14.11 in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { S = T _ { 0 } + \sqrt { \mu } T _ { 1 } + \mu T _ { 2 } + \cdots , } \\ { S ^ { \prime } = T _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + \sqrt { \mu } T _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \mu T _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \cdots . . . } \end{array}
$$

The functions $\boldsymbol { T } _ { i }$ are developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and cot $( y / 4 )$ for the values of $y$ in the neighbourhood of $2 \pi$ ，and the functions $\boldsymbol { T _ { i } ^ { \prime } }$ are developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and tan $( y / 4 )$ for the values of $y$ in the neighborhood of 0. $s$ is the only function that is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and cot $( y / 4 )$ satisfying the equation (56). Similarly $S ^ { \prime }$ is the only function that is developable in powers of ε and tan $( y / 4 )$ satisfying the equation (56).On the other hand, the functions $\boldsymbol { T } _ { i }$ can，according to the method discussed at the end of Section .14.11, be put in the form of a series proceeding in sines of multiples of $y / 2$ .Hence it is developable in powers of $\varepsilon$ and cot $( y / 4 )$ for values of $y$ in the neighborhood of $2 \pi$ ，and in powers of ε and tan $( y / 4 )$ for values of $y$ in the neighborhood of O.Hence $T _ { i } = T _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ . If the expansion （7O） were convergent, then we should have $S = S ^ { \prime }$ . Hence the expansion （7O) should be divergent. Thus we conclude that the expansions treated at the end of Section 15.17 are no longer convergent.

We have supposed that $\phi ( y )$ vanishes for $y = 0$ . This restriction is not essential. If $\phi ( 0 )$ does not vanish but is equal to $\scriptstyle A _ { 0 }$ ，then it is .sufficient to add a term $\sqrt { \mu / 8 } \cdot A _ { 0 } / ( 2 \alpha )$ to the expansions (66) and (67) and the same term to the integral (64) defining $\psi$ and $\psi ^ { \prime }$

Indeed， this seems to be a very long proof of the divergence.But Poincaré had in mind at the same time to show how the expansions stated in the beginning can be passed to the expansions for the asymptotic solutions through the intermediary of the expansions for the functions $\psi$ and $\psi ^ { \prime }$ . The singularities which appeared in the above discussion are related to the periodic solutions of the second genus and the doubly asymptotic solutions,to both of which we shall return for discussion in Chapters 18 and 19,in particular in Sections 19.11 and 19.18.

# ASYMPTOTIC INTEGRALS OF LITTLEWOOD

# 15.20 LITTLEWOOD'S THEOREMS

Litlewood (1959） proposed the question: given that the initial disturbance in coordinates and velocities is of order $\varepsilon$ ，for how long a time in terms of $\varepsilon$ can we say the disturbance will remain of order $\pmb { \varepsilon } ?$ He found that for almost all values of $\mathbf { m } < \mathbf { m } _ { 0 }$ ， such that ${ \bf m } _ { 0 } ( 1 + { \bf m } _ { 0 } ) ^ { - 2 }$ $= 1 / 2 7$ ， this time is as long as exp $( A \varepsilon ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon | ^ { - 3 / 4 } )$ ,where $\pmb { A }$ depends only on m,in the case of Lagrange's equilateral configuration in the plane restricted three-body problem，where $\mathbf { m } < 1$ denotes the ratio of the two finite masses $\boldsymbol { J }$ and $s$ It is known that for $\mathbf { m } < \mathbf { m } _ { 0 }$ the configuration is stable over a time $O ( 1 )$ and that there are real periods $2 \pi / \lambda$ and $2 \pi / \mu$ of normal oscillations,in which $\gimel$ and $\mu$ are taken positive (see Sections 17.2, 18.24,18.25,21.17).

Let $x , y$ be coordinates referred to axes with the equilibrium point of the infinitesimal mass $\pmb { P }$ as origin and rotating with the equilibrium triangle ; they can be so chosen that the differential equations of motion are

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d t } - \varpi _ { 1 } x = \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial x } , \qquad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d x } { d t } - \varpi _ { 2 } y = \frac { \partial \Omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial y } ,
$$

$$
\Omega = \left( \frac { 1 } { S P } + \frac { \bf { m } } { J P } \right) \bigg / \left( 1 + \bf { m } \right) = 1 + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \Omega _ { n } ,
$$

where $\Omega _ { n }$ denotes the aggregate of terms of degree $_ n$ in the expansion of $\Omega$ in powers of $x$ and $y$ and where $\begin{array} { r } { \Omega ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { n = 2 } ^ { \infty } \Omega _ { n } } \end{array}$

When $\mathbf { m } < \mathbf { m } _ { 0 }$ the equations (71) with $\Omega = 0$ have general solutions of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { { x } / { \varepsilon } = a \cos { ( \lambda t - \alpha ) } + b \cos { ( \mu t - \beta ) } , } \\ & { { y } / { \varepsilon } = - \kappa _ { a } a \sin { ( \lambda t - \alpha ) } - \kappa _ { \mu } b \sin { ( \mu t - \beta ) } , } \end{array}
$$

where the denominator ε corresponds to the condition that (72) repre-sents a first approximation over time $O ( 1 )$ to the solution of （71） when

the initial displacements and velocities are of order $\varepsilon$ . The constants $a , b$ ， are taken to be nonnegative, and $\alpha , \beta$ are the four arbitrary constants,and

$$
\kappa _ { \iota } = ( \varpi _ { 1 } + \lambda ^ { 2 } ) / ( 2 \lambda ) , \qquad \kappa _ { \iota } = ( \varpi _ { 2 } + \mu ^ { 2 } ) / ( 2 \mu ) ,
$$

both being positive.The constants $\varpi _ { 1 } , \varpi _ { 2 } , \lambda , \mu , \kappa _ { \lambda } , \kappa _ { \mu }$ are all functions of the only parameter m of the problem. We suppose that $\mathbf { m }$ belongs to the set of almost all real numbers,for which the continued fractions for $\lambda / \mu$ and $\mu / \lambda$ have reduced fractions (Section 15.3) satisfying ${ a _ { n } } =$ $O ( n \log ^ { 2 } n )$

Transform $x , y , d x / d t , d y / d t$ to the new variables by

$$
x = \varepsilon ( X + Z ) , ~ y = - \varepsilon ( \kappa _ { \lambda } Y + \kappa _ { \mu } W ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = - \varepsilon ( \lambda Y + \mu W ) , \frac { d y } { d t } = - \varepsilon ( \lambda \kappa _ { \lambda } X + \mu \kappa _ { \mu } Z ) .
$$

Let the respective initial values $h , k , l , m$ of these new variables $X , Y$ ， $z , W$ satisfy $h ^ { 2 } + k ^ { 2 }$ ， $l ^ { 2 } + m ^ { 2 } \leq R ^ { 2 }$ ，where $R > 0$ is fixed.Littlewood (1959) proved the following theorems :

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :If m is such that $\lambda / \mu$ is irrational,and if $\varepsilon < B _ { \mathrm { 1 } } / R$ ， there are two asymptotic integrals of the equations of motion (71） in terms of $X , Y , S , W$ . We have, for $0 \leq t \leq 4 \pi$ and any positive integer $N$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle { F _ { N - 1 } ( X , Y , Z , W ) \bigg | _ { 0 } ^ { t } = \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { n } f _ { n } ( X , Y , Z , W ) \bigg | _ { 0 } ^ { t } = O ( R ^ { N + 2 } \varepsilon ^ { N } ) , } } \\ & { \displaystyle { G _ { N - 1 } ( X , Y , Z , W ) \bigg | _ { 0 } ^ { t } = \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { n } g _ { n } ( X , Y , Z , W ) \bigg | _ { 0 } ^ { t } = O ( R ^ { N + 2 } \varepsilon ^ { N } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $f _ { n } , g _ { n }$ depend only on m and $_ n$ ，and $f _ { 0 } = X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 }$ ， $g _ { 0 } = Z ^ { 2 } +$ $W ^ { 2 }$ The constant $B _ { 1 }$ and the constants of the $o \mathfrak { s }$ depend only on m and $N$

Theorem 2: If $\lambda / \mu$ is irrational, there exist functions $\phi ( \varepsilon )$ and $\psi ( \varepsilon )$ ， tending to infinity faster than any power of $\varepsilon ^ { - 1 }$ ，and such that when $\varepsilon < A _ { 1 } / R$ we have, for any time $t$ satisfying $0 \le t \le T = \phi ( \varepsilon R )$ ，

$$
F _ { N - 1 } | _ { 0 } ^ { t } , G _ { N - 1 } | _ { 0 } ^ { t } = O ( R ^ { N + 2 } \varepsilon ^ { N } ) \qquad ( N < 4 ( \varepsilon R ) ) .
$$

In particular,

A ${ \bf \tau } _ { \cdot } ^ { r 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } = h ^ { 2 } + k ^ { 2 } + O ( R ^ { 3 } \varepsilon ) , \qquad Z ^ { 2 } + W ^ { 2 } = l ^ { 2 } + m ^ { 2 } + O ( R ^ { 3 } \varepsilon ) ,$ the constant $\pmb { A } _ { 1 }$ and the constants of the $o \mathfrak { s }$ depending only on m. The rate of increase of $\phi$ depends on $\lambda / \mu$

Theorem 3: We have, for $\varepsilon < A _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } / R$ and for $\sigma \leq t \leq 4 \pi ;$

$$
F _ { N - 1 } | _ { 0 } ^ { t } , G _ { N - 1 } | _ { 0 } ^ { t } = O ( R ^ { 2 } ) \{ A R { N } ^ { 2 } \log ^ { 3 / 2 } { ( N + 1 ) } \} ^ { N _ { \varepsilon } N }
$$

for $N < A _ { 2 } ( \varepsilon R ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \left( \varepsilon R \right) | ^ { - 3 / 4 }$ , where the constant $\ b { A } _ { 2 }$ depends only on m.

Theorem 4: If $\varepsilon < A _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } / R$ , then for the time

$$
t \le T = \exp \left\{ { \cal A } _ { 2 } ( \varepsilon R ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \left( \varepsilon R \right) | ^ { - 3 / 4 } \right\}
$$

we have

$$
F _ { N - 1 } | _ { 0 } ^ { t } , G _ { N - 1 } | _ { 0 } ^ { t } = O ( R ^ { 2 } ) [ A _ { 3 } R N ^ { 2 } \log ^ { 3 / 2 } ( N + 1 ) ] ^ { N } \varepsilon ^ { N }
$$

for $N < A _ { 4 } ( \varepsilon R ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log { ( \varepsilon R ) } | ^ { - 3 / 4 }$ ， where the constants $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , A _ { 3 } , A _ { 4 }$ depend only on m.In particular, for $t \leq T$ ，

$$
X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } , Z ^ { 2 } + W ^ { 2 } = h ^ { 2 } + k ^ { 2 } + O ( R ^ { 3 } \varepsilon ) ,
$$

and a fortiori $X , Y , Z , W$ are bounded and the original $x , y$ are $O ( \varepsilon )$

Theorems 1 and 3 are about a single time step of length $\leq 4 \pi$ . Littlewood's method of proof operates with such steps，and at the end the individual steps are added together to arrive at Theorems 2 and 4, which are about exponentially long times.At frst Littlewood proved the formal result that, e.g., $f _ { m }$ exist with $f _ { 0 } = X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 }$ such that

$$
F _ { N - 1 } { \bigg | } _ { 0 } ^ { t } = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { n } f _ { n } ( X , Y , Z , W ) { \bigg | } _ { 0 } ^ { t }
$$

has zero coefficients of $\varepsilon ^ { n }$ for $n < N$ .He fixed $N$ and referred to the limiting process $\varepsilon \to 0$ . In Theorems 1 and 3,which are about a step $t = 0$ to $t \leq 4 \pi$ ，he needs a succession of steps,not in general equal, $\mathtt { n o r } \le 4 \pi$ ，by proceeding from O to $t _ { 1 }$ ，to $t _ { 1 } + t _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ to a final $_ T$ ,by two different successions of steps.The proof of Theorem 1 is in four parts,each based on reductio ad absurdum.He proved the lemma that if $f ( X , Y , Z , W )$ is a polynomial of degree $_ n$ in four complex variables and $| f | \leq { M }$ for $X , Y , Z , W$ satisfying $| X | ^ { 2 } + | Y | ^ { 2 }$ ， $| Z | ^ { 2 } +$ $\vert W \vert ^ { 2 } \le R ^ { 2 }$ ，then for $\sigma = p + q + r + s \geq 1$ and $X , Y , Z , W$ satisfying the same conditions,we have

$$
\left| \frac { \partial ^ { \sigma } f } { \partial X ^ { p } \partial Y ^ { q } \partial Z ^ { r } \partial W ^ { s } } \right| \leq [ ( 4 e ) ^ { 2 } R ^ { - 1 } n ] ^ { \sigma } ,
$$

where $e$ is the base of Napier's logarithm.Littlewood referred to Bernstein's theorem from function theory (Hardy，Littlewood,and P6lya,1934) that for any trigonometric polynomial $F ( \theta )$ of degree $\pmb { n }$ we have $\left| F ^ { \prime } ( \theta ) \right| \leqq n M$ if $| F | \leq M$ ，or more generally that if $f ( \zeta )$ isa polynomial of degree $_ n$ and $| f ( \zeta ) | \leq M$ for $| \zeta | \leq r$ ，then

$$
\left| \frac { d ^ { p } f ( \zeta ) } { d \zeta ^ { p } } \right| \le n ( n - 1 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( n - p + 1 ) M | \zeta | ^ { - p } \le M ( n | \zeta | ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { p }
$$

for $| \zeta | \le r$

Also,in passing to the ləng time interval he referrad to Liouville's theorem that a volume of the phase space of representative points of the motion remains constant (Section 1.15). The proof, as Littlewood himself admits,is somewhat roundabout.

The result is that a displacement of order ε in coordinates and velocities remains of this order for a time as long as

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \exp \left( K \varepsilon ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon | ^ { - 3 / 4 } \right) . } \end{array}
$$

This is a corollary of a theorem stating that there exist two asymptotic integrals of the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal F } _ { n - 1 } ^ { * } = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } f _ { m } ^ { * } = a ^ { 2 } + { \cal O } ( \varepsilon ^ { n } ) } , } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal G } _ { n - 1 } ^ { * } = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } g _ { m } ^ { * } = b ^ { 2 } + { \cal O } ( \varepsilon ^ { n } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

for $t < T = \exp \left\{ A \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon _ { 1 } | ^ { - 3 / 4 } \right\}$ and $n < A \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon _ { 1 } | ^ { - 3 / 4 }$ ，where $\varepsilon _ { 1 } = \varepsilon \operatorname* { m a x } \left( a , b \right)$ , and where the $A ' \mathsf { s }$ are positive constants depending only on $\mathbf { m }$ ，and the $o \mathfrak { s }$ depend on $_ n$ in an explicit manner. Also, $f _ { m } ^ { * }$ ， $g _ { m } ^ { * }$ are homogeneous polynomials in $x , y , d x / d t , d y / d t$ of degree $m + 2$ ，and $f _ { 0 } ^ { * } , \ g _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ are of the form

$$
\left[ \left( A x \pm A \frac { d y } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( A y \pm A \frac { d x } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \Big / \varepsilon ^ { 2 } .
$$

Birkhoff,as will be shown in Sections 21.1 and 21.2,discussed similar estimations in the case of motion near an equilibrium position.

Whitaker (Section 14.15)，in discussing his adelphic integral, observed that for a simple model system there is convergence,and conjectured on the strength of the evidence that there is convergence in the actual problem whenever $\lambda / \mu$ is irrational. Recent development in stellar dynamics,by means of that powerful tool, the electronic computer (Section 14.16),shows the existence of nonisolating integrals, not uniformly convergent for all values of the parameters involved.According to Littlewood, this is clearly impossible since small divisors can be arbitrarily small for sufficiently extreme forms of an irrational $\lambda / \mu$ ； but small divisors do not rule out the possibility of convergence for almost all $\lambda / \mu$ as Bruns (Section 15.4) has remarked.Littewood's procedure is to take the problem of convergence to the point of finding explicit bounds for the nth terms of the series.These bounds are not good enough for convergence,but they do establish an explicit exponentially large time interval.Littlewood thinks it only fair to add that his procedure shows how diffcult the convergence problem is, and even leads him to doubt whether convergence ever occurs, except in special cases such as for periodic orbits.

Littlewood has since improved these theorems,as follows. Let the A's stand for positive constants depending only on m，while the constants of the $o$ 's depend only on $\mathbf { m }$ and the constants $a , b$ . A symbol $\pmb { A }$ followed by a suffix is a special $\pmb { A }$

Fundamental Theorem: Let $\varepsilon _ { 1 } = \varepsilon \operatorname* { m a x } { ( a , b ) }$ ，where ε is small, say $\varepsilon _ { 1 } < A _ { 1 }$ . Let

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { N _ { \varepsilon } = ( A _ { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon _ { 1 } | ^ { - 3 / 4 } ) , } \\ & { ~ T = T ( \varepsilon ) = A _ { 3 } [ \operatorname* { m a x } { ( a , b ) } ] ^ { - 1 } \exp { \{ A _ { 4 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon _ { 1 } | ^ { - 3 / 4 } \} } , } \end{array}
$$

where $A _ { 2 } , A _ { 3 } , A _ { 4 }$ are certain $\pmb { A }$ 's depending only on m. There exist certain functions $\lambda _ { m } , \mu _ { m }$ ，where $\lambda _ { 0 } = \lambda$ ， $\mu _ { 0 } = \mu$ ， $\lambda _ { m } = \mu _ { m } = 0$ when $_ { m }$ is odd, and $\lambda _ { 2 m } , \ \mu _ { 2 m }$ are homogeneous polynomials of degree $m$ in $a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 }$ ，depending only on $\mathbf { m }$ and $_ m$ . If now

$$
\Lambda = \Lambda _ { N _ { \varepsilon } } = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { N _ { \varepsilon } - 1 } \lambda _ { m } \varepsilon ^ { m } , \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad M = M _ { N _ { \varepsilon } } = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { N _ { \varepsilon } - 1 } \mu _ { m } \varepsilon ^ { m } ,
$$

then there is an orbit, reducing to (72) as $\varepsilon \to 0$ ， which is asymptotically of the form

$$
x = \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } \phi _ { m } + O ( E ) , \qquad y = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } \psi _ { m } + O ( E ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { c } { E = ( A _ { 5 } K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { n } + \exp { \{ - \frac { 1 } { 2 } N _ { \varepsilon } \} } , } \\ { K _ { n } = ( n + 1 ) ^ { 2 } \log ^ { 3 / 2 } { ( n + 2 ) } , } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

for all $n \leq N _ { \epsilon }$ and for $0 \leq t \leq T$ . The coefficients $\phi _ { m } , \psi _ { m }$ are homogeneous polynomials of degree $_ m$ in the four variables

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { a \exp { \{ ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } & { { } } & { a \exp { \{ - ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } \\ { b \exp { \{ ( \beta - M t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } & { { } } & { b \exp { \{ - ( \beta - M t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } \end{array}
$$

and depend only on m and $_ m$ . In particular $\phi _ { 1 } , \ \psi _ { 1 }$ are the right-hand members of (72) with $\Lambda$ for $\lambda$ and $M$ for $\pmb { \mu }$

The most important cases of (73) are the extreme ones, $n = N _ { \epsilon }$ and $n = 1$ .In the first case the error $O ( E )$ becomes

$$
O \left[ \exp \left\{ - A _ { 6 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon _ { 1 } | ^ { - 3 / 4 } \right\} \right] ,
$$

while the mth terms $\varepsilon ^ { m } \phi _ { m } , \varepsilon ^ { m } \psi _ { m }$ are $O [ ( A _ { 5 } K _ { m } ) ^ { m + 1 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { m } ]$ . In the second, we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { x = \varepsilon a \cos \left( \Lambda t - \alpha \right) + \varepsilon b \cos \left( M t - \beta \right) + O ( \varepsilon ^ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { y = - \kappa _ { \iota } \varepsilon a \sin \left( \Lambda t - \alpha \right) - \kappa _ { \mu } \varepsilon b \sin \left( M t - \beta \right) + O ( \varepsilon ^ { 2 } ) } } \end{array}
$$

over $t \leq T$

All results, in particular (75),are true in formally differential form.

The functions $\phi _ { m } , \psi _ { m }$ are actually uniquely determined, so that (74) determines an orbit uniquely, to error $O ( \varepsilon ^ { n } )$ ， for any given $a , b , \alpha , \beta$

We now prove the Fuudamental Theorem, following Littlewood.

# 15.2I PROOF OF THE FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM

We change the variables $X , Y , Z , W$ of (73） to

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \zeta = X + \sqrt { - 1 } Y , \qquad \omega = Z + \sqrt { - 1 } W , } } & { { } } \\ { { \bar { \zeta } = X - \sqrt { - 1 } Y , \qquad \bar { \omega } = Z - \sqrt { - 1 } W ; } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

then the differential equations are

$$
\frac { d \zeta } { d t } + \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda \zeta = \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { \infty } \varepsilon ^ { m } U _ { m } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \omega } { d t } + \sqrt { - 1 } \mu \omega = \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { \infty } \varepsilon ^ { m } V _ { m } ,
$$

together with their complex conjugates, where $U _ { m } , V _ { m }$ are homogeneous polynomials of degree $m + 1$ in $x / \varepsilon , y / \varepsilon$ ，and also in $\zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \varpi ;$ ， or in $X , Y , Z , W$ ， and where the multiple series on the right-hand sides of (77) have associated radii of convergence of the form

$$
| X | , | Y | , | Z | , | W | < A _ { 2 } , \qquad | \varepsilon | < A _ { 1 } ,
$$

where $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$ is greater than any given $\pmb { A }$ if $\pmb { A } _ { 1 }$ is sufficiently small.It follows that, subject to $| X | , | Y | , | Z | , | W | \cong 1$ ，we have $| U _ { m } | , | V _ { m } | <$ $A ^ { m + 1 }$ ,and,because of homogeneity,

$$
| U _ { m } | , | V _ { m } | < [ A \operatorname* { m a x } { ( | X | , | Y | , | Z | , | W | ) } ] ^ { m + 1 }
$$

for any $X , Y , Z , W$ . Alternatively the right-hand member can be written

$$
[ A \ \operatorname* { m a x } \ ( | \zeta | , | \bar { \zeta } | , | \omega | , | \bar { \omega } | ) ] ^ { m + 1 } .
$$

We fix $_ n$ and suppose that the constants of $o \mathfrak { s }$ depend on $_ n$ as well as on ${ \mathbf { m } } , a , b$

Lemma 1: For an arbitrary fixed $_ n$ and for $0 \leq t \leq : 1$ we have four asymptotic integrals of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { F _ { n - 1 } ( \zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \bar { \omega } ) = \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } f _ { m } ( \zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \bar { \omega } ) } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = a \exp \{ ( \alpha - \Lambda _ { n - 1 } t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} + O ( \varepsilon ^ { n } ) , } \\ & { } & { G _ { n - 1 } ( \zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \bar { \omega } ) = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } g _ { m } ( \zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \bar { \omega } ) } \\ & { } & { = b \exp \{ ( \beta - M _ { n - 1 } t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} + O ( \varepsilon ^ { n } ) , } \end{array}
$$

together with the conjugate forms. The $f _ { m } , g _ { m }$ are homogeneous polynomials of degree $m + 1$ in the variables indicated, depending only on m and $_ { m }$ ; and

$$
\Lambda _ { n - 1 } = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \lambda _ { m } \varepsilon ^ { m } , \qquad M _ { n - 1 } = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \mu _ { m } \varepsilon ^ { m } ,
$$

where $\lambda _ { m } , \mu _ { m }$ are functions of $a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 }$ as in the statement of Theorem 1.

The first pair $f _ { 0 } , g _ { 0 }$ are $f _ { 0 } = \zeta , g _ { 0 } = \omega$ ， so that the integrals reduce to

$$
\zeta _ { 0 } = a \exp { \{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , ~ \omega _ { 0 } = b \exp { \{ ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ,
$$

when $\varepsilon \to 0$ ,and $a , b , \alpha , \beta$ are the arbitrary constants $( a , b \geq 0 )$

For $m > 0 , f _ { m } , g _ { m }$ satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle \int \int f _ { m } [ a \exp \{ \theta \sqrt { - 1 } \} , a \exp \{ - \theta \sqrt { - 1 } \} , } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle b \exp \{ \phi \sqrt { - 1 } \} , b \exp \{ - \phi \sqrt { - 1 } \} ] } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle ~ \times [ a \exp \{ \theta \sqrt { - 1 } \} ] ^ { - 1 } \cdot d \theta d \phi = 0 , } } } \end{array}
$$

the integrations being over $0 \leq \theta \leq 2 \pi$ ， $0 \leq \phi \leqq { 2 \pi }$ .This determines the $f _ { m }$ and $g _ { m }$ uniquely.

This lemma provides four asymptotic integrals of the form $f ( x , y , d x / d t , d y / d t ) = \phi ( t , a , b , \alpha , \beta )$ ，whereas Theorem 1 provides explicit equations for the orbit $x = X ( t , a , b , \alpha , \beta )$ ， $y = Y ( t , a , b , \alpha , \beta )$ ， together with their differentiated forms.We pass from Lemma 1 to Theorem 1 for a range $t \leqq 1$ by solving the equations (79) for $\zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega ,$ $\bar { \omega }$ or for $x , y , d x / d t , d y / d t$

In order to prove Lemma 1 we suppose, by mathematical induction, that the lemma is true up to $n - 1$ and derive it for $_ n$ . We have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle H _ { n - 1 } = \exp \left\{ - \Lambda _ { n - 1 } t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} \cdot \frac d { d t } ( F _ { n - 1 } \exp \left\{ \Lambda _ { n - 1 } t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } \left( \dot { f } _ { m } + \sqrt { - 1 } \dot { f } _ { m } \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \lambda _ { r } \varepsilon ^ { r } \right) } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \dot { f } } _ { m } = \frac { \partial f _ { m } } { \partial \zeta } \left( - \lambda \sqrt { - 1 } \zeta + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { \infty } \varepsilon ^ { r } \boldsymbol { U } _ { r } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { \partial f } { \partial \bar { \zeta } } \left( \lambda \sqrt { - 1 } \bar { \zeta } + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { \infty } \varepsilon ^ { r } \overline { { { \boldsymbol { U } } } } _ { r } \right) + \ldots } } \end{array}
$$

the unwritten terms being derived from $\omega , \varpi$ in place of $\zeta , { \bar { \zeta } }$ . By hypothesis $H _ { n - 1 } = O ( \varepsilon ^ { n } )$ . The expansion of $H _ { n - 1 }$ in powers of ε resulting from (81) with substitution from (82) is of the form $\sum \infty C _ { m } \varepsilon ^ { m }$ ， where $C _ { m }$ is a function of $\zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \varpi$ with coeficients involving $\lambda _ { r }$ and $\mu _ { r }$ Since this is true identically in $\varepsilon$ , we have $C _ { m } \equiv 0$ for $m \leq n - 1$ .

Consider

$$
H _ { n } = \exp { \{ - \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \} } \cdot [ d ( F _ { n } \exp { \{ \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ) / d t ] ,
$$

where $f _ { n }$ and $\lambda _ { n }$ are to be determined. We have

$$
I _ { n } = H _ { n - 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { n } \bigg ( \dot { f } _ { n } + \sqrt { - 1 } f _ { n } \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { r } \varepsilon ^ { r } \bigg ) + \varepsilon ^ { n } \lambda _ { n } \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \sqrt { - 1 } f _ { r } \varepsilon ^ { r }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { = \varepsilon ^ { n } \bigg [ \sum _ { \tau = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \bigg ( \frac { \partial f _ { \tau } } { \partial \zeta } U _ { n - \tau } + \cdots + \frac { \partial f _ { \tau } } { \partial \widetilde { \omega } } \overline { { V } } _ { n - \tau } \bigg ) } } \\ & { } & { + \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \sqrt { - 1 } f _ { \tau } \lambda _ { n - \tau } + \dot { f } _ { n } + \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } f _ { n } + \sqrt { - 1 } \zeta \lambda _ { n } \bigg ] ^ { ( 8 ) } } \\ & { } & { + O ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } } \end{array}
$$

from (81) and (82). If

$$
\zeta = a \exp { \{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , ~ \omega _ { 0 } = b \exp { \{ ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ,
$$

then we have $\zeta = \zeta _ { 0 } + O ( \varepsilon )$ ， $\omega = \omega _ { 0 } + O ( \varepsilon )$ over $t \leq 1$ . Substituting from these for $\zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \varpi$ in (83) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { H _ { n } = \varepsilon ^ { n } [ \dot { f } _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \bar { \zeta } _ { 0 } , \omega _ { 0 } , \varpi _ { 0 } ) + \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , . . . , \varpi _ { 0 } ) } \\ { + \sqrt { - 1 } \zeta _ { 0 } \lambda _ { n } - J ( \zeta _ { 0 } , . . . , \varpi _ { 0 } ) ] + { \cal O } ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { \jmath }$ is a homogeneous polynomial of degree $n + 1$ in the variables indicated, given by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { J = } & { - \Bigg [ \displaystyle \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \zeta } U _ { n - r } + \cdots + \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \bar { \omega } } \overline { { V } } _ { n - r } \right) } \\ & { + \displaystyle \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \sqrt { - 1 } f _ { r } \lambda _ { n - r } \Bigg ] _ { \zeta _ { 0 , \cdots , \bar { \omega } _ { 0 } } } . } \end{array}
$$

The bracket in (84) is

$$
\zeta _ { 0 } \Big [ \frac { d } { d t } \Big ( \frac { f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \overline { { { \omega } } } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } \Big ) + \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } - \frac { J ( \zeta _ { 0 } , . . . , \overline { { { \omega } } } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } \Big ] .
$$

We must find $f _ { n } , \lambda _ { n }$ such that, with $f _ { n } = f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots )$ and $f _ { n }$ satisfying (80),this becomes zero.

Now $J = J ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \varpi _ { 0 } )$ is of form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \sum c _ { p q r s } [ a \exp { \{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ] ^ { p } [ a \exp { \{ - ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ] ^ { q } } } \\ & { } & { \times \ [ b \exp { \{ ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ] ^ { r } [ b \exp { \{ - ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ] ^ { s } } \end{array}
$$

with $p + q + r + s = n + 1$ ，and where $J / \zeta _ { 0 }$ is the same sum with the index $p$ replaced by $p - 1$ . Write $R = { R } _ { 1 } + \sqrt { - 1 } R _ { 2 }$ for the sum of those terms of $J / \zeta _ { 0 }$ that have $p - 1 = q$ ， $r = s$ If $_ n$ is even, then $\pmb R$ is a constant and is a homogeneous polynomial $R ( a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 } )$ of degree $\scriptstyle { { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } n }$ in $a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 }$ given by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { R = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } } \int \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } J [ a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} , a \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} , } } \\ { { b \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \phi \right\} , b \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \phi \right\} ] } } \\ { { \times [ a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} ] ^ { - 1 } \cdot d \theta d \phi . } } \end{array}
$$

If $_ n$ is odd, then there is no term with $p - 1 = q$ ， $r = s$ ，and $\pmb R$ is identically zero. In either case,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { J } { \zeta _ { 0 } } = \displaystyle { et { } { ' } \sum _ { \ell \geqslant q \tau _ { s } } } [ a \exp \{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} ] ^ { p } \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \times [ b \exp \{ - ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} ] ^ { s } + R _ { 1 } + \sqrt { - 1 } R _ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\Sigma ^ { \prime }$ has no term with $p - 1 = q$ ， $r = s$ . Now， since $\mu / \lambda$ is irrational,it follows that (i) $\Sigma ^ { \prime }$ is of the form

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { Q [ a \exp \{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} , . . . , b \exp \{ - ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} ] } { a \exp \{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } \right)
$$

$$
= \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { Q ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots \ldots , \overline { { \omega } } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } \right) ,
$$

where $Q$ may have small divisors $h \lambda + k \mu$ but no zero divisor ; and (i)

$$
\int \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } { \frac { Q ( a \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta \right\} , \ldots , b \exp \left\{ - { \sqrt { - 1 } } \phi \right\} ) } { a \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta \right\} } } d \theta d \phi = 0 .
$$

Then we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { J ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \dots , \overline { { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { Q ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \dots , \overline { { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } \right) } \\ { \displaystyle ~ + ~ R _ { 1 } ( a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 } ) + \sqrt { - 1 } ~ R _ { 2 } ( a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

We shall prove that the real part $R _ { 1 }$ is identically zero.

Assuming that this is done, we can make (85) equal to zero by choosing

$$
\lambda _ { n } = R _ { 2 } ( a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 } ) , \qquad f _ { n } ( \zeta , \ldots , \overline { { { \omega } } } ) = Q ( \zeta , \ldots , \overline { { { \omega } } } ) ,
$$

noting that $f _ { n }$ duly satisfies (8O) and is uniquely determined.Then we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( F _ { n } \exp \left\{ \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} \right) = { \cal O } ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } ) ,
$$

and there is no asymptotic integral of the form

$$
F _ { n } \exp \left\{ \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} = a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \alpha \right\} + O ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } )
$$

over $t \leq 1$ that reduces to $\zeta = a \exp { \{ ( \alpha ^ { ' } - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} }$ as $\varepsilon \to 0$ ，with the $f _ { m }$ up to $m \div \ u = n$ satisfying (80). The step from $_ n$ to $n + 1$ is completed for $\pmb { F }$ . We can proceed similarly for $G$

Incidentally, we have seen that, when $R _ { 1 } = 0$ is proved, then

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \overline { { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } \right) + \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda _ { n } - \frac { J ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \overline { { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } = 0 ,
$$

$$
4 \pi ^ { 2 } { \sqrt { - 1 } } \lambda _ { n } = \int \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } { \frac { J ( a \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta \right\} , \ldots , b \exp \left\{ - { \sqrt { - 1 } } \phi \right\} ) } { a \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta \right\} } } d \theta d _ { \Omega }
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { J ( \zeta , \dots , \overline { { { \omega } } } ) = - \displaystyle \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \zeta } U _ { n - r } + \cdots + \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \overline { { { \omega } } } } \overline { { { V } } } _ { n - r } \right) } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \sqrt { - 1 } f _ { r } \lambda _ { n - r } . } } \end{array}
$$

We now proceed to the proof that $R _ { 1 } = 0$ . Choose $\lambda _ { n } = R _ { 2 }$ ， $f _ { n } = Q$ in (85),which becomes $- \zeta _ { 0 } R _ { 1 }$ by (86) and we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } ( F _ { n } \exp { \{ \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ) = - \varepsilon ^ { n } \zeta _ { 0 } \exp { \{ \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \} } { \cdot } R _ { 1 } + O ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } ) .
$$

If

$$
F _ { n } ^ { * } = | F _ { n } | ^ { 2 } = F _ { n } \exp { \{ \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \} } { \cdot } \bar { F } _ { n } \exp { \{ - \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ,
$$

then

$$
\frac { d F _ { n } ^ { * } } { d t } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( F _ { n } \exp \left\{ \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} \right) \cdot \bar { F } _ { n } \exp \left\{ - \Lambda _ { n } t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \Delta F _ { \pi } ^ { * } = F _ { \pi } ^ { * } ( t ) - F _ { \pi } ^ { * } ( 0 ) = - 2 \varepsilon ^ { n } t a ^ { 2 } R _ { 1 } ( a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 } ) + O ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } ) } \\ { ( 0 \leq t \leq 1 ) , } \end{array}
$$

and similarly for $G _ { n } ^ { * }$

For real $X , Y , Z , W$ ，the function $F _ { n } ^ { * }$ is nonnegative and of the form ${ \sum } _ { m = 0 } ^ { 2 n } { \varepsilon } ^ { m } f _ { m } ^ { * } ( X , Y , Z , W )$ ,where $f _ { m } ^ { * }$ depends only on m and $_ m$ ,and $f _ { 0 } ^ { * } = X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 }$ . The function $G _ { n } ^ { * }$ is of a similar form with $g _ { 0 } ^ { \ast } = Z ^ { 2 } +$ $W ^ { 2 }$ .

To show that $R _ { 1 } = 0$ identically in $a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 }$ ，we need Jacobi’s integral. This is of the form

$$
\sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { \infty } k _ { m } ^ { * } \varepsilon ^ { m } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad \mathrm { o r } \qquad \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { \infty } \Delta k _ { m } ^ { * } \varepsilon ^ { m } = 0 ,
$$

where $k _ { m } ^ { * }$ is a homogeneous polynomial in $X , Y , Z ,$ W of degree $m + 2$ satisfying $k _ { m } ^ { * } = O ( A ^ { m + 2 } )$ , where $\pmb { A }$ denotes a positive constant depending only on $\mathbf { m }$ ，and $k _ { 0 } ^ { * } = g _ { 0 } ^ { * } - A _ { 1 } f _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ .If $_ n$ has the same meaning as stated in the lemma we define

$$
g _ { m } ^ { * * } = A _ { 1 } f _ { m } ^ { * } + k _ { m } ^ { * } \qquad ( m \leq n ) ,
$$

so that $g _ { 0 } ^ { * * } = g _ { 0 } ^ { * } = Z ^ { 2 } + W ^ { 2 }$

Consider the volume in the $( h , k , l , m )$ -space contained in the surface $\pmb { S } ( 0 )$ ，or

$$
L ( h , k , l , m ) = c ^ { - 2 } F _ { \pi } ^ { * } ( h , k , l , m ) + G _ { \pi } ^ { * * } ( h , \ldots , m ) = 1 ,
$$

where， say, $1 / 2 \lessgtr c \lessgtr 1$ . For small $\varepsilon$ the surface $L =$ constant is closed,and expands as the constant increases. The limit surface $S _ { \mathfrak { d } } ( 0 )$ as $\varepsilon \to 0$ is, in polar coordinates,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { h = r \cos { \theta } , \quad } & { k = r \sin { \theta } , \quad \quad l = \rho \cos { \phi _ { 1 } } , \quad \quad m = \rho \sin { \phi _ { 1 } } , } \\ & { L _ { 0 } ( h , k , l , m ) = c ^ { - 2 } r ^ { 2 } + \rho ^ { 2 } = 1 . } \end{array}
$$

Denote by $S ( t )$ the locus of $( X , Y , Z , W )$ ，where $X , Y , Z , W$ are the values at time $t \leq 1$ corresponding to the initial values $( h , k , l , m )$ belonging to $s ( 0 )$ at $t = 0$ . By using $\Delta$ for an increment from $t = 0$ to $t$ we have asymptotically,as $\varepsilon \to 0$ ，

$$
\Delta k _ { n } ^ { * } = - \Delta \sum _ { m = n + 1 } ^ { \infty } k _ { m } ^ { * } \varepsilon ^ { m } = { \cal O } ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \Delta { ( c ^ { - 2 } F _ { n } ^ { * } + G _ { n } ^ { * * } ) } = ( c ^ { - 2 } + A _ { 1 } ) \Delta F _ { n } ^ { * } + { \cal O } ( \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } ) \sim \sigma \varepsilon ^ { n } t , } } \\ { { \sigma = \sigma ( a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 } ) = - 2 ( c ^ { - 2 } + A _ { 1 } ) a ^ { 2 } R _ { 1 } ( a ^ { 2 } , b ^ { 2 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The distance $\pmb { \tau }$ from $( h , k , l , m )$ of $S ( 0 )$ to $S ( t )$ ， reckoned positively outwards from $S ( 0 )$ , is asymptotically

$$
\tau \sim \varepsilon ^ { n } t \sigma \Big ( \sum L _ { h } ^ { 2 } \Big ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \sim \varepsilon ^ { n } t \sigma \Big ( \sum L _ { 0 h } ^ { 2 } \Big ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } .
$$

The difference between the volumes contained in $S ( t )$ and $s ( 0 )$ is asymptotically $\int \tau d S$ ， the surface integral being taken over $S _ { \mathfrak { o } } ( 0 )$ Since this difference is zero by Liouville's theorem (Sections 1.15 and 10.36),we have

$$
\int _ { S _ { 0 } ( 0 ) } \sigma \left( \sum L _ { 0 h } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } d S = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\int \int \int \sigma ( r ^ { 2 } , \rho ^ { 2 } ) d \theta \rho d \rho d \phi = 0 ,
$$

where the limits of integration are from O to $2 \pi$ for $\pmb \theta$ and $\phi$ ，and from 0 to 1 for $\pmb \rho$ ，and where $r$ is the function of $\pmb { \rho }$ given by $r ^ { 2 } = c ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } )$ It follows from (91） that

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) R _ { 1 } [ c ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } ) , \rho ^ { 2 } ] d \rho = 0 .
$$

Since $R _ { 1 } ( r ^ { 2 } , \rho ^ { 2 } )$ is a polynomial $\sum a _ { m } r ^ { 2 m } \rho ^ { n + 2 - 2 m }$ for an even $\pmb { n }$ and zero for an odd $_ n$ ，the left-hand member of (92）is a polynomial in $c ^ { 2 }$ Equating the coefficients of the various $c ^ { 2 m }$ to zero, we obtain $a _ { m } = 0$ and $R _ { 1 } \equiv 0$ as desired.

Similarly we can prove that $S _ { 1 } \equiv 0$ ，and Lemma 1 is established.

The four integrals of Lemma 1 are uniquely determined to within an error $O ( \varepsilon ^ { n } )$ by $a , b , \alpha , \beta$

Henceforth the constants of the $O ^ { \prime } { \bf s }$ are supposed to depend on m and a, $^ { b }$ . We must now consider complex values of $X , Y , Z , W , a , b$ Identities are naturally carried over to the complex field.

Let $M _ { r }$ be the upper bound of $| f _ { r } ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , z _ { 3 } , z _ { 4 } ) |$ subject to $\mid z _ { 1 } \mid , \mid z _ { 2 } \mid ;$ $\vert z _ { 3 } \vert , \vert z _ { 4 } \vert \le 1$ ，which we shall call Condition $C$ . Let $m _ { \tau }$ be the upper bound of $| \lambda _ { \tau } |$ for $| a | , | b | \leq 1$ . For the z's, $M _ { r }$ is attained with $| z _ { 1 } | = | z _ { 2 } | = | z _ { 3 } | = | z _ { 4 } | = 1$ ，which we shall call Condition $C ^ { * }$ ，and $M _ { \tau }$ is the upper bound of $| f _ { r } / z _ { 1 } |$ subject to $C ^ { * }$ ，while $m _ { \tau }$ is attained for $| a | = | b | = 1$

Because of homogeneity we have $| f _ { r } | \equiv M _ { \tau } [ \operatorname* { m a x } { ( | a | , | b | ) } ] ^ { r + 1 }$ for the $z ' s$ ， subject to $| z _ { 1 } | , | z _ { 2 } | \leq | a | , | z _ { 3 } | , | z _ { 4 } | \leq | b |$ ，and we have $| \lambda _ { \tau } | \leqq$ $m _ { r } [ \operatorname* { m a x } { ( | a | , | b | ) } ] ^ { r }$

With the obvious meaning of $U ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , z _ { 3 } , z _ { 4 } )$ , etc.， we have

$$
| U _ { \tau } | , | \overline { { U } } _ { \tau } | , | V _ { \tau } | , | \overline { { V } } _ { \tau } | < A ^ { \tau + 1 }
$$

subject to $C ^ { * }$ . Also subject to $C ^ { * }$ we have from a theorem of Bernstein (Hardy,Littewood and P6lya, 1934),

$$
\left| \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial z _ { i } } \right| \leq ( r + 1 ) M _ { r } .
$$

Hence we have from (89)

$$
| J ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , z _ { 3 } , z _ { 4 } ) | < A \sum _ { \tau = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } ( r + 1 ) M _ { r } A ^ { n - r + 1 } + \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } M _ { \tau } m _ { n - \tau } = o
$$

say, subject to $C$ or $C ^ { * }$

Taking $| a | = | b | = 1$ in the identity (88),we derive from (93)

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { | \lambda _ { n } | \leq m _ { n } \leq \sigma _ { n } , \qquad \mathrm { s u b j e c t ~ t o } \qquad | a | , | b | \leq 1 , } } \\ { { | \lambda _ { n } | \leq \sigma _ { n } \left[ \operatorname* { m a x } \left( | a | , | b | \right) \right] ^ { n } \qquad \mathrm { f o r ~ a n y } \qquad a , b . } } \end{array}
$$

From (87) we have

$$
\left| \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \bar { \omega } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } \right) \right| \equiv 2 \sigma _ { n } ,
$$

Lemma 2: Let

$$
K _ { r } = ( r + 1 ) ^ { 2 } \log ^ { 3 / 2 } \left( r + 2 \right) \qquad ( r \geq 0 ) ;
$$

then we have

$$
M _ { n } , m _ { n } < ( A _ { 1 } K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } .
$$

Subject to Condition $C$ ， the absolute value $| f _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { 4 } ) |$ attains its upper bound $M _ { n }$ for $z ' \mathbf { s }$ of the form a exp $\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \alpha \}$ ， $a \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \alpha \right\}$ ， $b \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \beta \right\}$ ， $b \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \beta \right\}$ ，with real $\alpha , \beta$ and complex $a , b$ satifying $| a | = | b | = 1$ .If

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \zeta _ { 0 } = a \exp { \{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } & { } & { \bar { \zeta } _ { 0 } = a \exp { \{ - ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } \\ { \omega _ { 0 } = b \exp { \{ ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } & { } & { \bar { \omega } _ { 0 } = b \exp { \{ - ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } , } \end{array}
$$

then we have

$$
M _ { n } = f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \varpi _ { 0 } ) \mid _ { t = 0 } .
$$

We should have either $| \Re ( f _ { n } / \zeta _ { 0 } ) | \geq \frac { 1 } { 2 } M _ { n }$ or $| \mathfrak { F } ( f _ { n } / \zeta _ { 0 } ) | \geq \frac { 1 } { 2 } M _ { n }$ at $t =$ O,say the former

$$
\Re [ f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \overline { { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) / \zeta _ { 0 } ] \big | _ { t = 0 } \geq \frac { 1 } { 2 } M _ { n } .
$$

Now from (80) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { [ f _ { \pi } ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} , \ldots , b \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \phi \right\} ) / ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} ) ] \medskip } \\ & { } & { \times d \theta d \phi = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Hence there exist $\theta _ { 0 } , \phi _ { 0 }$ such that

$$
\Re [ f _ { n } ( a \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \} , \ldots , b \exp \{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \phi _ { 0 } \} ) ] = 0 .
$$

Next, for fixed $\phi$

$$
\Re [ f _ { n } ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \right\} , \dots , b \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \phi _ { 0 } \right\} ) / ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} ) ]
$$

is a trigonometric polynomial in $\pmb \theta$ of degree $n + 2$ at most, absolutely bounded by $M _ { n }$ . The same is true with $\theta$ and $\phi$ interchanged. By Bernstein's theorem

$$
\left| { \frac { \partial } { \partial { \boldsymbol { \phi } } } } \Re [ f _ { n } / ( a \exp \left\{ { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta \right\} ) ] \right| \leq ( n + 2 ) M _ { n } .
$$

Since $\lambda / \mu$ has a continued fraction with $a _ { n } = O ( n \log ^ { 2 } n )$ ， it follows that we can find a $t _ { 1 } < A ( n + 1 ) \log ^ { 3 / 2 } ( n + 2 )$ such that

$$
\alpha - \lambda t _ { 1 } \equiv \theta _ { 0 } , \beta = \mu t _ { 1 } = \phi _ { 0 } + \mathcal { S } / [ 4 ( n + 2 ) ] ,
$$

$$
| \vartheta | \equiv 1 \qquad ( { \mathrm { m o d } } 2 \pi ) ,
$$

where $\zeta _ { \mathfrak { d } } ( t _ { 1 } ) , \omega _ { \mathfrak { d } } ( t _ { 1 } )$ are respectively equal to $a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \right\}$ exactly and to $b \exp { \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \phi _ { 0 } \} }$ approximately.Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \left. { \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \widehat { \boldsymbol { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) / \zeta _ { 0 } ] \left. _ { t = t _ { 0 } } \right. } } \\ & { } & { - \left. \Re [ f _ { n } ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \right\} , \ldots , b \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \phi _ { 0 } \right\} ) \right/ } \\ & { } & { ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta _ { 0 } \right\} ) ] } \\ & { } & { \leq \left| \int \frac { \partial } { \partial \phi } \Re [ f _ { n } ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} , \ldots , b \exp \left\{ - \sqrt { - 1 } \phi _ { 0 } \right\} ) \right/ } \\ & { } & { ( a \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\} ) ] \cdot d \phi \right| , } \end{array}
$$

where the integral is taken over an interval of length $< 1 / [ 4 ( n + 2 ) ]$ Since the second term on the left-hand side is zero,we can derive from (98)

$$
\Re [ f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , . ~ . ~ . ~ , \varpi _ { 0 } ) / \zeta _ { 0 } ] | _ { t = t _ { 1 } } \le { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } M _ { n } .
$$

Combining this with (97),we have

$$
A _ { n } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } m _ { n } < \Re [ f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \overline { { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) / \zeta _ { 0 } ] _ { t = 0 } ^ { \lvert t _ { 1 } } \le \int _ { 0 } ^ { t _ { 1 } } \left. \frac { d } { d t } \Bigl [ \frac { f _ { n } ( \zeta _ { 0 } , \ldots , \overline { { \omega } } _ { 0 } ) } { \zeta _ { 0 } } \Bigr ] \right. d t .
$$

Hence,from (95) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { 4 } M _ { n } \leq t _ { 1 } 2 \sigma _ { n } < A n ^ { * } \sigma _ { n } , } \end{array}
$$

where $n ^ { * } = ( n + 1 ) \log ^ { 3 / 2 } ( n + 2 )$ and $\sigma _ { n }$ is given by (93). Thus

$$
M _ { n } < A n ^ { * } \left[ \sum _ { \tau = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } ( r + 1 ) M _ { \tau } A ^ { n - \tau + 1 } + \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } M _ { \tau } m _ { n - \tau } \right] .
$$

From (93),(94), introducing an extra factor $n ^ { * }$ ， we have the same in-equality for $m _ { n }$ ：

$$
m _ { n } < A n ^ { * } \left[ \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } ( r + 1 ) M _ { r } A ^ { n - r + 1 } + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } M _ { r } m _ { n - r } \right] .
$$

It is not diffcult to derive Lemma 2 from (99) and (100).Let

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { M _ { n } ^ { \prime } = \operatorname* { m a x } { ( M _ { 0 } , \ldots , M _ { n } , m _ { 0 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) } , } \\ & { } & { L _ { n } ^ { \prime } = M _ { n } ^ { \prime } ( K _ { n } ) ^ { - ( n + 1 ) } , \qquad L _ { n } = \operatorname* { m a x } _ { t ^ { \prime } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $K _ { n }$ is given by (96). Then (99) and (100) give

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { L _ { n } < A n ^ { * } \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } { ( r + 1 ) L _ { n - 1 } A ^ { n - r + 1 } K _ { r } ^ { r + 1 } K _ { n } ^ { - ( n + 1 ) } } } } \\ { \displaystyle { + A n ^ { * } \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \operatorname* { m a x } _ { 1 \leq \nu \leq n - 1 } L _ { \nu } L _ { n - \nu } \right) K _ { r } ^ { r + 1 } K _ { n - r } ^ { n - r + 1 } K _ { n } ^ { - ( n + 1 ) } = S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

Now in $S _ { 1 }$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } { ( r + 1 ) A ^ { n - r + 1 } K _ { r } ^ { r + 1 } } < A n \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } { A ^ { n - r - 1 } K _ { n } ^ { r + 1 } } } \\ { = A n K _ { n } ( K _ { n } ^ { n } - A ^ { n } ) / ( K _ { n } - A ) . } \end{array}
$$

For $n \geq n _ { 0 }$ and for an $\pmb { A }$ ， positive constant depending only on $\mathbf { m }$ the denominator exceeds $\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } K _ { n }$ ，and the sum is less than $A n K _ { n } ^ { n }$ . Hence $S _ { 1 } < A n ^ { * } L _ { n - 1 } K _ { n } ^ { - 1 } < A L _ { n - 1 }$ for $n \geq n _ { 0 }$

In $S _ { z }$ it is easily verified by logarithmic differentiation that in the range $1 \leq r \leq n - 1$ the expression $K _ { r } ^ { r + 1 } K _ { n - r } ^ { n - r + 1 }$ has its common greatest value at the ends $r = 1$ and $r = n - 1$ . Hence

$$
S _ { 2 } < A n ^ { * } \Big ( \operatorname* { m a x } _ { 1 \leq \nu \leq n - 1 } L _ { \nu } L _ { n - \nu } \Big ) A n K _ { n - 1 } ^ { n } K _ { n } ^ { - ( n + 1 ) } < A \operatorname* { m a x } _ { 1 \leq \nu \leq n - 1 } L _ { \nu } L _ { n - \nu } .
$$

It follows that

$$
L _ { n } < A \operatorname* { m a x } _ { 1 \leq \nu \leq n - 1 } L _ { \nu } L _ { n - \nu }
$$

for $n \geq n _ { 0 }$ . We repeat this inequality on $L _ { y }$ or $L _ { n - \nu }$ or on both if either has a $\operatorname { s u f f i x } \geq n _ { 0 }$ . Since the sum of the suffixes at each stage is $_ n$ ，we must arrive,after at most $n$ operations,at $L _ { n } < A ^ { n } \prod _ { \mid \nu \mid } L _ { \nu }$ with all $\nu \equiv \operatorname* { m a x } \left( 1 , n _ { 0 } \right)$ and,at most, $_ n$ factors in the product. This gives $L _ { n } < A _ { 1 } ^ { n }$ ，which establishes Lemma 2.

Now we return to the real variables $X , Y , Z , W , a , b$ . Let $c =$ max $( a , b )$ so that $\varepsilon _ { 1 } = \varepsilon$ max $( a , b ) = c \varepsilon$ .We assume that $\varepsilon$ is small. Actually $\varepsilon _ { 1 }$ is less than a suitable quantity $\pmb { A }$ ，depending only on m. We fix the constant $\pmb { A } _ { 1 }$ of Lemma 2 and prove Lemma 3,a result of asymptotic integral type.

Lemma 3: Let

$$
N = N _ { \varepsilon } = [ A _ { 2 } | \varepsilon _ { 1 } | ^ { - 1 / 2 } | \log \varepsilon _ { 1 } | ^ { - 3 / 4 } ] ,
$$

$$
\Lambda = \Lambda _ { N _ { \varepsilon } - 1 } = \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \lambda _ { r } \varepsilon ^ { r } , \qquad M = M _ { N _ { \varepsilon } - 1 } = \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \mu _ { r } \varepsilon ^ { r } ,
$$

where the square bracket denotes the integral part. Let

$$
K _ { \tau } = ( r + 1 ) ^ { 2 } \log ^ { 3 / 2 } { ( r + 2 ) } .
$$

Then for a suitable, sufficiently small $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \cdot 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n + 1 } ) ^ { n + 2 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { n + 1 } / \lbrack ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { n } ] < e ^ { - 1 } ; } \\ & { \quad ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { n } < e ^ { - n } \qquad ( n < N ) . } \end{array}
$$

For $0 \leq t \leq 1$ the orbit determined by $a , b , \alpha , \beta$ satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { F _ { N - 1 } ( \zeta , \dots , \overline { { \omega } } ) - a \exp \left\{ ( \alpha - \lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} = O ( e ^ { - N } ) , } } \\ { { G _ { N - 1 } ( \zeta , \dots , \overline { { \omega } } ) - b \exp \left\{ ( \beta - \mu t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} = O ( e ^ { - N } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Furthermore, for $0 \leq t \leq 1$ and $n \leq N$ ，

$$
F _ { n - 1 } ( \zeta , \dots , \bar { \omega } ) - a \exp { \{ ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} }
$$

$$
= O [ ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n + 1 } ) ^ { n + 2 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { n } ] = O ( e ^ { - n } ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { G _ { n - 1 } ( \zeta , \ldots , \varpi ) - b \exp { \left\{ ( \beta - M t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} } } \\ & { \qquad = O [ ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n + 1 } ) ^ { n + 2 } \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { n } ] = O ( e ^ { - \pi } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Of the two forms of error terms in (103),the first is appropriate for a fixed $_ n$ like 2,3, the second for, e.g., $n = N - 1$

The results are all for a sufficiently small $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$ . They remain true when $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$ is chosen successively smaller.

Because of homogeneity we may assume without loss of generality that $c = 1$ ， and so $\varepsilon _ { 1 } = \varepsilon$ .Over $t \leq 1$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \zeta = a \exp \left\{ ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} + O ( \varepsilon ) , } } \\ { { \omega = b \exp \left\{ ( \beta - M t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} + O ( \varepsilon ) , } } \end{array}
$$

So $| \zeta | < 2 , | \omega | < 2$ for a suffciently small $\varepsilon$ . Hence we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \exp \left\{ - \Lambda t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} \cdot \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( F _ { N - 1 } \exp \left\{ \Lambda t \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} \right) } \\ & { \qquad = \displaystyle \sum _ { \tau = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { \tau } \Biggl [ \sqrt { - 1 } \ f _ { \tau } \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \lambda _ { m } \varepsilon ^ { m } } \\ & { \qquad + \ \displaystyle \frac { \partial f _ { \tau } } { \partial \zeta } \biggl ( - \lambda \sqrt { - 1 } \zeta + \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { \infty } \varepsilon ^ { m } U _ { m } \biggr ) + \cdots \Biggr ] } \\ & { \qquad = \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 0 } ^ { \infty } C _ { N + s } \varepsilon ^ { N + s } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
C _ { N + \nu } = \sum _ { \tau = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \Big [ \sqrt { - 1 } f _ { \tau } \lambda _ { N + \nu - \tau } + { \frac { \partial f _ { \tau } } { \partial \zeta } } U _ { N + \nu - \tau } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \Big ] ,
$$

the coefficients of $\varepsilon ^ { 0 } , \ldots , \varepsilon ^ { N - 1 }$ being zero.In this formula we have $\left| f _ { r } \right| \leq 2 ^ { r + 1 } M _ { \tau ; }$ ， $| \lambda _ { \tau } | \leq 2 ^ { r } m _ { \tau }$ .From Bernstein's theorem again, we obtain

$$
\left| \frac { \partial f _ { \tau } } { \partial \zeta } \right| \leq ( r + 1 ) 2 ^ { { \tau } + 1 } M _ { \tau } ,
$$

Also $| U _ { m } | , | V _ { m } | < A ^ { m + 1 }$ ， as was assumed in the beginning. Hence (104） gives

$$
_ { \tau + \nu } | \leq \sum _ { \tau = \nu } ^ { N - 1 } 2 ^ { \tau + 1 } M _ { \tau } 2 ^ { N + \nu - \tau } m _ { N + \nu - \tau } + \sum _ { \tau = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } 4 ( r + 1 ) 2 ^ { \tau } M _ { r } A ^ { N + \nu - \tau + 1 }
$$

the first term being interpreted as O if $\nu > N - 1$ . The second term is easily seen to be smaller than $\pmb { A } ^ { N + \nu } \pmb { K } _ { N } ^ { N }$ , and the first is smaller than

$$
A ^ { N + \nu + 2 } \sum _ { \tau = \nu } ^ { N - 1 } K _ { \nu } ^ { \tau + 1 } K _ { N + \nu - \tau } ^ { N + \nu - \tau + 1 } .
$$

As in the proof of Lemma 2, the summand is greatest at one of the extreme values $r = \nu$ ， $N - 1$ . It is easily verified that the term is smaller than $A ^ { N + \upsilon } \cdot A N K _ { N } \cdot K _ { N } ^ { N + \upsilon + 1 }$ ， and we may absorb the middle factor in a new $A ^ { N + \upsilon }$ . Thus

$$
| C _ { N + \nu } | \varepsilon ^ { N + \nu } \leq \varepsilon ^ { - 1 } [ A K _ { N } \varepsilon ] ^ { N + \nu + 1 } .
$$

Now

$$
