\begin{array} { r l r } {  { ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n + 1 } ) ^ { n + 2 } \varepsilon ^ { n + 1 } / [ ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } \varepsilon ^ { n } ] } } \\ & { } & { \quad \leq 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { N } \varepsilon ( K _ { n + 1 } / K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } < A K _ { N } \varepsilon < e ^ { - 1 } , } \end{array}
$$

if $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$ is chosen sufficiently small. This proves the first part of (101): and the second part follows after a new choice of $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$

Also, choosing $\ b { A } _ { 2 }$ sufficiently small, we have

$$
| C _ { N + \nu } | \varepsilon ^ { N + \nu } < \varepsilon ^ { - 1 } \exp \{ - 2 ( N + \nu + 1 ) \} ,
$$

and then

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  {  \frac { d } { d t } ( F _ { N - 1 } \exp \{ \Lambda t \sqrt { - 1 } \} )  } } \\ & { } & { \qquad \leq \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { \infty } \varepsilon ^ { - 1 } \exp \{ - 2 ( N + \nu + 1 ) \} < A \varepsilon ^ { - 1 } e ^ { - 2 N } < A e ^ { - N } , } \\ & { } & { \qquad F _ { N - 1 } \exp \{ \Lambda t \sqrt { - 1 } \} = a \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \alpha \} + O ( e ^ { - N } ) . \qquad ( 1 } \end{array}
$$

Also for $t \leq 1 , n \leq N$ ,we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { | F _ { n - 1 } - F _ { N - 1 } | \le \sum _ { m = n } ^ { N - 1 } | f _ { m } | \varepsilon ^ { m } } } \\ & { } & { \ \le \sum _ { m = n } ^ { N - 1 } 2 ^ { m } M _ { m } \varepsilon ^ { m } \le \sum _ { m = n } ^ { N - 1 } ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { m } ) ^ { m + 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\leq ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } \varepsilon ^ { n } \sum _ { m = n } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \left( m - n \right) } = O [ ( 2 A _ { 1 } K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } \varepsilon ^ { n } ] .
$$

The equations (105),(106) give

$$
F _ { n - 1 } - a \exp \left\{ ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} = O [ ( 2 A K _ { n } ) ^ { n + 1 } \varepsilon ^ { n } ] = O ( e ^ { - \pi } ) ,
$$

which is the equation (103) for $F$ . Similarly we can deal with $G$

The equation (1O2) is a part of the special case $n = N$ of (103); thus we have completed the proof of Lemma 3.

Now we solve the equations (103) for $\zeta$ and $\omega$

Assume that $c = \operatorname* { m a x } \left( a , b \right)$ ，normalized to $c = 1$ . Let $N = N _ { \epsilon }$ be the $N$ of Lemma 3.Write

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \zeta = z _ { 1 } , \quad } & { { } \bar { \zeta } = z _ { 2 } , \quad } & { \omega = z _ { 3 } , \quad } \bar { \omega } = z _ { 4 } ,  \\ { u _ { 1 } = a \exp \left\{ ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} , \quad } & { { } u _ { 2 } = a \exp \left\{ - ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} } \\ { u _ { 3 } = b \exp \left\{ ( \beta - M t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} , \quad } & { { } u _ { 4 } = b \exp \left\{ - ( \beta - M t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} } \end{array}
$$

with $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , r _ { 3 } , r _ { 4 }$ for the errors in (103) and their conjugates, and

$$
v _ { 1 } = u _ { 1 } + r _ { 1 } , \qquad \ldots , \qquad v _ { 4 } = u _ { 4 } + r _ { 4 } .
$$

With $n \leq N _ { \varepsilon }$ ，we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { z _ { 1 } = - \displaystyle \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } f _ { m } ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , z _ { 3 } , z _ { 4 } ) + v _ { 1 } , . . . , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { z _ { 4 } = - \displaystyle \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } \bar { g } _ { m } ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , z _ { 3 } , z _ { 4 } ) + v _ { 4 } . } } \end{array}
$$

The polynomials $f _ { m } , \ldots , \bar { g } _ { m }$ are each majorized by

$$
M _ { m } ( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } + z _ { 3 } + z _ { 4 } ) ^ { m + 1 } .
$$

Hence the solution of (107） for $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , z _ { 3 } , z _ { 4 }$ as power series in $\varepsilon$ with polynomials in the $v ' s$ as coefficients are each majorized by the expansion of $Z = z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } + z _ { 3 } + z _ { 4 }$ determined by

$$
Z = \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \varepsilon ^ { m } ( A _ { 1 } K _ { m } ) ^ { m + 1 } Z ^ { m + 1 } + V , ~ V = v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + v _ { 3 } + v _ { 4 }
$$

Temporarily we put $Z = V z$ ; then this equation becomes

$$
z \biggl [ 1 - \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } ( A _ { 1 } K _ { m } ) ^ { m + 1 } ( \varepsilon V z ) ^ { m } \biggr ] = 1 ,
$$

with a z-expansion

$$
z = 1 + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { \infty } C _ { r } ^ { ( n ) } ( \varepsilon V ) ^ { r } .
$$

The nonnegative coefficients $C _ { \tau } ^ { ( n ) }$ are independent of $V$ ， and those with $r \leq n$ are indepedent of $_ n$

Lemma 4: We have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { C _ { r } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( n ) } < ( A K _ { \tau } ) ^ { \scriptscriptstyle \tau + 1 } \quad ( r \leq n ) , \qquad C _ { r } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( n ) } < ( A K _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathscr { n } } ) ^ { \scriptscriptstyle \tau + 1 } \quad ( r \geq n ) . } \end{array}
$$

First it is enough to prove the second result, since for $r \leq n$ we have $C _ { r } ^ { ( n ) } = C _ { r } ^ { ( r ) }$ ， which is covered by the second result. Secondly, for a given $r$ ， the coefficient $C _ { \tau } ^ { ( n ) }$ increases with $_ n$ ，as does $K _ { n }$ . It is therefore sufficient to prove the second result for a large $_ n$ ， $n > A _ { 0 }$ ， since it will be made true for $n \leq { \cal A } _ { 0 }$ by a new choice of the $\pmb { A }$ in $A K _ { n }$

Furthermore let us suppose that $V = 1$ . Consider now the function $z ( \eta )$ defined by（108） with $V = 1$ as a function of a complex $\eta - \varepsilon \exp \left\{ \sqrt { - 1 } \theta \right\}$ . For a given $\varepsilon$ ，the function $| z ( \eta ) |$ has its maximum $z ( \varepsilon )$ when $\theta = 0$ . Choose ε so that $n = N _ { \mathfrak { s } } = N$ . Then $z = z ( \varepsilon )$ is less than or equal to the solution of

$$
z = 1 + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { N - 1 } ( A _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } K _ { r } ) ^ { r + 1 } \varepsilon ^ { r } z ^ { r + 1 } ,
$$

and by (101) $z ( \varepsilon )$ is less than the solution of

$$
z = 1 + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { N - 1 } 2 ^ { - ( r + 1 ) } e ^ { - r } z ^ { r + 1 } ,
$$

or of $z = 1 + \Sigma _ { 1 } ^ { \infty }$ . This is $\begin{array} { r } { 1 - ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 1 } ) z + \frac { 3 } { 4 } e ^ { - 1 } z ^ { 2 } = 0 } \end{array}$ ，whose positive solution is of the form $z = A$ . It follows that $| z ( \eta ) | \equiv z ( \varepsilon ) < A$ for $\varepsilon$ with $N _ { \varepsilon } = n + 1$ . By Cauchy's inequality,we have

$$
C _ { r } ^ { { \scriptscriptstyle ( n ) } } \varepsilon ^ { r } < A , \qquad C _ { r } ^ { { \scriptscriptstyle ( n ) } } < A \varepsilon ^ { - r } < ( A K _ { \scriptscriptstyle \pi } ) ^ { r } ,
$$

since $ { \varepsilon } ^ { - 1 } < A K _ { n }$ . A fortiori, $C _ { r } ^ { ( n ) } < ( A K _ { n } ) ^ { r + 1 }$ ，which includes the second result of the lemma.Thus Lemma 4 is established.

We continue the normalization to $c = 1$ and consider the range $t \leq 1$ . It follows from the majorizations and Lemma 4, if we take the extreme value $n = N - 1$ ， that we have expansions

$$
\zeta = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { \infty } \phi _ { m } ( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } , ~ \omega = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { \infty } \psi _ { m } ( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } ,
$$

and their conjugates,in which

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { | \phi _ { m } | , | \psi _ { m } | \le C _ { m } ^ { ( N - 1 ) } ( | v _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | v _ { 4 } | ) ^ { m + 1 } } } \\ & { } & { \le \{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle ( A K _ { m } ) ^ { m + 1 } \bigg ( \sum _ { | } | v | \bigg ) ^ { m + 1 } \qquad ( m < N ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \Big ( A K _ { N - 1 } ) ^ { m + 1 } \Big ( 1 + \sum _ { | } | v | \Big ) ^ { m + 1 } \qquad ( m \ge N ) , } \end{array}  } \end{array}
$$

where $v _ { i } = u _ { i } + r _ { i } , u _ { 1 } = a \exp { \{ ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \} } ,$ etc., and,by (103):

$$
r _ { i } = O [ e ^ { - N } ] \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 ) .
$$

By the nature of the majorizations we also have e.g.,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \phi _ { m } } { \partial v _ { i } } \bigg | \leq \left[ \frac { d } { d V } \left( C _ { m } ^ { ( N - 1 ) } V ^ { m + 1 } \right) \right] _ { V = \Sigma ^ { 1 } v ^ { 1 } } = ( m + 1 ) C _ { m } ^ { ( N - 1 ) } \Big ( \sum _ { \scriptstyle | \displaystyle } | v | \Big ) ^ { m } } \\ { \leq \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle ( A K _ { m } ) ^ { m + 1 } \Big ( \sum _ { \scriptstyle | \displaystyle } | v | \Big ) ^ { m } \qquad ( m < N ) , } & { \quad \displaystyle ( 1 1 } \\ { \displaystyle ( A K _ { N - 1 } ) ^ { m + 1 } \Big ( \sum _ { \scriptstyle | \displaystyle } | v | \Big ) ^ { m } \qquad ( m \geq N ) . } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

We have $\textstyle \sum | v | \leq \sum | u | + O ( \varepsilon ) \leq 4 + O ( \varepsilon ) < 5$ Moreover,if $v _ { i } =$ $u _ { i } + \tau r _ { i }$ ， $0 \leq \tau \leq 1$ ，then we have likewise $\Sigma \left| v ^ { \prime } \right| < 5$ . If now we choose a new $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$ sufficiently small, then (110), (112) give

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \left| \phi _ { m } ( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { 4 } ) \right| \varepsilon ^ { m } , \left| \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 4 } ) \right| \varepsilon ^ { m } , \ } \\ & { } & { \left| \frac { \partial \phi _ { m } } { \partial v _ { i } } \right| \varepsilon ^ { m } , \ \left| \frac { \partial \phi _ { m } } { \partial v _ { i } } \right| _ { v _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , v _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } } \varepsilon ^ { m } < \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \left( e ^ { - 3 } K _ { m } / K _ { N - 1 } \right) ^ { m + 1 } } & { ( m < N ) , } \\ { e ^ { - 3 ( m + 1 ) } } & { ( m \ge N ) , } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

with similar inequalities in $\psi ^ { \ast } \mathbf { s }$

From (109) and (113) we have, for a sufficiently small $\pmb { A } _ { 2 }$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \xi = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \phi _ { m } ( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } + { \cal O } ( e ^ { - N } ) } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \omega = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \psi _ { m } ( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } + { \cal O } ( e ^ { - N } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

For $m \le N - 1$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { | \phi _ { m } ( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { 4 } ) - \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 4 } ) | \varepsilon ^ { m } } \\ & { \qquad = | \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } + r _ { 1 } \tau , \ldots , u _ { 4 } + r _ { 4 } \tau ) - \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 4 } ) | \varepsilon ^ { m } } \\ & { \qquad \leq 2 \Bigl ( \displaystyle \sum _ { 0 \leq \tau \leq 1 } \left| r _ { i } \right| \Bigr ) \operatorname* { m a x } _ { 0 \leq \tau \leq 1 } \left| \frac { \partial \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } + r _ { 1 } \tau , \ldots , u _ { 4 } + r _ { 4 } \tau ) } { \partial u _ { i } } \right| \varepsilon ^ { m } } \\ & { \qquad < e ^ { - 3 ( m + 1 ) } \cdot O ( e ^ { - N } ) , } \end{array}
$$

because of (111) and (113). Hence, from (114) we obtain

$$
\zeta = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } + O ( e ^ { - N } ) \qquad ( t \leq 1 ) .
$$

This proves Lemma 4.

We now turn to complete the proof of Littlewood's Fundamental Theorem.

Owing to these special forms of the u's, a $\gg \gg \{ ( \alpha - \Lambda t ) \sqrt { - 1 } \}$ ,etc., it follows at once from (115) that for the range $t \le e ^ { N / 2 }$ we have

$$
\zeta - \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { N - 1 } \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } = O \left[ \operatorname* { m a x } \left( 1 , t \right) \cdot e ^ { - N } \right] = O [ e ^ { - N / 2 } ] .
$$

Since $| u _ { i } | \leq c$ for all $t$ of the new range, itfollows that $| \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $u _ { 4 } ) |$ satisfies (11O),and we have for $n < N$

$$
\Big | \zeta - \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n } \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } \Bigg | < O [ e ^ { - N / 2 } ] + \sum _ { m = n + 1 } ^ { N - 1 } ( e ^ { - 3 } K _ { m } / K _ { N - 1 } ) ^ { m }
$$

In the last sum, the ratio of the term in $m + 2$ to that in $m + 1$ is

$$
( e ^ { - 3 } K _ { m + 1 } / K _ { N - 1 } ) ( K _ { m + 1 } / K _ { m } ) ^ { m + 1 } .
$$

The second factor is less than exp $[ 2 + O ( 1 ) ]$ for a large $^ { m }$ ，and the product is less than $e ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ for a sufficiently small $\varepsilon$ , for different reasons according as, say, $m < { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } N$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { m \gt \geq \frac { 1 } { 2 } N } \end{array}$ . Thus

$$
\sum _ { m = n + 1 } ^ { N - 1 } < A ( K _ { n } / K _ { N - 1 } ) ^ { n + 1 } ,
$$

$$
\zeta = \sum _ { m = 0 } ^ { n } \phi _ { m } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 4 } ) \varepsilon ^ { m } + O [ ( K _ { n } / K _ { n - 1 } ) ^ { n + 1 } ] + O ( e ^ { - N / 2 } ) ;
$$

and similarly for $\omega$

Returning to a general $^ c$ ， the upshot now is that we have results of the type originally asserted for $x , y$ in the Fundamental Theorem, but now for $\zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \varpi$ . Since $x , y , d x / d t , d y / d t$ are linear functions of $\zeta , \bar { \zeta } , \omega , \varpi$ ， it is easily seen that we obtain the actual results of the Fundamental Theorem with new functions $\phi , \psi$

# Stability Problem

# LIAPOUNOV STABILITY

# 16.1DEFINITION OF STABILITY

In the theory of differential equations there is an endeavor to obtain a definitive solution or one of definitive type,either in closed form, which is rarely possible, or else by some process of approximation; this latter approach has been the main topic of the previous chapters. On the other hand, one seeks to obtain information about the whole class of solutions instead of reaching either exact or approximate solutions. This latter area of study was initiated by Poincaré (1885, 1879,1899).A major problem in such qualitative theory is the relation of a solution to its neighbors.A solution in general represents a curve or a trajectory $C$ .The question is whether the trajectories $C ^ { \prime }$ starting near $C$ are recurrent or periodic,in particular,whether they tend to remain in the immediate neighborhood $B$ of $C$ ，in which case $C$ is stable,or depart from $\pmb { B }$ ， in which case $C$ is unstable,or whether $C ^ { \prime }$ fills up a finite restricted domain everywhere densely,in which case it is said to be stable in Poisson's sense or ergodic. The topological theory that will be treated later in Chapters 22 to 27 is of such a qualitative character. The notion of stability appears in all such qualitative theories (Routh，1877,1884,1905).Liapounov(1907, 1949,1966) in 1892 consolidated the theory of stability by refining Poincaré's idea on characteristic exponents (Section 19.1).Liapounov called the first method the derivation of an explicit solution known in definite series form which is only applicable to some restricted but important cases.Liapounov's second method, called direct, does not require the knowledge of the solution itself；instead,it demands to formulate the so-called Liapounov function or the Liapounov functional (Section 15.10) for the criterion of stability (Malkin,1952;Bellman, 1953；Hurewicz,1958；Hahn，1959；Cesari,1959；La Salle and Lefschetz，1961；Cetaev，1961；Hale，1963；Krasovskii，1963; Zubov,1964； Coppel,1965； Shtokalo,1961; Liu and Leake,1967; Pliss,1966； Roseau, 1966；Bhatia and Szego,1970).

Let $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { n }$ be $_ n$ independent variables defining the position of a dynamical system,and ${ \dot { q } } _ { 1 } , \dots , { \dot { q } } _ { n }$ be their time derivatives. Suppose that a particular solution $q _ { i } = f _ { i } ( t )$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ is obtained to which corresponds the determinate motion of the dynamical system called the nondisturbed motion. Let $\boldsymbol { q } _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dot { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ be the values of $q _ { i } , \dot { q } _ { i }$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ，and put $q _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = f _ { i } ( t _ { 0 } ) + \varepsilon _ { i } , \dot { q } _ { i } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } = f _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( t _ { 0 } ) + \varepsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime } ,$ where $\varepsilon _ { i } , \varepsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ are real constants,called the perturbations,defining a disturbed motion. Let $Q _ { j } \ ( j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ be $_ n$ given real and continuous functions of $q _ { i } , \dot { q } _ { i }$ which reduce to $F _ { f }$ for the nondisturbed motion.The $Q _ { j }$ are functions of $t , \varepsilon _ { i } , \varepsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ，while the $Q _ { j } - F _ { j }$ reduce to zero for $\varepsilon _ { i } = \varepsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime } = 0$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ . The question is: Can we assign sufficiently small upper bounds to $Q _ { j } - F _ { j } ?$ It depends on the character of the nondisturbed motion and the choice of $Q _ { j }$ and $t _ { 0 }$

Let $L _ { 1 } , \ldots , L _ { n }$ be given positive numbers. Suppose that for all values of these numbers,however small,we can choose positive numbers $E _ { 1 } , \ldots , E _ { n } , E _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , E _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ such that from the inequalities $| \varepsilon _ { i } | < E _ { i }$ and $| \varepsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime } | < E _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \right)$ it follows that $| Q _ { s } - F _ { s } | < L _ { s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ for all values of $t , \ t \ > t _ { 0 }$ . Then the nondisturbed motion is said to be stable with respect to $Q _ { 1 } , \ldots , Q _ { n }$ ; in the contrary case,it is said to be unstable with respect to $Q _ { 1 } , \ldots , Q _ { n }$ ，according to Liapounov (1907,1949).

If the bounds $E _ { i } , E _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ can be found when the perturbations are restricted to satisfy the relations of the form $f = 0$ or $f \geq 0$ ，where $f$ is a function of $\varepsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \varepsilon _ { n } , \varepsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \varepsilon _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ ， vanishing with these constants, then the unstable motion is said to be conditionally stable.

Write

$$
Q _ { s } - F _ { s } = x _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ;
$$

then we obtain the equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = X _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $X _ { s }$ are known functions of $t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ，vanishing with $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ . Assume that the $X _ { s }$ are given for real and complex values of $x _ { s }$ with very small moduli,and that the $X _ { s }$ can be expanded in positive integral powers of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ in series absolutely convergent for all values of $x _ { s }$ satisfying $| x _ { s } | \leq A _ { s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ，where $\pmb { A } _ { s }$ are nonzero constants or nonvanishing functions of $t$ ,for all values of $t _ { i }$ ， $t > t _ { 0 }$ .Then the $X _ { s }$ are holomorphic functions of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ and can be put in the form

$$
X _ { s } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } x _ { n } + \sum P _ { s } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \dots , m _ { n } ) } x _ { 1 } ^ { m _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot x _ { n } ^ { m _ { n } } ,
$$

where the sum is to extend over all nonnegative integers $m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ such that $m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + \cdots + m _ { n } > 1$ .The coefficients $p _ { s i }$ and $P _ { s } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) }$ are functions of $t$ that should remain finite and real for all values of $t , t > t _ { 0 }$ ，according to the assumption.Assume that the coeffcients are continuous,and also that we have

$$
| P _ { s } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) } | < { \frac { M _ { s } } { A _ { 1 } ^ { m _ { 1 } } \ldots \cdot A _ { n } ^ { m _ { n } } } } ,
$$

where $M _ { s }$ denote the upper bounds of the moduli of the aggregate of terms of dimension higher than the frst for all complex values of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ whose moduli are $A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { n }$ ，respectively.

By supposing that the initial values of the variables $_ x$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ are sufficiently small we refer to successive approximations, and expand in the form

$$
x _ { s } = x _ { s } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } + x _ { s } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 2 ) } + \cdots \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

according to the orders of approximation, i.e., $\pmb { x } _ { s } ^ { ( m ) }$ and the derivatives with respect to $t$ are of the mth order. Substitute these expansions in (1) and equate the terms of the same order, then we get

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { s } ^ { ( 1 ) } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } + p _ { s 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \cdots + p _ { s n } x _ { n } ^ { ( 1 ) } } } \\ { { \qquad \quad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { s } ^ { ( m ) } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } ^ { ( m ) } + p _ { s 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { ( m ) } + \cdots + p _ { s n } x _ { n } ^ { ( m ) } + R _ { s } ^ { ( m ) } } } \\ { { \qquad \quad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

where the $R _ { s } ^ { ( m ) }$ are rational entire functions of $x _ { s } ^ { ( \mu ) }$ ， $\mu < m$ ，containing the sum of products of $P _ { s } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) }$ multiplied by positive integers.

With arbitrary constants $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ the linear equations (4) are solved by

$$
x _ { s } ^ { ( 1 ) } = a _ { 1 } x _ { s 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { n } x _ { s n } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Then the equations (5) can be solved successively for $m = 2 , 3 , \ldots$ At each stage a new set of constants of integration appears,which

should be determined by the condition that $x _ { s } ^ { ( m ) } = 0$ ， $m > 1$ ，for $t = t _ { 0 }$ .Put

$$
\Delta = { \left| \begin{array} { l l l l } { x _ { 1 1 } } & { x _ { 2 1 } } & { } & { x _ { n 1 } } \\ { \dots } & { } & { } & { } \\ { x _ { 1 n } } & { x _ { 2 n } } & { \dots } & { x _ { n n } } \end{array} \right| } ;
$$

then $\Delta$ as a function of $t$ cannot vanish for any value of $t$ ,because we have

$$
\Delta = C \exp \bigg \{ \int \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } p _ { s s } d t \bigg \} ,
$$

or, if we denote the minor of this determinant corresponding to $x _ { i j }$ by $\Delta _ { i j }$

$$
x _ { s } ^ { ( n ) } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { s j } \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } { \frac { \Delta _ { i j } } { \Delta } } R _ { i } ^ { ( m ) } d t \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

which remains determinate and continuous for all values of $t$ The question is the convergence of (3).

We take for $A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { n } , M _ { 1 } , \ldots , M _ { n }$ functions of $\pmb { t }$ such that for each $\boldsymbol { A } _ { s }$ there exists a nonzero lower bound and for each $M _ { s }$ an upper bound, for $t _ { 0 } < t < T$ .Then it can be shown that the series arranged in powers of $a _ { s }$ are absolutely convergent, however great $_ { T }$ is,if the $\left| a _ { s } \right|$ are less than a certain bound depending on $_ T$ . In fact, let

$$
\vert x _ { i i } - 1 \vert , \qquad \vert x _ { i j } ( i \neq j ) \vert < K ,
$$

$$
\bigg | \frac { \Delta _ { i i } } { \Delta } - 1 \bigg | , \qquad \bigg | \frac { \Delta _ { i j } } { \Delta } ( i \neq j ) \bigg | < K ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and

$$
x _ { i i } = 1 , \qquad x _ { i j } = 0 \qquad ( i \ne j ; i , j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Denote by $[ u ]$ the result of this substitution in an entire function $\pmb { u }$ of $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ ； then we have

$$
[ x _ { s } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } ] < ( 1 + n K ) a , \qquad | a _ { 1 } | , | a _ { 2 } | , \dotsc , | a _ { n } | < a ,
$$

$$
[ x _ { s } ^ { { ( m ) } } ] < \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { T } [ R _ { s } ^ { { ( m ) } } ] d t + ( K + K ^ { \prime } + n K K ^ { \prime } ) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { T } [ R _ { i } ^ { { ( m ) } } ] d t .
$$

Let

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { [ x _ { 1 } ^ { ( \mu ) } ] , [ x _ { 2 } ^ { ( \mu ) } ] , \dots , [ x _ { n } ^ { ( \mu ) } ] \le x ^ { ( \mu ) } , } \\ & { [ R _ { 1 } ^ { ( m ) } ] , [ R _ { 2 } ^ { ( m ) } ] , \dots , [ R _ { n } ^ { ( m ) } ] \le R ^ { ( m ) } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
| P _ { i } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) } | \leq P ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ;
$$

then

$$
[ x _ { s } ^ { ( m ) } ] < ( 1 + n K ) ( 1 + n K ^ { \prime } ) ( T - t _ { 0 } ) R ^ { ( m ) } .
$$

Hence we can take

$$
\begin{array} { l } { x ^ { ( 1 ) } = ( 1 + n K ) a , } \\ { x ^ { ( m ) } = ( 1 + n K ) ( 1 + n K ^ { \prime } ) ( T - t _ { 0 } ) R ^ { ( m ) } \qquad ( m = 2 , 3 , \ldots ) , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
P ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) } = { \frac { M } { A ^ { m _ { 1 } + \cdots + m _ { n } } } } ,
$$

where $M$ is an upper bound of all $M _ { s }$ and $\pmb { A }$ is a lower bound of all $\pmb { A } _ { s }$ in the $\scriptstyle { \mathbf { } } t \mathbf { } \cdot { \mathbf { } }$ -interval we consider.Replacing $M _ { s }$ and $A _ { s } ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ by these bounds the aggregates of terms of orders higher than the first in $X _ { s }$ become identical to the corresponding terms of the expansion of the majorant series

$$
M \biggl [ \frac { 1 } { \left( 1 - \frac { x _ { 1 } } { A } \right) \left( 1 - \frac { x _ { 2 } } { A } \right) \cdot \cdot \cdot \left( 1 - \frac { x _ { n } } { A } \right) } - 1 - \frac { x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + x _ { n } } { A } \biggr ] .
$$

Consequently，with such a choice of $P ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) }$ the quantity $R ^ { ( m ) }$ represents the aggregate of terms of the mth order with respect to the indices of $\pmb { x } ^ { ( s ) }$ in the expansion of

$$
M \biggl [ \biggl ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { A } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { ( s ) } \biggr ) ^ { - n } - 1 - \frac { n } { A } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { ( s ) } \biggr ] .
$$

Hence, if we put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x = ( 1 + n K ) a + A h \bigg [ \bigg ( 1 - \frac { x } { A } \bigg ) ^ { - n } - 1 - n \frac { x } { A } \bigg ] , } } \\ { { \displaystyle h = ( 1 + n K ) ( 1 + n K ^ { \prime } ) M ( T - t _ { 0 } ) / A , } } \end{array}
$$

then the series

$$
x ^ { ( 1 ) } + x ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

represents the expansion in positive integral powers of $^ { a }$ of the root $x$ of this equation, which vanishes with $^ { a }$ .Thus the series is convergent if

$$
a \leq g = \frac { A } { 1 + n K } \left\{ 1 - ( n + 1 ) h \bigg [ \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { n h } + 1 \bigg ) ^ { n / ( n + 1 ) } - 1 \bigg ] \right\} ,
$$

which is the smallest modulus of $^ { a }$ for which (9) has a multiple root. Since this series is convergent, the series (3) is absolutely convergent, provided that $\lvert a _ { s } \rvert \leqq g$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$

# 16.2CHARACTERISTIC NUMBERS

A function which is limited in absolute value for $t > t _ { 0 }$ by an upper bound is called a limited function (otherwise an unlimited function) after Liapounov (1907, 1949).A limited function that tends to zero as $t$ increases indefinitely is called a vanishing function.If $x$ is a limited function，then $x e ^ { - \lambda t }$ is a vanishing function，whatever the positive constant $\lambda$ is.If $x$ is not a vanishing function, then $x e ^ { \lambda t }$ is an unlimited function whatever the positive constant  is. Suppose that $z = x e ^ { \lambda t }$ for $\lambda = \lambda _ { 1 }$ is a vanishing functicn and for $\lambda = \lambda ^ { \prime }$ an unlimited one； then we can find a real number $\lambda _ { 0 }$ such that $z$ for $\lambda = \lambda _ { 0 } + \varepsilon$ is unlimited or vanishing, according $\mathtt { a s } \mathtt { \pmb \varepsilon }$ is positive or negative, however small $| \varepsilon |$ is. Liapounov called such a number $\lambda _ { 0 }$ a characteristic number. For example,the characteristic numbers of

$$
t ^ { m } , \ \exp \ \left\{ t \cos \frac { 1 } { t } \right\} , \ \exp \ \left\{ - t \cos \frac { 1 } { t } \right\} ,
$$

$\exp { \{ \pm t \sin t \} } , \exp { \{ - t \exp \left( \sin t \right) \} } , t ^ { t } .$ and $t ^ { - t }$ are respectively $0 , - 1 , + 1 , - 1 , + 1 / e , - \infty$ ,and $+ \infty$ . If $f ( t )$ is real and  is a real constant, such that $| \lambda - f ( t ) |$ can be made as small as. we please by an arbitrary choice of $t$ ，however large,and if for a positive constant $\varepsilon$ ， however small, we can find a bound $_ { T }$ such that $\lambda - f ( t ) < \varepsilon$ for $t > T$ ，then $\lambda$ is the characteristic number of exp $\{ - t f ( t ) \}$

The characteristic number of the sum of two functions is equal to the smaller characteristic number of each function if the two numbers. are different,and is not smaller than those numbers if the two are equal. The characteristic number of the product of two functions is. not less than the sum of the characteristic numbers of each function. In order that the characteristic numbers of $x$ and $1 / x$ be equal to zero for a function $x = \exp \left\{ - t ( f + \sqrt { - 1 } \phi ) \right\}$ ，where $f$ and $\phi$ are real functions of $t$ ,it is necessary and sufficient that $f$ should have a limit when $t$ increases indefinitely.If the sum of the characteristic numbers. of $x$ and ${ 1 / x }$ is equal to zero, then the characteristic numbers of the: product of $x$ and an arbitrary function is equal to the sum of the characteristic numbers of the latter two functions.The characteristic number of an integral is not less than the characteristic number of the. integrand.

Consider the system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } x _ { n } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :Any solution of (1O) other than the trivial solution $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ has finite characteristic numbers.

In fact, the equations (1O), by writing

$$
z _ { s } = x _ { s } e ^ { \lambda t } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

with real constant $\lambda$ ， are transformed to

$$
\frac { d z _ { s } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } z _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( p _ { s s } + \lambda ) z _ { s } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } z _ { n }
$$

whence we obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } z _ { s } ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } ( p _ { s s } + \lambda ) z _ { s } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { n } ( p _ { s \sigma } + p _ { \sigma s } ) z _ { s } z _ { \sigma } .
$$

The right-hand member is a quadratic form of $z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ with coefficients depending on $\lambda$ and $t$ .Since the $p _ { s \sigma }$ are limited,we can find the values of $\lambda$ such that this quadratic form should be positive-definite for all values of $t$ ，remaining always larger than

$$
\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } N ( z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + z _ { n } ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

where $N$ is an arbitrary positive number. Similarly we can find the values of $\lambda$ such that for the same value of $t$ this quadratic form is negative,always remaining larger in absolute value than the function (11a).For each value of λ of the first set we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum z _ { s } ^ { 2 } > N \sum z _ { s } ^ { 2 } , ~ \sum z _ { s } ^ { 2 } > C e ^ { \kappa t }
$$

with a positive constant $C$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ . For each value of $\gimel$ of the second set we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum z _ { s } ^ { 2 } < - N \sum z _ { s } ^ { 2 } , \sum z _ { s } ^ { 2 } < C e ^ { - N t } .
$$

Hence $\sum z _ { s } ^ { 2 }$ increases indefinitely with $t$ for the first set but tends to zero for the second set as $t \to + \infty$ . Thus we see that we can always find such values of $\lambda$ that there are some which are unlimited among the functions (11),and that there are such values of $\lambda$ that all these functions are vanishing. Hence among all real solutions $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ other than the trivial solutions we can find the functions with finite characteristic numbers,but not any function with characteristic number $- \infty$ . Consequently the characteristic number of the group of functions $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ is always finite.

Theorem 2: The system of equations (1O) cannot admit more than $\pmb { n }$ solutions,other than the trivial solution $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = 0$ ，whose characteristic numbers are all distinct.

Suppose that we have $_ n$ independent solutions of (1O). Suppose that when making all possible linear combinations of these solutions there occurs one whose characteristic number is greater than the characteristic number of the group of the combined solutions. Then one of the solutions,whose characteristic number is equal to the characteristic number of the group,is replaced by such a linear combination.By repeating this operation,we arrive at a system of linear combinations each of whose characteristic numbers is equal to the characteristic number of the group of the solutions that compose the linear combination. Such a system is called normal.A system of $_ n$ solutions whose characteristic numbers are all distinct is a normal system.

Theorem 3: Let $x _ { 1 k } , x _ { 2 k } , \ldots , x _ { n k }$ $( k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ be a system of $_ n$ independent solutions,and let

$$
z _ { 1 k } , z _ { 2 k } , . . . , z _ { n k } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

be

$$
z _ { s k } = x _ { s , k } + \alpha _ { k , 1 } x _ { s , k + 1 } + \alpha _ { k , 2 } x _ { s , k + 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { k , n - k } x _ { s , n } ,
$$

where $\alpha _ { k , 1 } , \alpha _ { k , 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { k , n - k }$ are constants such that the characteristic number of the solutions $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ with

$$
x _ { s } = x _ { s , k } + \beta _ { 1 } x _ { s , k + 1 } + \beta _ { 2 } x _ { s , k + 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \beta _ { n - k } x _ { s , n } ,
$$

$\beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \ldots , \beta _ { n - k }$ being arbitrary constants，is not greater than the characteristic numbers of the solutions $z _ { 1 , k } , z _ { 2 , k } , \ldots , z _ { n , k }$ . Then the system of solutions (12) is normal.

In fact, if the system (12) were not normal, then it would be possible to find among the solutions a set of solutions with a common characteristic number $\gimel$ such that a linear combination with a characteristic number greater than  could be obtained.But by definition there exists no such solution.

Let $k$ be the number of distinct characteristic numbers $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { k }$ for (10). Let $n _ { s }$ be the number of solutions with characteristic number $\lambda _ { s }$ in an arbitrary system of $\pmb { n }$ independent solutions,where some of $\pmb { n } _ { s }$ may be zero, but $n _ { 1 } + n _ { 2 } + \cdots + n _ { k } = n$ . Suppose that $\lambda _ { 1 } < \lambda _ { 2 }$ $< \cdots < \lambda _ { k }$ and let $N _ { s }$ be the upper bound of the number of independent solutions with characteristic number $\lambda _ { s }$ ，then $N _ { 1 } > N _ { 2 } > \cdots$ $> N _ { k } , N _ { 1 } = n , n _ { s } + n _ { s + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + n _ { k } \leq N _ { s } ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , k )$ .Hence we obtain

Theorem 4: For any normal system of solutions we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { = n - N _ { 2 } , \qquad n _ { 2 } = N _ { 2 } - N _ { 3 } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { n _ { k - 1 } = N _ { k - 1 } - N _ { k } \qquad n _ { k } = N _ { k } . } } \end{array}
$$

In fact,each solution is a linear combination of solutions in the normal system.To derive a solution with characteristic number $\lambda _ { s }$ we must consider linear combinations of the solutions whose characteristic numbers are not less than $\lambda _ { s }$ . Hence the number of the independent solutions with characteristic number $\lambda _ { s }$ cannot be greater than $n _ { s } +$ $n _ { s + 1 } + \cdots + n _ { k }$ ，corresponding to a normal system. Thus $n _ { s } + n _ { s + 1 }$ $+ \cdot \cdot \cdot + n _ { k } = N _ { s }$

Theorem 5: The sum $S = n _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } + n _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + n _ { k } \lambda _ { k }$ of the characteristic numbers of all solutions of a system of $_ n$ independent solutions attains its upper bound for a normal system.

Theorem 6: Each system of $_ n$ independent solutions for which the sum of the characteristic numbers of all solutions belonging to the system attains its upper bound is a normal system.

Theorem 7: The sum of the characteris:ic numbers of independent solutions of the system (1O) is not greater than the characteristic number of the function

$$
\exp \left\{ \int \sum p _ { s s } d t \right\} .
$$

In fact, the determinant $\Delta$ formed of $_ n$ arbitrary independent solutions satisfies

$$
\exp \left\{ \int \sum p _ { s s } d t \right\} = C \Delta ,
$$

with a constant $C$ . The characteristic number of $\Delta$ has been found to be not less than $n _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } + \dots + n _ { k } \lambda _ { k }$

Accordingly a system of $_ n$ independent solutions for which the sum of the characteristic numbers of all solutions belonging to the system is equal to thecharacteristic number of exp $\left\{ \int \sum p _ { s s } d t \right\}$ is a normal system.

Let $s$ be the sum of the characteristic numbers of the solutions of a normal system, and $\mu$ the characteristc number of exp $\left\{ - \int \sum p _ { s s } d t \right\}$ If $S + \mu = 0$ ，then the system of linear differential equations is said to be regular,otherwise irregular.

Theorem 8: In order that the system

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = p _ { 1 1 } x _ { 1 } , \qquad \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = p _ { 2 1 } x _ { 1 } + p _ { 2 2 } x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
\frac { d x _ { n } } { d t } = p _ { n 1 } x _ { 1 } + p _ { n 2 } x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { n n } x _ { n }
$$

shall be regular it is necessary and sufficient that the sum of the characteristic numbers of exp $\left\{ \int p _ { s s } d t \right\}$ and exp $\left\{ - \int p _ { s s } d t \right\}$ be equal to zero for all values of $\pmb { s }$ ； in other words,each of the functions

$$
\frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } p _ { s s } d t \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \dots , n )
$$

must tend to a determinate limit for $t \to \infty$

In fact, the system (13) admits $_ n$ independent solutions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x _ { 1 } = \exp \left\{ \int p _ { 1 1 } d t \right\} , } } \\ { { \displaystyle x _ { s } = \exp \left\{ \int p _ { s s } d t \right\} \cdot \int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } p _ { i s } x _ { i } \exp \left\{ - \int p _ { s s } d t \right\} \cdot d t } } \end{array}
$$

$$
S _ { n } \colon \quad x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdots = x _ { n - 1 } = 0 , \qquad x _ { n } = \exp \left\{ \int { p _ { n n } d t } \right\} .
$$

Suppose for fixing the idea that all integrals in the exponents are zero for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ,and that $x _ { k + 1 } , x _ { k + 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ in ${ \pmb S } _ { \pmb { k } }$ reduce to given constants $\alpha _ { k , 1 } , \alpha _ { k , 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { k , n - k }$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ； then, if $( x _ { 1 , k } , x _ { 2 , k } , \ldots , x _ { n , k } )$ is the kth

solution with the condition that all $\alpha$ are zero, then the solution becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { s } = x _ { s , k } + \alpha _ { k , 1 } x _ { s , k + 1 } + \alpha _ { k , 2 } x _ { s , k + 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { k , n - k } x _ { s , n } } \\ { ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) } \end{array}
$$

for an arbitrary $_ { \alpha }$ .By Theorem 3 this system of solutions is normal for a suitable choice of $_ { \alpha }$ . Denote the characteristic numbers by $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { n } ;$ andthecharacteristicnumbersof exp $\left\{ \int p _ { s s } d t \right\}$ exp $\left\{ - \int p _ { s s } d t \right\} , \exp \left\{ \int \sum _ { s } p _ { s s } d t \right\}$ , and exp $\left\{ - \int \sum _ { s } p _ { s s } d t \right\}$ respectively by $\lambda _ { s } , \lambda _ { s } ^ { \prime } , S$ and $S ^ { \prime }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ； then we have $\mu _ { s } \leqslant \lambda _ { s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ . If the system (13) is regular, then we have $\textstyle \sum \mu _ { s } = S$ The sum $\sum \lambda _ { s }$ cannot be greater than $s$ ， contrary to what we have seen, so that we should have $\sum { \lambda _ { s } } = S$ Similarly we have $S + S ^ { \prime } = 0$ Hence, because of the properties of characteristic numbers,we see that the characteristic number of exp $\left\{ \int \sum p _ { s s } d t \right\} \cdot \exp \ \left\{ - \int p _ { k k } d t \right\}$ is equal to $S + \lambda _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ .Thus $S + \lambda _ { k } ^ { \prime } \ge \sum \lambda _ { s } - \lambda _ { k }$ ,or $\lambda _ { k } + \lambda _ { k } ^ { \prime } \ge 0$ ，or $\lambda _ { k } +$ $\lambda _ { k } ^ { \prime } = 0$ since this sum cannot be positive.This is the necessary condition for the theorem.

In order to prove that this condition is also sufficient, we suppose that the characteristic number of the integrand of

$$
\int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } p _ { s i } x _ { i } \exp \left\{ - \int p _ { s s } d t \right\} \cdot d t
$$

is positive and that any integral of this form tends to zero for $t \to \infty$ Since any integral in the system of solutions possesses a characteristic number not less than the characteristic number of the integrand, the function $x _ { k }$ possesses the characteristic number equal to $\lambda _ { k }$ in the kth solution. If $\lambda _ { s } + \lambda _ { s } ^ { \prime } = 0 ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ and if $\lambda _ { k }$ is the characteristic number of $x _ { k }$ ， then the characteristic numbers of all the remaining functions constituting this solution are not less than $\lambda _ { k }$ . Hence $\lambda _ { k }$ is the characteristic number of the kth solution.

In general, we have

$$
\sum { \lambda _ { s } } \le S \le - S ^ { \prime } \le - \sum { \lambda _ { s } ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Thus

$$
\sum 2 _ { s } + \sum 2 _ { s } ^ { \prime } = 0 .
$$

Accordingly

$$
\sum { \lambda _ { s } } + S ^ { \prime } = 0 .
$$

Therefore the system (13) is regular and normal.

Suppose that the system (1O) is transformed by a linear substitution

$$
z _ { s } = q _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + q _ { s n } x _ { n } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $q _ { s \sigma }$ are continuous and bounded functions of $t$ ， together with their first-order derivatives with respect to $t$ ，and that the reciprocal of the determinant formed of the coeffcients is bounded. Suppose also that the system admits $n _ { 1 }$ characteristic numbers $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ n _ { 2 }$ characteristic numbers $\lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots , n _ { k }$ characteristic numbers $\lambda _ { k }$ . The group of characteristic numbers of a transformed system of equations is identical with the group of characteristic numbers of the original system.Hence the characteristic numbers are invariant with respect to linear transformations.The regularity of the system is conserved by such transformations.

If a system of equations can be transformed to a system with constant coefficients by suitable substitution， then the system is said to be reducible. We can show that only a regular system can be reducible.A system of equations with periodic coefficients all of a common real periodin $t$ isreducible.Let $x _ { 1 s } , \ x _ { 2 s } , \ldots , \ x _ { n s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ be a normal system in which the jth solution has characteristic numbers $\lambda _ { j } ( j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ . Let $\Delta$ be the determinant formed of $x _ { i j }$ ，and suppose that

$$
\exp \left\{ - \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { s } t \right\} \bigwedge \quad \mathrm { ~ a n d ~ } \quad \quad x _ { i j } \exp \left\{ \lambda _ { j } t \right\}  \\  \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

are bounded. Then the system is reducible.

In fact, if $\Delta _ { i j }$ is the minor of $\Delta$ with respect to $\boldsymbol { x } _ { i j }$ , then the condition is that $( \Delta _ { i j } / \Delta )$ exp $\{ - \lambda _ { j } t \} ( i , j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ is bounded.Then the first derivatives are bounded, because $\Delta _ { 1 j } / \Delta , \ldots , \Delta _ { n j } / \Delta ( j = 1 , 2 ,$ $\cdots , n )$ satisfy the system of adjoint linear differential equations (Section 3.7).Hence the substitution

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { z _ { s } = \displaystyle \frac { \Delta _ { 1 s } } { \Delta } \exp \left\{ - \lambda _ { s } t \right\} \cdot x _ { 1 } + \displaystyle \frac { \Delta _ { 2 s } } { \Delta } \exp \left\{ - \lambda _ { s } t \right\} \cdot x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \frac { \Delta _ { n s } } { \Delta } \exp \left\{ - \lambda _ { s } t \right\} \cdot x _ { n } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) } } \end{array}
$$

possesses all properties of the substitution in question,and the result of transformation is

$$
\frac { d z _ { s } } { d t } + \lambda _ { s } z _ { s } = 0 \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

with constant coefficients.

Suppose that the coefficients $p _ { s \sigma }$ and $P _ { s } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) }$ are real, continuous, and bounded functions of $t$ ， and that we can find positive constants $M$ and $\pmb { A }$ such that

Thus the series (21) is absolutely convergent if a positive number $\pmb q$ can be found such that $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { k }$ satisfy

$$
| q _ { s } | \equiv q \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Hence we obtain

Theorem 9: If we replace $\alpha _ { s }$ in (14） by the expressions in terms of $\pmb { q } _ { s }$ such as

$$
\alpha _ { s } \exp \left\{ - ( \lambda _ { s } - \varepsilon ) t \right\} = q _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k ) ,
$$

then the new series (22) arranged in ascending powers of $q _ { s }$ has the following property: for any positive value of $\varepsilon$ however small, we can find positive constants $Q ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { k } ) }$ such that we have

$$
| Q _ { s } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { k } ) } | < Q ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { k } ) } \qquad ( t > t _ { 0 } )
$$

and such that the series

$$
\sum { Q } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { k } ) } { q } _ { 1 } ^ { m _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \ q _ { k } ^ { m _ { k } }
$$

are convergent, provided that the moduli of $q _ { s }$ do not exceed a certain bound $q$ distinct from zero.

Corollary: A positive constant $_ { \alpha }$ exists,such that the series (14) is absolutely convergent for $t > t _ { 0 }$ and represents the continuous function of $t$ satisfying（1） for $| \alpha _ { s } | \le \alpha ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ·

Hence we arrive at the following two theorems.

Theorem 1O: If a system of differential equations in the first approximation is regular and if all its characteristic numbers are positive,then the nondisturbed motion is stable.

In such a case the disturbed motions tend asymptotically to the nondisturbed motion. Such a state is called asymptotically stable.

Theorem 11: If a system of differential equations of the first approximation is regular and if some of the characteristic numbers are positive, then the nondisturbed motion always presents conditional stability.If the number of the positive characteristic numbers is $k$ ， then a sufficient condition for stability is that the initial values $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 }$ ， $\cdot \cdot , a _ { n }$ of the unknown functions should satisfy $n - k$ equations of the form

$$
F _ { j } ( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n } ) = 0 \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n - k ) ,
$$

where the $F _ { j }$ are holomorphic functions of the $a _ { s }$ ，vanishing for $a _ { 1 } =$ $a _ { 2 } = \cdots = a _ { n } = 0$ . These equations are such that we can derive all the $a _ { s }$ as real holomorphic functions of $k$ real independent quantities (see Erugin,1966).

# 16.3LIAPOUNOV'S DIRECT METHOD

As the second direct method for examining the stability of the motion represented by differential equations,Liapounov introduced the following notion.

Consider a real function of real variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , $ $t$ with the condition that

$$
t \geq t _ { 0 } , \qquad | x _ { s } | \leq H \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $t _ { 0 }$ is a constant, however large,and $\pmb { H }$ is a constant, however small. When a function $V$ satisfying this condition (24） remains continuous and uniform,vanishing for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ ，and can have exclusively only one sign, then $V$ is said to be a function of fixed sign，or a positive or a negative function according to the sign. Otherwise it is a function of variable sign.If $V$ is independent of $t$ ， and $\pmb { H }$ can be as small as we please under the condition (24),and if $V = 0$ can only occur for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ ，then $V$ is called definite，or positive-definite or negative-definite according as $V$ is positive or negative.A function $V$ depending on $t$ is said to be definite if one can find a function $W$ independent of $t$ such that $V - W$ or $- V - W$ is positive-definite.Liapounov called a function $V$ ，for which $\pmb { H }$ can be chosen sufficiently small for an upper bound to exist for $\vert V \vert$ under the condition (24),a limited function.A limited function can be such that, a positive constant $\varepsilon$ being arbitrarily chosen, we can assign a positive constant $h$ sufficiently small so as to satisfy the condition $\vert V \vert \leq \varepsilon$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ， $| x _ { s } | \leq h$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ . Such a function is said to admit an infinitely small upper bound.If $V$ admits an infinitely small upper bound and if the variables satisfy $t \geq t _ { 0 } , | V | \geq l ,$ where $\iota$ is a positive constant, then we can conclude that there exists another positive constant $\lambda$ which cannot be smaller than the largest of the quantities $| x _ { 1 } | , \ldots , | x _ { n } |$

Together with the function $V$ we associate

$$
{ \dot { V } } = { \frac { d V } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } X _ { 1 } + { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } X _ { 2 } + \cdots + { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { n } } } X _ { n } + { \frac { \partial V } { \partial t } } ,
$$

which represents the total derivative of $V$ with respect to $t$ ,taken with the assumption that $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are functions of $t$ satisfying disturbed equations of the form (1).We suppose that $V$ and $\dot { V }$ ，as functions of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t .$ ，are continuous and uniform under the condition (24).

Extending Lagrange's theorem， which was elegantly proved by Lejeune Dirichlet (Routh，1877，1905；Hagihara，1970） on the stability of an equilibrium in the case when the force function exists, Liapounov proved

Theorem 1(Liapounov's First Theorem for Stability): If the differential equations for a disturbed motion are such that it is possible to find a definite function $V$ ，whose derivative $\dot { V }$ is a function of fixed sign with the sign opposite to that of $V$ ,or reduces identically to zero, then the nondisturbed motion is stable.

Suppose that $V$ is_ positive-definite and that $\dot { V }$ is negative-definite or identically zero ； then we can find $t _ { 0 }$ and $\pmb { H }$ such that

$$
{ \dot { V } } \leq 0 , \qquad V \geq W
$$

for all values of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ;$ ，satisfying (24)，where $W$ is a positive function of $x _ { s }$ ,independent of $t$ and vanishing only for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n }$ $= 0$ under the condition (24). Denoting the value of $V$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ by $V _ { 0 }$ we can see that if (24) is satisfied in the interval from $t _ { 0 }$ to $t$ ，then $x _ { s }$ satisfy $W \leq V _ { 0 }$ in the same interval, where $V _ { 0 }$ can be made as small as we please by choosing the values $\xi _ { s }$ of $x _ { s }$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ sufficiently small in absolute value. Let $x$ be the largest quantity among $| x _ { 1 } | , \ldots ,$ $\vert x _ { n } \vert$ and $\varepsilon$ be a positive constant as small as we please. Consider the values of $x _ { s }$ satisfying $x = \varepsilon$ . Let $\iota$ be the lower bound of $W$ under this condition.Then $\boldsymbol { l }$ should be different from zero but should be positive, because $W$ can become neither negative nor zero under the condition $x = \varepsilon$ ，and $\iota$ should be necessarily one of the values the function can take under this condition.Hence we can make $V _ { 0 }$ smaller than l.Furthermore,we can find a positive constant λ such that $V _ { 0 } < l$ is satisfied whenever the values $\xi _ { s }$ satisfy $| \xi _ { s } | \le \lambda ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ Suppose that $\xi _ { s }$ are chosen so as to satisfy this last condition. Since λ is less than $\varepsilon$ ， the functions $x _ { s }$ satisfy $| x _ { s } | < \varepsilon ( s = 1 , 2 , \dots , n )$ for all values of $t$ in the neighborhood of $t _ { 0 }$ .But these functions, being con-tinuous in $t$ ， can cease to satisfy these inequalities only when they attain the values satisfying $x = \varepsilon$ ．This is incompatible with the inequality $W \leqeq V _ { 0 } ,$ since $V _ { \mathfrak { o } } < l$ .Hence we conclude that $x _ { s }$ should satisfy $| x _ { s } | < \varepsilon$ for all values of $t > t _ { 0 }$ whatever the $\xi _ { s }$ that satisfy $| \xi _ { s } | \leq \lambda$ may be.

If the integrals $U _ { 1 } , \dots , U _ { m }$ ， vanishing for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ ，are known for the differential equations (1) of the disturbed motion，and if $V$ satisfies the condition (25) only for the values of the variables satisfying $U _ { 1 } = U _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = U _ { m } = 0$ ，then the nondisturbed motion is stable at least for perturbations satisfying these latter conditions. When $V$ is itself an integral and $V , U _ { 1 } , \dots , U _ { m }$ do not contain $t$ explicitly, then the proposition reduces to Routh's (1884).

We can prove

Theorem 2: Let $V$ be a function of $x _ { s }$ and $t$ with the properties: (i) $V$ admits an infinitely small upper bound; (ii) $\dot { V }$ is a definite function ； and (iii) $V$ can take the sign of $\dot { V }$ for the values of $t$ beyond a certain bound, however small $x _ { s }$ may be in absolute value. If such 'a function $V$ can be formed by means of the differential equations of the disturbed condition, then the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

In fact, suppose that $\dot { V }$ is positive-definite. Then we can assign constants $_ { T }$ and $\pmb { H }$ such that for all values of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ;$ in the interval (24) we have ${ \dot { V } } \geq W , | V | < L$ ，where $\pmb { L }$ is a positive constant and $W$ is a function of $x _ { s }$ ， independent of $t$ which is positive and becomes zero only for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ Since $V - V _ { \circ } = \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \dot { V } \ d t ,$ we obtain $V \geq V _ { 0 }$ for all values of $t > t _ { 0 }$ such that (24) is satisfied throughout the interval $t _ { 0 }$ to $t$ By choosing $\xi _ { s }$ such that $| \xi _ { s } | < \varepsilon$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ，where $\varepsilon$ is as small as we please，we can suppose $t _ { 0 }$ sufficiently large so that $V _ { \mathfrak { d } } > 0$ ，by the property (iii)． Then by the property (i) we can find a positive constant $\gimel$ such that $\lambda$ is smaller than the value which the largest $x$ among $| x _ { s } |$ can take under the condition $V \geq V _ { 0 } , t > t _ { 0 }$ .Let $\imath$ be a positive number smaller than all possible values of $W$ under the condition $\lambda \leq x \leq H$ ，then we have $V > V _ { 0 } + l ( t - t _ { 0 } )$ ，which is valid for $t > t _ { 0 }$ provided that the condition (24） remains to hold in the interval $t _ { 0 }$ to $t$ .Under the same conditions $V$ remains less than $\cal L$ in absolute value.But this occurs simultaneously with $V > V _ { 0 } + l \left( t - t _ { 0 } \right)$ only for $t > \tau$ ， $\tau = t _ { 0 } +$ $( L - V _ { \mathrm { 0 } } ) / l$ .Hence in the interval $t _ { 0 }$ to $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ there exists a value of $t$ beyond which at least one of the conditions in (24） ceases to be satisfied. Thus, however small $\varepsilon$ may be,provided that it does not exceed $| \xi _ { s } |$ ，we can choose $| \xi _ { s } |$ so that during the subsequent motion at least one of $| x _ { s } |$ attains its fixed bound $\pmb { H }$ .This shows the instability of the nondisturbed motion.

Consider, for example, the system of equations for disturbed motion

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $V$ is a holomorphic function of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ， independent of $t _ { i }$ ， beginning with second-degree terms. Then

$$
{ \frac { d V } { d t } } = \left( { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \ \left( { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \ \cdot \cdot \cdot + \ \left( { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { n } } } \right) ^ { 2 } .
$$

If $V$ is negative-definite, then the nondisturbed motion is stable.If $V$ is not such a function,then the motion is unstable unless, for real values of $x$ ，

$$
{ \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } = \cdots = { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { n } } } = 0 .
$$

This exceptional case needs special study ; it only occurs if the Hessian of $V$ vanishes for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$

As the next example,consider

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { s } ^ { \prime } } - \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { s } } = 0 , \qquad \frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = x _ { s } ^ { \prime } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k ) ,
$$

$$
F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } v _ { i j } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } x _ { j } ^ { \prime } + U , \qquad v _ { i j } = v _ { j i } ,
$$

where $U$ is a holomorphic function of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { k }$ ，independent of $t$ and vanishing for $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { k } = 0$ Put $U = U _ { m } + U _ { m + 1 }$ $+ \cdot \cdot \cdot$ ，the subscripts indicating the degree of each term.Let

$$
V = x _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } } + x _ { 2 } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } } + \cdots + x _ { k } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { k } ^ { \prime } } } ,
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d V } { d t } } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { s } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { s } } } + \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { s } ^ { \prime } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { s } ^ { \prime } } } } } \\ { { = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \left( v _ { i j } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { s } { \frac { \partial v _ { i j } } { \partial x _ { s } } } \right) x _ { i } ^ { \prime } x _ { j } ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \qquad + m U _ { m } + ( m + 1 ) U _ { m + 1 } + \cdots . . . } } \end{array}
$$

If $U _ { m }$ is a positive-definite function, then $d V / d t$ is positive-definite in the variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } , x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , x _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ . The conditions of Theorem 2 are satisfed.Hence the non-disturbed motion is unstable.

Theorem 3 (Liapounov's Second Theorem on Stability): If for a differential equation of a disturbed motion it is possible to find a function $V ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ of fixed sign or with an infinitesimal upper bound, whose total derivative $\dot { V }$ is also of fixed sign opposite to that of $V$ ， then the nondisturbed motion is asymptotically stable.

In fact,by supposing $V$ positive-definite and $\dot { V }$ negative-definite, let ε be an arbitrary positive number smaller than the constant $\pmb { H }$ .By Theorem 2 the motion is stable. Therefore there exists a positive number $\lambda ( \varepsilon )$ such that for each solution of the equation (1） for which $| \xi _ { s } | \leq \lambda$ we have $| x _ { s } ( t ) | < \varepsilon$ for all $t > T$ . Since the solution $x _ { s } ( t )$ cannot become zero simultaneously, $\dot { V }$ remains negative at all time $t > t _ { 0 }$ and does not become zero for any value of $t$ Hence $V$ is monotonically decreasing and tends to a limit $_ { \pmb { \alpha } }$ for $t \to \infty$ ，so that $V > \alpha$

Now suppose that $\alpha > 0$ . Since $V$ is continuous,

$$
x ( t ) = \operatorname* { m a x } \left\{ | x _ { 1 } ( t ) | , \ldots , | x _ { n } ( t ) | \right\}
$$

is larger than a certain positive number.

Since $\dot { V } < 0$ ， this would give ${ \dot { V } } \leq - b$ where $^ { b }$ is positive. Then we should have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { V [ t , x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t ) ] = V [ t _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } ( t _ { 0 } ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t _ { 0 } ) ] + \displaystyle \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \dot { V } d t } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \end{array}
$$

which is impossible,because the right-hand member would become negative for large values of $t$ Hence $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } V [ t , x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t ) ] = 0 . } \end{array}$ Consequently $V > \alpha = 0$

Since the equality signs in $V \geq 0 , \dot { V } \leq 0$ are possible only for $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ ，we conclude that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } x _ { s } ( t ) = 0 \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Hence the nondisturbed motion is asymptotically stable.

Theorem $^ { 4 }$ (Liapounov's First Theorem on Instability): If for the differential equations of disturbed motion it is possible to find a function $V ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ such that $\dot { V }$ is a function of fixed sign and $V$ itself is not of fixed sign opposite to that of $\dot { V }$ ,then the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

In fact, assuming that $\pmb { H }$ is small in the interval (24)，and that $\dot { V } > 0$ ,we shall show that however small λ may be it is possible to find a system of initial values in the interval $| \xi _ { s } | \le \lambda$ such that the solutions $x _ { s } ( t )$ with this initial condition leave the interval (24) at a certain $t$

We choose $\xi _ { s }$ so as to satisfy $| \xi _ { s } | \le \lambda$ and also $V ( T , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } ) > 0$ ， since $V$ is not negative-definite.If the disturbed motion with this initial condition were to remain always in the interval (24), then we should have $V [ t , x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t ) ] > V ( T , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } )$ since we assumed $\dot { V } > 0$ From this we should obtain

$$
x ( t ) = \operatorname* { m a x } \left\{ | x _ { 1 } ( t ) | , \ldots , | x _ { n } ( t ) | \right\} \geq \lambda , \qquad \lambda > 0 .
$$

We know that $V$ is continuous and becomes zero for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n }$ $= 0$ . But we should also have ${ \dot { V } } \geq l$ with a sufficiently small positive number $\iota$

Hence for such a solution we should have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { V [ t , x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t ) ] = V [ t _ { 0 } , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } ] + \displaystyle \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \dot { V } d t } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \end{array}
$$

which shows that $V$ would increase without limit. This contradicts the condition that $x _ { s } ( s )$ remain in (24). Hence the motion leaves from the interval (24) for a certain time $t > t _ { 0 }$

The second theorem on instability is given here in the form in which Liapounov stated it:

Theorem 5 (Liapounov's Second Theorem on Instability): Let $V$ be a function of $x _ { s }$ and $t$ with the following properties:(i) $V$ isa limited function ; (ii) $\dot { V }$ is of the form

$$
\dot { V } = \lambda V + W ,
$$

where $\lambda$ is a positive constant and $W$ is a function of fixed sign ； and (ii) $V$ may take the sign of $W$ for all values of $t$ beyond a certain bound, however smal the $x _ { s }$ may be in absolute value.If such a function can be formed by means of the differential equations of a disturbed motion, then the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

In fact, supposing that $W$ is a positive function，we can choose $t _ { 0 }$ and $\pmb { H }$ such that $| V | < L$ ， $W \geq 0$ for the variables satisfying (24), where $L > 0$ .Furthermore,we can suppose $t _ { 0 }$ to be sufficiently large to make the value $V _ { 0 }$ of $V$ at $t = t _ { 0 }$ positive by suitable choice of $\xi _ { s }$ ， the values of $x _ { s }$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ . We see from (26) that, for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，the function $V$ ，regarded as a function of $t$ through the equations of motion, satisfies

$$
\frac { d V } { d t } - \lambda V \geq 0
$$

for all values of $t$ for which (24) remains valid.If (24) does not cease to be satisfied from $t _ { 0 }$ to $t$ ,we have $V \geq V _ { 0 } \exp { \{ \lambda ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} }$ ,accordingly $L > V _ { 0 }$ exp $\{ \lambda ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \}$ . Since $V _ { \mathfrak { 0 } } > 0$ ， this inequality can be valid only for $t$ such that

$$
t < \tau = t _ { 0 } + \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \log \frac { L } { V _ { 0 } } .
$$

Hence, in the interval $t _ { 0 }$ to $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ ， the condition (24) cannot remain valid.   
Thus the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

Such a function $V$ is called Liapounov function (Hahn,1959;Cesari，1959；Hale，1963，1963a；La Salle and Lefschetz，1961;Krasovskii，1963；Zubov，1964；Malkin,1938,1942,1944,1952;Antosiewicz,1958 ；Bhatia and Szego,1970).

Construct a hypersurface $V ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) = c$ in the $_ n$ -dimensional Euclidean space.For $^ { c }$ sufficiently small, this hypersurface surrounds the origin if $V$ is positive-definite. For $c = 0$ we have $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n }$ $= 0$ ,the origin. As $^ c$ increases, the hypersurface encloses the hyper-surfaces with smaller values of $^ { c }$ .If $\dot { V } \leq 0$ ， then any integral curve of the differential equations cannot intersect $V = c$ from its inner side by starting from an immediate neighborhood of the origin,but can intersect from its outer side, in which case the integral curve approaches the origin as $t$ increases, and the motion is asymptotically stable.

When the diferential equations admit an integral $V ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) =$ constant, where $V$ is definite, then ${ \dot { V } } = 0$ and the function $V$ satisfies the properties of Theorem 1, and the motion is accordingly stable.

When the force function $U ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ has a maximum at $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ ，the equilibrium point， then the equations of motion are

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } } , { \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } } , y _ { i } = { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

$$
H ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { n } ) = T - U ,
$$

where the kinetic energy $T$ is a positive-definite quadratic form of $y _ { i }$ and $U$ is a positive-definite function of $x _ { i }$ ，accordingly $\pmb { H }$ is a positivedefinite function of $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ . The differential equations admit an integral $\pmb { H } =$ constant. This is Lagrange's theorem.

If $\boldsymbol { U }$ has a minimum $U = 0$ at $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ ，then we expand $\pmb { U }$

$$
U = U _ { m } + U _ { m + 1 } + \cdots \quad \quad ( m \geq 0 )
$$

in homogeneous-degree polynomials of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ . The function $U _ { m }$ should be positive-definite.The kinetic energy is positive-definite in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ . We have

$$
2 T = \sum _ { i , j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } + \sum _ { i , j = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i j } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) y _ { i } y _ { j } ,
$$

where the $\boldsymbol { A } _ { i j }$ become zero for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ . Consider the function $\begin{array} { r } { V = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { s } y _ { s } } \end{array}$ and form

$$
\frac { d V } { d t } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { s } \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { s } } - \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { s } \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { s } }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { = \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { s } \frac { \partial T } { \partial y _ { s } } - \sum _ { s , i , j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { s } \frac { \partial A _ { i j } } { \partial x _ { s } } y _ { i } y _ { j } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad + \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { s } \Big ( \frac { \partial U _ { m } } { \partial x _ { s } } + \frac { \partial U _ { m + 1 } } { \partial x _ { s } } + \cdots \Big ) , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d V } { d t } = \sum _ { i , j = 1 \atop i + \{ m U _ { m } + ( m + 1 ) U _ { m + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \} . } ^ { n } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } \Bigg \} } \\ { \displaystyle + \ \{ m U _ { m } + ( m + 1 ) U _ { m + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \} . } \end{array}
$$

This is positive-defnite, because the $\boldsymbol { A } _ { i j }$ become zero for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots =$ $x _ { n } = 0$ and small in the neighborhood of the origin. Hence $\dot { V }$ is positive-definite with respect to the $2 n$ variables $x _ { s }$ and $y _ { s }$ .On the other hand, $V$ is variable in sign． Thus $V$ satisfies the conditions of Theorem 4 and the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

Theorem 6 (Cetaev's Theorem, I934): Let there exist a function $V ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ such that (i) for an arbitrary large value of $t$ in an arbitrarily small neighborhood of the origin there exists a region $V > 0$ ， (ii) in the region $V > 0$ the function is limited,and (ii) in the region $V > 0$ the derivative $\dot { V }$ takes positive values for all values of $t ,$ ， $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ such that $V ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \geq \alpha _ { : }$ $\alpha > 0$ ，and $\dot { V } \geq l _ { : }$ $l = l ( \alpha ) > 0$ .Then the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

In fact, the region $V > 0$ exists in the neighborhood of the origin. Consider the solution $x _ { s } ( t )$ with the initial values $\xi _ { s }$ satisfying $V ( t _ { 0 } , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } ) = V _ { \circ } > 0$ In this region $\dot { V } > 0$ and $V$ increases. The solution curve remains in this region at least until the condition (24) is no longer valid.

If (24) were never violated, then we should have at all times $V [ t , x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t ) ] > V _ { 0 }$ and $\dot { V } \geq l _ { \ast }$ where $\iota$ is positive but nonzero. From this inequality we should obtain

$$
V [ t , x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t ) ] \geq V _ { 0 } + l ( t - t _ { 0 } ) ,
$$

which is impossible,since $V$ within the region $V > 0$ is limited by assumption.Hence the solution curve must leave from the region $V > 0$ at a certain instant, and the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

In order to see the definiteness of a quadratic form, we shall present some useful theorems below.Any form of an odd degree is a function of variable sign.

A quadratic form

$$
2 V = \sum _ { i , j = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i j } x _ { i } x _ { j } , \qquad c _ { i j } = c _ { j i } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }
$$

can be transformed by a linear substitution with a nonvanishig determinant into

$$
V = \lambda _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \dots + \lambda _ { n } y _ { n } ^ { 2 } .
$$

If $\lambda _ { s }$ are all positive,then $V$ is positive-definite,and if they are all negative， then $V$ is negative-definite,where a certain number of $\lambda _ { s }$ may be zero.If some $\lambda _ { s }$ are positive and others negative, then $V$ is of variable sign.In order that the quadratic form (27) be positive-definite it is necessary and sufficient that the principal minors of its discriminant, i.e.,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l r l } & { c _ { 1 1 } , } & { \left| \begin{array} { l l } { c _ { 1 1 } } & { c _ { 1 2 } } \\ { c _ { 1 1 } } & { c _ { 1 2 } } \\ { c _ { 1 2 } } & { c _ { 2 2 } } \end{array} \right| , } & { \dots \dots , } & { \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l l l } { c _ { 1 1 } } & { c _ { 1 2 } } & { \dots } & { c _ { 1  n } } & & \\ { c _ { 1 2 } } & { \cdot } & { c _ { 2 2 } } & & & { c _ { 2 n } } \\ { \dots } & & & & { } & \\ { c _ { 1 n } } & { c _ { 2 n } } & & & & { c _ { n n } } \end{array} \right| } \end{array}
$$

should be positive. This is Sylvester's theorem.

For an equation of the nth degree,

$$
a _ { 0 } x ^ { n } + a _ { 1 } x ^ { n - 1 } + \dotsb + a _ { n - 1 } x + a _ { n } = 0
$$

we consider the determinants

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \Delta _ { 1 } = a _ { 1 } , \qquad \Delta _ { 2 } = \biggl | a _ { 1 } \quad a _ { 0 } \biggr | , \qquad \Delta _ { 3 } = \biggl | a _ { 3 } \quad a _ { 2 } \quad a _ { 1 } \biggr | , } \\ { \qquad \Delta _ { 3 } = a _ { 3 } , \qquad \Delta _ { 2 } = \biggl | a _ { 3 } \quad a _ { 2 } \biggr | , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\Delta _ { n } = \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l l } { a _ { 1 } } & { a _ { 0 } } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { \dots } & { 0 } \\ { a _ { 3 } } & { a _ { 2 } } & { a _ { 1 } } & { a _ { 0 } } & { \dots } & { 0 } \\ { \dots } & & & & & \\ { a _ { 2 n - 1 } } & { a _ { 2 n - 2 } } & { a _ { 2 n - 3 } } & { a _ { 2 n - 4 } } & { \dots } & { a _ { n } } \end{array} \right| = a _ { n } \Delta _ { n - 1 } ,
$$

with $a _ { i } = 0$ for $i > n$ in $\Delta _ { n }$ . In order that all roots of the equation shall have negative real parts, it is necessary and sufficient that

$$
\Delta _ { 1 } > 0 , ~ \Delta _ { 2 } > 0 , ~ \dots , ~ \Delta _ { n - 1 } > 0 , ~ a _ { n } > 0 .
$$

For $n = 3$ we have

$$
a _ { 1 } > 0 , \qquad \left| { \begin{array} { l l } { a _ { 1 } } & { a _ { 0 } } \\ { a _ { 3 } } & { a _ { 2 } } \end{array} } \right| = a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } - a _ { 3 } a _ { 0 } > 0 , \qquad a _ { 3 } > 0 .
$$

For $n = 4$ we have

$$
a _ { 1 } > 0 , ~ a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } - a _ { 3 } a _ { 0 } > 0 , ~ a _ { 3 } ( a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } - a _ { 0 } a _ { 3 } ) - a _ { 4 } a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } > 0 , ~ a _ { 4 }
$$

or

$$
a _ { 1 } > 0 , ~ a _ { 3 } > 0 , ~ a _ { 3 } ( a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } - a _ { 0 } a _ { 3 } ) - a _ { 4 } a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } > 0 , ~ a _ { 4 } > 0 .
$$

This is called Hurwitz's theorem (1895). It is useful when we are investigating the stability character of a given motion.

The geometrical meaning of Liapounov's function $V ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ is : consider the $( n + 1 )$ -dimensional surface $z = V ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ near the origin $x _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0 ; V > 0$ presents the general aspect of a parabolic mirror pointing upward or of a cup on a table and $V < 0$ the cup just upside down or seen as if the table were a mirror.

Liapounov function has been discussed by Massera (1949)，and by Massera and Schäffer (1958,1959,1959a, 1966) for linear differ-ential equations by means of techniques of functional analysis. Massera and Schäffer considered

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } + A ( t ) x = f ( t )
$$

defined for $t \geq 0$ , where $A ( t )$ is continuous and bounded. They showed that Liapounov's function $V ( x , t )$ exists,that it is positive-definite and has an infinitely small upper bound,and that if its total derivative

$$
\dot { V } ( x , t ) = \frac { d V } { d t } - \operatorname* { d e t } \bigg \| \frac { \partial V } { \partial x } \bigg \| A x ,
$$

where det $\| \partial V / \partial x \|$ denotes the Jacobian，is negative-definite， then there are two positive constants $N$ and $\nu$ such that for any solution $x ( t )$ of

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } + A ( t ) x = 0
$$

and for any $t \geq t _ { 0 } > 0$ we have

$$
\| x ( t ) \| \leq N \exp \left\{ - \nu ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \right\} \cdot \| x ( t _ { 0 } ) \| .
$$

If this relation is satisfied for all solutions $x ( t )$ of the homogeneous differential equation and for any $t \geq t _ { 0 } \geq 0$ ,then the nonhomogeneous differential equation has at least one solution for every bounded continuous right-hand member $f ( t )$ and the homogeneous equation has $k = n$ linearly independent bounded solutions.

Likins and Roberson (1971） developed a matrix method for the Liapounov stability analysis of discrete holonomic mechanical systems with cyclic or ignorable coordinates (Section 1.3） and with the

Hamiltonian free of explicit time dependence.The final result is an expression for the quadratic approximation of the dynamical potential energy that is calculated more readily than by scalar analysis.From the condition for positive-definiteness of this function the Liapounov stability conditions are available.

Lefschetz (1958） extended the Liapounov functions.Denote by $f ( p , t )$ the trajectory $M$ starting at the initial position $p$ .
