We assume the continuity of $f ( p , t )$ with respect to $p$ and that the path $\gamma _ { p }$ of $M$ is also the path of every point of $\gamma _ { p }$ , accordingly, that if $q$ is not in $\gamma _ { p }$ then $\gamma _ { p }$ and $\gamma _ { q }$ do not intersect, that, if $\gamma _ { p } ^ { + }$ is the half-trajectory of $\gamma _ { p }$ for $t \geq 0$ and $\gamma _ { p } ^ { - }$ that for $t \leq 0$ , the sets $\Omega$ and $\pmb { A }$ ， such that $\Omega ( \gamma ) =$ $\cap _ { \mathfrak { p } } \overline { { \gamma _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { + } } }$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { A ( \gamma ) = \bigcap _ { p } \overline { { \gamma _ { p } ^ { - } } } } \end{array}$ ， are closed and if the space $\boldsymbol { R }$ is compact then they are connected, that the closure $\widetilde { M }$ of an invariant set $M$ is an invariant set, that the boundary of an invariant set is an invariant set, and that, if $M , N$ are invariant sets, so are their union $M \cup N$ ,intersection $M \cap N$ ，and difference $M - N$ ，and accordingly that $\Omega ( \gamma )$ and $A ( \gamma )$ are invariant sets.

Let $M$ be a closed invariant set of the dynamical system $f ( p , t )$ ， $S ( \varepsilon )$ be a sphere at center $M$ and with radius $\varepsilon$ ，and $H ( \varepsilon )$ be its boundary.We so define $M$ that (i) it is stable whenever,given $\varepsilon > 0$ ， there is an $\eta > 0$ such that if $p \in S ( \eta )$ then $\{ f ( p , t ) ; t \geq 0 \} \subset S ( \varepsilon )$ ，(ii) it is asymptotically stable whenever it is stable and in addition under the preceding conditions that $f ( p , t ) \to M$ as $t \to + \infty$ ，and (i） it is unstable whenever for any $\varepsilon > 0$ and whatever $\eta > 0$ and $\eta ( \varepsilon )$ there is a point $p \in S ( \eta )$ such that $f ( p , t )$ reaches $H ( q )$ for some $t > 0$ . The domain of asymptotic stability $\pmb { A }$ of $M$ is an open invariant set con-taining a sphere $S ( \eta )$ , and the closure $\bar { A }$ ,and accordingly its boundary, is an invariant set.

We see that the necessary and sufficient condition for the stability of $M$ is that given any $\varepsilon > 0$ and $p$ outside $S ( \varepsilon )$ there is a $\xi ( \varepsilon ) > 0$ such that $\gamma _ { p }$ remains outside $S ( \xi )$ ，and that the necessary and sufficient condition for asymptotic stability of $M$ in addition to the conditions for stability，is that there exists a neighborhood of $M$ free from complete paths other than those in $M$ itself.

The generalized Liapounov functions of Lefschetz are defined in a certain set $S ( \alpha )$ such that (i) given any $0 < \varepsilon < \alpha$ there exists a $\lambda > 0$ that satisfies $V > \lambda$ for $p$ outside $S ( \varepsilon )$ ， (ii) given $\eta > 0$ there exists an $\eta ( \lambda ) > 0$ that satisfies $V < \lambda$ for $p \in S ( \eta )$ ，and (iii) $V [ f ( p , t ) ]$ is in $S ( \alpha )$ a nonincreasing function of $t$ .Such a function $V$ is a generalization of Liapounov's conception of the positive-definite function $V$ with negative $\dot { V }$

Lefschetz proved the following theorems : A necessary and sufficient condition for the stability of $M$ is the existence of a generalized Liapounov function $V$ . A necessary and sufficient condition for asymptotic stability of $M$ is that there exists a $V ( p )$ defined for $p$ in a certain $S ( \alpha )$ such that $V [ f ( p , t ) ] \to 0$ as $t \to + \infty$ . A necessary and sufficient condition for instability of $M$ is the existence of a function $V ( p )$ defined in a certain $S ( \alpha )$ such that (i) $\vert V ( p ) \vert$ is bounded in $S ( \alpha )$ ， (ii) every $S ( \alpha )$ contains at least one point $p$ where $V ( p ) > 0$ ，and (iii) at every point $p \in S ( \alpha )$ along its path the derivative $\dot { V }$ is defined and $\dot { V } = \lambda V + W ( p )$ ，where $\lambda > 0$ and $W ( p )$ is nonnegative in $S ( \alpha )$

Asymptotic stability means that,given any $\xi$ and a sufficiently small ε,any path $\gamma _ { p }$ from $p \in S ( \varepsilon )$ remains in $S ( \xi )$ after a certain $T _ { p } = T$ Asymptotic stability is said to be uniform if we can choose $T _ { p } = T$ the same for all $p \in S ( \alpha )$ ．A necessary and sufficient condition for uniform asymptotic stability of $M$ is the existence of a function $V ( p )$ such that， in addition to the condition for asymptotic stability, $V [ f ( p , t ) ] \to 0$ as $t \to + \infty$ uniformly in $p$ for $p \in S ( \varepsilon )$ .If $M$ has a neighborhood $S ( \alpha )$ with compact closure,then asymptotic stability is always uniform.

LaSalle and Lefschetz (1961） gave several simple examples for Liapounov functions.Ghizzetti (1963） and Infante (1967) derived for nth order homogeneous differential equations the stability criteria depending on only $_ n$ parameters that determine a family of elliptic paraboloids in the $_ n$ -dimensional space of the coefficient functions.

# 16.4CONVERSE OF LIAPOUNOV'S THEOREMS

The criteria of stability and instability in the foregoing are only sufficient conditions and do not show any definite way to compute the function $V$ . It is still an open question whether such a function exists at all.The problem is the converse, that is,in which condition such a function can be found, or whether the sufficient conditions are also necessary.Soviet mathematicians are working on this problem.The first converse was studied by Persidskii on the basis of weak stability and by Massera on the basis of asymptotic stability.Massera's converse to Liapounov's second theorem on stability was argued by Malkin, who held that Massera's proof was not for restricting the differential equations but for showing the uniformity of stability. The converse theorems were established by Malkin,Barbashin,Krasovskii, Kurcveil, Zubov and others (Hahn,1959).

The study needs a refinement of the idea of stability.

We denote the solutionof the diferential quations by $x ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ $x _ { n } )$ and the initial values $\pmb { \xi } = ( \pmb { \xi } _ { 1 } , \pmb { \xi } _ { 2 } , \dots , \pmb { \xi } _ { n } )$ so that $x _ { s } = \xi _ { s } \left( s = 1 , 2 , \right.$ $\cdots , n )$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ Denote the solution with $x = \xi$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ by $p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } )$ . The question is whether the function

$$
\mu ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) = \operatorname* { s u p } _ { u \leq t } | p ( t _ { 0 } + u , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) |
$$

for a fixed $t _ { 0 }$ and $\pmb { \xi } _ { 0 }$ is bounded with respect to $t$ for stability，or monotonically tends to zero uniformly with respect to $\xi _ { s }$ and with respect to $t _ { 0 }$ for asymptotic stability. For the converse the existence of Liapounov function is shown with the analytic method for constructing such a function. The procedure is transfinite.Another procedure is to refer to the topology of dynamical systems. Zubov constructed Liapounov function by solving a partial differential equation． The converse for the criteria of instability can be established more easily.

The equilibrium point,or the trivial solution $x = 0$ ，is stable in Liapounov's sense if for any $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \varepsilon \qquad ( t \geq t _ { 0 } )
$$

follows from $| \xi | < \delta$ ，as defined in Section 16.1.If there exists a $\delta _ { 0 } > 0$ such that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) = 0
$$

follows from $| \xi | < \delta _ { 0 }$ ， then the equilibrium point is asymptotically stable.

The equilibrium point of the differential equation

$$
{ \frac { d x } { d t } } = f ( x , t ) , \qquad f ( 0 , t ) = 0 , \qquad f \in E ,
$$

is called uniformly stable if for each $\varepsilon > 0$ we can find a number $\delta = \delta ( \varepsilon ) > 0$ that depends on ε such that from $| \xi | < \delta$ there follows the inequality

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \varepsilon , \qquad t \geq t _ { 0 }
$$

for all $t _ { 0 } \geq 0$ (Krasovskii, 1963).

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :The equilibrium point of (29) is uniformly stable if and only if there exists a function $\rho ( r )$ such that (i) $\rho ( r )$ is definite, continuous,and monotonically increasing in the interval $0 \leq r \leq r _ { 1 } ,$ (ii) $\rho ( r )$ vanishes for $r = 0$ ，and (iii) for $| \xi | < r _ { 1 }$ we have (Persidski)

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | \leq \rho ( | \xi | ) .
$$

Consider for a given $\varepsilon > 0$ the upper bound $\tilde { \delta } ( \varepsilon )$ of all numbers $\delta$ belonging to $\varepsilon$ .If $| \xi | \le \tilde { \delta } ( \varepsilon )$ , then we have $| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | \geq \varepsilon$ and for each $\delta _ { 1 } > \tilde { \delta }$ there is at least an initial value $\tilde { \xi } _ { \mathfrak { d } }$ with $| \tilde { \xi } | \leq \delta _ { 1 }$ such that $| p ( t , \tilde { \xi } , t _ { 0 } ) |$ exceeds ε somewhere once. Also, $\tilde { \delta } ( \varepsilon )$ is positive for $\varepsilon > 0$ and tends to zero as $\varepsilon \to 0$ ，and may be discontinuous.We choose a continuous monotonically increasing function $\hat { \delta } ( \varepsilon )$ so that $\hat { \delta } ( \varepsilon ) \leq \tilde { \delta } ( \varepsilon )$ · This function possesses an inverse function that satisfies the assumptions of the theorem.

If

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | \le H
$$

holds for all points $\xi$ ，at which a solution curve starts,then the asymptotic stability is said to be global (im Ganzen).

If the assumptions of Theorem 1 are satisfied for any arbitrarily large $r _ { 1 }$ ，then the equilibrium point is said to be uniformly stable globally (im Ganzen).

Theorem 2: If the equilibrium point of an autonomous or periodic differential equation is stable,then it is alsc uniformly stable,and global uniform stability follows from global stability (Hahn, 1959).

In fact,if the initial values $\xi$ are chosen from a fixed sphere $K _ { \eta } \colon | x _ { s } | \leq r .$ then $\mu ( 0 , \xi , t _ { 1 } )$ defined in (28) with $t = 0$ is bounded by the assumption.The diffcrential equation admits a period $_ T$ ，which is arbitrary in the autonomous case, so that $p ( t + T , \xi , t _ { 0 } + T ) =$ $p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } )$ . Hence $\mu$ is periodic in $t _ { 0 }$ . By choosing $r$ suffciently small, we vary $u , t _ { 0 } , \xi$ in $\rho ( r ) = \operatorname* { s u p } | p ( t _ { 0 } + u , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) |$ according to $u \geq 0$ ， $t _ { 1 } \leq t _ { 0 } \leq t _ { 1 } + T , | \xi | \leq r$ ，where $t _ { 1 } \geq 0$ is arbitrary；then such a function $\rho ( r )$ satisfies the assumptions of Theorem 1.

The equilibrium point of (24a) is called equi-asymptotically stable if a number $\tau$ can be found for a fixed $t _ { 0 }$ and for any $\varepsilon > 0$ such that

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \varepsilon , \qquad t > t _ { 0 } + \tau ,
$$

provided that $\xi$ belongs to a sphere $\textstyle K _ { \eta }$ whose radius $\eta$ is independent of $\varepsilon$ (Massera,1949).The stability defined in Section 16.1 follows from this equi-asymptotic stability,but not the uniform stability.If there exists a $\tau = \tau ( \mathfrak { s } , t _ { 0 } )$ for any $t _ { 0 } \geq t _ { 1 } \geq 0$ such that the inequality (31) is satisfied, then the stability is said to be uniformly asymptotic with regard to the spatial coordinates. If $\tau ( \varepsilon )$ depends only on ε but not on $t _ { 0 }$ and if the stability is uniform,then the equilibrium point is called uniformly asymptotically stable.

Consider $\sigma ( r )$ such that (i) $\sigma ( r )$ is defined, continuous and monotonically decreasing for all $r \geq 0$ ，(ii) $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { r \to \infty } \sigma ( r ) = 0 } \end{array}$ ，and (ii) $| x _ { s } ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | \le \sigma ( t - t _ { 0 } )$ for all $\pmb { \xi }$ in the fixed sphere $\scriptstyle K _ { \eta }$ . Let $\kappa ( r )$ be a function satisfying the conditions (i) and (ii) of Theorem 1.

Theorem 3: The necessary and suffcient condition for uniformly asymptotic stability of the equilibrium point is that there exist two functions $\sigma ( r )$ and $\kappa ( r )$ , just defined,and that

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | \leq \kappa ( | \xi | ) \sigma ( t - t _ { 0 } )
$$

for $\xi$ of a fixed sphere $\textstyle K _ { \eta }$ (Hahn).

The equilibrium point is called uniformly asymptotically stable globally if (i) it is uniformly stable globally，(ii) for any $\delta _ { 1 } > 0$ and $\delta _ { 2 } > 0$ there is a number $\tau ( \delta _ { 1 } , \delta _ { 2 } )$ such that $| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \delta _ { 2 }$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ $+ \ \tau ( \delta _ { 1 } , \delta _ { 2 } )$ ， $| \xi | < \delta _ { 1 }$ . It can be shown that if the equilibrium point of an autonomous or periodic diferential equation is asymptotically stable,or asymptotically stable globally,then the asymptotic stability is uniform (Barbashin and Krasovskii).

Analogous theorems for a differential equation with almost periodic coefficients are not yet known.Yoshizawa (1955） considered equistability. Soviet mathematicians call the asymptotic stability in the large if it is in the phase space with $x _ { s } ( t )$ ， $y _ { s } ( t ) = d x _ { s } / d t$ .If $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every motion $p ( t , \xi , t _ { 1 } )$ with $0 < | \xi | < \varepsilon ,$ $t _ { 1 } \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，reaches the sphere $| x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } | = \varepsilon$ in a finite time,then the equilibrium is called completely unstable.A solution for which, from $| \hat { \xi } | < \delta$ for all $t \geq T$ ，the inequality $| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \phi ( \delta )$ follows where $\phi ( r )$ is an arbitrary continuous real function，which vanishes for $r = 0$ and increases strictly monotonically with $r$ ,in the closed interval $0 \leq r \leqq$ $h , h > 0$ ，is called an $s .$ -motion. Let $S ( r )$ be the measure of the sphere $\begin{array} { r } { | \sum _ { s } \xi _ { s } ^ { 2 } | ^ { 1 / 2 } \le r . } \end{array}$ and $M _ { s } ( \delta )$ be the measure of the point in this sphere from which an $s$ -motion starts.The limit of $M _ { s } ( \delta ) / S ( \delta )$ for $\delta \to 0$ is called the stability probability by Moisseiev (Hahn,1959);it depends on the choice of $\phi ( \delta )$ . Let $\textstyle E ( R , r )$ be the measure of the set of points $\xi _ { s }$ of the sphere for which sup $| p ( t , \xi , 0 ) |$ for $t \geq 0$ is not larger than $\pmb R$ . Stepanov (Hahn,1959) called

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { \pi \to 0 } \operatorname* { l i m } _ { r \to 0 } { \frac { E ( R , r ) } { S ( r ) } }
$$

the stability probability.It is equal to unity in the case of Liapounov's stability, but the converse is not true.

Let $\mathfrak { G }$ be an open subset of the phase space containing the origin and $\mathfrak { F }$ be its complementary set.Denote by $\rho ( x , \mathfrak { F } )$ the distance of the point $x$ from $\mathfrak { F }$ ，and write

$$
\omega ( x ) = \mathrm { m a x } \left( | x | , \frac { 1 } { \rho ( x , \mathfrak { F } ) } - \frac { 2 } { \rho ( 0 , \mathfrak { F } ) } \right) .
$$

The equilibrium point is said to be strongly stable in $\mathfrak { G }$ if for any positive numbers $\beta$ and $\varepsilon$ there exist two positive numbers $\boldsymbol { b } = \boldsymbol { b } ( \varepsilon )$

and $\tau = \tau ( \beta , \varepsilon )$ such that (i) $b ( \beta )$ tends monotonically to zero as $\beta \to 0$ ，and (ii） if $q ( t ) = ( q _ { 1 } ( t ) , q _ { 2 } ( t ) , \ldots , q _ { n } ( t ) )$ is a solution of the differential equation in the interval $t _ { 0 } \equiv t \equiv t _ { 1 } < \infty$ with the initial condition $\omega ( q ( t _ { 0 } ) ) \cong \beta$ ,then there exists a solution $p ( t )$ , for all $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ， that coincides with $q ( t )$ in the interval $[ t _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 } ]$ and satisfes the conditions $\omega ( p ( t ) ) < b$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ and $\omega ( p ( t ) ) < \varepsilon$ for $t \ge t _ { 0 } + \tau$

The converses of the stability theorem depend on the nature of the differential equation,on the assumptions made concerning the equilibrium point,and on the nature of Liapounov function， especially on the existence and the continuity of its partial derivatives.

A function $V ( x )$ is called positive-semi-definite [or negative-semidefinite] if $V ( 0 ) = 0$ and if in the neighborhood $K _ { h }$ of the origin we have $V ( x ) \geq 0$ $[ \mathsf { o r } \le 0 ]$ ，where $X _ { h }$ is the sphere $| x | \leq h$ .If $V ( 0 ) = 0$ and $V ( x ) > 0$ $[ \mathsf { o r } < 0 ]$ for $x \neq 0$ ,then the function is positive-definite [or negative-definite]. The sphere $| x | \leq h , t \geq { t _ { 0 } }$ is denoted by $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } }$ We quote the converse theorems on weak stability.

Theorem 4: Let $f _ { i } ( x , t )$ and $\partial f _ { i } / \partial x _ { j }$ be continuous in $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } }$ ，and the equilibrium point of (29) be stable.Then there exists a positivedefinite function $V ( x , t )$ that possesses continuous first derivatives with respect to all variables,and also a negative-semi-definite derivative $\dot { V } ( x , t )$ for (29) (Kurcveil).

In fact,owing to the stability,we can choose $\delta > 0$ such that the inequality $| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < h / 2$ follows from $| \xi | < \delta$ Let $\mathfrak { G } _ { 1 } ^ { \xi }$ and $\mathfrak { G } _ { 2 }$ be the set of points $( x , \tau )$ from $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } }$ for which $| p ( t _ { 0 } , \xi , \tau ) | < \delta / 2$ and $| p ( t _ { 0 } , \xi , \tau ) | < \delta$ ，respectively,and let $\mathfrak { G }$ be the complement to $\mathfrak { G } _ { 1 }$ in $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } }$ . Denote by $\gamma ( r )$ a positive function which is continuously differentiable in $r > 0$ and vanishes for $r \le 0$ and is equal to a constant $\gamma ( r ) = \delta ^ { 2 }$ for $r \geq \delta / 2$ . Then Liapounov function is defined by

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { V ( x , \tau ) = \widehat { \vartheta } ^ { 2 } } & & { ( ( x , \tau ) \in \widetilde { \mathfrak { G } } ) , } \\ & { V ( x , \tau ) = \gamma ( | p ( t _ { 0 } , x , \tau ) | ) } & & { ( ( x , \tau ) \in \mathfrak { G } _ { 2 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

In the common region the two definitions coincide. Thus $V$ is nonnegative and vanishes at the origin.It is continuously differentiable in $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } }$ ， and its derivative $\dot { V }$ is identically zero since $V$ is constant throughout any motion.If $0 < \varepsilon < h$ and $\bar { \delta } < \delta$ are determined so that the inequality $| p ( t , x , t _ { 0 } ) | < \varepsilon$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ follows from $| x | < \bar { \delta }$ , then for $\varepsilon \leq | x | \leq h$ ， $( x , \tau ) \in { \mathfrak { G } } _ { 2 }$ we have $\left| x _ { 1 } \right| \geq \bar { \delta }$ for $x _ { 1 } = p ( t _ { 0 } , x , \tau )$ because $| x _ { 1 } | \geq \bar { \delta }$ by $p ( t , x _ { 1 } , t _ { 0 } ) = p ( t , x , \tau )$ ， and accordingly

$$
V ( x , \tau ) \geq \operatorname* { m i n } { ( \delta ^ { 2 } , \gamma ( | \bar { \delta } | ) ) } .
$$

If, further, the stability in Theorem 4 is uniform, then in any region $K _ { h _ { 1 } , t _ { 0 } }$ $( 0 < h _ { 1 } < h )$ ，there exists a positive-definite decrescent (see below） function $V ( x , \tau )$ which possesses continuous first derivatives with respect to all variables and whose derivative $\dot { V }$ is negative-semidefinite (Kurcveil, Krasovskii).

The conditions of Theorem 4 are necessary and suffcient for uniform stability.A function $V ( x , t )$ is called decrescent if there exists a function $\psi ( r )$ such that,if the function $\psi ( r )$ is real,continuous in the interval $0 \leq r \leq h$ ，vanishes for $r = 0$ ， and increases strictly mono-tonically with $r$ ，then we have $| V ( x , t ) | \leq \psi ( | x | )$ in $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } }$

The converse theorems on asymptotic stability are

Theorem 5: Let the equilibrium point of (29) be uniformly asymptotically stable and $f \in C _ { 0 }$ in $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } }$ . Then there exists a positivedefinite decrescent function $V$ ，whose derivative $\dot { V }$ is negative-definite and which possesses partial derivatives of an arbitrary order (Massera).

Theorem 6: If the equilibrium point is uniformly asymptotically stable globally，and $f \in C _ { 0 }$ ，then there exists a positive-definite, decrescent, and radially unbounded function $V$ ，with a negativedefinite derivative, which possesses partial derivatives of any arbitrary order (Massera).

For the asymptotic but not uniform stability Zubov (1964） gave several theorems in which_instead of (32) a more general form of velocities is adopted. Let $\Phi ( t )$ and $\Psi ( t )$ be two positive continuous auxiliary functions for $t \geq 0$ . The integral

$$
J ( t , t _ { 0 } ) = \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \frac { \Psi ( \tau ) } { \Phi ( \tau ) } d \tau
$$

is bounded for $t \geq t _ { 0 } \geq 0$ and tends to infinity for $t \to \infty$ . Furthermore suppose that the function

$$
\kappa ( t , t _ { 0 } ; \alpha , \beta ) = \Big [ \frac { \Phi ( t _ { 0 } ) } { \Phi ( t ) } \Big ] ^ { 1 / ( \alpha + 1 ) } \exp { \{ - \beta J ( t , t _ { 0 } ) \} } ,
$$

depending on two parameters $\alpha , \beta$ ，tends to zero for $t \to \infty$

Theorem 7: If the equilibrium point of (29) is asymptotically stable,and for all sufficiently small $| \xi |$ we have

$$
l _ { 1 } \kappa ( t , t _ { 0 } ; \alpha , \beta _ { 1 } ) | \boldsymbol { \xi } | ^ { 2 } \leq | p ( t , \boldsymbol { \xi } , t _ { 0 } ) | ^ { 2 } \leq l _ { 2 } \kappa ( t , t _ { 0 } ; \alpha , \beta _ { 2 } ) | \boldsymbol { \xi } | ^ { 2 }
$$

with positive constants $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 }$ ，then it is necessary and suffcient for such behavior of the solution that there exist two functions

$$
V = | x | ^ { 2 \alpha } \Phi ( t ) x ^ { T } A ( x , t ) x , \qquad W = | x | ^ { 2 \alpha } \Psi ( t ) x ^ { T } B ( x , t ) x ,
$$

such that (i) they are defined and continuous in the domain ${ { K } _ { h , t _ { 0 } } }$ (ii) for sufficiently small $| x |$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { a _ { 1 } | x | ^ { 2 } \leq x ^ { T } A ( x , t ) x \leq a _ { 2 } | x | ^ { 2 } } & { \quad ( a _ { 1 } > 0 , a _ { 2 } > 0 ) , } \\ & { b _ { 1 } | x | ^ { 2 } \leq x ^ { T } B ( x , t ) x \leq b _ { 2 } | x | ^ { 2 } } & { \quad ( b _ { 1 } > 0 , b _ { 2 } > 0 ) , } \end{array}
$$

and (ii) $d V / d t = - W$ (Zubov,1964)，where $x ^ { { \tau } }$ is the transposed matrix of $x$

The converse theorems on instability are

Theorem 8: For the existence of Liapounov function $V$ whose derivative $\dot { V }$ is definite it is necessary and sufficient that there exists a neighborhood of the origin in the phase space in which neighborhood no complete phase trajectory $- \infty < t < + \infty$ iscontained (Krasovskii, 1963).

Theorem 9: Let the equilibrium point be unstable. For the existence of a function $V$ that can take negative values in any neighborhood of the origin and whose derivative $\dot { V }$ is negative-definite,it is necessary and sufficient that there exists a neighborhood of the origin in which neighborhood no complete phase trajectory $- \infty < t < + \infty$ is contained (Krasovskii, 1963).

The stability theory of ordinary differential equations is extended to functional differential equations by using a Liapounov function by Razumikhin (1956),Hale (1965) and Yorke (1970).Let $v ( t ; \xi ) =$ $V ( t , x ( t ) )$ be a Liapounov function, and $\dot { v } ( t , \xi )$ be the derivative $\dot { V } ( t , x )$ of $V ( t , x )$ along the solution. Write

$$
V ( t , \tau ; \xi ) = \operatorname* { s u p } \left\{ v ( s ; \xi ) ; t - \tau \le s \le t \right\}
$$

for constant $\tau \geq 0$ . Kato (1971） extended Razumikhin's theorem under the same assumption as of Yorke.

Consider the system of differential equations

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \dot { u } ( t ) = U ( t , u ( t ) ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $U ( t , u )$ is a continuous scalar function on $[ 0 , \infty ] \times R ^ { 1 }$ ， $R ^ { n }$ being an $_ n$ -dimensional set of real numbers.Denote by $u ( t ; s , \alpha )$ and $r ( t ; s , \alpha )$ the maximal solution of this equation through $( s , \alpha )$ to the right and to the left,respectively. Suppose that

if

Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \dot { v } ( t ; \xi ) \equiv U ( t , v ( t ; \xi ) ) } \\ & { } \\ & { v ( t ; \xi ) \equiv r ( s ; t , v ( t ; \xi ) ) , \qquad s \in [ t - \tau , t ] . } \\ & { } \\ & { v ( t ; \xi ) \equiv u ( t ; a , \alpha ) } \end{array}
$$

forall $t \geq a$ ，where $( a , \alpha )$ satisfies $v ( s ; \xi ) \le r ( s ; a , \alpha )$ for all $s \in [ a - \tau , a ]$ . Kato proved the theorems:

If a Liapounov function $v ( t ; \xi )$ satisfies the condition that

$$
\dot { v } ( t ; \xi ) \leq - c ( t , v ( t ; \xi ) )
$$

when

$$
f ( t , v ( t , \xi ) ) \geq V ( t , \tau ; \xi )
$$

with continuous functions $c ( t , u )$ and $f ( t , u )$ such that $f ( t , u ) \geq u$ and $c ( t , u ) \geq 0$ for any $\pmb { t }$ and any $u \geq 0$ ,and $c ( t , u )$ and $f ( t , u ) / u$ are nondecreasing in $\boldsymbol { u }$ for any fixed $t _ { i }$ ，then there exists a Liapounov function $w ( t ; \xi )$ such that

$$
v ( t ; \xi ) \leq w ( t ; \xi ) \leq V ( t , \tau ; \xi )
$$

and that

$$
\dot { w } ( t ; \xi ) \leq U ( t , w ( t ; \xi ) )
$$

for a continuous function $U ( t , u )$ satisfying

$$
U ( t , u ) = - \operatorname* { m i n } { \{ c ( t , u ) , u \alpha ( t , u ) \} } ,
$$

where

$$
\alpha ( t , u ) = \{ { \log { [ u / f ^ { - 1 } ( t , u ) ] } } \} / { \tau }
$$

and $f ^ { - 1 } ( t , u )$ is the inverse function of $f ( t , u )$ with respect to $\pmb { u }$ for each fixed $t$

Suppose further that $f ( t , u ) = f ( u )$ is independent of $t , f ( u ) > u$ for $u > 0$ and

$$
c ( t , u ) \leq K ( u ) , \int ^ { \infty } c ( t , u ) d t = \infty
$$

for any constant $u > 0$ ，where $K ( u )$ is continuous.If $v ( t ; \xi )$ satisfies the same condition as in the above, then $v ( t ; \xi )$ is asymptotically stable.

Kato extended the theorems to a system of functional differential equations

$$
\dot { { \boldsymbol x } } ( t ) = { \boldsymbol F } ( t , { \boldsymbol x } _ { t } )
$$

and its perturbed system

$$
\dot { x } ( t ) = F ( t , x _ { t } ) + X ( t , x _ { t } ) ,
$$

where $x _ { t } ( \theta ) = x ( t + \theta )$ for $\theta \in [ - h , 0 ] , h > 0$ and $F ( t , \phi )$ and $X ( t , \phi )$ are continuous $R ^ { n }$ -valued functions defined on

$$
[ 0 , \infty ) \times C ( [ - h , 0 ] , R ^ { n } ) .
$$

Yoshizawa (1971） pointed out that the boundedness of solutions does not necessarily imply the existence of almost periodic solutions. Deysach and Sell (1965) discussed the existence of an almost periodic solution by assuming that the periodic system has a bounded solution which is uniformly stable.Miller（1965） assumed that the bounded solution is totally stable (see below） and Seifert (1966) assumed the ∑-stability which is equivalent to the stability under disturbances introduced by Sell (1967).Coppel (1967） and Yoshizawa (1969) assumed asymptotical almost periodicity of the solution.Yoshizawa (1971) discussed the relations between the stability of various kinds, such as uniform stability,uniform asymptotic stability, total stability, and stability under disturbances from a hull,for almost periodic systems.

Let $\phi ( t )$ be a solution of the system ${ d x } / { d t } = f ( t , x )$ defined on $\pmb { I }$ and assume that $\lVert \phi ( t ) \rVert \leq B$ for all $t \geq 0$ where $B < B ^ { * }$ . Denote the solution of this differential equation through $( t _ { 0 } , x _ { 0 } )$ by $x ( t ; x _ { 0 } , t _ { 0 } )$ . The solution $\phi ( t )$ is said to be weakly uniformly asymptotically stable if it is uniformly stable and if there is a $\delta _ { \circ } > 0$ such that if $t _ { 0 } \in I$ and $\lVert \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) - x _ { 0 } \rVert < \delta _ { 0 }$ then $\| \phi ( t ) - x ( t ; x _ { 0 } , t _ { 0 } ) \| \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ . $\phi ( t )$ is said to be totally stable if for any $t _ { 0 } \geq 0$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a $\delta ( \varepsilon )$ $> 0$ such that if $h ( t )$ is continuous on $[ t _ { 0 } , \infty )$ and satisfies $\| h ( t ) \| <$ $\delta ( \varepsilon )$ for all $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ and if $y _ { 0 } \in S _ { B ^ { * } }$ ， $S _ { B ^ { \ast } } = \{ x ; x < B ^ { \ast } \}$ ， $I = [ 0 , \infty ]$ ， satisfies $\| \phi ( t _ { 0 } ) - y _ { 0 } \| < \delta ( \varepsilon )$ ，then any solution $y ( t )$ through $( t _ { 0 } , y _ { 0 } )$ of $d x / d t = f ( t , x ) + h ( t )$ satisfies $\| \phi ( t ) - y ( t ) \| < \varepsilon$ for all $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ (Kato and Yoshizawa,1970). $\phi ( t )$ is said to be stable under disturbances from $H ( f )$ , the hull of $\textstyle f ( t , x )$ ， with respect to $s$ ，a given compact set in $S _ { B ^ { * } }$ ,for $\iota \geq 0$ , if for any $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a $\delta ( \varepsilon ) > 0$ such that $\| \phi ( t + \tau ) - x ( t ; x _ { 0 } , 0 , g ) \| \le \varepsilon$ for $t \geq 0$ ，whenever $g \in H ( f )$ ， $| | \phi ( \tau )$ $- x _ { 0 } \| \leqq \delta ( \varepsilon )$ and $\rho ( f _ { \varepsilon } , g ; S ) \cong \delta ( \varepsilon )$ for some $\tau \geq 0$ ，where $f _ { \ast } =$ $\textstyle f ( t + \tau , x )$ and $x ( t ; x _ { 0 } , 0 , g )$ is a solution of ${ d x } / { d t } = g ( t , x )$ such that $x ( 0 ; x _ { 0 } , 0 , g ) = x _ { 0 }$ and $x ( t ; t _ { 0 } , 0 , g ) \in S$ for all $t \geq 0$ ，where $g \in H ( f )$ ， $p \in H ( f )$ and

$$
\rho ( g , p ; S ) = \operatorname* { s u p } { \big \{ } \| g ( t , x ) - p ( t , x ) \| ; t \in R , x \in S { \big \} } .
$$

Suppose that $\textstyle f ( t , x )$ is defined on $R \times S _ { B ^ { * } }$ ， $R = ( - \infty , \infty )$ ， all real numbers,and is almost periodic in $t$ uniformly for all $t \in R$ ， $x \in S _ { B ^ { * } }$ If $\phi ( t )$ is totally stable for an almost periodic system then it is stable under disturbances from $H ( t )$ with respect to $\kappa$ ，where $\kappa$ is the set of $x$ such that $\| x \| \le B _ { 1 } , B < B _ { 1 } < B ^ { * }$ , thus a compact set in $S _ { B ^ { * } }$ ，and it is also uniformly stable. Suppose that the almost periodic system has a solution $\phi ( t )$ defined on $\pmb { I }$ such that $\lVert \phi ( t ) \rVert \leq B$ ， $B < B ^ { * }$ ，for $t \geq 0$ . If the solution $\phi ( t )$ is asymptotically almost periodic, then the almost periodic system has an almost periodic solution (Section 14.24).

Nakajima (1971) weakened the conditions of Fink and Seifert (1969) for the existence of some Liapounov function in order to prove the existence of almost periodic solutions.Sugiyama (1971） considered the asymptotic stability of the solution of a difference equation by applying Liapounov functions.

# 16.5DYNAMICAL SYSTEMS

The introduction of the idea of dynamical systems facilitates the development of the stability_theory as originally worked out by Liapounov and recently by Zubov (1964； See also Nemytskii and Stepanov, 1960).

Definitions: Consider a vector function $q ( x , t )$ in the $_ n$ -dimensional phase space such that (i) the components of the vector function are defined as single-valued continuous functions in any finite $x$ in the interval $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ， (ii) $q ( x , 0 ) = x$ ，and (ii) $q [ q ( x , t _ { 1 } ) , t _ { 2 } ] =$ $q ( x , t _ { 1 } + t _ { 2 } )$ .Then we say that the function $\scriptstyle q ( x , t )$ represents a dynamical system in the phase space. The totality of points $q ( x , t )$ with a fixed $x$ is called a trajectory of the system. A trajectory can be regarded as a single-parameter group of continuous mappings into itself in the phase space $x \to q ( x , t )$ . A point set $M$ of the phase space consisting only of trajectories is called an invariant set of the system. Then from $x \in M$ there follows $q ( x , t ) \in M$ for all $t$ Let $\rho ( x , M ) =$ $\mathrm { i n f } _ { y \in M } \mid x - y \mid$ be the distance of the point $x$ from the set $M$ The set of all points $_ x$ with $0 < \rho ( x , M ) < r$ constitutes a neighborhood $U ( M , r )$ of the set $M$

This is a dynamical system in the Euclidean space.

An analogous idea can be extended to a function space,such as Banach's (Section 16.18；Sobolev, 1963).A set $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ is called a normed linear space if $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ is a linear system and if to each element $x \in E$ there corresponds a nonnegative number $\| { \boldsymbol x } \|$ ,called the norm, such that (i) $\| { \boldsymbol { x } } \| = 0$ if and only if $x = 0$ ，(ii) $\| a x \| = | a | \| x \|$ ，where $^ { a }$ is complex, and (iii) $\| x + y \| \leq \| x \| + \| y \|$ .If $\rho ( x , y ) = \| x - y \|$ ， then a metric is defined in the set $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ . Consider a linear partial differential equation

$$
\frac { \partial u } { \partial t } = a u + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { i } \frac { \partial u } { \partial x _ { i } } ,
$$

where $a , b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n }$ are real constants.We form that space $\Phi$ of a continuously differentiable function $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ defined for $X ( x _ { 1 } ,$ $\dots , x _ { n } ) \in E _ { n }$ ，where $E _ { n }$ is a normed linear space,and such that $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \to L _ { \phi }$ as $\| X ^ { 2 } \| \longrightarrow + \infty$ ,where $L _ { \phi }$ is a finite real constant. We put $\| \phi \| = \operatorname { s u p } _ { X \in E _ { n } } | \phi |$ .For any function $\phi \in \Phi$ the partial differential equation admits a solution $u ( \phi , t )$ such that $u ( \phi , 0 ) = \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \dots ,$ $x _ { n } )$ and

$$
u ( \phi , t ) = e ^ { a t } \phi ( x _ { 1 } + b _ { 1 } t , \ldots , x _ { n } + b _ { n } t ) .
$$

The aggregate of such solutions is a single-parameter family of transformations of the space $\Phi$ into itself, generated by the single-parameter family of operators $u _ { t } ( \phi ) = u ( \phi , t )$ . Such a family has the following properties: (i) $u ( \phi , t )$ is continuous at any point $( \phi , t )$ both in $\phi$ and t, i.e., for any $\varepsilon > 0$ we can find $\delta _ { \mathrm { 1 } } > 0$ ， $\delta _ { 2 } > 0$ such that $\| u ( \phi _ { 1 } , b _ { 1 } )$ $- \ u ( \phi _ { 2 } , t _ { 2 } ) \| < \varepsilon$ for $| t _ { 1 } - t _ { 2 } | < \delta _ { 1 }$ ， $| | \phi _ { 1 } - \phi _ { 2 } | | < \delta _ { 2 }$ (ii) $u ( u ( \phi , t _ { 1 } ) , t _ { 2 } ) =$ $u ( \phi , t _ { 1 } + t _ { 2 } )$ ,and (ii) for any function $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \in \Phi$ there exists only one solution $u ( \phi , t )$ that is uniformly differentiable with respect to $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ， $t$ such that $u ( \phi , 0 ) = \phi$ .The single-parameter family of operators $u ( \phi , t )$ with these three properties (i), (ii),and (ii) is called a dynamical system in the function space $\Phi$ (Section 16.18). It gives a group of transformations of the space $\Phi$ into itself.

Similarly we can define a dynamical system in a metric space $R$ (Section 16.18),which is a single-parameter family of operators $\textstyle F _ { t }$ ， $t \in ( - \infty , + \infty )$ ，defined in $R$ such that (i) on any element $x \in R$ the family of operators $\boldsymbol { F } _ { t }$ assumes the value $\textstyle F _ { t } ( x ) \in R$ for $t \in ( - \infty , + \infty )$ so that $\begin{array} { r } { F _ { 0 } ( x ) = x } \end{array}$ ，(ii) the value of the operator $\boldsymbol { F } _ { t } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is a continuous function in the aggregate $( t , x )$ ,and (ii) $F _ { t _ { 1 } } ( F _ { t _ { 2 } } ( x ) ) = F _ { t _ { 1 } + t _ { 2 } } ( x )$ . Some-times $\boldsymbol { F } _ { t } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is denoted by $q ( x , t )$ . The function $q ( x , t )$ for a fixed $x$ is called a motion. The set of all points $q ( x , t ) , t \in ( - \infty , + \infty )$ ，with a fixed $x$ is called the trajectory of the dynamical system.

Consider the motion $q ( x , t )$ . For any $\varepsilon > 0$ and for any $T > 0$ we can find a $\delta > 0$ such that for $\rho ( x , \eta ) < \delta$ we have $\rho ( q ( x , t ) , q ( y , t ) ) <$ $, t \in [ 0 , T ]$ (Nemytskii and Stepanov, 1960).

The set $M \subset R$ is said to be invariant with respect to a dynamical system $\scriptstyle q ( x , t )$ if it consists of the trajectories such that from $x \in M$ it follows that $q ( x , t ) \in M$ ， $t \in [ - \infty , + \infty ]$ .The closure $\overline { { M } }$ of the invariant set $M$ is an invariant set. Since a boundary is $\overline { { M } } \backslash M$ the boundary of an open invariant set consists of trajectories of the dynamical system $q ( x , t )$ ，where $N \backslash M$ means the set $N - N \cap M$

A point $y$ is called an $\pmb { \omega }$ -limit point of the motion $q ( x , t )$ if there exists a sequence of numbers, $t _ { n } \to + \infty$ as $n \to \infty$ ， such that $q ( x , t _ { n } )$ $ y$ as $n  + \infty$ . The point $y$ is called an $\alpha \cdot$ -limit point if there exists a sequence $\bar { t } _ { n } \to - \infty$ such that $q ( x , \bar { t } _ { n } ) \to y$ as $n  + \infty$ (Section

26.1). Now, if the motion $q ( x , t )$ has $\omega$ -limit [or $\alpha$ -limit] points,then the set of all its $\omega$ -limit [or $\alpha \cdot$ -limit] points is invariant and closed (Birkhoff,1927).An equilibrium point in the foregoing discussion is invariant.

A closed invariant set $M$ of a dynamical system is called stable if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ a $\delta > 0$ can be found so that for all $t > 0$ we have $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] < \varepsilon$ for $\rho ( x , M ) < \delta$ .If $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  0 } \rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] = 0 } \end{array}$ then $M$ is called asymptotically stable.If there exists a number $\delta _ { \mathfrak { d } }$ with the property that for every $\varepsilon > 0$ a $\tau = \tau ( \varepsilon ) > 0$ can be found such that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] < \varepsilon$ for $t > \tau$ when $\rho ( x , M ) < \delta _ { 0 }$ ，then the invariant set $M$ is called uniformly asymptotically stable.

If there exists an $\varepsilon > 0$ with the property that for every $\delta > 0$ at least a point $x _ { 0 }$ in $U ( M , \delta )$ and a $t _ { 1 } > 0$ can be found such that $\rho [ q ( x _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 } ) , M ] \geq \varepsilon$ ，then the invariant set is said to be unstable.An asymptotically stable closed invariant set with a compact neighborhood is uniformly asymptotically stable.

An asymptotically stable closed invariant set $M$ of a dynamical system $q ( x , t )$ is called uniformly attracting if it is possible to find a number $\delta _ { 2 } > 0$ such that for any $0 < h < \delta _ { 2 }$ there exist numbers $T > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$ such that when $h < \rho ( x , M ) < \delta _ { 2 }$ ， $t \in [ 0 , T ]$ we have $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] > \alpha$ 、An asymptotically stable closed invariant set $M$ of a dynamical system $q ( x , t )$ with a sufficiently small compact neighborhood is uniformly asymptotically stable and uniformly attracting.The following theorems can be established analogously to those in Section 16.3:

A necessary and sufficient condition that a closed invariant set $M$ be stable is that

$$
\delta ( \varepsilon ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \stackrel { t \leq 0 } { \rho ( x , M ) = \varepsilon } } \rho ( q ( x , t ) , M )
$$

be positive for arbitrary sufficiently small $\varepsilon > 0$

In order for a closed invariant set $M$ of a dynamical system $q ( x , t )$ with a suffciently small compact neighborhood to be asymptotically stable,it is necessary and sufficient to satisfy the following conditions : (i) there exists no motion $q ( x , t )$ having $\alpha$ -limit points in $M , x \notin M$ ， that is, $x$ does not belong to $M$ ，and (ii） there exists a sufficiently small neighborhood of the set $M$ that does not contain any trajectory of the dynamical system $\boldsymbol { q } ( \boldsymbol { x } , t )$ ·

In order for the trivial solution $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ of the differential equations

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } = f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )
$$

to be asymptotically stable, it is necssary and sufficient to satisfy the following conditions: (i) there exists no integral curve of this system of differential equations $x = x ( t , \pmb { \xi } )$ ， $| \xi | \neq 0$ such that $| x | \to 0$ as $t \to - \infty$ ，and (ii) there exists a sufficiently small neighborhood of the point $x = 0$ that contains no integral curve $x = x ( t , \xi )$ ， $t \in ( - \infty$ ， $+ \infty )$ ， $| \xi | \neq 0$

For the proof, we change the independent variable

$$
\begin{array} { r } { d s = d t \sqrt { 1 + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { i } ^ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

and obtain

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d s } = \frac { f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) } { \sqrt { 1 + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { j } ^ { 2 } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

This system defines in the space $E _ { n }$ a dynamical system $x = x ( s , \pmb { \xi } )$ for which $x = 0$ is a closed invariant set.

Let $M$ be an invariant closed set of the given dynamical system $q ( x , t )$ ，and $U = U ( M , r )$ be a sufficiently small neighborhood of $M$ Liapounov's theory is applied to such dynamical systems as follows.

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ :The necessary and sufficient condition for stability of $M$ is the existence of a function $V ( x )$ such that (i) $V ( x )$ is defined in $U$ ， (ii) to every sufficiently small number $c _ { 1 } > 0$ there is a $c _ { 2 } > 0$ such that $V ( x ) > c _ { 2 }$ if $x \in U$ and $\rho ( x , M ) > c _ { 1 }$ ，(ii) for every sufficiently small number $d _ { 2 } > 0$ there is a $d _ { \mathrm { 1 } } > 0$ such that $V ( x ) < d _ { 2 }$ if $x \in U$ and $\rho ( x , M ) < d _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { 1 } }$ and (iv) $V ( q ( x , t ) )$ is non-increasing for $t \geq 0$ if $q ( x , t )$ remains in $\boldsymbol { U }$

The necessary and sufficient condition for the asymptotic stability of $M$ is the existence in $U$ of a function $V ( x )$ that satisfies,in addition to the foregoing conditions, that (v) $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  0 } V ( q ( x , t ) ) = 0 } \end{array}$ for $q ( x , t ) \in U$ and $t \in [ 0 , + \infty )$

The necessary and sufficient condition for the uniformity of the stability just mentioned,in addition to the foregoing five conditions, isthat (vi） there exists a neighborhood $U ( M , \delta _ { 1 } )$ such that $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  0 } V ( q ( x , t ) ) = 0 } \end{array}$ holds uniformly for every $x \in U ( M , \delta _ { 1 } )$

In order for a closed invariant set $M$ of a dynamical system $\boldsymbol { q } ( \boldsymbol { x } , t )$ to be uniformly attracting,it is necessary and sufficient that all the conditions (i)—(v) should be satisfied and furthermore that (vii) for any quantity $h > 0$ it is possible to find $T > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$ such that

$$
\rho ( x , M ) \geq h V ( q ( x , t ) ) > \alpha
$$

The necessary and sufficient condition for the instability of $M$ is the existence of a function $V ( x )$ with the properties that (i) $V$ is defined and bounded in $M$ ，(ii) in every arbitrarily small neighborhood of $M$ there exist points $_ x$ with $V ( x ) > 0$ ，and (iii) for every point $x$ we have the equation $\dot { V } = g V + W$ with a positive number $g$ where

$$
{ \dot { V } } ( x ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  0 + } { \frac { V ( q ( x , t ) ) - V ( x ) } { t } } ,
$$

and $W$ is a nonnegative function defined in $U$

In order for the trivial solution $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ of the system

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } , t ) \qquad ( i = 1 , \dots , n )
$$

to be unstable, it is necessary and suffcient that there exists a function $V ( x , t )$ defined in the region $B ( T , h ) \colon t \geq T$ ， $0 < | x | \leq h$ ，with the properties that: (i) $V ( x , t )$ is bounded in $B ( T , h )$ ，(ii) there exist points $( x , t ) \in B ( T , h )$ such that $V ( x , t ) > 0$ ，and (ii) $d V ( x ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) , t ) / d t =$ $\lambda V ( x ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) ) \ + \ W ( x ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) , t )$ ，where $\lambda > 0$ ， $W \geq 0$ are defined in $B ( T , h )$

Theorem 2 (Zubov's Theorem): The necessary and sufficient condition for an open invariant set $A \subset R$ containing a certain neighborhood of the closed invariant set $M \subset R$ to be the region of asymptotic stability of a uniformly asymptotically stable and uniformly attracting set $M$ ，is that there exist two functions $V ( x )$ and $\Phi ( x )$ with the properties: (i) the function $V ( x )$ is defined and continuous in $\pmb { A }$ and the function $\Phi ( x )$ is defined and continuous in $\pmb R$ ，(ii) $- 1 < V ( x ) < 0$ for $x \in { \cal A }$ and $\Phi ( x ) > 0$ for $x \in R$ ， $\rho ( x , M ) \neq 0$ and $\Phi ( x ) = 0$ for $x \in M$ ，(ii) for any sufficiently small $d _ { 2 } > 0$ it is possible to find $d _ { \imath }$ and $\alpha _ { 1 }$ such that $V ( x ) < - d _ { 1 }$ for $\rho ( x , M ) \geq d _ { 2 }$ and $\Phi ( x ) > \alpha _ { 1 }$ for $\rho ( x , M ) \geq d _ { 2 }$ ，(iv) the functions $V$ and $\Phi$ tend to zero as $\rho ( x , M ) \to 0$ ， (v） if there exists a point $y \notin M$ ， $y \in { \overline { { A } } } \backslash A$ ，then $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \rho ( x , y ) \to 0 } V ( x ) = } \end{array}$ $^ { - 1 }$ ，and (vi) $\dot { V } | _ { t = 0 } = \Phi ( x ) [ 1 + V ( x ) ]$

The proof of this theorem is rather long. Since the invariant set $M$ is uniformly asymptotically stable,there exist $\delta _ { 1 } > 0$ and $\pmb { L } ( t )$ ， of which the latter is continuous and strictly monotonically decreasing from $+ \infty$ to zero such that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] \equiv L ( t )$ for $\rho ( x , M ) < \delta _ { 1 }$ We examine the functions

$$
\Phi ( x ) = x \exp \{ - L ^ { - 1 } ( x ) \} , \qquad V ( x ) = \exp \left\{ - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \Phi ( q ( x , t ) ) \ d t \right\} \ -
$$

where $\Phi ( q ( x , t ) ) = \Phi [ \rho ( q ( x , t ) , M ) ]$ . We can see from (ii) of the definition of a dynamical system at the beginning of Section 16.5 that

$$
1 + V [ q ( x , t ) ] = [ 1 + V ( x ) ] \exp \left\{ \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \Phi [ q ( x , t ^ { \prime } ) ] d t ^ { \prime } \right\} .
$$

It can be shown that the integral in the exponent converges for $x \in A$ and that the above defined $V ( x )$ represents a function for $x \in A$ . In fact, we can find $T ( x ) \geq 0$ such that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] < \delta _ { 1 }$ for $t \geq T ( x )$ . We then divide the integral in question into two,i.e.,an integral from 0 to $_ T$ and an integral from $_ T$ to $+ \infty$ . Since $\rho ( x _ { 1 } , M ) < \delta _ { 1 }$ with $x _ { 1 } = q ( x , t )$ we have $L ( t ) > \rho [ q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) , M ]$ for $t \geq T ( x )$ ，whence $t < L ^ { - 1 } \{ \rho [ q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) , M ] \}$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \rho [ q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) , M ] e ^ { - t } > \rho [ \rho ( x _ { 1 } , t ) , M ] \exp { \{ - L ^ { - 1 } [ \rho ( q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) , M ) ] \} } } \\ & { \qquad = \Phi [ q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) ] . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \Phi [ q ( x _ { 1 } , \tau ) ] d \tau < \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \rho [ q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) , M ] e ^ { - t } d t < \delta _ { 1 } .
$$

We can show the continuity of

$$
V _ { 1 } ( x ) = - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \Phi [ q ( x , t ^ { \prime } ) ] d t ^ { \prime } , \qquad V _ { 1 } ( x ) = \log { [ 1 + V ( x ) ] } ,
$$

for $x \in A$ , by forming $| V _ { \mathrm { 1 } } ( x ) - V _ { \mathrm { 1 } } ( y ) |$ for $x , y \in A$ . In fact, for a given $\pmb { \varepsilon } _ { 1 }$ we can find an $r _ { 1 }$ such that $\lvert V _ { 1 } ( x ) - V _ { 1 } ( y ) \rvert \le 3 \varepsilon _ { 1 }$ for $\rho ( x , y ) < r _ { 1 } ;$ ， which means that $V ( x )$ is continuous in $\pmb { A }$

Next, for $d _ { 2 } > 0$ we can find a $d _ { 1 } > 0$ such that $V ( x ) < - d _ { 1 }$ for $\rho ( x , M ) > d _ { 2 }$ .In fact,since the set $M$ is uniformly attracting,we can find $T > 0$ ， $\alpha > 0$ for any $c _ { 2 } > 0$ such that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] > \alpha$ for $t \in [ 0 , T ]$ and $\rho ( x , M ) > c _ { 2 }$ .Then we can find $\beta > 0$ such that $\Phi [ q ( x , t ) ] \geq \beta$ for $t \in [ 0 , T ]$ . Hence, from our definition of $V ( x )$ ，it follows that

$$
V ( x ) < \exp \left\{ - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \tau } \beta d t \right\} - 1 < - c _ { 1 } , \qquad c _ { 1 } > 0 .
$$

We can show that $V ( x ) \to 0$ as $\rho ( { \boldsymbol { x } } , { \boldsymbol { M } } ) \to 0$ ，because for a sufficiently small $\varepsilon _ { 1 } > 0$ we can find a $\delta ( \varepsilon _ { 1 } ) > 0$ such that

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \Phi d t \le \varepsilon _ { 1 } \qquad \mathrm { f o r } \qquad \rho ( x , M ) < \delta ( \varepsilon _ { 1 } ) .
$$

Thus $V ( x ) > e ^ { - \varepsilon _ { 1 } } - 1$ as $\rho ( x , M ) < \delta ( \varepsilon _ { 1 } )$ ·

Finally we show that lim $V ( x ) = - 1$ as $\rho ( x , y )  0$ ,where $y \in { \overline { { A } } } \backslash A$ and $y \notin M$ . In fact,by taking $\varepsilon > 0$ ，so that $\delta ( \varepsilon ) > 0$ ，we choose a sequence of points $x _ { n }$ ，such that $x _ { n } \in A$ ， $x _ { n } \to y$ and $\rho ( x _ { n } , M ) > \delta ( \varepsilon )$ for all $_ n$ We define $\tau _ { n }$ by $\rho [ q ( x _ { n } , t ) , M ] > \delta ( \varepsilon ) / 2$ for $t \in [ 0 , \tau _ { n } )$ and $\rho [ q ( x _ { n } , \tau _ { n } ) , M ] = \delta / 2$ . Suppose tentatively that we had $\tau _ { n } < \tau < + \infty$ $( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ . Then we could find an $r _ { 1 } > 0$ for given $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ and $r _ { 2 } > 0$ such that $\rho [ q ( y , t ) , q ( y _ { 1 } , t ) ] < r _ { 2 }$ for $t \in [ 0 , \tau ]$ where $\rho ( y , y _ { 1 } ) < r _ { 1 }$ The quantity $r _ { 2 }$ could be chosen so small that $\rho [ q ( y _ { 1 } , t ) , M ] < \delta / 2 .$ ， $t \in [ 0 , \tau ]$ . The number $N$ is chosen so large that for $n > N$ we should have $\rho ( x _ { n } , y ) < r _ { 1 } .$ and $\rho [ q ( x _ { n } , \tau _ { n } ) , M ] > \delta / 2$ for $n > N$ ，which is impossible. Hence the sequence $\tau _ { n }$ is unbounded,and $\tau _ { n } \to + \infty$ as $n  + \infty$ ·

We find from the property (iv) (Theorem 2) that $\varepsilon > 0$ and $\delta ( \varepsilon )$ is so small that $V ( x ) > - 1 / 2$ for $\rho ( x , M ) = \delta ( \varepsilon ) / 2$ at the same time, $V ( x ) < - c _ { 1 } < 0$ From our definition and (33) we have

$$
1 + V [ q ( x _ { n } , \tau _ { n } ) ] = [ 1 + V ( x _ { n } ) ] \exp \left\{ \int _ { 0 } ^ { \tau _ { n } } \Phi [ q ( x , t ^ { \prime } ) ] d t ^ { \prime } \right\} ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \exp \{ - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \tau _ { n } } \Phi [ q ( x _ { n } , t ^ { \prime } ) ] d t ^ { \prime } \} < 1 + V ( x _ { n } ) } } \\ & { } & { < ( 1 - c _ { 1 } ) \exp \{ - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \tau _ { n } } \Phi [ q ( x _ { n } , t ^ { \prime } ) ] d t ^ { \prime } \} . } \end{array}
$$

By assumption $\Phi [ q ( x _ { n } , t ^ { \prime } ) ] > \alpha > 0$ for $t ^ { \prime } \in [ 0 , \tau _ { n } ]$ . Hence $1 + V ( x _ { n } )$ $ 0$ as $n \to \infty$ . Thus the necessity of the condition of Theorem 2 is established.

As the second step we show that the set $M$ is asymptotically stable. For $\varepsilon > 0$ we find $\begin{array} { r } { \lambda = \operatorname* { s u p } _ { \rho ( x , M ) = \varepsilon } V ( x ) } \end{array}$ .For this $\lambda$ we find a $\delta > 0$ such that for $\rho ( x , M ) < \delta$ we have $V ( x ) > \lambda$ by the property (i). From (vi) we have $V [ q ( x , t ) ] > V ( x )$ for $t > 0$ .If $\rho ( x , M ) < \delta$ ，then $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] < \varepsilon$ for $t \geq 0$ because $V [ q ( x , t ) ] > \lambda$ for $t > 0$

It can be shown that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] \to 0$ as $t \to + \infty$ and for $\rho ( x , M )$ $< \delta$ . In fact, if we assume that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] > \delta ^ { \prime }$ for $t \geq 0$ ，then $\Phi [ q ( x , t ) ] > \beta ( \delta ^ { \prime } )$ .From (vi) we should have $1 + V [ q ( x , t ) ] \geq$ $( 1 + \lambda ) e ^ { \beta t }$ , from which it would follow that $V [ q ( x , t ) ]  + \infty$ as $t \to$ $+ \infty$ ，which is impossible.

For $L > 0$ we can find an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $\Phi ( x ) < L$ for $\rho ( x , M )$ $< \varepsilon$ because $\Phi ( x )  0$ as $\rho ( { \boldsymbol { x } } , M ) \to 0$ Take $0 < h < \delta ( \varepsilon )$ .For a given $h$ ，a $c _ { 1 } ( h )$ can be found such that for $\rho ( x , M ) > h$ we have $V ( x ) < - c _ { 1 } ( h )$ ， $c _ { \mathrm { i } } ( h ) > 0$ . Choose $\tau > 0$ so that $[ 1 - c _ { 1 } ( h ) ] e ^ { \delta \tau } =$ $1 - \gamma , \gamma > 0$ .Consider the set of points $M$ such that $h < \rho ( x , M ) < \delta$ Then from (vi) we have for the points of this set $1 + V [ q ( x , t ) ] \leqq$ $[ 1 + V ( x ) ] e ^ { L \tau }$ ，and hence $V [ q ( x , t ) ] < - \gamma$ for $t \in [ 0 , \tau ]$ . That is, there exists an $\alpha > 0$ such that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] \ge \alpha > 0$ for $t \in [ 0 , \tau ]$ . Thus the set $M$ is uniformly attracting.

We can show that $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] \to 0$ as $t \to + \infty$ . Otherwise we should have $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] > \delta ( \varepsilon )$ and $\Phi [ q ( x , t ) ] > \beta ( \delta )$ for $t > 0$ Applied to the equation (33） this would give $1 + V ( x ) = 0$ ，which is impossible.Hence for $p \in { \cal A }$ we should have $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] \to 0$ as $t \to + \infty$

Let $x _ { 1 } \in R$ ， $x _ { 1 } \notin M$ ; then for $\rho [ q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) , M ] \to 0$ as $t \to + \infty$ we have $x _ { 1 } \in A$ . In fact， since $\rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] \to 0$ ，there is a point $x _ { 2 }$ on the trajectory $q ( x _ { 1 } , t )$ such that $\rho ( x _ { 2 } , M ) < \delta ( \varepsilon )$ and the entire trajectory $q ( x _ { 1 } , t )$ is contained in $\pmb { A }$ ，that is, $q ( x _ { 1 } , t ) \in A$ ，which follows from the invariance of $\pmb { A }$ .

Thus the proof of Zubov's theorem is complete.

Liapounov's second method as applied to the study of the stability of invariant sets of dynamical systems defined in metric space enables us to devise a method of estimating the distance from the motion $q ( x , t )$ to the invariant set $M$ .Assume that outside this set a function $V ( x )$ is defined such that there exist two functions $m _ { 1 } ( x )$ and $m _ { 2 } ( x ) \ ( x \geq 0 )$ continuous and strictly monotonically increasing from zero to $+ \infty$ for $x \in [ 0 , + \infty ]$ ，and satisfying

$$
m _ { 1 } [ \rho ( x , M ) ] \leq V ( x ) \leq m _ { 2 } [ \rho ( x , M ) ] .
$$

Hence

$$
m _ { 1 } [ \rho ( q ( x , t ) , M ) ] \equiv V [ q ( x , t ) ] \equiv m _ { 2 } [ \rho ( q ( x , t ) , M ) ] ,
$$

or

$$
m _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } [ V ( q ( x , t ) ) ] \le \rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] \le m _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } [ V ( q ( x , t ) ) ] .
$$

f $V ( x )$ satisfies (34)，we obtain the estimate (35).

Assume that in a certain neighborhood $U ( M , r )$ of the set $M$ two functions $V _ { 1 }$ and $W _ { 1 }$ are defined such that

$$
a _ { 1 } V ^ { \iota _ { 1 } } \leq V _ { 1 } \leq a _ { 2 } V ^ { \iota _ { 2 } } \qquad \mathrm { f o r } \qquad x \in U ( M , r ) ,
$$

where $a _ { i } , l _ { i } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 )$ are positive constants,

$$
- b _ { 1 } V ^ { k _ { 1 } } \leq W \leq - b _ { 2 } V ^ { k _ { 2 } } \qquad \mathrm { f o r } \qquad x \in U ( M , r ) ,
$$

where $b _ { i } , k _ { i } ( i = 1 , 2 )$ are positive constants,

(i） $V _ { \parallel } [ q ( x , t ) ]$ is continuously differentiable with respect to $t$ for $q ( x , t ) \in U ( M , r )$ and

$$
d V _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } / d t = W ,
$$

(iv） $k _ { i }$ and $l _ { i }$ are related by $\lambda _ { i } = k _ { i } / l _ { i } > 1 ( i = 1 , 2 ) .$

Multiplying (38) by $V _ { \mathrm { 1 } } [ q ( x , t ) ] ^ { - \lambda } \left( \lambda > 1 \right)$ and integrating from O to $t ,$ for $q ( x , t ) \in U ( M , r )$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { V _ { 1 } [ q ( x , t ) ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phantom { \sum } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phantom { \sum } } } \end{array}
$$

Choose $\lambda _ { 1 } > 1 , \lambda _ { 2 } > 1$ and apply (36), (37) to this equation; then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { V _ { 1 } ( x ) [ 1 + ( \lambda _ { 1 } - 1 ) V _ { 1 } ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } - 1 } ( x ) b _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } } t ] ^ { - ( \lambda _ { 1 } - 1 ) } \leq V _ { 1 } [ q ( x , t ) ] } \\ & { \qquad \leq V _ { 1 } ( x ) [ 1 + ( \lambda _ { 2 } - 1 ) V _ { 1 } ^ { \lambda _ { 2 } - 1 } ( x ) b _ { 2 } a _ { 2 } ^ { - \lambda _ { 2 } } t ] ^ { - ( \lambda _ { 2 } - 1 ) } , } \end{array}
$$

or, by (36),

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { [ a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } V ^ { l _ { 1 } } ] ^ { 1 / l _ { 2 } } [ 1 + ( \lambda _ { 1 } - 1 ) ( a _ { 2 } V ^ { l _ { 2 } } ) ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } - 1 } b _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } ^ { - \lambda _ { 1 } } t ] ^ { - ( \lambda _ { 1 } - 1 ) } \le V [ q ( x , t ) ] } \\ & { \qquad \le [ a _ { 2 } a _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } V ^ { l _ { 2 } } ] ^ { 1 / l _ { 1 } } [ 1 + ( \lambda _ { 2 } - 1 ) ( a _ { 1 } V ^ { l _ { 1 } } ) ^ { \lambda _ { 2 } - 1 } b _ { 2 } a _ { 2 } ^ { - \lambda _ { 2 } } t ] ^ { - ( \lambda _ { 2 } - 1 ) } . } \end{array}
$$

Next let $\lambda _ { 1 } = 1$ ， $\lambda _ { 2 } > 1$ ； then,multiplying (38) by $V [ q ( x , t ) ] ^ { - 1 }$ and integrating,we obtain

$$
V _ { \mathrm { 1 } } [ q ( x , t ) ] = V _ { \mathrm { 1 } } ( x ) \exp \left\{ \int _ { \mathrm { 0 } } ^ { t } W V ^ { - 1 } d \tau \right\} .
$$

By (36) and (37) this equation (42) becomes

$$
( a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } V ^ { l _ { 1 } } ( x ) \exp { [ - b _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } t ] } ) ^ { l _ { 2 } } \leq V [ q ( x , t ) ] \leq g ( x , t ) ,
$$

where $g ( x , t )$ denotes the right-hand member of (41).

Now let $\lambda _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 2 } = 1$ ； then,again applying (36) and (37) to (42), we find

$$
\begin{array} { r } { g _ { 1 } ( x , t ) \equiv V [ g ( x , t ) ] \stackrel { } { \equiv } ( a _ { 2 } a _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } V ^ { l _ { 2 } } ( x ) \exp { [ - b _ { 2 } a _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } t ] } ) ^ { l _ { 1 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $g _ { 1 } ( x , t )$ denotes the left-hand member of (43).Then we obtain the following theorems :

Theorem 3: If in the neighborhood $U ( M , r )$ of a closed invariant set $M$ of a dynamical system $q ( x , t )$ there exist two functions $V _ { 1 }$ and $W$ ，with the properties (i)-(iv) of Theorem 2, then the invariant set $M$ is asymptotically stable.For any motion $q ( x , t ) \in U ( M , r )$ we have (i) the inequality (41) for $\lambda _ { 1 } > 1 , \lambda _ { 2 } > 1$ ，(ii) the inequality (43） for $\lambda _ { 1 } = 1$ ， $\lambda _ { 2 } > 1$ ,and (ii) the inequality (44) for $\lambda _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 2 } = 1$ ·

Theorem 4: In order for the closed invariant set $M$ to be asymptotically stable and,at the same time,in order to have the estimates

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { c _ { 1 } V ( x ) [ 1 + c _ { 2 } t V ^ { l - 1 } ( x ) ] ^ { - ( l - 1 ) } \le V [ q ( x , t ) ] \quad ~ } & { } \\ { \le d _ { 1 } V ( x ) [ 1 + d _ { 2 } t V ^ { l - 1 } ( x ) ] ^ { - ( l - 1 ) } \quad \quad ( l > 1 ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { p _ { 1 } V ( x ) e ^ { - p _ { 2 } t } \le V [ q ( x , t ) ] \le q _ { 1 } V ( x ) e ^ { - q _ { 2 } t } , } & { \quad } \\ { \quad t > 0 , \quad } & { \rho [ q ( x , t ) , M ] < r \quad \quad ( l = 1 ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $c _ { i } , p _ { i } , q _ { i } , d _ { i } \ ( i = 1 , 2 )$ are positive constants, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist in a certain neighborhood $U ( M , r )$ of the set $M$ two functions $V _ { 1 }$ and $W$ ， satisfying the following conditions :

$$
\begin{array} { r } { a _ { 1 } V ( x ) \le V _ { 1 } ( x ) \le a _ { 2 } V ( x ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
- b _ { 1 } V ^ { l } ( x ) \le W ( x ) \le - b _ { 2 } V ^ { l } ( x ) , \qquad l \ge 1 , a _ { i } , b _ { i } > 0 ,
$$

and

(ii） $V _ { \mathrm { 1 } } [ q ( x , t ) ]$ is continuously differentiable for $q ( x , t ) \in U ( M , r )$ and $d V _ { \mathrm { 1 } } / d t = W$

For showing the necessity of the conditions we suppose that the closed invariant set $M$ is asymptotically stable and that the estimates (45) and (46) are satisfied. Put $W = - V ^ { \ l }$ ,and take $r > \varepsilon > 0$ From the definition of stability we can find a $\delta > 0$ such that for $\rho ( x , M )$ $< \delta$ we have $q ( x , t ) \in U ( M , r )$ for $t \geq 0$ for this $\varepsilon$ .Hence such motions satisfy either (45) or (46).Put

$$
V _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } ( x ) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } V ^ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathit { l } } [ q ( x , t ) ] d t
$$

for $x \in { U ( M , \delta ) }$ and let $\lambda > 1$ .By (45) this equation becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { c _ { 1 } ^ { \iota } V ^ { \iota } ( x ) \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } [ 1 + c _ { 2 } t V ^ { \iota - 1 } ( x ) ] ^ { - [ \iota / ( \iota - 1 ) ] } d t \leq V _ { 1 } ( x ) } \\ & { } & { \leq d _ { 1 } ^ { \iota } V ^ { \iota } ( x ) \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } [ 1 + d _ { 2 } t V ^ { \iota - 1 } ( x ) ] ^ { - [ \iota / ( \iota - 1 ) ] } d t . } \end{array}
$$

Write

$$
1 + c _ { 2 } t V ^ { \imath - 1 } ( x ) = \theta _ { 1 } , \qquad 1 + d _ { 2 } t V ^ { \imath - 1 } ( x ) = \theta _ { 2 } ;
$$

then we get

$$
a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } V ( x ) \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \theta _ { 1 } ^ { - \left[ l / ( l - 1 ) \right] } d \theta _ { 1 } \leq V _ { 1 } ( x ) \leq a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } V ( x ) \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \theta _ { 2 } ^ { - \left[ l / ( l - 1 ) \right] } d \theta _ { 2 } .
$$

It follows that such a $V _ { \mathfrak { r } } ( x )$ satisfies the condition (i) of Theorem 4.

Similarly the conditions (ii) and (iii) are shown to be satisfied.

For showing the sufficiency of these conditions we consider two functions $V _ { 1 }$ and $W$ satisfying the conditions (i)-(iii) in a certain neighborhood $U ( M , r )$ of the set $M$ . These functions are particular cases of the functions in Theorem 3 for $l _ { 1 } = l _ { 2 } = 1$ ， $k _ { 1 } = k _ { 2 } = l$

The equilibrium position $x = 0$ of a vector differential equation

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = f ( x , t )
$$

is called exponentially stable, if there exist two positive constants $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ ，independent of the initial values,such that for suffciently small initial values we have

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \beta | \xi | \exp { \{ - \alpha ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } .
$$

The equilibrium point is called exponentially unstable if there exist two fixed positive numbers $\alpha , \beta$ and the initial points $( \xi , t _ { 0 } )$ in any domain $K _ { h , t _ { 0 } } \colon | x | \leq h$ ， $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，with arbitrarily small $h$ and arbitrarily large $t _ { 0 }$ such that

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | > \beta | \xi | \exp { \{ \alpha ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } \qquad ( t \geq \mathfrak { t } _ { 0 } \mathrm { o r } t \leq t _ { 0 } ) .
$$

If this relation holds for all initial values $\xi$ with sufficiently small· absolute values,then the equilibrium point is said to be completely exponentially unstable.

The motion defined by the differential equations (49) shows intensive behavior if any motion can be estimated

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | > \beta | \xi | \exp { \{ \alpha ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } , \qquad t \geq t _ { 0 } ,
$$

with $\alpha > 0 , \beta > 0$ . In the case of exponential stability or exponential instability the motion may be said to show pregnant behavior.

Theorem 5(Krasovskii's Theorem): For a motion to show intensive behavior it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a Liapounov's function satisfying

$$
V ( x , t ) < a _ { 1 } | x | ^ { \tau } , \qquad | \dot { V } | > a _ { 2 } | x | ^ { \tau } ,
$$

where $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \gamma$ are positive constants.

If a motion shows intensive behavior, then we have besides (52)

$$
\left| { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { i } } } \right| < a _ { 3 } | x | ^ { r - 1 } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ; a _ { 3 } > 0 , r > 1 ) .
$$

In fact, supposing that (52) are satisfied and that there exist the initial values such that $V ( \xi , t _ { 0 } ) > 0$ ， $\dot { V } ( \xi , t _ { 0 } ) \geq 0$ ，we introduce an increasing function $V ( t ) = V [ p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) , t ]$ .From (52) we have $V ( t ) \geq $ $a _ { 2 } | \xi | ^ { \tau } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) + V ( t _ { 0 } )$ . Hence we can choose $\tau > 0$ so that for $t _ { 1 } = t _ { 0 }$ $+ \tau$ we have $V ( t _ { 1 } ) > a _ { 4 } | \xi | ^ { \prime }$ ， $a _ { 4 } > 0$ . Then for $t > t _ { 1 }$ we have

$V ( t ) > V ( t _ { 1 } ) \exp { \{ a _ { s } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } = V ( t _ { 1 } ) \exp { \{ a _ { s } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } \cdot \exp { \{ - a _ { s } \tau \} } ,$ since

$$
\dot { V } ( t ) / V ( t ) > a _ { 2 } / a _ { 1 } = a _ { 5 } .
$$

Furthermore, for $t > t _ { 1 }$ we have

$$
a _ { 1 } | p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | ^ { r } > a _ { 4 } \exp { \{ - \tau a _ { 5 } \} } \cdot | \xi | ^ { r } \exp { \{ a _ { 5 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } ,
$$

since $a _ { 1 } | p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | ^ { \tau } > V ( t )$ . The last inequality is of the type (51).

If $V ( t _ { 0 } )$ and $\dot { V } ( t _ { 0 } )$ have the opposite sign, then only in the computation the sign is changed,while the inequality sign is reversed in the last inequality.We obtain

$$
| p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \beta | \xi | \exp { \{ - \alpha ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } \qquad ( t \geq t _ { 0 } ) .
$$

For showing the sufficiency we refer to the following theorem:

Theorem 6: Suppose that $\bar { W } ( x , t ) \in C _ { 1 }$ is defined in the space of motion, in which the motion takes place,and put

$$
V ( x , t ) = \int _ { t } ^ { t + h } W [ p ( \tau , x , t ) , \tau ] d \tau ,
$$

where $p$ remains on the integration curve in the space of motion, then for the motion represented by (49) we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \dot { V } = W [ p ( t + h , x , t ) , t + h ] - W ( x , t ) . } \end{array}
$$

In fact, if $\dot { V }$ is formed from the equation (49), then there appear, in addition to (53),two other terms whose integrand is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial W } { \partial p _ { i } } \dot { p } _ { i } + \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } = \sum _ { i , k } \frac { \partial W } { \partial p _ { k } } \frac { \partial p _ { k } } { \partial x _ { i } } \dot { x } _ { i } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial W } { \partial p _ { k } } \frac { \partial p _ { k } } { \partial t } } \\ { = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial W } { \partial p _ { k } } \bigg ( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial p _ { k } } { \partial x _ { i } } f _ { i } + \frac { \partial p _ { k } } { \partial t } \bigg ) , } \end{array}
$$

which vanish for all $k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n$ identically. Hence only the terms in (55) are left.

We obtain $V ( x , t )$ satisfying (51), (52), (53) with $\gamma = 2$ if we apply this theorem with $W = | x | ^ { 2 }$ .For exponential stability we set $h = \infty$ . The constructed function is positive.Put $| p | ^ { 2 } = \xi ( \tau )$ ，then

$$
\frac { d \xi } { d \tau } = 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } p _ { i } \frac { d p _ { i } } { d \tau } = 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } p _ { i } f _ { i } .
$$

Since $f ( x , t )$ is bounded in ${ { K } _ { h , t _ { 0 } } }$ we have

$$
| \xi ^ { \prime } | = \left| \frac { d \hat { \xi } } { d \tau } \right| < c | p | = c \sqrt { \bar { \xi } } \qquad ( c > 0 ) .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { V ( x , t ) = \int _ { \tau = t } ^ { \tau = \infty } | p ( \tau , x , t ) | ^ { 2 } d \tau } } \\ { \displaystyle { \quad = \int _ { \xi = | x | ^ { 2 } } ^ { \xi = 0 } \frac { \xi } { \xi ^ { \prime } } d \xi \ge \frac { 1 } { c } \int _ { 0 } ^ { | x | ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { \xi } d \xi > c _ { 1 } | x | ^ { 3 } \qquad ( c _ { 1 } > 0 ) , } } \end{array}
$$

i.e., $V$ is positive-definite. From (50) we have

$$
V ( x , t ) < | x | ^ { 2 } \beta ^ { 2 } \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \alpha ( \tau - t ) } \ d \tau < c _ { 2 } | x | ^ { 2 } ,
$$

which shows that $V ( x , t )$ is decrescent.

For unstable equilibrium points we choose $h > ( 1 / \alpha ) \log { ( 1 / \beta ) }$ .In this case Krasovskii gave

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { V ( x , t ) = \displaystyle \int _ { t + h } ^ { t } | p ( \tau , x , t ) | ^ { 2 } d \tau + \displaystyle \int _ { t - h } ^ { t } | p ( \tau , x , t ) | ^ { 2 } d \tau , } } \\ { { h = \displaystyle \frac { 4 } { \alpha } \log \frac { 2 } { \beta } , } } \end{array}
$$

which increases more rapidly on one branch than on the other.

If,besides the estimates (51) and (52), we still have

$$
V > a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | x | ^ { \tau } \qquad \mathrm { a n d } \qquad | \dot { V } | < a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } | x | ^ { \tau } ,
$$

then we can estimate the motion from the upper and the lower sides. For example,analogously to the inequality (44) for $t > t _ { 1 } = t _ { 0 } + \tau$ we obtain an inequality

$$
\dot { V } / V < a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } / a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = a _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } , ~ V < V ( t _ { 1 } ) \exp { \{ a _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } ( t - t _ { 1 } ) \} } ,
$$

and, also analogously,

$$
a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | ^ { \tau } < a _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } | \hat { \xi } | ^ { \tau } \exp { \{ - a _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } \tau \} } \cdot \exp { \{ a _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } .
$$

We obtain the generalization of Krasovskii's theorem:

Theorem 7: For every motion at least on one branch to admit an estimate of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \beta _ { 1 } | \xi | \exp { \{ \alpha | t - t _ { 0 } \} } < | p ( t , \xi , t _ { 0 } ) | < \beta _ { 2 } | \xi | \exp { \{ \alpha | t - t _ { 0 } \} } , } \end{array}
$$

it is necessary and sufficient that there exists a function $V$ that satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | x | ^ { \tau } < V ( x , t ) < a _ { 1 } | x | ^ { \tau } , } } \\ { { a _ { 2 } | x | ^ { \tau } < | \dot { V } ( x , t ) | < a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } | x | ^ { \tau } , } } \end{array}
$$

for sufficiently small $| x |$

# 16.6LIAPOUNOV'S FUNCTIONS

Let $V$ be a Liapounov's function. If $V { \dot { V } } \leq 0$ ，then the motion is stable,otherwise it is unstable.The generation of Liapounov's func-

tions is not always possible.No universally applicable method is known (Dearman and Le May,1966).

We begin with linear autonomous systems for which relatively simple methods are known for determining stability.

Let $V ( X )$ be a homogeneous quadratic form

$$
V ( X ) = X ^ { \prime } A X = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i j } x _ { i } x _ { j } ,
$$

where $\pmb { A }$ is an $n \times n$ symmetric matrix of constant elements $\alpha _ { i j }$ ，and $X ^ { \prime }$ is the transposed of $X$ . We have

$$
\dot { V } ( X ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { i } } \dot { x } _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { i j } x _ { i } x _ { j } = X ^ { \prime } B X ,
$$

where $\pmb { B }$ is an $n \times n$ matrix of constant elements $\beta _ { i , j }$ . For example, consider $\ddot { X } + a \dot { X } + b X = 0 .$ ，where $^ { a }$ and $^ { b }$ are real constants, constrain $\dot { V }$ to be positive-definite,and find $\alpha _ { i j }$ and $\beta _ { i j }$ so that $\dot { V }$ and $V$ satisfy either stability or instability criteria. We construct the quadratic $V$ and insert the differential equations $\dot { x } _ { 1 } = - b x _ { 2 }$ ， $\dot { x } _ { 2 } = x _ { 1 } -$ $a x _ { 2 }$ after differentiating with respect to $t$ ，and put

2 $b \alpha _ { 1 2 } = 1 , 2 \alpha _ { 1 2 } - 2 a \alpha _ { 2 2 } = - 1 , 2 \alpha _ { 1 1 } - 2 a \alpha _ { 1 2 } - 2 b \alpha _ { 2 2 } = 0 ,$ then we get

$$
\dot { V } = - x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

and

$$
V = \frac { a ^ { 2 } + b ( b + 1 ) } { 2 a b } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { b } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } + \frac { b + 1 } { 2 a b } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

which is of the form $V = X ^ { \prime } A X$ .The function $V$ is positive-definite if and only if

$$
\frac { \iota ^ { 2 } + b ( b + 1 ) } { 2 a b } > 0 , ~ \Big [ \frac { a ^ { 2 } + b ( b + 1 ) } { 2 a b } \Big ] \Big ( \frac { b + 1 } { 2 a b } \Big ) - \Big ( \frac { 1 } { 2 b } \Big ) ^ { 2 } > 0 .
$$

These inequalities require that $a > 0 , b > 0$

Suppose that the given differential equations have small nonlinearity, such as

$$
\ddot { X } + a \dot { X } + f ( X ) = 0 ,
$$

where $f ( x )$ is small. Let $f ( x )$ be $f ( x ) = h x + g ( x )$ with a small positive constant $h$ . The given differential equation is transformed to $\dot { x } _ { 1 } =$ $- h x _ { 2 } , { \dot { x } } _ { 2 } = x _ { 1 } - a x _ { 2 } - q ( x _ { 1 } )$ ，where $q ( x _ { \mathrm { 1 } } )$ is small. By putting

$$
\alpha _ { 1 1 } = \frac { a ^ { 2 } + h ( h + 1 ) } { 2 a h } , ~ \alpha _ { 1 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 h } , ~ \alpha _ { 2 2 } = \frac { h + 1 } { 2 a h } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
V = \frac { a ^ { 2 } + h ( h + 1 ) } { 2 a h } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { h } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } + \frac { h + 1 } { 2 a h } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
- \dot { V } = \frac { f ( x _ { 1 } ) } { h x _ { 1 } } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \Big ( - \frac { 1 + h } { a } + \frac { 1 + h } { a h } \frac { f ( x _ { 1 } ) } { x _ { 1 } } \Big ) x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

The derivative $\dot { V }$ is positive-definite if and only if

$$
\frac { f ( x _ { 1 } ) } { h x _ { 1 } } > 0 , \quad \frac { f ( x _ { 1 } ) } { h x _ { 1 } } > \frac { 1 } { 4 } \biggl [ - \frac { ( h x _ { 1 } - f ( x _ { 1 } ) ) ( 1 + h ) } { 2 h x _ { 1 } } \biggr ] ^ { 2 } ,
$$

that is,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { + 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 2 a ^ { 2 } - 2 \sqrt { a ^ { 4 } + a ^ { 2 } ( h + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } { ( h + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } < \displaystyle \frac { f ( x _ { 1 } ) } { h x _ { 1 } } } } \\ { { < \displaystyle \frac { ( h + 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 2 a ^ { 2 } + 2 \sqrt { a ^ { 4 } + a ^ { 2 } ( h + 1 ) } } { ( h + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } } } \end{array}
$$

This method is due to Aizerman.

In order that the trivial solution of ${ \dot { X } } = F ( X )$ be globally asymptotically stable, it is sufficient that there exists a positive symmetric matrix $A = ( a _ { i j } )$ with positive eigenvalues,such that the symmetric matrix $C = ( c _ { i j } )$ ，

$$
c _ { i j } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \alpha _ { i k } \frac { \partial f _ { k } } { \partial x _ { j } } + \alpha _ { j k } \frac { \partial f _ { k } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) ,
$$

has eigenvalues $\lambda _ { i } ( X ) \ ( i = 1 , \ldots , n )$ uniformly satisfying the conditions $\lambda _ { i } < - \gamma \left( \gamma > 0 ; i = 1 , \dots , n \right)$ for all $x _ { i }$ ，where $\gamma$ is a real constant.From this theorem we construct

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { V = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i j } f _ { i } f _ { j } = F ^ { \prime } A F , } } & { { } } & { { F = ( f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n } ) , \nonumber } } \\ { { \dot { V } = F ^ { \prime } ( A J + J ^ { \prime } A ) F , } } & { { } } & { { J = \left( \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

This method is due to Krasovskii (1963).

Zubov's method (Zubov，1964；Hahn，1959) is based on the following theorem: Let $\Delta$ be an open domain，containing the origin of the phase space,an $_ n$ -dimensional Euclidean space of the coordinates $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ,and let $\bar { \Delta }$ be its closure.The domain $\Delta$ is the exact domain of attraction of the equilibrium solution of ${ \dot { X } } = F ( X )$ if and only if there exist two functions $V ( X )$ and $\Phi ( X )$ with the properties : (i $V ( X )$ is defined and continuous in $\Delta$ ，and $\Phi ( X )$ is defined and continuous in the whole of the phase space,(ii) $\Phi ( X )$ is positivedefinite for all $X$ ，(iii) $V ( X )$ is negative-definite and for $X \in \Delta , X \neq 0$ ， the relation $- 1 < V ( X ) < 0$ holds，(iv）if $Y \in \overline { { \Delta } } - \Delta$ then $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { X \to Y } V ( X ) = - 1 } \end{array}$ ，(v) $\ \operatorname* { l i m } _ { | X | \to \infty } V ( X ) = - 1$ provided that this limit exists for $X \in \Delta$ ,and (vi) $V$ satisfies the partial differential equation

$$
\frac { \vert V [ P ( X , t , 0 ) ] } { d t } \bigg \vert _ { t = 0 } \equiv \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { i } } f _ { i } ( X ) = \Phi ( X ) [ 1 + V ( X ) ] \sqrt { 1 + F ^ { 2 } } (
$$

For example, consider

$$
\dot { x } _ { 1 } = - x _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } , \qquad \dot { x } _ { 2 } = - x _ { 2 } ;
$$

then the partial differential equation becomes

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \left( 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right) - \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 2 } } x _ { 2 } } \\ { = \displaystyle \Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ) ( 1 + V ) \sqrt { 1 + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + ( 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

We choose

$$
\Phi ( X ) = \Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ) = \frac { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 1 + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + ( 2 x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

then the solution of this partial differential equation is

$$
V ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ) = - 1 + \exp \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 1 - x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ) } \right\} .
$$

Thus $V$ is negative-definite and $\dot { V }$ is positive-definite.The boundary of stability is given by $x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } = 1$

Ingwerson's method (1961) for nonlinear systems is based on the derivation of Liapounov's functions for linear autonomous systems. Consider a linear system with constant coefficients

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } } + a _ { 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } x } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { n - 1 } { \frac { d x } { d t } } + a _ { n } x = 0
$$

and substitute $x _ { 1 } = x$ $\mathbf { \Psi } = x , x _ { 2 } = d x / d t , \ldots , x _ { n } = d ^ { n - 1 } x / d t ^ { n - 1 }$ ，then

$$
\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \dot { x } _ { 1 } } \\ { \dot { x } _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { \dot { x } _ { n } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c c c } { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { \dots } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x _ { 1 } } \\ { x _ { 2 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { x _ { n } } \end{array} \right] ,
$$

or

$$
{ \dot { X } } = B X .
$$

The Liapounov function

$$
V = X ^ { \prime } A X \qquad ( A ^ { \prime } = A )
$$

for this linear system possesses

$$
\dot { V } = X ^ { \prime } ( B ^ { \prime } A + A B ) X ,
$$

where $\pmb { A }$ and $\pmb { B }$ are $n \times n$ matrices with constant elements. The trivial solution $X = 0$ is asymptotically stable if and only if there exists a symmetric,positive-definite matrix $\pmb { A }$ which is the unique solution of the equation for $C$ ：

$$
B ^ { \prime } A + A B = - C ,
$$
