where $C$ is any symmetric,positive-definite matrix.Furthermore, it is known that $\pmb { A }$ is positive-definite if and only if the real parts of the eigenvalues of $B$ are negative.

Ingwerson relaxes the restriction of negative-definiteness for $\dot { V }$ to negative semi-definiteness,in which only one of the eigenvalues of $c$ need be nonzero.However,a necessary and sufficient condition that the eigenvalues of $\pmb { A }$ be positive is no longer that the eigenvalues of $\pmb { B }$ have negative real parts.Let $B$ be any real matrix which,under any permutation of its rows accompanied by the same permutation of its columns,cannot be partitioned into

$$
B = { \binom { B _ { 1 } } { 0 } } , \quad B _ { 2 } \Biggr ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { { \cal B } } _ { 3 }$ is a square matrix. Then the solution of the equation for $c$ is positive-definite if and only if the eigenvalues of $\pmb { B }$ have negative real parts and $C$ is positive-semi-definite and symmetric.This is a modified Liapounov method devised by Vogt,Li,and Ingwerson (1962).

Let the nonlinear autonomous matrix differential equation

$$
{ \dot { X } } = F ( X )
$$

be differentiated,to give

$$
\ddot { \pmb { X } } = \pmb { B } ( \pmb { X } ) \dot { \pmb { X } } ,
$$

where $B ( X )$ isan $n \times n$ matrix with elements $\partial f _ { i } / \partial x _ { j } \ ( i , j =$ $1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ . In the equation for $C$ Ingwerson replaces the constant matrix $\pmb { B }$ by $B ( X )$ and $\pmb { A }$ by $\pmb { A } ( \pmb { X } )$ ，and solves for $\pmb { A } ( X )$ . In the linear system $\dot { X } = B X$ ，the matrix $\pmb { A }$ issymmetric with elements ${ \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \partial ^ { 2 } V / \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } )$ . If this could be made true for $A ( X )$ by some artifice, then a Liapounov function could be formed by integration. The matrix $A ( X )$ is symmetric ；also

$$
\frac { \partial A _ { i j } } { \partial x _ { k } } = \frac { \partial A _ { i k } } { \partial x _ { j } } \qquad ( i , j , k = 1 , 2 , \dots , n )
$$

should hold for all elements $A _ { i j } , A _ { i k }$ of $A ( X )$ . In general this relation does not hold for matrices satisfying the equation for $C$ with $B ( X )$ replacing $\pmb { B }$

Ingwerson's procedure is to generate a matrix $A ( x _ { i } , x _ { j } )$ from $\pmb { A } ( X )$ by equating to zero all variables $x _ { k }$ in each element $\boldsymbol { A } _ { i j }$ of $A ( X )$ except those where the index $k$ is the same as.the index of either the row or column of $A ( X )$ in which $x _ { k }$ appears,and then evaluate

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { x } A ( x _ { i } , x _ { j } ) d X \equiv \nabla V ,
$$

where it is intended that the integration of each element in the jth column of $A ( x _ { i } , x _ { j } )$ be made with respect to $x _ { j } \ ( j = 1 , \ldots , n )$ ，while the other components of $X$ are held constant. The result is a vector $\nabla V$ of $_ n$ components such that

$$
\nabla \times \nabla V = 0 .
$$

Hence $\nabla V$ is the gradient of some scalar function $V$ determined by

$$
V = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } \nabla V \cdot d X
$$

along any path of integration.

The variable-gradient method of generating $V$ -functions devised by Schultz and Gibson (1962) is based on the existence of the gradient $\nabla V$ of a $V .$ -function with undetermined components

$$
\dot { V } = ( \Delta V ) ^ { \prime } \dot { X } , \qquad V = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } ( \Delta V ) ^ { \prime } d X .
$$

In order that $V$ be uniquely determined, it is suffcient to have

$$
\nabla \times \nabla V = 0 .
$$

This is equivalent to the expression showing the symmetric character of the matrix

$$
M = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \nabla V _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial \nabla V _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \frac { \partial \nabla V _ { n } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } \\ { \displaystyle \cdot \cdot } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \nabla V _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { n } } \frac { \partial \nabla V _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { n } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \frac { \partial \nabla V _ { n } } { \partial x _ { n } } } \end{array} \right] .
$$

Suppose that the proposed differential equations are

$$
B ( X ) = \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l l } { \dot { X } = B ( X ) X , } & { \dot { X } ( 0 ) = 0 , } & & & \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } & { \cdots } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } & & { 0 } \\ { \cdots } & & & & { \Big | , } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 0 } & & { 1 } \end{array} \right| ,
$$

where the $b _ { i } ( X )$ are assumed to be continuous in a region of phase space. The first step is to assume $\nabla V$ to be a column $_ n$ -vector of the form

$$
\nabla V = { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { a _ { 1 1 } x _ { 1 } + \dots + a _ { 1 n } x _ { n } } \\ { a _ { 2 1 } x _ { 1 } + \dots + a _ { 2 n } x _ { n } } \\ { \dots } \\ { { \big \lfloor } a _ { n 1 } x _ { 1 } + \dots + a _ { n n } x _ { n } { \big \rfloor } } \end{array} \right] } ,
$$

where the $a _ { i j }$ are restricted to be functions of $x _ { i }$ alone, except $a _ { n n }$ which is set equal to a constant,2. The $a _ { i j }$ are so chosen that they are positive and consist of constant parts $a _ { i j , c }$ and variable parts $a _ { i j , v }$

$$
a _ { i j } = a _ { i j , c } + a _ { i j , v } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } ) ,
$$

and that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { V ( x ) = x _ { n } ^ { 2 } + 2 \beta ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } ) x _ { n } + \alpha ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { = ( x _ { n } + \beta ) ^ { 2 } + \alpha - \beta ^ { 2 } ; } } \end{array}
$$

or，accordingly， that $V ( x )$ is positive-definite if $\alpha - \beta ^ { 2 }$ is positivedefinite. Thus we get

$$
\nabla V = { \binom { ( a _ { 1 1 , c } + a _ { 1 1 , v } ) x _ { 1 } + \dots + ( a _ { 1 n , c } + a _ { 1 n , v } ) x _ { n } } { \dots } } ,
$$

where the $a _ { i j , v }$ are functions of $x _ { i }$ alone.We choose $a _ { i j }$ to be positive and $a _ { n n } = 2$ . The variable-gradient method proceeds by successive steps:(i) write $\nabla V$ in this matrix form,(ii) derive $\dot { V }$ from $\dot { V } =$ $( \boldsymbol { \nabla } V ) ^ { \prime } \dot { \boldsymbol { X } }$ ， (iii) constrain $\dot { V }$ to be at least semi-definite,(iv) use the $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } n ( n - 1 )$ equations in $\nabla \times \nabla V = 0$ for determining the remaining unknown coefficients in $\nabla V$ in this matrix equation,(v) determine whether $\dot { V }$ is still at least semi-definite because the addition of terms as a result of carrying out the step (iv) may alter the situation,and (vi) calculate $V$ according to

$$
V = \int _ { 0 } ^ { X } ( \nabla V ) ^ { \prime } d X
$$

and test for positive-definiteness (see La Salle and Lefschetz,1961).

The method of Ku and Puri (Puri and Ku,1963；Puri and Weygandt, 1963) represents another attempt to generate Liapounov's functions.The function is assumed to be a quadratic form $V = X ^ { \prime } S X$ ， where $s$ is an $n \times n$ symmetric matrix such as

$$
S = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l l l } { \displaystyle { \frac { 3 c _ { 1 1 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 1 } } { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 c _ { 1 2 } x _ { 1 } + f _ { 1 2 } } { 2 x _ { 1 } } } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 c _ { 1 3 } x _ { 1 } + f _ { 1 3 } } { 2 x _ { 1 } } } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 c _ { 1 4 } x _ { 1 } + f _ { 1 4 } } { 2 x _ { 1 } } } } \\ { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 c _ { 1 2 } x _ { 1 } + f _ { 1 2 } } { 2 x _ { 1 } } } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { 3 c _ { 2 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + z _ { 2 2 } } { x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } } } & { c _ { 2 3 } } & { c _ { 2 4 } } \\ { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 c _ { 1 3 } x _ { 1 } + f _ { 1 3 } } { 2 x _ { 1 } } } } & { c _ { 2 3 } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { 3 c _ { 3 3 } x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + z _ { 3 3 } } { x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } } & { c _ { 3 4 } } \\ { \displaystyle { \frac { 2 c _ { 1 4 } x _ { 1 } + f _ { 1 4 } } { 2 x _ { 1 } } } } & { c _ { 2 4 } } & { c _ { 3 4 } } & { \displaystyle { \frac { 3 c _ { 4 4 } x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + z _ { 4 4 } } { x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } } \end{array} \right] ,
$$

where

$$
z _ { i j } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { j } } z _ { j } ( x _ { j } ) \mathop { } { d { x _ { j } } } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 )
$$

and $f _ { 1 2 } , f _ { 1 3 } , f _ { 1 4 }$ are taken as functions of $x _ { 1 }$ alone and $c _ { i j }$ are constants. It is assumed that the nonlinear system is of the form ${ \dot { X } } = A ( X ) X$ ， where $\pmb { A } ( X )$ is the $n \times n$ matrix

$$
\begin{array} { r } { A ( X ) = \left[ { \begin{array} { l l l l l l } { \ 0 } & { 1 } & { \ 0 } & { \ 0 } \\ { \ 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } & { \ 0 } \\ { \ \cdots } & { \ } & { \ } & { \ } & { \ } \\ { \ 0 } & { \ 0 } & { \ 0 } & { \ 1 } \\ { \ - a _ { 1 } ( X ) } & { - a _ { 2 } ( X ) } & { - a _ { 3 } ( X ) } & { \ \cdots } & { - a _ { n } ( X ) } \end{array} } \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Differentiating $\dot { V } = X ^ { \prime } S X$ with ${ \dot { X } } = A ( X ) X$ ，we obtain

$$
\dot { V } = X ^ { \prime } ( A ^ { \prime } S + S A + \dot { S } ) X .
$$

If $\beta _ { i j }$ denotes an element of $s$ ，then $V$ may be written for a fourthorder system

$$
\sum _ { i , j } \beta _ { i j } x _ { i } x _ { j } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \dot { V } = ( \nabla V ) ^ { \prime } \dot { X } . } \end{array}
$$

Ku and Puri set

$$
\nabla V = B \boldsymbol { X } ,
$$

where

$$
\mathbf { \Sigma } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l l l } { \underline { { 6 } c _ { 1 1 } x _ { 1 } + z _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } ) } } & { 2 c _ { 1 2 } + \bar { \jmath } _ { 1 2 } ^ { \prime } ( x _ { 1 } ) } & { 2 c _ { 1 3 } + \bar { \jmath } _ { 1 3 } ^ { \prime } ( x _ { 1 } ) } & { 2 c _ { 1 4 } + \bar { \jmath } _ { 1 4 } ^ { \prime } ( x _ { 1 } ) } \\ { \quad x _ { 1 } } & { } & { } & { } \\ { \underline { { 2 c _ { 1 2 } x _ { 1 } + \bar { \jmath } _ { 1 2 } ( x _ { 1 } ) } } } & { \underline { { 6 } c _ { 2 2 } x _ { 2 } + z _ { 2 } ( x _ { 2 } ) } } & { 2 c _ { 2 3 } } & { 2 c _ { 2 4 } } \\ { \quad x _ { 1 } } & { \quad x _ { 2 } } & { } & { } \\ { \underline { { 2 c _ { 1 3 } x _ { 1 } + \bar { \jmath } _ { 1 3 } ( x _ { 1 } ) } } } & { \quad 2 c _ { 2 3 } } & { \quad \underline { { 6 } c _ { 3 3 } x _ { 3 } + z _ { 3 } ( x _ { 3 } ) } } & { 2 c _ { 3 4 } } \\ { \quad x _ { 1 } } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { \quad \underline { { 2 c _ { 1 4 } x _ { 1 } + \bar { \jmath } _ { 1 4 } ( x _ { 1 } ) } } } & { \quad 2 c _ { 2 4 } } & { \quad 2 c _ { 3 4 } } & { \quad \underline { { 6 } c _ { 4 4 } x _ { 4 } + z _ { 4 } ( x _ { 4 } ) } } \end{array} \right]
$$

From $( \nabla X ) ^ { \prime } = X ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime }$ we obtain

$$
\dot { V } = X ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } \dot { X } = X ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } A X .
$$

Define ${ \pmb T } = { \pmb B } ^ { \prime } { \pmb A }$ ； then ${ \dot { V } } = X ^ { \prime } B ^ { \prime } A X$ and

$$
{ \cal T } = A ^ { \prime } S + S A + \dot { S } .
$$

After deriving the matrix $T$ we adjust its terms to ensure that $\dot { V }$ isat least negative-semi-definite. We do this by making the sum $\tau _ { i j } + \tau _ { j i }$ $= 0$ and $\tau _ { i i }$ negative or zero where the $\tau _ { i j }$ are the elements of the matrix $T$

Brayton and Moser (1964) constructed Liapounov's functions for application to electric circuit networks.By extending this idea to partial differential equations for a nonlinear mixed initial boundary value problem,Brayton and Miranker (1964) constructed Liapounov functions and showed that complete stability follows from the existence of a Liapounov function， giving a number of examples.A complete stability is an asymptotic stability in the large,in the sense of La Salle and Lefschetz (1961). In all linear systems the stability is always complete，but in nonlinear systems it may fail to be complete. Yoshizawa (1960,1960a,1963,1964,1966) constructed a Liapounov function, showing the existence of a bounded solution，and discussed the asymptotic behavior of the solution of a system of differential equations.He also obtained theorems on the extreme stability and almost periodic solutions of functional-differential equations,following Hale (1963,1964),La Salle $( 1 9 5 7 ; 1 9 6 7 )$ ,and La Salle and Lefschetz (1961).If there exists a Liapounov function $V ( t , x , y )$ that decreases along the solution of a functional-differential equation，such that $V$ satisfies $\| x - \phi \| \leq V ( t , x , y ) \leq K \| x - y \|$ ，then the system need not be linear.The extreme stability (La Salle and Lefschetz, 1961) holds if for any pair of solutions $x ( t ) , \bar { x } ( t )$ we have $x ( t ) - \bar { x } ( t )  0$ as $t \to \infty$ . Yoshizawa (196O) obtained such a function，which guarantees the existence of periodic solutions and almost periodic solutions of the functional-differential equation．Halkin (1964,1964a,1964b,1965) extended a theorem of Liapounov on the closedness and connectivity of a set.

La Salle (1967） discussed a stability theorem for nonautonomous systems of differential equations by modifying Yoshizawa's theorems (1963）and showed the invariance principle by extending Miller's (1965) and Hale's (1965a) studies for autonomous systems, thus gave a unified presentation of Liapounov functions. Consider the system ${ d x } / { d t } = f ( t , x )$ ，where $_ x$ is an $_ n$ -vector, $f$ is a continuous function on $R ^ { n + 1 }$ to $R ^ { n }$ and satisfies the uniqueness condition.Let $V ( t , x )$ be a $C ^ { 1 }$ function on $[ 0 , \infty ) \times R ^ { n }$ to $\pmb R$ ，and $G$ be any set in $R ^ { n } , \ V$ isa Liapounov function on $G$ for the above differential equation if $V ( t , x )$ $\geq 0$ and ${ \dot { V } } ( t , x ) \leq - W ( x ) \leq 0$ for all $t > 0$ and all $x$ in $G$ ，where $W$ is continuous on $R ^ { n }$ to $\pmb R$ . Define $E = \{ x ; W ( x ) = 0 , x$ in $\overline { { G } } \}$ ， where $\overline { { G } }$ is the closure of $G$ .La Salle showed that each solution $x ( t )$ of the differential equation that remains in $G$ for all $t > t _ { 0 } \geq 0$ approaches $E ^ { * } = E \cup \{ \infty \}$ as $t \to \infty$ ，provided that either (i) if for each point $p$ in $\overline { G }$ there is a neighborhood $N$ of $p$ such that $| f ( t , x ) |$ is bounded for all $t > 0$ and all $x$ in $N$ ；or (ii) if $W$ is $C ^ { 1 }$ and $\dot { W }$ is bounded from above or below along each solution which remains in $G$ for all $t > t _ { 0 } \geq 0$ If $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ is bounded then each solution that remains in $G$ for $t > t _ { 0 } \geq 0$ either approaches $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ or $\infty$ as $t \to \infty$ . This is what La Salle calls the invariance principle.

Seifert (196O),Fink and Seifert (1969) derived the conditions,in terms of Liapounov functions, sufficient for the uniqueness of bounded solutions of almost periodic systems of differential equations, according to the idea of La Salle (1968) and Yoshizawa (1968).These conditions impose a type of conditional stability on such bounded solutions.Also they obtained sufficient conditions that such bounded solutions be almost periodic.

The set

$$
L ( f , \mathfrak { X } ) = \ \left\{ \int _ { E } f \ d \mu ; E \in \mathfrak { X } \right\}
$$

is called convex if $\mathfrak { X }$ is an algebra of Borel subsets (Section 15.1） of [0,1] such that for every $\pmb { A } \in \mathfrak { U }$ there exists a class of sets $\{ D _ { \alpha } ; \alpha \in [ 0 , 1 ] \} \subset \mathfrak { X }$ with $D _ { 1 } = A$ ， $\mu ( D _ { \alpha } ) = \alpha \mu ( A )$ and $D _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } \subset D _ { \alpha _ { 2 } }$ when $\alpha _ { 1 } < \alpha _ { 2 }$ and if $f$ is a finite-dimensional vector-valued integrable function over [O,1]. Halkin proved that the set $L ( f , \mathfrak { X } )$ is closed and convex if $\mathfrak { X }$ is of a class of Borel subsets of [O,1] and $f$ is a finite-dimensional vector-valued integrable function on [0,1].

Pliss（1964) considered

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = A x + P ( x , y ) , \frac { d y } { d t } = B y + Q ( x , y ) ,
$$

where $P , Q$ are vector functions with the norms $\| P \| , \| Q \|$ small in comparison with those of $x$ and $y$ . It is assumed that the eigenvalues of $\pmb { A }$ all have zero real parts. Pliss showed the existence and gave the construction of an $_ n$ -dimensional vector function $P ^ { * } ( x )$ such that the problem of stability of this system of differential equations is equivalent to that of the trivial solution of the system

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = A x + P ^ { * } ( x ) .
$$

This is called the reduction principle by Malkin (1942).

According to Nemytskii and Stepanov (1960；Nemytskii, 1949), in a sufficiently small neighborhood $S ( 0 , r )$ of an isolated singular point $o$ every closed trajectory contained in $S ( 0 , r )$ contains $o$ in its interior. Suppose that $S ( 0 , r )$ is a small circle with center at $o$ and contains no singular point other than $o$ ，either in its interior or on its boundary. Suppose that $o$ is stable.Take a fixed radius $O P$ of $S ( 0 , r )$ and consider on $O P$ the set $\Sigma$ of all points through which pass closed trajectories entirely contained in $s ( 0 , r )$ . Let $M$ be the least upper bound of $\scriptstyle \sum$ ，then $M \in \textstyle \sum , \textstyle \sum \cup O \in O M$ ，and the complement $\Delta$ of $\Sigma \cup o$ in $o M$ consists of at most a denumerable number of open intervals $I _ { j } = ( a _ { j } , b _ { j } )$ .If $\Delta$ is not empty and if $p \in { I _ { j } }$ ，then the trajectory through $p$ spirals toward the two closed trajectories $\boldsymbol { \mathbf { \mathit { f } } } ( \boldsymbol { a } _ { j } , t )$ and $\mathsf { f } ( b _ { j } , t )$ ，where $f ( c , t )$ means the trajectory passing through $c$ Such a stable singular point is called a center-focus. If $\Delta$ is empty for a suffciently small $S ( 0 , r )$ ，then $o$ is called a central point after Poincaré (1885； Section 23.2).

If a half-trajectory has $o$ as its only $\omega \cdot$ or $\alpha$ -limit point in $S ( 0 , r )$ ， then we say that it adheres to $o$ (Sections 16.5 and 26.1). Consider a trajectory $f ( q , t )$ through a point $q$ on the fixed radius $O P$ of $S ( 0 , r )$ If either one of the half-trajectories $f ^ { + } ( \boldsymbol { q } , t )$ or $f ^ { - } ( \boldsymbol { q } , t )$ is contained in $S ( 0 , r )$ ， then either the $\omega$ [or the $\alpha \cdot ]$ limit set is contained in $S ( 0 , r )$ ， and $o$ is in the $\omega$ -limit set [or the $\alpha$ -limit set] and is its only singular point.Hence, if an isolated singular point $o$ is not stable,then there exists a half-trajectory which has $o$ as its only $\omega \mathrm { \mathrm { - } }$ [or $\alpha { - } ]$ limit point, and there exists a small enough neighborhood $S ( 0 , r )$ of an unstable singular point $o$ ，so that every half-trajectory $f ^ { + } ( q , t ) , q \in S ( 0 , r )$ ， either has $o$ as its only $\omega \cdot$ [or $\alpha \mathrm { - } \mathrm { ] }$ limit point or leaves $s$ in a finite time.Thus in a sufficiently small neighborhood of $S ( 0 , r )$ of an unstable singular point $o$ ，the trajectories $f ( p , t ) , p \in S ( 0 , r )$ ，fall into three classes: (i) elliptic trajectories,which are contained in $s ( 0 , r )$ and adhere to $o$ at both ends ； (ii) parabolic trajectories,whose one end, say $f ^ { + } ( p , t )$ ， is contained in $S ( 0 , r )$ and adheres to $o$ while the other half-trajectory $f ^ { - } ( p , t )$ leaves the neighborhood in a finite time； and (iii) hyperbolic trajectories,for which both half-trajectories $f ^ { + } ( p , t )$ and $f ^ { - } ( p , t )$ leave $S ( 0 , r )$ in a finite time.

Consider a system of differential equations of the second order $d x / d t = A x$ ，where $\pmb { A }$ is a constant matrix, $\| A \| \neq 0$ . If the characteristic roots $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 }$ are real,distinct,and of the same sign，then each trajectory is of parabolic type.For $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 }$ both negative the origin is a stable nodal point,and for $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 }$ both positive the origin is an unstable nodal point. If $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 }$ are of opposite sign， then each trajectory is hyperbolic with the axes as asymptotes,and the origin is a saddle point. If $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 }$ are equal, then the origin is a degenerate nodal point.If the characteristic roots of $\pmb { A }$ are complex conjugate with negative real parts,then the origin is a stable focal point,and if they have positive real parts, then the origin is an unstable focal point, although the focal point is an unstable singular point in either case (Section 23.2).

Kudaev (1962), in order to construct Liapounov functions,divided the neighborhood of the equilibrium point $o$ into $_ m$ regions $\omega _ { s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )$ which are polygonal cones $\pmb { H } _ { l }$ $( l = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k )$ with vertices at $o$ . A function $V ( x )$ with constant sign,which is not zero in the region $\omega _ { s }$ and keeps the same sign in each of the cones,is called the generalized Liapounov function if $d V / d t$ is an alternating function also preserving its sign in the region $\omega _ { s }$ and changing it at each passage through the surface $\pmb { H } _ { l }$ $( l = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k )$ , and if we have on the surface $\textstyle \bigcup _ { l = 1 } ^ { k } H _ { l } V ( x ) > 0$ and $d V / d t = 0$ . These cones $\pmb { H } _ { l }$ that enter into the boundary of the region $\omega _ { s }$ are called adjacent. He proved that:

If $V$ is a generalized Liapounov function and $d ^ { 2 } V / d t ^ { 2 } > 0$ [or $d ^ { 2 } V / d t ^ { 2 } < 0 ]$ on the cones $\pmb { H } _ { l }$ $( l = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k )$ , then the given system of differential equations is hyperbolic [or elliptic].

Suppose that (i) $V$ is positive-definite in the whole neighborhood, (ii) $d V / d t$ is equal to zero on the surface $\cup _ { l = 1 } ^ { k } H _ { l }$ ，keeps its sign in each region $\omega _ { s }$ ，and changes its sign on passing through the surface $\pmb { H } _ { l }$ $\mathbf { \Lambda } _ { \ i } ( l = 1 , \dots , k )$ ，(ii) $d ^ { 2 } V / d t ^ { 2 }$ preserves its sign on each cone $\pmb { H } _ { l }$ but there is at least one pair of adjacent cones entering the boundary surfaces of some region $\omega _ { s }$ ， on which $d ^ { 2 } V / d t ^ { 2 }$ has opposite signs, and (iv) there is a supplementary cone $h _ { r }$ within $\omega _ { s }$ with vertex $O , h _ { r } \backslash O \subset \omega _ { s } ,$ which separates that pair of cones and on which the condition $d ^ { 2 } V / d t ^ { 2 }$ $= 0$ ， $d V / d t$ ， $d ^ { 3 } V / d t ^ { 3 } > 0$ are satisfied，or there exists a pair of supplementary cones $h _ { \tau _ { i } }$ ， $( { h _ { r } } _ { i } \backslash O ) \subset \omega _ { r }$ $( i = 1 , 2 )$ ,on which the conditions $d ^ { 2 } V / d t ^ { 2 } = 0$ ， $d V / d t$ ， $d ^ { 3 } V / d t ^ { 3 } < 0$ are satisfied,and the cones $h _ { \tau _ { i } }$ in question are not separated by any other supplementary cones. Then the given system of differential equations is elliptic-hyperbolic, that is,the region contains all three classes of integral curve.In addition， the region $\omega _ { r }$ has a subset open in the $_ n$ -dimensional space, where $_ n$ is the order of the given differential equations,that encloses either the surface $h _ { \tau } \backslash O$ or the surface $h _ { \tau _ { i } } \backslash O$ entirely consisting of the points of parabolic trajectories.

Moisseiev (1936a) proved that Liapounov's theorems are valid even when the derivative $d V / d t$ jumps by a finite amount without changing its sign at points with measure zero.

Moisseiev (1936b,1937,1937a) introduced the notion of stability-probability in Liapounov's theory. Consider an autonomous system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = X _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

$$
X _ { s } ( 0 , \ldots , 0 ) = 0 ,
$$

so that $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ is a trivial solution,where $X _ { s }$ are singlevalued,continuous,and holomorphic inside the sphere $x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots +$ $x _ { n } ^ { 2 } = \rho ^ { 2 }$ .Let $M _ { r }$ be the set of points inside a sphere of radius $r$ with center $O \colon x _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ in the $_ n$ -dimensional space where the solution curves starts at the initial epoch $t = t _ { 0 }$ . If no point $x _ { s } = x _ { s } ( t )$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ on any solution curve with the initial point $x _ { s } = x _ { s } ( t _ { 0 } )$

inside the sphere of radius $\pmb { R } _ { 1 }$ escapes from the inside of the sphere with radius $\pmb { R } _ { 2 }$ ,where $\pmb { R } _ { 2 }$ is a continuous, single-valued, non-increasing function of $R _ { 1 }$ tending to zero for $R _ { 1 } \to 0$ ，then the solution curve is said to correspond to an $s$ -motion, otherwise an $\pmb { I }$ -motion. Correspondingly we define the sets $S M _ { r }$ and $I M _ { \tau }$ ,where $M _ { r }$ is the set of the initial points inside the $r$ -sphere. The limit for $r \to 0$ of the ratio of the measures of $s M$ and $M$ is called the stability-probability $S P$ by Moisseiev. If the set $M _ { r }$ is divided into two pure,non-reducible, nonintersecting subsets $M _ { r } S$ and $M _ { \tau } I$ ，then the instability-probability $I P$ is defined as the limit for $r \to 0$ of the ratio of the measures of $M _ { r } I$ and $M _ { \tau }$ ，and we have $P S + P I = 1$

Let $z$ be a pure subset of $M _ { r }$ whose derived set $z ^ { \prime }$ contains the origin. Consider the set of numbers $\overrightarrow { R } ( P )$ corresponding to the points of the set $z$ in an $r .$ -sphere.Let the upper bound of this set $\begin{array} { r } { \overline { { \cal R } } ( P ) } \end{array}$ be $\overleftarrow { R } ( r , z )$ ， then any set z corresponds to non-negative function $R =$ $\overbar { R } ( r , z )$ which is non-increasing for decreasing $r$ It may occur that $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { r  0 } \bar { R } ( r , z ) = 0 } \end{array}$ .In this case the set $z$ of the initial points $P$ and the set of motion $B ^ { \ast } ( P ( z ) )$ starting from the point of this set $z$ belong to the $s .$ class. If the function $\textstyle { \overrightarrow { R } } ( r , z )$ does not satisfy this limit condition, then the set belongs to the $I { \big . }$ -class.

The set $z$ of the $s { \mathrm { . } }$ -class cannot be reduced, that is, from a set $z$ of the $s$ -class a set of the same $z \cdot$ -type and of the $\pmb { I }$ -class cannot be separated. But the sets of the $\pmb { I }$ -class can be reduced，that is,from such a z-set of the $I .$ -class a $z \mathrm { . }$ -subset of the $s .$ -class can be separated. Hence, if there is a non-empty z-set of the $\pmb { I }$ -class,then a non-reducible z-subset of the $s$ -class should be separated.Thus，if $z _ { I }$ is a nonreducible z-set of the $I -$ -class and $z _ { S }$ a $z \mathrm { . }$ -set of the $s { \mathrm { . } }$ -class，then the derived set $D ^ { \prime } ( z _ { s } z _ { I } )$ of the common set $D ( z _ { s } z _ { \tau } )$ of these sets does not contain the origin.

The function $\overline { { R } } ( r , z _ { S } )$ for a z-set of $s$ -class is called the limitdeviation (Grenzabweichungsfunktion),and

$$
\frac { d \bar { R } ( r , z _ { S } ) } { d r } \bigg | _ { r = 0 } = G P ( z _ { S } )
$$

is called the limit-deviation gradient (Grenzabweichungsgradient) of the set z. If the set $M _ { r }$ is represented as the sum of two pure,nonreducible, non-intersecting subsets $M _ { \rho } S$ and $M _ { \rho } I$ of the $S -$ and $\pmb { I }$ -classes, then a subset $M _ { \rho } S$ possesses the limit-deviation $R = \overline { { R } } ( r , M _ { \rho } S )$ ，and all these limit-deviations give the same limit-deviation gradient $d \bar { R } ( r , M _ { \rho } S ) / d r | _ { r = 0 }$ ，which depends only on the particular solution in question and the set $M _ { \rho }$ and is called the limit-deviation gradient GP for the particular solution.

Consider a single-valued continuous function $W = W ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ ， $W ( 0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 ) = 0$ ，whose first-order partial derivatives are singlevalued and continuous in the region $T$ except in an $( n - l )$ -dimensional manifold $A ( 1 \leq l \leq n )$ ，such that

$$
\left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \mathbf { \Omega } \cdot \mathbf { \Omega } \cdot \mathbf { \Omega } + \mathbf { \Omega } \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { n } } \right) ^ { 2 }
$$

is different from zero except for the origin $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ .Denote by $W ^ { 0 }$ the geometrical region $W ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) = 0$ .We can find a sphere $\textstyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } = R _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ ， $\rho \geq { R } _ { 1 } > 0$ ， in the region where $X _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ are continuous, holomorphic,and single-valued.Consider those cone-like mantles of the surface $W ^ { 0 }$ in going through which $W$ changes its sign. In these domains it may happen that

$$
\dot { W } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { s } } X _ { s }
$$

vanishes only at the origin and has a definite sign on the boundaries. The sign of $\dot { W }$ on the boundary is the same as the sign of $W$ in crossing the boundary from outside toward the inside, in which case we say that the region is detached from inside.

Consider further a function $V = V ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ which satisfies the same condition as $W$ . Along the integral curve we construct

$$
\dot { V } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { s } } X _ { s } .
$$

The regions detatched from inside by the function $W$ are divided into four regions : (i) $A _ { \scriptscriptstyle W V }$ -regions,asymptotic regions,where $V$ and $\dot { V }$ are definite with opposite signs inside and on the boundary; (ii) $S _ { \varpi v ^ { - } }$ regions， stable regions，where $\dot { V }$ is non-negative or non-positive according as $V$ is negative or positive, which contain the $\scriptstyle A _ { \scriptscriptstyle { W V } }$ -region; (iii) $I _ { w \nu }$ -regions,unstable regions，where $V$ is non-negative or nonpositive while $\dot { V }$ is negative-definite or positive-definite with the same sign as $V$ ; and (iv) $D _ { w v }$ -regions,ambiguous.

For the $S _ { w r }$ -regions Liapounov's theorems (Section 16.3） say that for any $\pmb R$ of $0 < R < R _ { 1 }$ there is an interval $0 < r < r ( R )$ such that $r ( R ) \to 0$ as $R \to 0$ and any point starting in an $S _ { w v }$ -region of the $\boldsymbol { r }$ -sphere never leaves the inside of the $\pmb { R }$ -sphere, no matter how large $t - t _ { 0 }$ is (Section 16.3, Theorem 2).

The proposition is concerned, not with the whole neighborhood of the origin as in Liapounov's work,but with a part of the neighborhood,and gives the bound $W ^ { 0 }$ . The totality of the regions detached from inside the $S _ { W V }$ -regions is simply a part of the whole z-set of the $s$ -class.We have

$$
m ( M _ { r } S _ { W V } ) \leq m ( M _ { r } S ) , \qquad P S \geq \operatorname * { l i m } \frac { m ( M _ { r } S _ { W V } ) } { m ( M _ { r } ) } .
$$

Any motion starting at a point of the $\boldsymbol { A } _ { \boldsymbol { W } \boldsymbol { V } }$ -region tends to the origin asymptotically as $t \to + \infty$ (Section 16.3, Theorem 3).We have

$$
P A = \operatorname * { l i m } \frac { m ( M _ { r } A ) } { m ( M _ { r } ) } , \qquad m ( M _ { r } A _ { \scriptscriptstyle { W V } } ) \leq m ( M _ { r } A ) ,
$$

and

$$
P A \geq \operatorname* { l i m } _ { r  0 } \frac { m ( M _ { r } A _ { w v } ) } { m ( M _ { r } ) } .
$$

For any $r$ of $0 < r < R _ { \mathrm { { 1 } } }$ there is an $\pmb R$ -value such that any motion starting from a point of an $I _ { w r }$ -region of the $r .$ -sphere leaves the $\pmb R$ -sphere in a finite time. This is an extension of Cetaev's theorem (Section 16.3, Theorem 6).We have

$$
m ( M _ { r } I _ { w v } ) \leq m ( M _ { r } I ) , \qquad P I \geq \operatorname * { l i m } _ { r  0 } \frac { m ( M _ { r } I _ { w v } ) } { m ( M _ { r } ) } ,
$$

where $P I$ denotes the instability-probability.

The trivial solution $x _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ is said by Moisseiev (1937b) to be stable or unstable of the third type with respect to the amplitude $\bar { e }$ of the initial deviation and the amplitude ä of the final deviation on the system of surfaces $W ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) = W$ ,if the characteristic of the largest deviation $\overline { { W } } ( \bar { e } )$ for the motion defined by the points of the set $\overline { { M } } _ { \bar { e } }$ satisfies either $\overline { { \overline { { W } } } } ( \bar { e } ) \leqq \bar { \varepsilon }$ or $\overline { { W } } ( \bar { e } ) > \bar { \varepsilon }$

In problems of stability of motion in the neighborhood of an equilibrium point there can exist a solution which, though periodic,goes outside the area,inside which the solutions of the linearized equations of motion are restricted. On the other hand, for an unstable equilibrium point there can exist a finite neighborhood in which the solution remains for suffciently long time. Such an equilibrium point could be considered as stable but not in the sense of Liapounov. Olszewski (1971) proposed a concept of practical stability defined by four constants : two relating to time $t _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 1 } , t _ { 0 } < t _ { 1 }$ ， and two relating to the neighborhood $r$ and $R , r < R$ . Consider a vector equation $\dot { \boldsymbol { y } } = \boldsymbol { Y } ( t , \boldsymbol { y } )$ , where $Y ( t , y )$ is continuous and continuously differentiable in $t$ and $\mathfrak { y }$ ，with a particular solution $f ( t )$ . The solution $f ( t )$ is said to be practically stable with respect to $t _ { 0 } , \ t _ { 1 } , \ r$ and $\pmb R$ if for every other solution $y = y ( t )$ with $y ( t _ { 0 } )$ such that $\| y ( t _ { 0 } ) - f ( t _ { 0 } ) \| \le r$ we have $\| y ( t ) - f ( t ) \| < R$ for $t _ { 0 } \leqq t \leq t _ { 1 }$ ；and to be practically unstable if there exists at least one other solution with the initial value $y ( t _ { 0 } )$ ， $\| y ( t _ { 0 } ) - f ( t _ { 0 } ) \| \geq r _ { \ast }$ ，and at least one instant of time $t ^ { * }$ ， $t _ { 0 } < t ^ { * } \leq t _ { 1 } ,$ such that $\| y ( t ^ { * } ) - f ( t ^ { * } ) \| = R$

Olszewski employed a function $V ( t , x )$ and its derivative $\dot { V } ( t , x )$ in the Liapounov direct method.Let

$$
V ( t , x ) = x A ( t ) x ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where $\mathbf { \nabla } \_ { A \left( t \right) }$ is the matrix of this quadratic form with elements $a _ { i j } ( t )$ ， and a prime means the transposed. Changing the coordinate system by

$$
x = y - f ( t ) , \qquad X ( t , x ) = Y ( t , y ) - f ( t )
$$

with $X ( t , 0 ) = 0$ ，the equations of motion ${ \dot { \boldsymbol { x } } } = { \boldsymbol { X } } ( t , { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ , i.e., the equations for the perturbed motion with perturbation $s ( t , x )$ become

$$
\dot { x } ^ { \prime } = P ( t ) x ^ { \prime } + s ^ { \prime } ( t , x ) ,
$$

where $P ( t )$ is a time-dependent matrix and $s ( t , x )$ is a perturbation vector with $s ( t , 0 ) = 0$ Suppose that $| s _ { i } ( t , x ) | \leqq \eta _ { i }$ for $\| x \| \leq R$ ， $t _ { 0 } \leqq$ $t \leq t _ { 1 } ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ . Then we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \dot { V } ( t , x ) = x [ P ^ { \prime } ( t ) A ( t ) + \dot { A } ( t ) + A ( t ) P ( t ) ] x ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { + s ( t , x ) A ( t ) x ^ { \prime } + x A ( t ) s ^ { \prime } ( t , x ) , } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
B ( t ) = P ^ { \prime } ( t ) A ( t ) + \dot { A } ( t ) + A ( t ) P ( t ) .
$$

Let for $t = t ^ { * }$

$\lambda _ { A \operatorname* { m a x } } ( t ^ { * } )$ be the maximum of the eigenvalues of $\gimel [ A ( t ) \ + \ A ^ { \prime } ( t ) ]$ ， $\lambda _ { A \operatorname* { m i n } } ( t ^ { * } )$ be the minimum of the eigenvalues of $\frac { 1 } { 2 } [ A ( t ) + A ^ { \prime } ( t ) ] .$ ， $\lambda _ { B \tt m a x } ( t ^ { * } )$ be the maximum of the eigenvalues of $\frac { 1 } { 2 } [ B ( t ) + B ^ { \prime } ( t ) ]$ ， $\lambda _ { B \mathrm { m i n } } ( t ^ { * } )$ be the minimum of the eigenvalues of $\frac { 1 } { 2 } [ B ( t ) + B ^ { \prime } ( t ) ]$

Olszewski proved the followings :

(i） If for the perturbed motion a matrix $\mathbfcal { A } ( t )$ and two constants $m _ { \tau }$ and $m _ { R }$ ， $m _ { r } \le m _ { \mathcal { R } }$ ，can be found such that $\lambda _ { A \operatorname* { m a x } } ( t ) \leq m _ { r } / r ^ { 2 }$ for $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t _ { 1 } ,$ $\lambda _ { A \operatorname* { m i n } } ( t ) > m _ { R } / R ^ { 2 }$ for $t _ { 0 } \leqq t \leq t _ { 1 }$ and

$$
\lambda _ { B \operatorname* { m a x } } ( t ) \leq - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \Bigg \{ R \sum _ { i } \eta _ { i } \sqrt { \sum _ { j } { [ a _ { i j } ( t ) + a _ { j i } ( t ) ] ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { m _ { R } - m _ { r } } { t _ { 1 } - t _ { 0 } } \Bigg \}
$$

for $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t _ { 1 } ,$ ，then the solution $x ( t ) = 0$ for the perturbed motion is practically stable with respect to $t _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 } , r$ and $\pmb R$

(ii） If for the perturbed motion a matrix $\mathbf { \nabla } _ { A \left( t \right) }$ and two constants ${ \mathbf { } } m _ { \tau }$ and $m _ { R }$ ， $m _ { r } \leqslant m _ { \mathcal { R } }$ ，can be found such that $\lambda _ { A \mathrm { m a x } } ( t _ { 0 } ) = m _ { r } / r ^ { 2 } ;$ ， $\lambda _ { A \operatorname* { m a x } } ( t ^ { * } ) \leq m _ { R } / R ^ { 2 }$ for $t ^ { * }$ in the interval $[ t _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 } ] , \lambda _ { A \operatorname* { m a x } } ( t ) \leq m _ { r } / r ^ { 2 }$ for $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t ^ { * }$ ，and

$$
\lambda _ { B \operatorname* { m i n } } ( t ) \equiv \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \left\{ R \sum _ { i } \eta _ { i } \sqrt { \sum _ { j } { [ a _ { i j } ( t ) + a _ { j i } ( t ) ] ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { m _ { R } - m _ { r } } { t - t _ { 0 } } \right\}
$$

for $t _ { 0 } \leqq t \leq t ^ { * }$ , then the solution $x ( t ) = 0$ for the perturbed motion is practically unstable with respect to $t _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 } , r$ and $\pmb R$

(iii) If for the perturbed motion a matrix $\mathbfcal { A } ( t )$ and two constants ${ \mathbf { } } m _ { \tau }$ and $m _ { R } , m _ { r } \leqq m _ { R }$ ,can be found such that $\lambda _ { A \mathrm { m i n } } ( t ^ { * } ) = m _ { R } / R ^ { 2 }$ for $t ^ { * }$ in the interval $[ t _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 } ] , \lambda _ { A \mathrm { m i n } } ( t ) \geq m _ { R } / R ^ { 2 }$ for $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t ^ { * }$ ， $\lambda _ { A \operatorname* { m i n } } ( t _ { 0 } )$ $\geq m _ { \tau } / r ^ { 2 }$ ，and

$$
\lambda _ { B \operatorname* { m i n } } ( t ) \equiv \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \left\{ R \sum _ { i } \eta _ { i } \sqrt { \sum _ { j } { [ a _ { i j } ( t ) + a _ { j i } ( t ) ] ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { m _ { R } - m _ { \tau } } { t - t _ { 0 } } \right\}
$$

for $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t ^ { * }$ ，then the solution $x ( t ) = 0$ for the perturbed motion is practically unstable with respect to $t _ { 0 } , t _ { 1 } , r$ and $\pmb R$

Now by Duboshin's proposal,according to Olszewski, if the matrix $\pmb { B } ( t )$ is given, then the matrix $\mathbfcal { A } ( t )$ can be found as a solution of

$$
\dot { A } ( t ) = B ( t ) - P ^ { \prime } ( t ) A ( t ) - A ( t ) P ( t ) .
$$

The stability is judged by a particular solution of this equation obtained by a computer according to either of the above three theorems. Olszewski chose $m _ { r } = m _ { R }$ ， $\boldsymbol { A } ( 0 ) = \boldsymbol { I } , \boldsymbol { B } ( v ) = 0$ for the linearized system

$$
\frac { d x ^ { \prime } } { d v } = P ( v ) x ^ { \prime } ,
$$

so that every condition of the three theorems is satisfied.The differential equation for the function $V ( t , x )$ reduces to

$$
\frac { d A ( v ) } { d v } = - P ^ { \prime } ( v ) A ( v ) - A ( v ) P ( v ) \qquad ( 0 \le v \le 2 \pi ) ,
$$

with $A ( 0 ) = I$ ， The solution can be found by means of the Runge-Kutta method. The values of $\lambda _ { A \mathrm { m i n } } ( v )$ and $\lambda _ { A \tt m a x } ( v )$ are found by means of the eigenvalues.

Olszewski obtained for the restricted three-body problem the result :

(i）for $r / R = 0 . 0 3 5$ the triangular points $L _ { * }$ and $L _ { 5 }$ are practically unstable in all cases considered of the mass ratio $\mu$ and the eccentricity $^ e$ ，

(ii）for $r / R = 0 . 0 3$ they are practically unstable in all cases considered except $e = 0$ and $\mu = 0 . 0 2$ ,0.025,...,0.035,

(iii)for $r / R = 0 . 0 2 5$ the triangular points which are practically unstable are obtained.

He compared the result with those of Grebenikov,Danby and Bennett (Section 18.24). The pairs of $\mu$ and $e$ values for which the triangular point is practically unstable with $r / R = 0 . 0 2 5$ form an area, similar to that formed by the pairs for which the triangular point is. unstable in the sense of Liapounov (Sections 18.24,18.25,21.17). Thus there is a correspondence between both definitions of stability in the case of triangular points for $r / R \lessapproxeq 0 . 0 2 5$ ,that is,the triangular points may be considered as stable if for any other solution $x ( v )$ of the transformed linearized equation for $0 \leq v \leq 2 \pi$ the distance $\lVert x ( v ) \rVert$ is not larger than $4 0 \| x ( 0 ) \|$ and there are solutions of this equation with distances increasing over 3O times for all values of $\pmb { \mu }$ and $e$ with a few exceptions.

The Liapounov function can be applied for control systems(Bellman，1967；Sworder，1966；Leitmann，1965，1967；Section16.21).Consider a disturbed system

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = X ( x , t ) ,
$$

and the modified disturbed system

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = X ( x , t ) + R ( x , t ) .
$$

Suppose that the characteristic roots all have negative real parts, then the system is stable.In order to maintain the stability of the origin $x = 0$ we use a compensating mechanism, that is,a control.Let $\xi$ be a scalar parameter and let $\xi = 0$ be a state of no control. The new equations of motion corresponding to the linearized system $\dot { y } = A y$ from $\dot { y } = Y ( y , t )$ are

$$
x = \dot { y } = A y + \xi b , \dot { \xi } = f ( \sigma ) , \sigma = c ^ { T } y - r \xi ,
$$

where $b , c$ are $\pmb { n }$ -vectors, $c ^ { T }$ is the transposed matrix of $^ c$ ，and $r$ isa scalar.We introduce $\pmb { \sigma }$ as an intermediate.The function $f ( \sigma )$ is the characteristic of the servomechanism.We assume that $\sigma f ( \sigma ) > 0$ for $\sigma \neq 0 , f ( 0 ) = 0 , f ( 0 )$ being continuous,and

$$
\int ^ { \pm \infty } f ( \sigma ) d \sigma = + \infty .
$$

A Liapounov function $V ( { \dot { y } } , t ) \equiv V ( x , \sigma )$ such that

$$
{ \dot { V } } = { \frac { \partial V } { \partial t } } + Y \cdot \operatorname { g r a d } V
$$

is, in this case,

$$
V ( x , \sigma ) = x ^ { T } \cdot B x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } f ( \sigma ) d \sigma , \qquad B > 0 , \qquad B ^ { T } = B ,
$$

where $\pmb { B } ^ { T }$ is symmetric.

16.7DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS FOR A DISTURBED MOTION

Consider

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } x _ { n } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

with constants $p _ { s \sigma }$ $\mathbf { \omega } _ { ; \sigma } \left( s , \sigma = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n \right)$ . The determinant

$$
D ( \kappa ) = \left| \begin{array} { l l l l } { p _ { 1 1 } - \kappa } & { p _ { 1 2 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { p _ { 1 n } } \\ { p _ { 2 1 } } & { p _ { 2 2 } - \kappa } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { p _ { 2 n } } \\ { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { } & { } & { } \\ { p _ { n 1 } } & { p _ { n 2 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { p _ { n n } - \kappa } \end{array} \right| = 0
$$

is called the fundamental determinant by Liapounov (1907,1949). To each root $\pmb { \kappa }$ of (57),there corresponds a solution of (56)

$$
x _ { 1 } = K _ { 1 } e ^ { \varepsilon t } , \qquad x _ { 2 } = K _ { 2 } e ^ { \varepsilon t } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad x _ { n } = K _ { n } e ^ { \varepsilon t }
$$

with constants $K _ { i }$ . If the roots $\kappa _ { 1 } , \kappa _ { 2 } , \ldots , \kappa _ { n }$ of (57） are all simple, then these $\pmb { n }$ solutions with such values of $\pmb { \kappa }$ are independent.If the roots are multiple, then (56) admits solutions of the form

$$
x _ { 1 } = f _ { 1 } ( t ) e ^ { \kappa t } , \qquad x _ { 2 } = f _ { 2 } ( t ) e ^ { \kappa t } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad x _ { n } = f _ { n } ( t ) e ^ { \kappa t } ,
$$

where $f _ { i } ( t )$ are polynomials in $t$ of degree not exceeding the multiplicity of the corresponding root $\pmb { \kappa }$ minus 1.

Consider a homogeneous polynomial $V$ of degree $_ m$ in $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ satisfying

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \mathbf { \alpha } \cdot \mathbf { \alpha } \cdot \mathbf { \beta } + p _ { s n } x _ { n } \right) \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { s } } = \kappa V .
$$

Let the number of the coefficients in $V$ be

$$
N = ( n + m - 1 ) ! / [ n ! \cdot ( m - 1 ) ! ] .
$$

Equating the coefficients of $x _ { 1 } ^ { m _ { 1 } } \cdots x _ { n } ^ { m _ { n } }$ on both sides of (58） and eliminating the coefficients of $V$ we obtain

$$
D _ { m } ( \kappa ) = \left| \begin{array} { l l l l } { a _ { 1 1 } - \kappa } & { a _ { 1 2 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { a _ { 1 N } } \\ { a _ { 2 1 } } & { a _ { 2 2 } - \kappa } & { } & { a _ { 2 N } } \\ { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { } & { } & { } \\ { a _ { N 1 } } & { a _ { N 2 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { a _ { N N } - \kappa } \end{array} \right| = 0 ,
$$

where $a _ { i j }$ are linear forms of $p _ { s \sigma }$ ，and $D _ { 1 } ( \kappa )$ is the fundamental determinant $D ( \kappa )$ . The roots of $D _ { m } ( \kappa ) = 0$ can be obtained from $\kappa = m _ { 1 } \kappa _ { 1 }$ $+ \cdots + m _ { n } \kappa _ { n }$ ，where $m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ are all given non-negative integer values such as $m _ { 1 } + \cdots + m _ { n } = m$ ， provided that one and the same: system of values can occur once and only once. If there is no relation of the form $m _ { 1 } \kappa _ { 1 } + \ldots + m _ { n } \kappa _ { n } = 0$ with non-negative integer coefficients $m _ { i }$ ，where $m _ { 1 } + \cdots + m _ { n } = m$ is a given positive integer, then we can find in one and only one way a form $V$ of degree $_ m$ of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ satisfying

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + p _ { s n } x _ { n } \right) { \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { s } } } = U ,
$$

where $U$ is an arbitrarily given form of the same degree $_ m$ . If the real parts of all roots $\kappa _ { s }$ are negative and if $U$ is of a definite form of an even degree $m$ ，then $V$ of the same degree satisfying (59) is also definite and the sign is opposite to that of $U$ . If among these $\kappa _ { s } \mathrm { ' s }$ there are some whose real parts are positive and the other roots have negative real parts,and if $U$ is a definite form $\pmb { U }$ of degree $m$ ，then $V$ of the same degree satisfying (59) cannot have a fixed sign opposite to that of $\pmb { U }$

Consider the differential equations of the disturbed motion

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } x _ { n } + X _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array}{c} X _ { s } = \sum _ { \begin{array} { l } { \scriptstyle { m _ { 1 } + \cdots + m _ { n } \geq 2 } } \\ { \scriptstyle { ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) . } } \end{array} } P _ { s } ^ { ( m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { n } ) } x _ { 1 } ^ { m _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \ x _ { n } ^ { m _ { n } }  \\ { \quad \quad { ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) } . } \end{array}
$$

If the fundamental determinant corresponding to (6O) admits only roots with negative real parts, then the non-disturbed motion is stable, and a motion for which the perturbation is sufficiently small tends asymptotically to the non-disturbed motion.Further, if the determinant admits roots with negative real parts, then there is conditional stability, whatever the other roots may be.If it admits roots with positive real parts,then the nondisturbed motion is unstable.

Suppose that the fundamental determinant for differential equations of a disturbed motion of order $n + 1$ admits one zero root,and the remaining $_ n$ roots all have negative real parts. The system is transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d x } { d t } = X , \frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } x _ { n } + p _ { s } x + X _ { s } } \\ { ( s = 1 , 2 , \cdot \cdot . . , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

where $X , X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are holomorphic in $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ . The system

$$
p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } x _ { n } + p _ { s } x + X _ { s } = 0 \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

can be solved for $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ in holomorphic functions of $x$ ,vanishing for $x = 0$ .We substitute these expressions for $x _ { s }$ in $X$ and expand it in powers of $x$ .If the lowest power of $x$ in the expansions thus obtained is even，the nondisturbed motion is unstable,and if it is odd,the nondisturbed motion is unstable when the corresponding coeffcient is positive and stable when the coefficient is negative. In the latter case the disturbed motion asymptotically approaches the nondisturbed motion if the perturbation is sufficiently small.

Suppose next that the fundamental determinant of the differential equations of order $n + 2$ admits two conjugate imaginary roots $\sqrt { - 1 } \lambda$ and $- { \sqrt { - 1 } } \lambda$ and $_ n$ roots with negative real parts. The equations are transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d x } { d t } = - \lambda y + X , \frac { d y } { d t } = \lambda x + Y , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \dots + p _ { s n } x _ { n } + \alpha _ { s } x + \beta _ { s } y + X _ { s } } \end{array}
$$

$$
( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $X , Y , X _ { s }$ are holomorphic in $x , y , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ，and $\ p _ { s \sigma } , \alpha _ { s } , \beta _ { s }$ are real constants such that the determinant equation $D ( \kappa ) = 0$ formed by $p _ { s \sigma }$ admits only roots with negative real part. Then，with a rational entire function $f$ in $x , y , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ，consider its expression

$$
2 x X + 2 y Y + \left( - \lambda y + X \right) { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x } } + \left( \lambda x + Y \right) { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y } }
$$

$$
+ \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \left( p _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + p _ { s n } x _ { n } + \alpha _ { s } x + \beta _ { s } y + X _ { s } \right) \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { s } } .
$$

We can make disappear all terms either up to as high a degree as we please or up to those terms for which the sum of the exponents is less than a certain even integer 2N. In the first case the equations admit a continuous family of periodic solutions with the nondisturbed motion as a family member. Such a motion is stable,and all disturbed motions sufficiently near the nondisturbed tend asymptotically to one of the periodic solutions.In the second case if the lowest-degree terms reduce to $G ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { \mathrm { y } }$ ， $G > 0$ ， then the disturbed motion is unstable,and if $G < 0$ ，the motion is stable and alldisturbed motions sufficiently near the nondisturbed tend to it asymptotically.

Malkin (1938) discussed the solutions of (65) and Moisseiev (1936) the stability of the trivial solutions of (65).

Suppose that the coefficients $p _ { s \sigma }$ of (60) are periodic in t. Liapounov concluded that if the fundamental determinant admits only roots with moduli less than unity， then the nondisturbed motion is stable such that all disturbed motions sufficiently nearby approach the nondisturbed asymptotically,but if the fundamental determinant admits further roots with moduli greater than unity， then the motion is unstable.

If the fundamental determinant admits a root unity and the remaining $_ n$ roots with moduli less than unity, then the equations are transformed to the form (62).By a further transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { } & { = z + u ^ { ( 2 ) } z ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ^ { ( m - 1 ) } z ^ { m - 1 } + v z ^ { m } , } \\ { } & { = u _ { s } ^ { ( 1 ) } z + u _ { s } ^ { ( 2 ) } z ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u _ { s } ^ { ( m - 1 ) } z ^ { m - 1 } + z _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m ) , } \end{array}
$$

they are reduced to

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } = Z , \frac { d z _ { s } } { d t } = p _ { s 1 } z _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { s n } z _ { n } + Z _ { s }
$$

If

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { Z = g z ^ { m } + P ^ { ( 1 ) } z + \cdot \cdot \cdot + P ^ { ( m - 1 ) } z ^ { m - 1 } + Q + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ & { Z _ { s } = g _ { s } z ^ { m } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + P _ { s } ^ { ( 1 ) } z + P _ { s } ^ { ( 2 ) } z ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

then the Liapounov function $V$ should satisfy, for even values of $_ { m }$

$$
\frac { d V } { d t } = g ( z ^ { m } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + z _ { n } ^ { 2 } ) + S ,
$$

$$
S = v z ^ { m } + \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { n } v _ { s \sigma } z _ { s } z _ { \sigma } ,
$$

and, for odd values of $m$

$$
\frac { d V } { d t } = g ( z ^ { m + 1 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + z _ { n } ^ { 2 } ) + S ,
$$

$$
S = v z ^ { m + 1 } + \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { n } v _ { s \sigma } z _ { s } z _ { \sigma } ,
$$

where $v , v _ { s \sigma }$ are functions of $t , z , z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } ;$ ，vanishing for $z = z _ { 1 } =$ $\cdots = z _ { n } = 0$ ，periodic in $t$ ，and uniformly holomorphic in $z , z _ { s }$ for all real values of $t$ Thus if $_ m$ is even, $V$ satisfies all the conditions mentioned above,and the nondisturbed motion is unstable,and if $_ m$ is odd， the nondisturbed motion is stable and a disturbed motion asymptotically approaches the nondisturbed motion for sufficiently small perturbations for $g < 0$ ，and the nondisturbed motion is unstable for $g > 0$

Suppose next that the fundamental determinant admits two conjugate imaginary roots with moduli equal to unity,and that the remaining roots have moduli less than unity.Let

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \exp \left\{ \lambda \omega \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} \qquad \mathrm { a n d } \qquad \exp \left\{ - \lambda \omega \sqrt { - 1 } \right\} } \end{array}
$$

be the two roots where $\lambda$ is real but $\lambda \omega / \pi$ is incommensurable.The system of differential equations is transformed to the form (64). If $g$ is a nonzero constant, then the nondisturbed motion is unstable for $g > 0$ and stable for $g < 0$ . In the latter case all disturbed motions asymptotically approach the nondisturbed motion for sufficiently small perturbations.

Liapounov (1966) in two papers published in 1893 and in his posthumous work found in his archives after his death and published by Basov,discussed the cases in which the fundamental determinant admits a double zero-root and the remaining roots have negative real parts.The still missing subcase was solved by Pliss (Liapounov, 1966). Liapounov considered

$$
{ \frac { d x } { d t } } = y + X , \frac { d y } { d t } = Y , \frac { d z _ { s } } { d t } = a _ { s 1 } z _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { s k } z _ { k } + Z _ { s }
$$

(s = 1,2,...,k),

where $X , Y , Z _ { s }$ are holomorphic in $x , y , z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { k }$ $\overset { \prime } { k } = n - 2 )$ and the expansions of $X , Y , Z _ { s }$ contain no term of degree lower than the second,and where one of the first minors of the determinant formed of $a _ { s \sigma }$ is not zero and the fundamental determinant admits only roots with negative real parts. The equation $y + X = 0$ is solved in the form $y = A _ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } + A _ { 3 } x ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ with constants $A _ { 2 } , A _ { 3 } , \ldots$ Let $f ( x )$ be the result of replacing $y$ by this series in the expression for $\pmb { Y }$ ，and $\phi ( x )$ be the result of the same substitution in' the expression for $\partial X / \partial x + \partial Y / \partial y$ .We have

$$
Y = f ( x ) + \phi ( x ) y + \cdots ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { f ( x ) = a x ^ { \alpha } + a _ { 1 } x ^ { \alpha + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } & & { ( a \neq 0 , \alpha \geq 2 ) , } \\ & { \phi ( x ) = b x ^ { \beta } + b _ { 1 } x ^ { \beta + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } & & { ( \beta \geq 1 ) . } \end{array}
$$

Liapounov (1966) discussed possible cases for the values of $\alpha , \beta , a , b$ as to the stability of the motion. Pliss supplemented the missing cases.

Artemiev (1938) and Andronov and Witt (1933) considered the nonlinear variational equations from the standpoint of a periodic solution and discussed the stability according to Liapounov's theory. Adamov (1935） studied the periodic solutions,basing his work on Sturm's. oscillation theorem (Ince,1927).

Moustakhichev (1968) discussed the stability of the equilibrium position of a Hamiltonian system of two equations with periodic coefficients when the two roots of the fundamental determinant are equal to unity in modulus.He derived a sufficient condition for the instability by constructing the Liapounov or Cetaev function，and obtained a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of principal resonance.

Moser (1958） generalized Liapounov's theorem to a Hamiltonian system with equilibrium solution $x _ { \nu } = y _ { \nu } = 0 \ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ on the basis of Siegel's theory (Section 20.6) on the convergence of formal transformation of Birkhoff (Section 20.1)． Cherry's proof (1928; Section 14.17) is unsatisfactory.

# MOTION NEAR AN EQUILIBRIUM POINT

# 16.8BOHL'S THEOREMS

Bohl (1904,1906,and especially 1910) discussed similar equations of mechanics for obtaining solutions in quasi-periodic functions (1893; Section 2.1O).Consider the system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = a _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { s n } x _ { n } + X _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $a _ { s \sigma }$ are constants such that the characteristic equation

$$
\left| \begin{array} { l l l l } { a _ { 1 1 } - \kappa } & { a _ { 1 2 } } & { \cdots } & { a _ { 1 n } } \\ { a _ { 2 1 } } & { a _ { 2 2 } - \kappa } & { \cdots } & { a _ { 2 n } } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { } \\ { a _ { n 1 } } & { a _ { n 2 } } & { \cdots } & { a _ { n n } - \kappa } \end{array} \right| = 0
$$

admits only roots with nonzero real parts,and the $X _ { s }$ are continuous functions of $t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ， satisfying the condition (A): $a _ { s } < x _ { s } < b _ { s } ,$ or $a _ { s } < x _ { s }$ or $x _ { s } < b _ { s }$ ，or $x _ { s }$ arbitrary, $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ for real values of t, $t > \bar { t } _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ (Case I)，or $t < \bar { t }$ (Case II)，or $t$ arbitrary (Case III). Further we assume that a number $\gamma > 0$ can be chosen such that $x _ { s }$ whose absolute values are not larger than $\gamma$ satisfy the condition (A) and that

$$
\left| \frac { \partial X _ { s } } { \partial x _ { \sigma } } \right| < \Delta _ { 1 } , \qquad | X _ { s } ^ { ( 0 ) } | < \Delta _ { 2 } \gamma
$$

for $- \gamma < x _ { s } < + \gamma$ ，where $X _ { s } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ denote the values of $X _ { s }$ for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots$ $= x _ { n } = 0$ ，and $\Delta _ { 1 } , \Delta _ { 2 }$ are positive constants determined by $a _ { s \sigma }$ . This assumption is verified, for example, by

$$
\frac { d y _ { s } } { d t } = Y _ { s } + \eta _ { s } ~ ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

if, for the values of $y$ satisfying the conditions (B): $\alpha _ { s } < y _ { s } < \beta _ { s }$ $( \alpha _ { s } < 0 , \beta _ { s } > 0 )$ ， $\boldsymbol { Y } _ { s }$ are functions of $y _ { j }$ vanishing for $y _ { 1 } = \cdots = y _ { n }$ $= 0$ ，and $\partial Y _ { s } / \partial y _ { j }$ exist and are continuous for the values of $y$ satisfying the condition (B). If the $a _ { s \sigma }$ are the values of $\partial Y _ { s } / \partial y _ { \sigma }$ for $y _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot$ $= y _ { n } = 0$ ，then the characteristic equation admits only those roots with nonzero real parts, and $\eta _ { s }$ are given functions of $t , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ for the values of $y$ satisfying the condition (B) and for all values of $\pmb { t }$ Also $\eta _ { s }$ and $\partial \eta _ { s } / \partial y _ { j }$ are finite and continuous for those values of $\mathfrak { y }$ and $t$ ：

By a linear substitution with constant coefficients whose determinant is not zero, we introduce $_ n$ linear forms $y$ with respect to $x$

$$
y _ { s } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { s \mu } x _ { \mu } .
$$

Then the transformed system (67) is divided into two groups :

$$
\frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } = \lambda z _ { 1 } + \zeta _ { 1 } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d z _ { 2 } } { d t } = z _ { 1 } + \lambda z _ { 2 } + \zeta _ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d z _ { * } } { d t } = z _ { * - 1 } + \lambda z _ { * } + \zeta _ { * } ;
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle \frac { d u _ { 1 } } { d t } = g u _ { 1 } - h v _ { 1 } + \eta ~ , } } & { { \displaystyle \frac { d v _ { 1 } } { d t } = g v _ { 1 } + h u _ { 1 } + \vartheta _ { 1 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d u _ { 2 } } { d t } = u _ { 1 } + g u _ { 2 } - b v _ { 2 } + \eta _ { 2 } , } } & { { \displaystyle \frac { d v _ { 2 } } { d t } = v _ { 1 } + g v _ { 2 } + h u _ { 2 } + \vartheta _ { 2 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle . . . } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d u _ { e } } { d t } = u _ { e - 1 } + g u _ { e } - h v _ { e } + \eta _ { e } , } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d v _ { e } } { d t } = v _ { e - 1 } + g v _ { e } + h u _ { e } + \vartheta _ { e } , } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

where $\lambda$ is a real root and $g \pm h { \sqrt { - 1 } }$ a pair of conjugate imaginary roots of the characteristic equation. The number of equations of the group (I) is equal to the order of multiplicity of the root $\lambda$ ，and that of the group (I) is equal to twice the order of multiplicity of the root $g + h { \sqrt { - 1 } }$ ，and $\zeta , \eta , \vartheta$ are linear homogeneous expressions in $X _ { s }$ whose coefficients depend only on $a _ { s \sigma }$

For the group (I) we form

$$
G = \sum _ { \alpha = 1 } ^ { \nu } \lambda ^ { 2 \alpha - 1 } \mathcal { Z } _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { d G } { d t } = \lambda ^ { 2 } z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \lambda ^ { 2 \nu } z _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { \alpha = 2 } ^ { \nu } ( \lambda ^ { \alpha } z _ { \alpha } + \lambda ^ { \alpha - 1 } z _ { \alpha - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + G _ { 1 } ,
$$

with a bilinear form of $z + \zeta$ whose coeffcients depend only on $a _ { s { \sigma } }$ For the group (II) we form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal H } = \sum _ { \beta = 1 } ^ { \rho } g ^ { 2 \beta - 1 } ( u _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { { \displaystyle \frac { d H } { d t } = g ^ { 2 } ( u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) + g ^ { 2 \rho } ( u _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } ) } } } \\ { { { ~ + ~ \sum _ { \beta = 2 } ^ { \rho } [ ( g ^ { \beta } u _ { \beta } + g ^ { \beta - 1 } u _ { \beta - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( g ^ { \beta } v _ { \beta } + g ^ { \beta - 1 } v _ { \beta - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } ] ~ + H _ { 1 } . } } } \end{array}
$$

Then we divide $y$ into two systems : $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { k }$ which correspond to the roots with positive real parts, and $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l }$ which correspond to the roots with negative real parts.Add to each system the quantities $G$ and $\pmb { H }$ respectively. Then, with $d > 0$ and $f < 0$ which depend only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ ，we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv P _ { 1 } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } - P _ { 2 } \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { s } ^ { 2 } } , } & \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \equiv Q _ { 1 } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } - Q _ { 2 } \sqrt { \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { s } ^ { 2 } } , } & \end{array}
$$

where $P _ { 1 } , Q _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } , Q _ { 2 }$ depend only on $a _ { s \sigma } , P _ { 1 } > 0 , Q _ { 1 } > 0$

If $X _ { s }$ admit derivatives with respect to $x$ up to the order $m$ ，which are continuous functions of $t$ and $x$ ，then $x _ { s }$ also admit continuous derivatives with respect to the initial values up to the order $_ m$ . Then $y _ { s }$ also admit continuous derivatives with respect to the initial values up to the order $_ m$ .

Introduce the domain

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \equiv - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad ( \delta > 0 ) ,
$$

where $\delta$ is sufficiently small, chosen so that $p , q$ belonging to this domain are inside the domain indirectly defined by $_ y$ through the inequality $- \gamma < x _ { s } < \gamma$ .Let $\Delta _ { 1 } > 0$ ， $\Delta _ { 2 } > 0$ satisfy

$$
| P _ { 2 } | n ( \Delta _ { 2 } + \Delta _ { 1 } n ) < \sqrt { \frac { \delta } { d _ { 0 } } } P _ { 1 } , | Q _ { 2 } | n ( \Delta _ { 2 } + \Delta _ { 1 } n ) < \sqrt { \frac { \delta } { | f _ { 0 } | } } Q _ { 1 } ,
$$

where $\Delta _ { 1 } , \Delta _ { 2 }$ depend only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ ，and $d _ { 0 } , \vert f _ { 0 } \vert$ are the largest numbers among $d _ { \mu } , \vert f _ { \nu } \vert$ ，respectively. We suppose that in the domain $- \gamma < x _ { s }$ $< \gamma$ we have $| \partial X _ { s } / \partial x _ { j } | < \Delta _ { 1 } , | X _ { s } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } / \gamma | < \Delta _ { 2 }$ ; then

$$
X _ { s } = X _ { s } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 0 ) } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \ L ^ { n } } \left\{ { \frac { \partial X _ { s } } { \partial x _ { \mu } } } \right\} x _ { \mu } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $\{ \partial X _ { s } / \partial x _ { \mu } \}$ denotes the value of $\partial X _ { s } / \partial x _ { \mu }$ for a certain set of values of $x _ { s }$ in $- \gamma < x _ { s } < \gamma$ also,

$$
\sqrt { \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { s } ^ { 2 } } \subseteq \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | X _ { s } | < n \Delta _ { 2 } \gamma + n ^ { 2 } \Delta _ { 1 } \gamma .
$$

From (74) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { | P _ { 2 } | \sqrt { \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { s } ^ { 2 } } < \sqrt { \frac { \overline { { \delta } } } { d _ { 0 } } } \cdot P _ { 1 } \gamma , \qquad | Q _ { 2 } | \sqrt { \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { s } ^ { 2 } } < \sqrt { \frac { \overline { { \delta } } } { | f _ { 0 } | } } Q _ { 1 } \gamma . } \end{array}
$$

First,consider the case in which the coordinates $p , q$ of the solution satisfy

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } = \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \equiv - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } ,
$$

for a certain value of t. From (72), the inequalities (75), and

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } \ : p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \frac { \delta } { d _ { 0 } } \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

obtained from (76),we find after calculation

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } > 0 .
$$

If, as the second case, for a certain value of $t$ ，we have

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } = - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } ,
$$

then we find similarly

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } > 0 .
$$

We now present several new theorems.

Theorem 1: By choosing a degree of smallness conveniently we can associate with any system of $q$ belonging to the domain

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \geq - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

and with each admissible value of $t$ ，at least one system of $p$ belonging to the domain

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } < \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

such that p,q,t, taken as the initial values, determine a solution that remains for all larger values of $t$ inside the domain

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } > - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } < \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } .
$$

If the interval is $t < \tau$ or $t$ arbitrary, then the role of $p$ and $q$ should be interchanged.

In the case in which the system $q$ does not exist, we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } > 0
$$

if $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } = \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }$ . If the admissible values of $t$ are defined by $t > \tau$ or $t$ arbitrary,then there exists for any admissible value of $t _ { 0 }$ of $t$ a solution that remains in the domain

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ .If the admissible interval of $t$ is defined by $t < \tau$ 0r $t$ arbitrary, then a solution which for a value $t$ is in the above domain (a) remains in that domain for all smaller values of $t$

In the case in which the system $p$ does not exist, we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } > 0
$$

if $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } = - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ . If the admissible value of $t$ is defined by $t > \tau$ or $t$ arbitrary,then any solution that is in the domain

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \geq - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

for an arbitrary value of $t$ remains in the same domain (b) for all larger values of $t$ 、If the admissible interval of $t$ is defined by $t < \tau$ or $t$ arbitrary, then for any admissible value $t _ { 0 }$ of $t$ there exists a solution that remains in the same domain (b) for all smaller values of $t$

The constant $\delta > 0$ depends on $a _ { s { \sigma } }$ and is chosen so that $\pmb { p }$ in the domain (a) or $\pmb q$ in the domain (b) leads to the values of $x$ such that $\vert x \vert < \gamma$

Auxiliary Lemma: Let $_ n$ functions $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ be given， satisfying for all values of $t > \tau$ [or $t < \tau$ or $t$ arbitrary] the system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( x ) + X _ { s 0 } + X _ { s } ~ ( s = 1 , \dots , n ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { L }$ denotes the linear expressions of $x$ in (67)，and $X _ { s 0 } , X _ { s }$ are functions of $t$ satisfying

$$
\begin{array} { r } { | X _ { s 0 } | < d , \qquad | X _ { s } | \le \kappa \sqrt { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $d$ is an arbitrary constant, and $\kappa > 0$ a constant to be chosen later so that $\pmb { \kappa }$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ . Let $y , p , q$ be the same linear expressions of $x$ ，and $d _ { \mu } , f _ { \nu }$ be the constants just spoken of.

Put

$$
\sigma = \sqrt { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { j } ^ { 2 } } , \qquad s = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

and form $d s / d t$ by means of (80). The term $\pmb { L }$ of (80) gives a positivedefinite quadratic form which i $\mathrm { i } \mathsf { s } \equiv \kappa _ { 1 } \sigma ^ { 2 }$ ，where $\kappa _ { 1 } > 0$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ . The term $X _ { s 0 }$ gives an expression whose absolute value $\leq \kappa _ { 3 } d \sigma$ ， where $\kappa _ { 3 } \geq 0$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ ，and $X _ { s }$ gives a term numerically $\leq \kappa _ { 2 } \kappa \sigma ^ { 2 }$ ，with $\kappa _ { 2 } \geq 0$ depending only on $a _ { s { \sigma } }$ . Thus

$$
\frac { d s } { d t } \geq \kappa _ { 1 } \sigma ^ { 2 } - \kappa \kappa _ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } - \kappa _ { 3 } d \sigma .
$$

Put $r = \kappa _ { 1 } - \kappa \kappa _ { 2 } > 0$ ，then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d s } { d \bar { t } } } \geq r \sigma ^ { 2 } - \kappa _ { 3 } d \sigma } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\left| \frac { d \sigma ^ { 2 } } { d t } \right| \leq r _ { 0 } \sigma ^ { 2 } + r _ { 1 } d \sigma ,
$$

where $r _ { \mathrm { 0 } } > 0$ ， $r _ { 1 } > 0$ are determined by $a _ { s _ { \sigma } }$ .Introduce ${ \pmb \sigma } _ { 0 } =$ $( \kappa _ { 3 } + \varepsilon _ { 0 } ) d / r$ ，where $\varepsilon _ { \boldsymbol { 0 } } > 0$ depends only on $a _ { s { \sigma } }$ but is arbitrary，and suppose that for a certain value of $t$ we have $\sigma \geq \sigma _ { 0 }$ ; then

$$
\frac { d s } { d t } \geq \sigma ( r \sigma - \kappa _ { 3 } d ) \geq \sigma _ { 0 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } d .
$$

Thus we obtain

Theorem 2: Case I: If $\sigma = \Sigma \geq \sigma _ { 0 }$ for a given value of $t$ ，then for all values exceeding $t$ we have $\sigma < g \Sigma$ .Case II: If $\sigma = \Sigma \geq \sigma _ { 0 }$ for a value of $t$ ，then for all values of $t$ we have $\sigma < g \Sigma$ . Case III:If $\pmb { \sigma }$ is never equal to $\Sigma \geq \sigma _ { 0 }$ ，then for all values of $\pmb { t }$ we have $\sigma < g \Sigma$ The number $g$ depends only on $a _ { s { \sigma } }$

If $X _ { s 0 }$ are all zero,then from (81) and (81a) we have

$$
\frac { d s } { d t } \geq r \sigma ^ { 2 } , \left| \frac { d \sigma ^ { 2 } } { d t } \right| \\leq r _ { 0 } \sigma ^ { 2 } .
$$

Hence we arrive at the following :

In Case III al functions $x$ are zero.In Case I we have $s \leqq c \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }$ ， $c > 0$ ， accordingly $s \geq 0$ ， and for sufficiently large values of $t$ we have $\sigma <$ ${ \mathfrak { g } } { \mathfrak {sigma } } _ { 0 }$ ，accordingly $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \sigma ^ { 2 } = 0$ .Thus all functions $x$ , and consequently the function $s$ ，tend to zero as $t$ increases indefinitely.Similarly in Case II we have $s \geq 0$ ，and all functions $x$ ， together with $\pmb { \sigma }$ and $s$ ， tend to zero as $t$ decreases indefinitely.

From this theorem it follows that, if $s$ is zero for a value of $t$ ，then all functions $x$ are zero for all values of $t$

Consider two solutions $x$ and $x + z$ of the system (67). These two solutions remain in the domain $- \gamma < x _ { s } < + \gamma$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ [or for $t < t _ { 0 }$ or for all values of $t ]$ . Let $\pmb { L } ( z )$ of (80） be homogeneous linear forms in $z$ of the same form as those in $x$ in the right-hand members of (67). Then $z$ satisfies

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } = L ( z ) + X ( x + z , t ) - X ( x , t ) .
$$

Under the same assumptions as those in the foregoing discussion we see that all quantities $z$ are zero in Case III, satisfy $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } z = 0$ in Case I, and satisfy $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to - \infty } z = 0$ in Case II. Hence the two solutions tend asymptotically to each other in Case I as $t$ increases indefinitely, tend asymptotically to each other in Case I as $t$ decreases indefinitely, and coincide in Case III.

Let $\pi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pi _ { k } , \rho _ { 1 } , \ldots , \rho _ { l }$ be the quantities formed from $z$ in the same way as the quantities $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { k } , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l }$ are formed from $z$ ， Put

$$
s = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } \pi _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } \rho _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } .
$$

Obviously $s \leq 0$ in Case I, and $s \geq 0$ in Case II. If once $\rho _ { 1 } = \cdots$ $\mathbf { \rho } = \rho _ { \mathfrak { t } } = 0$ in Case I or if the group $\rho$ does not exist, then we have

$$
s = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } \pi _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv 0 ,
$$

where $\pi _ { 1 } = \cdots = \pi _ { k } = 0$ if the group $\pi$ exists.Consequently all the $z ^ { \ast } s$ are zero for all admissible values of $t .$ 、Thus the two solutions coincide if for an arbitrary value of $t$ they give the same values of $\pmb q$ or if the system $q$ does not exist, since $\rho$ is the difference between the quantities $q$ corresponding to the two solutions. Similarly the two solutions coincide if for an arbitrary value of $\pmb { t }$ the values of $\pmb { p }$ coincide or if the system $p$ does not exist.

We have sen that there exists at least one solution remaining in the domain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \leq \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , } \\ { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \geq - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } } \end{array}
$$

for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ ，where $\tau _ { 0 }$ is an arbitrarily given value of $t$ Let $\tau$ be an arbitrary determinate value of $t$ Choose for $\tau _ { 0 }$ successively $\tau _ { 0 } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ ， $\tau _ { 0 } ^ { ( 2 ) } , \dots ,$ forming a sequence of indefinitely decreasing quantities. To each value $\tau _ { 0 } ^ { ( \mu ) }$ let there correspond a solution remaining in the domain (84) for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 } ^ { ( \mu ) }$ . Denote by $[ y ] _ { \mu }$ the set of values which $y$ of the μth solution takes for $t < \tau$ Since all $[ y ] _ { \mu }$ remain in the domain (84) they have at least one point of accumulation in the domain.Let $[ Y ]$ be such a point.It can be shown that the solution taking the value $[ Y ]$ for $t = \tau$ always remains in the domain (84). Hence the existence of at least one solution remaining always in the domain (84) has been proved. That there exists only one such solution was shown in the foregoing discussion. Thus we have the

Fundamental Theorem: Let $_ n$ differential equations (67） with constants $a _ { s \sigma }$ be given,for which the characteristic equation does not admit any root with a zero real part. The functions $X _ { s }$ are given functions of $_ x$ and $t$ for all values of $x$ in the domain $a _ { s } < x _ { s } < b _ { s }$ ，and for all values of $t$ or for those satisfying $t > \tau$ or $t < \tau$ .Also $X _ { s }$ admit the derivatives $\partial X _ { s } / \partial x _ { j }$ which, together with $X _ { s }$ ， are continuous in $x$ and $t$ in that domain.Finally,suppose that there is a domain $- \gamma <$ $x < + \gamma \left( \gamma > 0 \right)$ ，whose bounds are contained in the above domain, where $X _ { s } / r$ for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ and $\vert \partial X _ { s } / \partial x _ { j } \vert$ are smaller than certain numbers $\Delta _ { \imath } > 0$ or $\Delta _ { 2 } > 0$ determined by $a _ { s \sigma }$
