Introduce a linear substitution from $x$ to y with constant coefficients depending only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ and with a nonzero determinant. Separate y into two systems $p$ and $q$ ，the first system corresponding to the roots of the characteristic equation with positive real parts and the second system to those with negative real parts.

Case I. Suppose that the admissible values of $t$ are defined by $t > \tau$ . To each set of values $[ q ]$ of $q$ in the domain (84b) and to each admissible value $\tau _ { 0 }$ of $t$ there corresponds one and only one set of values $[ p ]$ of $\pmb { p }$ in the domain (84a),such that the solution with the initial values $[ p ] , [ q ]$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ remains in the domain (84a,b) for all values $t > \tau _ { 0 }$ . If the characteristic equation does not admit any root with a positive real part,that is,if the system $\pmb { p }$ does not exist, then any solution in the domain (84a,b) for a value of $t$ remains in that domain for all larger values of $t$ If the characteristic equation does not admit any root with a negative real part, that is, if the system $\pmb q$ does not exist, then there is one and only one solution remaining in the domain (84a,b).Finally,two arbitrary solutions remaining in the domain (84a,b) tend asymptotically to each other as $t \to \infty$

Case II. Suppose that the admissible values of $t$ are defined by $t < \tau$ ，then we obtain an analogous theorem by inverting the role of $p$ and $q$ and replacing $t = \infty$ by $t = - \infty$ ， $t > \tau _ { 0 }$ by $t < \tau _ { 0 }$ ，and larger values of $t$ by smaller values of $t$

Case III. In the case where $t$ is arbitrary, the above two theorems hold simultaneously. Furthermore, there exists one and only one solution remaining in the domain (84a,b) for all values of $t$ Any other solution remaining finally in the domain (84a, b) for $t = \pm \infty$ asymptotically approaches this solution.

A solution corresponding to $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l } , t _ { 0 }$ means in Case I the solution whose elements take the values $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l } , [ p _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ p _ { k } ]$ for $t = t _ { 0 } ,$ where $[ p ]$ are the values of $p$ corresponding to $t _ { 0 } , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l }$ A solution corresponding to $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { k } , \tau _ { 0 }$ means in Case II the solu-tion whose elements take the values $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { k } , [ q _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ q _ { l } ]$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ where $[ q ]$ are the values of $q$ corresponding to $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { k } , \tau _ { 0 }$ Let $\{ q \} _ { 1 }$ ， $\{ q \} _ { 2 }$ be two arbitrary sets of values of $q$ belonging to the domain (84b), and $\{ p \} _ { 1 } , \{ p \} _ { 2 }$ be two arbitrary sets of values of $p$ belonging to the domain (84a),then $t _ { 0 }$ or $\tau _ { 0 }$ is one of the admissible values of $t$ in Case I or II, respectively. Two solutions corresponding to $\{ q \} _ { 1 } , t _ { 0 }$ and $\{ q \} _ { 2 } , t _ { 0 }$ remain in the domain (84a,b) for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ and hence in the domain $- \gamma < x < \gamma$ for the values of $t$ in $t > t _ { 1 }$ [or $t _ { 1 } < t _ { 0 } ]$ . Two solutions $\{ p \} _ { 1 } , \tau _ { 0 }$ and $\{ p \} _ { 2 } , \tau _ { 0 }$ remain in the domain (84a,b) for $t \leqq \tau _ { 0 }$ and hence in the domain $- \gamma < x < \gamma$ for $t < \tau _ { 1 }$ [or $\tau _ { 1 } > \tau _ { 0 } ]$ . Accordingly we apply the previous result and denote by $\pi$ and $\rho$ ,respectively,the difference of $p$ and $q$ for the same value of $t .$ Let

$$
s = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \ k } d _ { \mu } \pi _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \ \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } \rho _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sigma ^ { 2 } = \ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } \pi _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \ \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } \rho _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

and let $\pi _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , \pi _ { k } ^ { 0 } , \rho _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , \rho _ { l } ^ { 0 }$ be the values of $\pi$ and $\rho$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ [or

$t = \tau _ { 0 } ]$ . If the solutions in each pair are different, then in Case I we have $s < 0 , d t / d s > 0 , | \sigma ^ { 2 } / s | \le r _ { 0 } / r$ for $t > t _ { 1 }$ ,and in Case II we have $s > 0 , d s / d t > 0 , | \sigma ^ { 2 } / s | \le r _ { 0 } / r$ for $t < \tau _ { 0 }$ . Denote the value of $\pmb { s }$ at $t = t _ { 0 }$ [or $t = \tau _ { 0 } ]$ by $s _ { 0 }$ ，then in Case I,

| s _ { 0 } | \leq | F | \cdot \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \ell } ~ ( \rho _ { \nu } ^ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } ,

where $\left| F \right|$ and $_ D$ are the largest values among $| f |$ and $^ d$ respectively. Since $\left| s \right| \leqq \left| s _ { 0 } \right|$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ [or $t \le \tau _ { 0 } ]$ ，we obtain $\sigma ^ { 2 } / | s _ { 0 } | \le t _ { 0 } / r$ for the same value of $t$ ,hence, in Case I for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，

$$

\sigma ^ { 2 } \leq { \frac { r _ { 0 } } { r } } D \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } ( \pi _ { \mu } ^ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

These relations prove the

Lemma: The quantities $[ p ]$ are continuous functions of the quantities $q$ in the domain (84b),and the quantities $[ q ]$ are continuous functions of $p$ in the domain (84a） for an arbitrarily chosen value of $t$

Take the equations

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d \bar { p } _ { \mu } } { d t } } = L _ { \mu } ( \bar { p } ) + H _ { \mu } ~ } } & { { ~ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k ) , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d \bar { q } _ { \nu } } { d t } } = I _ { \nu } ( \bar { q } ) + K _ { \nu } ~ } } & { { ~ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , l ) . } } \end{array}
$$

By hypothesis we can assign to $| \partial H / \partial p | , | \partial H / \partial q | , | \partial K / \partial p | , | \partial K / \partial q |$ a degree of smallness arbitrarily chosen,depending only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ . In Case I we choose an arbitrary value $t _ { 0 }$ and an arbitrary set $\{ q \}$ belonging to the domain

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } < - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } .

Similarly in Case II we choose an arbitrary value $\tau _ { 0 }$ and an arbitrary set $\{ p \}$ belonging to the domain

$$

The solutions corresponding to $t _ { 0 } , \left\{ q \right\} \left[ \mathrm { o r } \tau _ { 0 } , \left\{ p \right\} \right]$ ,extend to the interval $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ [or $t \leq \tau _ { 0 } ]$ and can be prolonged to the interval $t _ { 1 } < t < t _ { 0 }$ [or $\tau _ { 1 } > \tau > \tau _ { 0 } ]$ such that they remain in the domain $Y$ ，provided that $t _ { 1 }$ and $\tau _ { 1 }$ are suitably chosen.The equations for variations relative to these solutions are

$$
\frac { d \bar { p } _ { \mu } } { d t } = { \cal L } _ { \mu } ( \bar { p } ) + \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial p _ { \lambda } } \bar { p } _ { \lambda } + \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { \ l } \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial q _ { \sigma } } \bar { q } _ { \sigma }
$$

$$
\frac { d \bar { q } _ { \nu } } { d t } = I _ { \nu } ( \bar { q } ) + \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial K _ { \nu } } { \partial p _ { \lambda } } \bar { p } _ { \lambda } + \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { l } \frac { \partial K _ { \nu } } { \partial q _ { \sigma } } \bar { q } _ { \sigma }
$$

From these we find

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \Bigg [ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } \bar { p } _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } \bar { q } _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \Bigg ] = Q _ { 1 } + Q _ { 2 } ,
$$

where $Q _ { 1 }$ and $Q _ { 2 }$ are quadratic in $\overline { { p } } , \overline { { q } }$ .The function $Q _ { 1 }$ is a positivedefinite form obtained from $L _ { \mu } ( \bar { p } ) , I _ { \nu } ( \bar { q } )$ , whose coefficients depend only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ . The coefficients of $Q _ { 2 }$ can be assigned a degree of smallness in absolute value, depending only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ . Put

$$
S = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } \overline { { { p } } } _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } \overline { { { q } } } _ { \nu } ^ { 2 }
$$

then

$$
| S | \leq c \bigg ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } \bar { p } _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } \bar { q } _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \bigg ) ,
$$

where $c > 0$ is the largest among $^ d$ and $| f |$ ，and we obtain

$$
\frac { d S } { d t } \geq \frac { R } { c } | S | .
$$

Thus it can be proved that two solutions of (87a), (87b) that remain between finite bounds for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in Case I [or for $t \leqq _ { \tau _ { 0 } }$ in Case II] and have the same values of $\bar { q }$ [or $\overline { { p } } ]$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ [or $t = \tau _ { 0 } ]$ must coincide.

Let $\rho _ { 0 }$ and $\pi _ { 0 }$ be the respective values of $\rho _ { \nu }$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ and of $\pi _ { \mu }$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ ，where $\rho _ { 0 }$ and $\pi _ { 0 }$ are by hypothesis different from zero.By (85a),(85b) the quantities $\pi _ { \mu } / \rho _ { 0 } , \rho _ { \nu } / \rho _ { 0 }$ remain between finite bounds which depend only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ for all values $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in Case I,and for all values $t \leqq \tau _ { 0 }$ in Case II. Let the equations (83) be

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( x ) + X _ { s } ~ ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $L _ { s }$ are homogeneous linear expressions in $x$ ，and $X _ { s }$ are continuous functions of $t$ for all values of $t > t _ { 0 }$ or $t$ arbitrary,and remain between finite bounds for those values of $t$ .The equations (91) are a particular case of (67) with $\boldsymbol { \gamma }$ sufficiently large,accordingly，given an admissible value $t _ { 1 }$ of $t$ and an arbitrary set $q _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , q _ { l } ^ { 0 }$ ，there exists one and only one solution that remains between finite bounds for $t \geq t _ { 1 }$ and takes the values $q _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , . . . , q _ { l } = q _ { l } ^ { 0 }$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$ . In the case in which $t$ is arbitrary there exists one and only one solution that remains between finite bounds for all values of $t$ ，The equations are divided into the groups (I) and (II) as defined at the beginning of this section, where $\pmb { p }$ correspond to positive values of $\lambda$ and $\pmb { g }$ ，and $q$ correspond to negative values of $\lambda$ and $g$ . The solution leads to the integration of

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } = \lambda z + \zeta ,
$$

$$
\frac { d u } { d t } = g u - h v + \psi , \frac { d v } { d t } = g v + h u + \phi ,
$$

where $\zeta , \psi , \phi$ are continuous functions of $t$ that remain between finite limits for $t \geq t _ { 1 }$ or $t$ arbitrary, and where $\lambda > 0 , g > 0$ .We have then

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { z = - e ^ { 2 t } \displaystyle \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \imath t } \zeta d t , } } \\ { { \displaystyle u = - \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \imath ( y - t ) } \{ \psi ( y ) \cos { [ h ( y - t ) ] } + \phi ( y ) \sin { [ h ( y - t ) ] } \} d y , } } \\ { { \displaystyle v = \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \varrho ( y - t ) } \{ \psi ( y ) \sin { [ h ( y - t ) ] } - \phi ( y ) \cos { [ h ( y - t ) ] } \} d y . } } \end{array}
$$

For $\lambda < 0 , g < 0$ ，we replace the upper limit of integration by $- \infty$

For simplicity, denote the domain $- \gamma < x _ { s } < \gamma ~ ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ by $( X )$ ，and consider the solution of (91) that remains in $( X )$ . Let an approximate solution be given for which the elements remain in $( X )$ for $t > \tau$ and which admits continuous finite derivatives for those values of $t$ and satisfies the equations (67) with an error numerically less than $d > 0$ . Suppose further that the elements $x _ { 0 }$ of this approximate solution have the same values as the elements $x _ { 0 } + z$ corresponding to the exact solution for a certain value $\tau _ { 0 }$ of $t$ 、The equations (67) give, for $| r _ { s } | < d$ ，

$$
\frac { d z _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( z ) + X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } + z ) - X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } ) + r _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

If we attribute to $| \partial X / \partial x |$ a degree of smallness conformable to the previous hypothesis,then the Auxiliary Lemma can be applied to $z$ Introduce $\bar { p } , \bar { q }$ depending on $z$ in the same way as $p , q$ depend on $_ x$ ， and note that $\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } = 0$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ ， then we see that $| z | < e d$ for $\pmb { t } \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ . Thus we can estimate the accuracy of the approximation. Let

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } < \delta _ { 1 } \gamma ^ { 2 } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \nu } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } > - \delta _ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

be the two domains.We can choose the degree of smallness such that to a set of quantities $q$ in the domain (92b) and to any value $\tau _ { 0 }$ in the admissible interval of $t$ there corresponds one and only one set of quantities $p$ belonging to the domain (92a） such that the solution whose elements take the values $p , q$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ and remain in the domain (92a) for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ . Choose $\delta _ { 1 }$ and $\delta _ { 2 }$ conformably to this condition,such that all quantities $| x |$ corresponding to the set $p , q$ of (92a) and (92b) are smaller than $\gamma / 2$ . Given a set of values $( q ) _ { 0 }$ of $\pmb q$ belonging to (92b),we consider the solution that remains in (92a),(92b) for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ and whose coordinates become $( q ) _ { 0 }$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ . We can prolong this solution for $t < \tau _ { 0 }$ so that it remains in $( X )$ if $t$ is restricted to the values sufficiently near $\tau _ { 0 }$ .Let $t > \tau _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ be an approximate solution, where $\tau _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ is sufficiently near $\tau _ { 0 }$ so that the exact solution with its prolongation has its determinate sense also for $t > \tau _ { 0 }$ .Let $^ d$ be sufficiently small so that $e d < \gamma / 4$ or $d < \gamma / ( 4 e )$ . For $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ we have $| x _ { 0 } + z | <$ $\gamma / 2$ and $\vert z \vert < e d < \gamma / 4$ ，accordingly, $\vert x _ { 0 } \vert < \frac { 3 } { 4 } \gamma$

Consider the solution of

$$
\frac { d \bar { z } _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( \bar { z } ) + r _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

which satisfies the conditions $\bar { q } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \dots = \bar { q } _ { \mathit { l } } ^ { \prime } = 0$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ and remains between finite bounds for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ ，where the $\overline { { q } } ^ { \prime }$ are formed from z in the same way as $q$ are formed from $x$ . Since $| r _ { s } | < d$ for $t > \tau _ { 0 }$ ，it follows from what has just been shown that $| \bar { z } | < e d$ for $\scriptstyle t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ ；accordingly， $| \bar { z } | < \gamma / 4$ ，and $| x _ { 0 } + \bar { z } | < \gamma$ .This inequality together with $| \bar { z } | < e d$ is satisfed in a certain interval $\tau _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } < \tau _ { 1 } < t <$ $\tau _ { 0 }$ . Now take $x _ { 0 } + \bar { z }$ as an approximate solution for $t > \tau _ { 1 }$ ，since $x _ { 0 }$ $+ ~ \bar { z }$ remains inside the domain $( X )$ for $t > \tau _ { 1 }$ ，and the quantities formed by means of $x _ { 0 } + \bar { z }$ ， similarly to $q$ formed by means of $x$ ,take the values $( q ) _ { 0 }$ for $t = \tau _ { 0 }$ . Then we get

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ( x _ { 0 s } + \bar { z } _ { s } ) } { d t } = L _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } ) - r _ { s } + X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } ) + L _ { s } ( \bar { z } ) + r _ { s } } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { = L _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } + \bar { z } ) + X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } + \bar { z } ) - [ X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } + \bar { z } ) - X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } ) ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , } } } \end{array}
$$

where $x _ { 0 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 0 n }$ are the elements of the approximate solution $x _ { 0 }$ Suppose that in $( X )$ and for all admissible values of $t$ we have $\lvert \partial X / \partial x \rvert$ $\leq \Delta$ . Then，putting $\bar { r } _ { s } = X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } + \bar { z } ) - X _ { s } ( x _ { 0 } )$ ， we have $| \bar { r } _ { s } | < n e \Delta d$ for $t > \tau _ { 1 }$ . Choosing $\Delta$ such that $\omega = n e \Delta < 1 / 2$ ，we obtain

Lemma 1: Given an approximate solution $x _ { 0 }$ with the conditions just indicated， the approximation for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ is characterized by $| x _ { s } - x _ { 0 s } | < e d .$ ，where $e$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ . From the solution of the linear differential equation (93) we can form a second approximate solution with the same conditions satisfying the differential equations with a numerical error less than $\omega \boldsymbol { d }$ ，where $\omega < 1$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ . The approximation for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ is thus characterized by eωd. Proceeding similarly we can find approximate solutions of the same nature as the previous solutions but whose deviation for $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$ is characterized by $e \omega ^ { 2 } d$ ，and so forth.Since $0 < \omega < 1$ ， we can reach any approximation we please in the interval $t \geq \tau _ { 0 }$

Consider an approximate solution $x _ { 0 }$ remaining inside $( X )$ and satisfying the differential equations without the $r _ { s }$ terms that are numerically less than $d > 0$ .The difference between the elements $x$ of the exact solution and these elements $x _ { 0 }$ is numerically less than $e _ { 1 } d$ ， where $e _ { 1 }$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ , as is shown by the Auxiliary Lemma, provided that the degree of smallness is suitably chosen. Suppose that $d < \gamma / ( 4 e _ { 1 } )$ ， then $\vert x _ { 0 } \vert < \frac { 3 } { 4 } \gamma$ .We form the equations (93) and derive the solution that remains between finite bounds. The elements of the solution remain numerically less than $e _ { \mathrm { { 1 } } } d$ . Hence $| \bar { z } | < \gamma / 4$ ； accordingly, $x _ { 0 } + \bar { z }$ remains in $( X )$ for all values of $t$ If we take $x _ { 0 } + \bar { z }$ as an approximate solution,the differential equations are satisfied with an error numerically less than $n e _ { \mathrm { 1 } } d \Delta _ { \mathrm { 1 } }$ ,if $| \partial X / \partial x | < \Delta _ { \mathrm { { 1 } } }$ inside $( X )$ and for all values of t. Choose $\Delta _ { 1 } > 0$ ， depending only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ ，so that $\omega _ { 0 } =$ $n e _ { 1 } \Delta _ { 1 } < 1 / 2$ . Then the new approximate solution satisfies the differential equations with an error numerically less than $\omega _ { 0 } d$ ，where $\omega _ { 0 } < 1$ and the deviation of the new approximate solution from the exact solution is characterized by $e _ { 1 } \omega _ { 0 } d$ .Continuing this process，we obtain a solution whose deviation is characterized by $e _ { 1 } \omega _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } d$ ， and so forth.

It remains to find the first approximation solution for which $d <$ $\gamma / ( 4 e _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } )$ . For that purpose we form the equations

$$
\frac { d \bar { x } _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( \bar { x } ) + X _ { s 0 } ~ ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $X _ { s 0 }$ are the values of $X _ { s }$ for $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ .We derive the solution that remains between finite bounds for all values of t. Similarly to the preceding we can prove that $| \bar { x } | < \gamma / 2$ for that solution and that it satisfies the differential equations (67) without the terms $X _ { s } ( \bar { x } )$ $- \ X _ { s 0 }$ . But, since

$$
\begin{array} { r } { | X _ { s } ( \bar { x } ) - X _ { s 0 } | < n \Delta _ { 1 } \gamma / 2 = \omega _ { 0 } \gamma / ( 2 e _ { 1 } ) < \gamma / ( 4 e _ { 1 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

this solution of (94） satisfies all conditions imposed on the first approximate solution in question.

In the following we refer to the fundamental theorem for the existence of implicit functions.

Lemma 2: Let m functions $f _ { \mu } ( y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { m } , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } )$ of $m + n$ variables be given in the domains $( \alpha )$ and $( \beta )$ defined by

$$
\sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } \leq p ,
$$

$$
- \alpha _ { i } \leqq x _ { i } \leqq \alpha _ { i } ,
$$

where $p , \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ are positive numbers and $\textstyle \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j }$ is a positivedefinite quadratic form. Suppose that $\dot { f } _ { \mu }$ are continuous functions of $y _ { i }$ for each particular set of values of $x$ belonging to the domain $( \beta )$ and that they admit continuous first-order partial derivatives with respect to $y$ in the domain

$$
\sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } < p .
$$

Suppose also that the fundamental determinant $_ D$ of $f _ { \mu }$ with respect to $y _ { i }$ is different from zero and $| \Delta / D | < \delta$ ，where $\Delta$ is any first-order minor of $_ D$ and $\delta > 0 , y > 0 , x > 0$ in this domain,and that each of the derivatives $\partial f / \partial y$ takes the values different by at least ${ \varepsilon } =$ $1 / ( 3 m ^ { 2 } \delta )$ at two points $y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , y _ { m } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ and $y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , . . . , y _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } , x _ { 1 } , . . . ,$ $x _ { n }$ , and finally that

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } \ [ f _ { \mu } ( 0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) ] ^ { 2 } \equiv \frac { m \varepsilon ^ { 2 } p } { Q } ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { Q }$ is a positive number satisfying

$$
\sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } \leq Q \cdot \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } .
$$

Under these conditions there exists a set of values of $y _ { i }$ belonging to the domain $\textstyle \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } < p$ and satisfying

$$
f _ { \mu } ( y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { m } , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ) = 0 \qquad ( \mu = 1 , \dots , m )
$$

for any set of values of $x$ of the domain $( \beta )$ ， where the set is the only one in the domain $( \alpha )$ satisfying these equations.

To prove this lemma, Bohl expanded

$$
f _ { s } ( y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \dots , y _ { m } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ) - f _ { s } ( y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \dots , y _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } )
$$

$$
= \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial f _ { s } } { \partial y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime \prime } } ( y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } - y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime \prime } ) + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } p _ { s \mu } ( y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } - y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime \prime } ) ( s = 1 , \dots , n ) ,
$$

where the set $y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , y _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime }$ belongs to the domain (95） and $| p _ { s \mu } | < \varepsilon$ Put

$$
S = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { m } | y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } - y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime \prime } | ,
$$

then we get

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle m \delta \cdot \sum _ { s } | f _ { s } ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) - f _ { s } ( y ^ { \prime \prime } , x ) | \equiv S ( 1 - \delta \varepsilon m ^ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle m \delta \cdot \sum _ { t } | f _ { s } ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) - f _ { s } ( y ^ { \prime \prime } , x ) | \equiv \frac 2 3 S . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence, if all $f _ { \mu }$ vanish for $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ and for a set of values of $y$ belonging to (95), this set is the only one in the domain $( \alpha )$ satisfying this condition.

The relation (96) can be obtained by reductio ad absurdum. Accordingly for the values of $y$ satisfying $\textstyle \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } = p$ we have

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } \ [ f _ { s } ( y , x ) ] ^ { 2 } > \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } \ [ f _ { s } ( 0 , x ) ] ^ { 2 } .
$$

For a determinate set of values of $x$ arbitrarily chosen in the domain$( \beta )$ ， the function

$$
\omega = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } { [ f _ { s } ( y , x ) ] ^ { 2 } }
$$

takes its smallest value at least at a point of the domain $( \beta )$ ，where we cannot have $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } y _ { i } y _ { j } = p } \end{array}$ owing to (98).Hence we should have (95) at this point.Thus all partial derivatives $\partial \omega / \partial y$ vanish at this point,when we find $_ m$ solutions

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { m } f _ { s } \frac { \partial f _ { s } } { \partial y _ { \mu } } = 0 \qquad ( \mu = 1 , \dots , m ) .
$$

Since the functional determinant of $f$ with respect to $y$ is not zero, we should have $f _ { 1 } = f _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = f _ { m } = 0$ at this point.

Lemma 2 is also true if we limit the interval to the values of $\pmb { t }$ by $t > \tau$ [or $t$ arbitrary].

Theorem 4: To any point $( \omega _ { 1 } , \ldots , \omega _ { l } )$ of a certain domain

$$
- \sigma \gamma \le \omega _ { \rho } \le \sigma \gamma \qquad ( \rho = 1 , \ldots , l ) ,
$$

where $\sigma > 0$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ and on the nature of $\phi$ ，and to all values $t _ { 0 }$ of the admissible interval, there corresponds one and only one set of quantities $p , q$ belonging to the domain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \equiv - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

and satisfying

$$
\phi _ { \nu } ( p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { k } , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l } ) = \omega _ { \nu } \qquad ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , l ) ,
$$

such that the solution whose elements take the values $p , q$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ remains for $t > t _ { 0 }$ in the domain $( \alpha )$ ，where $d _ { \mu } , f _ { \nu } , \delta$ have the same meaning as in the foregoing discussion.

The proof starts with the determination of a region around $( 0 , \ldots ,$ $0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 )$ defined by

$$
A _ { \mu } \leq u _ { \mu } \leq B _ { \mu } , \qquad C _ { \nu } \leq v _ { \nu } \leq D _ { \nu } ,
$$

such that

$$
\begin{array}{c} D = | \cdots | \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial v _ { 1 } } +  \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial u _ { \rho } } \varepsilon _ { \rho } ^ { 1 } , \ldots , \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial v _ { l } } +  \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial u _ { \rho } } \varepsilon _ { \rho } ^ { 1 } |  \\ { D = | \cdots | } \\ { | \frac { \partial \phi _ { l } } { \partial v _ { 1 } } +  \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial \phi _ { l } } { \partial u _ { \rho } } \varepsilon _ { \rho } ^ { 1 } , \ldots , \frac { \partial \phi _ { l } } { \partial v _ { l } } +  \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial \phi _ { l } } { \partial u _ { \rho } } \varepsilon _ { \rho } ^ { l } |  \neq 0 } \end{array} 
$$

for the values of $\pmb { u }$ and $v$ belonging to the domain （1OO） and for the values of $\pmb { \varepsilon } _ { \eta } ^ { \vartheta }$ satisfying $- E \leq \varepsilon _ { \eta } ^ { \vartheta } \leq E$ . We can find a number $\lambda > 0$ such that for the same values of $u , v$ ，and $\pmb { \varepsilon } _ { \eta } ^ { \vartheta }$ we have $| \Delta / D | < \lambda$ ， where $\Delta$ is any one of the first minors of $_ D$ . Put $\varepsilon = 1 / ( 3 l ^ { 2 } \lambda )$ . We define by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \alpha _ { \mu } \equiv u _ { \mu } \equiv \beta _ { \mu } , \quad } & { { } \gamma _ { \nu } \equiv v , \leq \delta _ { \nu } , } \\ { - s \equiv \varepsilon _ { \eta } ^ { \vartheta } \equiv s \quad } & { { } ( 0 < s \equiv E ) } \end{array}
$$

a domain, as regards $\pmb { u }$ and $v$ , contained in (101) and around $( 0 , \ldots ,$ $0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 )$ such that the difference of an element of $_ D$ in two points in (101a) is less than $\varepsilon$ . Now we attribute to $\gamma$ a degree of smallness sufficient for all $u$ and $v$ in the domain

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \leq \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } | f _ { \nu } | v _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \leq \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

to belong to (101a). We know that, to any set of values $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l }$ of $\pmb q$ ，taken in the domain

$$
\sum _ { y = 1 } ^ { l } | f _ { \nu } | q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

and for any admissible value of $t _ { i }$ ， there corresponds one and only one set of values $[ p _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ p _ { k } ]$ of $p$ in the domain

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } < \delta r ^ { 2 } ,
$$

such that by taking $[ p _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ p _ { k } ] , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l }$ as a system of initial values we obtain a solution remaining in the domain (1O3a) and (103b) for all values of $t$ We know also that $[ p ]$ , considered as functions of $q$ and $t$ ，are continuous in the domain (1O3a) and admit in the domain

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { l } | f _ { \nu } | q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } < \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

the continuous first-order derivatives with respect to $q$

Now suppose that the degree of smallness is so chosen that $| \partial [ p ] / \partial q |$ $< s$ independently of the value of $t$ 、By a suitable choice of this degree of smallness we find that, for all values of $q$ belonging to （102a） and for any value of $t$ ，we have

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } d _ { \mu } [ p _ { \mu } ] ^ { 2 } \leq \delta _ { 0 } \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad \delta _ { 0 } = \frac { \delta N } { 3 6 C ^ { 2 } l ^ { 5 } \lambda ^ { 2 k } Q } ,
$$

where $C$ is positive and larger than $| \partial \phi _ { s } / \partial u _ { \mu } | \ ( s = 1 , \ldots , l ; \mu = 1 $ ， $\cdots , k )$ for the values of $u$ and $v$ in the domain (101), $N$ is the smallest of the $d _ { \mu }$ and $Q$ is the largest of the $| f _ { \nu } |$

We form the functions

$$
\chi _ { s } = \phi _ { s } ( [ p _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ p _ { k } ] , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l } ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , l ) ,
$$

which are continuous functions of $q$ and $t$ ，defined in (103a)，and admit continuous first-order derivatives with respect to $q$ for the values of $q$ belonging to (103c).Comparing the expressions for these $\partial x _ { \nu } / \partial q _ { \nu }$ with the elements of $_ D$ ，we see that the functional determinant $\pmb R$ of $\chi$ with respect to $q$ does not vanish, that $| P / R |$ ,where $P$ is a first-order minor of $\pmb R$ , is less than $\lambda$ ，and that the values of $\partial \chi / \partial q$ at two points of (103c) differ from each other by at least $\varepsilon = 1 / ( 3 l ^ { 2 } \lambda )$

For $q _ { 1 } = \cdots = q _ { l } = 0$ we have

$$
\chi _ { s } ( 0 ) = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } \left\{ \frac { \partial \phi _ { s } } { \partial u _ { \mu } } \right\} [ p _ { \mu } ] _ { 0 } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , l ) ,
$$

where the $\{ \}$ indicates the value for a set of values of $u , v$ belonging to (101) and $[ p _ { \mu } ] _ { 0 }$ is the value of $[ p _ { \mu } ]$ for $q _ { 1 } = \cdots = q _ { l } = 0$ Since $\mid \{ \partial \phi _ { s } / \partial u _ { \mu } \} \mid < C$ by the definition of $C$ ,the equations (106) give

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { l } | \chi _ { s } ( 0 ) | \leq l C \sqrt { k } \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { k } | p _ { \mu } | _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \leq l C \sqrt { k } \frac { \sqrt { \delta _ { 0 } } } { \sqrt { N } } \tau , } \end{array}
$$

whence

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { l } \vert \chi _ { s } ( 0 ) \vert \leq l \sigma \gamma , \qquad \sigma = \frac { \sqrt { \bar { \delta } } \varepsilon } { 2 \sqrt { l } \sqrt { \mathcal { Q } } } .
$$

Introducing $\omega _ { 1 } , \ldots , \omega _ { l }$ ，we obtain from (99)

$$
\sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { \ell } \vert \omega _ { \rho } \vert \leq l \sigma \gamma .
$$

Hence

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \iota } [ \chi _ { \nu } ( 0 ) - \omega _ { \nu } ] ^ { 2 } \leq \left( \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \iota } | \chi _ { \nu } ( 0 ) | + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \iota } | \omega _ { \nu } | \right) ^ { 2 } \leq 4 l ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } \gamma ^ { 2 } ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \ell } ~ [ \chi _ { \nu } ( 0 ) - \omega _ { \nu } ] ^ { 2 } \leq \frac { l \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } } { Q } .
$$

Thus the $\iota$ continuous functions $x _ { \nu } ( q ) - \omega _ { \nu }$ are given in the domain defined by(1O3a) and (99),and admit continuous first-order derivatives with respect to $q$ in the domain (103c).Let $R$ be the functional determinant of these $\imath$ functions with respect to $q$ and $P$ be any firstorder minor of $\pmb R$ ; then $| P / R | < \lambda$ .The values of the derivative

$$
\frac { \partial ( \chi _ { 2 } - \omega _ { 2 } ) } { \partial q } = \frac { \partial \chi _ { 2 } } { \partial q }
$$

at two points of the domain (103c) differ by at least $\varepsilon = 1 / ( 3 \lambda l ^ { 2 } )$ · These $\iota$ functions satisfy (1O7). Hence from Lemma 2 we obtain

Lemma 3: To any set of values $\omega$ taken in the domain (99) and to any value of $t$ in the admissible interval there corresponds a system of $q$ belonging to the domain (103c) and satisfying

$$
\chi _ { \nu } ( q ) = \omega _ { \nu } ( \nu = 1 , . . . , l ) .
$$

This system is unique in the domain (1O3a) that satisfies (108).

Thus Theorem 4 is established, since $\pmb { \sigma }$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ and $\phi _ { \nu }$ Hence the domain (99) is of the character imposed on the domain for $\pmb { \omega }$ in the statement of the theorem. If a set of values $\omega$ is chosen in the domain (99) and $t$ ，then there exists in the domain (103c) a system of $\pmb q$ satisfying (108). If the system of $q$ is completed by the system $p _ { 1 } = [ p _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , p _ { k } = [ p _ { k } ]$ ，and if the system of $q$ and $[ p ]$ is taken as the initial system corresponding to the chosen value of $t$ ，then the corresponding solution remains in the domains (103a) and (103b） for larger values of t. From(1O8) we have

$$
\chi _ { \nu } = \phi _ { \nu } ( [ p _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ p _ { k } ] , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { l } ) \qquad ( \nu = 1 , \ldots , l ) .
$$

This system of initial values satisfies the conditions of the theorem.It is easy to prove that this system is unique that satisfies these conditions. In fact， by admitting the existence of a second system $p _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , p _ { k } ^ { \prime } ;$ $q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , q _ { l } ^ { \prime }$ with the same properties we have

$$
p _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = [ p _ { 1 } ] ^ { \prime } , . . . , p _ { k } ^ { \prime } = [ p _ { k } ] ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where the right-hand members denote the values of $[ p _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ p _ { k } ]$ for $q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , q _ { l } ^ { \prime }$ and the chosen values of $t$ Then we get

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \omega _ { \nu } = \phi _ { \nu } ( p _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \dots , p _ { k } ^ { \prime } , q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \dots , q _ { l } ^ { \prime } ) } \\ & { \quad = \phi _ { \nu } ( [ p _ { 1 } ] ^ { \prime } , \dots , [ p _ { k } ] ^ { \prime } , q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \dots , q _ { l } ^ { \prime } ) = \chi _ { \nu } ^ { \prime } \qquad ( \nu = 1 , 2 , \dots , l ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\chi _ { \nu } ^ { \prime }$ denotes the value of $\gamma _ { \nu }$ for $q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , q _ { l } ^ { \prime }$ and the chosen value of $t$ Hence $q _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , q _ { l } = q _ { l } ^ { \prime } ;$ ，and accordingly

$$
[ p _ { 1 } ] ^ { \prime } = [ p _ { 1 } ] , . . . , [ p _ { k } ] ^ { \prime } = [ p _ { k } ] .
$$

Thus the two systems $( p , q )$ coincide.

16.9SOLUTIONS REPRESENTABLE BY QUASI-PERIODIC FUNCTIONS

Consider again the system of equations (91) or (67)

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( x ) + X _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

for the values of $x$ in the domain $( \mathrm { I } ) \colon a _ { s } < x _ { s } < b _ { s }$ or $\begin{array} { r } { x _ { s } > a _ { s } , x _ { s } < b _ { s } , } \end{array}$ or $x _ { s }$ arbitrary. Suppose that the derivatives $\partial X / \partial x$ exist and satisfy $\left| \partial X / \partial x \right| \leqq \kappa / \sqrt { n }$ ，where $\pmb { \kappa }$ is defined in the Auxiliary Lemma (Section 16.8). Suppose also that $X _ { s }$ are the result of substitution $u _ { 1 } = t / \alpha _ { 1 } ,$ $\dots , u _ { m } = t / \alpha _ { m }$ in certain functions $\rho _ { s } ( x , u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { m } )$ given for all values of $x$ in the domain (I) and for all values of $\boldsymbol { u }$ ， as uniformly continuous functions of $x$ and $\boldsymbol { u }$ ， periodic with respect to $u$ of period

1；and $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m }$ are nonzero numbers such that there is no linear homogeneous relation with integer coefficients among $1 / \alpha$ Finally, suppose that there exists a solution of (91） for all values of $t$ in the domain defined by $\gamma _ { s } \cong x _ { s } \cong \beta _ { s }$ , contained in the domain (I). As was proved in the Auxiliary Lemma, there is no other solution that remains in the domain (I) and between finite bounds for all values of $t$ Let the elements of the solution be denoted by $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$

The functions $y _ { s } = x _ { s } ( t + \tau )$ ， $\tau =$ constant, $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ satisfy

$$
\frac { d y _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( y ) + X _ { s } ( t + \tau , y ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \dots , n ) ,
$$

where the $X _ { s }$ are reduced to $\rho _ { s } ( y , u + [ \tau / \alpha _ { 1 } ] , \dots , u _ { m } + [ \tau / \alpha _ { m } ] )$ by the solution $u _ { 1 } = t / \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { m } = t / \alpha _ { m }$ Put

$$
\frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } \equiv v _ { 1 } , \dots , \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } = v _ { m }
$$

As was shown in Section 2.8 (Bohl,1893), if m numbers $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m }$ are such that there is no linear homogeneous relation with integer coefficients among the reciprocals of these m numbers $\alpha _ { s }$ ，and if also m arbitrary numbers $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { m }$ are given, then we can find a number $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ such that the differences $( \tau / \alpha _ { 1 } ) - r _ { 1 } , \dots , ( \tau / \alpha _ { m } ) - r _ { m }$ differ from integers by an amount as small as we please (Kronecker's theorem; Section 2.8). This is a particular case of the following theorem.

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :If there are $m ( m + 1 )$ quantities

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { a _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } } } & { { a _ { 1 } ^ { 1 } } } & { { a _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } ~ \cdot ~ \cdot ~ \cdot ~ a _ { m } ^ { 1 } } } \\ { { \ } } & { { \ } } & { { \ } } \\ { { a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } & { { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } & { { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ~ \cdot ~ \cdot ~ \cdot ~ a _ { m } ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \ } } & { { \ } } & { { \ } } \\ { { \cdot ~ \cdot ~ \cdot } } & { { \ } } & { { \ } } \\ { { a _ { 0 } ^ { m } } } & { { a _ { 1 } ^ { m } } } & { { a _ { 2 } ^ { m } ~ \cdot ~ \cdot ~ \cdot ~ a _ { m } ^ { m } } } \end{array}
$$

such that there is no linear homogeneous.relation with integer coeffcients among $m$ determinants of order $m$ formed of the $m ( m + 1 )$ quantities，and if also $_ { m }$ arbitrarily chosen numbers $r _ { 1 } , \ldots , r _ { m }$ are given, then we can find $m + 1$ integers $n _ { 0 } , n _ { 1 } , \ldots , n _ { m }$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { n _ { 0 } a _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } + n _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } ^ { 1 } + \dots + n _ { m } a _ { m } ^ { 1 } = r _ { 1 } + \varepsilon _ { 1 } , } } \\ { { n _ { 0 } a _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + n _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \dots + n _ { m } a _ { m } ^ { 2 } = r _ { 2 } + \varepsilon _ { 2 } , } } \\ { { \dots } } \\ { { n _ { 0 } a _ { 0 } ^ { m } + n _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } ^ { m } + \dots + n _ { m } a _ { m } ^ { m } = r _ { m } + \varepsilon _ { m } , } } \end{array}
$$

where the $\varepsilon ^ { \prime } s$ are quantities that can be chosen as small as we please.

From this theorem it follows that the set of quantities V1, U,..., Um defined by

$$
{ \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } } = v _ { 1 } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad { \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } } = v _ { m } \qquad ( \mathrm { m o d } \ 1 )
$$

forms, as $\tau$ varies,a manifold，which has the point defined by $m$ arbitrary coordinates $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { m }$ as the limit point.For all values of $v$ we have

$$
| \rho _ { s } ( x + z , v ) - \rho _ { s } ( x , v ) | \leq \kappa \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } { z _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

if $x + z$ and $_ x$ belong to the domain (I).

Suppose that all symbols congruent to each other with modulus unity, such as $| a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot a _ { m } | \equiv | b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot b _ { m } |$ with $a _ { 1 } \equiv b _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } \equiv b _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { m } \equiv$ $b _ { m }$ (mod 1),belong to a class.If a class of symbols is congruent to $| ( t / \alpha _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( t / \alpha _ { m } ) |$ ，then it is said to belong to a value of $t$ or $t$ is said to belong to a class. If a class belongs to a value of $t _ { i }$ ，then the class or the corresponding symbol is said to be of the first category，otherwise of the second category (Bohl, 1893； Sections 2.8 and 2.10).

If the quantities $v$ form a set of the first category in this sense, then the relation (110a) follows from the hypothesis. If the quantities $v$ form a different set, then

$$
| \rho _ { s } ( x + z , v ) - \rho _ { s } ( x , v ) | > \kappa \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } z _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } }
$$

cannot occur,because,if this relation (11Ob） were satisfied for a certain set of $v$ ， then we could find a system $v$ of the first category as near as we please to the first set and for which we should have (110a), and this is contradictory， because of the uniform continuity of $\rho$ Hence for all values of $\pmb { u }$ we have the relation

$$
\vert \rho _ { s } ( x + z , u ) - \rho _ { s } ( x , u ) \vert \equiv \kappa \cdot \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } | z _ { \mu } | .
$$

We form the equations

$$
\frac { d \omega _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( \omega ) + \gamma _ { s } ( \omega , t ) ( s = 1 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where the $\eta _ { s }$ are given for all values of $t$ and for the values of $\omega$ belonging to the domain (Ia),which is obtained by replacing $x$ by $\omega$ in the definition of the domain (I).Also $\eta _ { s }$ are the result of substituting

$$
u _ { 1 } = \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad u _ { m } = \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } }
$$

in the functions $\rho _ { s } ( \omega , u _ { 1 } + v _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { m } + v _ { m } )$ ，where $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m }$ are any numbers.

Whatever the values of $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m }$ in (111) may be,there exist sets of $v$ as near as we please to the former set and for which (111) admits a solution extending to all values of $t$ and remaining in the domain $\gamma \le \omega _ { s } \le \beta _ { s }$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ ， since such a solution exists for any set $v$ of the first category.

In fact, choose a sequence of sets $v$ of the first category $S _ { 1 } , S _ { 2 } , S _ { 3 }$ ， ... tending to the given set $V$ . To each set $S _ { p }$ let there correspond a solution $I _ { p }$ with the stated properties. The set of values that the elements $\omega$ of the solution take for an arbitrary value $t _ { 0 }$ of $t$ has at least one limit point $\omega _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , \omega _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ in the domain $\gamma _ { s } \cong \omega _ { s } \cong \beta _ { s }$ . Regarding the values of the set $V$ as the quantities $v$ in（111） we derive the solution ^ whose initial values for $t = t _ { 0 }$ are $\omega _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , \omega _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ . From the relation (11Oc) we see that such a solution exists for all values of $t$ including $t _ { 0 }$ and remains in the domain $\gamma _ { s } \cong \omega _ { s } \cong \beta _ { s }$ Two solutions of this property for (111） corresponding to two different sets of $v$ differ from each other by an amount as small as we please, provided that the two sets differ by a sufficiently small amount, as a consequence of the Auxiliary Lemma of Section 16.8.Hence there exists for an arbitrarily chosen set $v$ one and only one solution of (111） that is defined for all values of $t$ and remains in the domain $\gamma _ { s } \cong \omega _ { s } \cong \beta _ { s }$ Denote the elements of this solution by $\omega _ { s } ( t , v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m } )$ $( s = 1 , 2 ,$ ， $\cdots , n )$ and put $\sigma _ { s } = \omega _ { s } ( t + \tau , v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m } )$ with an arbitrary number $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ .Then we have

$$
\frac { d \sigma _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( \sigma ) + \rho _ { s } \left( \sigma , \frac { t + \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + v _ { 1 } , \ldots , \frac { t + \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } + v _ { m } \right)
$$

Comparing (111) with (112) we obtain

$$
\omega _ { s } ( t + \tau , v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m } ) = \omega _ { s } \Bigl ( t , v _ { 1 } + \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , v _ { m } + \frac { \tau } { \alpha _ { m } } \Bigr ) ,
$$

or

$$
\omega _ { s } ( t , 0 , \ldots , 0 ) = \omega _ { s } \Bigl ( 0 , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } \Bigr ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

The left-hand members are the elements $x _ { s }$ of the solution of (91). Hence

$$
x _ { s } ( t ) = \omega _ { s } \Big ( 0 , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , \frac { t } { \alpha _ { m } } \Big ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

On the other hand,the functions $\omega _ { s } ( 0 , v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m } )$ are continuous functions of $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m }$ for all values of $v$ and are periodic with respect to $v$ with period 1. Hence each of the functions $\omega _ { s } ( 0 , v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m } )$ can be expanded in uniformly convergent series for all values of $v$ in the form

$$
\omega _ { s 1 } + \omega _ { s 2 } + \ldots ,
$$

in which each term $\omega _ { s \nu }$ is a rational entire function of cos $2 \pi v _ { \mu }$ and sin $2 \pi \mathfrak { v } _ { \mu }$ $( \mu = 1 , \ldots , m )$ . Thus we have

Theorem 2: The elements $x _ { s } ( t )$ of the solution can be expanded in series

$$
x _ { s } ( t ) = x _ { s 1 } + x _ { s 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

which are uniformly convergent for all values of $t$ and each term of which is a rational entire function of cos $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ and sin $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ $( \mu = 1 , \ldots , m )$

We arrive at the same conclusion by basing our investigation on the propositions of Section 2.8.We replace the hypothesis as regards the functions $X$ of (91) by the hypothesis that to any number $d > 0$ we can make a number $\varepsilon > 0$ correspond such that the $\vert X _ { s } ( x , t + \tau ) -$ $X _ { s } ( x , t ) |$ remain smaller than $d$ if $\tau / \alpha _ { 1 } , \dots , \tau / \alpha _ { m }$ differ from integers by amounts smaller than $\varepsilon$ . Suppose then that there exists a solution which remains in the domain (I) and between finite bounds for all values of $t$ .Denote the elements of the solution by $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ and write $x _ { s } ( t + \tau ) - x _ { s } ( t ) = z _ { s }$ ，where $\tau$ is at first an arbitrary constant. Then we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { d z _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( z ) + X _ { s } ( x + z , t + \tau ) - X _ { s } ( x , t + \tau ) } } \\ { \displaystyle { + X _ { s } ( x , t + \tau ) - X _ { s } ( x , t ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Write

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { u _ { s } = X _ { s } ( x , t + \tau ) - X _ { s } ( x , t ) , } \\ & { v _ { s } = X _ { s } ( x + z , t + \tau ) - X _ { s } ( x , t + \tau ) , } \end{array}
$$

then we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { | v _ { s } | \leq \kappa \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } { Z _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } } . } \end{array}
$$

If $| u _ { s } | < \delta , \delta > 0 ( s = 1 , \dots , n )$ , for a certain value of $\pmb { \tau }$ and for all values of $x$ ，then $\vert z _ { s } \vert < e \delta \left( s = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ according to the Auxiliary Lemma,where $^ e$ depends only on $a _ { s \sigma }$ .Hence, to any number $D > 0$ we can make a number $E > 0$ correspond such that $| x _ { s } ( t + \tau ) - x _ { s } ( t ) |$ $< D \left( s = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ ,if $\tau / \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \tau / \alpha _ { m }$ differ from integers by amounts smaller than $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ . Consequently the functions $x _ { s } ( t )$ are expanded in trigonometric series $x _ { s 1 } + x _ { s 2 } + \ldots$ ，of which each term is a rational entire function of cos $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ and sin $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )$ ， and the series are uniformly convergent for all values of $t$

We can further modify the hypothesis. In the foregoing proof we supposed that for all values of $t$ and for admissible values of $x$ we can make $| X _ { s } ( x , t + \tau ) - X _ { s } ( x , t ) |$ as small as we please by choosing $\tau / \alpha _ { 1 } , \dots , \tau / \alpha _ { m }$ suffciently near certain integers respectively. It can be shown (Bohl, 1893,1917; Section 2.1O) that this hypothesis is satisfied by $X$ ，if $X$ are expanded in series $g _ { 1 } + g _ { 2 } + \cdots$ which are uniformly convergent for all values of $t$ and for admissible values of $x$ and each term of which is a rational entire function of cos $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ and sin $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ with functions of $x$ only as coefficients.We suppose that $X$ remains between finite bounds.

We now return to the hypothesis stated at the beginning of this Section and suppose that $\rho _ { s } ( x , u )$ admit derivatives with respect to $u$ and $x$ up to a certain high order $\nu$ ，and that the derivatives are continuous for all values of $u$ and for admissible values of $x$ .We have $| \partial \rho / \partial x | \leq x / \sqrt { n }$ .For the points $u$ of the first category this relation follows from the hypothesis. The inequality $| \partial \rho / \partial x | > \kappa / \sqrt { n }$ cannot be satisfied for any other point $u$ . In fact, if this inequality were satisfied at a point $( x , u )$ then it would be satisfied for points sufficiently nearby,but according to the continuity of $\partial \rho / \partial x$ this is incompatible with the fact that we can find in the neighborhood, however close to any point $( x , u )$ ， points for which $\pmb { u }$ form a set of the first category.

Referring to the equations (111) we define the domains for $\omega$ and $\nu$ .Let Ω be a domain for $\omega$ defined by $\smash { \varGamma _ { s } < \omega _ { s } < B _ { s } }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ and so chosen that it contains the domain $\gamma _ { s } \cong \omega _ { s } \cong \beta _ { s }$ and is contained in the domain (Ia).Let $V$ denote the domain of $v$ defined by $M _ { \mu } < v _ { \mu } < N _ { \mu }$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )$ with the condition $| M _ { \mu } - N _ { \mu } | > 1$ Then the equations (1i1） satisfy,relative to the domains $\Omega$ and $V$ ， with $t$ arbitrary, conditions analogous to those satisfied by the equations (91) relative to the domains such as $m _ { s } < x _ { s } < n _ { s }$ and $a < \alpha <$ $A , b < \beta < B , \ldots ,$ ，with $t$ arbitrary.The quantities $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m }$ are regarded as new parameters.Hence we have :

Theorem 3: The functions $\omega _ { s }$ are uniformly continuous for all values of $t$ and $v$ . They admit derivatives with respect to $v$ up to a certain high-order $\nu$ ，which are also uniformly continuous functions of $t$ and $v$ and remain between finite bounds for all values of $t$ and $v$ ·

This theorem is valid for the domain $V$ but can be extended to all values of $v$ owing to the periodicity of $\omega _ { s }$ .If $\nu \geq 2 m$ ， then ${ \omega } _ { s } ( 0 , v _ { \imath } ,$ $\ldots , v _ { m } )$ can be expanded in absolutely and uniformly convergent trigonometric series for all values of $v$ , and indeed the series are Fourier series of several variables,each term of which is

$$
A \cos 2 \pi ( \nu _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \eta _ { 1 } ) \cos 2 \pi ( \nu _ { 2 } v _ { 2 } + \eta _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \cos 2 \pi ( \nu _ { m } v _ { m } + \eta _ { m } ) ,
$$

where the ${ \boldsymbol \nu } ^ { \prime } { \boldsymbol \mathsf { s } }$ are integers, positive or zero,and $\eta = 0$ or $1 / 4$ The relation $x _ { s } ( t ) = \omega _ { s } ( 0 , t / \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , t / \alpha _ { m } )$ shows that $x _ { s } ( t )$ can be expanded in trigonometric series of the type

$$
\sum { A \cos 2 \pi \Big ( \frac { \upsilon _ { 1 } t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \eta _ { 1 } \Big ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \cos 2 \pi \Big ( \frac { \upsilon _ { m } t } { \alpha _ { m } } + \eta _ { m } \Big ) }
$$

absolutely and uniformly convergent for all values of $t$ . These series can be obtained by substituting $v _ { 1 } = t / \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m } = t / \alpha _ { m }$ in the series for $\omega _ { s } ( 0 , v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m } )$

Let $\eta _ { s } ( x , t ) ~ ( s = 1 , \dots , n )$ be $_ n$ functions of $x$ and $t$ ，given for all values of $x$ in the domain (I) and for the interval of $t$ defined by $t > \tau$ ， where $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ is a determinate number. Suppose that $| Y | < d$ for an arbitrary $^ d$ and for sufficiently large values of $t$ and for all values of $x$ . If the system

$$
\frac { d y _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( y ) + X _ { s } ( y , t ) + Y _ { s } ( y , t ) ( s = 1 , \dots , n )
$$

admits at least for sufficiently large values of $t$ a solution [y] that remains between finite bounds, then this solution tends asymptotically to the solution $[ x ]$ as $t$ increases indefinitely.

In fact, by putting $x _ { s } = y _ { s } + z _ { s }$ we obtain

$$
\frac { d z _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( z ) + X _ { s } ( y + z , t ) - X _ { s } ( y , t ) - Y _ { s } ( y , t )
$$

By hypothesis

$$
\begin{array} { r } { | X _ { s } ( y + z , t ) - X _ { s } ( y , t ) | \leq \kappa \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } z _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Also,we have $| Y _ { s } ( t ) | < d$ for an arbitrarily chosen positive number $^ d$ and for sufficiently large values of $t$ Hence for sufficiently large values of $t$ we have $| z _ { s } | < e d$

The results of Sections 16.8 and 16.9 are summarized·in

Theorem 4: Let the equations (91):

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = L _ { s } ( x ) + X _ { s } ~ ( s = 1 , \ldots , n )
$$

be given. Suppose that the foregoing conditions stated in Section 16.8 are satisfied for all values of $t$ ，and that $X _ { s }$ result from the substitution $u _ { 1 } = t / \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { m } = t / \alpha _ { m }$ in $\rho _ { s } ( x , u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { m } )$ ，which are continuous for all values of $\boldsymbol { u }$ and for the values of $_ x$ in the domain $- \gamma < x _ { s } < \gamma$ and periodic with respect to $\boldsymbol { u }$ with period 1. Suppose further that $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { m }$ are nonzero and such that there is no linear homogeneous relation with integer coefficients among their reciprocals.

If the degree of smallness is suitably chosen, then there exists one and only one solution of (91) that remains in the domain $- \gamma < x _ { s } <$ $\boldsymbol { \gamma }$ for all values of $t .$ ，such that its elements are expanded in trigonometric series

$$
x _ { s } ( t ) = x _ { s 1 } + x _ { s 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \quad \quad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

uniformly convergent for all values of $t$ ，whose terms are in the form of rational entire functions of cos $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ and sin $( 2 \pi t / \alpha _ { \mu } )$ $( \mu = 1$ ， $\ldots , m )$ . If the functions $\rho$ admit continuous derivatives with respect to $x$ and $u$ up to a certain high order $\nu$ ， $\nu \geq 2 m$ ，then each element $x _ { s } ( t )$ of the solution can be written in the form

$$
x _ { s } ( t ) = \sum \ A \cos \ : 2 \pi \Big ( \frac { \nu _ { 1 } t } { \alpha _ { 1 } } + \eta _ { 1 } \Big ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \cos \ : 2 \pi \Big ( \frac { \nu _ { m } t } { \alpha _ { m } } + \eta _ { m } \Big ) ,
$$

where $\nu \geq 0$ are integers and $\eta$ are zero or $1 / 4$ . These series are absolutely and uniformly convergent for all values of $t$ and represent quasi-periodic functions (Section 2.10; Erugin, 1966).

Bohl (191O） applied the theory to the study of the differential equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \phi } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 q \frac { d \phi } { d t } + p \phi = \nu \Big ( \phi , \frac { d \phi } { d t } , t \Big ) ,
$$

where $q > 0 , p > 0$ are constants and $\psi$ represents the perturbation given in the domain $( \mathbf { A } ) \colon \alpha < \phi < \beta , \alpha _ { 1 } < d \phi / d t < \beta _ { 1 }$ ，containing the point (O,O), for all values of $t$ and admits the derivatives ${ \partial \psi } / { \partial \phi } ;$ ， $\partial \psi / \partial ( d \phi / d t )$ ， continuous together with $\psi$ ， with respect to $\phi , d \phi / d t , t$ Suppose that the absolute values of these derivatives are smaller than $\Delta _ { 1 }$ for $| \phi | < \gamma , | d \phi / d t | < \gamma .$ and that $| \psi ( 0 , 0 , t ) | < \Delta _ { 2 } \gamma$ ，where $\gamma$ isa positive constant,and where the domain $| \phi | < \gamma , | d \phi / d t | < \gamma$ is contained inside the domain (A） and $\Delta _ { 1 }$ and $\Delta _ { 2 }$ are positive constants depending only on $q , p$ . The equation can be replaced by

$$
\frac { d \chi } { d t } = - 2 q \chi - p \phi + \psi ( \phi , \chi , t ) , \frac { d \phi } { d t } = \chi .
$$

According to the foregoing theory we assume that all the roots $- q \pm \sqrt { - p + q ^ { 2 } }$ of the characteristic equation have negative real parts.Then we see that there exists one and only one solution $\Phi$ for all values of $t$ for which $- \gamma < \phi < \gamma , - \gamma < d \phi / d t < \gamma$ If for an arbitrary value of $t$ we choose the initial values $\phi _ { 0 }$ and $( d \phi / d t ) _ { 0 }$ with suffciently small absolute values, then the corresponding solution remains inside the domain $- \gamma < \phi < \gamma , - \gamma < d \phi / d t < \gamma$ for all larger values of $t$ and tends asymptotically to that solution $\Phi$ for $t $ $+ \infty$ . If $\pmb { t }$ is contained in trigonometric form in $\psi ( \phi , \chi , t )$ ，then the solution $\Phi$ can be represented by a trigonometric series uniformly convergent for all values of $t$

As a particular case, the existence of the solution exhibiting beats of Helmholtz's equation in his theory of sound,

$$
- m \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = a x + b x ^ { 2 } + f \sin { ( p t ) } + g \sin { ( q t + c ) } , \qquad a > 0 ,
$$

is established.

Bohl (1910,1904) discussed the motion of a mechanical system submitted to a force derivable from a potential in the neighborhood of a point where the potential is a minimum.

Suppose that (i) the position of a dynamical system is represented by $_ n$ parameters $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ ，(ii) the force function $V$ and its first- and second-order derivatives are uniform and continuous functions of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ in the neighborhood of the position $( 0 , \ldots , 0 )$ in the domain $a _ { s } < u _ { s } < b _ { s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ，and (ii) $V$ is a minimum for $u _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = u _ { n } = 0$ ，and so can be represented by a Taylor series beginning with a positive-definite quadratic form.Let the kinetic energy be

$$
2 T = \sum _ { i , k } A _ { i k } \dot { u } _ { i } \dot { u } _ { k } \qquad ( i , k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ; A _ { i k } = A _ { k i } ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { A } _ { i k }$ and their derivatives of the frst two orders are uniform and continuous in $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ in the same domain of $\pmb { u }$ . The equations of motion are

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { u } _ { s } } = \frac { \partial ( T + V ) } { \partial u _ { s } } + U _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $U _ { s }$ denote the perturbations and are continuous in $t , u _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $u _ { n } , { \dot { u } } _ { 1 } , \dots , { \dot { u } } _ { n }$ and admit first-order derivatives with respect to $u _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $u _ { n } , { \dot { u } } _ { 1 } , \dots , { \dot { u } } _ { n }$ .We distinguish two cases: Case I,in which $a _ { s } < u _ { s } <$ $b _ { s }$ ， $a _ { s } ^ { \prime } < \dot { u } _ { s } < b _ { s } ^ { \prime } ,$ ， $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ ； and Case II, in which $a _ { s } < u _ { s } < b _ { s }$ but $\dot { \pmb u } _ { s }$ are unlimited and the perturbations: $U _ { s }$ are supposed to be limited between finite bounds for all values of $u , \dot { u } , t$ A degree of smallness is assigned depending on the nature of $_ T$ and $V$ in Case I and,in Case II, on $T$ and $V$ and also on the finite bounds between which $U$ remains.

The equations of motion are replaced by

$$
\frac { d u _ { s } } { d t } = \dot { u } _ { s } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \cfrac { \ d } { \ d t } \overline { { d t } } = \mathbb { c } _ { s 1 } u _ { 1 } + \dots + \mathbb { c } _ { s n } u _ { n } + \mathbb { c } _ { s } + \mathbb { s } _ { s } , } \\ & { C _ { s \nu } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { D _ { \mu s } ^ { 0 } } { D ^ { 0 } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { \mu } \partial u _ { \nu } } , ~ S _ { s } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { D _ { \mu s } } { D } U _ { \mu } , } \\ & { R _ { s } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { D _ { \mu s } } { D } \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial u _ { \mu } } + W _ { \mu } \right) - C _ { s 1 } u _ { 1 } - \dots - C _ { s n } u _ { n } , } \end{array}
$$

where an index O indicates the value for $u _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = u _ { n } = 0$ Also, $\pmb { R } _ { s }$ are continuous functions of $u$ and $\dot { \pmb u }$ ，admit continuous derivatives of the first order with respect to $u$ and $\dot { u }$ ，and depend only on the nature of $_ T$ and $V$ . The $R _ { s }$ and their derivatives vanish for $u _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot$ ${ \bf \Psi } = u _ { n } = \dot { u } _ { 1 } = { \bf \Psi } \cdot { \bf \dot { \bf \alpha } } \cdot { \bf \beta } = \dot { u } _ { n } = 0$ Finally,the $S _ { s }$ are continuous functions of $u , \dot { u } , t$ ，while their derivatives of the first order with respect to $\boldsymbol { u }$ and $\dot { u }$ are continuous.

The determinant

$$
\scriptstyle { \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l l l l } { - { \omega } } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - { \omega } } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { \cdots } & { - { \omega } } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { C _ { 1 1 } } & { C _ { 1 2 } } & { \cdots } & { C _ { 1 n } } & { - { \omega } } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { C _ { 2 1 } } & { C _ { 2 2 } } & { \cdots } & { C _ { 2 n } } & { 0 } & { - { \omega } } & { \cdots } & { 0 } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { C _ { n 1 } } & { C _ { n 2 } } & { \cdots } & { C _ { n n } } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { \cdots } & { - { \omega } } \end{array} \right| } = 0
$$

plays the role of the characteristic equation. Since

$$
\sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { \nu \tau } A _ { \nu s } ^ { 0 } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { \tau s } ^ { 0 } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } \Bigg [ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { D _ { \mu r } ^ { 0 } } { D ^ { 0 } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { \mu } \partial u _ { \nu } } \Bigg ] A _ { \nu s } ^ { 0 } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { \tau s } ^ { 0 }
$$

$$
= \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { \circ } } { \partial u _ { r } \partial u _ { s } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { r s } ^ { \circ } ,
$$

we obtain from this determinant (117) the equation:

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { l l l l } { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { 1 1 } ^ { 0 } } & { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { 1 } \partial u _ { 2 } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { 1 2 } ^ { 0 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { 1 } \partial u _ { n } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { 1 n } ^ { 0 } } \\ { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { 2 } \partial u _ { 1 } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { 2 1 } ^ { 0 } } & { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { 2 2 } ^ { 0 } } & { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { 2 } \partial u _ { n } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { 2 n } ^ { 0 } } \\ { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { \cdot \cdot } & { \cdot } \\ { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { n } \partial u _ { 1 } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { n 1 } ^ { 0 } } & { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { n } \partial u _ { 2 } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { n 2 } ^ { 0 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { n } ^ { 2 } } - \omega ^ { 2 } A _ { n n } ^ { 0 } } \end{array} \biggr | = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

The sums

$$
\sum _ { i , k } \ : A _ { i k } ^ { 0 } x _ { i } x _ { k } , \quad \ : \sum _ { i , k } \frac { \partial W _ { \ast } } { \partial u _ { i } \partial u _ { k } } x _ { i } x _ { k } \quad \quad ( i , k = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

are positive-definite quadratic forms with respect to $x$ .The determinant equation admits $_ n$ real positive roots for $\omega ^ { 2 }$ .Hence all roots of the characteristic equation (117) are real,nonzero and paired, the roots of any pair differing only in sign.

In Case I all the roots of (117) have nonzero real parts. Let $\Delta _ { 1 } , \Delta _ { 2 }$ correspond to those for the functions $X _ { s }$ in (91） such that $\vert \partial X _ { s } / \partial x _ { j } \vert$ $< \Delta _ { 1 }$ ， $| X _ { s } | _ { 0 } < \Delta _ { 2 } \gamma$ for $- \gamma < x _ { s } < + \gamma$ .We so choose the positive number $\boldsymbol { \gamma }$ that $- \gamma < u _ { s } < \gamma , - \gamma < \dot { u } _ { s } < \gamma$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ ，which is the domain contained in the interval $a _ { s } < u _ { s } < b _ { s } ,$ ， $a _ { s } ^ { \prime } < \dot { u } _ { s } < b _ { s } ^ { \prime }$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ .Suppose that the absolute values of the first-order derivatives of $R _ { s }$ with respect to $\pmb { u }$ and $\dot { \pmb u }$ are less than $D _ { 1 } / 2$ . These derivatives are assumed to be continuous functions of $u , \dot { u }$ and to vanish with $u _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = u _ { n } = \dot { u } _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \dot { u } _ { n } = 0$ . The degree of smallness is so chosen that in the above domains for $u _ { s } , \dot { u } _ { s }$ bounded by $\pm \gamma$ and for all values of $t$ we have $| \partial S _ { s } / \partial u _ { k } | , | \partial S _ { s } / \partial \dot { u } _ { k } | < D _ { 1 } / 2 .$ $| S _ { s } ^ { 0 } | < D _ { 2 } \gamma$ . Then we can apply the theorem.

In Case II we first note the following properties. In any domain contained in the domain $a _ { s } < u _ { s } < b _ { s } ,$ the roots of $\| A _ { i j } - \sigma \varepsilon _ { i j } \| = 0$ are contained between two positive numbers. Consequently for any such domain we have

$$
K \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \dot { u } _ { s } ^ { 2 } \le T \le G \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \dot { u } _ { s } ^ { 2 } , ~ K > 0 , ~ G > 0 .
$$

If the motion is extended in the interval $t _ { 1 } \leq t < t _ { 2 }$ ，then $_ { T }$ cannot take any large value when the system does not take the position as near as we please to the limit of the domain $a _ { s } < u _ { s } < b _ { s } ( s = 1 , \ldots ,$ $n )$ . If the motion cannot be extended for all values of $t > \tau$ ，but can be extended to the interval $\tau \leq t < \vartheta$ and not farther than that, then for values of $t$ sufficiently near $\vartheta$ the system takes positions as near as we please to the limit of the domain $a _ { s } < u _ { s } < b _ { s }$ Let $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ be linear homogeneous functions of $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { n } , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { n }$ with constant coefficients that depend only on the nature of $_ T$ and $V$ . Denote these functions by $\phi _ { 1 } ( p , q ) , \ldots , \phi _ { n } ( p , q )$ or by $\phi _ { i } ( w _ { 1 } , \ldots , w _ { n }$ ， $v _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ $v _ { n } )$ $( i = 1 , \ldots , n )$ . Choose $\delta _ { 1 } > 0$ ， $\delta _ { 2 } > 0$ ， $\delta _ { 3 } > 0$ so that $| \partial X / \partial x | <$ $\delta _ { 1 } , | X _ { 0 } | / \gamma < \delta _ { 2 } , \gamma < \delta _ { 3 }$ ； also choose $D _ { 1 } > 0$ ， $D _ { 2 } > 0$ ， $D _ { 3 } > 0$ depending only on $_ T$ and $V$ ，such that $\delta _ { \scriptscriptstyle 1 } , \delta _ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 } , \delta _ { \scriptscriptstyle 3 }$ correspond to $a _ { s \sigma }$ and $\phi _ { i } ( u , v )$ Let $\overline { { w } } > 0$ correspond to $a _ { s \sigma }$ and $\phi ( w , v )$ such that $\pmb { \sigma }$ corresponds to $a _ { s \sigma }$ and $\phi ( w , v )$ ，where $\bar { w }$ depends on $T$ and $V$

Now choose $\gamma _ { 1 } < D _ { 3 }$ ， $0 < \gamma _ { 1 } < \gamma _ { 0 }$ ，such that

$$
\left| \frac { \partial R _ { \mu } } { \partial u _ { \nu } } \right| < \frac { D _ { 1 } } { 2 } , \qquad \left| \frac { \partial R _ { \mu } } { \partial \dot { u } _ { \nu } } \right| < \frac { D _ { 1 } } { 2 } ,
$$

in the domain $- \gamma _ { 1 } < u _ { s } < \gamma _ { 1 } , - \gamma _ { 1 } < \dot { u } _ { s } < \gamma _ { 1 } ( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ . Choose $\gamma _ { 2 } , 0 < \gamma _ { 2 } < \gamma _ { 1 }$ ，such.that

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \delta \gamma ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \equiv - \delta \gamma ^ { 2 }
$$

in the interval

$$
- \gamma _ { 2 } < u _ { s } < \gamma _ { 2 } , \qquad - \gamma _ { 2 } < \dot { u } _ { s } < \gamma _ { 2 } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Let a quantity $\bar { g }$ be such that

$$
0 < \bar { g } \leq \gamma _ { 2 } , \qquad \bar { g } < \frac { 1 } { 4 n [ P + ( Q / \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ) ] } .
$$

The degree of smallness of $\lvert \partial U _ { s } / \partial u _ { k } \rvert , \lvert \partial U _ { s } / \partial \dot { u } _ { k } \rvert , \lvert U _ { s } ^ { 0 } \rvert$ is so chosen that $| \partial S _ { s } / \partial u _ { k } | < D _ { 1 } / 2 , | \partial S _ { s } / \partial \dot { u } _ { k } | < D _ { 1 } / 2 , | S _ { k } ^ { 0 } | / \bar { g } < D _ { 2 }$ in the domain $- \gamma _ { 1 } <$ $u _ { s } < \gamma _ { 1 } , - \gamma _ { 1 } < \dot { u } _ { s } < \gamma _ { 1 }$ .We have seen that to any point of the domain $- \overline { { w } } \overline { { g } } \cong u _ { s } \cong \overline { { w } } \overline { { g } }$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ there corresponds one and only one point of the domain

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { \mu } p _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv \delta \bar { g } ^ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { \nu } q _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } \equiv - \delta \bar { g } ^ { 2 } \qquad ( \overline { { { w } } } < 1 ) ,
$$

and that the solution whose elements have the values $p , q$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$ remains in this domain for $t > t _ { 1 }$ . Thus we have

Theorem 5: To any point $\boldsymbol { u }$ of the domain $- \overline { { w } } \overline { { g } } \cong u _ { s } \cong \overline { { w } } \overline { { g } }$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ and to any value $t _ { 0 }$ of $t$ there corresponds one and only one solution starting at the point for $t = t _ { 0 }$ and remaining for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in the domain $- \bar { g } < u _ { s } < \bar { g }$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ .There exists one and only one solution that remains in this domain for all values of $t .$ Two solutions remaining for sufficiently large values of $t$ in this domain asymptotically approach each other for $t \to + \infty$ . The number $\overline { { w } } > 0$ depends only on the nature of $T$ and $V$ ，and $\bar { g } > 0$ depends only on the limits of $U _ { s }$ and the nature of $_ T$ and $V$

Given $\bar { h } > 0$ such that $\bar { h } \leq \bar { g }$ ，the_solution corresponding to the points in the domain $- \overline { { w } } \bar { h } \le u _ { s } \le \overline { { w } } \bar { h } \left( s = 1 , \dots , n \right)$ remains in the domain $- \bar { h } < u _ { s } < \bar { h } , - \bar { h } < \dot { u } _ { s } < \bar { h } ( s = 1 , . .$ if $| S _ { s } ^ { 0 } | / { \bar { h } } < D _ { 2 }$ Hence we obtain

Theorem 6: Suppose that in the hypothesis of Case I the functions $U _ { s }$ disappear from the equations (116). Then， given an arbitrary number $\bar { h }$ satisfying $0 < \bar { h } ^ { - } < \bar { z } , \bar { z } > 0$ ，depending only on the nature of $T$ and $V$ ,there exists,for any point of the domain $- \overline { { w } } \bar { h } \leqq u _ { s } \leqq$ $\overline { { w } } \bar { h } \ ( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ and for all values $t _ { 1 }$ of $t$ ，a solution that remains for $t > t _ { 1 }$ in the domain $- \bar { h } < u _ { s } < \bar { h } , - \bar { h } < \dot { u } _ { s } < \bar { h } \left( s = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ and has the coordinates $\boldsymbol { u } _ { s }$ of that point as a partial system of the initial values for $t = t _ { 1 }$ . This solution is unique that remains for $t > t _ { 1 }$ in the domain $- \bar { z } < u _ { s } < \bar { z } ( s = 1 , \dots , n )$ and has these initial values.The unique solution remaining in this domain for all values of $t$ is $u _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = u _ { n } = 0$ .For all solutions remaining in this domain for sufficiently large values of $t$ we have $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } u _ { s } = 0$ and $\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \dot { u } _ { s }$ $= 0 \left( s = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ .

A similar conclusion can be drawn for $t < t _ { 1 }$ and $t \to - \infty$

Bohl (1910,1907) applied this existence theorem to the motion of the particles in Saturn's rings (Sections 12.20 and 12.21),and to the differential equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } - [ \alpha + \phi ( t ) ] z = \psi ( t ) ,
$$

where $\phi ( t ) , \psi ( t )$ are continuous for all values of $t$ and contain $t$ in the argument of trigonometric functions (see Chapter 12).

Murray (1925) extended Bohl's theorems to canonical systems of differential equations by proving the existence of trajectories that remain in the neighborhood of an equilibrium point when the kinetic energy is a definite quadratic form in the velocity and the seconddegree terms of the force function are nonsingular. Then Murray (1926) studied the families of orbits of an arbitrary mass in the threebody problem on the basis of invariantive relations (Section 4.6) instead of the method of variation of constants,in particular the orbits in the neighborhood of the equilateral-triangular solution and of the isosceles-triangular solution (see Section 19.7).

Harasahal (1951,1962) studied a system of linear differential equations with quasi-periodic coefficients (Sections 14.19-14.23)，by showing that such a system can be reduced to a system with constant coefficients by means of a nonsingular linear substitution with quasiperiodic coefficients.The necessary and sufficient condition that the system ${ d x } / { d t } = P ( t ) x$ with quasi-periodic function matrix $P ( t )$ be reducible to a system with constant coefficients is that the constant matrix $_ D$ such that $x ( t + \omega _ { 1 } , \ldots , t + \omega _ { n } ) = x ( t , \ldots , t ) D$ should be independent of $\omega$ (see Hale, 1963).

# 16.10 STABLE AND UNSTABLE COORDINATES

Consider the motion near an equilibrium point represented by

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { q } _ { i } } \right) - \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { i } } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

$$
T = \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } { \dot { q } } _ { i } { \dot { q } } _ { j } \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

By a linear transformation with constant coefficients, the variables and the coefficients can be made to be such that

$$
( a _ { i i } ) _ { 0 } = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } , \qquad q _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = q _ { n } ^ { 0 } = 0 ,
$$

$$
( a _ { i j } ) _ { \boldsymbol 0 } = 0 \qquad ( i \neq j ) , \qquad \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } U } { \partial q _ { i } \partial q _ { j } } \right) _ { \boldsymbol 0 } = 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , \dots , n
$$

We distinguish the coordinates $q$ in three categories according as

$$
\left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } U } { \partial q _ { i } ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { 0 } \lessgtr 0 ,
$$

and call them stable, unstable,and singular coordinates, respectively. We put aside the singular coordinates for which the Hessian of $U$ is zero.In the nonsingular category we classify three cases :

Case I. Only stable coordinates are present. By choosing $p$ positive we see that there exists an $e > 0$ such that any motion with the initial values $| q _ { i } | < e , | \dot { q } _ { i } | < e ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ lies for all values of $t$ inside the region Ω and is always such tha $| q _ { i } | < p , | \dot { q } _ { i } | < p ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$

Case II.Only unstable coordinates are present. There are two regions inside Ω,that is,the subregion $H \colon - a < q _ { i } < a$ and the subregion $\eta \colon - b < q _ { i } < b , b <$ ，such that the system from an arbitrary position belonging to the sub-region $\eta$ at an arbitrary instant can remain inside the subregion $\pmb { H }$ for all larger values of $t$ in one and only one way,such that $q _ { i } , \dot { q } _ { i }$ can remain arbitrarily smaller in absolute value for the subsequent motion when we choose the initial position sufficiently near the equilibrium position，and all $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { i }$ tend to zero as $t \to \infty$ . This is the only case of motion that remains inside $\pmb { H }$ for all $t$ (Bohl,1910;Kneser,1895,1897).

Case III. Stable and unstable coordinates are both present. Let the region $M \colon - a _ { i } < q _ { i } < a _ { i }$ be chosen sufficiently small around the equilibrium position.There are an infinite number of motions that remain in the region $M$ for sufficiently large t. If a set of all $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { \pmb q } _ { i }$ of the stable coordinates is so chosen that all $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { i }$ are smaller in absolute value than a positive number belonging to $M$ ，otherwise arbitrary,then there is one and only one motion that is always inside $M$ by starting with given values of $q _ { i } , { \dot { q } } _ { i }$ at a given instant. If the initial values of $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { i }$ are chosen sufficiently small, then the motion can be such that all $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { i }$ remain arbitrarily small in absolute value.
