There are an infinite number of motions that remain inside $M$ for all t.If the stable coordinates and the corresponding momenta are chosen sufficiently small in absolute values,otherwise arbitrary, then there is one and only one motion that takes the given values of $q _ { i } , \dot { q } _ { i }$ at a given instant and remains inside $M$ for all t. If these given values of $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { \pmb q } _ { i }$ are sufficiently small in absolute values, then all $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { q } _ { i }$ can be arbitrarily small for all t. Corresponding to each motion of this latter category there are an infinite number of motions that approach it asymptotically as $t \to \infty$ ·

The relation of these two categories of motion is characterized by the following properties.Any motion that remains from an instant in a sufficiently small, otherwise arbitrarily chosen,neighborhood $N$ of the equilibrium position, tends asymptotically as $t \to \infty$ to a motion that remains in a neighborhood $P$ of the equilibrium position corresponding to the region $N$ ，where the asymptotic approach to zero occurs in the manner in which $e ^ { - p t }$ ， $p > 0$ ，does.For these motions the energy constant $C = T - T _ { 1 }$ is equal to or larger than zero.There is only one motion for $C = 0$ that remains inside $M$ for all $t$ This is the motion that remains in the equilibrium position. Among the motions that remain inside $M$ for sufficiently large $t$ there are always infinitely many for which the stable coordinates tend to zero as $t $ $\infty$ . In such a motion all $q _ { i }$ and $\dot { q } _ { i }$ should tend to zero as $t \to \infty$

The quantities $\left( { \partial ^ { 2 } U } / { \partial q _ { i } ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { 0 }$ corresponding to the unstable coordinates are all different ； thus for any such motion one of the unstable coordinates $q _ { \rho }$ is such that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { q _ { i } } { q _ { \rho } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \dot { q } _ { i } } { q _ { \rho } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { q _ { i } } { \dot { q } _ { \rho } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \dot { q } _ { i } } { \dot { q } _ { \rho } } } = 0 \qquad ( i \neq \rho ) ,
$$

and either

$$
\operatorname * { l i m } _ { t  \infty } \frac { \dot { q } _ { \rho } } { q _ { \rho } } = \sqrt { ( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } U } { \partial q _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } } ) } _ { 0 } ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } \frac { \dot { q } _ { \rho } } { q _ { \rho } } = - \sqrt { ( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } U } { \partial q _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } } ) } _ { 0 } . } \end{array}
$$

In particular,if only one stable coordinate $q _ { \sigma }$ exists,then such a neighborhood of the equilibrium point can be assigned so that all motions remaining inside this neighborhood for all $t$ are periodic.The period can be chosen to differ from $2 \pi / \sqrt { - ( \partial ^ { 2 } U / \partial q _ { \sigma } ^ { 2 } ) _ { 0 } }$ by an arbitrarily small amount. Such a case was studied by Hadamard (1897, 1898 ; Section 23.13) for the case of one unstable coordinate.A motion starting with a position near an equilibrium position for which the force function is not a maximum recedes from it by a finite quantity, except in the following cases : (i) the deviations of the unstable coordinates and the corresponding momenta remain small compared with those of the other coordinates and momenta,(ii) the motion is asymptotic to this kind of motion,and (i) the motion approaches the equilibrium position or any other equilibrium position， if present. Kneser (1895,1897) showed the existence of asymptotic trajectories toward an equilibrium position in the case when the force function is a minimum for dynamical systems with two degrees of freedom.

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ : Let a region $G \colon - a _ { i } \leqq x _ { i } \leqq a _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ be given,in which $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ are continuous functions of $_ x$ and do not vanish simultaneously. Then there is a place $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ in the neighborhood of $G$ such that

$$
f _ { i } ( u _ { 1 } , \dots , u _ { n } ) = N u _ { i } \qquad ( N < 0 ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \dots , n ) .
$$

A set of values $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ or $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ，or $x , y , \xi , \eta$ is called a place (Stelle) (see Section 2.20).

To prove this theorem Bohl (1893） started with the properties of implicit functions.

Bohl (1904) further proved that

1． There cannot exist continuous functions $F _ { 1 } , \ldots , F _ { n }$ defined in the region $G \colon - a _ { i } \leq x _ { i } \leq a _ { i } ( i = 1 , \ldots , n , a _ { i } > 0 )$ that do not vanish simultaneously and give $\ F _ { i } = x _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , \ldots , n )$ for the places on the boundary of $G$

2.Let $F _ { 1 } , \ldots , F _ { n }$ be continuous functions in the region $G$ and suppose that $\pmb { F } _ { i } = \pmb { x } _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , \ldots , n )$ on the boundary of $G$ Let $\gamma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \gamma _ { n }$ be a set representing a place of $G$ . Then there is a place in $G$ for which $\boldsymbol { F } _ { i } = \boldsymbol { \gamma } _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , \ldots , n )$ .

3．Take a closed set $M$ from a region $\pmb { A }$ containing all real values of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ , whose coordinates are bounded by finite bounds. If there is a place of $\pmb { A }$ not belonging to $M$ in the neighborhood of a place $\mu$ of $M$ ，then $\mu$ is called a boundary of $M$ , otherwise an inner place.If $\gamma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \gamma _ { n }$ is an inner place, then there exists also an inner place for which ${ { f } _ { i } } = { { \gamma } _ { i } } \left( i = 1 , \ldots , { { n } } \right)$

From these properties follow the propositions :

I.Given $G \colon x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } \leq R ^ { 2 }$ , let a place in $G$ be $\xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n }$ Take $0 < r < R$ and choose a continuous function $\psi$ ，such that $\psi$ coincides with $\xi$ in $x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } \leq r ^ { 2 }$ ，is given by

$$
\psi _ { i } = \xi _ { i } + { \frac { \rho - r } { R - r } } ( x _ { i } - \xi _ { i } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

$$
\rho = \sqrt { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

in $r ^ { 2 } < x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } \leq R ^ { 2 }$ ，and reduces to $_ x$ for a place on the boundary of $G$ .By the above properties $\psi$ should be zero at least at one place in $G$ ，and indeed in $r ^ { 2 } < x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } \leq R ^ { 2 }$ or $( R - \rho ) \xi _ { i }$ $+ ~ ( \rho - r ) x _ { i } = 0$ .Since $R - \rho \neq 0$ ，we have $\xi _ { i } = \upsilon x _ { i } ( \upsilon < 0 ; i = 1$ 、 $\cdots , n )$ with

$$
\nu = - { \frac { | \xi _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | \xi _ { n } | } { | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | } } .
$$

Consider a sequence $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 }$ ，... converging to $\pmb R$ , and let a sequence $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots .$ be the corresponding $x$ -values. We have

$$
\xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \nu ^ { \prime } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } , ~ \nu ^ { \prime } = - \frac { | \xi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | + \dots + | \xi _ { n } ^ { \prime } | } { | x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | + \dots + | x _ { n } ^ { \prime } | } ,
$$

The places $x ^ { \prime } , x ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,... possess at least one limit place $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ We choose a subsequence $\bar { x } , \bar { \bar { x } } , \ldots$ from $x ^ { \prime } , x ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，... so that the places of the subsequence converge to $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ . The values of $\rho$ corresponding to $x ^ { \prime } , x ^ { \prime \prime }$ 。... converge to $R$ ， since $R \ge \rho ^ { \prime } > r _ { 1 } , R \ge \rho ^ { \prime \prime } >$ $r _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ The values of $\rho$ corresponding to $\bar { x } , \bar { \bar { x } } , \ldots$ converge to $\sqrt { X _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + X _ { n } ^ { 2 } }$ .Hence we have ${ \sqrt { X _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + X _ { n } ^ { 2 } } } = R$ . Accordingly the place $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ lies on the boundary of $G$ . Similarly the corresponding sequence $\overline { { \xi } } , \overline { { \overline { { \xi } } } } ,$ ... converges to $\Xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \Xi _ { n }$ ; accordingly, the corresponding values of $\nu$ converge to

$$
N = - { \frac { | \Xi _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | \Xi _ { n } | } { | X _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | X _ { n } | } } .
$$

Hence there exists a place $X$ on the boundary of $G$ for which

$$
\Xi _ { i } = N X _ { i } , \qquad N < 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where E are the values of $\xi$ corresponding to the place $X$

II. Let $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ be continuous functions of $x$ in $G$ that do not vanish simultaneously. Introduce the region $H \colon x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + x _ { n } ^ { 2 } \leq 1$ Let $x _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ be a place on the boundary of $G$ ，and $\xi _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ be a place on the boundary of $H$ . Suppose that these two places correspond to each other through

$$
\xi _ { i } ^ { 0 } = \frac { x _ { i } ^ { 0 } } { \sqrt { ( x _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( x _ { n } ^ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } } } .
$$

As can be seen, $x _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ are continuous functions of $\xi _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \dots , \xi _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ such that $x _ { i } ^ { 0 } = \phi _ { i } ( \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } ^ { 0 } )$ , and vice versa.Denote the region $\pmb { H }$ devoid of the place $x _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { n } = 0$ by $H ^ { \prime }$ .Let the correspondence of $H ^ { \prime }$ to $G$ be

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x _ { i } = \sqrt { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } } } \\ & { \qquad \cdot \phi _ { n } \biggl ( \frac { \xi _ { 1 } } { \sqrt { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } } } , \ldots , \frac { \xi _ { n } } { \sqrt { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \ \cdots \ + \ \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } } } \biggr ) , } \end{array}
$$

so that the $x ' s$ are continuous functions of the $\xi ^ { \ast } { \mathbf s }$ In particular, $\xi _ { 1 } = \dots = \xi _ { n } = 0$ corresponds to $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ .Denote such functions $x$ of $\xi$ by $[ x _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ x _ { n } ]$ . Let a place of $\pmb { H }$ correspond to $f _ { i } ( [ x _ { 1 } ] , \dots , [ x _ { n } ] )$ . Then there exists a place $\Xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \Xi _ { n }$ such that

$$
\mathfrak { f } _ { i } ( [ X _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ X _ { n } ] ) = \nu \cdot \Xi _ { i } \qquad ( \nu < 0 ) ,
$$

on the boundary of $\pmb { H }$ ， where the $[ X _ { i } ]$ are the values of $[ x _ { i } ]$ at $\Xi _ { i }$

We have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { [ X _ { i } ] = \sqrt { \Xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdots + \Xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } } } \\ & { \qquad \cdot \phi _ { i } \biggl ( \frac { \Xi _ { 1 } } { \sqrt { \Xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \ \cdots \ + \ \Xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } } } , \ldots , \frac { \Xi _ { n } } { \sqrt { \Xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \ \cdots \ + \ \Xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } } } \biggr ) , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
[ X _ { i } ] = \phi _ { i } ( \Xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \Xi _ { n } ) .
$$

Since

$$
\Xi _ { i } = { \frac { [ X _ { i } ] } { \sqrt { [ X _ { 1 } ] ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ X _ { n } ] ^ { 2 } } } } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
f _ { i } ( [ X _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ X _ { n } ] ) = { \frac { \nu \cdot [ X _ { i } ] } { \sqrt { [ X _ { 1 } ] ^ { 2 } + \ \cdots \ + \ [ X _ { n } ] ^ { 2 } } } } ,
$$

where $[ X _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ X _ { n } ]$ is a place on the boundary of $G$ . Thus there exists a place $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ on the boundary of $G$ such that

$$
f _ { i } ( u _ { 1 } , \dots , u _ { n } ) = N \cdot u _ { i } , \qquad N < 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \dots , n ) .
$$

After these preliminary propositions，we consider the differential equations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial T } { \partial { \dot { \omega } _ { i } } } - \frac { \partial T } { \partial \omega _ { i } } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial \omega _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , \dots , n ) , } \\ { T = \displaystyle \sum _ { r , s } A _ { r s } { \dot { \omega } _ { r } } { \dot { \omega } _ { s } } , } \end{array}
$$

where the $\boldsymbol { A } _ { \tau s }$ are continuous functions of $\omega$ with continuous first- and second-order derivatives in a neighborhood of a place $\omega _ { 1 0 } , \ldots , \omega _ { n 0 } ,$ and $\boldsymbol { U }$ is a function of similar character. For $\omega _ { 1 } = \omega _ { 1 0 } , \ldots , \omega _ { n } = \omega _ { n 0 }$ the derivatives ${ \partial U } / { \partial \omega }$ vanish, so that $( \omega _ { 1 0 } , \ldots , \omega _ { n 0 } )$ is an equilibrium position.If necessary, by a linear transformation $\pmb { U }$ can be put in the form $2 U = \gamma _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \gamma _ { n } \omega _ { n } ^ { 2 } + \phi$ with constant $\gamma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \gamma _ { n }$ ， and

$$
2 T = \dot { \omega } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \dot { \omega } _ { n } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { r , s } a _ { r s } \dot { \omega } _ { r } \dot { \omega } _ { s } ,
$$

where $\phi$ and $a _ { r s }$ are continuous together with the derivatives of the first two orders in a neighborhood of $\omega _ { 1 } = \cdots = \omega _ { n } = 0$ For $\omega _ { 1 } =$ $\dotsb = \omega _ { n } = 0$ we have

$$
a _ { r s } = \phi = \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \omega _ { \mu } } = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \phi } { \partial \omega _ { \lambda } \partial \omega _ { \mu } } = 0 ,
$$

but $T = 0$ only for all $\dot { \omega } = 0$ ，otherwise $T > 0$ . According $\mathsf { a s } ~ \gamma _ { i }$ is negative， positive，or zero，the coordinates $\omega _ { i }$ are called stable, unstable, or singular. We assume that no singular coordinate is present. A stable coordinate is denoted by $x _ { i }$ and an unstable by $y _ { \pmb { \mu } }$

The Lagrangian equations are solved in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \ddot { \omega } _ { i } = \gamma _ { i } \omega _ { i } + \Omega _ { i } , } \\ { \displaystyle \Omega _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \omega _ { i } } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { i \mu } \Bigl ( \gamma _ { \mu } \omega _ { \mu } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial \omega _ { \mu } } \Bigr ) + \sum _ { \tau , s } b _ { r s } ^ { i } \omega _ { r } \dot { \omega } _ { s } , } \end{array}
$$

where $b _ { r s } ^ { i }$ and $\beta _ { i , \mu }$ are continuous functions of $\omega$ with continuous firstorder derivatives in a certain neighborhood of $\omega _ { 1 } = \cdots = \omega _ { n } = 0$ For $\omega _ { 1 } = \cdots = \omega _ { n } = 0$ all $\beta$ vanish.The functions $\Omega _ { i }$ are continuous in the same region with continuous first-order derivatives with respect to $\pmb { \omega }$ and $\dot { \omega }$ . For $\omega _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \omega _ { n } = \dot { \omega } _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \dot { \omega } _ { n } = 0$ ,the functions $\Omega _ { i }$ vanish together with the first-order derivatives with respect to $\omega$ and $\dot { \omega }$ . We denote $\dot { x } _ { i }$ and $\dot { y } _ { \mu }$ by $\hat { \xi } _ { i }$ and $\eta _ { \pmb { \mu } }$ . Then we obtain

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \xi _ { i } , \qquad \frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } = - p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } + X _ { i } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d y _ { \mu } } { d t } = \eta _ { \mu } , \qquad \frac { d \eta _ { \mu } } { d t } = q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } + Y _ { \mu }
$$

$$
( i = 1 , \dots , M ; \mu = 1 , \dots , N ; M + N = n )
$$

where $X$ and $\pmb { Y }$ are continuous functions of $x , y , \xi , \eta$ for all $\xi , \eta$ and for a certain neighborhood of $x _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = x _ { M } = y _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = y _ { N } = 0 ,$ with continuous first-order derivatives with respect to $x , y , \xi , \eta$

We have the energy integral

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + R = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

$$
R = \sum _ { \lambda , \rho } a _ { \lambda \rho } \dot { \omega } _ { \lambda } \dot { \omega } _ { \rho } - \phi ,
$$

where $\pmb R$ is a continuous function of $x , y , \xi , \eta$ with continuous firstand second-order derivatives with respect to $x , y , \xi , \eta$ in a certain neighborhood of $x = y = 0$ and for all $\xi , \eta$ .For $x = y = \xi = \eta = 0$ the function $\pmb R$ vanishes together with the first- and second-order derivatives.

We choose positive numbers $\overline { { a } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \overline { { a } } _ { n }$ such that the domain $\overline { { T } } \colon | \omega _ { 1 } | < \bar { a } _ { 1 } , \ldots , | \omega _ { n } | < \bar { a } _ { n }$ ，together with the boundary,is contained in the domain just defined in the neighborhood of $\omega _ { 1 } = \cdots = \omega _ { n } =$ 0.In the following we restrict ourselves to the consideration of the $\pmb { \omega }$ -valuesin the domain $\overline { T }$ . Put

$$
\iota _ { \mu } = \eta _ { \mu } + q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu } , ~ v _ { \mu } = \eta _ { \mu } - q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu } , ~ u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } = 2 [ \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } ] ,
$$

and form

$$
\frac { d u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { d t } = 2 q _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + 2 u _ { \mu } Y _ { \mu } , \frac { d v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { d t } = - 2 q _ { \mu } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + 2 v _ { \mu } Y _ { \mu } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d } { d t } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) = 2 \xi _ { i } X _ { i } .
$$

Consider two motions with simultaneous values of the coordinates $x _ { i } , \xi _ { i } , y _ { \mu } , \eta _ { \mu }$ and $x _ { i } + x _ { i } ^ { \prime } , \xi _ { i } + \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } , y _ { \mu } + y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } , \eta _ { \mu } + \eta _ { \mu } ^ { \prime }$ . Denote the differences of $X , Y$ for these two places by $X _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and $Y _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ，then

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } , \qquad \frac { d \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = - p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } + X _ { i } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \eta _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } , \qquad \frac { d \eta _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } + Y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } .
$$

Write

$$
u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } = \eta _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } + q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } , v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } = \eta _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } - q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } , u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } + v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } = 2 [ \eta _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } + q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } .
$$

then we have

$$
\frac { d u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } } { d t } = 2 q _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } + 2 u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } Y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } , \frac { d v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } } { d t } = - 2 q _ { \mu } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } + 2 v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } Y _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d } { d t } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } ) = 2 \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } X _ { i } ^ { \prime } .
$$

After these preparations Bohl (1904) proved the following auxiliary theorem.

Let $l _ { 1 } , \dots , l _ { M } , m _ { 1 } , \dots , m _ { N } , \sigma$ be arbitrary positive numbers， and $s _ { 1 } , \ldots , s _ { M } , t _ { 1 } , \ldots , t _ { M } , z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { N }$ be real variables，and $\bar { k }$ be the smallest number among $l _ { i } , l _ { i } p _ { i } , m _ { \mu } , 2 q _ { \mu } / ( 1 + q _ { \mu } )$ ，and $\bar { g }$ be the largest among $l _ { i } , m _ { \mu }$ . Put

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle { T = \frac { \sqrt { 2 M + N } } { \bar { k } } , \qquad \Delta = \bar { g } \sqrt { M + N } . } } \end{array}
$$

We choose such a neighborhood $\Omega$ of $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ that the first-order derivatives of $X _ { i } , Y _ { \mu }$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are in absolute value smaller than

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { \displaystyle { \frac { q } { \left( { T + \frac { 1 + q } { 2 q } \sqrt { N } } \right) ( \Delta + \sqrt { N } ) } } , \qquad \frac { \sigma } { 2 \sqrt { N } \left( { T + \frac { 1 + q } { 2 q } \sqrt { N } } \right) } } .  \end{array}
$$

This neighborhood $\Omega$ possesses the following properties :

(i) If in two motions the $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ -places for $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t _ { 1 }$ lie in Ω and for $t = t _ { 0 }$

$$
r ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } l _ { i } ^ { 2 } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } ) + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } m _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } \leq 0 ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } \neq 0 ,
$$

then we have $r ^ { \prime } < 0$ for the interval $t _ { 0 } < t \le t _ { 1 } ;$

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } > ( 2 q - \sigma ) \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 }
$$

for the interval $t _ { 0 } \leqq t \leq t _ { 1 : }$ and

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } > \exp \left\{ ( 2 q - \sigma ) ( t _ { 1 } - t _ { 0 } ) \right\} \cdot \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } ,
$$

where the indices 1 and O denote the values for $t = t _ { 1 }$ and $t = t _ { 0 }$ respectively.

(ii)If in two solutions the $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ -places for $t _ { 0 } \geq t \geq t _ { 1 }$ lie in Ω and for $t = t _ { 0 }$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { s ^ { \prime } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } l _ { i } ^ { 2 } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } ) \ + \ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } m _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } - \ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } \leq 0 , \hfill } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } \neq 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

then we have $s ^ { \prime } < 0$ for the interval $t _ { 0 } > t \geq t _ { 1 }$ ，

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 } < - ( 2 q - \sigma ) \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 }
$$

for the interval $t _ { 0 } \geq t \geq t _ { 1 } ,$ and

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { v } _ { \mu 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } > \exp \left\{ ( 2 q - \sigma ) ( t _ { 0 } - t _ { 1 } ) \right\} \cdot \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { v } _ { \mu 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } .
$$

(ii) If in one of the motions the $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ -places for $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t _ { 1 }$ lie in $\Omega$ and for $t = t _ { 0 }$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } l _ { i } ^ { 2 } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) \ + \ \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } m _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } - \ \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv 0 , \hfill } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \neq 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

then we have $r < 0$ for the whole interval $t _ { 0 } < t \leq t _ { 1 }$ ，

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } > ( 2 q - \sigma ) \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 }
$$

for the interval $t _ { 0 } \leq t \leq t _ { 1 }$ and

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } > \exp \left\{ ( 2 q - \sigma ) ( t _ { 1 } - t _ { 0 } ) \right\} \cdot \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu 0 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

(iv) If in one of the motions the $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ -places for $t _ { 0 } \geq t \geq t _ { 1 }$ lie in $\Omega$ and for $t = t _ { 0 }$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { s = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } l _ { i } ^ { 2 } ( \hat { \xi } _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) \ + \ \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } m _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } - \ \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv 0 , \nonumber } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \neq 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

then we have $s < 0$ for the whole interval $t _ { 0 } \geq t \geq t _ { 1 }$ ，

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } < - ( 2 q - \sigma ) \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 }
$$

for the interval $t _ { 0 } \geq t \geq t _ { 1 }$ and

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { v } _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } > \exp \left\{ ( 2 q - \sigma ) ( t _ { 0 } - t _ { 1 } ) \right\} \cdot \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { v } _ { \mu 0 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

We choose $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , \dots , l _ { M }$ ， $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = m _ { N } = m < 1 , \sigma _ { \cdot }$ and a neighborhood $\Omega _ { 0 }$ corresponding to these numbers.

From these properties we obtain

Theorem 2: Consider two motions for $t _ { 0 } - \tau \le t \le t _ { 0 } + \tau , \tau > 0 ,$ in the domain $\Omega _ { 0 }$ Denote by $\kappa$ the larger of the values of $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 }$ for $t = t _ { 0 } + \tau$ and $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 }$ for $t = t _ { 0 } - \tau$ Then the values of $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 }$ and $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { \prime 2 }$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ are

$$
\leq \frac { 2 } { 1 - m ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } l _ { i } ^ { 2 } ( \hat { \xi } _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } ) + K \exp { \{ - ( 2 q - \sigma ) \tau \} } ,
$$

where $\pmb { \sigma }$ is an arbitrary small number.

Theorem 3: If a motion for $t _ { 0 } - \tau \le t \le t _ { 0 } + \tau , \tau > 0$ lies in $\Omega _ { 0 }$ and if we denote by $G$ the larger of the values of $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 }$ for $t =$ $t _ { 0 } + \tau$ and of $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 }$ for $t = t _ { 0 } - \tau _ { \mathrm { ~ } }$ ，then the values of $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 }$ and $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 }$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ are

$$
\leq \frac { 2 } { 1 - m ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } l _ { i } ^ { 2 } ( \hat { \xi } _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) + G \exp { \{ - ( 2 q - \sigma ) \tau \} } .
$$

Consider a neighborhood of $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ such that

$$
| R | \leq \left[ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \varkappa } \left( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \nu } \left( \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \right) \right] \frac { 5 } { 2 2 + 3 6 M } ,
$$

where the equality sign occurs for $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ .Such a neighborhood $E$ exists,depending only on the initial values of the motion. The region $E$ is contained in the combined region, denoted by $( F _ { 1 } , G )$ ， of $F _ { 1 }$ and $G$ ，where

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l r l } & { F _ { 1 } \colon } & & { - \delta \le \xi _ { i } \le \delta , } & & { - \delta \le p _ { i } x _ { i } \le \delta , } & & { } \\ & { G \colon } & & { - \gamma \le \eta _ { \mu } \le \gamma , } & & { - \gamma \le q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu } \le \gamma , } & & { \gamma \le \frac { \delta } { 2 \sqrt { N } } . } \end{array}
$$

Theorem 4: If the $( \bar { x } _ { i } , \bar { \xi } _ { i } , \bar { y } _ { \mu } , \bar { \eta } _ { \mu } )$ -place, which is the $( x _ { i } , \xi _ { i } , y _ { \mu } , \eta _ { \mu } ) .$ place for $t = t _ { 1 }$ ，lies in the region $( F _ { 1 } , G )$ and the place for $t = t _ { 2 }$ lies in the region $( F _ { 2 } , G )$ ，such that

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } ( \bar { \xi } _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } \bar { x } _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) > \delta ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where

$$
- \frac { \delta } { 3 \sqrt { M } } \leq \pounds _ { i } \leq \frac { \delta } { 3 \sqrt { M } } , \qquad - \frac { \delta } { 3 \sqrt { M } } \leq p _ { i } x _ { i } \leq \frac { \delta } { 3 \sqrt { M } } ,
$$

then the partial place $( x , \xi )$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$ does not lie on the boundary of $\boldsymbol { F } _ { 1 }$

We consider a neighborhood $\pmb { H }$ around $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ with the properties that all places of $\pmb { H }$ are in the region $E$ , and that in any place of $\pmb { H }$ the partial derivatives of $X _ { i } , Y _ { \mu }$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are smaller in absolute value than

$$
\frac { q } { \Big [ \sqrt { 2 M + N } \Big ( \frac { p + 1 } { p } + \frac { 1 } { 2 q } \Big ) + \frac { 1 + q } { 2 q } \sqrt { N } \Big ] [ \sqrt { M + N } + \sqrt { N } ] } ,
$$

where $p$ is the smallest value of $p _ { \mu }$ . Suppose that with an arbitrary positive number $d$ the region

$$
- d \leq \xi _ { i } \leq d , \qquad - d \leq p _ { i } x _ { i } \leq d ,
$$

$$
- \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \leq \eta _ { \mu } \leq \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } , \qquad - \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \leq q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu } \leq \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } }
$$

lies inside $\pmb { H }$ . Introduce two regions for $x , \xi$

$$
A : \qquad - d \leq \xi _ { i } \leq d , \qquad - d \leq p _ { i } x _ { i } \leq d ;
$$

$$
\alpha : - \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } \leq \xi _ { i } \leq \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } , \qquad - \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } \leq p _ { i } x _ { i } \leq \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } ;
$$

and a region for $u , v$

$$
B : - \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \leq u _ { \mu } \leq \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } , - \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \leq v _ { \mu } \leq \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } ;
$$

and also a region for $\eta , y$

$$
Z : ~ - { \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } } \leq \eta _ { \mu } \leq { \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } } , ~ - { \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } } \leq q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu } \leq { \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } } .
$$

A region for $x , \xi , u , v$ is a combined region $( A , B )$ of $\pmb { A }$ and $\pmb { B }$ Similarly there are other combined regions.

If $( x , \xi , u , v )$ lies for $t = t _ { 2 }$ in $( \alpha , Z )$ and for $t = t _ { 1 }$ in $( A , Z )$ and if $u _ { \rho } = \pm d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$ ，where $\rho$ is one of the numbers 1,2,..., $N$ ， then we have $d u _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } / d t > 0$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$

If $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ lies for $t = t _ { 2 }$ in $( \alpha , Z )$ and for $t = t _ { 1 }$ in $( A , Z )$ and if $v _ { \rho } = \pm d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$ ，where $\rho$ is one of the numbers 1,2,.., $N$ ，then we have $d v _ { \rho } ^ { 2 } / d t < 0$ for $t = t _ { 1 }$

Theorem 5: To each $( x , \xi , v )$ -place of the region

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle - \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } \le \xi _ { i } \le \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } , \qquad - \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } \le p _ { i } x _ { i } \le \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle - \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \le v _ { \mu } \le \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } } } \end{array}
$$

we adjoin an $u$ -place in the region $- d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } ) \le u _ { \mu } \le d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ to complete an $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ -place，which,if taken as the initial place, gives a solution remaining in the region $( A , B )$ for increasing $t$

To each $( x , \xi , u )$ -place of the region

$$
\vert \xi _ { i } \vert \leq \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } , \qquad p _ { i } \vert x _ { i } \vert \leq \frac { d } { 3 \sqrt { M } } , \qquad \vert u _ { \mu } \vert \leq \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } }
$$

we adjoin a $_ v$ -place in the region $\lvert v _ { \mu } \rvert \leq d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ to complete an $( { \pmb x } , { \pmb \xi } , { \pmb u } , { \pmb v } )$ -place,which,if taken as the initial place,gives a solution remaining in the region $( A , B )$ for decreasing $t$

This theorem can be proved by reductio ad absurdum.Put

$$
\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 1 + q ( p + 2 ) / ( p ( q + 1 ) ) } , ~ \varepsilon = \frac { 2 \sqrt { N } q } { \sqrt { M + N } + \sqrt { N } } .
$$

If we suppose that $l _ { 1 } = l _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = l _ { M } = 1$ ， $m _ { 1 } = \cdots = m _ { N } = \lambda .$ ， ${ \pmb \sigma } = { \pmb \varepsilon }$ ，then the region $\pmb { H }$ possesses the same character as the region $\Omega$ in the foregoing discussion.Thus we obtain

Theorem 6: If two motions remain in $\pmb { H }$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，and the $( x , \xi , v )$ -coordinates of these two motions coincide for such a.value of $t _ { i }$ ，then the two motions coincide.If two motions remain in $\pmb { H }$ for $t \leqq t _ { 0 }$ and the $( x , \xi , u )$ -coordinates of these two motions coincide for such a value of $t$ ，then the two motions coincide.The correspondence of $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { N }$ to $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { M } , \xi _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \xi _ { M } , u _ { 1 } , \dotsc , u _ { N }$ is one-to-one and continuous.

Choose an $( x , \xi )$ -place in the region $\alpha$ ，then $u _ { \mu } - [ u _ { \mu } ] , v _ { \mu } - [ v _ { \mu } ]$ $( \mu = 1 , \ldots , N )$ are continuous functions of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { N } , v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { N }$ in the region $\pmb { B }$ . There should be a $( u , v )$ -place in $\pmb { B }$ ，in which the $2 N$ functions vanish simultaneously.Hence

Theorem 7: To each place of $\alpha$ we adjoin a $( u , v )$ -place in $B$ to complete an $( x , \xi , u )$ -placə,which,if taken as the initial place,gives a solution remaining in $( A , B )$ for all values of $t$ If two motions remain in $\pmb { H }$ for all valuss of $t$ ，and the $( x , \xi )$ -coordinates of the two motions coincide for a certain value of $t$ ，then the two motions generally coincide.The correspondence of $u , v$ to $x , \xi$ is one-to-one and continuous.

# 16.11ASYMPTOTIC SOLUTIONS

Choose $g > h > 0$ and a region

$$
\begin{array} { r } { : \quad | \mathfrak { f } _ { i } | , p _ { i } | x _ { i } | \le A _ { i } , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \le B _ { \mu } \qquad ( A _ { \mu } , B _ { \mu } > 0 ) , } \end{array}
$$

in the region $\bar { \tau }$ ， so that the partial derivatives of $X , Y$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are smaller than $h T / [ 2 ( M + N ) ]$ ， $( g - h ) T / [ 2 ( M + N ) ]$ ， where $T = \operatorname* { m i n } { [ p , 1 , 2 q / ( 1 + q ) ] }$ . Consider a motion $\pmb { \sigma }$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in the region

$$
| \xi _ { i } | , p _ { i } | x _ { i } | \le A _ { i } - H , \qquad | u _ { \mu } | , | v _ { \mu } | \le B _ { \mu } - H ,
$$

where $\pmb { H }$ is a positive constant. Denote by $v _ { \mathfrak { d } }$ the $v$ -place given by this motion for $t = t _ { 0 }$

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :If we choose an arbitrary $v \cdot$ -place whose distance from ${ \mathfrak { v } } _ { 0 }$ is equal to or smaller than $\pmb { H }$ ，then there exists one and only one motion,that gives this $v$ -place for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ，remains in $\pmb { J }$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ and tends to this motion $\pmb { \sigma }$ asymptotically for $t \to \infty$ so that the differences of the simultaneous coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ for these two motions,when multiplied by $e ^ { h t }$ , remain smaller in absolute values than a finite bound for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$

We define a neighborhood of $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ belonging to $\pmb { H }$ such that the derivatives of $X _ { i } , Y _ { \mu }$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are smaller in absolute values than

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { q \displaystyle { \Big / \left\{ \left[ \sqrt { 2 M + N } \sqrt { M + N } \cdot 6 \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { p } + \frac { 2 } { 3 q } \right) \right. \right. } } } \\ { { \left. \left. + \frac { 1 + q } { 2 q } \sqrt { N } \right] \left[ \frac { 1 } { 6 } + \sqrt { N } \right] \right\} . } } \end{array}
$$

Choose a region

$$
p _ { i } | x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \leq e , \qquad q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | , | \eta _ { \mu } | \leq \frac { e } { 2 \sqrt { N } }
$$

inside $\pmb { Y }$ where the derivatives of $X _ { i } , Y _ { \mu }$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are smaller in absolute values than

$$
h \frac { T } { 2 ( M + N ) } , \qquad ( q - h ) \frac { T } { 2 ( M - N ) } ,
$$

where $0 < h < q , e > 0$ ，and $T$ satisfy the conditions stated at the beginning of Section 16.8. Bohl (1904) proved the following theorem.

Theorem 2: If a motion $\beta$ remains for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in the region

$$
p _ { i } | x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \leq \bigg ( \frac { 1 } { 3 \sqrt { M } } - \frac { 2 } { 9 \sqrt { M + N } } \bigg ) e ,
$$

$$
| u _ { \mu } | \leq \biggl ( \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { N } } - \frac { 2 } { 9 \sqrt { M + N } } \biggr ) e , \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \leq \biggl ( \frac { e } { 9 \sqrt { M + N } } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } ,
$$

then there exists one and only one motion $\varepsilon$ that remains for all $t$ in the region

$$
p _ { i } | x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \le e , | u _ { \mu } | , | v _ { \mu } | \le \frac { e } { 2 \sqrt { N } } ,
$$

with the property that the motion $\beta$ tends to the motion ε as $t \to \infty$ so that the differences of the simultaneous $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ -coordinates for the motions $\beta$ and $\varepsilon$ multiplied by $e ^ { \hbar t }$ remain between finite bounds in absolute value for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ (Bohl, 1904).

With those properties we enter the discussion on the motions that tend to an equilibrium position.

Choose a constant $D > 0$ such that the region

$$
| \xi _ { i } | , \ p _ { i } | x _ { i } | \leq D , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , \ q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq D
$$

is contained in $\bar { \tau }$ and the derivatives of $X , Y$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are smaller than $q T / [ 4 ( M + N ) ]$ in the region (124)，where $T =$ min $[ p , 1 , 2 q / ( 1 + q ) ]$ ·

Theorem 3: Let $0 < d \leq D$ . Any $v$ -place $( v )$ of the region $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \leq d ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ corresponds to one and only one motion that gives the place $( v )$ at the assigned instant $t _ { 0 }$ as the partial initial condition, remains in (124) for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ， and tends to the place $x = \pmb { \xi } = \pmb { y } = \pmb { \eta }$ $= 0$ for $t \to \infty$ . This motion remains for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in

$$
| \xi _ { i } | , \ p _ { i } | x _ { i } | \leq d , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , \ q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq d .
$$

The coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ multiplied by $e ^ { \alpha t }$ tend to zero for $t \to \infty$ ,if $\pmb { \alpha }$ is a positive constant smaller than $q$

In order to prove this theorem we refer to what has been described in the beginning of Section 16.10 for asymptotic solutions. By putting $h = q / 2$ we see that the former condition $\pmb { J }$ is replaced by (124), (125),and the former solution $\pmb { \sigma }$ by $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$

We can show that if a motion remains in the region $T$ for $t \geq t _ { 1 }$ or $t \leqq t _ { 1 }$ with the energy constant $C = 0$ then the motion tends either for $t \to \infty$ or for $t \to - \infty$ to the place $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ such that the coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ multiplied by exp $\{ [ q - ( \sigma / 2 ) ] [ t - t _ { 1 } ] \}$ remain between finite bounds chosen once for all for the region $_ { T }$ . From this property and from Theorem 1 we see that the motion is the only one that gives the place $( v )$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ，remains in(125） for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，and tends to the place $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ for $t \to \infty$ ，such that the coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ multiplied by exp $\{ q t / 2 \}$ are contained between finite bounds.We obtain, in such cases for suffciently large $t$ and for $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \neq 0$

$$
\operatorname * { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } = \frac 1 4 , \qquad \operatorname * { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { V } _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } = \frac 1 4 ,
$$

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { \hat { \xi } _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { V } _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { x _ { i } } { \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { V } _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { u _ { \mu } } { \sqrt { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \mathcal { V } _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } } = 0 .
$$

Assume that not all of the $q$ quantities are equal to each other. Consider a motion that tends to the place $x = \hat { \xi } = y = \eta = 0$ but is not always at the same place.We take

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \mathcal { M } } \left( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \equiv K \cdot \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } , \qquad K > 0 ,
$$

and divide the $q$ -quantities into two groups. The $q$ quantities corresponding to the $v$ -quantities of the group I are smaller than those corresponding to the group II. Denote by $\omega _ { 1 }$ the sum of the squares of $v$ of the group I formed from the given motion. Let $\omega _ { 2 }$ be the corresponding sum of the group II. Denote by $r _ { 1 }$ the largest $q$ -value in the group I and by $r _ { 2 }$ the smallest $q$ -value in the group II. Since the motion remains in the neighborhood of $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ ,and since

$$
\frac { d v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { d t } = - 2 q _ { \mu } v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + 2 v _ { \mu } Y _ { \mu } ,
$$

it follows that $\omega _ { 1 }$ and $\omega _ { 2 }$ satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d \omega _ { 1 } } { d t } \geq - 2 r _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 } - \gamma ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { } \\ & { \frac { d \omega _ { 2 } } { d t } \leq - 2 r _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } + \gamma ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\gamma$ is a function of $t$ that tends to zero for $t \to \infty$ . Put

$$
\sigma = \frac { \omega _ { 1 } } { \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } } , ~ 1 - \sigma = \frac { \omega _ { 2 } } { \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } } , ~ 0 \le \sigma \le 1 ,
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { d \sigma } { d t } = \frac { \omega _ { 2 } ( d \omega _ { 1 } / d t ) - \omega _ { 1 } ( d \omega _ { 2 } / d t ) } { ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \geq \frac { \omega _ { 2 } [ - 2 r _ { 1 } \omega _ { 1 } - \gamma ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } ) ] + \omega _ { 1 } [ 2 r _ { 2 } \omega _ { 2 } - \gamma ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } ) ] } { ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\frac { d \sigma } { d t } \geq 2 ( r _ { 2 } - r _ { 1 } ) \sigma ( 1 - \sigma ) - \gamma ,
$$

where $r _ { 1 } < r _ { 2 }$ . By choosing $0 < \varepsilon < 1 / 2$ ， otherwise arbitrary，we see that $\gamma < ( r _ { 2 } - r _ { 1 } ) \varepsilon ( 1 - \varepsilon )$ for sufficiently large $t$ Once we have $\varepsilon \leqq$ $\sigma \le 1 - \varepsilon$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ ，then we have at that instant

$$
\frac { d \sigma } { d t } \geq ( r _ { 2 } - r _ { 1 } ) \varepsilon ( 1 - \varepsilon ) .
$$

Here $\pmb { \sigma }$ is equal to $1 - \varepsilon$ after a certain instant and is always larger than $1 - \varepsilon$ later.

There are two possibilities : either $\sigma > 1 - \varepsilon$ or $\sigma < \varepsilon$ for sufficiently large t.It follows that either $\sigma \to 0$ or $\sigma \to 1$ for $t \to \infty$ . Hence for $t \to \infty$ one of the quantities $\omega _ { 1 } / ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } )$ and $\omega _ { 2 } / ( \omega _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } )$ converges to O and the other to 1.

We arrange equal $q \mathrm { . }$ -quantities in one set and thus we have m sets in all. Denote the sums of the squares of $v$ belonging to each group by $\rho _ { 1 } , \rho _ { 2 } , \ldots , \rho _ { m }$ ，in the order of the increasing values.Put $\rho _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ $+ \ \rho _ { m } = \omega$ . Each of $\rho _ { 1 } / \omega$ ， $( \rho _ { 1 } + \rho _ { 2 } ) / \omega , \ldots , ( \rho _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \rho _ { m } ) / \omega$ tends either to O or 1 as $t \to \infty$ .Let the 𝜆th of this sequence be the first that tends to 1. If $\lambda = 1$ ， then $\rho _ { 1 } / \omega \to 1$ ， $( \rho _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \rho _ { m } ) / \omega \to 0$ for $t \to \infty$ ，and $\rho _ { 1 } / \omega \to 0$ ， $\rho _ { 2 } / \omega  0 , \dots , \rho _ { m } / \omega  0$ If $\lambda = m$ ，then $( \rho _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \rho _ { m - 1 } ) / \omega \to 0$ ， $( \rho _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \rho _ { m } ) / \omega \to 1$ ，and $\rho _ { \mathrm { 1 } } / \omega \to 0 _ { \mathrm { : } }$ ， $\rho _ { 2 } / \omega \to 0 , \dots , \rho _ { m - 1 } / \omega \to 0$ ， $\rho _ { m } / \omega \to 1$ .If $1 < \lambda < m$ ，then $( \rho _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot$ $+ \rho _ { z - 1 } ) / \omega \to 0$ ， $( \rho _ { \lambda + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \rho _ { m } ) / \omega \to 0$ and $\rho _ { 1 } / \omega \to 0$ ， $\rho _ { 2 } / \omega \to 0$ ， $\dots , \rho _ { \lambda - 1 } / \omega \to 0$ ， $\rho _ { \lambda } / \omega \to 1$ ， $\rho _ { \lambda + 1 } / \omega \to 0 , \dots , \rho _ { m } / \omega \to 0$ . Thus we have such a $\lambda$ that $\rho _ { \lambda } / \omega \to 1$ ， $\rho _ { \vartheta } / \omega  0$ ， $\vartheta \neq \lambda$ for $t \to \infty$ ，and $\rho _ { \vartheta } / \rho _ { \lambda }  0 \mathrm { . }$ $[ \sum \left( x _ { i } ^ { 2 } + \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) + \sum u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } ] / \rho _ { \lambda } \longrightarrow 0$ Put $\rho _ { \lambda } = v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 }$ ，then $v _ { \alpha } , v _ { \beta } , \ldots , v _ { \tau }$ correspond to one and the same $q$ -value，which is denoted by $k$ . Thus for $t \to \infty$ we obtain

$$
\frac { ( v _ { \alpha } \pm u _ { \alpha } ) ^ { 2 } + ( v _ { \beta } \pm u _ { \beta } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( v _ { \tau } \pm u _ { \tau } ) ^ { 2 } } { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } {  } 1 ,
$$

$$
\frac { \eta _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \eta _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } \textcircled { + } \frac { 1 } { 4 } , \qquad \frac { y _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + y _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + y _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } \textcircled { + } \frac { 1 } { 4 k ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } \quad ^ { + } 4 ^ { \ast } \qquad v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \\ & { \frac { x _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } \to 0 , \qquad \frac { \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } \to 0 , } \\ & { \frac { y _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } \to 0 , \qquad \frac { \eta _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } { v _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + v _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } \to 0 , } \\ & { \frac { \eta _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \eta _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } { y _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot + y _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } } \to k ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ is not equal to any of the $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \gamma$

If the $q$ -values are all different from each other, then there is such an index $\gimel$ that, for $t \to \infty$ ，

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { x _ { i } } { y _ { \lambda } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \xi _ { i } } { y _ { \lambda } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { y _ { \tau } } { y _ { \lambda } } } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \eta _ { \tau } } { y _ { \lambda } } } = 0 \qquad ( \tau \neq \lambda ) ,
$$

where the denominator can be replaced by $\eta _ { \lambda }$ and also where

$$
\operatorname * { l i m } _ { t  \infty } \frac { \eta _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } } { y _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } } = q _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } .
$$

Karstens (1901） discussed asymptotic solutions according to Kneser's work (1895,1897; Section 16.10).

# 16.12MOTIONS THAT REMAIN IN THE NEIGHBORHOOD OF AN EQUILIBRIUM POSITION

Denote the coordinates $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { M } , y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ by $\omega _ { 1 } , \ldots , \omega _ { n }$ in general， and the coordinates $\xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { M } , \eta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \eta _ { N }$ by $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ Choose $S > 0 , P > 0 , Q > 0$ such that the region $\begin{array} { r } { | \omega _ { i } | \leq S \ ( i = 1 , } \end{array}$ $\cdots , n )$ is contained in theregion $\overline { T }$ entirely,and that if $0 \leq D \leqeq S _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ $| \omega _ { i } | < D \left( i = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ , then $\begin{array} { r } { \vert d \lambda _ { i } / d t \vert < R \sum \lambda _ { i } ^ { 2 } + Q D \left( i = 1 , \dots , n \right) } \end{array}$ ， where $D > 0$ ， $D \leq S$ ， $D < 1 / ( 8 n P )$ .Bohl (1904） showed that $\sum \lambda _ { i } ^ { 2 }$ $< 8 n Q D ^ { 2 }$ for a motion that remains in the region Ω, that is, $| \omega _ { i } | < D$ for $t \geq T$ . The proof is carried out by reductio ad absurdum on the basis of the inequality (126).

I.We consider the neighborhood Ω as before.The region $p | x | , | \xi |$ $\leq d , | u | , | v | \leq d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ is in Ω.As before,we introduce $[ u _ { 1 } ] , \ldots ,$ $[ u _ { N } ]$ ，which are single-valued continuous functions of $x , \xi , v$ in the region $\Pi \colon p | x | , | \xi | \leq d / ( 3 \sqrt { M } ) , | v | \leq d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ ，such that $| [ u ] | \leqq$ $d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ . The system $x , \xi , v$ from $\pi$ completed by the corresponding $[ u _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ u _ { N } ]$ ，if taken as the initial place,gives a solution that remains in the region $p | \boldsymbol { x } | , | \boldsymbol { \xi } | \leq d , | \boldsymbol { u } | , | \boldsymbol { v } | \leq d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ . Let $y _ { 1 0 } , \ldots ,$ $y _ { x 0 }$ be_the $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { N }$ -values taken from the region $q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq$ $( 1 - \sqrt { N } \sqrt { l ^ { 2 } + m ^ { 2 } } ) / 2 \cdot d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ , and put

$$
[ u _ { \mu } ] - v _ { \mu } - 2 q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu 0 } = \phi _ { \mu } ( x , \xi , v ) \qquad ( \mu = 1 , \ldots , N ) .
$$

Bohl (1904) proved the following theorems.

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :If $x , \xi$ are chosen fixed under the condition $p | x | , | \xi |$ $\leq d / ( 3 \sqrt { M } )$ ， then $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { N }$ can be determined under the condition $| v | \leq d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ such that all $\phi _ { \mu }$ vanish simultaneously.

Denote the $v$ -place when all $\phi _ { \mu }$ vanish simultaneously by $v _ { 1 0 } , \ldots ,$ ${ \boldsymbol { v } } _ { N 0 }$ ，and the corresponding $[ u _ { 1 } ] , \ldots , [ v _ { N } ]$ by $[ u _ { 1 } ] _ { 0 } , \ldots , [ u _ { N } ] _ { 0 }$ Taking $x , \xi , v _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { i 0 } } , \ldots , v _ { N 0 } , [ u _ { 1 } ] _ { 0 } , \ldots , [ u _ { N } ] _ { 0 }$ as the initial values,we obtain a motion that remains in the region $p | \boldsymbol { x } | , | \boldsymbol { \xi } | \leq d , | \boldsymbol { u } | , | \boldsymbol { v } | \leq d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ with increasing $t _ { i }$ ，when

$$
g _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | = \frac { | u _ { \mu } - v _ { \mu } | } { 2 } \leq \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | = \frac { | u _ { \mu } + v _ { \mu } | } { 2 } \leq \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { N } } .
$$

Theorem 2: If a system $x _ { i 0 } , \xi _ { i 0 }$ is chosen from the region $p | x | , | \xi |$ $\leq d / ( 3 \sqrt { M } )$ and a system $y _ { \mu 0 }$ from the region

$$
q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq \frac { 1 - \sqrt { \overline { { { N } } } } \sqrt { \overline { { { l ^ { 2 } + m ^ { 2 } } } } } } { 2 } \cdot \frac { d } { 2 \sqrt { \overline { { { N } } } } } ,
$$

then we can find a motion that gives $x _ { i } = x _ { i 0 } , \xi _ { i } = \xi _ { i 0 } , y _ { \mu } = y _ { \mu 0 }$ for $t = \tau$ and remains in the region $p | \boldsymbol { x } | , | \boldsymbol { \xi } | \leq d , | \boldsymbol { u } | , | \boldsymbol { v } | \leq d / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ for $t = \tau$

Take $\Delta > 0 , \Delta \le S , \Delta = 1 / ( 8 n P )$ . The region $| x | , | y | < \Delta , \sum \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } +$ $\sum \eta _ { i } ^ { 2 } < 8 n Q \Delta ^ { 2 }$ is contained in $\pmb { H }$

Theorem 3: If $0 < \delta \le \Delta$ ，then for each $x , \xi$ ,y-system of the region $p _ { i } | x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \le g \delta / ( 3 \sqrt { M } ) , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \le g \delta / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ one and only one motion can be found that gives the assigned place for $t = t _ { 0 }$ and remains in the region $| x | , | y | < \Delta$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ . For this motion we have, for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，

$$
x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \overset { triangledown } { \equiv } g \widehat { \varrho } , \qquad | u _ { \mu } | , | v _ { \mu } | \overset { \triangledown } { \equiv } \frac { g } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \widehat { \varrho } , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \overset { \triangledown } { \equiv } \frac { g } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \widehat { \varrho } .
$$

Theorem 4: For each system $x , \xi$ satisfying

$$
p _ { i } | x _ { i } | , | \hat { \xi } _ { i } | \leq \frac { g } { 3 \sqrt { M } } \delta
$$

there is one and only one motion that gives this system $x , \xi$ as the partial initial place for an assigned instant and remains in the region $| x | , | y | < \Delta$ for all $t$ .For this motion we have

$$
| x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \leq g \delta , \qquad | u _ { \mu } | , | v _ { \mu } | \leq \frac { g } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \delta , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq \frac { g } { 2 \sqrt { N } } \delta .
$$

Such a motion has the following properties :

Theorem 5: Let $h < q$ and consider the region $J \colon | \xi _ { i } | , p _ { i } | x _ { i } | \le A _ { i }$ ， $\{ \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \le B _ { \mu }$ . Choose $\pmb { H }$ such that $A _ { i } , B _ { \mu } > H > 0$ ，and $L > 0$ such that

$$
L \leq S , \qquad L < { \frac { 1 } { 8 n P } } , \qquad p _ { i } L \leq { \cal A } _ { i } - { \cal H } ,
$$

$$
\sqrt { 8 n Q } \cdot L \le A _ { i } - H , \qquad ( \sqrt { 8 n Q } + q _ { \mu } ) L \le B _ { \mu } - H .
$$

If $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { i } | < L$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ , then there are an infinite number of motions that remain in $\pmb { J }$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ and tend to this motion asymptotically for $t \to \infty$ ，such that the differences of the simultaneous values of the coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ for these two motions multiplied by $e ^ { \hbar t }$ converge to zero for $t \to \infty$ .

Theorem 6: Let s = max $( p _ { i } , q _ { \mu } )$ ， $g = 1 / [ 9 n ( \sqrt { 8 Q } + s ) ]$ . Let $h$ and $^ e$ be such that

$$
0 < h < q , \qquad q e \equiv S , \qquad g e < \frac { 1 } { 8 n P } ,
$$

with an arbitrary $e > 0$ satisfying the condition in (12O). If a motion $\pmb { B }$ remains in the region $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < g e$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ,then there is one and only one motion $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ that remains in the region $p _ { i } | x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \le e , | u _ { \mu } | , | v _ { \mu } |$ $\leq e / ( 2 \sqrt { N } )$ for all $t$ with the property that the motion $\pmb { B }$ tends to the motion $\pmb { \cal E }$ such that the differences of the simultaneous values of the coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ for $\pmb { B }$ and $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ remain smaller in absolute values than a finite bound for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ . This motion $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ remains always in the region

$$
p _ { i } | x _ { i } | , | \xi _ { i } | \leq e , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq \frac { e } { 2 \sqrt { \cal N } } .
$$

Furthermore, the differences of the coordinates multiplied by $e ^ { h t }$ converge to zero for $t \to \infty$

Theorem 7: Consider the neighborhood $_ T$ of the place $x = \xi =$ $y = \eta = 0$ Choose $\Delta$ so that $\Delta > 0$ ， $\Delta \leq s$ ， $\Delta < 1 / ( 8 n P )$ . All places such as $\begin{array} { r } { | x | , | y | < \Delta , \sum \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + \sum \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } < 8 n Q \Delta ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ are in $_ { T }$ .If $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < \Delta$ for $t \geq \tau$ ，then the energy constant $C$ should be $\geq 0$ If $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < \Delta$ and the energy constant $C = 0$ ，then $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ for all $t$ for such a motion. If $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < \Delta$ and the energy constant $C = 0$ for $t \geq \tau$ ，then the motion tends to $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ for $t \to \infty$ . Take $0 < d < q$ ，then the $( x , \xi , y , \eta )$ -quantities multiplied by $e ^ { h t }$ tend to zero for $t \to \infty$

There are further properties of the motions that remain in the neighborhood of an equilibrium position.

Theorem 8: To any positive number $\omega$ there corresponds a motion from $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ such that

$$
\sum \left( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) = C ( 1 + \eta ) , \qquad | \eta | \leq \omega ,
$$

for any motion that remains in this neighborhood for all $t ,$ ，where $c$ is the energy constant for this motion.

In fact,we have from the energy integral of Section 16.9 a neighborhood in which

$$
| R | \leq k \Biggl [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } \left( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \left( \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \right) \Biggr ]
$$

for any $k > 0$ .There exists a neighborhood $V$ around $x = \xi = y =$ $\eta = 0$ for each $\omega > 0$ with the property that,if a motion remains in $V$ for all $t$ ，then this motion satisfes

$$
\left| \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } \left( \gamma _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + q _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \right) + R \right| \leq \frac { \omega } { 1 + \omega } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } \left( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) .
$$

Hence for this motion we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \sum _ { i } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) - \frac { \omega } { 1 + \omega } \sum _ { i } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) \le C } } \\ & { } & { \displaystyle \le \sum _ { i } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) + \frac { \omega } { 1 + \omega } \sum _ { i } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
C = \frac { 1 + \vartheta \cdot 2 \omega } { 1 + \omega } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } \left( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) \qquad ( 0 \le \vartheta \le 1 ) ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } \ : ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) = C \biggl [ 1 + \omega \frac { 1 - 2 \vartheta } { 1 + 2 \vartheta \omega } \biggr ] .
$$

Since

$$
\left| \frac { 1 - 2 \vartheta } { 1 + 2 \vartheta \omega } \right| \leq 1 ,
$$

we have

$$
\left| \omega \frac { 1 - 2 \vartheta } { 1 + 2 \vartheta \omega } \right| \leq \omega .
$$

Theorem 9: To any number $\omega > 0$ there corresponds a number $H > 0$ with the property that, if $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < H$ for all $t$ for a motion, then we have also

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } \left( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) = C ( 1 + \eta ) , \qquad | \eta | \leq \omega .
$$

Theorem $_ { I 0 }$ :To each number $\kappa > 0$ there corresponds a number $G > 0$ with the property that, if $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < G$ for all $t$ for a motion, then we have always

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } ( u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } ) , \ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } ( \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } ) \leq \kappa \cdot \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } ( \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

If for two motions we have $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < G$ for all $t$ ， then we have always

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } ( u _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } ) , \ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } ( \gamma _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } ) \leq \kappa \cdot \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } ( \xi _ { i 1 } ^ { 2 } + p _ { i } ^ { 2 } x _ { i 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

where $u _ { \mu 1 } , v _ { \mu 1 } , \eta _ { \mu 1 } , y _ { \mu 1 } , \xi _ { i 1 } , x _ { i 1 }$ are the differences of the simultaneous values of the coordinates $u _ { \mu } , v _ { \mu } , \eta _ { \mu } , y _ { \mu } , \xi _ { i } , x _ { i }$ for the two motions.

I1. So much for the motions that remain in the neighborhood of an equilibrium position.Now we consider the solutions that tend to an equilibrium position as $t \to \infty$ ·

Let $m > 1$ and $T = \operatorname* { m i n } \left[ p , 1 , 2 q / ( 1 + q ) \right]$ . Take the region $\pmb { H }$ such that the region

$$
| \xi _ { i } | , p _ { i } | x _ { i } | \leq H , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq H
$$

is contained inside $\overline { T }$ and that the partial derivatives of $X , Y$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are smaller in absolute values than

$$
{ \frac { 9 T } { 4 ( M + N ) n } } .
$$

Theorem 11: Let $0 < \gamma \leq H$ Any $y .$ -place in the region $q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } |$ $\leq ( m - 1 ) \gamma / ( 2 \sqrt { N } m )$ corresponds to one and only one motion that gives this $y .$ -place as the partial initial place for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ，remains in (127) for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ ，and tends to the place $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ for $t $ $\infty$ . This motion remains for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in

$$
| \xi _ { i } | , p _ { i } | x _ { i } | \leq \gamma , \qquad | \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq \gamma .
$$

The coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ multiplied by $e ^ { \alpha t }$ tend to zero for $t \to \infty$ if $_ \alpha$ is a positive constant smaller than $q$

For the proof Bohl (1904) wrote

$$
( u _ { \mu } ) - v _ { \mu } - 2 q _ { \mu } y _ { \mu 0 } = \psi _ { \mu } ( v ) \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , \ldots , N ) ,
$$

where $y _ { 1 0 } , \ldots , y _ { N 0 }$ are the coordinates of the $y .$ -place and $( x ) , ( \xi ) , ( u )$ are those introduced in the discussion (Section 16.1O) of the solution tending to an equilibrium position.Then Bohl proved that there should exist in the region $\textstyle \sum v _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } \leqq r ^ { 2 }$ a $v$ -place for which all $\boldsymbol { \psi } _ { \mu } = 0$

Now we introduce the region $\pmb { H }$ again, obtained by puting $l _ { 1 } = \cdots$ $= l _ { M } = 1$ ， $m _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = m _ { N } = \lambda$ ， $\sigma = \varepsilon$ in Ω.

Theorem 12: If a motion remains in $\pmb { H }$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ and the coordinates $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { M }$ tend to zero for $t \to \infty$ ，then the corresponding $\xi , y , \eta \mathrm { . }$ -coordinates should all tend to zero for $t \to \infty$

Take $\Delta > 0 , \Delta \le S , \Delta < 1 / ( 8 n P )$ again，and choose the region $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < \Delta , \sum \xi _ { i } ^ { 2 } + \textstyle \sum \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } < 8 n Q \Delta$ as the region (127) and $\pmb { H }$ in the above discussion. Let $p \Delta \leq H$ ， $q \Delta \leq H$ ，and $\bar { g } = \operatorname* { m i n } \left( \Delta p , \Delta q \right)$

Theorem 13: Let $0 < h < \bar { g }$ .Each $y$ -place of the region $q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } |$ $\leq ( m - 1 ) h / ( 2 \sqrt { N } \cdot m )$ corresponds to one and only one motion that gives this $\mathfrak { y }$ -place as the partial initial values for $t = t _ { 0 }$ and remains in the region $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { \mu } | < \Delta$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ and whose coordinates $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { M }$ tend to zero for $t \to \infty$ . This motion remains for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ in $\vert \xi _ { i } \vert , p _ { i } \vert x _ { i } \vert$ $\leq h , | \eta _ { \mu } | , q _ { \mu } | y _ { \mu } | \leq h$ and tends to the equilibrium position for $t \to \infty$ such that the coordinates $x , \xi , y , \eta$ multiplied by $e ^ { \alpha t }$ converge to zero for $t \to \infty$ ,if $_ { \alpha }$ is a positive constant smaller than $q$

III. Finally, we consider the cases in which one stable coordinate exists.From our discussion at the end of Section 16.9 we know that there exists a neighborhood $\pmb { A }$ of $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ such that we cannot have $x = \xi = 0$ for any motion that remains in the neighborhood for all $t$ .We consider the motions that remain in $\pmb { A }$ for all $t$ but for which $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ does not hold, that is,not stationary at $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$

Theorem 14: Corresponding to each positive number $\omega$ there exists a neighborhood $\pmb { L }$ of $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ with the property that if a motion lies in $\pmb { L }$ for all $t$ then we never have $x = \xi = 0$ for this motion,because this motion would otherwise be represented by $x = \xi =$ $y = \eta = 0$ for all $t$ 、If the latter is not the case, then

$$
{ \frac { \xi ( d x / d t ) - x ( d \xi / d t ) } { \xi ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } } = 1 - \nu , \qquad | \nu | < \omega .
$$

In fact, from Section 16.9 we have

$$
\frac { \xi ( d x / d t ) - x ( d \xi / d t ) } { \xi ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } = 1 - \frac { x X } { \xi ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

and

$$
| X | \leq \varepsilon _ { 1 } \biggl [ | x | + | \xi | + \sum \left( | y _ { \mu } | + | \eta _ { \mu } | \right) \biggr ] ,
$$

$$
\sum { ( \eta _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + y _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } ) } \leq \varepsilon _ { 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } )
$$

with $\varepsilon _ { 1 } , \varepsilon _ { 2 } > 0$

Take $\omega _ { 0 }$ ， $0 < \omega _ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } < 1$ ，and let the corresponding neighborhood be $L _ { \mathfrak { o } }$ . Choose a neighborhood $\pi$ so that places of $\pi$ are in $L _ { 0 }$ ，that the second-order derivatives of $\pmb R$ with respect to $x , \xi , y , \eta$ are equal to or smaller than $\kappa$ in absolute values for all $t$ in the region $\Pi$ ，and that two motions in $\pi$ for all $\scriptstyle t$ satisfy

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { N } ( u _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { \mu 1 } ^ { 2 } ) \leq h ( \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

Denote the values of $x , \xi$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ by $x _ { 0 } , \xi _ { 0 }$ ; then it can be seen that

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \xi = \sqrt { \xi ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } \cdot \cos \Big [ \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } p ( 1 - \upsilon ) d t + \alpha \Big ] , } \\ { p x = \sqrt { \xi ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sin \Big [ \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } p ( 1 - \upsilon ) d t + \alpha \Big ] , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\frac { p x _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { \xi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } = \sin \alpha , ~ \frac { \xi _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { \xi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } \overline { { { x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } } } } = \cos \alpha .
$$

Since $1 - \nu > 1 - \omega _ { 0 }$ ，the integral in （129） increases with $t$ and there should be a value $\tau$ for which the integral between $t _ { 0 }$ to $t _ { 0 } + \tau$ is equal to $2 \pi$

Theorem 15: Any motion remaining in $\Pi$ for all $t$ that is not represented by $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ is periodic.There is a smallest period for such a motion,which is obtained by choosing $t _ { 0 }$ arbitrarily, but $\tau$ by

$$
\int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 0 } + \tau } p ( 1 - \nu ) d t = 2 \pi .
$$

For such a value of $\pmb { \tau }$ we see that $\sqrt { \xi ^ { 2 } + p ^ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } }$ has the same value at $t = t _ { 0 }$ and $t = t _ { 0 } + \tau$ .By (129)， the values of $\xi$ and $_ x$ have the same values at $t _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 0 } + \tau$ . Similarly it can be shown that the values of $\eta _ { \mu }$ and $y _ { \mu }$ possess the same values at $t _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 0 } + \tau$ . The period $\pmb { \tau }$ is determined by

$$
\int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 0 } + \tau } p ( 1 - \nu ) d t = 2 \pi , \qquad | \nu | < \omega _ { 0 } ,
$$

or

$$
p ( 1 - \sigma ) \tau = 2 \pi , \qquad | \sigma | < \omega _ { 0 } .
$$

Thus $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ differs from $2 \pi / p$ by a small amount.

Theorem 16: A neighborhood of $x = \xi = y = \eta = 0$ can be found with the property that all motions remaining in this neighborhood for all $t$ are periodic.Each of such motions possesses a smallest period,and a neighborhood can be chosen so that all smallest periods of such motions differ from $2 \pi / p$ by arbitrarily small amounts.

Theorem $^ { I 7 }$ :A neighborhood $x = y _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = y _ { N } = 0$ can be found such that all motions remaining in this neighborhood for all $\pmb { t }$ are periodic. Each such motion, except those for which always $x =$ $y _ { \mu } = 0$ ，possesses a smallest period.A neighborhood can be found such that these periods differ from $2 \pi / p$ by arbitrarily small amounts.

# 16.13COTTON'S INTEGRAL EQUATIONS

Cotton (1911） transformed the differential equations

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = - l _ { i } x _ { i } + P _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { M } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { N } , t ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d y _ { j } } { d t } = m _ { j } y _ { j } + Q _ { j } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { M } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { N } , t ) \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , } \end{array}
$$

where $l _ { i }$ and $m _ { j }$ are positive and real constants, to

$$
\begin{array} { l } { x _ { i } ( t ) = a _ { i } \exp \{ - l _ { i } t \} } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \int _ { t _ { 1 } } ^ { t } \exp \{ - l _ { i } ( t - \alpha ) \cdot P _ { i } [ x _ { 1 } ( \alpha ) , \dots , y _ { N } ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha ,  } \\ { \displaystyle y _ { j } ( t ) = b _ { j } \exp \{ m _ { j } t \} } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad +  \int _ { t _ { 2 } } ^ { t } \exp \{ m _ { j } ( t - \alpha ) \} \cdot Q _ { j } [ x _ { 1 } ( \alpha ) , \dots , y _ { N } ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha , } \end{array}
$$

which are integral equations of Volterra's type (Volterra,191O). By substituting in the right-hand members the $( s \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 1 ) { \mathrm { t h } }$ approximation $x _ { 1 } ^ { ( s - 1 ) } ( \alpha ) , \dots , x _ { M } ^ { ( s - 1 ) } ( \alpha )$ ， $y _ { 1 } ^ { ( s - 1 ) } ( \alpha )$ ,·., $y _ { N } ^ { ( s - 1 ) } ( \alpha )$ for $x _ { 1 } ( \alpha )$ ,..., $x _ { M } ( \alpha )$ ， $y _ { 1 } ( \alpha ) , \dots , y _ { N } ( \alpha )$ ，respectively， we obtain the sth approximation $x _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( s ) } ( \alpha ) , \ldots , x _ { \scriptscriptstyle M } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( s ) } ( \alpha ) , y _ { 1 } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( s ) } ( \alpha ) , \ldots , y _ { N } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( s ) } ( \alpha )$ . This is Picard's method (Picard, 1908) of successive approximation.

Cotton considered the equations in matrix form

$$
\frac { d X } { d t } = - l X + \varepsilon ( X + Y ) + l a , \frac { d Y } { d t } = m Y - \varepsilon ( X + Y ) + m
$$

where $l , m , \varepsilon , a , b$ are real positive constants. The solution satisfies

$$
l ( X - a ) = m ( Y - b ) = \varepsilon ( X + Y )
$$

and is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X = a + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { l } \displaystyle \frac { \varepsilon } { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { Y = b + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { m } \displaystyle \frac { \varepsilon } { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } , } } \end{array}
$$

and, if

$$
\varepsilon \left( { \frac { 1 } { l } } + { \frac { 1 } { m } } \right) < 1
$$

is developable in integral power series of $\varepsilon$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X = a + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { l } \varepsilon + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { l } \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \Big ( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { l } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m } \Big ) + \cdots } } \\ { { \phantom { X = a + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { l } \varepsilon ^ { 4 } \Big ( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { l } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m } \Big ) ^ { i - 1 } + \cdots } } } \\ { { \phantom { X = a + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { m } \varepsilon + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { m } \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \Big ( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { l } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m } \Big ) \ + \ \cdots } } } \\ { { \phantom { X = a + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { l } \varepsilon ^ { 4 } \Big ( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { l } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m } \Big ) ^ { i - 1 } + \ \cdots } } } \end{array}
$$

The corresponding integral equations are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X = a + \varepsilon \int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } \exp { \{ l ( \alpha - t ) \} \cdot ( X + Y ) } d \alpha , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { Y = b + \varepsilon \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp { \{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \} \cdot ( X + Y ) } d \alpha . } } \end{array}
$$

Similarly, we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \overline { { X } } _ { i } = a + \varepsilon \displaystyle \int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot ( \overline { { X } } _ { i - 1 } + \overline { { Y } } _ { i - 1 } ) d \alpha , } \\ { \overline { { Y } } _ { i } = b + \varepsilon \displaystyle \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot ( \overline { { X } } _ { i - 1 } + \overline { { Y } } _ { i - 1 } ) d \alpha . } \end{array}
$$

The solution of

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { X ( t ) = \bar { a } ( t ) + \varepsilon \displaystyle \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot \left[ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha , } & \\ & { \quad } & { 0 < \bar { a } ( t ) < a , \smallskip } \\ & { Y ( t ) = \bar { b } ( t ) + \varepsilon \displaystyle \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot \left[ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha , \smallskip } \\ & { } & { 0 < \bar { b } ( t ) < b , \smallskip } \end{array}
$$

is successively

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle X _ { 1 } ( t ) = \bar { a } ( t ) , \qquad Y _ { 1 } ( t ) = \bar { b } ( t ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle X _ { i } ( t ) = \bar { a } ( t ) + \varepsilon \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp \{ l ( \alpha - t ) \} \cdot [ X _ { i - 1 } ( \alpha ) + Y _ { i - 1 } ( \alpha ) ] d \alpha , } } \\ { { \displaystyle Y _ { i } ( t ) = \bar { b } ( t ) + \varepsilon \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \} \cdot [ X _ { i - 1 } ( \alpha ) + Y _ { i - 1 } ( \alpha ) ] d \alpha , } } \end{array}
$$

which tend uniformly to $X ( t ) , Y ( t )$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { 0 < X ( t ) < a + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { l } \displaystyle \frac { \varepsilon } { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } , } \\ { 0 < Y ( t ) < b + \displaystyle \frac { a + b } { m } \displaystyle \frac { \varepsilon } { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } . } \end{array}
$$

The solutions are convergent and it can be shown that these are the only solutions and that they are bounded for all values of $t$ if $\varepsilon$ satisfies (131).

Consider more generally the system

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = - l x + P ( x , y , t ) , \frac { d y } { d t } = m y + Q ( x , y , t ) .
$$

If $P = Q = 0$ ，then the system admits a single-parameter family of asymptotic solutions asymptotic to zero for $t \to \infty$ . To prove this, we make the following hypothesis :

(i) The functions $P , Q$ for $t > t _ { 0 } , | x | < A , | y | < A$ are definite and continuous,and admit partial derivatives with respect to $x , y$ that are continuous and bounded inferiorly in absolute value by $1 / [ ( 1 / l )$ $+ \left( 1 / m \right) ]$ for $x = y = 0$ .Thus we can determine $\sigma < A$ such that for $| x | \leq \sigma , | y | \leq \sigma$ these derivatives are bounded in absolute value by $\varepsilon < 1 / [ ( 1 / l ) + ( 1 / m ) ]$

(i） $P ( 0 , 0 , t )$ and $Q ( 0 , 0 , t )$ are zero.The integral equations corresponding to the solution of (132) asymptotic to zero for $t \to + \infty$ are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x ( t ) = A ^ { * } \exp \left\{ - l ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \right\} } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot P [ x ( \alpha ) , y ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha , } } \\ { { \displaystyle y ( t ) = \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot Q [ x ( \alpha ) , y ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha , } } \end{array}
$$

where we suppose that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle | A ^ { * } | \left[ 1 + \frac \varepsilon l \frac 1 { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } \right] < \sigma , } \\ { \displaystyle | A ^ { * } | \left[ \frac \varepsilon m \frac { 1 } { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } \right] < \sigma . } \end{array}
$$

Consider for comparison

$$
X ( t ) = \lvert A ^ { * } \rvert \exp \left\{ - l ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \right\}
$$

$$
+ \varepsilon \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot \left[ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha ,
$$

$$
Y ( t ) = \varepsilon \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot \left[ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha .
$$

These are of the type (132), as we see by puting

$$
\bar { a } ( t ) = | A ^ { * } | \exp \{ - l ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} , \bar { b } ( t ) = 0 ,
$$

so that the first approximations for (134) with (134a) are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { x _ { 1 } ( t ) = A ^ { * } \exp { \{ - l ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } , \qquad y _ { 1 } ( t ) = 0 , } \\ & { } & { X _ { 1 } ( t ) = | A ^ { * } | \exp { \{ - l ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \} } , \qquad Y _ { 1 } ( t ) = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and the successive approximations are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x _ { i + 1 } ( t ) = A ^ { * } \exp \left\{ - l ( t - t _ { 0 } ) \right\} } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + ~ \varepsilon \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot P [ x _ { i } ( \alpha ) , y _ { i } ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha , } } \\ { { \displaystyle y _ { i + 1 } ( t ) = \varepsilon \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot Q [ x _ { i } ( \alpha ) , y _ { i } ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha . } } \end{array}
$$

For $t > t _ { 0 }$ ，we have

$$
