\begin{array} { r l } & { | P ( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , t ) | = | P ( x , y , t ) - P ( 0 , 0 , t ) | < \varepsilon [ | x _ { 1 } | + | y _ { 1 } | ] = \varepsilon ( X _ { 1 } + \mathrm { ~ } ^ { } } \\ & { | Q ( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , t ) | < \varepsilon ( X _ { 1 } + { ~ Y _ { 1 } } ) . } \end{array}
$$

We see that $| x _ { 2 } | , | y _ { 2 } |$ are smaller than $X , Y$ solutions of (135)，and accordingly smaller than the left-hand member of (134a)，and hence than $\pmb { \sigma }$ . Thus the existence of $x _ { 3 } , y _ { 3 }$ follows，and accordingly $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ,$ $\ldots , x _ { i } , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { i }$ satisfy

$$
| x _ { i + 1 } - x _ { i } | < X _ { i + 1 } - X _ { i } , | y _ { i + 1 } - y _ { i } | < Y _ { i + 1 } - Y _ { i } .
$$

Hence $| x _ { i } | , | y _ { i } |$ are smaller than $\pmb { \sigma }$ Thus $P ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } , t )$ ， $Q ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } , t )$ are continuous and bounded,and $x _ { i + 1 } , y _ { i + 1 } , \dotsc$ exist.Consequently the successive approximations $x _ { i } ( t ) , y _ { i } ( t )$ tend uniformly to the limits $x ( t )$ ， $y ( t )$ that satisfy (135） and the differential equations (133).These solutions are asymptotic to zero for $t \to \infty$ . The solutions $x , y$ depend on the parameter $A ^ { * }$ ，which is the value of $x$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$

The hypothesis (ii) that $P , Q$ vanish for $x = y = 0$ for all $\pmb { t }$ is not necessary for establishing the convergence of the successive approximations，but the bound is not necessarily asymptotic to zero.If $| P ( 0 , 0 , t ) | , | Q ( 0 , 0 , t ) | < M$ such that

$$
\frac { M } { l } \frac { 1 } { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } < \sigma , ~ \frac { M } { m } \frac { 1 } { 1 - \varepsilon ( 1 / l + 1 / m ) } < \sigma ,
$$

then (132） admits one and only.one single-parameter family of solutions which are bounded for $t \to \infty$ ，so that $x ( t _ { 0 } ) = A ^ { * }$ and $| x |$ $| y | < \sigma$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ . It can be seen that for $l > 0$ ， $m = 0$ and under the hypotheses (i) and (i) the system (132) admits a family of solutions asymptotic to zero for $t \to \infty$

The same reasoning can be applied to the case in which more than two equations are present.

If the roots of the characteristic equation are distinct but not necessarily real,we can transform the given equations to the form (130),but they will not necessarily be real and bounded. Even if there is an imaginary pair, they can be transformed to aform similar to (130).

If the characteristic equation admits multiple roots, then it is impossible to reduce it to a similar form. Suppose that there are three equations with double real roots and a simple positive root. Then the system is reduced to

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = - l x + P ( x , y , z , t ) , \frac { d y } { d t } = x - l y + Q ( x , y , z , t ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { d z } { d t } = m z + R ( x , y , z , t ) .
$$

We consider

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x ( t ) = A ^ { * } e ^ { - \imath t } + \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp { \{ l ( \alpha - t ) \} } \cdot P [ x ( \alpha ) , y ( \alpha ) , z ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha , } } \\ { { \displaystyle y ( t ) = A ^ { * } t e ^ { - \imath t } + B ^ { * } e ^ { - \imath t } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phantom { \frac { t } { t _ { 0 } } } + \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp { \{ l ( \alpha - t ) \} } \cdot Q [ x ( \alpha ) , y ( \alpha ) , z ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phantom { \frac { t } { t _ { 0 } } } + \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } \exp { \{ l ( \alpha - t ) \} } \cdot ( \imath - \alpha ) P [ x ( \alpha ) , y ( \alpha ) , z ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha , } } \\ { { \displaystyle c \infty } } \end{array}
$$

$$
z ( t ) = C ^ { * } e ^ { m t } - \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot R [ x ( \alpha ) , y ( \alpha ) , z ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha .
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { X ( t ) = a + \varepsilon \int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot \left[ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) + Z ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { Y ( t ) = b + \varepsilon \int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot \left[ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) + Z ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { + \varepsilon \int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ l ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot ( t - \alpha ) [ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) + Z ( \alpha ) ] d \alpha } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { { \stackrel { c \infty } { \sim } } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
Z ( t ) = c + \varepsilon \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \exp \left\{ - m ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot \left[ X ( \alpha ) + Y ( \alpha ) + Z ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha ,
$$

where $a , b , c , \varepsilon$ are positive constants. This last system admits a particular solution satisfying

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d X } { d t } = - l ( X - a ) + \varepsilon ( X + Y + Z ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d Y } { d t } = - l ( Y - b ) + ( X - a ) + \varepsilon ( X + Y + Z ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d Z } { d t } = m ( Z - c ) + \varepsilon ( X + Y + Z ) , } \end{array}
$$

that is,

$$
\varepsilon ( X + Y + Z ) = l ( X - a ) = m ( Z - c ) = { \frac { l ^ { 2 } ( Y - b ) } { 1 + l } } .
$$

The variables $x , y , z$ can be expanded in integral power series in $\varepsilon$ and, since the series have positive terms, the successive approximation is convergent, and we see that under a suitable hypothesis regarding $P , Q , R$ there exists a solution of the system (136) that is either bounded for $t \to + \infty$ or asymptotic to zero，according to the hypothesis we make.

Cotton considered further the hypothesis (ii): if we assume $t _ { 0 } =$ $- \infty$ then the system admits a solution that is bounded for all values of $t$

Asymptotic solutions have been studied by Poincaré (1886,1892; Sections 19.7,19.8). Picard(1908) generalized the result under weaker conditions.Liapounov's result (1907) is less stringent. The hypotheses (i) and (ii) are more general than Poincaré's and Picard's,but we have supposed  constant, in contrast to Liapounov，who regarded  as continuous and bounded functions of $\pmb { t }$ for $t > t _ { 0 }$ and expanded it in powers of $\alpha _ { i }$ exp $\{ ( \rho _ { i } + \varepsilon ) t \}$ . where $- \rho _ { i }$ are the characteristic exponents with negative real parts and $\alpha _ { i }$ are small in absolute value. This solution has been proved to be convergent and gives solutions asymptotic to zero.Bohl (191O) was the first to assume (ii) and (iii). His work depends on certain functions that keep a constant sign. Bohl assumed that none of the characteristic exponents has zero real parts (Section 16.8). Cotton (1911) generalized Liapounov's result.

Consider a system

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d z _ { i } } { d t } - \lambda _ { i 1 } z _ { 1 } - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \lambda _ { i n } z _ { n } = L _ { i } ( z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { n } , t ) } \\ { ( i = 1 , 2 , \dots , n ) , } \end{array}
$$

and let

$$
z = z _ { 1 h } ( t ) , \ldots , z _ { n } = z _ { n h } ( t ) \qquad ( h = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

be $\pmb { n }$ linearly independent solutions of （137） in which $L _ { i } = 0$ $( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ ，that is, the determinant

$$
\Delta ( t ) = { \left| \begin{array} { l l l l } { z _ { 1 1 } } & { z _ { 2 1 } } & { \cdots } & { z _ { n 1 } } \\ { z _ { 1 2 } } & { z _ { 2 2 } } & & { z _ { n 2 } } \\ { \cdots } & & & { } \\ { z _ { 1 n } } & { z _ { 2 n } } & { \cdots } & { z _ { n n } } \end{array} \right| } \neq 0 .
$$

Let $\boldsymbol { A } _ { i j }$ be the minor corresponding to $z _ { i j } ( t )$ of this determinant ; then the expression

$$
z _ { s } ( t ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { j } z _ { s j } ( t ) + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \int _ { t _ { j } } ^ { t } z _ { s j } ( t ) \frac { A _ { i j } ( \alpha ) } { \Delta ( \alpha ) } f _ { i } ( \alpha ) d \alpha
$$

$$
( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $C _ { j }$ are arbitrary constants and $t _ { j }$ are the integration limits, represents the general solution of

$$
\frac { d z _ { i } } { d t } - \lambda _ { i 1 } z _ { 1 } - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \lambda _ { i n } z _ { n } = f _ { i } ( t ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Suppose that (138) is a normal system (Section 16.2) and let $l _ { 1 } , \ldots , l _ { n }$ be the characteristic numbers of the system. Suppose further that the homogeneous system corresponding to （137） with $\textstyle L _ { i } = 0$ is regular (Section 16.2). Then we can show that the characteristic number of $A _ { i j } ( t ) / \Delta ( t )$ is not smaller than $- l _ { j }$ ，and that of $z _ { i j } ( t )$ is not smaller than $l _ { j }$ . To an arbitrarily small positive number $r$ we can make a number $k$ correspond such that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left| z _ { s j } ( t ) \frac { A _ { i j } ( \alpha ) } { \Delta ( \alpha ) } \right| < K \exp \left\{ ( - l _ { j } + r ) t \right\} \cdot \exp \left\{ ( l _ { j } + r ) \alpha \right\} } \\ & { \qquad = K \exp \left\{ ( l _ { j } + r ) ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot e ^ { 2 r t } } \\ & { \qquad = K \exp \left\{ ( l _ { j } - r ) ( \alpha - t ) \right\} \cdot e ^ { 2 r \alpha } , } \end{array}
$$

for any indices $s , i , j$ and $_ { \alpha }$ and for $t _ { 0 } < t < \infty$

Suppose that the linear and homogeneous equations for (137) form a regular system.Cotton (1911） proved that the solutions of (137) satisfy the integral equation

$$
z _ { s } ( t ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { j } z _ { s j } ( t )
$$

$$
+ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \int _ { t _ { j } } ^ { t } z _ { s j } ( t ) { \frac { A _ { i j } ( \alpha ) } { \Delta ( \alpha ) } } L _ { i } [ z _ { 1 } ( \alpha ) , . . . , z _ { n } ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha
$$

$$
( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

and applied the result to the study of solutions asymptotic to zero for $t \to + \infty$ that satisfy the condition that the coefficients $\lambda$ of (137) are for $t > t _ { 0 }$ real,continuous,and bounded functions of a real variable $t$ ，If the characteristic number $l _ { j }$ is positive,it is evident that the solution $z _ { 1 j } ( t ) , \ldots , z _ { n j } ( t )$ of the homogeneous system corresponding to (137) is composed of functions asymptotic to zero for $t \to + \infty$ Suppose that the constants $C _ { j }$ in （141） corresponding to the solutions of the homogeneous system with characteristic number $l _ { j }$ negative or zero are all zero.The number $r$ of the inequalities in (14O) is chosen to be smallr than one-third of the positive characteristic numbers $l _ { j }$ Thus to nonzero numbers $l _ { j }$ there correspond various $l _ { j } - 3 r$ with the same sign,and to zero numbers $l _ { j }$ there correspond various negative $l _ { j } - 3 r$ .If $l _ { j }$ is negative or zero,then we take $t _ { j } = t _ { 0 }$ ，and if $l _ { j }$ is positive then we take $t _ { j } = + \infty$ ; and we denote by $t _ { j } ^ { \prime }$ such a value of $t _ { j }$ .Further suppose that the $L _ { j } ( z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { n } , t )$ satisfy the conditions that (i) for $t > t _ { 0 } , | z _ { 1 } | < Z , \ldots , | z _ { n } | < Z$ the functions are definite and continuous and admit first-order partial derivatives and bounded second-order derivatives with respect to $z _ { i }$ ，and (ii) the $\scriptstyle { L _ { i } }$ and their first-order derivatives with respect to $z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n }$ are zero for $z _ { 1 } = \cdots$ $= z _ { n } = 0$ .Then we can choose a number $^ { h }$ for an arbitrary positive number $\varepsilon$ for $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ such that

$$
| z _ { i } | < h e ^ { - 2 r t } , \qquad h e ^ { - 2 r t } < Z \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )
$$

implies that

$$
\left| \frac { \partial L _ { i } ( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { n } , t ) } { \partial z _ { j } } \right| < \frac { \varepsilon } { n } e ^ { - 2 r t } ( i , j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

and thus

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { | L _ { i } ( z _ { 1 } , \dotsc , z _ { n } , t ) - L _ { i } ( z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \dotsc , z _ { n } ^ { \prime } , t ) | } \\ & { \qquad < \displaystyle \frac { \varepsilon } { n } e ^ { - 2 r t } \cdot ( | z _ { 1 } - z _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | + \dots + | z _ { n } - z _ { n } ^ { \prime } | ) . } \end{array}
$$

For $z _ { 1 j } , \dotsc , z _ { n j }$ with positive characteristic numbers,whose numbers are larger than $_ { 2 r }$ ，we have for $t _ { 0 }$ sufficiently large

$$
\left| \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \bar { C } _ { j } ^ { \prime } \bar { z } _ { s j } \right| < b _ { s } e ^ { - 2 \tau t } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $| C _ { j } ^ { \prime } |$ are chosen sufficiently small.

For studying the integral equation

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle z _ { s } ( t ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { j } ^ { \prime } z _ { s j } ( t ) } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \int _ { t _ { j } ^ { \prime } } ^ { t } z _ { s j } ( t ) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { A _ { i j } ( \alpha ) } { \Delta ( \alpha ) } L _ { i } [ z _ { 1 } ( \alpha ) , \dots , z _ { n } ( \alpha ) , \alpha ] d \alpha } \end{array}
$$

we consider the linear integral equation

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle Z _ { s } ( t ) = b _ { s } e ^ { - 2 \tau t } + \varepsilon K \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \pm 1 \right) \int _ { t _ { / \prime } ^ { \prime } } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ ( l _ { j } - r ) ( \alpha - t ) \right\} } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \times \left[ Z _ { 1 } ( \alpha ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot + Z _ { n } ( \alpha ) \right] d \alpha } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where we take $t _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } = + \infty$ or $^ { - 1 }$ according as the double sign is plus or minus when $l _ { j }$ is negative or zero, and $t _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } = - \infty \ \mathrm { o r } + 1$ according as the double sign is plus or minus when $l _ { j }$ is positive.This is analogous to(131） and admits solutions

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { Z _ { 1 } ( t ) = \zeta _ { 1 } e ^ { - 2 r t } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad Z _ { n } ( t ) = \zeta _ { n } e ^ { - 2 r t } , \nonumber } } \\ { { \mid \zeta _ { 1 } \mid , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \mid \zeta _ { n } \mid > 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

By substituting in (143) and dividing by $e ^ { - 2 \tau t }$ ， we obtain

$$
\zeta _ { s } - b _ { s } = \varepsilon K \cdot \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { 1 } { | l _ { j } - 3 r | } ( \zeta _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \zeta _ { n } ) ,
$$

or

$$
\zeta _ { s } = b _ { s } + ( b _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + b _ { n } ) \frac { \varepsilon K \cdot \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } 1 / | l _ { j } - 2 r | } { 1 - n \varepsilon K \cdot \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } 1 / | l _ { j } - 3 r | } .
$$

If $\varepsilon$ satisfies

$$
\varepsilon K \cdot \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { 1 } { | l _ { j } - 3 r | } < \frac { q } { n } < \frac { 1 } { n } ,
$$

then (143） admits the solution (144)，where $\zeta$ is developable as an integral power series of $\varepsilon$ If $q < n / ( n + 1 )$ and $b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n } <$ $h / ( n + 1 )$ ， then $| Z _ { s } | < h e ^ { - 2 r t }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ and the first approximation is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { z _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) = b _ { 1 } e ^ { - 2 r t } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad z _ { n } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( t ) = b _ { n } e ^ { - 2 r t } . } \end{array}
$$

By applying the method of successive approximation to (142） and taking as the first approximation

$$
z _ { s } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle ( 1 ) } ( t ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { j } ^ { \prime } z _ { s j } ( t ) ,
$$

we can see that the series is uniformly convergent. We choose $\varepsilon , q _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ and $h$ to satisfy the foregoing inequalities ；then,by comparing (142) with (143),we can show that the series obtained by such successive approximation are uniformly convergent to the limit $z _ { s } ( t )$ such that $| z _ { s } ( t ) | < h e ^ { - 2 r t }$ . These series tend asymptotically to zero for $t \to \infty$ ， and depend on the constants $C ^ { \prime }$ whose number is equal to the number of times $\imath$ in which the positive characteristic numbers occur.

Wiman (1936),Seifert (1958) and Atkinson (1958） studied the stability problem and asymptotic behavior of the solution. In particular,the stability of differential equations of pendulum type has been discussed by Seifert (1955,1959) and Lillo and Seifert (1956).

# PERRON'S STABILITY CRITERION

# 16.14PERRON'S STABILITY CRITERION

Perron (1928,1929,1930,1930a,1930b； see also Lefschetz, 1949; Bellman, 1953) discussed stability under less stringent conditions than those of Poincaré,Liapounov, and Cotton.

Consider the system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = a _ { s 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { s n } x _ { n } + \phi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , x _ { n } , t ) ,
$$

$$
\phi _ { s } ( 0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 , t ) = 0 \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where $t$ is real and $\geq 0$ ，while $x _ { s } , a _ { s _ { \mu } } , \phi _ { s }$ may be complex. The system admits the integral if the $\phi _ { s }$ are continuous for suffciently small $\pmb { t }$ It admits the trivial solution $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$

If we can assign a positive number $^ { a }$ such that to any $\varepsilon$ between 0 and $^ { a }$ a number $\varepsilon _ { 1 }$ ， $0 < \varepsilon _ { 1 } < \varepsilon$ ，can be found such that any solution for which $\begin{array} { r } { 0 < \sum _ { s } | x _ { s } ( 0 ) | < \varepsilon _ { 1 } } \end{array}$ exists for all values of $t \geq 0$ and satisfies $\sum | x _ { s } ( t ) | < \varepsilon$ ,then the trivial solution $x _ { 1 } = \cdots = x _ { n } = 0$ is said to be stable.Otherwise it is unstable.If there is a positive number ε such that any solution, for which $0 < \sum | x _ { s } ( 0 ) | < \varepsilon _ { \cdot }$ ，ceases to exist once necessarily for a finite value of $t$ ，or at least once satisfies $\textstyle \sum | x _ { s } ( t ) |$ $\ = \varepsilon$ ，then the trivial solution is said to be completely unstable. Between stability and complete instability there occurs conditional stability.If there exists a positive number $^ { a }$ such that to any number $\varepsilon$ between O and $^ { a }$ a number $\varepsilon _ { 1 }$ $( 0 < \varepsilon _ { 1 } < \varepsilon )$ can be found such that a certain number of, but never all, solutions with $0 < \sum | x _ { s } ( 0 ) | < \varepsilon _ { 1 }$ continue to exist for $t \geq 0$ and satisfy $\sum | x _ { s } ( t ) | < \varepsilon$ ，then the trivial solution is called conditionally stable.

If $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t )$ is definite and continuous for $| x _ { 1 } | \leq a , \ldots , | x _ { n } |$ $\leq a , \ t \geq 0 ( a > 0 )$ ,and if $| \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) | \leq K ( | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | )$ ， where $\kappa$ is a positive constant, then the function $\phi$ is said to satisfy the condition (A)，where $^ { a }$ is the first constant and $\kappa$ the second constant.

If $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } , t ) / ( | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | ) \to 0$ for $| x _ { 1 } | + \cdots + | x _ { n } | $ 0, $t \to \infty$ , that is, if to any positive number we can assign $\delta _ { c }$ and $T _ { \varepsilon }$ such that $| \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } , t ) | \leq \varepsilon ( | x _ { 1 } | + \dots + | x _ { n } | )$ for $| x _ { 1 } | \leqq \delta _ { \varepsilon } , \dots ,$ $| x _ { n } | \leqq \delta _ { \varepsilon }$ ， $| x _ { 1 } | + \cdots + | x _ { n } | > 0$ ， $t \geq T _ { \varepsilon }$ ,then the function $\phi$ is said to satisfy the condition (B).The condition (B) is satisfied if we have uniformly

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { x _ { 1 } \to 0 , \dots , x _ { n } \to 0 } { \frac { \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } , t ) } { | x _ { 1 } | + \dots + | x _ { n } | } } = 0 ,
$$

or if we have uniformly

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } , t ) } { | x _ { 1 } | + \dots + | x _ { n } | } } = 0
$$

for $| x _ { 1 } | \leq a , \ldots , | x _ { n } | \leq a , | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | > 0 .$

If for a given positive number $\varepsilon$ we can assign two positive constants $\delta _ { \varepsilon }$ and $T _ { \epsilon }$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { | \phi ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { \prime } , t ) - \phi ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { \prime \prime } , t ) | } \\ & { \qquad \leq \varepsilon ( | x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } | + \cdots + | x _ { n } ^ { \prime } - x _ { n } ^ { \prime \prime } | ) } \end{array}
$$

for $| x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | \leq \delta _ { \varepsilon }$ $\smash { \dot { \xi } _ { \varepsilon } , | x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } | \le \delta _ { \varepsilon } , \ldots , | x _ { n } ^ { \prime } | \le \delta _ { \varepsilon } , | x _ { n } ^ { \prime \prime } | \le \delta _ { \varepsilon } , t \ge T _ { \varepsilon } } .$ then the function $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t )$ is said to satisfy the condition $\left( \mathbf { C } \right)$ . If a function satisfies the conditions (A） and $\left( \mathbf { C } \right)$ , then it satisfies the condition (B).

If two functions

$$
\begin{array} { r } { a _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + a _ { n } x _ { n } + \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) , } \\ { b _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + b _ { n } x _ { n } + \psi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) } \end{array}
$$

with constants $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n } , b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n }$ ，are identical,and if $\phi$ and $\psi$ satisfy the condition (B),then we have necessarily $a _ { 1 } = b _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ $= b _ { n }$ and $\phi = \psi$

If the functions $\phi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t )$ in the system (145) satisfy the condition (B), then the constants $a _ { s \mu }$ are single-valued and the roots of

$\| a _ { i j } - \varepsilon _ { i j } \rho \| = 0$ are uniquely determined,which we have called the characteristic roots.

We can easily prove the following preparatory theorems.

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :If the functions $x _ { 1 } ( t ) , \ldots , x _ { n } ( t )$ defined in the interval $t _ { 0 } \leqq t \leq t _ { 1 }$ satisfy

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \phi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $\phi _ { s }$ satisfy the condition (A), then for any two values of $t$ in the same interval we have

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } ( t _ { 1 } ) | ^ { 2 } \leq \exp \left\{ 2 n K | t _ { 1 } - t _ { 0 } | \right\} \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } ( t _ { 0 } ) | ^ { 2 } .
$$

Theorem 2: If such functions $x _ { s } ( t )$ do not vanish simultaneously for any value of $t$ ， then they cannot vanish simultaneously for any value of $t$

Theorem 3: If $\phi _ { s }$ in the system (146) satisfy the condition (A), then to any pair $T , \varepsilon$ we can assign a positive number $\delta = \delta ( T , \varepsilon )$ such that the solution with an initial condition such as $| x _ { 1 } ( 0 ) | \le \delta , \ldots ,$ $| x _ { n } ( 0 ) | \leq \delta$ ， can be prolonged at least up to $t = T$ and can continue to satisfy $| x _ { 1 } ( t ) | \leq \varepsilon , \ldots , | x _ { n } ( t ) | \leq \varepsilon$ up to $t = T$

Perron's criterion for stability is summarized in Theorems 4 and 5.

Theorem 4: In the system of differential equations,or the diferential system in short,

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \rho _ { s } x _ { s } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } c _ { s i } x _ { i } + \psi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) \qquad \\ { \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) \qquad } \quad { }
$$

the constants $\rho _ { s }$ have negative real parts, and the three numbers $\gamma , C , \alpha$ are defined by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { - \gamma = \operatorname* { m a x } { \Re ( \rho _ { s } ) } , \qquad C = \operatorname* { m a x } | c _ { s i } | , } \\ { \alpha = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l l } { \operatorname* { m i n } \Big ( 1 , \frac { \gamma } { 2 ( n - 1 ) C } \Big ) } & { \mathrm { ~ i f } } & { ( n - 1 ) C > 0 , } \\ { 1 } & { \mathrm { ~ i f } } & { ( n - 1 ) C = 0 . } \end{array} \right. } \end{array}
$$

If $\psi _ { s }$ satisfies the condition $( \mathbf { A } )$ and the inequalities

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \vert \psi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , t ) \vert \equiv \frac { \gamma } { 2 n } \left( \vert x _ { 1 } \vert + \alpha \vert x _ { 2 } \vert + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha ^ { n - 1 } \vert x _ { n } \vert \right) } } \\ { \displaystyle { ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) , } } \end{array}
$$

then the trivial solution $x _ { s } = 0$ is stable and any solution with the initial condition $x _ { s } ( 0 )$ sufficiently small in absolute value tends to zero, $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } x _ { 1 } = 0 , \dots , \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } x _ { n } = 0 . } \end{array}$

In fact, by multiplying the differential system (147) by $\bar { x } _ { s }$ and taking the real part,we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Re ( \rho _ { s } ) | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } \Re ( c _ { s i } \bar { x } _ { s } x _ { i } ) + \Re ( \bar { x } _ { s } \psi _ { s } ) } } \\ & { } & { \leq - \gamma | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } | x _ { s } x _ { i } | + \frac { \gamma } { 2 n } | x _ { s } | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { i - 1 } | x _ { i } | . } \end{array}
$$

By summing over $s$ after multiplying by $\alpha ^ { 2 s }$ ， we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \equiv - \gamma \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + C \sum _ { s , i ( s > i ) } ^ { 1 \cdots n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } x _ { i } | } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle { \frac { \gamma } { 2 n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | \cdot \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { i - 1 } | x _ { i } | } , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \sum _ { s , i ( s > t ) } ^ { 1 \cdots n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } \left| x _ { s } x _ { i } \right| \leq \alpha \displaystyle \sum _ { s , i ( s > t ) } ^ { 1 \cdots n } \alpha ^ { s + i } \big | x _ { s } x _ { i } \big | } \\ & { \qquad \leq \displaystyle \frac { \alpha } { 2 } \displaystyle \sum _ { s , i ( s > t ) } ^ { 1 \cdots n } [ ( \alpha ^ { s } | x _ { s } | ) ^ { 2 } + ( \alpha ^ { i } | x _ { i } | ) ^ { 2 } ] } \\ & { \qquad = \displaystyle \frac { ( n - 1 ) \alpha } { 2 } \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | \cdot \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \dot { \alpha } ^ { i - 1 } | x _ { i } | \le \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { s + 1 } | x _ { s } x _ { i } | } \\ & { \qquad \le \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } [ ( \alpha ^ { s } | x _ { s } | ) ^ { 2 } + ( \alpha ^ { i } | x _ { i } | ) ^ { 2 } ] } \\ & { \qquad = n \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \frac d { d t } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \leq \left[ - \gamma + C \frac { ( n - 1 ) \alpha } 2 + \frac 7 2 \right] \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } } \\ { \leq - \frac \gamma 4 \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } } \end{array}
$$

from the definition of $_ { \alpha }$ . Hence

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( e ^ { \tau t / 2 } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \right) \equiv 0 ,
$$

and we see that the function in the bracket decreases monotonically ; accordingly,

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \leq e ^ { - \tau t / 2 } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } ( 0 ) | ^ { 2 } \leq e ^ { - \tau t / 2 } \varepsilon ^ { 2 } .
$$

Theorem 5: If the functions $\phi _ { s }$ in the differential system (145) satisfy the conditions (A) and (B) and if all the characteristic roots have negative real parts,then the trivial solution $x _ { s } = 0$ is stable, and any solution with the initial conditions $x _ { s } ( 0 )$ ，sufficiently small in absolute value,satisfies

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } x _ { 1 } = 0 , \dots , \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } x _ { n } = 0 .
$$

In fact, the matrix

$$
A = { \binom { a _ { 1 1 } } { \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \qquad { \overset { } { a _ { 1 n } } }  \\ { a _ { n 1 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } \quad a _ { n n } { \biggr ) }
$$

can be transformed by a nonzero matrix $\pmb { B }$ to

$$
B ^ { - 1 } A B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l l l } { \rho _ { 1 } } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { c _ { 2 1 } } & { \rho _ { 2 } } & { } & { } & { 0 } \\ { } & { } & { \cdot } & { } & { } \\ { } & { } & { } & { } & { \cdot } \\ { c _ { n 1 } } & { c _ { n 2 } } & { \cdot \cdot \cdot } & { c _ { n , n - 1 } } & { \rho _ { n } } \end{array} \right] ,
$$

where $c _ { i j }$ may be zero if the characteristic roots of $\pmb { A }$ are all distinct, otherwise some are not zero.By a linear transformation $\boldsymbol { x } = B \boldsymbol { y }$ the differential system is reduced to

$$
\frac { d y _ { s } } { d t } = \rho _ { s } y _ { s } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } c _ { s i } y _ { \langle } + \psi _ { s } ( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } , i ) \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n
$$

where the $\psi _ { s }$ satisfy the conditions (A） and (B).From Theorem 4 and (148) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { | \psi _ { s } ( y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { n } , t ) | \leq \displaystyle \frac { \gamma \alpha ^ { n - 1 } } { 2 n } \left( | y _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | y _ { n } | \right) } & { } \\ { \qquad } & { } \\ { \leq \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 2 n } \left( | y _ { 1 } | + \alpha | y _ { 2 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha ^ { n - 1 } | y _ { n } | \right) , } \end{array}
$$

if $t$ is sufficiently large and $| y _ { s } |$ are sufficiently small,i.e., if $t \geq T$ ， $| y _ { 1 } | \leq \delta , \ldots , | y _ { n } | \leq \delta$

On the ther hand,Perron's criterion for instabilityis given by

Theorem 6: In the differential system (147) let the coefficients $\rho _ { s }$ be such that $\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) \geq \gamma > 0$ for $s \geq k$ and $\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) \leq 2$ for $s > k , k \ne n$ Define $C$ and $_ { \alpha }$ by (148). If $\psi _ { s }$ satisfies the condition (A） and

$$
| \psi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) | \equiv \frac { \gamma } { 4 n } \left( | x _ { 1 } | + \alpha | x _ { 2 } | + \cdots + \alpha ^ { n - 1 } | x _ { n } | \right) ,
$$

then the trivial solution $x _ { s } = 0$ is unstable. If $k = n$ ，then the system is also unstable.

For the proof, we form from the differential system the inequalities

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \le \Re ( \rho _ { s } ) | x _ { s } ^ { 2 } | + C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } | x _ { s } x _ { i } | + | x _ { s } \psi _ { s } | } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \geq \Re ( \rho _ { s } ) | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } - C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } | x _ { s } x _ { i } | - | x _ { s } \psi _ { s } | , } \end{array}
$$

and define

$$
\Phi ( t ) = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } , \qquad \Psi ( t ) = \sum _ { s = k + 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } .
$$

Then we get by referring to (149)

$$
\Phi ^ { \prime } ( t ) - \Psi ^ { \prime } ( t ) \geq 2 \gamma \Phi ( t ) - \Big [ ( n - 1 ) C \alpha + \frac { \gamma } { 2 } \Big ] [ \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) ] .
$$

From the definition of $_ { \alpha }$ we have

$$
\Phi ^ { \prime } ( t ) - \Psi ^ { \prime } ( t ) \equiv 2 \gamma \Phi ( t ) - \gamma [ \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) ] = \gamma [ \Phi ( t ) - \Psi ( t ) ] .
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left\{ e ^ { - \tau t } \cdot \left[ \Phi ( t ) - \Psi ( t ) \right] \right\} \geq 0 .
$$

Since the function in the bracket increases monotonically, we obtain

$$
\Phi ( t ) - \Psi ( t ) \geq [ \Phi ( 0 ) - \Psi ( 0 ) ] e ^ { \tau t } ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) \geq [ \Phi ( 0 ) - \Psi ( 0 ) ] e ^ { \tau t } .
$$

Theorem 7: If the functions $\phi _ { s }$ in the differential system (145) satisfy the conditions (A) and (B) and if at least one of the characteristic roots has a positive real part, then the trivial solution $x _ { s } = 0$ is unstable. If all roots have positive real parts, then the instability is complete.

Perron has given the following three theorems for the criterion of conditional stability.

Theorem 8: In the differential system (147) let the characteristic roots be such that $\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) \geq \gamma$ for $s \leq k < n$ and $\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) \leqq - \gamma$ for $s > k$ ， where $\gamma > 0$ and $C , \alpha$ are defined by (148). If the functions $\psi _ { s }$ satisfy the condition (A） and the inequalities

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { | \psi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { \prime } , t ) - \psi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { \prime \prime } , t ) | } \\ & { \qquad \quad \le \displaystyle \frac { \vartheta \gamma } { 2 n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { i - 1 } | x _ { i } ^ { \prime } - x _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } | , } \end{array}
$$

where $0 < \vartheta < 1$ ，then the trivial solution $x _ { s } = 0$ is conditionally stable. Let $\varepsilon$ be an arbitrarily suffciently small positive number and $c _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ be arbitrary constants for which $\vert c _ { s } \vert \equiv ( ( 1 - \vartheta ) / 2 ) \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s }$ $( s = k + 1 , \ldots , n )$ ； then there exists just one solution $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ that continues to exist for $t \geq 0$ and satisfies $x _ { k + 1 } ( 0 ) = c _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ( 0 ) =$ $\mathfrak { c } _ { n } , | x _ { s } ( t ) | \le \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } \left( s = 1 , \ldots , n \right)$ .

In fact,we obtain, from (147) and from the last condition in the theorem,

$$
\begin{array} { c l } { x _ { s } = \displaystyle \exp \left\{ \rho _ { s } t \right\} \cdot \Biggl \{ - \rho _ { s } t \} } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { x _ { s } = \displaystyle \exp \left\{ \rho _ { s } t \right\} } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle } &  \qquad \cdot \left\{ c _ { s } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \exp \left\{ - \rho _ { s } t \right\} \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } c _ { s i } x _ { i } + \gamma _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) \right] d t \right\} \quad ~ ( s \equiv k ) , } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle } \end{array} \end{array}
$$

We solve these equations successively， starting with $x _ { s 0 } = 0$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } } _ { 1 } = \exp \{ \rho _ { s } t \} \cdot \displaystyle \sum _ { 0 } ^ { t } \exp \{ - \rho _ { s } t \} } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \cdot [ \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } c _ { s i } x _ { i j } +  \psi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 j } , \ldots , x _ { n j } , t ) ] d t \quad \quad ( s \leq k ) } , } \\ { { \displaystyle _ { 1 } = \exp \{ \rho _ { s } t \} } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \cdot \{ c _ { s } + \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \exp \{ - \rho _ { s } t \} \cdot [ \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } c _ { s i } x _ { i j } +  \psi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 j } , \ldots , x _ { n j } , t ) ] d t \} } } \end{array}
$$

We see that $| x _ { i j } | \leq \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - i }$ , because for $s \leq k$ we have $\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) = \gamma _ { s } \geq \gamma$ and

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { | x _ { s , j + 1 } | \le \exp \{ \gamma _ { s } t \} \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \exp \{ - \gamma _ { s } t \} \cdot [ C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - i } + \frac { \vartheta _ { \gamma } } { 2 n } \frac { n \varepsilon } { \alpha } ] d t } } \\ & { } & { \le ( \frac { \gamma \varepsilon } { 2 } \alpha ^ { - s } +  \frac { \vartheta _ { \gamma \varepsilon } } { 2 } \alpha ^ { - s } ) \frac { 1 } { \gamma } = \frac { 1 + \vartheta } { 2 } \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } < \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } , \qquad } \end{array}
$$

and for $s > k$ we have $\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) = - \gamma _ { s } \leq - \gamma$ and

$$
| x _ { s , j + 1 } | \leq | c _ { s } | + \left[ C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - i } + \frac { \vartheta \gamma } { 2 n } \frac { n \varepsilon } { \alpha } \right] \frac { 1 } { \gamma _ { s } }
$$

$$
\leq \frac { 1 - \vartheta } { 2 } \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } + \frac { 1 + \vartheta } { 2 } \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } = \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } .
$$

Also

$$
| x _ { s , j } - x _ { s , j - 1 } | \leq \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } \Big ( \frac { 1 + \vartheta } { 2 } \Big ) ^ { j - 1 } ,
$$

because, by(151) for $s \leq k$ ，

$$
| x _ { s , j + 1 } - x _ { s , j } | \leq \varepsilon \Big ( \frac { 1 + \vartheta } { 2 } \Big ) ^ { j - 1 } \Bigg [ C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } \alpha ^ { - i } + \frac { \vartheta \gamma } { 2 \alpha } \Bigg ] \frac { 1 } { \gamma _ { s } } ,
$$

and, for $s > k$ ，

$$
| x _ { s , j + 1 } - x _ { s , j } | \leq \varepsilon \Big ( \frac { 1 + \vartheta } { 2 } \Big ) ^ { j - 1 } \Bigg [ C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } \alpha ^ { - i } + \frac { \vartheta \gamma } { 2 \alpha } \Bigg ] \frac { 1 } { \gamma _ { s } } ,
$$

or, for all $\pmb { s }$ ，since $\alpha \leq 1 , \gamma _ { s } \geq \gamma$ ，we have

$$
| x _ { s , j + 1 } - x _ { s , j } | \leq \varepsilon \Big ( \frac { 1 + \vartheta } { 2 } \Big ) ^ { j - 1 } \Big ( \frac { \gamma } { 2 } e ^ { - s } + \frac { \vartheta \gamma } { 2 } \alpha ^ { - s } \Big ) \frac { 1 } { \gamma } = \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s } \Big ( \frac { 1 + \vartheta } { 2 } \Big ) \cdot
$$

From (153) we see that we have uniformly for $t \geq 0$

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { j \to \infty } x _ { s , j } = x _ { s } = x _ { s } ( t ) .
$$

Hence the $x _ { s } ( t )$ exist as continuous functions of $t .$ Since $| x _ { s , j } | \leq \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s }$ ， we see that $| x _ { s } | \le \varepsilon \alpha ^ { - s }$ and that the $x _ { s }$ satisfy (152).

Similarly we can show that the solution is unique.

Theorem 9: The functions $\phi _ { s }$ in the differential system (145) satisfy the conditions (A) and $\left( \mathbf { C } \right)$ ，accordingly the condition (B),and if the $k$ of the characteristic roots have positive real parts and the $n - k$ have negative real parts,where $k \geq 1$ ， $n - k \geq 1$ ，then the trivial solution $x _ { s } = 0$ is conditionally stable,and,if $\eta$ is a sufficiently small positive number, the solutions that remain in $\lvert x _ { s } \rvert \le \eta$ form a family with just $n - k$ arbitrary constants.

Theorem $l o$ :The functions $\phi _ { s }$ in the differential system (145) satisfy the conditions (A） and $\left( \mathbf { C } \right)$ ，accordingly the condition (B),and if just $n - k$ of the characteristic roots have negative real parts where $k \geq 1$ ， $n - k \geq 1$ ，then the trivial solution $x _ { s } = 0$ is at least conditionally stable,and,if $\eta$ is an arbitrary suficiently small number,the solutions that remain in $\lvert x _ { s } \rvert \le \eta$ form a family with at least $n - k$ arbitrary constants.

Perron (1929） further discussed the asymptotic behavior of the solutions.

Theorem 11: If the functions $\phi _ { s }$ of the differential system (145) satisfy the conditions (A) and (B) and if the real parts of the characteristic roots $\rho _ { 1 } , \ldots , \rho _ { n }$ are not all positive, then there exists for each solution $\pmb { x } _ { s }$ for which $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } x _ { 1 } = 0 , \dots , \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } x _ { n } = 0 , } \end{array}$ an index $\iota$ such that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \log \left( | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | \right) } { t } } = \Re ( \rho _ { l } ) \leq 0 .
$$

Further,if the functions $\phi _ { s }$ satisfy the condition (C)，then conversely there exists a solution of this nature corresponding to an index $\iota$ for which $\Re ( \rho _ { l } ) < 0$ ； and such solutions for which

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \log \left( | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | \right) } { t } } \leq - r < 0
$$

form a family of just $h$ arbitrary constants,where $h$ is the number of characteristic roots for which $\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) \leqq - r$

Let $\Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) \geq \Re ( \rho _ { 2 } ) \geq \cdot \cdot \cdot \geq \Re ( \rho _ { n } )$ . By the linear transformation $\boldsymbol { x } = B \boldsymbol { y }$ in the proof of Theorem 5,the differential equations are transformed to (146a),where the functions $\psi _ { s }$ satisfy the conditions (A) and (B),and then the estimates are derived in the form

$$
g ( | y _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | y _ { n } | ) \leq | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot | x _ { n } | \leq G ( | y _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | y _ { n } | )
$$

with two constants $g$ and $G$ . We show that there exists an index $\boldsymbol { l }$ such that

$$
\varlimsup _ { t  \infty } \frac { \log ( | y _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | y _ { n } | ) } { t } = \Re ( \rho _ { l } ) \leq 0
$$

among the solutions satisfying $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } y _ { s } = 0 ( s = 1 , \dots , n ) } \end{array}$

From the differential equations,we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle \left( \Xi \Re ( \rho _ { s } ) | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } | y _ { s } y _ { i } | + | y _ { s } | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { i } | y _ { i } | , \right. } \\ { \displaystyle \left. \frac 1 2 \frac d { d t } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \right\} \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty } | C _ { s i } | , \qquad } \\ { \displaystyle \equiv \Re ( \rho _ { s } ) | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } - C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s - 1 } | y _ { s } y _ { i } | - | y _ { s } | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { i } | y _ { i } | , } \end{array}
$$

with $0 < \alpha < 1$ and,for a sufficiently large $t \geq T _ { \alpha }$ by the condition (B),or by summing over $s$ after multiplying by $\alpha ^ { 2 s }$ and referring to (150),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \leq \Big [ \Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) + C \frac { ( n - 1 ) \alpha } { 2 } + n \alpha \Big ] \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \geq \Big [ \Re ( \rho _ { n } ) - \frac { C ( n - 1 ) \alpha } { 2 } - n \alpha \Big ] \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Consequently we obtain

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left\{ \exp \left\{ - [ 2 \Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) + C ( n - 1 ) \alpha + 2 n \alpha ] t \right\} \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \right\} \equiv 0
$$

for $t \geq T _ { \alpha } .$

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left\{ \exp \left\{ - [ 2 \Re ( \rho _ { n } ) - C ( n - 1 ) \alpha - 2 n \alpha ] t \right\} \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \right\} \equiv 0
$$

for $t \geq T _ { \alpha }$

Hence for $t \geq T _ { \alpha }$ the function within the large braces of the first inequality decreases monotonically and we have

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \leq K _ { \alpha } \exp \{ [ 2 \Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) + C ( n - 1 ) \alpha + 2 n \alpha ] t \} .
$$

Accordingly,

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log \left( \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \right) \leq 2 \Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) + C ( n - 1 ) \alpha + 2 n \alpha .
$$

Similarly,

$$
\varlimsup _ { t  \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log ( \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } ) \equiv 2 \Re ( \rho _ { n } ) - C ( n - 1 ) \alpha - 2 n \alpha .
$$

Thus the estimates are

$$
( | y _ { 1 } | + \cdots + | y _ { n } | ) ^ { 2 } { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \leq n \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \leq n \alpha ^ { - 2 n } \cdot \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } , } \\ { \displaystyle \geq \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \geq \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } . } \end{array} \right. }
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \small _ { 3 } = \underset { t  \infty } { \overline { { \operatorname* { l i m } } } } \frac { \log ( | y _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | y _ { n } | ) } { t } \ \{ \begin{array} { l } { \leq \Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ( n - 1 ) \alpha + n \alpha , } \\ { \geq \Re ( \rho _ { n } ) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ( n - 1 ) \alpha - n \alpha , } \end{array}  } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) \geq \beta \geq \Re ( \rho _ { n } ) .
$$

Suppose that there were no index $\iota$ such that $\Re ( \rho _ { l } ) = \beta$ ； then we should have

$$
\Re ( \rho _ { k } ) > \beta > \Re ( \rho _ { k + 1 } ) .
$$

Put

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Phi ( t ) , \qquad \sum _ { s = k + 1 } ^ { n } \alpha ^ { 2 s } | y _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Psi ( t ) ;
$$

then, by (155),

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { \log \left[ \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) \right] } { t } = 2 \beta ,
$$

$$
\operatorname * { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \exp \left\{ - 2 ( \beta + \alpha ) t \right\} \cdot \left[ \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) \right] = 0 ,
$$

and

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \exp \left\{ - 2 ( \beta + \alpha ) t \right\} \cdot \left[ \Phi ( t ) - \Psi ( t ) \right] = 0 .
$$

From the second inequality of (154) we should obtain, by summing over $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ from 1 to $\pmb { n }$ ，

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \Phi ^ { \prime } ( t ) \geq \Re ( \rho _ { k } ) \Phi ( t ) - \Big [ C \frac { ( n - 1 ) \alpha } { 2 } + n \alpha \Big ] [ \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) ] .
$$

Similarly, from the first inequality of (154) we should obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \Psi ^ { \prime } ( t ) \equiv \Re ( \rho _ { k + 1 } ) \Psi ( t ) + \Big [ C \frac { ( n - 1 ) \alpha } { 2 } + n \alpha \Big ] [ \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) ] .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ \Phi ^ { \prime } ( t ) - \Psi ^ { \prime } ( t ) ] \geq [ \Re ( \rho _ { k } ) - C ( n - 1 ) \alpha - 2 n \alpha ] \Phi ( t ) } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } - [ \Re ( \rho _ { k + 1 } ) + C ( n - 1 ) \alpha + 2 n \alpha ] \Psi ( t ) . } \end{array}
$$

Choose $\alpha$ so small that

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \Re ( \rho _ { k } ) - C ( n - 1 ) \alpha - 2 n \alpha > \beta + \alpha , } \\ { \Re ( \rho _ { k + 1 } ) + C ( n - 1 ) \alpha + 2 n \alpha < \beta - \alpha ; } \end{array}
$$

then we should have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \{ \exp \left\{ - 2 ( \beta + \alpha ) t \} \cdot \left[ \Phi ( t ) - \Psi ( t ) \right] \right\} \\geq 0 \qquad \mathrm { f o r } \qquad t \geq T _ { \alpha } .
$$

Because of (157) the expresson within the braces should tend to zero, so that the expression should be negative, i.e., $\Phi ( t ) \leq \Psi ( t )$ for $t \geq T _ { \alpha }$ But from(158) and by our construction of $_ { \alpha }$ we should have

$$
\frac 1 2 \Psi ^ { \prime } ( t ) \leq \Re ( \rho _ { k + 1 } ) \Psi ( t ) + \Big [ C \frac { ( n - 1 ) \alpha } { 2 } + n \alpha \Big ] 2 \Psi ( t ) \leq ( \beta - \alpha ) \Psi ( t ) .
$$

or

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left[ \exp \left\{ - 2 ( \beta - \alpha ) t \right\} \cdot \Psi ( t ) \right] \leq 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { \log \Psi ( t ) } { t } \leq 2 ( \beta - \alpha ) .
$$

Since $\Phi ( t ) \leq \Psi ( t )$ for $t \geq T _ { \alpha } .$ ， we should have

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \log \left[ \Phi ( t ) + \Psi ( t ) \right] } { t } } \leq 2 ( \beta - \alpha ) .
$$


<!-- chunk 0009: pages 561-630 -->
This is contradictory to (156). Therefore $\beta$ is equal to $\Re ( \rho _ { l } )$ with a certain index l. The first part of Theorem 11 is thus established.

Theorem 12: If the functions $\phi _ { s }$ of the differential system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \rho _ { s } x _ { s } + \phi _ { s } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

satisfy the conditions (A） and (B),and if $\Re ( \rho _ { 1 } ) > \Re ( \rho _ { 2 } ) > \cdots >$ $\Re ( \rho _ { n } )$ ，then for each solution $x _ { s }$ for which

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } x _ { 1 } = 0 , \dots , \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } x _ { n } = 0
$$

there is an index $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \underset { t  \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } ( \frac { x _ { s } } { x _ { l } } ) = 0 \qquad \mathrm { f o r } \qquad s \neq l , } \\ & { \underset { t  \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } ( \frac { 1 } { x _ { l } } \frac { d x _ { l } } { d t } ) = \rho _ { l } , \qquad \Re ( \rho _ { l } ) \leq 0 . } \end{array}
$$

If the functions $\phi _ { s }$ satisfy also the condition (C),then conversely there exists a solution of this nature corresponding to an index $\imath$ for which $\Re ( \rho _ { l } ) < 0$ ，and such solutions for $l \geq j$ form a family with just $n -$ $j + 1$ arbitrary constants.

Choose a fixed number $c > 0$ such that

$$
\Re ( \rho _ { s } ) - c > \Re ( \rho _ { s + 1 } ) + c \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n - 1 ) .
$$

Since the $\phi _ { s }$ satisfy the condition (B),we have

$$
| \phi _ { s } | \leq { \frac { c \varepsilon } { 2 n } } \left( | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | \right) ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } \phi _ { s } | \leq \frac { c \varepsilon } { 2 n } ( | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | ) ^ { 2 } \leq \frac { c \varepsilon } { 2 } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } .
$$

Assume $l > 1$ and put

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { l - 1 } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Phi ( t ) , \qquad \sum _ { s = l } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Psi ( t ) ,
$$

then from the given differential equations we obtain after computation

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } [ \Phi ( t ) - \varepsilon \Psi ( t ) ] \geq [ \Re ( \rho _ { \rho - 1 } ) - c ] [ \Phi ( t ) - \varepsilon \Psi ( t ) ] .
$$

Hence

$$
\exp \left\{ - 2 [ \Re ( \rho _ { l - 1 } ) - c ] t \right\} \cdot \left[ \Phi ( t ) - \Psi ( t ) \right]
$$

increases monotonically for $t \geq T _ { \varepsilon }$ ， but this cannot be positive for $t \geq T _ { \varepsilon }$ ， as can be seen by reasoning similar to the proof of Theorem 11. Thus we have $\Phi ( t ) \leq \varepsilon \Psi ( t )$ for $t \geq T _ { \varepsilon }$ . Since $\Phi ( t )$ and $\Psi ( t )$ cannot vanish simultaneously by Theorem 2,we have

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { \Phi ( t ) } { \Psi ( t ) } } = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { l - 1 } | ^ { 2 } } { | x _ { l } | ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | ^ { 2 } } = 0 \qquad \mathrm { i f } \qquad l > 1 .
$$

Assume $l < n$ and put

$$
\sum _ { s = l + 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Omega ( t ) ,
$$

then from the differential equations and (159) we obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } [ \varepsilon | x _ { l } | ^ { 2 } - \Omega ( t ) ] \geq [ \Re ( \rho _ { l } ) - c ] [ \varepsilon | x _ { l } | ^ { 2 } - \Omega ( t ) ]
$$

fort≥T≥T..

Hence

$$
\mathrm { e x p } \left\{ - 2 [ \Re ( \rho _ { l } ) - c ] t \right\} \cdot [ \varepsilon | x _ { l } | ^ { 2 } - \Omega ( t ) ]
$$

increases monotonically for $t \geq T _ { \varepsilon } ^ { \prime }$ . We have

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { | x _ { l + 1 } | ^ { 2 } + \mathbf { \cdots } + | x _ { n } | ^ { 2 } } { | x _ { l } | ^ { 2 } } } = 0 \qquad { \mathrm { i f } } \qquad l < n .
$$

Thus for any value of $\imath$ we have (Perron,1913)

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } { \frac { x _ { s } } { x _ { l } } } = 0 \qquad ( s \neq l ) .
$$

By the condition (B) we obtain,from the Ith differential equation,

$$
\displaystyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { x _ { t } } \frac { d x _ { t } } { d t } = \rho _ { t } + \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \left( \frac { \phi _ { l } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t ) } { | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | } \frac { | x _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | x _ { n } | } { x _ { l } } \right) = \rho ,
$$

Perron (1929) then applied the theory to

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } [ a _ { s i } + \phi _ { i s } ( t ) ] x _ { i } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

16.15PERRON'S ORDER NUMBER OF DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

Consider the diferential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { s i } ( t ) x _ { i } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $f _ { s i } ( t )$ are bounded, $| f _ { s i } ( t ) | \le c$ ．Multiplying（160） by the conjugate complex $\bar { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { s }$ , we.obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \Re \Big ( \bar { x } _ { s } \frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } \Big ) \leq | x _ { s } | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c | x _ { i } | \leq c \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + | x _ { i } | ^ { 2 } } { 2 } ,
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { d } { d t } } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \equiv - | x _ { s } | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c | x _ { i } | \equiv - c \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + | x _ { i } | ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } .
$$

Put

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \phi ( t ) ;
$$

then

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } { \phi } ^ { \prime } ( t ) \left\{ \mathop { = } ^ { \le n c { \phi } ( t ) } , \right.
$$

Similarly,

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left[ \phi ( t ) e ^ { - 2 \pi c t } \right] \leq 0 , \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \phi ( t ) e ^ { 2 \pi c t } \right] \geq 0 .
$$

Thus we get

$$
\phi ( t ) e ^ { - 2 \pi c t } \leq \phi ( 0 ) , ~ \phi ( t ) e ^ { 2 \pi c t } \geq \phi ( 0 ) ,
$$

or

$$
- 2 n c \equiv \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log \phi ( t ) \equiv \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log \phi ( t ) \equiv 2 n c ,
$$

or

$$
2 \gamma \equiv \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log \phi ( t ) \begin{array} { l } { \{ \equiv 2 n c ,  } \\ {  \geq - 2 n c . } \end{array} 
$$

It can be seen that there are at most $\pmb { n }$ distinct possible values of $\gamma$ . Suppose that there were $n + . 1$ distinct values of $\gamma \colon \gamma _ { 1 } < \gamma _ { 2 } < \cdots$ $< \gamma _ { n + 1 }$ . Let $\boldsymbol { x } _ { s } = \boldsymbol { x } _ { s , \lambda }$ be a solution with $\gamma = \gamma _ { 2 }$ ; then

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log \operatorname* { m a x } _ { s = 1 } ^ { \infty } | x _ { s , \cdot } | = \gamma _ { \imath } ~ ( \lambda = 1 , \ldots , n + 1 ) .
$$

There should be a linear homogeneous relation among $n + 1$ solutions $x _ { s , 1 } , \ldots , x _ { s , n + 1 }$ ，such as

$$
C _ { 1 } x _ { s , 1 } + C _ { 2 } x _ { s , 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + C _ { n + 1 } x _ { s , n + 1 } = 0 \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Let $C _ { p + 1 }$ be the last nonzero coefficient, $p + 1 > 1$ . Then $C _ { p + 1 } = - 1$ and

$$
x _ { s , p + 1 } = \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { p } C _ { \lambda } x _ { s , \lambda } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

From these relations we should have with a suffciently small positive $\varepsilon$

$$
\operatorname* { m a x } _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s , \lambda } | < \exp \left\{ ( \gamma _ { \lambda } + c ) t \right\} \leq \exp \left\{ ( \gamma _ { p } + \varepsilon ) t \right\}
$$

for all sufficiently large t. Then we should have

$$
\operatorname* { m a x } _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s , p + 1 } | \leq \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { p } | C _ { \lambda } | \exp \left\{ ( \gamma _ { p } + \varepsilon ) t \right\}
$$

for all sufficiently large t. Hence

$$
\gamma _ { p + 1 } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log \operatorname* { m a x } _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s , p + 1 } | \leq \gamma _ { p } + \varepsilon ,
$$

or $\gamma _ { p + 1 } \leqq \gamma _ { p }$ ， which is contrary to our construction.

A number $\gamma _ { \lambda }$ such as

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { 1 } { t } } \log \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | = \gamma _ { \lambda }
$$

is called the order number (Ordnungszahl) of the solution $x _ { s }$ (Perron, 1930).

The order number of a linear combination of a finite number of solutions, whose order numbers are $\leq \gamma _ { \lambda }$ ,is also $\leq r _ { 2 }$

Let $e _ { 1 }$ be the number of linearly independent solutions whose order numbers are the smallest possible, i.e., $\gamma _ { 1 }$ ，among the linear independent solutions and their linear combinations.Let $e _ { 1 } + e _ { 2 }$ be the number of linearly independent solutions whose order numbers are equal to either $\gamma _ { 1 }$ or $\gamma _ { 2 }$ , and so forth. Thus $e _ { \lambda }$ is the multiplicity of $\gamma _ { \lambda }$ ， and $e _ { 1 } + \cdots + e _ { n } = n$

Theorem 1: The number of the order numbers of linear differential equations (16O) is just equal to $_ n$ if the multiplicity is properly counted.

Theorem 2: Let $\gamma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \gamma _ { n }$ be the order numbers of the differential equations (16O)； then we have the estimate

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } \tau _ { s } \geq \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } \Re ( f _ { \lambda \lambda } ( \tau ) ) \ d \tau .
$$

In fact, we have for an arbitrary positive number $\varepsilon$

$$
\left| x _ { s , { \lambda } } \right| < \exp \left\{ \left( \gamma _ { \lambda } + \frac { \varepsilon } { n } \right) t \right\} .
$$

The determinant is

$$
X = \| x _ { s , \ i } \| < n ! \exp \bigg \{ \bigg ( \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } r _ { \lambda } + \varepsilon \bigg ) t \bigg \} .
$$

Hence

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { 1 } { t } } \log | X | \leq \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } \gamma _ { \lambda } .
$$

On the other hand,since

$$
X = a \exp \left\{ \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { \lambda \lambda } ( \tau ) d \tau \right\} \qquad ( a = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log | X | = \varlimsup _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } \Re ( f _ { \lambda \lambda } ( \tau ) ) \ d \tau .
$$

Thus the theorem is established.

Consider an adjoint system (Section 3.8) to the system (160):

$$
\frac { d y _ { s } } { d t } = - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { i s } ( t ) y _ { i } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Theorem 3: Let the order numbers of the system (160) be $\left( \Gamma \right)$ ： $\gamma _ { 1 } \leq \gamma _ { 2 } \leq \cdot \cdot \cdot \leqq r _ { n }$ ，and those of (160a) be $( \Delta ) \colon \delta _ { 1 } \leq \delta _ { 2 } \leq \cdot \cdot \cdot \leq \delta _ { n } ,$ then we have always $\gamma _ { s } + \delta _ { s } \geq 0$ $( s = 1 , \ldots , n )$

In fact, we have

$$
\frac { d X } { d t } = F X , \qquad F = \| f _ { s i } ( t ) \| , \qquad X = \| x _ { s , i } \| ,
$$

and

$$
\frac { d Y } { d t } = - Y F , \qquad Y = | | y _ { s i } | | ;
$$

accordingly

$$
Y { \frac { d X } { d t } } + { \frac { d Y } { d t } } X = 0 .
$$

The integral is

$$
Y X = C , \quad \quad C = \| c _ { i j } \| = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { \lambda s } x _ { s \mu } = c _ { \lambda \mu } \qquad ( \lambda , \mu = 1 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Since by the definition of order numbers we have

$$
| x _ { s , \lambda } | \leq \exp { \{ ( \gamma _ { \lambda } + \varepsilon ) t \} } , \qquad | y _ { \lambda , s } | < \exp { \{ ( \delta _ { \lambda } + \varepsilon ) t \} } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
| c _ { \lambda \lambda } | \equiv \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | y _ { \lambda , s } x _ { s , \lambda } | \equiv n \exp { \{ ( \gamma _ { \lambda } + \delta _ { \lambda } + 2 \varepsilon ) t \} } .
$$

Hence $\gamma _ { \lambda } + \delta _ { \lambda } \geq 0$ if $c _ { \scriptscriptstyle { \lambda \lambda } } \neq 0$ ·

Let

$$
X A = X ^ { * } , \qquad A = { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l l l } { 1 } & { 0 } & { } & { } & { 0 } \\ { a _ { 2 1 } } & { 1 } & { } & { } & { 0 } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { a _ { n 1 } } & { a _ { n 2 } } & { \cdots } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] } ,
$$

where the $a _ { s \lambda }$ are arbitrary constants for $s > \lambda$ . Then the elements $\boldsymbol { x } _ { s 2 } ^ { * }$ of $X ^ { * }$ form the fundamental system of solutions of (160).We have

$$
B Y = Y ^ { * } , \qquad B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l l } { 1 } & { 0 } & { \cdots } & { 0 } \\ { b _ { \scriptscriptstyle 2 1 } } & { 1 } & { } & { 0 } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } \\ { b _ { \scriptscriptstyle n 1 } } & { b _ { \scriptscriptstyle n 2 } } & { \cdots } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] ,
$$

where the $b _ { \scriptscriptstyle { 2 s } }$ are arbitrary constants for $\lambda > s$ .The elements $y _ { \lambda s } ^ { * }$ of $\ b { Y } ^ { * }$ form the fundamental system of solutions of (160a).

From $X ^ { * } = X A$ ， $Y ^ { * } = B Y$ we obtain $Y ^ { * } X ^ { * } = B Y X A = B C A$ $= C ^ { * }$ ，or

$$
\begin{array}{c} \begin{array} { r l } & { \begin{array} { c c c c c } { 0 } \\ { 0 } \\ { 0 } \\ { \left[ c _ { 2 1 } \right]} & { c _ { 2 2 } } & { c _ { 2 n } } \\ { c _ { 1 } - } \\ { c _ { n 1 } } & { c _ { n 2 } } & { c _ { n n } } \end{array}  \left[ \begin{array} { c c c c c } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { a _ { 2 1 } } & { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { } \\ { a _ { n 1 } } & { a _ { n 2 } } & { \cdots } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] } \end{array}  \\ & { \begin{array} { r l } & { \begin{array} { r l } { \left[ c _ { 1 1 } ^ { \ast } \right]} & { c _ { 1 2 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { 1 n } ^ { \ast } } \\ { c _ { 2 1 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { 2 2 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { 2 n } ^ { \ast } } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { \ddots } \\ { c _ { n 1 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { n 2 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { n 1 } ^ { \ast } } \end{array}  } \end{array} } \\ & { = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c c } { c _ { 1 1 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { 2 1 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { 2 n } ^ { \ast } } \\ { \cdots } & { } & { } & { } \\ { c _ { n 1 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { n 2 } ^ { \ast } } & { c _ { n 2 } ^ { \ast } } \end{array} \right] . } \end{array}
$$

We can see that it is possible to choose $a _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mu k } , b _ { \scriptscriptstyle \ k \lambda }$ so that all $c _ { \kappa k } ^ { * }$ are nonzero.

If the $f _ { s i } ( t )$ are all constant, then $\gamma _ { s } + \delta _ { s } = 0$ and the order numbers of the systems (160) and (160a) are equal and of opposite sign. More generally we have

Theorem 4: The relation $\gamma _ { s } + \delta _ { s } \geq 0$ in Theorem 3 is replaced by $\gamma _ { s } + \delta _ { s } = 0$ if and only if the limit

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } \Re ( f _ { \lambda \lambda } ( \tau ) ) \ d \tau
$$

exists and is equal to $\sum { \mathfrak { z } } _ { = 1 } ^ { n } \gamma _ { \mathfrak { s } }$

Consider a system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } [ f _ { s i } ( t ) + \phi _ { s i } ( t ) ] x _ { i } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

beside the system (160),where the $\phi _ { s i } ( t )$ tend to zero for sufficiently large values of $t \geq t _ { 0 }$ .Then all solutions of (160b) tend to the solutions of (160) for $t \to \infty$ . The order numbers of the two systems are equal. This is true for $n = 1$ , but not always so for $n > 1$

Let the order numbers of the system (160b) be $\gamma _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \leq \gamma _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \leqq \cdot \cdots \leqq \gamma _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ . If for any positive ε a positive $\delta$ can be chosen so that for $| \phi _ { s i } ( t ) | \leq \delta ;$ at least for large values of $t$ we have $| \gamma _ { \ast } ^ { \prime } - \gamma _ { \ast } | \leq \varepsilon$ ，then the number $\gamma _ { k }$ is said to be unstable. If $\gamma _ { k }$ is stable,then $\gamma _ { k } ^ { \prime } = \gamma _ { k }$ follows from $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \phi _ { s i } ( t ) = 0 } \end{array}$

Thus a small change in the coefficients yields a large change in the order numbers. Perron (1930) made estimates of the order numbers.

Theorem 5: If the coefficients of the differential equations (160) satisfy

$$
\Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( t ) ) \geq P ( t ) + 2 ( n - 1 ) Q ( t ) ,
$$

$$
P ( t ) = \operatorname* { m a x } _ { \lambda = 2 } ^ { n } \Re ( f _ { \lambda \lambda } ( t ) ) , \qquad Q ( t ) = \operatorname* { m a x } _ { s \neq i } | f _ { s i } ( t ) |
$$

for sufficiently large values of $t \left( t \geq \mathbf { } t _ { 0 } \right)$ , then the largest order numbei $\gamma$ is contained between the limits

$$
\begin{array} { r }  \gamma \{ \begin{array} { l l }  \displaystyle \leq \frac { \displaystyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } [ \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( \tau ) ) + ( n - 1 ) Q ( \tau ) ] d \tau , } \\ { \displaystyle \geq \frac { \displaystyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } [ \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( \tau ) ) - ( n - 1 ) Q ( \tau ) ] d \tau . } \end{array}  } \end{array}
$$

Theorem 6: If the coeffcients of the differential equations (160) satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \underset { t  \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } f _ { s i } ( t ) = 0 } & { ( s \neq i ) , } \\ & { \underset { ( ) \times ( f _ { 1 1 } ( t ) ) } { \operatorname* { s u p } } \cong \Re ( f _ { \lambda \lambda } ( t ) ) + c } & { ( c > 0 ) , } \end{array}
$$

for $t \geq t _ { 0 } ( \lambda = 2 , . . . , n )$ , then the largest order number is stable and is equal to

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( \tau ) ) \ d \tau .
$$

In fact, from the differential equations we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Re ( f _ { s i } ( t ) \bar { x } _ { s } x _ { i } ) } } \\ & { } & { \quad \le \Re ( f _ { s s } ( t ) ) | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + Q ( t ) \sum _ { i ( i \ne s ) } \frac { 1 } { 2 } \{ | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + | x _ { i } | ^ { 2 } \} , } \end{array}
$$

or by the assumption of Theorem 5

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \leq \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( t ) ) \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } + ( n - 1 ) Q ( t ) \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \bigg ( | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } - \sum _ { s = 2 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \bigg ) } } \\ & { } & { \geq [ \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( t ) ) - ( n - 1 ) Q ( t ) ] \bigg ( | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } - \sum _ { s = 2 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \bigg ) . } \end{array}
$$

From these relations it follows that

$$
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \exp \left\{ - 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left[ \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( \tau ) ) + ( n - 1 ) Q ( \tau ) \right] d \tau \right\}
$$

decreases monotonically, and

$$
\left( | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } - \sum _ { s = 2 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \right) \exp \left\{ - 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left[ \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( \tau ) ) - ( n - 1 ) Q ( \tau ) \right] d \tau \right\}
$$

increases monotonically. Hence, if we so choose the initial value that $\scriptstyle | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } - \sum _ { 1 } ^ { n } = 2 \displaystyle | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 }$ is positive for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ,then the solution is positive and

$$
\varlimsup _ {  \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log [ | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } - \sum _ { s = 2 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } ] \equiv \varlimsup _ { t  \infty } \frac { 2 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } [ \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( \tau ) ) - ( n - 1 ) Q ( \tau ) ]
$$

or

$$
\varlimsup _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \log \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { s } | ^ { 2 } \equiv \varlimsup _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 2 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left[ \Re ( f _ { 1 1 } ( \tau ) ) - ( n - 1 ) Q ( \tau ) \right] d \tau .
$$

Theorem 7:If the coefficients of (160) satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { \underset { t  \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } f _ { s i } ( t ) = 0 } & { ( s \neq i ) , } \\ { \ncong \mathrm { ~ } } & { } \\ { \Re [ f _ { s - 1 , s - 1 } ( i ) - f _ { s s } ( t ) ] \geq c > 0 } & { ( t \geq t _ { 0 } ; s = 2 , \dots , n ) , } \end{array}
$$

and if we put

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { t } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \Re ( f _ { s s } ( \tau ) ) d \tau = \gamma _ { s } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

then the order numbers are $\gamma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \gamma _ { n }$

# 16.16PERRON'S GENERALIZATION

Perron (1930b) generalized the discussion of stability to the differential system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { s i } ( t ) x _ { i } + \phi _ { s } ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \quad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $f _ { s i } ( t )$ are arbitrary continuous and bounded functions of $t$ The question is whether the results obtained for the system in which $\begin{array} { r } { f _ { s i } ( t ) = a _ { s i } = } \end{array}$ constant $( s , i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n )$ can be transferred to the more general system (161). Perron's answer was in the negative. Perron added as the hypothesis that the system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { s i } ( t ) x _ { i } + \psi _ { s } ( t ) ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

admits always at least one bounded solution for arbitrary continuous bounded functions $\psi _ { s } ( t )$ . Whether (16la) admits always a bounded solution is known from the behavior of the homogeneous system (161) with $\phi _ { s } \equiv 0$ (Section 16.14).

The negative answer comes from the example

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = - a x _ { 1 } , \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = ( \sin \log t + \cos \log t - 2 a ) x _ { 2 } ,
$$

which admits general integrals

$$
x _ { 1 } = C _ { 1 } e ^ { - a t } , \qquad x _ { 2 } = C _ { 2 } \exp { \{ ( \sin \log t - 2 a ) t \} } .
$$

If $2 a < 1$ , then the single-parameter solution with $C _ { 2 } = 0$ has only the property that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \left( | x _ { 1 } | + | x _ { 2 } | \right) = 0 , \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \frac { | x _ { 1 } | + | x _ { 2 } | } { e ^ { - c t } } = 0
$$

for $c < a$ If $2 a > 1$ ，then these limit relations hold only if $c < \operatorname* { m i n } { ( a , 2 a - 1 ) }$

The perturbed system

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = - a x _ { 1 } , \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = ( \sin \log t + \cos \log t - 2 a ) x _ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }
$$

admits solutions $x _ { 2 }$ which converge to zero for $t \to \infty$ when $2 a < 1$ There is no solution satisfying the above limit relations.When $2 a > 1$ the solutions $x _ { 2 }$ tend to zero for $t \to \infty$ only when $C _ { 1 } = 0$

First, Perron discussed the stability character of a special system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } g _ { s i } ( t ) x _ { i } + \omega _ { s } ( t ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $g _ { s i } ( t )$ are equal to zero for $s \geq i$ ，that is,the elements below the principal diagonal of the matrix formed by $g _ { s i } ( t )$ are zero.Thus the first $\pmb { s }$ equations contain only the first $\pmb { s }$ unknown variables $x _ { i }$ ·

Theorem $\boldsymbol { \mathit { 1 } }$ :For the case in which the $g _ { s i } ( t ) , 1 \leq i \leq s \leq n _ { \cdot }$ are continuous and bounded for $t \geq 0$ the necessary and sufficient condition,for the system (162) to admit at least one bounded solution for any choice of the continuous and bounded functions $\omega _ { s } ( t )$ , is that the functions $g _ { s i } ( t )$ should satisfy the conditions with

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { t } g _ { s s } ( \tau ) d \tau = G _ { s } ( t ) ,
$$

and that for any indices $s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n$ ，either

(A)exp $\{ G _ { s } ( t ) \}$ and

$$
\exp \left\{ G _ { s } ( t ) \right\} \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left| \exp \left\{ - G _ { s } ( \tau ) \right\} \right| d \tau
$$

be both bounded, or

(B)exp $\{ G _ { s } ( t ) \}$ be unbounded but

$$
\exp \left\{ G _ { s } ( t ) \right\} \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left| \exp \left\{ - G _ { s } ( \tau ) \right\} \right| d \tau
$$

be existing and bounded.

Theorem 2: If the system (162) admits at least one bounded solution for any choice of the continuous bounded functions $\omega _ { s } ( t )$ ,i.e., if the conditions of Theorem 1 are satisfied, then for each bounded solution of the homogeneous system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } g _ { s i } ( t ) x _ { i } \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n )
$$

the relation

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { i } ( t ) | = 0
$$

holds.

For the proof of these two theorems Perron referred to the Preparatory Theorem: If $G ( t )$ is continuous for $t \geq 0$ and if

$$
e ^ { G ( t ) } , \qquad e ^ { G ( t ) } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left| e ^ { - G ( \tau ) } \right| d \tau
$$

are bounded, then

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } e ^ { G ( t ) } = 0 .
$$

In fact, we have

$$
L _ { 2 } \geq | e ^ { G ( t ) } | \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { d \tau } { L _ { 1 } } = | e ^ { G ( t ) } | \frac { t } { L _ { 1 } } ,
$$

where

$$
L _ { 1 } \geq \vert e ^ { G ( t ) } \vert , \qquad L _ { 2 } \geq \vert e ^ { G ( t ) } \vert \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \vert e ^ { - G ( \tau ) } \vert d \tau .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left| e ^ { G ( t ) } \right| \leqq L _ { 1 } L _ { 2 } / t . } \end{array}
$$

Theorems 1 and 2 are proved by induction. It can be seen that the theorems are true for $n = 1$ . Suppose that the theorems are true for $n - 1$ . The nth equation of (162) is

$$
\frac { d x _ { n } } { d t } = g _ { n n } ( t ) x _ { n } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } g _ { n i } ( t ) x _ { i } + \omega _ { n } ( t ) .
$$

We have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { x _ { n } = \exp { \{ G _ { n } ( t ) \} } \cdot \bigg ( c _ { n } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } g _ { n i } ( \tau ) x _ { i } ( \tau ) + \omega _ { n } ( \tau ) ] } } \\ & { } & { \times \exp { \{ - G _ { n } ( \tau ) \} } \cdot d \tau \bigg ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { c _ { n } = x _ { n } ( 0 ) . } \end{array}
$$

If we put $\omega _ { 1 } ( t ) = \cdots = \omega _ { n - 1 } ( t ) = 0 \mathrm { , }$ ， then we get

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } | x _ { i } ( t ) | = 0 .
$$

Choose $\omega _ { n } ( t )$ so that $\omega _ { n } ( t ) = \vert \exp \left\{ - G _ { n } ( t ) \right\} \vert / [ \exp \left\{ - G _ { n } ( t ) \right\} ]$ ; then the solution $x _ { n }$ is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { x _ { n } = \exp { \{ G _ { n } ( t ) \} } \cdot \Big ( c _ { n } + \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } [ 1 + H ( \tau ) ] | \exp { \{ - G _ { n } ( \tau ) \} } | d \tau \Big ) , } \\ & { } & { H ( t ) = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } g _ { n i } ( t ) x _ { i } ( t ) \frac { \exp { \{ - G _ { n } ( t ) \} } } { | \exp { \{ - G _ { n } ( t ) \} } | } . } \end{array}
$$

We see that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { t  \infty } H ( t ) = 0 .
$$

Hence for the existence of a bounded solution with bounded $e ^ { G ( t ) }$ the function

$$
\exp \left\{ { \cal G } _ { n } ( t ) \right\} \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } | \exp \left\{ - { \cal G } _ { n } ( \tau ) \right\} | d \tau
$$

should be bounded. If exp $\{ G _ { \pi } ( t ) \}$ is unbounded, then the solution $x _ { n }$ should be

$$
- \exp { \{ G _ { n } ( t ) \} } \cdot \int _ { \tau } ^ { \infty } | 1 + H ( \tau ) | \cdot | \exp { \{ - G _ { n } ( \tau ) \} } | d \tau
$$

for a definite choice of $c _ { n }$ . The boundedness and existence of this function are equivalent to those of

$$
\exp \left\{ G _ { n } ( t ) \right\} \cdot \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } | \exp \left\{ - G _ { n } ( \tau ) \right\} | d \tau .
$$

Thus the boundedness of this latter function is necessary. It is easily shown to be also sufficient if we refer to the preparatory theorem.

Theorem 3: If the conditions of Theorem 1 are satisfed, then in the general bounded solution of the system (162) the number of arbitrary constants is equal to the number of the indices $\pmb { s }$ for which the condition (A） is satisfied.The initial values $x _ { s } ( 0 ) = c _ { s }$ can be arbitrarily chosen so that their index s satisfies the condition (A).If the condition (A) is satisfed for all indices $\pmb { S }$ ，then all solutions are bounded. If the condition (B) is satisfied for all indices $s$ ，then there is only a single bounded solution.

Theorem 4: If,among the conditions of Theorem 1, the $k$ indices $\pmb { s }$ satisfy the condition (A） and the remaining $n - k$ indices $\pmb { s }$ satisfy the condition (B),then the linear homogeneous system (162） with $\omega _ { 1 } ( t ) = \cdots = \omega _ { n } ( t ) = 0$ admits just $k$ linearly independent bounded solutions $( k = 0 , 1 , \ldots , n )$ . When $k = 0$ there is no bounded solution except the trivial solution $x _ { s } ( t ) = 0$

Next consider the differential system

$$
\frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { s } g _ { s i } ( t ) x _ { i } + \chi _ { s } ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ) \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

where the $\chi _ { s } ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ are continuous and bounded in the domain: $t \geq 0 , x _ { j }$ arbitrary $( j = 1 , \ldots , n )$

Theorem 5: Let $g _ { s i } ( t ) \left( 1 \leq i \leq s \leq n \right)$ be continuous and bounded for $t \geq 0$ .The necessary and sufficient condition for the differential system (163) with any arbitrary functions $\chi _ { s } ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ of the above nature to admit only bounded solutions is that the functions

$$
\exp { \{ G _ { s } ( t ) \} } \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \left| \exp { \{ - G _ { s } ( \tau ) \} } \right| d \tau \qquad ( s = 1 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

with

$$
G _ { s } ( t ) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } g _ { s s } ( \tau ) d \tau ,
$$

should be bounded.

If this condition is satisfied and if the ${ \pmb \chi } _ { \pmb s }$ satisfy further conditions

$$
